<a id="t-ffda49d1aa"></a>
## Thread: C++ Translation to Cobol — 12 msgs

### C++ Translation to Cobol

- **From:** "Joseph Lazarus" <brenjoe@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T02:20:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w3pH6.6162$kA1.293981@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Can anyone help me translate this code in C++ to Cobol, much appreciated.

#include <iostream.h>

void main()

{
int sum = 0;
int x;
cout<< "Enter a number: ";

cin>> x;

for (int i = 1; i <= x; ++i)

sum = sum + i;

cout <<sum  <<endl;

}
```

#### ↳ Re: C++ Translation to Cobol

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T03:36:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-e7LG5bYlZM8W@24-108-102-82.ivideon.com>`
- **References:** `<w3pH6.6162$kA1.293981@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Tue, 1 May 2001 02:20:12, "Joseph Lazarus" 
<brenjoe@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> Can anyone help me translate this code in C++ to Cobol, much appreciated.
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

If this is a contest do I get a prize?

       identification division.
       program-id. thing.
       author.     Lorne Sunley.

       environment division.
       input-output section.
       file-control.

       data division.
       file section.

       working-storage section.
       01  in-number      pic 9(9).
       01  work-total      pic 9(9).
       procedure division.
       001-start section.
       010-start.

            display "enter a number: " with no advancing
            accept in-number
            move zero to work-total
            perform varying idx from 1 by 1 until idx = in-number

                compute work-total = work-total + idx

            end-perform
            display " "
            display total

       end program thing.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: C++ Translation to Cobol

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T11:43:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5E7622B230FFF1B2.B8D7E54ECAF29D85.7A7ADF67B501B584@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<w3pH6.6162$kA1.293981@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-e7LG5bYlZM8W@24-108-102-82.ivideon.com>`

```
On Tue, 01 May 2001 03:36:30 GMT, lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca enlightened
us:

>On Tue, 1 May 2001 02:20:12, "Joseph Lazarus" 
><brenjoe@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
>       end program thing.

Sorry you don't win the prize because your Cobol code has an error!

Line in error:

display total

total was neither previously defined nor part of a calculation.
Correct statement should be:

display work-total.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

wrote:

:>Can anyone help me translate this code in C++ to Cobol, much appreciated.

:>#include <iostream.h>

:>void main()

:>{
:>int sum = 0;

:>cin>> x;

:>for (int i = 1; i <= x; ++i)

:>sum = sum + i;

else sum = ((x + 1) * x) / 2;

:>cout <<sum  <<endl;

:>}

##### ↳ ↳ Re: C++ Translation to Cobol

- **From:** Anonymous <anonymous@anonymous.anonymous>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T02:02:44+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3af0164d_2@anonymous>`
- **References:** `<w3pH6.6162$kA1.293981@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <miqsetg8eogrgs09d4j2fcj5s0dmhkrkn8@4ax.com>`

```
Congratulations Binyamin!

(As usual, your solution is efficient, effective, and elegant.)

I was wondering if anyone would use this method.

The story goes that when Gauss was 8 years old, he and his classmates were
misbehaving. The teacher gave them an imposition. Add up all the numbers
from 1 to 100.

Gauss immediately wrote down 5050 and exclaimed, "There it lies!" (probably
"Da, ist er!" actually...<G>) He had intuitively realised that he could "add
the extremities and fold in the middle". To this day it is known as Gauss'
algorithm.

I believe that the lesson here is that it is important to UNDERSTAND what is
required, not just do what is asked. The exercise was "Translate this C++
into COBOL". What it should have been was "Duplicate this C++ functionality
in COBOL".

Your solution was infinitely better.

Pete Dashwood (lurking as Anonymous...)

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T20:27:58+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-ACD79B.20275801052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AED6D61.AC16BEF7@brazee.net>`

```
In article <3AED6D61.AC16BEF7@brazee.net>,
 Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> Jerry@Westrick.com wrote:
> 
> If you are trying to be unreadable, Cobol can't touch C.
> 
Rubbish! APL is the ultimate write once programming language.
Unfortunately, the designers of C and C++ decided to go down the road of 
using special characters for syntax elements FOR is a classic. The brace 
"{" is another. Just like APL. Just like an engineer (fine people that 
they are). Unlike COBOL which is ALL english words.

But the complaint is that it's too wordy. If you feel COBOL is too 
wordy, then I respectfully submit you should get another job. The vast 
majority of Software engineering is writing documentation and 
specifications that other people can understand. This is done in english 
words. All other attempts at communication having been tried, have 
failed. The actual programming effort is quite small.

By using a combination of literate programming and COBOL it is possible 
to write the documentation into the program at the same time as 
constructing the code. Because COBOL is english like, the code can be 
used to illustrate the documentation.

The vast amount of comments in these more concise programming languages 
are the programmer describing the really nifty short cuts used to 
performe a task. This can even occur in COBOL programs, especially with 
STRING and UNSTRING.

e.g.
* move input record to output record.
   input_record.a = output_record.a;
   input_record.b = output_record.b;

instead of

   MOVE CORRESPONDING input-record to output-record.
```

which were cryptic and unreadable I still think C would win hands down.

> The vast 
>majority of Software engineering is writing documentation and 
>specifications that other people can understand. This is done in english 
>words. All other attempts at communication having been tried, have 
>failed. The actual programming effort is quite small.

this is true, however, if you need to actually produce physical
product, instead of spinning wheels on your computer, you need to
interface to C, which handles device drivers. cobol works for
databases well, and it can handle classes ,documentation, help files
and even compilation pretty well. but if you have two clocks which
have to fire in certain timing, you're going to have to pass it thru
c, or you could write a cobol program to make a compilation script.
i'd guess, done correctly, 95% of any computer code can be done in
cobol.

G Moore wrote:

> > The vast

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

Some drivers have been written in assembler.  I don't know what most of my
IBM mainframe interfaces have been written in.   But anyway, I am a big
advocate of not using one tool to do all things.   If you want a GUI,
business rules, database rules, and a report, why not use something like
Java, Cobol, SQL, and Crystal Reports for your application - all plugged into
drivers written in languages closer to the hardware?

What's the best tool for building a house?   Well, we could graft a saw blade
to the back of a hammer and have a hammer-saw...

#### ↳ Sv: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** "Per Trangb���k" <per@trangbaek.dk>
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T20:38:03+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<koDH6.49$T22.6685@news010.worldonline.dk>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local>`

```
Hi Jerry
I'm afraid the only Cobol dialect I know of is Cobol 370 from IBM
mainframes.

In this you can have local variables in forms of inline programs (?)

       identification division.
         program-id. test.
       data division.
       working-storage section
       01 x pic x.
       procedure division.
           call 'my-inline-test2' using x, y, z
           goback.

       identification division.
         program-id my-inline-test2
       data division.
       linkage section
       01 x pic x.

       procedure division using x
           display x.
           exit.
       end program test2.
       end program test.

Per

Life is just another example of bad programming
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T22:39:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local>`

```


Jerry@Westrick.com wrote:

> Hello Everybody.
>
> First off, I'm not a newbie (over 25 years expirience
> programming in over a dozen languages).

   [snipped a lot of disclaimer]

>

> Having said all that, here is my 2 bits:
>
> 1) Cobol can be written in such a way as to be extreemly readable,
>    and thereby extreemly maintainable.  When done so it is (in my
>    opinion) THE most maintainable language I've ever used.

Prior to the ANSI-85 standard, it was impossible to write proper
structured code in COBOL.   Most attempts to do so resulted in a
hideous mess.   Contemporary COBOL is actually pretty good.
There is still too much temptation to construct programs built
on global variables and unparameterized Peform statements, but
I see better coding being done by newer COBOL programmers
who are accustomed to other languages.   Those who know only
COBOL tend to write sloppy code.

As for maintainable, I would count Ada as the most maintainable
language I have ever used.   It even includes a decimal type and a
set of libraries compatible with COBOL.

C is its own virus!   C++ continues to be as messy as C, including a
lot of pathological constructs(e.g., the silly design of the switch
statement).

Eiffel is quite good, but does not have the business numerics built-in the
way COBOL and Ada do.

Java has been called C++ minus minus.  It continues to support much of the
silliness found in the rest of the C family of languages.

C# looks like it might be pretty good.  Lots of mistakes one can make too
easily with other members of the C family have been eliminated.

ObjectCOBOL might turn out to be a good thing, but it is too early to tell
since there is not sufficient (make that mature) support for it yet.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T06:53:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Lao Xiao Hai wrote:

> C# looks like it might be pretty good.  Lots of mistakes one can make too
> easily with other members of the C family have been eliminated.

Maybe.  But its chances of success depend more on our attitudes about
Microsoft than anything else.

PL/I was a language which was perceived as being twice as good as CoBOL and
controlled by IBM.   (perception is everything in marketing).   Twice as good
isn't good enough to make us switch (ask people who use CoBOL's Report
Writer), and people were tired of the perception that IBM was telling them
what to do.

C# appears to have a similar political background with the added image of "If
I can't steal Java from you, then I will make my own game".

The amount of reality in these perceptions and appearances really doesn't
matter.  We all know what it is like to get our users' approval to sell our
systems.


Which brings us back to CoBOL.   Will CoBOL's image be improved by the next
standards?   Does OO CoBOL make it available to more shops?  Does returning
Report Writer to CoBOL do anything except making compilers more expensive?

It doesn't matter how good these improvements are (as long as they work
correctly) - how will the marketplace accept them?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-05T10:57:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> Lao Xiao Hai wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Microsoft than anything else.

Good point.  It will be interesting to see whether Microsoft's influence
extends to the point of being able to introduce an entirely new language.
It is my understanding that there is already dissent within its own
ranks among the Visual Basic crowd.

> PL/I was a language which was perceived as being twice as good as CoBOL and
> controlled by IBM.   (perception is everything in marketing).   Twice as good
> isn't good enough to make us switch (ask people who use CoBOL's Report
> Writer), and people were tired of the perception that IBM was telling them
> what to do.

PL/I was OK for its time.  Looking at it now, compared to available alternatives
such as Eiffel and Ada 95, it seems pretty weak.

> C# appears to have a similar political background with the added image of "If
> I can't steal Java from you, then I will make my own game".

Perhaps.  On the positive side, the Microsoft developers did learn a thing or
two from Java and avoided some mistakes from the C family that persist in Java.
Also, whereas Sun seems to have concluded ISO standardization is not a good
idea for Java, Microsoft has indicated its willingness to explore that avenue.
It is very strange for Microsoft to pursue standardization, so this is a major
departure from its previous policies.

> Which brings us back to CoBOL.   Will CoBOL's image be improved by the next
> standards?   Does OO CoBOL make it available to more shops?  Does returning
> Report Writer to CoBOL do anything except making compilers more expensive?

This kind of question came up for Ada during the design of the 1995 ISO standard.

The decision was to develop a set of annexes to the language that could
accomodate
a wide variety of special needs.  The annexes add no new syntax or semantics but
simply use the existing language definition to describe them.   One consequence
was
that the original Ada Language Reference Manual (1983)  went from 14 chapters to
thirteen.

Object COBOL could probably benefit from this approach.   In my humble opinion,
some of what we in COBOL could be deferred to similar kinds of annexes,
especially
the Report Writer.   In this way, the annexes could be defined as special needs
libraries
that can be augmented and updated independently of changes to the language.

> It doesn't matter how good these improvements are (as long as they work
> correctly) - how will the marketplace accept them?

The marketplace rules, I suppose.   We still use the QWERTY keyboard even though
it is
less efficient than other designs, in particular, the DVORAK keyboard.   People
still write
programs in COBOL-68 (Yes, I ran across a shop like that not long ago) even
though the
compilers are no longer supported.   It seems the ENTER SYBOLIC feature was
removed
from the COBOL 74 compiler and this shop has huge amounts of code that use it.

It is rare that anyone makes the language decision on the basis of technical
superiority. If
they did, the language landscape would look much different.  In truth, few of
those making
language choices really understand much about programming language design.   Most
just
take the path of least resistance or fall in love with what seems to be pretty at
first sight.
By the time they are married to their decision, discovering they must live with
the previously
unseen ugliness beneath the surface, they are supporting a software progeny of
such huge
proportions it is too expensive (or too embarrassing) to get a divorce.
```

Interesting observation about having annexes to the language standard. As I *know*

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-07T13:10:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com...

<<Interesting observation about having annexes to the language standard. As
I *know* there are at least five members of J4 participating here, (Bill,
Chuck, Don, Stephen and Thane) - I hope they will take note of your comment,
digest and consider.>>

I wasn't there for the votes on this subject, but I have discussed it at
length with some of my fellow J4 members on more than one occasion.

One of the complaints that has been leveled at the earlier standards
(specifically '85) is that the modularity of the compiler (originally eight,
subsequently nine "standard" modules and four "optional" ones), multiple
levels of implementation in all modules but one (RPW),  and three categories
of COBOL implementation subsets independent of the presence or absence of
optional modules, led to a WIDE variety of *conformant* implementations that
were incompatible with each other.

Stated another way, the only constructs that could be guaranteed compilable
by any ANSI-85-compliant compiler were the minimum subsets of the NUC, SEQ
and IPC modules; it was the implementors choice what else to provide, at
which level, and in which combinations.  It was perfectly legitimate
according to the standard to have a compiler that didn't support Indexed
I/O, but DID support Report Writer and every "widget" of Sort-Merge.   It
was perfectly OK to compile RD's but syntax READ...NEXT RECORD.

One of the main goals of COBOL is portability, and the optionality of
features, levels and components within the language is counter to that goal
because it *supports* the development of incompatible implementations and
limits severely the choices of a user who wishes to produce an application
that's truly portable across implementations.  That is the overriding sense
that J4 seems to have received from the *user* community.   This was
discussed again and again and again until it became a greasy spot on the
pavement that used to be a horse quite a long time ago.    And, from all I
can determine, the issue of application and program portability won out.

There are still some who feel some of the features of the FCD should be
optional or even absent altogether (the most common facility I've heard this
about is the screen facility, but I have heard grumblings about others).
But I think all the arguments in favor of having a multiplicity of dialects
of "standard" COBOL have been carefully considered by J4, and rejected in
favor of a *single* definition of what "standard" COBOL contains, precisely
to foster inter-implementation portability.

In any case, the decision as to what features of the new standard COBOL the
user will include in his program remain that user's decision.     But I
believe the intent of J4 is that for all implementations conforming to the
new standard, the *user* will make that choice, not the *compiler
implementor* claiming that his compiler conforms to that standard.     To
the application designer who thinks, for example, that ACCEPT <screen> is
ten years behind the times, he is free to direct that it be eschewed, just
as another application designer who thinks it's the bee's knees is free to
direct that it be embraced.  Making the construct optional has the effect of
limiting the second application designer's choices, at the expense of the
first.

The point here is to maximize the *users'* choices, and that has the
side-effect of limiting the *implementor's* choices as to which features he
will include in his standards-compliant COBOL compilers.  And I think that's
the right objective, and the right choice.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-07T13:23:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d707v$94s$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
I wrote:

<< Making the construct optional has the effect of limiting the second
application designer's choices, at the expense of the first.>>

I lost track of my point here.  Please read instead:

<<Making the construct optional has no negative impact on the first
application designer's choices at all, but places limits on those of the
second designer.>>

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-07T19:21:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
I agree with Chuck's summary of the "motivation" behind the removal of
"modules" and "subsets" from the draft of the next Standard.

HOWEVER, it is worth "mentioning" that the "Processor Dependent" list *does*
explicitly allow a CONFORMING implementation to "omit" certain features -
depending on any "unstated" reason - or because of "market decisions".  Some
of the features that are in this list (as the FCD stands today) are:

   "Character based" Screen Section (Accept/Display)
   File sharing/ record locking
   READ PREVIOUS

HOWEVER, neither OO or Report-Writer are *not* in this category
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-08T13:09:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d9cpv$209$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3AF7EF9A.D61E900F@brazee.net>`

```
For clarity
  Report Writer is not in the processor dependent list
  OO is not in the processor dependent list

Unlike
  Screen Section IS in the processor dependent list
  File share/record locking is in the processor dependent list
  READ PREVIOUS is in the processor dependent list

In addition,
  USAGE BINARY, PACKED-DECIMAL, BINARY-short/-long/etc FLOAT-short/long/etc
     are in the processor dependent list
  REEL and FOR REMOVAL are in the processor dependent list
  DELETE statement is in the processor dependent list

In total, there are 30 items currently in the "Processor Dependent list"

FYI - just to confuse matters (slightly)
  There is still ONE thing in the OPTIONAL category.  That is the
(identified as OBSOLETE) Communication Section/Facility.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-08T10:29:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net...

<<HOWEVER, it is worth "mentioning" that the "Processor Dependent" list
*does* explicitly allow a CONFORMING implementation to "omit" certain
features - depending on any "unstated" reason - or because of "market
decisions".  Some of the features that are in this list (as the FCD stands
today) are:

    "Character based" Screen Section (Accept/Display)
    File sharing/ record locking
    READ PREVIOUS

HOWEVER, neither OO or Report-Writer are *not* in this category>>

Yes, that's right.

In fact, "processor dependent" looks in many ways a whole lot like
"optional", and I note from the J4 meeting minutes that at one point there
was some sentiment to call it that.  I wasn't yet on J4 at the time the most
recent vote on this subject was taken, but I strongly suspect at least part
of the reason it was defeated is that in the case of the term "processor
dependent" there's some impetus for the implementor to be placed in a
position of explaining why the feature isn't present on some concrete
grounds; in the case of the latter term, "because I didn't feel like it, so
there!" could be perceived as an entirely adequate response.

I think J4 felt the reasons for *omitting* a processor-dependent facility
had to be rather stronger than mere whim, though the "market positioning"
phrase in the FCD is arguably a huge loophole.

My personal suspicion is that many if not most of the final negotiations,
and perhaps the most contentious discussions, between national
representatives at WG4 in the development of the International Standard will
involve what the "processor dependent" list should and should not include.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net>`

standard except, say Report Writer - how could it best market that product?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-08T13:48:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net>`

```
The draft Standard is CLEAR that if an "add-on product" provides support for
some "required" feature, then that compiler could "claim" full conformance.

Therefore, if (for example) IBM were to continue to "support" (use?) the RW
"add-on" product, then they could claim "full conformance" to the next
Standard (assuming they do everything else).

On the other hand, my crystal ball says that very few (if any) compiler
vendors will actually implement the FULL draft Standard.  and those that do,
certainly will NOT in the "short-term" (i.e. before 2005 or later).  I could
be totally wrong on this - but

 As there are no current plans for a new FIPS (US government) Standard that
will match this revision:
 As there are no current plans for any validation suite for the new Standard
 As *some* of the required features are either controversial (RW and
VALIDATE) or hard to implement (Standard arithmetic and exception handling)

   I just question that when/if there will ever be ANY "fully conforming"
implementations

HOWEVER, only time will tell
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-08T15:06:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d9qk7$cu0$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...

<<On the other hand, my crystal ball says that very few (if any) compiler
vendors will actually implement the FULL draft Standard.  and those that do,
certainly will NOT in the "short-term" (i.e. before 2005 or later).  I could
be totally wrong on this - but

As there are no current plans for a new FIPS (US government) Standard that
will match this revision:>>

I believe it would be inappropriate for FIPS to move on this standard, one
way or another, before it was adopted.  That they have not yet done so is
not a surprise; indeed, if they had done so, I would view that as an action
entirely out of order.   If ISO/IEC and ANSI adopt the standard, it does not
make sense to me for FIPS explicitly to reject it (although well they
might); more importantly, arguments based on their failure to move to
conform to a standard that hasn't been adopted yet strike me as premature.

<<As there are no current plans for any validation suite for the new
Standard>>

It's true that the *US Government* discontinued sponsorship of compiler
validation some years back.    While I'm not *personally aware* of any plans
to produce a validation suite for the new Standard, I find the flat
assertion that out of six billion people on this planet *not one* is
currently planning to produce such a suite at any point in the future smacks
a bit more of omniscience than makes me comfortable!      ;-)

Without a *mandated* validation suite, conformance to the standard becomes
something of a "gentleman's agreement" among the implementors, I agree, but
I don't necessarily think that's a bad thing.  I would personally like to
see a validation suite made available and a formal process of validation
reinstated in a more public way than is currently the case.

Anyway, I suspect strongly the Corrections Amendment isn't reflected by
tests in the last mandated version of the validation suite, and I'm not even
sure the Intrinsic Functions amendment was.    The process of validation was
moved from "mandatory compliance with government mandate" to "voluntary
submission to verification by a private enterprise" quite a while ago.
This is hardly a new issue with the FCD.

<<As *some* of the required features are either controversial (RW and
VALIDATE) or hard to implement (Standard arithmetic and exception
handling)>>

Specifically as to RPW and Validate:  I've already noted elsewhere that at
least one company has seen a market for these features and provides software
to handle that feature.  The combination of a compiler lacking (only) such a
feature and a software product providing it comprise an implementation of
COBOL that conforms to the standard.  The absence of RPW and Validate in an
otherwise-conforming *compiler* is irrelevant if a combination of that
compiler and another software product form a conforming *implementation* of
COBOL, whether the compiler vendor markets the third-party product or not!

I happen to agree that both standard arithmetic and exception handling are
not easy on the compiler implementor.  But I also would contend that these
are BIG improvements to COBOL, in the first case for portability, in the
second case for program reliability.   And it was pointed out to me by a J4
member *many* years ago that whether something is difficult for the
implementor is fundamentally irrelevant to what is best for the COBOL
community.

<<I just question that when/if there will ever be ANY "fully conforming"
implementations>>

As far as I'm concerned,  I firmly believe there *will* be "fully
conforming" implementations once the standard is approved, though I agree it
won't be the day after it's published.  There may not be as many such
implementations as there are for '85 COBOL, or '74 COBOL, or '68 COBOL for
that matter, but I do believe firmly there will be more than one such
implementation.

<<HOWEVER, only time will tell>>

ONLY once the standard has been approved, and only IF the standard has been
approved.   That's one of my big concerns about a "fatalistic attitude"
toward the FCD and its complexities.

UNTIL such time as it is adopted, the question as to how well it will be
accepted and whether users will embrace its features simply can't be
answered.

To paraphrase and expand on "Field of Dreams":

If we don't "build" the new standard, we KNOW noone will flock to it; there
won't be anything solid for implementors to conform to, and the users will
have no single design to depend on.   A rejected standard is oxymoronic.

If we do "build" the new standard, I see four fundamental, and real,
possibilities:  implementors will ignore the standard; implementors will
build conformant compilers that nobody will use; implementors will build
conformant compilers whose new features nobody (or few) will use;
implementors will build conformant compilers whose new features will be
embraced.

Why prevent implementors from even exploring the possibility of the latter
two eventualities by short-circuiting the standards process?    Why
discourage or even prevent an implementor from marketing a
COBOL2002-compliant compiler to a (say) COBOL85 user who has no intention of
using *any* of the new features, but simply wants to make his code *more
portable*?   If that implementor sees the possibility of making a profit at
it, why not let him?

Time will indeed tell, unless we decide first what it is that time will tell
us and act upon that prediction.  I'd rather see the standard adopted first
and see from there how widely its features are accepted.

Is it possible no implementors at all will build 100%-conformant compilers?
Yes, I think it is, although I think that eventuality unlikely.   Does the
failure of any implementor to produce such a compiler mark the  demise of
COBOL?  No, I don't think so; for example, if experience shows that certain
features really should have been "optional" instead of mandatory, future
amendments and revisions can reflect that, and implementors could well
simply "skip a standard".

Do I believe the FCD is a perfect document?  No; I do not believe *any*
human endeavor is perfect.    To paraphrase one wag, "the number of errors
in a document is inversely proportional to its triviality".  I do not
believe the standard is trivial, and despite whatever flaws,
inconsistencies, discrepancies, omissions or tardiness of which it might be
rightly or wrongly accused, I strongly believe continued progress toward its
adoption benefits the COBOL community far more than further delays to ensure
its absolute perfection, and I strongly believe abandoning the adoption of
this standard on the grounds that nobody will care would be a disaster for
the future of COBOL.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Warren G. Simmons <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-08T23:03:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103_989359414@micron-450>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9qk7$cu0$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
On Tue, 8 May 2001 15:06:15 -0700, "Chuck Stevens" 
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> 
> William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> vendors will actually implement the FULL draft Standard.  and those 
that do,
> certainly will NOT in the "short-term" (i.e. before 2005 or later).  I could
> be totally wrong on this - but
> 
> As there are no current plans for a new FIPS (US government) Standard 

---------------------very large clip supporting the standards effort------------

I believe 
Stevens has covered a lot of ground in this post. Vendors want to provide 
what the customer wants.  Of course the customer does not always 
understand or care about the standard part so long as his but is covered.

I am aquainted with hardware features that have developed to satisfy 
customer needs including those to make COBOL easier to compile, and 
faster to execute.  Because the people working on the standard are by in 
large vendor related people, they hesitate to return to their boss and say 
"we have to engineer something into our computers to let the users have 
this feature." Often the designers of the hardware are not talking to their 
customers as much as the compiler writers do. These are problems I 
don't think, as a group, we can solve. However, letting things work out in 
the market place has a way of bringing about what is needed.  Sherman 
Anti-trust laws may make it difficult for vendors to agree on something to 
level the field, but those responsible for designing COBOL and approving 
vendors based upon software specifications should exercise some 
"authority."  I see it as a two way street. Consequently, it would not be 
unusual for "users" to gather together to construct a set of tests for 
compilers, and give those that pass a good housekeeping seal. As I watch 
the TCOBOL project proceed, it occurs to me at least that compiler 
writers have the tests needed, but will not share them.  How does that 
keep the tax payers of the country from developing their own set, and 
rating them?

Leadership need not be G.W. style, nor Clinton style. IT can be group 
style. It will take someone willing to spend some money (time), and ignore 
the on going efforts of the official paths.  It would be noticed like the 
Consumer's Union, etc.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-08T15:35:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d9sbe$ei1$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...

<<As there are no current plans for a new FIPS (US government) Standard that
will match this revision:>>

I believe this to be a red herring.  According to
http://www.itl.nist.gov/fipspubs.withdraw.htm :

"The former Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS) listed below
have been withdrawn [the list included the FIPS COBOL standard]. Many of
these FIPS had adopted voluntary industry standards for Federal government
use, but had not been updated to adopt current or revised industry
standards. In some cases, commercial products implementing the standards are
widely available.

"It is no longer necessary for the government to mandate standards that
duplicate industry standards. Federal government departments and agencies
are directed by the National Technology Transfer and Advancement Act of 1995
(P.L. 104-113), to use technical industry standards that are developed in
voluntary consensus standards bodies."

If the FCD is adopted by any recognized industry standards group, FIPS is
expected to conform to it unless it explicitly decides to adopt something
different, as I read the citation.  There is no need for FIPS to take
explicit action to adopt it; they are bound by it simply because it's a
formal industry standard adopted by a recognized consensus standards body.

        -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T12:45:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9der39$sd3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I alluded to this earlier, but I realized I wasn't really clear, and my
response was also less complete than I would have liked.

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...

<<On the other hand, my crystal ball says that very few (if any) compiler
vendors will actually implement the FULL draft Standard.  and those that do,
certainly will NOT in the "short-term" (i.e. before 2005 or later).  I could
be totally wrong on this - but   <premises snipped>  I just question that
when/if there will ever be ANY "fully conforming" implementations>>

In another response, I wrote:

<<If we do "build" the new standard, I see four fundamental, and real,
possibilities: ...>>

Actually, I see FIVE basic possibilities (and different ones may apply to
different implementors, and different groups of users):

(1)  implementors will ignore the standard altogether;
(2)  implementors will build conformant compilers that nobody will use;
(3)  implementors will build conformant compilers whose new features nobody
(or few) will use;
(4)  implementors will build conformant compilers whose new features will be
embraced;
(new):  (5)  implementors will build compilers that include features from,
and (in isolation) compliant with, the standard.

To take a couple of fairly trivial examples, I believe a COBOL compiler that
includes the in-line comment and standard compiler directive features of the
FCD is arguably more attractive to the user than a COBOL compiler without
these features.   The features, in and of themselves, are an enhancement to
the *language*, regardless of which dialect of that language is being
discussed.

The same applies to most of the major and minor facilities of the FCD; the
features (insofar as they do not directly interact) are *independently*
valuable, and *any* compiler that contains them has a higher feature content
than an otherwise-identical compiler that does not.

To me, the specification of the rules for and semantics of the *individual
features* in and of itself is sufficient justification for the adoption of
the standard whether or not anyone actually implements all the features.
The COBOL standards have always been used as a "rule book" by implementors
to guide them in building enhancements to their compilers (I've already
mentioned a specific example of this elsewhere), and I think that the
presence of that document recording consensus as to how things *ought to be*
implemented is a Good Thing.

While I do believe that there will be more than one COBOL compiler
implemented in total conformance with the final standard, my point is that
if SOMEBODY produces a fully-featured OO facility, or all the syntax and
semantics of VALIDATE, or of RPW, or of Standard Arithmetic, or even in-line
comments and standard compiler directives, in a way that is syntactically
and semantically conformant with the new standard,  in combination or in
isolation, the user of that feature in such a compiler, and thus the COBOL
commununity,  has benefited from that standard.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T15:37:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9deu6e$6ae$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9der39$sd3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
I agree with Chuck that it is HIGHLY likely that many (most?) COBOL vendors
will implement at least SOME of the features from the FCD in a "compliant"
way.  (Some as "alternatives" to continued support for existing features,
e.g.

 -SET ADDRESS OF linkage-section-without-based-clause will continue to be
supported by those vendors that support it today
 - RETURNING will be supported on the EXIT PROGRAM (and GoBack) statements -
as well as on the Procedure Division header
 - AcuCorp will continue to support GUI screen I/O via Accept/Display,
    etc, ....

I do disagree (however) with Chuck that the "best" way for portability of
such features is to have a FULL Standard that "few if any" implementors
actually implement.  It seems LIKELY to me (and I certainly COULD BE WRONG)
that many (most?) vendors will use this Standard the same way that they used
the "old" JOD (Journal of Development), i.e. will "pick and choose" which
features to implement - and will implement in a "conforming" manner those
items that they DECIDE to implement.

As to the "question" of which features which implementors will provide MUCH
LESS which features programmers will actually use, even my "crystal ball" is
pretty murky on this one.  I would *guess* that the "small features" will
see actual implementations faster than the "biggies" - but when it comes to
OO, I just don't know.  Will those implementors with "conflicting"
implementations provide a changed or optional version?  Will those
implementors who don't provide OO COBOL today, actually add it?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T13:30:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dhi3g$lq9$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9der39$sd3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9deu6e$6ae$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9deu6e$6ae$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net...

<<I do disagree (however) with Chuck that the "best" way for portability of
such features is to have a FULL Standard that "few if any" implementors
actually implement.>>

No, Bill, I don't claim that.  Please don't put words in my mouth.

What I assert is that a COBOL program that contains "<<LISTING ON" is more
portable, with respect to standard Compiler Directives, to other vendors'
systems when those target systems also have standard Compiler Directives
than a program that contains "$ SET LIST" would be.   And furthermore, I
think that's true whether the compiler in question is otherwise conformant
to Third, Second or even First Standard COBOL.

<<It seems LIKELY to me (and I certainly COULD BE WRONG) that many (most?)
vendors will use this Standard the same way that they used the "old" JOD
(Journal of Development), i.e. will "pick and choose" which  features to
implement - and will implement in a "conforming" manner those items that
they DECIDE to implement.>>

I agree that that is likely.  And while the *best* approach to portability
is for every potential vendor of a COBOL compiler to build a product that's
100% compliant with the standard and ensure that it is available on the day
after the standard is adopted, I remain unconvinced that expecting that to
be the case is anything but quixotically idealistic.   In the meantime,
providing compliant *facilities*, one facility at a time, is OK by me.

<<As to the "question" of which features which implementors will provide
MUCH LESS which features programmers will actually use, even my "crystal
ball" is  pretty murky on this one.  I would *guess* that the "small
features" will  see actual implementations faster than the "biggies">>

... given that I am convinced that if I wanted to and if I wanted to go to
the trouble to convince my management that it was worthwile doing so, I
could implement, for example, inline comments in a matter of a very few
days, in a COBOL(68) compiler of my acquaintance, I'd assert that the fact
that easy-to-implement features are likely to show up more quickly than
harder-to-implement ones should not be grounds for alarm or concern, or even
a surprise, to anyone.

<< - but when it comes to OO, I just don't know.  Will those implementors
with conflicting" implementations provide a changed or optional version?
Will those implementors who don't provide OO COBOL today, actually add it?>>

And I don't think we're likely to find out until and unless the standard is
adopted.

    -Chuck Stevens

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T21:12:24+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-2C7E4A.21122312052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9der39$sd3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9deu6e$6ae$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9dhi3g$lq9$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
I have been following this thread with interest.

Everybody seems to be arguing about whether the COBOL standard should be 
ratified or not, or whether the standard is good or not, or wether the 
standard was produced in a timely fashion or not.

THOSE are NOT the problem.

The problem is (OO enhancements not withstanding) that the language is 
not close enough to those who use it. One of the advantages of C is that 
there are no language elements to implement IO. So a LIBRARY was created 
to accomplish this task. Any new type of IO is added quickly and the C 
community moves onto the task of implementing the problem at hand.

I am not advocating this approach for COBOL, but it seems to me that the 
COBOL community would be better served with compilers developed on the 
GPL Open Source lines. New language elements could be added by request 
to the code managers - who would decide if and how to implement the 
request.

In todays environment, is there ANY reason why COBOL should not have 
native statements to interface to ALL of the operating system features? 
(NOT merely IO (DISK predominately) as in the past).

Why can't we EXECUTE a unix or MVS command.
Why can't we DELETE or RENAME files or datasets.
Why can't we COMMNICATE with other processes.
Why can't we WAIT or LOCK on semaphores or ECB's.
I could go on....

As I see it, only an open source compiler will allow us the freedom to 
pursue these issues and create compilers that are better in sync with 
their operating environments.

By the way - WHO SAYS portablility is a desirable thing?
JAVA is supposed to be portable, I don,t see it doing anything other 
than enhancing the user interface. It's interpretation model is too slow 
for server purposes - It's ok to use 90% of a 400 Mhz pentium to do 
something for a single user, quite another thing to use 90% of a 
mainframe to support the same single user.

But I digress. Portability has an overated pedigree.
Portability necessarily means removing all the various enhancements that 
manufacturers make to improve their machines performance over the other 
manufacturers. Why as a user, would you invest millions in a platform 
and then deliberately not make use of it's best features.

It is my contention that companies that change platform do not do so for 
performance or economic reasons, but usually for fashion reasons.

If management put as much effort into upgrading the existing 
applications and facilities as they do experimenting with and attempting 
to impliment the latest fashon, then maybe their companies would be able 
to offer the investors more than a 2 to 3 percent return on investment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-13T02:42:57+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3afd4c1e_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9der39$sd3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9deu6e$6ae$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9dhi3g$lq9$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <josephk-2C7E4A.21122312052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
Joe,

This is an excellent post and you raise very good points.

Let's suppose you are right and we all back the moves to get facilities like
POST and WAIT on semaphores (just as an example) to be added to the
Language.

Do you have any idea how to go about doing this? Have you any idea of the
forms that must be filled, approaches that must be made, long waits while
committees sit and consider the case, then re-sit, then argue over syntax,
then re-sit, then argue over requirement, then re-sit, then offer a draft
for public review or (more likely...) the judgement comes down from Valhalla
"Sorry, we can't make this part of the standard until 2020"? (Translation:
"Please go away and stop bothering us; we have loftier things on our
minds...")

I endorse your point about Open Source style review 100%. And this is just
one of a number of possible scenarios that could be used to make COBOL a
dynamic, vibrant Language which was responsive to the User base.

And that is the nub of it. Currently the User Base have no real input into
what gets developed (they do indirectly, in that vendors will respond to
Market force, but there is little direct involvement of the User Base with
formulation and review of standards for the Language and there are no
procedures being proposed to facilitate this in the future) and there is no
answerability or responsibility to the User Base by the unelected Guardians
of the COBOL standard (ANSI).

Until there is, we will have the same stagnant moribund situation that we
are currently experiencing.

I don't entirely agree with your arguments regarding Java, and portability,
and I think it is important to realise that COBOL was never intended to be
close to any given machine, as C was. But your questions as to "Why can't we
have..." are answered above.

Pete


Joseph J Katnic wrote in message ...
>I have been following this thread with interest.

…[54 more quoted lines elided]…

Hi Peter,

The exchange between you and Joe prompts me to express my 
happyness with the effort going on with TinyCOBOL.  While they are 
trying to create COBOL '85, some extensions are considered.  Moreover,
This is the beginning of the end of the current standards problems as I 
see it.

Warren Simmons

> >In todays environment, is there ANY reason why COBOL should not 
have
> >native statements to interface to ALL of the operating system 
features?
> >(NOT merely IO (DISK predominately) as in the past).
> >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Portability necessarily means removing all the various enhancements 
that
> >manufacturers make to improve their machines performance over the 
other
> >manufacturers. Why as a user, would you invest millions in a platform
> >and then deliberately not make use of it's best features.
> >
> >It is my contention that companies that change platform do not do so 
for
> >performance or economic reasons, but usually for fashion reasons.
> >
> >If management put as much effort into upgrading the existing
> >applications and facilities as they do experimenting with and 
attempting
> >to impliment the latest fashon, then maybe their companies would be 
able
> >to offer the investors more than a 2 to 3 percent return on investment.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T02:35:40+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3afe9afc_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9der39$sd3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9deu6e$6ae$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9dhi3g$lq9$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <josephk-2C7E4A.21122312052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <3afd4c1e_6@Usenet.com> <1103_989683748@micron-450>`

```

Warren G. Simmons wrote in message <1103_989683748@micron-450>...
>
>The exchange between you and Joe prompts me to express my
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I sincerely hope you're right Warren, although I believe the standards
fiasco is far from over yet.

It needs a number of things to resolve it:

1. Acceptance by ANSI that they are answerable to the COBOL Community.

2. Acceptance by ANSI that COBOL cannot be copyrighted or commandeered by
ANY organisation, and any attempt to do so will result in the withdrawal of
the standard from their control.

3. Acceptance by ANSI that their procedures and Management practices are
hopelessly out of date and need review, so that future changes to the
standard can be made and tested openly within specific timeframes.
(Personally, I'd like to see some RAD management on the Standards committee,
with prioritised goals and unslippable timeboxes in which to achieve them.)

4. Publication of the standard at the earliest possible time.

There has been no evidence to date that any of the first 3 points are likely
to happen. (In fact, ANSI still don't believe that they are culpable for the
delay in presenting this standard. It was an "administrative glitch"
exacerbated by Bill Klein... Nothing about what they were doing for 10
years...

The fourth point will probably not be achieved until there is at least some
movement on the first three.

If you are waiting to see this standard implemented, don't hold your
breath...

Meantime, despite dire warnings and forebodings from members of the J4
committee that vendors will drop out of the game unless the standard is
ratified, all of the major vendors are proceeding to work on the "next
release" of their respective COBOLs.

I certainly wish Tiny COBOL every possible success.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T13:46:48+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3afb497d_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9der39$sd3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```

Chuck Stevens wrote in message <9der39$sd3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>...
<snipped>
>
>While I do believe that there will be more than one COBOL compiler
>implemented in total conformance with the final standard, my point is that
>if SOMEBODY produces a fully-featured OO facility, or all the syntax and
>semantics of VALIDATE, or of RPW, or of Standard Arithmetic, or even
in-line
>comments and standard compiler directives, in a way that is syntactically
>and semantically conformant with the new standard,  in combination or in
>isolation, the user of that feature in such a compiler, and thus the COBOL
>commununity,  has benefited from that standard.
>
Any such implementation will no doubt be of benefit to the COBOL Community.
The question is whether the "standard" deserves the credit for it... I would
argue that the FUNCTIONALITY described in the standard is definitely
valuable, and has been derived in response to Market demand.

Unfortunately it has taken more than 10 years for the Standards people to
document it and encapsulate it into this standard, so you'll understand if I
am reluctant to commend this exercise.

Fortunately, the vendors, who are much more sensitive to Market forces, have
gone ahead and implemented much of this functionality as "extensions" in
anticipation of the proposed standard. If they hadn't, COBOL would be dead.

For me, the issue is not about what the proposed standard documents, it is
about whether or not COBOL can continue to be "served" by a Bureaucracy that
is not responsive, dynamic, or effective.

It is true that the proposed standard contains some very good stuff. (It
also contains some very doubtful stuff, but I believe that Market forces
will iron this out; vendors will not implement what is not likely to help
sell their product).

What we need to do now is to ensure that the MECHANISM for producing a
standard, is revised, so that the current farce can never be repeated.

It is no wonder that respected members of the community are advocating "NO
standard" rather than to continue with the current "wading through molasses"
approach to a COBOL Standard.

Pete.


Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T13:55:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dhji9$n1e$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9der39$sd3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3afb497d_1@Usenet.com>`

```

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@free.net.nz> wrote in message
news:3afb497d_1@Usenet.com...

<<Any such implementation will no doubt be of benefit to the COBOL
Community.  The question is whether the "standard" deserves the credit for
it... I would argue that the FUNCTIONALITY described in the standard is
definitely  valuable, and has been derived in response to Market demand.>>

I believe the fact that a standard that is independent of the overriding and
authoritative influence of a single implementation or implementor defines
*both* the syntax *and* the functionality means that the consensus of users
and implementors that the standard represents does indeed deserve the credit
for the *standardization* of both.   It is not just the syntax and the
functionality that the standards define, it is also the relationship between
the two.

<<Unfortunately it has taken more than 10 years for the Standards people to
document it and encapsulate it into this standard, so you'll understand if I
am reluctant to commend this exercise.>>

I understand your reluctance to commend the process; what I don't understand
is why you want to reinvent the process *right now* so that it will take
*another* ten years before a replacement standard can be promulgated!

<<Fortunately, the vendors, who are much more sensitive to Market forces,
have gone ahead and implemented much of this functionality as "extensions"
in  anticipation of the proposed standard. If they hadn't, COBOL would be
dead.>>

And I've already gone on record as saying I think that's a Good Thing.

<<For me, the issue is not about what the proposed standard documents, it is
about whether or not COBOL can continue to be "served" by a Bureaucracy that
is not responsive, dynamic, or effective.>>

If the FCD is of value, it should be approved, as expeditiously as possible.

<< It is true that the proposed standard contains some very good stuff. (It
also contains some very doubtful stuff, but I believe that Market forces
will iron this out; vendors will not implement what is not likely to help
sell their product).>>

I agree.  And I believe that *some* vendors who would, or even might, be
producing, or planning to produce, a fully conformant implementation of
COBOL according to the new standard will almost certainly drop that effort
like a hot rock  if the standard is not adopted.  There is no reason to
implement "standard" (e.g.) OOCOBOL features if there is no *consensus*
among implementors as to what the (e.g.) OOCOBOL features should look like.

<<What we need to do now is to ensure that the MECHANISM for producing a
standard, is revised, so that the current farce can never be repeated.>>

I'm not convinced that it's all that much of a farce, but I understand the
impatience over the production of the new standard.  But slapping
J4/NCITS/ANSI/WG4/ISO/IEC's indivudal and collective hands in a fit of pique
with the cries "Bad ANSI!!!   Naughty NCITS!!!!" and then discarding many
years of hard work by a significant number of dedicated and talented people
does not strike me as rational behavior.

<<It is no wonder that respected members of the community are advocating "NO
standard" rather than to continue with the current "wading through molasses"
approach to a COBOL Standard.>>

As indicated above, I think I can understand their concerns with the
processes.  And I believe the appropriate approach, if indeed the process is
flawed, is to seek to revise it at an appropriate point in the standards
development process.

But I also strongly believe that the effect of the abandonment of standards
for COBOL -- the "NO standard" approach -- will have the direct consequence
that a great many users will abandon the use of the language altogether; in
other words, "NO standard" will, to many users, mean "No COBOL in MY
shop!!!".

I have lived with COBOL compilers that made no claim to conformance with any
standard.   Believe me.  You really don't want to go there.

COBOL may well be passing from favor; I've said that before.  But derailing
the standards won't stem that process; I remain convinced that it will
accelerate it dramatically.   Maybe that's what you want, but it's not what
I want.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T13:22:54+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3afc9500_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net> <9d9f36$ltm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9der39$sd3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3afb497d_1@Usenet.com> <9dhji9$n1e$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```

Chuck Stevens wrote in message <9dhji9$n1e$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>...
>
>Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@free.net.nz> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>I believe the fact that a standard that is independent of the overriding
and
>authoritative influence of a single implementation or implementor defines
>*both* the syntax *and* the functionality means that the consensus of users
>and implementors that the standard represents does indeed deserve the
credit
>for the *standardization* of both.   It is not just the syntax and the
>functionality that the standards define, it is also the relationship
between
>the two.
>
Nope. The fact that the "standard" is implementor independent simply means
that it reflects the collective wishes of the Market place. It is just a
document which in effect says: "As far as we can ascertain, this is what we
think is needed in COBOL, based on experience, trends in the industry, and
feedback from current users."

So I don't argue the value of the document. (Well, maybe in some parts, and
in the presentation of it, but those are very minor matters compared to the
real issue, which is "Can we continue to take 10 years to produce a COBOL
standard?")

My issue is with the preparation of the document.

If the people entrusted with preparing it are not responsive, it WON'T
contain the things outlined above. If they are not "approachable" (or the
procedures for approaching them are so moribund and complex that the average
COBOL user decides NOT to do it...) then, again, they are not achieving
their objectives. If they take an inordinate amount of time to write the
document, much of what is in it will be overtaken by events and the document
will be seen to be irrelevant.

><<Unfortunately it has taken more than 10 years for the Standards people to
>document it and encapsulate it into this standard, so you'll understand if
I
>am reluctant to commend this exercise.>>
>
>I understand your reluctance to commend the process; what I don't
understand
>is why you want to reinvent the process *right now* so that it will take
>*another* ten years before a replacement standard can be promulgated!
>
I don't want to reinvent the process, I just want to ensure that it doesn't
take 10 years in future. I certainly want the procedures for producing it
changed. If ANSI can't accept suggestions as to how the procedures should be
changed, and how the committees responsible should be managed, then I would
certainly argue that ANSI is an unsuitable body to be entrusted with the
future of COBOL.

Based on the current track record I see nothing to indicate they should
continue to be responsible for the COBOL standard. If they ARE to continue
being responsible, then they must show openly and publicly that they are
prepared to accept the need for change.

So far, we have never seen an acceptance of responsibility for the lateness
of this standard, an acceptance of responsibility for the "administrative
glitch" which led to the necessity for re-affirmation, or any indication
that lessons have been learned and it won't happen again.

No sign of any apology to the COBOL Community, no sign of requesting
suggestions from the user Community as to how the standards process could be
improved, no sign of even accepting answerability to the COBOL Community;
instead, we should be grateful that this wonderful standard has at last been
produced.

Sorry, I for one am not buying it.

The document may be of value; the system that produced it stinks.

Besides, I never advocated a replacement standard (it would be extremely
foolish to throw the baby out with the bath water), and if I was given the
task of preparing a replacement standard, I can assure you it wouldn't take
10 years... so your "lack of understanding" is based on desires you have
attributed to me, which are incorrect and which I have certainly never
expressed.

><<Fortunately, the vendors, who are much more sensitive to Market forces,
>have gone ahead and implemented much of this functionality as "extensions"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>And I've already gone on record as saying I think that's a Good Thing.

OK, so we agree.
>
><<For me, the issue is not about what the proposed standard documents, it
is
>about whether or not COBOL can continue to be "served" by a Bureaucracy
that
>is not responsive, dynamic, or effective.>>
>
>If the FCD is of value, it should be approved, as expeditiously as
possible.
>
Hmmm...maybe.  I don't think it should be unconditionally approved. The
document should be "approved" on the basis that future documents of this
nature will be prepared in a manner and a timeframe acceptable to the
community at which the document is aimed. There should be SOME guarantee
that we don't get a repeat of what happened this time.

><< It is true that the proposed standard contains some very good stuff. (It
>also contains some very doubtful stuff, but I believe that Market forces
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I strongly disagree here. Vendors will implement whatever the Market
requires whether it is "standard" or not. They are not Philanthropic
Societies. If they can't make a profit they won't do it. So it is ultimately
Market driven. Any vendor who "withdraws" because there is no "official"
COBOL standard in effect, shouldn't have been in the business in the first
place. It certainly leaves the remaining ones with a larger market share.
Whether there is an "official" (read, "ANSI") standard or not, there is a
consensus as to where COBOL should be going. This is backed by the User
Community who are the Marketplace. The current document is a useful step
towards it, whether it is "approved" or not. Anyone delivering to the
Marketplace, having identified what is needed and wanted, is likely to make
a profit, with or without ANSI approval.

(There is an argument that OO COBOL should not have been part of the
standard anyway. It could've had it's own standard (and not necessarily an
ANSI standard). A standard made available to the User Community via the
Internet, for comment and review, would be a much better and useful model
than something produced by a closed door committee in an ivory tower. And it
could've been derived from user field testing and feedback, similar to the
very successful model Sun MicroSystems have adopted with Java.)

><<What we need to do now is to ensure that the MECHANISM for producing a
>standard, is revised, so that the current farce can never be repeated.>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>J4/NCITS/ANSI/WG4/ISO/IEC's indivudal and collective hands in a fit of
pique
>with the cries "Bad ANSI!!!   Naughty NCITS!!!!" and then discarding many
>years of hard work by a significant number of dedicated and talented people
>does not strike me as rational behavior.

It's not a fit of pique. And I'm not interested in slapping hands or
"punishing" anyone (though I appreciate your use of rhetoric to make it
appear that I am...<G>) Neither is my criticism intended to be personal.

I say it again: "The COBOL Community must be assured that what has happened
here will NOT happen again." A simple assurance will not cut it. It must be
SEEN to be guaranteed. That means communication between ANSI and the COBOL
community with open public debate and discussion. New procedures and
Management techniques. Opening the evaluation process up using today's
technology. Moving into the 21st Century.
>
><<It is no wonder that respected members of the community are advocating
"NO
>standard" rather than to continue with the current "wading through
molasses"
>approach to a COBOL Standard.>>
>
>As indicated above, I think I can understand their concerns with the
>processes.  And I believe the appropriate approach, if indeed the process
is
>flawed, is to seek to revise it at an appropriate point in the standards
>development process.

"And I believe the appropriate approach, if indeed the process is
flawed, is to seek to revise it at an appropriate point in the standards
development process."

For Chrissake, listen to yourself! "IF the process is flawed..?!!!" If it
WASN'T flawed it wouldn't take 10 bloody years to produce a simple document,
or even a complex document...!

So just when is  "...an appropriate point in the standards development
process...."?!

Should I translate that as "Anytime except NOW!"

You'll be kissing babies next...<G>

>
>But I also strongly believe that the effect of the abandonment of standards
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
On the basis of what evidence?

If a shop uses COBOL they do so because it is the best language for the job;
not because it has an official standard.  As long as we are still dependent
on maintaining Source code, there will be a place for COBOL. Many shops are
going to move away from COBOL in the coming years whether it has a standard
or not. This has to do with factors pertaining to the way commercial
computer systems will be developed in the future, and nothng to do with the
innate value of the COBOL language. (see my article on Components, "Meet the
Future" www.aboutlegacycoding.com ). You cannot blame the "decline" of COBOL
on the fact that the standard was not endorsed.

>I have lived with COBOL compilers that made no claim to conformance with
any
>standard.   Believe me.  You really don't want to go there.
>
No, YOU don't want to go there...I've been to much worse places...<G>


>COBOL may well be passing from favor; I've said that before.  But derailing
>the standards won't stem that process; I remain convinced that it will
>accelerate it dramatically.   Maybe that's what you want, but it's not what
>I want.
>
Ah, a rather cheap parting shot, Chuck.

My credentials as a supporter of COBOL, and things COBOL, are a matter of
record. For the last 35 years I have used, promoted, debated, taught,
encouraged, supported, and struggled to improve COBOL. And I continue to do
so. We really have very similar goals. The issue is in how we implement
them.

Pete.
