###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-08T12:19:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d9gqu$2m2$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <9d6ves$8hc$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9d7e6m$n5q$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9d9ad5$pea$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3AF83D8E.39325292@brazee.net>`

```
<<If a company produced a CoBOL compiler which met everything in the next
ANSI standard except, say Report Writer - how could it best market that
product?  Not mention ANSI, but tout its features?>>

Best way?  I'm not a marketing type.  But with specific reference to Report
Writer (and, I believe, Validate as well), as I understand it, there's a
"front end preprocessor" program on the market that will provide this
functionality by reading FCD-compliant COBOL source containing these
features and producing FCD-compliant COBOL source that accomplishes the same
result without actually using the features represented in the original
source program.

Since the FCD does not specify what steps are necessary to produce the
object code, nor does it specify what components comprise an "implementation
of standard COBOL", I see no reason why a cooperative effort between a
compiler vendor and such a company could not result in a "conforming
implementation" of the new standard COBOL utilizing multiple components from
disparate vendors even if the compiler doesn't have Report Writer (or
Validate).

I know you're speaking more generically than just Report Writer, but I can
certainly envision the "front-end preprocessor" approach as part of a valid
"implementation of standard COBOL" for a given "processor" for several of
the features described in the FCD.

And regardless of whether such a "front-end" was available, so long as the
compiler is '85 compliant, I personally see no particular reason offhand
that such a compiler couldn't be marketed as complying with the '85 standard
and including as extensions many or most of the features of the new
standard.   The Unisys A Series COBOL(68) compiler incorporated four of the
ANSI-74 modules as extensions and described and marketed them as such.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Stephen J Spiro <donotreply@interbulletin.bogus>
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T04:44:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFF62AA.1A197CD0@interbulletin.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com>`

```
Everyone is unhappy about how long it takes to update the COBOL standard, and the lack of sensitivity by the "guardians" of COBOL to the COBOL "community".

Ok, here is the truth.
Since I joind the J4 COBOL Committee in 1995, membership has ranged from 10 to 15 members at a time.  Currently, we have 12 members.
6 from compiler makers:
IBM
Merant (MicroFocus)
Fujitsu
Unisys
Compaq (Tandem)
Hewlett-Packard
1 from a foreign committee - Information Technology Standards Committee of Japan
1 from a user company - EDS
4 independent consultants:
Stephen J Spiro (ME!) from Wizard Systems
Barry Tauber (self)
William Klein (self)
Bob Karlin (Karlinskorner).

The independent consultants not only pay their own way, and pay an $800/year documentation fee to NCITS, we also LOSE billing time.  Barry and I contribute to the discussions , but not many papers.  Bill's and Bob's contributions (prodigious lately!) sometimes depend on other factors such as how much their clients need of their time.    
 
There are some other individuals/companies who SOMETIMES contribute papers or discussion.  
 
SOME of the other people on the committee get a lot of time from their companies to work on the standard, some do not. Or it can vary.

The COBOL Standard has been completely rewritten from the 1985 edition... the format has changed to be much more readable.  It is an incredible effort, by a relatively small number of people.  
It is NOT the fault of NCITS or ANSI or ISO/IEC that it has taken so long.  It is because we have added -- and argued over -- an enormous number of new features.  
SPECIFIC changes must be written up in a "Full Revision Change Proposal", which is then discussed at a J4 meeting.  Remember, we are writing a STANDARD, which means the language must be complete, accurate and unambiguous.  All other places in the standard which may be impacted must be referenced.  Impacts (if any) on previous standards must be documented. Etc, etc, etc.  After discussion, the proposal is "accepted", "accepted as modified" (which requires a rewrite, but goes directly to the project editor), "accepted, but must be reviewed after suggested changes are made", or "rejected".
THIS IS A LOT OF WORK! 

You want it to go faster? Join the committee. Get your employer to sponsor you for the committee.  Write papers even if you are NOT on the committee.  Joe Katnic, too late for this standard, but if YOU write up the syntax for the Operating System interfaces (we will get you a template for the format), there is a good chance the features would be in the next standard.

------------
Personally, in spite of all the really good things in the standard, I think there are enough BAD features that I voted AGAINST advancing the standard at the J4 meeting last month.  Features are being picked up by compiler makers in response to client requests....   I would rather have the new standard "Excellent and later", rather than "fair and now". 
------------

If you are not contributing to the solution, I cannot take your complaints about delay seriously. Bill Klein I take seriously.  I have no reason to believe that better/faster progress would be made if the work of the committee were under different sponsorship.  In spite of ISO's grumblings, COBOL really IS in the Public Domain.  Anyone out there want to start their own committee to develop the language?  If you can convince me that it will do a better job, I'll join you --- but I don't think anyone will take me up seriously.

Stephen J Spiro
President, Wizard Systems
Box 4451
Metuchen, NJ 08840
USA
(732) 549-8965
stephenjspiro at hotmail.com

Member, J4 COBOL Committee
_______________________________________________
Submitted via WebNewsReader of http://www.interbulletin.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T13:11:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fW9M6.877$oi1.79800@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <3AFF62AA.1A197CD0@interbulletin.com> <josephk-29A465.20384614052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <3B00C5E2.5F8ECF7F@interbulletin.com> <josephk-6F7A52.20254915052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
> > Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:
> I don't think management give a damm about code portablilty and never
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Joe Katnic

Joe,

Where, pray tell, have you worked?

I've programmed in COBOL since the earth cooled, mostly contracting to the
largest banks in the US.  From talking to friends, I know that ANZ in Oz is
exactly the same...

I've never, EVER seen a large shop that wasn't hysterical about portability.
More precisely, they are concerned about programmer portability - the
ability to hire a programmer off the street and have instant productivity.

That's why shops use COBOL, grit their teeth and get CA-whatever stuff and
stay away from the language du jour.  Most even buy vendor packages (SAS,
D&B and such) for their applications, again so they can hire ready-made
staff.

I know one large bank that built its own file cataloging system twenty years
ago.  It's so ingrained in their system not that they can't use any restart
systems like OPC, Zebb or CA-11.  They're merging with another bank now, and
their DP shop won't survive because it's gridlocked.

When a new place contacts me for contracting work, they want to know my
experience on various packages, from COBOL to the basest vendor package.
Nobody will probably ever ask me if I know Katnic COBOL.

Cliff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Stephen J Spiro <donotreply@interbulletin.bogus>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T14:53:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0142E7.79CBD231@interbulletin.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <3AFF62AA.1A197CD0@interbulletin.com> <josephk-29A465.20384614052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <3B00C5E2.5F8ECF7F@interbulletin.com> <josephk-6F7A52.20254915052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
The principal "guardians of portability" on the standards committee seem to be the representatives of the compiler makers.  Who else is more in touch with the market as a whole? You? Me? They are pushing for what their clients want.

Portability does not only mean portability among competing ompiler makers, but upward compatibility with the previous standard.  If a feature is to be dropped from the standard, it is usually declared "archaic" in one standard, and it is always declared "obsolete" in the standard before the one it is dropped.  Meanwhile, there is an annex to the standard called "Substantive changes potentially affecting existing programs".  Most of these deal with obscure issues.  Some are as a result of requests for interpretation of previous standards, and most do actually resolve prior ambiguities. Sometimes (as with new reserved words), there is simply no way to know what is the impact. But the standard MUST document all of it.

Many of the features of the new standard have already been adopted by various compiler makers.  The standards committee encourages this.  Implementors do not have to wait until the "official" international standard is published.  Any implementor (or anyone at all) is free to download the complete draft standard at any phase in the development.

Implementors are free to add extensions ... if they work, they may be added to the next official standard.

I am one member of the committee who would rather take longer and get it right...  

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Committee




_______________________________________________
Submitted via WebNewsReader of http://www.interbulletin.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T07:14:22+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B021ABE.1555B62B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <3AFF62AA.1A197CD0@interbulletin.com> <josephk-29A465.20384614052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <3B00C5E2.5F8ECF7F@interbulletin.com>`

```
> Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in article
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >domain, where the users can tell me if I got it write or wrong.

Well actually that is what the compiler writers _actually_ do.  They
incorporate extensions into their products and then see which of them
are taken up and which are rejected by the users.  They then formulate
these as peoposals to the standards committees and also become a member
so that they can argue the case for these.

For example with OO several compiler vendors have had this for some
years, yet all somewhat differently.  The best working and most accepted
parts will become the standard and the compilers will then change to
match.

You could have been part of this process instead of just complaining
about it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T14:22:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9drvjn$7k8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <3AFF62AA.1A197CD0@interbulletin.com> <josephk-29A465.20384614052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <3B00C5E2.5F8ECF7F@interbulletin.com> <3B021ABE.1555B62B@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard,
  Do you have some basis of thinking that vendors take the "best" (most
widely used? desired?) features from their implementations and make
proposals for the (next) Standard?  This certainly has NOT been my
perception during the decade-plus that I have been involved with the
Standard.

IN FACT,  there are a variety of examples of where the current FCD is HIGHLY
incompatible with *all* existing implementations.

For example,

1) The OO that you mention is EXACTLY the opposite of what really happened.
There were "draft proposals" for OO COBOL that the different vendors
implemented (and the reason they are "incompatible" is that they adopted
different editions of the ever-changing specification).  What is in the
current draft is actually incompatible with ALL the existing
implementations - although it is my understanding that Fujitsu users will
have the "easiest" time moving to the new specification - while IBM users
will have the biggest problem.

2) Ask Stephen Spiro about "based storage" and "SET ADDRESS OF" and see how
much "attention" was paid to existing implementation (or - in my opinion
"user-input") on how this should work.

3) Check out how ALL existing implementations have extensions for
"RETURNING" and compare it to what is in the FCD.

  ***

Whether it is a good or bad thing is "open to discussion" - but one thing
that the standards committee do is NOT "pay attention" to existing
extension - IF they think another (incompatible) specification is
THEORETICALLY better.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-19T16:02:33+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B068B09.FB91AAF7@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <3AFF62AA.1A197CD0@interbulletin.com> <josephk-29A465.20384614052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <3B00C5E2.5F8ECF7F@interbulletin.com> <3B021ABE.1555B62B@Azonic.co.nz> <9drvjn$7k8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> 1) The OO that you mention is EXACTLY the opposite of what really happened.
> There were "draft proposals" for OO COBOL that the different vendors
> implemented (and the reason they are "incompatible" is that they adopted
> different editions of the ever-changing specification). 

And who is it that made these proposals ?  Users ?  Completely
independant 'computer scientists' ? Or the vendors ?  Or, in fact all of
these.

It may well be that the vendors 'draft proposals' preceeded any actual
implementation and that these were parallel activities by the vendors.

> What is in the
> current draft is actually incompatible with ALL the existing
> implementations - 

That's what committees are _for_     ;-)

> although it is my understanding that Fujitsu users will
> have the "easiest" time moving to the new specification - while IBM users
> will have the biggest problem.

One of the problems of being early (IBM) is that committes have longer
to change to what others want.
 
> Whether it is a good or bad thing is "open to discussion" - but one thing
> that the standards committee do is NOT "pay attention" to existing
> extension - IF they think another (incompatible) specification is
> THEORETICALLY better.

This is, of course, by vote.  Each vendor is likely to vote for
whichever system they think is 'best', this is likely to also be what
they have implemented for exactly the same reasons.  If they cannot
convince others as to why theirs is 'better' then they lose that feature
to some other mechanism.

Of course the result is an accumulation of items that is not just one
vendor's or proposer's idea, but is parts of all of them and
incompatible with everyones.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-05-19T23:21:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0105192221.3f40fede@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <3AFF62AA.1A197CD0@interbulletin.com> <josephk-29A465.20384614052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <3B00C5E2.5F8ECF7F@interbulletin.com> <3B021ABE.1555B62B@Azonic.co.nz> <9drvjn$7k8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B068B09.FB91AAF7@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in article 
-
>William M. Klein wrote:
>> 
>> 1) The OO that you mention is EXACTLY the opposite of what really
happened.
>> There were "draft proposals" for OO COBOL that the different
vendors
>> implemented (and the reason they are "incompatible" is that they
adopted
>> different editions of the ever-changing specification). 
------------->
>And who is it that made these proposals ?  Users ?  Completely
>independant 'computer scientists' ? Or the vendors ?  Or, in fact all
of
>these.
-------------
Pretty much all of these.  OO development was mostly handled by an "ad
hoc" subcommittee of J4 (at that time, X3J4), because not enough of
the regular members had sufficient expertise.  Meetings were held in
conjunction with J4 (and sometimes alone).  But since there were no
meeting fees, no NCITS fees, and no commitment, companies and
individuals were willing to participate on this limited basis.
At one time, the new standard was scheduled for 1996. However, about
this time, X3J4, in consultation with the OO ad hoc, decided that the
syntax/organization of the OO features was flawed and "too much out of
synch with the developing OO techniques."  A conscious decision was
made to make the COBOL OO format match the CORBA model, even tho that
meant a major re-write of the features, and a major delay in the
standard.  CORBA was chosen over COM because CORBA was not
proprietary.
------------->
>It may well be that the vendors 'draft proposals' preceeded any
actual
>implementation and that these were parallel activities by the
vendors.
----------------->
No, the drafts came first.  In fact, if you look at the features in
the vendor products, you can tell exactly which draft on which it is
based.  All the vendors knew we were working on OO, and wanted to be
as close to the final version as possible.
-----------------
<snip>
-----------------
>> Whether it is a good or bad thing is "open to discussion" - but one
thing
>> that the standards committee do is NOT "pay attention" to existing
>> extension - IF they think another (incompatible) specification is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>convince others as to why theirs is 'better' then they lose that
feature
>to some other mechanism.
------------------->
No, Bill Klein is right, here.  I am truly amazed sometimes at how
vendor representatives do NOT vote to implement extensions the same
way that their companies have implemented them. There are vendor
representatives who have told me that they believe their own companies
implemented a feature wrongly, and this is the time to do it "right".
--------------------
>Of course the result is an accumulation of items that is not just one
>vendor's or proposer's idea, but is parts of all of them and
>incompatible with everyones.
---------------------
Very often the truth.

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems

Member, J4 COBOL Committee

stephenjspiro at hotmail.com

PS: Join the committee .... you WILL have an impact!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T23:22:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vSiM6.25355$t12.1911404@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <3AFF62AA.1A197CD0@interbulletin.com> <josephk-29A465.20384614052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
"Joseph J Katnic" wrote:
> If I'm going to put time into designing the language elements, you can
> bet your life I'm not going to be sitting aroung waiting for some agreed
> solution. I'm going to build the compiler and get it out in the public
> domain, where the users can tell me if I got it write or wrong.

Let me tell you the result:  No major mainframe shop would use it.  It's not
standard, and it's not supported by a major vendor.  Oh, and it's not IBM.

Most PC shops would probably not use it for the same reasons, particularly
since most of them prize their mainframe compatibility.

We're not talking about Turbo Pascal here.  There's no grassroots demand -
just a bunch of companies trying to write code, most led by non-technical
managers.

The difference between young and old COBOL programmers is that the old ones
build applications, the young ones aspire to build new compilers.

Cliff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T20:05:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dsjn7$khp$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <3AFF62AA.1A197CD0@interbulletin.com> <josephk-29A465.20384614052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <vSiM6.25355$t12.1911404@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Interesting that you should make this claim about what IBM mainframe shops
would (or would not) do.

Sometime, look into the (sad but true) history of "MetaCOBOL" product (that
as far as I know was ONLY available on IBM mainframes).

Now, if you want to look at ADF, Telon, Pacbase, APS, etc - it is also an
interesting study in what has and has not been "acceptable" in IBM mainframe
(mostly COBOL) shops.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T03:33:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aymM6.25597$t12.1937674@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF0032F.6E7A2F8D@brazee.net> <3AF43F27.7638295F@ix.netcom.com> <3AF60525.758E28DA@home.com> <3AFF62AA.1A197CD0@interbulletin.com> <josephk-29A465.20384614052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <vSiM6.25355$t12.1911404@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9dsjn7$khp$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I suppose that my viewpoint is colored by my background - I've worked in
over 20 IBM COBOL shops, all large banks and S&Ls.

None of them had anything but IBM COBOL.

One had used MetaCOBOL in the past, but only the resultant COBOL remained.
God, what a mess.  One tried a database environment called Model 204, but it
ended quickly and very, very badly.

Most of them don't even allow Easytrieve or Dyl-280 to be put into
production.

I guess we all have our perspectives, based on our work history.

Nonetheless, I would not place a lot of money on the possibility some dude
generating and releasing a public domain product that takes the mainframe
world by storm.  ("Sorry, boss, yes - we wrote that million dollar project
in Dorfman COBOL 'cause it looked so elegant.  Who knew that the author
would become a travel agent?")

Cheers,
Cliff


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9dsjn7$khp$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net...

(that
> as far as I know was ONLY available on IBM mainframes).

mainframe

agreed

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** Stephen J Spiro <donotreply@interbulletin.bogus>
- **Date:** 2001-05-05T23:09:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com> wrote in article 


>Prior to the ANSI-85 standard, it was impossible to write proper
>structured code in COBOL.   Most attempts to do so resulted in a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>COBOL tend to write sloppy code.


I learned structured programming from IBM when we were still using the 1968 compiler. It worked beautifully.  There were a couple of minor syntactical problems (AT END sometimes required a GO TO.  The program had to GO TO the EXIT of an INPUT or OUTPUT PROCEDURE).  Return codes in 1974 COBOL simplified that... but I don't think it was until 1985 that we could get around SORT EXIT.)  In-line PERFORM is nice, but not necessary for structured programming.
The programs I write today are not much different from what I wrote in the seventies, and I have received many compliments on the readability of my code.

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
_______________________________________________
Submitted via WebNewsReader of http://www.interbulletin.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-07T23:14:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com>`

```


Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com> wrote in article
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> The programs I write today are not much different from what I wrote in the seventies, and I have received many compliments on the readability of my code.

There is lots of disagreement about what is structured and what is not.   From my perspective,
each construct has an entry point and a well-defined scope.    Prior to ANSI-85, few constructs
had a well-defined scope.   Consider the selection constructs.  There were two:  alternation
in the form of an IF...ELSE, and multiway, in the form of a computed GO TO.    The IF statement
was horrible because there was no scope terminator.   All kinds of strange code passed for
"structured" but an open-ended IF caused no end of trouble.   One of the issues of structured
programming is that, when programming with elementary structures,  the scope terminator
allows one structure to be neatly enclosed within another.

As to the in-line Perform, I wrote a piece for the Communications of the ACM many years ago
with the title, "Perform Considered Harmful."    The idea behind that piece was that the
COBOL Perform was too dependent on global data and violated fundamental notions of
cohesion and coupling, another notion from structure programming.   ANSI-85 introduced
a modularity structure that allowed call by reference as well as call by value.   Few
programmers adopted this, mostly because they were not getting good guidance from
their mentors.  Those mentors often had no training in computer science and were quite
satisfied with the good old-fashioned Perform their great-grandfather had used when
he was programming in COBOL.

So, I reaffirm my statement that, prior to ANSI-85, it was impossible to write correctly
structured code in COBOL.    That was not possible until scope terminators were
included in the language.  It was not possible as long as programs were dependent on
global data.   It was not possible without a good model for parameterized subroutines.

Richard Riehle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>
.....
Structured programming has gotten consistently easier with each release of COBOL, but writing good structured code was not impossible with early releases, just harder.  My employer (Irving Trust Co, in NYC) had instructors come in and teach structured programming to the entire department ... a  two week course.  And then tried to enforce it with structured walkthrus.
You can write good structured code in almost any language.. I have done it in Assembler.  It takes discipline.  I have found that the more attention I pay to the formal disciplines, the more readable are my programs.

There are some BAD features in the proposed new COBOL Standard:
EXIT-PARAGRAPH and EXIT-SECTION allow multiple exits from a structure.
It was put in bevcause of a demand (request?) from bad programmers... same bad programmers who got them to eliminate SECTION A and SECTION B in the layout.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-08T07:23:40-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF7F35C.FF8F2A84@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com> <3AF7A6BE.751159AB@interbulletin.com>`

```
Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> There are some BAD features in the proposed new COBOL Standard:
> EXIT-PARAGRAPH and EXIT-SECTION allow multiple exits from a structure.
> It was put in bevcause of a demand (request?) from bad programmers... same bad programmers who got them to eliminate SECTION A and SECTION B in the layout.

But it will get rid of any excuse for EXIT paragraphs, and that is a good thing.  And EXIT-PARAGRAPH is much easier to understand and maintain than setting switches to do the same thing.    Compare a feature with what will be used as an alternative in the real world if it didn't exist.

Sure, good design will mean initial programs don't need these, but maintenance programming will use these a lot, trying for minimal changes.


Question:  Are there languages which don't allow early termination of performed processes?   Certainly, any language which allows error trapping has to allow an EXIT-PARAGRAPH equivalent.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-09T20:08:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFA063D.86EDD75A@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com> <3AF7A6BE.751159AB@interbulletin.com>`

```


Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> Structured programming has gotten consistently easier with each release of COBOL, but writing good structured code was not impossible with early releases, just harder.  My employer (Irving Trust Co, in NYC) had instructors come in and teach structured programming to the entire department ... a  two week course.  And then tried to enforce it with structured walkthrus.

I respectfully disagree.   What passed for structured programming, and I was one of those who
fell for it too, was nothing of the sort.    As long as the language fails to correctly support elementary
structures,  it is impossible to consistently apply the model.   I earlier mentioned the IF...ELSE
as an example.    With no scope terminator, the structure was inherently flawed.    As long as one
is dependent on global data, the language is inherently flawed.

I admit that it is all too easy, in hindsight, to be critical of the language architecture.   For a language
design of the late 1950's, COBOL was a significant advance.

> You can write good structured code in almost any language.. I have done it in Assembler.  It takes discipline.  I have found that the more attention I pay to the formal disciplines, the more readable are my programs.

This is a lot like saying one can write good object-oriented programs in any language, another widespread
fallacy.   Structured Programming implies well-formed structures.   COBOL, prior to ANSI-85 simply
did not support an appropriate model of well-formed structures.  Now it does.

I remember all too well how we struggled to implement "structured" programming using COBOL-68
and later COBOL-74.   We eliminated our GO TO constructs,  designed with PERFORM statements,
experimenting variously with Section Performs, Perform ... Through, etc. and still fell far short of
being able to create programs from well-formed elementary structures.  Everyone did.   It was not
the fault of the programmers.   It was a defect of the language, one that has now been corrected.

> There are some BAD features in the proposed new COBOL Standard:
> EXIT-PARAGRAPH and EXIT-SECTION allow multiple exits from a structure.

Potentially harmful, but only if they are implemented like a GO TO.  That is, if the calling
conventions are correctly maintained, early exit (really early return) from a Performed
routine is better than a GO TO to a dummy label.    In fact, PERFORM should not be used
for subroutine calls at all anymore.   Instead, PERFORM should be reserved for in-line
loops (With Test Before/With Test After), and subroutines should be constructed from
Calls (by value or by reference) with local data instead of global data.  This requires
a complete re-education of COBOL Programmers, but it is worth it.  COBOL continues
to be an excellent choice for business data processing solutions when used properly.

> It was put in bevcause of a demand (request?) from bad programmers... same bad programmers who got them to eliminate SECTION A and SECTION B in the layout.

Not sure I entirely agree, but I see your point.  From my perspective, bad programmers use the Perform
to perform paragraphs or sections instead of creating callable subroutines.   I have also seen the
Perform used to create a Perform block, much like a begin..end block in some other languages.  This
looks like,

                Perform
                   CALL ...
                   CALL ..
                End-Perform

An interesting construct that I noticed in a shop using the Tandem COBOL compiler where the
perform block was used in the imperative part of an Evaluate statement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-09T22:29:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dd1vp$vav$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com> <3AF7A6BE.751159AB@interbulletin.com> <3AFA063D.86EDD75A@ix.netcom.com>`

```
I am curious - what "definition" of "structured programming"

A) requires "local" data
B) prohibits PERFORM

The *only* definitions that I have seen of structured programming require

1) "single entry / single exit" from routines and structures
2) use of "specific" structures (which *does* include "DO loops" - both DO
UNTIL and DO WHILE)

I am *not* saying that it is "bad" to eliminate "many-level" deep PERFORMs -
but I have never seen this (before) as a requirement of any definition of
"structured" programming.  Similarly, "localizing" of data may (or may not)
be a "good thing" - but is NOT something that I have (previously) seen
required by "structured programming".

I certainly have NOT read all the "books" on theoretical structured
programming - but these simply are "constraints" that I am not used to in
such discussions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T11:32:50-04:00

- **Message-ID:** `<8E82621C522473BC.5413354DB6C652EC.ECE295F58C18E348@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com> <3AF7A6BE.751159AB@interbulletin.com> <3AFA063D.86EDD75A@ix.netcom.com> <9dd1vp$vav$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <0NvK6.227$uV4.28196@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
On Thu, 10 May 2001 12:25:32 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
<michael.mattias@gte.net> enlightened us:

>William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
>news:9dd1vp$vav$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Indeed.  Also, whomever wrote a book that got published defines what
structured programming is.

I've never been a real die hard proponent of structured programming
outside of a top-down design.  I'm no proponent of spaghetti code
either.  But who does structured programming benefit?  The computer
doesn't care.  It will happily execute instructions in any manner you
code them.  The compiler doesn't care.  It will parse and create
object code from any syntactically correct set of instructions you
write.  So the only one who really cares are us humans who have to
write and maintain the code.

Personally, I find structured programming (in many cases, but not all)
just as hard to follow as spaghetti code.  When you are trying to
debug a program that has PERFORM THIS and PERFORM THAT and PERFORM
SOMETHING-ELSE and PERFORM ANOTHER-THING before you ever read a
record, well it sends me screaming into the night. 

Give me a KISS program that's written top down with no deeper than 3
perform levels and a couple of go to exits and I'm as happy as a drunk
in Jack Daniels' garage.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Indecision is the key to flexibility.


DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge
Read the facts and sign a petition at:

http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

SkippyPB wrote in message

>Give me a KISS program that's written top down with no deeper than 3
>perform levels and a couple of go to exits and I'm as happy as a drunk
>in Jack Daniels' garage.
>
Amen!

I have followed this debate with some interest. My own belief is that
Structured Programming has been (or will be soon) replaced by Object
Oriented Programming. I realise there are many here who will vehemently
disagree and I don't care strongly enough about it to argue it.

Object Oriented Programming has already been pushed into Component
Development and it is at this point that the obsession with development and
maintenance of Source code becomes irrelevant.

How you write your COBOL really doesn't matter, because the way things are
going, NO ONE else will ever maintain it.

If you really want to argue this, please read my recent article ("Meet the
Future") on www.aboutlegacycoding.com. I will happily defend any points made
there, publicly or privately, but you better hurry because the site is due
to change features...<G>

Now,  I don't s'pose you could be a bit more specific as to the location of
Jack Daniels' garage, could you Skippy ...<G>?

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T20:29:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFB5C98.833E02EA@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com> <3AF7A6BE.751159AB@interbulletin.com> <3AFA063D.86EDD75A@ix.netcom.com> <9dd1vp$vav$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <0NvK6.227$uV4.28196@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8E82621C522473BC.5413354DB6C652EC.ECE295F58C18E348@lp.airnews.net> <3afb49a8_1@Usenet.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> SkippyPB wrote in message
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> disagree and I don't care strongly enough about it to argue it.

I agree that the object model is eventually going to replace the structured
model.   However, many fundamental ideas of the structured model will
persist.   In particular, the three elementary units of the structure theorem,
and their variants,  will continue to play an important role at the
implementation
level.

> Object Oriented Programming has already been pushed into Component
> Development and it is at this point that the obsession with development and
> maintenance of Source code becomes irrelevant.

There is certainly a movement toward creating wrapper components for
existing source code.   There is also progress in creating loosely-coupled
components that interface with legacy software entities, regardless of
their original language.   Indeed, one of the most important Annexes in the
current Ada standard is a language interfaces annex that details the techniques
for interfacing to COBOL, among several others.    Ada is unique in its
direct support for very environment targeted by COBOL.   The Information
Systems Annex even has an entire section devoted to PICTURE syntax.

There is still the educational issue.  It is quite difficult to get COBOL
programmers to learn a language that does not look like the one they
have been using all these years.  Object COBOL has the potential of
filling an important role in this respect.   Whether we can train the
existing COBOL programmers to think in terms of objects is still a
question to be answered.

How you write your COBOL really doesn't matter, because the way things are

> going, NO ONE else will ever maintain it.

This is probably not the case.   Legacy COBOL will be with us for a very
long time.   The real question is whether those who continue to develop in
COBOL will be able to make the transition to a new way of thinking about
programming.

> If you really want to argue this, please read my recent article ("Meet the
> Future") on www.aboutlegacycoding.com. I will happily defend any points made
> there, publicly or privately, but you better hurry because the site is due
> to change features...<G>

I have made the provocative statement that it was impossible to write true
structured
programs in COBOL prior to the ANSI-85 standard.   I use as my standard for
structured programming, not the absence of a GO TO construct but the larger body

of knowledge we gained in pursuit of defining a workable model for structured
programming.

In many respects, the COBOL world was analogous to the early Greek
mathematicians
who found it impossible to solve many fundamental problems in mathematics.
Although
they were excellent at geometric math, there was no way for them to understand
the
kind of math we use today for calculus, or even for elementary algebra.   The
reason
was quite simple:  no zero.   Every number in Greek mathematics was based on
some
geometric representation.   They had no way to represent zero with geometry.
Those
who try to do structured programming without a well-formed set of scope
terminators
are just as handicapped as those early Greeks.

The argument that the period is a scope terminator does not hold up.   It simply
is not
a correct interpretation of the construct.   The period does terminate a
sentence, but
that sentence can be quite convoluted, often is, before one ever reaches the
period.
Every COBOL programmer that has ever written serious code has run into this
issue.

If the model we use is conceptually deficient in representing the solution space
we
require, we learn to live with, work around it, and justify it to ouselves.
This is
what we did with COBOL prior to the ANSI-85 standard. This is what some of us
seem to want to continue to do.   The Greek mathematicians, cited earlier,
decided
to persuade themselves of the rectitude of their approach.   The Pythagoreans
even
put to death a few heretics who challenged them or revealed the inadequacy of
their
methods.

Richard Riehle








>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> I have followed this debate with some interest. My own belief is that
> Structured Programming has been (or will be soon) replaced by Object
> Oriented Programming. I realise there are many here who will vehemently
> disagree and I don't care strongly enough about it to argue it.

Then maybe somebody else will.

I have seen zero evidence that mainframe environments have any interest at all
in trying OO CoBOL.  I see some evidence that they are considering not using
CoBOL at all in replacement systems.   Are you thinking about PC CoBOL moving to
OO, or are you thinking of CoBOL being replaced by OO languages?


> Object Oriented Programming has already been pushed into Component
> Development and it is at this point that the obsession with development and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> going, NO ONE else will ever maintain it.

This may be where OO proponents and OO skeptics separate.  Enterprise
environments are mostly maintenance.  Components already are the hardest part of
maintenance when changing one component (a copy lib, for instance), involves
testing a dozen programs and their related systems.  The opposite of components
is isolation, and isolation makes maintenance much, much easier.

"NO ONE else will ever maintain it" is simply not an option in enterprise shops.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Maintain or replace?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T07:02:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFBE2FE.DADFC66D@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com> <3AF7A6BE.751159AB@interbulletin.com> <3AFA063D.86EDD75A@ix.netcom.com> <9dd1vp$vav$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <0NvK6.227$uV4.28196@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8E82621C522473BC.5413354DB6C652EC.ECE295F58C18E348@lp.airnews.net> <3afb49a8_1@Usenet.com> <3AFBE1E1.ADB62B31@brazee.net>`

```
> This may be where OO proponents and OO skeptics separate.  Enterprise
> environments are mostly maintenance.  Components already are the hardest part of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> "NO ONE else will ever maintain it" is simply not an option in enterprise shops.

But we see less and less maintenance of all kinds in the world.  The TV repairman
replaces a few boards until the TV works - if you take the TV in at all instead of
simply buying a new one.  When we have new PC needs, we buy a new PC.

Maybe this is what OO components will be like.   No maintenance.  When a system is
inadequate for new conditions, we don't adjust the existing system, but try plugging
in some parts - if they work, good.  Otherwise, we replace the system.

But I don't think the enterprise is ready for this.  Adjusting to the system is
simply too expensive.
```

Howard,

I believe this may be the first time in many years that I have found myself
in disagreement with you...<G> (I guess that's healthy...)

comments below.

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3AFBE1E1.ADB62B31@brazee.net>...
>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>I have seen zero evidence that mainframe environments have any interest at
all
>in trying OO CoBOL.  I see some evidence that they are considering not
using
>CoBOL at all in replacement systems.   Are you thinking about PC CoBOL
moving to
>OO, or are you thinking of CoBOL being replaced by OO languages?
>
Both.

Although COBOL will not be "replaced" by OO languages; it will work
alongside them and a slow transition through OO COBOL will be effected.

The Mainframe is always the last bastion of resistance to change. So has it
ever been. On this occasion, as the mainframe is slowly reduced to the role
of another (very poerful) server on the Enterprise LAN, the way we do things
today will no longer be financially viable.

The advent of OOP is INEVITABLE. Why? Because it makes sense and it extends
existing paradigms (like good modular structured programming). Most of the
people who dismiss it in this forum really don't understand it, and simply
dis it out of fear. The same people often believe "everything I want to do,
I can do in COBOL" (BTW, where IS Foodman?...<G>) so there is no need to
learn Java or anything else.

I have developed all these arguments at length in the article I referenced.
I don't want to keep referring to it because it looks like self-promotion,
however, if you read it, you will see exactly where I am coming from on
this.

>> Object Oriented Programming has already been pushed into Component
>> Development and it is at this point that the obsession with development
and
>> maintenance of Source code becomes irrelevant.
>>
>> How you write your COBOL really doesn't matter, because the way things
are
>> going, NO ONE else will ever maintain it.
>
>This may be where OO proponents and OO skeptics separate.  Enterprise
>environments are mostly maintenance.  Components already are the hardest
part of
>maintenance when changing one component (a copy lib, for instance),
involves
>testing a dozen programs and their related systems.  The opposite of
components
>is isolation, and isolation makes maintenance much, much easier.
>
Sorry Howard, Components ARE isolation (encapsulation is implemented in
every component). (A copy lib is NOT a component; an OCX or DLL may be...).

It is precisely BECAUSE Enterprises are mostly mantenance that they will
embrace Component Technology. But, please read the article.

>"NO ONE else will ever maintain it" is simply not an option in enterprise
shops.
No, you're correct as far as TODAY goes. That's because we are obsessed with
developing and maintaining SOURCE code. New technology is changing that.
TOMORROW will be a different story...<G> Again, please read the
article...<G>

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-08T07:16:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF7F1C8.E01F4D81@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com>`

```

Lao Xiao Hai wrote:

> There is lots of disagreement about what is structured and what is not.   From my perspective,
> each construct has an entry point and a well-defined scope.    Prior to ANSI-85, few constructs
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> allows one structure to be neatly enclosed within another.

Actually, a period is a scope terminator for an IF statement.  Complicated IF statements could be simplified by PERFORM clauses.

IF condition-1
     PERFORM DO-MORE-IF-CHECKS
ELSE
     PERFORM REAL-COMPLICATED-STUFF.

Now, we can put a bit more complicated stuff in our IF statement, but if things get real complicated - do the PERFORM!

If condition-1
      IF MY-CONDITION
             command-1
      END-IF
ELSE
      PERFORM REAL-COMPLICATED-STUFF
END-IF.

Not much different.


> So, I reaffirm my statement that, prior to ANSI-85, it was impossible to write correctly
> structured code in COBOL.    That was not possible until scope terminators were
> included in the language.  It was not possible as long as programs were dependent on
> global data.   It was not possible without a good model for parameterized subroutines.

Since period is a scope terminator, and that we could (and did) create good models for parameterized subroutines, those two arguments are invalid.

Your other argument is that programs were dependent on global data.   Why can't programs with global data have structure?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-05-08T21:15:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010508171500.03441.00000956@nso-mi.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3AF7F1C8.E01F4D81@brazee.net>`

```
In article <3AF7F1C8.E01F4D81@brazee.net>, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
writes:

>
>> So, I reaffirm my statement that, prior to ANSI-85, it was impossible to
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thank you Howard, I thoroughly agree with you here.
The previous poster seems to have bought into the PC-ish OO-Speak 
that continues to lambast the programming concepts that lead to these
new micro managed 'components'.  As far as I can tell the 'ideal' of 
localized variables in a function has always been a goal, but in an 
environment that was not entirely conducive to such - it is still possible
to segregate parameters for routines in global memory.  Just that some
in-house rules have to be adhered to regarding exclusivity of fields that
follow a predefined naming convention.
OO to me is simply the old structured programming techniques taken
to the nth degree.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-09T20:35:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFA0C70.91819B2@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3AF7F1C8.E01F4D81@brazee.net> <20010508171500.03441.00000956@nso-mi.aol.com>`

```


Sff5ky wrote:

> IThank you Howard, I thoroughly agree with you here.
> The previous poster seems to have bought into the PC-ish OO-Speak
> that continues to lambast the programming concepts that lead to these
> new micro managed 'components'.

The "previous poster" responds.   I have been programming for about
thirty years, long before there was PC-ish OO-Speak.   COBOL was
and is one of my favored languages.   I also program in some other
languages, Ada being among my favorites, C++ being among those I
most loathe.

I am not sure what you mean by micro-managed components.   If you are
suggesting that modularizing your software according to the principles
of low-coupling and high-cohesion are a bad idea, I would respectfully
disagree.    These ideas pre-date by some considerable time the PC,
the object technology model, and most of our current language versions.

> As far as I can tell the 'ideal' of
> localized variables in a function has always been a goal, but in an
> environment that was not entirely conducive to such - it is still possible
> to segregate parameters for routines in global memory.

This ideal was more difficult to achieve in COBOL prior to ANSI-85.  Worse,
it was really difficult for many of our programmers to make the transition
to this kind of thinking even after the capability was made available.

> Just that some
> in-house rules have to be adhered to regarding exclusivity of fields that
> follow a predefined naming convention.

Good encapsulation can reduce the dependency of your design on naming
conventions.

> OO to me is simply the old structured programming techniques taken
> to the nth degree.

OO does not abrogate structured programming.  It does, however, significantly
change the rules.  Object COBOL recognizes this.   We can create abstract
data types, encapsulate behavior,  protect data within well-formed modules,
and extend (specialize) existing code without breaking what is already there.
This was not easy to do even with the best of structured programming.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-09T20:24:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFA09EF.9F289443@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com> <3AF7F1C8.E01F4D81@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> Actually, a period is a scope terminator for an IF statement.  Complicated IF statements could be simplified by PERFORM clauses.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Now, we can put a bit more complicated stuff in our IF statement, but if things get real complicated - do the PERFORM!

The period, in 1958, was a pretty good idea for a scope terminator.  It turned out to be pretty
awful once one started to write real production code where nested if statements became the
staple of the day.   In fact, once we decided to eliminate GO TO statements from our code,
the nested if with no well-formed scope terminator started to look really goofy.  Our friends
who were using other languages did not fail to notice this.

> If condition-1
>       IF MY-CONDITION
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not much different.

For the simple example you give, not much different.    For code in which nested if statements
become necessary, it would be quite different.   I am sure you know that without me saying so.

> > So, I reaffirm my statement that, prior to ANSI-85, it was impossible to write correctly
> > structured code in COBOL.    That was not possible until scope terminators were
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Since period is a scope terminator, and that we could (and did) create good models for parameterized subroutines, those two arguments are invalid.

Granted that we had a model for parameterized subroutines in COBOL-74.  It improved dramatically
with ANSI-85.

As to the period as scope terminator, it just was not so.   It was, rather, a statement terminator.  The
scope of a nested IF, for example, was not terminated by the period.

> Your other argument is that programs were dependent on global data.   Why can't programs with global data have structure?

Every program has structure.  That does not make it a structured program in the way that term is
widely understood.  Part of structured programming includes the issues of coupling and cohesion.
Larry Constantine writes about this quite eloquently, and even tries to illustrate it with some
examples in COBOL.   The examples fail because he not using ANSI-85 COBOL.   They work
quite well when translated to ANSI-85.

Too often, Structured Programming is regarded as avoiding the GO TO.  Even Dijkstra, in his famous
letter, "Goto Considered Harmful," takes a slightly more moderate view.

I wrote a lot of code in COBOL-68 and COBOL-74.  I would not want to go back to those days.
Reflecting on the code I wrote, and the code I had to maintain, and the code my colleagues wrote
and maintained, I realize that our notion of structured programming was an illusion.  We were
really fooling ourselves.   We fooled ourselves very well.  Many of those colleagues believe, to
this day, they were doing structured programming.    Sighhhhhhhhhhhh.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T06:50:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFA8E9F.C5CFEBA6@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com> <3AF7F1C8.E01F4D81@brazee.net> <3AFA09EF.9F289443@ix.netcom.com>`

```
> Every program has structure.  That does not make it a structured program in the way that term is
> widely understood.  Part of structured programming includes the issues of coupling and cohesion.
> Larry Constantine writes about this quite eloquently, and even tries to illustrate it with some
> examples in COBOL.   The examples fail because he not using ANSI-85 COBOL.   They work
> quite well when translated to ANSI-85.

It is "widely understood" that structured CoBOL programs exist.  You choose to have a definition of structured programs which exclude it.

Either "widely understood" is a valid determinant of whether or not it is structured, or it isn't.


But I choose to evaluate the coding methods by evaluating their goals with mine and then seeing how well they work.  My goal isn't to have code which
follows a methodology, my goal is to have code which works right, is easily maintainable etc...

Of course everything is a trade off.  Having external pieces of code (calls, invokes, and even copies) have advantages and disadvantages.  Design can be
easy, but having to test everything that touches a module which gets maintained can be a nightmare.   It is easier to page down and see a paragraph
being performed than to find a subprogram being called.   If a program calls a program calls a program, and the business rules say the third program
needs to access more data than before, the advantages of global data are more manifest.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T20:53:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFB6220.B7783015@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com> <3AF7F1C8.E01F4D81@brazee.net> <3AFA09EF.9F289443@ix.netcom.com> <3AFA8E9F.C5CFEBA6@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> > Every program has structure.  That does not make it a structured program in the way that term is
> > widely understood.  Part of structured programming includes the issues of coupling and cohesion.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Either "widely understood" is a valid determinant of whether or not it is structured, or it isn't.

It isn't.  Once it was widely understood that the earth was flat.   You are quite able to call up
other historical instances of "widely understood."    What we widely understood, including me,
to be structured programming was simply a self-delusion.   It was a best a valiant attempt.  But
it fell short.

> But I choose to evaluate the coding methods by evaluating their goals with mine and then seeing how well they work.  My goal isn't to have code which
> follows a methodology, my goal is to have code which works right, is easily maintainable etc...

A laudable goal.   I strive for the same in my own code.    However, there are some principles we
have learned over the years that contribute to the maintainability of our code: abstraction, modularity,
localization, among them.    Whether we use these principles in the same way or not, those of us
who are effective use them.    I would bet that you do follow some strict methods that have worked
for you in achieving your goals.   Methodology is about discovering which of those methods
work consistently for a large number of people and codifying them into some coherent set of
principles, ideas, and idioms.

When the first principles of structured programming were being discovered, we did not stop with
the discovery process.   It continued.   For many, of course, it did not continue.   Sadly, many
of our colleagues are content to learn a few idioms and build their career on them.  Anything
new is rejected.   Even object technology continues to evolve with new ideas.

> Of course everything is a trade off.  Having external pieces of code (calls, invokes, and even copies) have advantages and disadvantages.

Calls do not have to be external.

> Design can be
> easy, but having to test everything that touches a module which gets maintained can be a nightmare.

I have never known design to be easy.

> It is easier to page down and see a paragraph
> being performed than to find a subprogram being called.

Perhaps.  However,  those paragraphs are eventually scattered thoughout the program as it is
maintained, and the global data is referenced from all over the program.  It soon becomes quite
a nightmare to decide how many places a particular variable is being updated.  Then, to avoid
this problem, we create temporary variables, also in global data space, with names that
are almost like the one we are trying to protect.   It should not be necessary to read 500
pages of source code with the help of a long cross-reference to change a few lines of
code in a single program.

> If a program calls a program calls a program, and the business rules say the third program
> needs to access more data than before, the advantages of global data are more manifest.

I am now beginning to doubt whether you are serious.   A program that calls another
subprogram does so through a calling convention, with parameters.   There was even
a time when the COBOL committees were considering parameterized Perform statements.
The called program has its own local data, inaccessible from outside.   The interface
of the called subprogram is the only thing you need to use it, unless that subprogram
uses some of the global data.

Global data makes it easier to write a program.   Global data makes a program much
harder to maintain.   Since we devote, in most shops, about eighty-percent of our
budget to maintaining existing code, it makes sense to focus our design on that part
of the lifecycle rather than on the development side.

Richard Riehle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T11:17:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFC1EBF.EA2A9049@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com> <3AF7F1C8.E01F4D81@brazee.net> <3AFA09EF.9F289443@ix.netcom.com> <3AFA8E9F.C5CFEBA6@brazee.net> <3AFB6220.B7783015@ix.netcom.com>`

```
> Global data makes it easier to write a program.   Global data makes a program much
> harder to maintain.   Since we devote, in most shops, about eighty-percent of our
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Richard Riehle

With maintenance, as with new code, determining the scope of the change is a bigger chore than making the actual coding change.  Your experience has been
that this is eased by use of local variables.  But I have found that this often makes it easier to overlook some of the places where that datum is used.
Passing a datum among various variables and programs can mean you don't see every place it impacts or is impacted.

This is similar to what happens when we have a hidden method.  You aren't SUPPOSED to care what is in that black box.  But when a business rule changes, we
have to peak inside to make sure the business rule isn't hidden inside.  Some of this searching is difficult - we know which applications use this business
rule, but searching through all the pieces is difficult - and we really hope that that module isn't used for some other business rule which will increase
our testing process tremendously.

I have seen cases where programmers do string searches on libraries and copymembers to find the variables they are interested in.  If a program doesn't
touch the variable, then they ignore that program.  The reason I have seen this is because this practice has a way of biting back when that variable
doesn't get changed by that name.

There is good and bad in the fact that modules hide stuff.   When someone has a definition of structured which says things should be hidden, I say fine -
but do we have empirical evidence that this costs the enterprise less than a methodology which tries to keeps a logical process in one program?

Where theory and reality disagree - I will go with reality.  Certainly I have seen that poor encapsulating is worse than poor structured code.  It may even
be more costly than poor spaghetti code, at least in an enterprise environment.   And there are lots of poor programmers who produce code which we buy from
vendors!  It may be that the nice theory of how CoBOL programs should be written will be demonstrated to be sufficiently better, even in an enterprise
environment.  It is hard to tell, as most pilot projects seem to have something to prove.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>Passing a datum among various variables and programs can mean you don't see every place it impacts or is impacted.

this is perhaps why one should always explicitly declare by reference
or by value in your calls. but if you have pointers (as cobol is
thinking of adding) you are further up a creek. what they taught us in
comp sci was, if it is at all possible not to use pointers, don't use
them.

best to encapsulate the use of pointers in a single module, much like
you encapsulate graphics and database.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T11:51:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFD8641.B1BACBDC@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<20010428.14284100@george.local> <3AEF9D8A.60DA5D29@ix.netcom.com> <3AF48822.4517C7BC@interbulletin.com> <3AF78EC7.8C854650@ix.netcom.com> <3AF7F1C8.E01F4D81@brazee.net> <3AFA09EF.9F289443@ix.netcom.com> <3AFA8E9F.C5CFEBA6@brazee.net> <3AFB6220.B7783015@ix.netcom.com> <3AFC1EBF.EA2A9049@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> > Global data makes it easier to write a program.   Global data makes a program much
> > harder to maintain.   Since we devote, in most shops, about eighty-percent of our
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Passing a datum among various variables and programs can mean you don't see every place it impacts or is impacted.

What you are identfying is the difficulty of designing with well-protected modules.   It is certainly
more difficult, at first, to do this kind of design.  It requires a change in the way we think about
the design protocols.   Someone else mentioned the importance of being explicity about "by value"
versus "by reference" semantics.    It is well-known that "by reference" semantics are more
vulnerable than "by value" even though "by reference" may often be more efficient.   Sometimes
we can combine the two protocols to gain increased efficiency while ensuring some confidence
in the integrity of the data.    By reference is a weakness of the C/C++/Java language set.   A newer
language from Microsoft, C#, has adopted the explicit "out" mode from Ada and that seems to
be a good compromise that avoids by reference while not losing efficiency.

COBOL could benefit from some of the above features.  It still suffers from the need for by-reference
protocol if one wants to get data returned.  There are certainly safe ways to do this, but the
programmer must exercise care.

I understand your concern about "don't see every place it impacts or is impacted."  This concern
has often been expressed by those making the transition to a more modular form of software
development.   It is one of the most widely-expressed concerns of those making the transition
to the object model.   The reality is, that as programmers become use to the model, their fears
diminish and the the concern proves to be unfounded.   This is especially true of large-scale
programs.

You mention "copy books."  This feature of COBOL was originally intended to improve modularity,
but it failed because of its lack of support for localization.   Modularity, to be truly effective, must
be complemented by localization.   I am sure you recall how, for many copybooks, we needed to
copy both a data division fragment and a procedure division fragment to make this feature
effective.   COPY ... REPLACING fell far short of good code reuse, but it was a good
step in the right direction.

I agree with your assertion that object technology is not going to help with the maintenance
of the huge inventory of existing COBOL code.   This will be an evolutionary process.  However,
improved structured techniques that support cohesive, loosely-coupled modules is an incremental
step that can be accomplished with existing tools, existing versions of compilers, and a little
bit of additional programmer education.

I am not a big fan of revolution.    However, I believe Peter Dashwood is correct in his vision
of the future of programming.   While the Object COBOL model has its flaws, it is a workable
design that can be useful in making the transition to the next stage of software practice with
COBOL.   I do not agree that COBOL is invulnerable to being replaced by other languages. That
is already happening.   The worst of these replacements is with the C++, an increasingly complicated
language design totally inappropriate for business data processing.   Other languages, though, are
pretty good candidates for replacing a lot of COBOL code.   Java is already doing so.   Several
shops I know of have converted completed from COBOL to Python.   In my mind, Ada is probably
the language that has the most properties needed to directly convert COBOL to an object-oriented
model.  One of the best designed languages in the OOP group is Eiffel, but it does not yet have
as large a following as it deserves.

Students graduating from our universities have been told by their professors about the horrors of
COBOL.   They joke about it in the classroom.    Most of these professors would not know a
COBOL program if one crawled into bed with them.  Almost none realize that COBOL is not
the language they once thought they knew.  The new students have their preferences.  The best
of them can make the choice.   We can attract them to the world of COBOL when we make it
clear that they are not making a giant step backward but are on the forefront of the new
technological innovations, even with contemporary COBOL.

For this to happen, those of us still stuck back in the 1970's need to take a few steps forward
too.    I have been as guilty as anyone in relying on old skills to create new kinds of solutions.
I do some teaching in community college and at some local universities.   My students help
keep me aware of the need to continually update my knowledge and skills even as I do my
day-to-day work.   Without the goading of those students, I would probably be doing exactly
the same thing today I was doing in 1978.

Richard Riehle


>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> environment.  It is hard to tell, as most pilot projects seem to have something to prove.
```

> Also, a couple of decades ago, storage was not as cheap as it is today.
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> > programming thought a couple of decades ago.

Yep.  A couple of decades ago, I was an experienced programmer.   CPU time was
more expensive than it is now.  Converting a zip code to packed numeric and back
to save two bytes cost CPU time.  The only time when I would consider such a
thing was when we had a fixed record size which would be expensive to change,
and no more room.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T22:24:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cqj15$86o$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <vjKG6.54890$vg5.2979578@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Out of curiosity,

A) What compiler and operating system

B) What was the "picture" clause and usage for your numeric fields?

C) Does you compiler offer an "optimize" option - and did you use it for
both tests?

   ***

Bottom-Line:
  I agree with those in this thread who say that in a PERFORMANCE SENSITIVE
"loop" then you should test the specific example.  In  all other cases, the
chances are that INITIALIZE will be easier to maintain - and will do what
you want even when you add new fields or change picture/usage for numeric
items.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T07:25:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol

```
I always thought the INITIALIZE command should return what was initialized to
the way it was when the program started.

Besides being logical, such an INITIALIZE could be very efficient, depending on
how the compiler set it up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T14:36:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D1eI6.21292$sP6.1080902@news3.aus1.giganews.com>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:3AF15C39.48DA929C@brazee.net...
> I always thought the INITIALIZE command should return what was
initialized to
> the way it was when the program started.
>

Don't think so. Beginning values are compiler-dependent whereas the
operation of INITIALIZE is defined. Not only that, but consider:

01  MYSTUFF VALUE SPACE.
   02  MYNUMBER   PIC 9(5).
....

Beginning execution MYNUMBER has blanks in it whereas after INITIALIZE
it has a zero.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
