"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:3AF17A14.B32218C0@brazee.net...
>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Don't think so. Beginning values are compiler-dependent whereas
the
> > operation of INITIALIZE is defined. Not only that, but consider:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Beginning execution MYNUMBER has blanks in it whereas after
INITIALIZE
> > it has a zero.
>
> Since you can control what in in your VALUE clauses, you would be in
> control of how the INITIALIZE would have worked, had it been
implemented
> that way.
>
> How are these compiler dependent?

I meant the explanation to cover two completely different situations.
The VALUE thingy is one thingy; compiler dependency is another thingy.

As for compiler dependency, some compilers initialize the code
corresponding to the data elements (like an INITIALIZE statement
would), other compilers just reserve storage and are unconcerned with
beginning values at execution time, and, of those compilers that do
try to prime storage with meaningful default values, I betcha they
might still differ on things like:

05  FILLER  PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T07:31:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF2AF1E.2450FAD1@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <vjKG6.54890$vg5.2979578@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <9cqj15$86o$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3AF15C39.48DA929C@brazee.net> <D1eI6.21292$sP6.1080902@news3.aus1.giganews.com> <3AF17A14.B32218C0@brazee.net> <0tnI6.90702$Xl3.5308538@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Jerry P wrote:

>
> > How are these compiler dependent?
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 05  FILLER  PIC S9(9) COMP-5.

Sure, if INITIALIZE were to set up initial values, this is undefined
(unless it was part of a higher level group which was defined).  That's
the way we work now when we start the program.   What I wanted INITIALIZE
to do was to make that initialized something as if we were just starting
the program.  The programmer is responsible for making that meaningful by
putting in appropriate VALUE clauses.   If it worked this way, it would be
efficient.  The compiler simply keeps a copy of it when it creates the
initial values, without run-time determinations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T10:12:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF19160.93C9C307@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <vjKG6.54890$vg5.2979578@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <9cqj15$86o$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3AF15C39.48DA929C@brazee.net> <D1eI6.21292$sP6.1080902@news3.aus1.giganews.com>`

```


Jerry P wrote:

> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
> news:3AF15C39.48DA929C@brazee.net...
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> it has a zero.

The beginning value of MYNUMBER is undefined.  In some implementations it
might be spaces in some it might be almost anything.

The performance tests mentioned earlier have meaning only on one
implementation.  Others may be faster or slower, may use more code or
less code, and may do this or that.  Admittedly my compiler is exactly
the same as a series of moves, but an enhancement I work on from time to
time but have not finished will do simple ones in one giant swoop as
opposed to a series of moves.  For example, if you have ten PIC 9(5) s in
a row it will do one move to fill them all, not ten moves.

Don Nelson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T13:09:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cs6t3$bfk$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <vjKG6.54890$vg5.2979578@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <9cqj15$86o$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3AF15C39.48DA929C@brazee.net>`

```
Actually,  I don't think that even with the "new" enhancements to INITIALIZE
there is anything "guaranteed" to do this. The problem is (as other have
stated) that if you have a field defined with NO "VALUE" clause, then its
value at program initialization is UNDEFINED.  This means that you may or
may NOT get the same values at the beginning of every execution of that same
program (same compiler, environment, etc).

On IBM mainframes (for example) there is a "fairly unusual" situation in
which "uninitialized" fields get WHATEVER is left in storage from the last
time that was "used".

You may use the "new and improved" initialized statement to "re-set" all
fields (including FILLER fields) to whatever VALUE clause was specified in
the program source code (including the "now allowed" uses in Linkage Section
and File Section) - but there is still no way to get the "same garbage" as
those fields had when the program first started - if they had no VALUE
clause for them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T13:58:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d3l9m$6pt$2@news.utelfla.com>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <vjKG6.54890$vg5.2979578@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <9cqj15$86o$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3AF15C39.48DA929C@brazee.net>`

```
In article <3AF15C39.48DA929C@brazee.net>, howard@brazee.net wrote...

>I always thought the INITIALIZE command should return what was initialized to
>the way it was when the program started.

That's what it does for me. It sets alphas to spaces and numerics to zero.

I haven't tried it out yet, but does it override filler and value stuff?
If so, it would keep me from having to code

INITIALIZE receipt_number with zeros for alpha.
(Or whatever the exact syntax is)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-07T06:56:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF69B82.E1BCE08F@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <vjKG6.54890$vg5.2979578@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <9cqj15$86o$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3AF15C39.48DA929C@brazee.net> <9d3l9m$6pt$2@news.utelfla.com>`

```


Ubiquitous wrote:

> In article <3AF15C39.48DA929C@brazee.net>, howard@brazee.net wrote...
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's what it does for me. It sets alphas to spaces and numerics to zero.

Yep, but that's not how programs start with VALUE statements.


> I haven't tried it out yet, but does it override filler and value stuff?
> If so, it would keep me from having to code

Nope.

> INITIALIZE receipt_number with zeros for alpha.
> (Or whatever the exact syntax is)

I wish it had always looked at the VALUE statements as the default
initialization.   (and then allow values in files and linkage).   Besides being
more logical, it is also more flexible and much more efficient.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INITIALIZE v MOVE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-07T14:04:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NXxJ6.40555$sP6.2483323@news3.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9c59u5$536$6@news.utelfla.com> <vjKG6.54890$vg5.2979578@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <9cqj15$86o$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3AF15C39.48DA929C@brazee.net> <9d3l9m$6pt$2@news.utelfla.com>`

```

"Ubiquitous" <weberm@polaris.net> wrote in message
news:9d3l9m$6pt$2@news.utelfla.com...
> In article <3AF15C39.48DA929C@brazee.net>, howard@brazee.net
wrote...
>
> >I always thought the INITIALIZE command should return what was
initialized to
> >the way it was when the program started.
>

Nope. INITIALIZE sets to space and zeroes, which may not have been the
values at start-up time. INITIALIZE ignores VALUE statements.
```

<a id="t-bb01d375f4"></a>
## Thread: "P" picture character — 11 this month / 31 total

_Thread started [2001-04](2001-04.md#t-bb01d375f4)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "P" picture character: Solution

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T16:45:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9csjhb$1nd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2L%G6.56299$JI6.320659@news1.rdc1.tx.home.com> <9ci314$t9f$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <el1H6.59643$181.9230589@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <9cj6t0$14c$1@scavenger.euro.net> <pVeH6.61662$181.9598107@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
For the '85 Standard (and any "conforming" implementation)

   Pic  999ppp
        is valid and is equivalent to
   Pic  999pppV

Similarly,
   Pic  ppp999
        is valid and is equivalent to
   Pic Vppp999

This "thread" has pointed out an UNINTENDED (minor) bug in the FCD (draft of
the next Standard) but everyone "agrees" that it is intended that these
"variations" will continue to be supported as long as COBOL supports P's in
PICTURE clauses.

The '85 Standard (along with the IBM documentation) EXPLICITLY states that
the "V" is redundant when it is at the very left or very right of a string
of P's.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "P" picture character: Solution

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Apollo" <swartenbroekxro@wanadoo.be>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "P" picture character

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T21:00:46-05:00

My understanding is that it is used (relatively frequently) in "devalued"
currencies (e.g. Lire, Pesos - at some times, etc).  For example,

   05 Some-Currency-Amt   Pic   9999ppp.

In some cases, this is moved to "edited" fields of the type,
   05  Num-Edit     Pic 9,999,000..
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "P" picture character

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T19:35:58+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gcok7y0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<2L%G6.56299$JI6.320659@news1.rdc1.tx.home.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gclvwo0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <3AEEC766.195C2227@brazee.net>`

```
On Tue, 01 May 2001 08:25:42 -0600, Howard Brazee wrote:

>
>Willem Clements wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, TIME-OF-DAY is a six-digit numeric field, containing HHMMSS, and I want
to include only HH.MM in the headers of my listings. I am in europe, so we
don't use a decimal point, but a decimal comma and we specify DECIMAL POINT
IS COMMA.

The above move formats in one operation the time in the desired format. The
PP at the end of the picture scales the field, so the MOVE truncates the
seconds insteead of the hours.

I used this a lot with IBM VSE COBOL.


Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "P" picture character

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T21:04:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cqebi$ffe$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2L%G6.56299$JI6.320659@news1.rdc1.tx.home.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gclvwo0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
I really do WONDER about any compiler supporting

    99.99pp   (unless you also have "comma" and period reversed)

P's to the right of the decimal point *must* be immediately to the left of
the decimal point (not after other digits) - at least in ANSI (current and
past) Standards.
```

Jerry P wrote in message ...
>
>You didn't miss anything; "P" is never part of a class.
>
That's right. You always leave the class to have a P....

Of course, Object Oriented P is definitely part of a Class; the object being
those little cakes of strong smelling disinfectant found in urinals, and it
is a Class act if you can dissolve or break one without touching it with
your fingers...

In over 30 years of writing COBOL I have never used this construct. In the
REALLY old days (mid-60s) we were told it could save space (which cost a
fortune)  but I still don't recall ever seeing it used.

Pete Dashwood.

<a id="t-0a6fad4025"></a>
## Thread: double ( C type) to COBOL — 11 msgs

### double ( C type) to COBOL

- **From:** "Robert Lascelle" <_vlad3@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T15:58:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w2BH6.676800$f36.19109814@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
the COBOL compiler that use don't know about comp-2, and i have no idea how
to use pic to have the same as comp-2.
someone know how?

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: double ( C type) to COBOL

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T16:23:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lqBH6.419$oy1.90626@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<w2BH6.676800$f36.19109814@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Robert Lascelle <_vlad3@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:w2BH6.676800$f36.19109814@news20.bellglobal.com...
> the COBOL compiler that use don't know about comp-2, and i have no idea
how
> to use pic to have the same as comp-2.
> someone know how?
>

COMP-2 is implementor specific; that is, there is no "COBOL" standard for
it.

What it *generally* means is an 18-significant-digit (mantissa) number like
an IEEE double-precision float. ( or 'C' "double").

BUt... if you are trying to read data created with another compiler, you're
going
to have to figure out what that vendor used for COMP-2.

And one more thing: if the data come from an IBM mainframe, there are two
possible formats: IEEE or HEX (aka NATIVE); this option is either set at IPL
(system startup) or may have been overridden when the program was compiled.

Most important thing for you to do now is cast aspersions on the validity of
the birth of the imbecile who stored data on disk in COMP-2 format when the
possiblity of a port existed.

More info: read http://www.flexus.com/ebd2asc.html

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: double ( C type) to COBOL

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5FBH6.215$k95.33092@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<w2BH6.676800$f36.19109814@news20.bellglobal.com> <lqBH6.419$oy1.90626@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

I was trying to use C and COBOL together.
Some function in C and the main project in COBOL, one of the C
function was declare as
double Tquart(double nb);
so, with these explanation, i think it will be impossible to use this C
function. So now i have to make it in cobol, and i think i have to
use PIC S9(8)V9(4).
Am i right?

I'm begining in COBOL, this pic is a signed 8.4 number ( am i right?)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: double ( C type) to COBOL

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T20:23:41+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<strtet4u1fhg4prpfmh0qoqiv22uhavm9b@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<w2BH6.676800$f36.19109814@news20.bellglobal.com> <lqBH6.419$oy1.90626@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <5FBH6.215$k95.33092@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
On Tue, 01 May 2001 16:39:29 GMT "Robert Lascelle" <_vlad3@sympatico.ca>
wrote:

:>I was trying to use C and COBOL together.
:>Some function in C and the main project in COBOL, one of the C
:>function was declare as
:>double Tquart(double nb);
:>so, with these explanation, i think it will be impossible to use this C
:>function. So now i have to make it in cobol, and i think i have to
:>use PIC S9(8)V9(4).
:>Am i right?

In IBM COBOL, a COMP-2 does not have a picture as it has a hardware format.
You can MOVE it to a s9(8)V9(4) field.

   [ snipped ]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: double ( C type) to COBOL

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T18:00:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010501140028.06927.00001676@ng-bh1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<5FBH6.215$k95.33092@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Robert Lascelle writes...

>Thanks.
>I was trying to use C and COBOL together.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Am i right?

If you use OS/390 C compiler and COBOL for OS/390 & VM or COBOL for MVS & VM,
it is not only easy to mix languages, but your COBOL program can call C
functions directly.

In your example, why not

       call 'tquart' using in-field returning out-field

where both in-field and out-field are defined as comp-2 (and, as someone
remarked, no PIC clause).

Regards,




Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_4EH6.1274$Gf1.282555@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<w2BH6.676800$f36.19109814@news20.bellglobal.com> <lqBH6.419$oy1.90626@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <5FBH6.215$k95.33092@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Robert Lascelle <_vlad3@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:5FBH6.215$k95.33092@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Thanks.
> I was trying to use C and COBOL together.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, you're not right. COMP-2 format is, as I said before,
"implementor-defined."

It sounds like what you are trying to do is call a C routine from a COBOL
program (?????).

If so, you need to check your COBOL compiler documentation to see how to
pass an IEEE double. Every C/C++ complier I've ever heard of (except IBM
mainframe) uses IEEE double-precision floats for type "double".

Even at that, be aware that a C routine declared that way is expecting 8
bytes on the stack BY VALUE in this format.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: double ( C type) to COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T21:52:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cqh6h$af0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<w2BH6.676800$f36.19109814@news20.bellglobal.com> <9cn4vk$s2g$1@news.netmar.com> <Cw%H6.5833$nC6.689884@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
It doesn't surprise me at all that TinyCOBOL does not *yet* support any
floating-point data-type (such as COMP-1 or COMP-2 - that are common in IBM
and IBM compatible compilers) - because the EXISTING ANSI COBOL Standard
doesn't support it.

HOWEVER, the draft of the next COBOL Standard *does* include support for
some NEW
    USAGE FLOAT-xxxx
data-types.

I suggest that you contact the TinyCOBOL people and ask them if they have
any "intention" to support this sooner than later.

ALTERNATIVELY, you can change your C program (if you have control over it)
to support a binary or decimal type field.  These are supported in ALL
ANSI/ISO conforming COBOL compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: double ( C type) to COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T00:35:13-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF0E001.9B8E5C29@arvotek.net>`
- **References:** `<w2BH6.676800$f36.19109814@news20.bellglobal.com> <9cn4vk$s2g$1@news.netmar.com> <Cw%H6.5833$nC6.689884@news20.bellglobal.com> <9cqh6h$af0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> It doesn't surprise me at all that TinyCOBOL does not *yet* support any
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> any "intention" to support this sooner than later.

There are no plans for TinyCOBOL to support COMP-1 or COMP-2 floating
point data types.

The reason for this is that, to my knowledge, no one has requested
support for these data types in TinyCOBOL. 

As a matter of fact I do not think anyone has even mentioned this on the
mailing list.


David Essex

TinyCOBOL developer
```

#### ↳ Re: Acucobol and RM Cobol conversions

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-01T19:05:59+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<geutets325931qq88p8tbaaacpmbf6etl0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<sYhG6.36$9w4.166927@sol.pdnt.net>`

```
On Fri, 27 Apr 2001 12:25:49 -0500, "Astro" <astrosky@gman.com> wrote:

>I would like to cnoovert Acucobol data to RM/Laint Cobol. Any one have a
>utility do to this?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Depending on the version you are using of Acucobol, and on the
functionality (extensions) you had used it may be impossible to do
this with a tool, as there are things that Acucobol allows that have
no possible correspondence in RM.

I had in times my source code working with both Aucobol and RM, and I
was using some of Acucobol's functionalities, and only had a few
diferent copybooks for each compiler.

If you require more info, please contact me by email.


Frederico
```

#### ↳ Re: Acucobol and RM Cobol conversions

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik-nws@siber.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<3AF9F27A.B8F938F4@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<sYhG6.36$9w4.166927@sol.pdnt.net>`

```
You can try converting RM Cobol data to CSV (comma-separated values)
files 
first using our Data2Flat converter.

Then you can load these data files into AcuCobol files,
say, by reading them with a program.

More on it at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Regards,
Vaidm Maslov

On Tue, 1 May 2001 13:39:53 -0500, "Scott Stembaugh"
<scott@purdue.edu> enlightened us:

>As an exercise I am looking for competeing products to CA Optimizer II for
>MVS 2.6.  Any pointers on where to look are appreciated.
>
>thanks,
>--scott

Wish I could help out a fellow Boilermaker but that may not be
possible.  The reason being two fold:

1)  If there was a company that offered a product similar to CA
Optimizer, CA would have already bought it;

2)  IBM (maybe as a slap in the face to CA) added a CBL option
OPTIMIZE which does essentially what CA's product does.

Maybe someone else knows of a competing product, but I was not able to
find one.

Regards,

Steve

>When did Realia become non-ANSI standard?  It used to be the best ANSI-compatible
>compiler around.  I haven't used it for a few years, though, so maybe things have
>changed.

Some of the Picture clauses (PIC Z for example)  won't work as with Ansi
Standard. Also some of the higher functions were cut down Ie: the "Using"
clause had limited functionality compared to other Ansi Cobol Compilers.

Or maybe it's just me being spoiled with a compiler that had greater
functionality before using Realia.

Regards,
Tornado.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cheap Ansi standard Cobol for single user.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** JD <pb@dazoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-05T10:51:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF41382.488B3717@dazoo.com>`
- **References:** `<3aeb6c63.4692762@news.totalserve.net> <3AEC2D28.82F9C565@dazoo.com> <3aef490e.101608141@news.totalserve.net> <3af29c76.29038694@news1.attglobal.net> <3af41d5d.79680380@news.totalserve.net>`

```
Since the purpose of PIC Z is to zero suppress a single digit, just use PIC 9 BLANK
WHEN ZERO if your compiler does not support the single-digit Z.  Since Realia does
in fact support PIC Z9, I'm surprised that it would not support PIC Z.

Can you post the actual code and resultant compile error?

tornado@totalserve.co.uk wrote:

> >Sounds to me as if you want a "more than Ansi standard" compiler.
> >Realia has always supported more syntax on the Call statement than the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Cheap Ansi standard Cobol for single user.

- **From:** "Charles" <charles@godwin.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T22:05:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ax%H6.1239$aV.20282@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3aeb6c63.4692762@news.totalserve.net>`

```
As development manager for the CA-Realia compiler I'm intrigued by your
statements about Realia not being "ansi" standard. I have read your reply
listing calling parameters and pic Z as some of your reasons.

The CA-Realia compiler may not support Pic Z but can support asciiz strings
if that's what you need and can support all calling conventions used by
Microsoft. At CA we use COBOL and C in mixed language systems regularly so I
wonder what more you need.

Please let me know specifics of what you need and / or what you're trying to
do and I will try to satisfy you.

I doubt, however, if I'll satisfy your price wishes however, but there are
few , if any, "cheap" compilers available that provide a solid development
platform for win32. If you're planning on going multi-user you should
reconsider your evaluation of "cheap" if you want to support your users in
the long term. you can save money now and pay in user support and
reliability or buy a good compiler now, either from CA or someone else and
get a solid platform for your operational code.

Our current version is Ca-Realia II Workbench 3.1 and the compiler is
version 6.0

Charles Godwin
Manager - Develpment
Computer Associates
charles.godwin@ca.com

recently.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cheap Ansi standard Cobol for single user.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T21:24:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cqfhq$h82$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3aeb6c63.4692762@news.totalserve.net> <ax%H6.1239$aV.20282@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Charles,
   Huh ????

PIC Z has *nothing* to do with ascii-z strings.  It has to do with
"numeric-edited picture strings -with zero suppression".

Are you thinking of Z-literals which are an IBM (and MERANT - and possibly
other) extensions - which *are* used for null-terminated strings.

I believe that CA-Realia *is* still ANSI conforming.  I would like to know
of any specific examples of where it is "not".  It is POSSIBLE that they
don't have "intrinsic functions" which is OPTIONAL in the ANSI Standard but
REQUIRED in the FIPS standard, but I thought they had that as well.

If there are any specific PICTURE clauses or USING phrases that are causing
problems, could the original poster please "post" them?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cheap Ansi standard Cobol for single user.

- **From:** "Charles" <charles@godwin.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T02:51:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Bill,

you got me. I WAS thinking of Z'literals, not Pic Z. We are ansi compatible
with almost all of the intrinsic functions supported. A very small number
are not yet done, but I'd have to check to tell you which ones.

<a id="t-891e080b68"></a>
## Thread: Internet Enabled COBOL - Not Spam — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### Internet Enabled COBOL - Not Spam

- **From:** merhottin@aol.com (Merhottin)
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T03:40:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010501234047.13328.00001776@ng-ci1.aol.com>`

```
Hi All,

     We are a COBOL shop, and outsourced our web site. To make a long story
short, It was written in Java Script,  a foreign language to us, it cost alot
of money, it couldnt handle our business applications like we would of liked
and it wasnt deleivered for over a year.
     We had purchased Fujitsu 5.0 and Flexus SP2 for some in house stuff.  We
were going to learn Java script, but decieded to try Flexus Thin Client.
     After 2 weeks, we had a rather large program, 23 files, over a million
records (history for a year + ) , ready to try via the internet.  One word:
INCREDIBLE.  
     Using a 56K modem from home, I was able to access our server, and from the
password screen to the first record I wanted, was under 20 seconds.  Another
programmer that has a cable modem said it was up in like 5 seconds.
    SP2 is also windows based, so it looks like the client is running the
application right from thier desk top.
    If you are a COBOL shop, and need to put applications on the web, this is
definatly the way to go.  Flexus offers a demo version of SP2 and Thin Client. 
They also offered technical assistance (always got an email back within 1 to 2
hours).
     We have decieded to scrap the web site, and Internet enable any
applications our clients need.  It was also very cost effective (for right now,
we only have between 5 - 8 clients)  It cost less to purchase the software then
it did to be held hostage by our web company.
     I can not tell you how happy we are with the results.  I hate to sound
like a salesperson, and didnt want to come off as one, but you have to try this
software. The demos are free, and again Flexus will offer support.  Any
questions, feel free to send my way, but I know Bob Wolfe from Flexus will
point you in the right direction.  It really is a joy going to work now, and
being appreciated from the boys upstairs ( they think we are incredibly smart
now, little do they know....)
     I havent been exposed to much web development, but do believe COBOL is
still the way to go for business applications.  The web developers always took
so long to make a correct change.  
     If one person tries this and uses it, my goodwill to others would have
been accomplished.  Thats just my 2 cents, for what its worth...........

Have a good one,

John
```

#### ↳ Re: Internet Enabled COBOL - Not Spam

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T16:04:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3af02e4b.9328181@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<20010501234047.13328.00001776@ng-ci1.aol.com>`

```
John:

Thank you very much for the compliments.  They are appreciated.

I think that the issues here are:

1.  Yes, you can accomplish the job in COBOL (whether or not you use
the Flexus products).

2.  There IS more than one way to "internet enable" your program and
it DOESN'T have to display the screens in a web browser.

3.  Sometimes displaying the screens in a web browser is a BAD idea
for a business-to-business application which needs to run across a
TCP/IP network.

Thanks again for the kind words.



merhottin@aol.com (Merhottin) wrote:

>Hi All,
>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>John

Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Internet Enabled COBOL - Not Spam

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@ont.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T12:49:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B081FB8.4B69315D@ont.com>`
- **References:** `<20010501234047.13328.00001776@ng-ci1.aol.com> <3af02e4b.9328181@news.epix.net>`

```
< Legacy propaganda snipped! >

How dare you suggest that a COBOL programmer can replace a staff of
people for an online business application. If this catches on, we could
have massive unemployment in headhunters, tools-people, java-people,
web-people, c/c++ people, and with smaller staffs-project managers  and
CTO's too.

Business programmers? None of the current internet-enabling staff in
most companies know anything about business, they are "engineers",
implementing the latest-and-greatest technologies, and don't have time
to concern themselves with business! 

Get with the current trends. Most companies now have a CTO to hide the
business side of the company from the internet developers, and to
out-source when staffs get too big, thus lowering budgets and getting a
bonus before he goes somewhere else to do it again. During an
application development, the rules and technologies change so fast it
causes a high turn-over rate in employees. This allows salaries to go
up, and delays to be  extended even longer. It also keeps headhunters
doing placements (at 25%-35%), and consultants on more payrolls to
explain why things are going so slowly. The engineers that cannot keep
up with the furious change rate will be added to the consultant ranks,
which provides a steady replacement for the consultants that move into
CTO positions. The consulting position is an OJT requirement for CTO
wannabees, because they have to gain the experience of hiding all
technical details and faults from the business people and management.

It has taken many years for us to arrange our "business" plan.  We first
had to start at Colleges and Universities, getting the Professors to
convince everyone that "legacy" systems had outlived their usefulness.
Then having them adopt the new wave of languages and technologies, which
not only gave them higher incomes, but brought in new students,
increasing classes, and therefore increasing the sizes of the CS
departments. We made every effort to get them to implement languages
that are not maintainable for years at a time. This helped to keep the
number of students up, because of the newer languages that had to be
taught, because they fixed the problems caused by the current languages. 

Because maintenance is impossible over a period of time, most
modifications are transformed into new development efforts, which again
involves increasing staff sizes. This really works! Look at the history
of just the last few years. COBOL had to be replaced by C,C by C++,C++
by JAVA/BEANS/JINI/JUNK, and so on. The rapid pace of change also
enables an "upgrade every two years" for commercial software developed
following our plans. 

Microsoft started as one of our early supporters by divorcing themselves
from "legacy" tools like COBOL, and has continued by forcing change upon
the industry. By modifying their API's (mainly to break the competitor's
software) they can not only sell new versions of their OS, but also
enabled them to reap a gold mine in new tools-sold to their competition!
We are hoping that Microsoft can complete their plan of destroying all
industry and ISO standards, and replacing them with theirs. We have
encouraged them to implement completely new languages, which would
insure that change is part of the industry, and that everything would
have to be developed from scratch every two years.

Gone are the days when people wrote BASIC programs because there was no
business reason (your term not ours) to develop it COBOL, or because
staff was too small. You brought it on yourself by using the term
"maintenance" when updating programs to reflect new laws, tax changes,
and business rules. Because you actually produced something and were
valuable to your company, you simply cost too much. (Remember,
management thinks that 80% of your just is fixed broken programs.) Now
they can pay many more people, many more dollars, to develop C/C++
programs which call JAVA (interpreted just like BASIC was...), and
format everything to be displayed on a web page-just because they can,
and it doesn't even have to work! Your out-moded concepts of business
programming, and requiring programmers to understand their company's
business, has no place in the current marketplace. 

If you continue promoting the idea of using existing staff to do the
work, using "legacy" tools with only a few add-ons, most of us will be
out of work, or even worse, *WE* may have to produce something! Today's
economy cannot support your new-fangled ideas.



Ok, I feel better now (:-)

Gary
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3B082C00.9697C50C@ont.com>`

Deek

#### ↳ Re: outside of class project

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T12:02:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jHSH6.75$X65.23536@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<20010502000551.26481.00002230@ng-bk1.aol.com>`

```
Deek163 <deek163@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010502000551.26481.00002230@ng-bk1.aol.com...
> AFter my exams are done i am going to try writing a COBOL program just for
the
> expierence and to gain extra knowledge as i am done with my cobol
requirements.
>  My question is i would like to find out someone that could help me with
any
> questions i may have, my problem is i dont want to post my full code for
fear
> some other student might get greedy and decide to borrow it.  Would
anybody be
> willing to help once i understake this task.  I just mean to ask questions
when
> i am stuck nothing major.
>

First off, you will not always need to post all your code (although that
will be necessary from time-to-time).

Second, as has been pointed out, since class is over your efforts don't rate
to help any other students.

Last, anyone who DOES copy your work as a means of "sliding through" is no
long-term threat to you or anyone else willing to do the work. Although,
when you are gainfully employed as a programmer, you WILL meet these people
for lunch. They will be asking if you want any fries with that.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: outside of class project

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T07:06:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF00662.F83B6B0F@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010502000551.26481.00002230@ng-bk1.aol.com>`

```
Deek163 wrote:

> AFter my exams are done i am going to try writing a COBOL program just for the
> expierence and to gain extra knowledge as i am done with my cobol requirements.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Deek

This doesn't make sense.  You are writing a program for yourself outside of your
class, but don't want other students seeing your work.

I'll pretend it makes sense:

1.  You need to come up with a reasonable project, and create requirements and/or
specs.    This is very important experience.   If you are serious, start a thread
to assist you in this endeavor.

2.  After you have good specs, write your test plan.   Again, this could be a good
newsgroup thread, which would be of interest to the rest of us.  Specs and test
plans are very important parts of programming.   These are the main part, with
coding the trivial part.

3.  Then start coding.  As you come to difficulties, post enough information so
that we know your environment, what you're trying to do, why you think what you're
getting is incorrect, and what you've done to try to resolve your difficulty.

We will give you clues to allow you to find your solutions.   We like to do this
publicly so that we all can learn from the project.


I would very much welcome thread(s) on "I am having trouble developing a
comprehensive test plan with my homework".   I suspect we don't have these threads
because the courses neglect the important stuff and concentrate on syntax.
```

- **References:** `<3AF00662.F83B6B0F@brazee.net>`

<a id="t-e57ddc9e46"></a>
## Thread: How do I disable Ctrl+C and Ctrl+Break in MS COBOL 5.0? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How do I disable Ctrl+C and Ctrl+Break in MS COBOL 5.0?

- **From:** "Leo Elsenberg" <elsenberg@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T09:17:05+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9coc8b$4js$07$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Could someone explain how one disables the Ctrl+C and Ctrl+Break keys in a
COBOL program. I am using Microsoft COBOL 5.0. I have tried Adiscf and
Keybcf, but no success. The sample program found in the MS COBOL 5.0 System
Reference, pages 42-46, does not seem to explain the problem fully.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I disable Ctrl+C and Ctrl+Break in MS COBOL 5.0?

- **From:** "Steven O. Ellis" <soellis@soltec.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T19:17:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF1F537.302884D4@soltec.net>`
- **References:** `<9coc8b$4js$07$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Alister seems to have covered it, for the MS compiler.

I just ran into the same type of problem running RM/COBOL on AIX.  I
wanted not to disable, but to capture Ctrl-C when firing off a system
call.  I wanted to know if the user pressed Ctrl-C while the system
command was executing.  No go.  I even put in a work request with Liant
Software, and they agreed that the return code was not getting picked up
by RM/COBOL on AIX, when the user presses Ctrl-C during a system call.

RM/COBOL always sees the return code from a UNIX script, however, so
coded my COBOL program to create a temporary script on the fly and then
fire off a system call to execute the script.

I got it to work, but the time investment testing the nonworking
function before I coded the work-around was considerable.  :(

Steven


Leo Elsenberg wrote:
> 
> Could someone explain how one disables the Ctrl+C and Ctrl+Break keys in a
> COBOL program. I am using Microsoft COBOL 5.0. I have tried Adiscf and
> Keybcf, but no success. The sample program found in the MS COBOL 5.0 System
> Reference, pages 42-46, does not seem to explain the problem fully.
```

<a id="t-9f1e695b0c"></a>
## Thread: RE- How do I disable Ctrl+C and Ctrl+Break in MS COBOL 5.0 ? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### RE- How do I disable Ctrl+C and Ctrl+Break in MS COBOL 5.0 ?

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T20:17:32+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cpinh$5th$2@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Got a 'Message to old' error when replying ?

This should do the trick as I just chopped it out of some code I
still use today. It is a good idea to keep it as a separate program
which is called and cancelled at the start of your system as it is
operating system specific.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.

         PROGRAM-ID. zzmenusz.
      *  AUTHOR.     Alister Munro, 01/03/1993.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

         CONFIGURATION SECTION.

           SPECIAL-NAMES.

         INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

           FILE-CONTROL.

       DATA DIVISION.

         FILE SECTION.

         WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

      *  ************
      *  * LITERALS *
      *  ************

           78  78-kb-io                VALUE x"B0".

      *  *********************************
      *  * Used to dissable CTRL + BREAK *
      *  *********************************

           01  ws-kb-io-grp.
             03  ws-kb-io-func         PIC 9(02)   BINARY.
             03  ws-kb-io-parm         PIC 9(02)   BINARY.

         LINKAGE SECTION.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

         INITIALISE SECTION.

           MOVE 4 TO ws-kb-io-func
           MOVE 1 TO ws-kb-io-parm
           CALL 78-kb-io USING ws-kb-io-func ws-kb-io-parm

         EXIT PROGRAM.

       STOP RUN.
```

#### ↳ Re: RE- How do I disable Ctrl+C and Ctrl+Break in MS COBOL 5.0 ?

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T20:19:30+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cpiq4$93l$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<9cpinh$5th$2@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Forgot to mention that this works with the old MF WB compilers
and should work with Microsofts.
```

## Thread: Extract COBOL records in C — 25 msgs

### Extract COBOL records in C

- **From:** "Jeff D. Hamann" <hamannj@ucs.orst.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T12:25:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cpmus$1oa$1@news.orst.edu>`

```
Hi all, I'm new to the group so be gentle with the flaming, please.

I have a collection of COBOL files (I'm not sure if that's the correct term
as I'm a C programmer) I would like to be able to "export" or translate into
a standard database package and would like to know if there are packages out
there that will help "reverse engineer" COBOL files. I can get the file
formats like


01 NEW-TEMP-1-RECORD.
    05 NT-SUB-KEY-1                    PIC X(10).
    05 NT-SUB-KEY-2                    PIC X(10).
    05 NT-SUB-KEY-3                    PIC X(10).
    05 NT-ALP-SPECIES               PIC X(3).
    05 NT-TOT-LENGHT                PIC -9(9).
     blah, blah, blah....


I just want to open the file, import the records (some file I do have the
file format for and some I don't) and close the file. Are there tools out
therre for doing that?

Thanks,
Jeff.




----------------------------------
Jeff D. Hamann
280 Peavy Hall
Department of Forest Resources
Oregon State University
Corvallis, Oregon 97331-8566 USA
541-740-5988
541-737-2375
hamannj@ucs.orst.edu
http://ucs.orst.edu/~hamannj
http://stimpy.cof.orst.edu

It goes on and on my friends

Not knowing what it was
Now everybody's singing it
And it goes on and on because
This is the song that never ends...
```

#### ↳ Re: Extract COBOL records in C

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T14:27:21-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF06DA9.3D349F56@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<9cpmus$1oa$1@news.orst.edu>`

```


"Jeff D. Hamann" wrote:

> Hi all, I'm new to the group so be gentle with the flaming, please.
>
> I have a collection of COBOL files (I'm not sure if that's the correct term
> as I'm a C programmer)

Pretty much - we call them "files", leaving out the COBOL part.   We can't tell
them apart from programs written by other languages such as EasyTrieve.  Newer
database based languages have meta data to help them out.


> I would like to be able to "export" or translate into
> a standard database package and would like to know if there are packages out
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> therre for doing that?

There are - when we get this question, others tell about their favorite products
or the products they produce.

When you have the file file format, it isn't too difficult to convert that file
format into something C can use, or you can write another CoBOL program to
export it in whatever format you like.   If you don't have the file format, you
need to do some work to infer from the way the data look to get a file format.

Another thing you need to know is the computer which created the data.
Different computers handle signs differently.

As long as you don't have packed or binary fields, this should be enough
information.   Otherwise, your work will be tougher.   Do you have a hex editor
to look at your data?    Tell us the computer source, and post some data showing
the hex values.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Extract COBOL records in C

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T23:20:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010502192005.10134.00002210@ng-ci1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<9cpqnn$26ui$1@scavenger.euro.net>`

```
Why do people talk about COBOL records and C records? Data is data. There are
records in a file and you can read just about any data in just about any file
from programs written in just about any language.

Maybe it's a PC thing. On the mainframe, you create a file, using a utility,
basic data entry, or a program written in any language.

The file doesn't give any indication how it was created. The data can be read
by programs in any other language.

You don't need to associate a language with a file.

Regards,

Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

including spreadsheets here loosely) which contains data that DESCRIBES
the Data that follows.

That's how Version X of Excel / Quattro knows which version you
are trying to read in.  Most 'C' programmers nowadays learned on
PCs and I'd bet a lot of them  only know 'C' and their native language.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Extract COBOL records in C

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T22:01:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cqhlt$bcq$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9cpqnn$26ui$1@scavenger.euro.net> <20010502192005.10134.00002210@ng-ci1.aol.com>`

```
Steve,
  This is a MAJOR difference between (IBM) mainframe environments and
"workstations" (both WinTel *and* UNIX).

On the IBM Mainframe, "file structures" (corresponding to COBOL sequential,
indexed and relative) are a part of the operating system software
(DFS-whatever).  All the 2nd, 3rd, 4th generation programming languages use
the SAME OS-provided file systems (and "access methods).

On Workstations, there are *usually*
   "line sequential" (carriage-return/line-feed termination for each record)
files

and that is *all* that the operating system REALLY knows about - and that is
all that is portable across "programming languages" (except "ANSI Standard
COBOL that does NOT know about this format).

Each COBOL vendor is *required* to come up with their own way to handle
"record sequential" files (probably easy and relatively portable) and
indexed and relative files (TOTALLY unportable across COBOL
implementations - and even - in some cases - across OS's for the same
compiler vendor).

There are a "couple" of vendors of specific file systems (usually indexed).
I can think of BTRIEVE and C-ISAM - and there may be a few others.  Many of
the workstation COBOL products have OPTIONS to use these systems, but not
all COBOLs support them, and they aren't NEARLY as wide-spread as the
WinTel/Unix systems are.

   ***

Clearly when you get to databases (relational, hierarchical, whatever) these
may or may not be "sold" by the same vendor that sells the compiler or the
operating system or something else.

   ***

Does this help explain the "different world views"?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Extract COBOL records in C

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T04:00:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010503000031.02866.00000543@nso-fm.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<9cqhlt$bcq$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <9cqhlt$bcq$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> writes:

>  This is a MAJOR difference between (IBM) mainframe environments and
>"workstations" (both WinTel *and* UNIX).
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am going to have to argue a point here , Bill.
I operate in both the PC and Mainframe world, and have had a brief
exposure to the IBM mainframe world as well.
When dealing with FLAT files, a file is a file is a file.
Granted that most COBOL record/file definitions are Record Sequential
versus the PC-ish default of Line Sequential.  I am certain that it is possible
to read/process record sequential files in any of the current languages.
It is just a matter of understanding how to define the 'structure' in the 
language dujior(?).
Yes Indexed, Relative, and DMS structures are very likely to be implementor
defined, but FLAT files are readable across platforms and languages
if you have an understanding of the record structure and field types.
My experience has been in converting data from multiple vendors on multiple
platforms.  All of which are/were able to produce a flatfile extract readable
either by PC based products written in C, Delphi, or COBOL and mainframe
based products written in C, PL/1, ALGOL, COBOL, RPG.
Even the odd Variable Length Record format file is able to be dealt with.
The biggest area of concern has to do with the hex representation of numeric
data and the BIG vs LITTLE Endian handling of such fields.

Even in the PC environment, you cannot send a dBase file to someone 
and expect them to be able to process the file without some knowledge
of its record composition or an application capable of understanding the
file structure provided in the file header.  dBase may be a poor choice
of an example, but the basic concept is there.  Even from dBase, you normally
would export to a CSV or other text based interchange format.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Extract COBOL records in C

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T21:36:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF0E069.1C3@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<9cqhlt$bcq$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <20010503000031.02866.00000543@nso-fm.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> The biggest area of concern has to do with the hex representation of numeric
> data and the BIG vs LITTLE Endian handling of such fields.

Even that isn't difficult, provided you know what you've got.   Even if you don't, a 
little experimentation soon tells you.

> Even in the PC environment, you cannot send a dBase file to someone
> and expect them to be able to process the file without some knowledge
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> would export to a CSV or other text based interchange format.

dBase contains all of the structure information you need in its header record, the 
format of which is widely known and easy to interpret. It identifies field sizes and 
types (including date unambiguous date fields). It's become almost as much of a standard 
as CSV for file exchange because of that. It's main drawback compared with CSV is file 
size, because it uses fixed length records.

You don't have to use any huge DLLs or add-in libraries to read and write it - I wrote 
my own dBase I/O routines for ParseRat (http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm) fairly 
quickly in C++.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Extract COBOL records in C

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T05:42:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9crcmv$4g2$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9cqhlt$bcq$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <20010503000031.02866.00000543@nso-fm.aol.com>`

```
However the USUAL (not only - but "usual") flat file on PC's and Unix is
"line sequential" (with carriage control and/or line feed at the end of each
record).  There are a VARIETY of reasons that this does *not* work correctly
with the ANSI/ISO COBOL "definition" of sequential.  Therefore, you must use
"record sequential" file processing in "Standard" COBOL - which leaves you
with files that canNOT be handled by other PC/Unix software.

Also, as you say, "variable length" flat (sequential files) are often NOT
supported in the OS (although there are SOME ways in which "line sequential"
files work like variable length files and some in which they work like fixed
length).

Bottom-Line:
  (as I originally stated) on Mainframes (at least IBM - and I think others)
the "file system" belongs to the operating system and has everything you
need for all types of "Standard" COBOL files - on PCs and Unix this is NOT
true.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Extract COBOL records in C

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

Sff5ky wrote:

> Even in the PC environment, you cannot send a dBase file to someone
> and expect them to be able to process the file without some knowledge
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> would export to a CSV or other text based interchange format.

I'm told that DB files are 'portable' - so please tell me more. Can you illustrate
with a (1) A COBOL record format (2) the DB2 structure and (3) MS Access structure
?

Further, can the problem be 'resolved' by holding numerics (e.g. 999.99) as text
fields within the DB records and treating same as 999v99 in the business logic
handling the data ? (I'm assuming here that any 'numeric' handling is done in
'Business' and not DB or SQL computations).

As background I'm currently reading up on SQL and see that DB2 allows for 'decimal'
fields, whereas Access has 'numeric' fields. Currently to overcome latter, I have
specified :-

    Data Type            = Currency
    Format                 = General Number
    Decimals Places    = 1 ( or 2, 3, ......)

the above of course gives me decimal points when I view the Access tables.

Jimmy
```

Sff5ky wrote in message <20010503000031.02866.00000543@nso-fm.aol.com>...
>
>Even in the PC environment, you cannot send a dBase file to someone
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>of an example, but the basic concept is there.  Even from dBase, you
normally
>would export to a CSV or other text based interchange format.
>
The point that's being missed is that Database "files" contain information
about their own "structure". And that includes DBase. You COULD send a DBase
file to someone else and they WOULD be able to "process it" even WITHOUT any
knowledge of its physical structure, provided they had an ODBC driver for
it. (To be fair, James qualified his statement saying: "or an application
capable of understanding the file structure provided in the file header." It
is important to realise that this is the LOGICAL structure, NOT the
PHYSICAL.

It is quite "wrong" to visualise Databases the same way you would visualise
a "file". The physical structure of the database is irrelevant (except to
DBAs who may want to do some optimizing...I'll qualify my statement:
"...irrelevant to the person or application wanting to use the data.")

Although you CAN use "import/export" to a CSV file, you certainly don't HAVE
to. I have written programs which read  2 ACCESS databases on a PC,  a DB2
database on a mainframe, and wrote records derived from joins of both these
systems to yet a third system on an ORACLE server. (No import/export
involved...direct creation of a new database on the ORACLE server using SQL
CREATE.)  (All systems on the same LAN, of course...<G>)
This can be done in PC COBOL and is no big deal...that's what ODBC is for.

The secret is to use ONLY COBOL and ODBC. DON'T use the binding tools
provided for database illiterate programmers...Data bound grids, ACCESS
BASIC, ORACLE Procedures, etc...in fact, don't use ANYTHING that is platform
specific! Just SQL and COBOL, and connection through an ODBC driver for the
required Database.

I don't want to be too hard on some of the tools (I have used Data bound
grids with Fujitsu PowerCOBOL for GUI DB applications just as an experiment,
and it worked very well), but everything you want to do can be done if you
keep to the simple, basic concept that the database is a repository for data
and you manipulate it with COBOL.

Coming back to the example of DBase, like most RDBs, if you point Crystal
Reports at it, it will immediately analyse the logical structure of the
database and return the tables and fields. It does the same with MSACCESS
and ORACLE and probably anything for which it has an ODBC driver. (I haven't
tried it with EVERYTHING...<G>).

My own DECLGEN software utilises the  logical structural information about
an MS  ACCESS database, which is stored on the database, to produce COBOL
record layouts  and Host Variable definitions for inclusion in COBOL
programs which want to access the DB. The same software can produce code to
maintain the database, making it, in effect, a self maintaining database
(you never need to write SQL to maintain it, only to access it.). This is
all possible because the RDB contains a full picture of the logical database
structure, including the Tables (Relations),  Columns (Fields), Rows
(Tuples), Indexes, Keys, and Relationships (one-to-many;many-to-one, and,
where available, many-to-many).

The important thing to remember is that the LOGICAL structure of a database
is all that matters to the user, and it could be far removed from the
PHYSICAL structure, which, unlike a Flat File, is irrelevant.

A modern Relational Database is as far removed from a Flat File as an F16 is
from a Sopwith Camel. They share common ancestry, are both useful and fun,
but when it comes to capability...don't mess with the F16.

Pete.

                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Try using ParseRat to extract data

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T21:26:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF0DE10.5AF5@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<9cpqnn$26ui$1@scavenger.euro.net> <20010502192005.10134.00002210@ng-ci1.aol.com>`

```
S Comstock wrote:
> 
> Why do people talk about COBOL records and C records? Data is data. There are
> records in a file and you can read just about any data in just about any file
> from programs written in just about any language.

Very true.  Our ParseRat program (http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm) can extract 
and export data from pretty much anything provided there is SOME structure to the file.

It happens to have been written in C++, but it could have been written in something 
else. I wouldn't really have WANTED to write it in COBOL, though - I can remember 
writing parsing routines in COBOL in the late 60's and it wasn't fun - but it WAS 
better than FORTRAN for text processing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Try using ParseRat to extract data

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T10:18:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010516061836.25461.00000004@ng-ca1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3B01DDE4.4E07@paralynx.com>`

```
>From: Ed Guy ed_guy@paralynx.com 
>Date: 2001-05-15 21:54 Eastern Daylight Time

>And if you didn't plan your OWN tests well in advance. Been there, done that,
>rejected 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>it a mistake or not?.

Well, under the American System of Management all blame is
affixed to the lowest ranking person who had any detectible
involvement.  
The story has a happy ending though--both companies have
now gone out of business.  

A similar case of "COBOL data" versus "Visual Basic data"
occurred at the DMV.  Fortunately, it was picked up much
earlier.  
The COBOL side had multiple layouts for different record types.
The VB guys quite rightly asked that each type be dumped
in a separate file.  The COBOL guys said
    IF RECORD-TYPE = NN
        MOVE INPUT-RECORD   TO OUTPUT-RECORD-TYPE-NN
        WRITE OUTPUT-RECORD
So then the VB guys got a brief lesson in the concept of FILLER.
The second time around it was learned that just because a
field *says* PIC 9999 does not mean that *is* PIC 9999.  If
the field was defined back at the design time, but then in fact
never used (or originally used, and then later not used, and
thenceforth ignored), it could be anything.  
I don't think this situation can arise in PC-oriented languages,
can it? 


Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Try using ParseRat to extract data

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T13:52:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ICvM6.175$AB1.99598@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3B01DDE4.4E07@paralynx.com> <20010516061836.25461.00000004@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20010516061836.25461.00000004@ng-ca1.aol.com...
> A similar case of "COBOL data" versus "Visual Basic data"
> occurred at the DMV.  Fortunately, it was picked up much
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not only "CAN" happen, but "DOES" happen.

Look at the most PC-oriented language possible: Windows itself.

There are numerous Win32-API calls where the size and record definition of
one parameter is contingent on the value of another parameter. There are
also many defined structures (i.e., "01 level records") where a structure
"size in bytes"  is included in the record so you can tell which version of
the structure is in use.
```

#### ↳ Re: Extract COBOL records in C

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik-nws@siber.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T01:51:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF9F461.3C4C4E3@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<9cpmus$1oa$1@news.orst.edu>`

```
You can do one of two things:

- Get DataAccess library from us and use it
It does just what you want -- it presents Cobol data files in a way
that can be read into a C++ prpgram. OK, C++ is not the sam eas C.
But it is close enough, you (or we) can write a translation layer
that would accomodate C++ to C.

- Convert your Cobol data files to CSV or DBF or other modern format.
You can do it using our tools Data2Flat, Data2Dbf.
Then you can read the converted files into the C program.

More details on both options at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Some background: Cobol files are hard to read if you are not in Cobol,
very hard.
If you are to do it yourself, it will take like a year and you still may
fail.
Cobol data files are often compressed and are otherwise made hard to
read
by vendors. 

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
Siber Systems


Jeff D. Hamann wrote:
>

…[40 more quoted lines elided]…

- **From:** "Charles" <charles@godwin.ca>

```
CA-Realia COBOL publishes the format of most of it's file types and provides
a documented C callable FileSystem API for reading, writing and creating
these files. CA-We also publish the internal format of COBOL type numeric
fields. We even provide as freeware a file copy utility to convert files
from one format to another. CA is NOT a company that makes COBOL data hard
to read.

Charles Godwin
Development Masager - Realia
Computer Associates International, Inc.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Extract COBOL records in C

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T13:22:12+12:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3afb4260_6@Usenet.com>`

```
Vadim,

This is a public forum and it is irritating to see advertising unless it is
really pertinent and describes a genuine solution to a problem.

Personally, I have no problem with people "plugging" a product as long as it
is pertinent. (Others here may have much less tolerance...)

So, describe what your product does and leave it at that.

Especially, don't pad the message with vacuous drivel... The people in this
forum for the most part are professional technicians and get enough
Marketing bullshit at their workplaces without having it here.

Comments below.


Vadim Maslov wrote in message <3AF9F461.3C4C4E3@siber.com>...
>You can do one of two things:

[translation - one of these two options will suit US very nicely...]
>
>- Get DataAccess library from us and use it
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Or you could use a competitor's products, or talk to your own vendor (CA I
think it was..) about what software they offer to do it, or any other option
limited only by your own imagination and diligence.


>More details on both options at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/
>

[totally unecessary "Bullshit Section"]


>Some background: Cobol files are hard to read if you are not in Cobol,
>very hard.
COBOL vendors provide utilities to read their file systems and MOST publish
the data formats so they can be read by byte stream IO. The formats are
usually a matter of record.

>If you are to do it yourself, it will take like a year and you still may
>fail.
Well, I don't know about you, but it has never taken me a year to read a
"foreign" COBOL file, even when I HAVEN'T had the vendor's format
description (particularly if I know what data is on the file). As for
failure, there is always a risk of that...even using either of the options
you offered.

>Cobol data files are often compressed and are otherwise made hard to
>read
>by vendors.
Yes, it is common practice to compress ISAM files. This is not to make them
hard to read, it is to save space and it is an OPTION. Given that you take
this option, you will need a decompression function from the vendor. Are you
saying that the software you are recommending is aware of EVERY
decompression provided by EVERY vendor? (If so, that is a valid point which
should have been made outside the "Bullshit Section" of this message.)

[End Bullshit Section]

I don't doubt that the software from Siber Systems is very good. (people
don't stay in business very long if their product ISN'T any good).

My issue is not with the product, it is with the way it has been presented
here.

Pete.

>
>Regards,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I have a collection of COBOL files (I'm not sure if that's the correct
term
>> as I'm a C programmer) I would like to be able to "export" or translate
into
>> a standard database package and would like to know if there are packages
out
>> there that will help "reverse engineer" COBOL files. I can get the file
>> formats like
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>> This is the song that never ends...

Vadim Maslov wrote in message <3AFCA85D.CD8FE5B@siber.com>...
>What is so irritating? Proposing a solution to a problem and
>solution is not free? Yeah, we are nasty little bastards,
>we dare to sell our software for money.

No one, least of all me, is questioning your right to sell software for
money. As for being a nasty little bastard...your words, not mine.

It is a question of presenting the capabiilities of your product fairly,
without Marketing hype or trying to put the "frighteners" on people in an
effort to get them to buy your software.

Say what it does, where it can be obtained, and, ideally, what it costs.

Your suggestion that it would take years to do it without your software, and
that COBOL files are generally difficult, is blatantly untrue. And
unnecessary.

It might work with people coming in to your showroom who don't know any
better, but this is a professional forum with  a fair sprinkling of highly
experienced people whose intelligence you insult with this kind of rubbish.

Many of us have done conversions at some point. It didn't take years and we
used tools which are freely available or wrote them if they weren't.

That is not to say there is no place for the software you are offering.
Offer it fairly and let it stand on its own merit. Or do you have a problem
with that?

>
>So why don't you propose a free solution to this guy,
>to close the matter, so to say?
>
I am happy to point out that the two alternatives you offered are not the
ONLY ones...It would have been much better if YOU had done so. Whether he
finally opts for free software or assistance, or buys software such as your
own is none of my business and I really don't care.

My only issue is with the unethical way in which you presented your case...
(but I thought I said that already...)

>Or, if we look at it from other prospective, is it true
>that this forum's sole purpose is to discusss whether
>Cobol is dead or when it will be dead?
>
>Just curious.

Look at it from any perspective you like, discuss anything you like, but if
you are here to sell product, keep it honest.

Pete.

- **Date:** 2001-05-14T05:25:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFF6CA4.7816FAE7@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<9cpmus$1oa$1@news.orst.edu> <3AF9F461.3C4C4E3@siber.com> <3afb4260_6@Usenet.com> <3AFCA85D.CD8FE5B@siber.com> <3afd2e4d_3@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> Vadim Maslov wrote in message <3AFCA85D.CD8FE5B@siber.com>...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Say what it does, where it can be obtained, and, ideally, what it costs.

This is all stated in URL that was provided.
Giving the entire price list in a posting is a waste of space.
Explaining why people would use the product is not a waste of space.

> 
> Your suggestion that it would take years to do it without your software, and
> that COBOL files are generally difficult, is blatantly untrue. And
> unnecessary.

My post said "like a year" which means "close to a year".
This is more or less true if you want to read compressed
Cobol data file in which non-display field types are used.
Also consider the fact that Cobol compiler vendors do not release
formats of their data files as a rule (Realia appears to be
an exception, but we do not have a Data Reader for Realia
(maybe for this very reason that they made their format public)).

So speaking about "blatantly untrue", you statement about my
statement is way more "blatantly untrue". My statement had some 
marketing in it, but it is not baseless, because I have seen 
Cobol compilers and Cobol file systems and I know them inside. 
Data Readers took more than a year to develop for us,
we started from the user perspective (no isider knowledge), 
and we "are knowledgeable in the art".


> 
> It might work with people coming in to your showroom who don't know any
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> with that?

It does stand on its own merit. 
If you converted 80-byte fixed records with DISPLAY data,
it indeed does not take much, no onversion is needed at all. 
But if you had to extract data from a compressed PC Cobol data file 
without having a copybook for it and without knowing what Cobol
compiler produced it, it might take a long time, 
to the point of not being economically feasible for a user.
We make it economically feasible.


> 
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> own is none of my business and I really don't care.

Well, did I say that my solution is the only one? No.
And if you indeed cared about "fairness" and what not, 
you would have descirbed competing solutions and 
you would have explained why they are better and cheaper than ours.


Vadim Maslov
```

---

## Thread: INVOKE IN COBOL — 5 msgs

### INVOKE IN COBOL

- **From:** "Raimundo Carvalho" <ray@mail.telepac.pt>
- **Date:** 2001-05-02T22:00:07+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cpsfr$5vg$1@venus.telepac.pt>`

```
Does somebody know where i can look for books or articles about the
instruction "Invoke" clalling class of Excel or Word with the goal of using
the Excel or Word to make list to the printer for example.

Thank you

Raimundo Carvalho
```

#### ↳ Re: INVOKE IN COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T01:06:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF0AF37.E14B6CD8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9cpsfr$5vg$1@venus.telepac.pt>`

```


Raimundo Carvalho wrote:

> Does somebody know where i can look for books or articles about the
> instruction "Invoke" clalling class of Excel or Word with the goal of using
> the Excel or Word to make list to the printer for example.
>

Although the word INVOKE is used loosely in this NewsGroup by some, implying
'calling',  INVOKE is one of the new RESERVED WORDS associated with OO for :-

    invoking classes
    invoking methods

PC-wise only currently available with Fujitsu and Merant (Net Express) OO
compilers, where the OO syntax is currently 'an extension' to COBOL prior to
COBOL 2002 being released.

Meanwhile, depending upon your PC compiler you can 'call' Excel or Word using
Windows API calls if your compiler has them, or even using COBOL 'support'
products such as Flexus SP2 or Norcom Screen IO.

Check out cobolreport.com, "Gene Webb's OO Tutorial"..

Jimmy
```

## Thread: Eliminating Evidence — 24 msgs

### Eliminating Evidence

- **From:** "bloggs" <bloggs@neil-1234.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T01:13:11+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cq7tl$qrg$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi,
    This subject was discussed a couple of days ago here but I missed it.
Heres my tuppence worth. You can download, for free, a copy of PGP. Mine is
the version 6.0.2ckt build 7.0 which is for non-commercial use, anyway this
package includes a "free space wipe" utility which writes random data to any
space freed up by deleting files. Another utility with the package is "file
wipe" which does a similar job. You can also define how many "passes" are to
be performed during the wipe. And this is for free.

Bloggs.
```

```
bloggs wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> be performed during the wipe. And this is for free.

The problem for those with guilty consciences is not just data left in clusters which 
are supposed to be empty.  Windows keeps fairly extensive hidden "audit trails" of where 
you've been, what you've done and who you did it with.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Eliminating Evidence

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T14:30:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<L1zI6.115914$vg5.6308735@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9cq7tl$qrg$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AF0E190.4CB7@paralynx.com> <9csml3$32q$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AF1ECA3.7CDB@paralynx.com>`

```

"Ed Guy" <ed_guy@paralynx.com> wrote in message
news:3AF1ECA3.7CDB@paralynx.com...
> Reformat it. (Or buy Evidence Eliminator <g>).

Reformatting won't do it. There exist Un-Format utilities. Conversely,
there also exist "Wipe" utilities (Norton has one, for example).

Regarding PGP or other encryption routines (some are quite handy in
that they encrypt/decrypt on the fly without any user intervention),
there is still a downside:

The government can compel you to provide the password. If law
enforcement has a valid warrant, courts have held that passwords or
encryption keys are equivalent to the combination of a safe and the
owner may be compelled to provide access. Remember, if you are ever in
a disupte with the government (criminal or civil), your records will
convict you irrespective of whether you're guilty. The only safe
technique is to put someone else's name on the data.

Of course if it's a question of sitting in jail for contempt or
divulging the planning documents for bombing the World Trade Center,
the choice of whether to provide the key may be clear.

Further, if the drive is broken, you can't 'wipe' it or reformat it or
anything else. The FBI can, though. Your only choice is to physically
destroy the drive.


>
> If I junk one of mine I'll just put a hammer through the drive.
>

I shoot 'em.
```

```
The statement 'your records will convict you...' came from the 1960's
book "Up The Organization" by Peter Townsend (president of Avis).

Today (5/4/01) CNN's "Burden Of Proof" program detailed the 30 yr
incarceration of an innocent person, put in prison on the word of a
single FBI snitch. The snitch tried to recant his testimony but the
FBI kept his recantation secret and even forced him to fire his
attorney, F. Lee Bailey.

And your 'more faith in our system...' belief may be the 'Triumph of
Hope over Reason." I hope things are as good as you trust they are.
Still, it doesn't hurt to be skeptical.

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:0bAI6.136$sL4.149577@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net...

1960's
> >book "Up The Organization" by Peter Townsend.
>
> A delightful reference... but how might it be supported?

Buy the book.

Under Townsend's presidency of Avis (he came from American Express),
Avis did such marvelous things as developing the slogan "We Try
Harder" and mandating that every employee spend one day a year at the
counter renting cars.


>
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> The 'word of a single FBI snitch... (who) tried to recant his
testimony'
> is not the records of the one convicted, is it?

 X (Joseph Barbosa) snitched out Y (Joseph Salvati). In return X got a
lesser sentence on unrelated crimes. Y got life for murder. X later
found out that perjured testimony in a murder case gets an automatic
life sentence for the perjurer. X, believing his false testimony will
ultimately be discovered,  hires F. Lee Bailey to handle the
recantation. X signs affidavit that X lied in the trial of Y.

Bailey gives affidavit to trial judge and U.S. Attorney. FBI visits X
and tells X to recant his recantation and fire Bailey.

Full transcript at:
http://www.cnn.com/TRANSCRIPTS/0105/04/bp.00.html


>
> But, such tedia aside, cut to the chase: what level of perfection is
to be
> expected from a system designed and implemented by humans?  I agree
that
> yes, cases are influenced by politics, evidence is planted, judges
fall
> asleep and suchlike... humans beings can be honest and human beings
can
> cheat.  This is news?

Which is precisely why, until all human behavior can be quantified and
analyzed by a computer, one should be circumspect with one's stuff.
```

the question of 'which provides superior analysis of human activities...
another human, which is all-too-human, or a computer, which is anything
*but* human?')

DD
```

There's also anecdotal evidence of Things Going Right, too... folks who
commit crimes get caught, tried fairly (notice that a 'fair' trial is
guaranteed, not a 'perfect' one) and punished appropriately... but that's
'dog bites man'.

Even though I was taught - dear me, in fifth grade or so! - that 'anyone
can accuse anybody of anything so an accusation is meaningless'... just
imagine what happens if a teenager points at you and says 'He's the
father!'... even *after* tests come back negating the assertion.  One's
own records are not needed at all, no.
