```
Jerry P <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:8YSI6.122048$vg5.6820892@news5.aus1.giganews.com...
> Under Townsend's presidency of Avis (he came from American Express),
> Avis did such marvelous things as developing the slogan "We Try
> Harder" and mandating that every employee spend one day a year at the
> counter renting cars.
>

IIRC (it's been about fifteen years since I read the book, "Up The
Organization"):

"We Try Harder" was a byproduct of a 'failed' attempt to spice up Avis'
marketing. Townsend went to a new ad agency, looking for a 'hook.' After
much research, the ad agency came back with, "Sorry. All we have really been
able to learn about your [Avis'] position in the market is that you are #2,
but you try harder."

The rest, as they say, is history........

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Eliminating Evidence

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson_2@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T02:15:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF4B338.9E308C7A@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<9cq7tl$qrg$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AF0E190.4CB7@paralynx.com> <9csml3$32q$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AF1ECA3.7CDB@paralynx.com> <L1zI6.115914$vg5.6308735@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
> 
> "Ed Guy" <ed_guy@paralynx.com> wrote in message
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> there also exist "Wipe" utilities (Norton has one, for example).
<snip>

Unless you know something I don't, a FORMAT will get rid of what
is on the drive. A "QUICK" format will not. But, if in doubt, do
a low-level format. For one of our 8GB SCSI drives start the
low-level and go to lunch (it might be done by the time you get
back). If you can find anything on that drive after that,
Evidence Eliminator wouldn't have helped, nor would the hammer
trick. BTW, I know of a certain Gov't entity that not only uses a
very high gauss generator, but crushes their disk drives
afterward. But then, they are also the ones that have the
equipment to do "magnetic domain" level reading of a disk drive.
```

```
"Steve Thompson" <sthompson_2@neo.rr.com

>
> Unless you know something I don't, a FORMAT will get rid of what
> is on the drive. A "QUICK" format will not.

see http://www.computerhope.com/unformat.htm

>But, if in doubt, do
> a low-level format. For one of our 8GB SCSI drives start the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> trick.

Not sure how many times a low-level format writes to a particular spot
on the drive, but if a low-level format only writes once or twice, I
betcha there's techniques for recovering the 'ghost' recordings
underneath the current data. "Wipe" utilities, so I'm told,
write-write-write etc. with several different bit patterns.

> BTW, I know of a certain Gov't entity that not only uses a
> very high gauss generator, but crushes their disk drives
> afterward. But then, they are also the ones that have the
> equipment to do "magnetic domain" level reading of a disk drive.
>

You know, this might be a valuable service to offer: A bonded,
insured, yak-yak service (maybe a division of Wells Fargo) that will
pick up your drive and squash it to about the size of a B-B. Then they
return it to you so you can verify the drive's destruction. I've heard
of companies that do bulk shredding, so maybe there's a market.
```

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca
>
> My lawyer was concerned about data on his disk drive when scrapping
it.  I
> told hime to remove it, hit it hard with a 12 pound sledge, then put
it in
> the landfill.  I suspect it works much better than some bullshit
software,
> and certainly cheaper than a bonded service.

Oh, I agree. Same with shredding old documents. But it depends on whom
you talk to. Owners are concerned with cost; subordinates are
concerned with minimization of risk/grief.

In my experience, the person who is RESPONSIBLE for the destruction is
almost never the one that actually DOES it. Since the person whose ass
is on the line doesn't care how much the destruction process costs, he
might be disposed to hire an outside agency.

I mean, can you imagine a law firm's office manager telling the hippie
in the mail room to destroy some (potentially valuable) equipment?

Me, I'll shoot the sucker (the drive, not the hippie -- although, come
to think on it...).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: table help needed for homework

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T14:12:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cshil$a32$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1V2F6.316$231.163449@wagner.videotron.net> <3AE5FD47.22D59A0C@home.com> <3ae6dda2_3@my.newsfeeds.com> <9cqkd7$js1$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3AF1B969.F7D9F585@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Can't speak to Merant, but can speak to the old Unisys A Series COBOL(68)
(only recently retired) which allowed OCCURS at Level 01 ...

The purpose here was to specify a "true" two-dimensional array  -- an array
of descriptors (associated with the first subscript) pointing to individual
memory spaces, instead of a single descriptor pointing to a single memory
space as for "normal" 01-level records.

Everything was pretty much OK if the descriptors pointed to arrays that
themselves had OCCURS clauses, but results got very strange when the only
OCCURS in the record was at the 01 level, particularly when there was no
subordinate elementary item with a PICTURE clause.

Ultimately (in 1989), the COBOL(68) compiler was modified to issue a syntax
error if a two-dimensional array was declared with fewer than two dimensions
...

Subsequent COBOL compilers for the A Series (COBOL74, COBOL85) eschewed this
particular extension as not providing enough benefit to outweigh the risks,
pitfalls and "gotchas" inherent in the design.

Best I can tell the standards have consistently disallowed OCCURS at Level
01.

        -Chuck Stevens

Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
news:3AF1B969.F7D9F585@melbpc.org.au...
> Bill,
> Can you give some examples of the odd semantics - I have followed
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Micro Focus (MERANT) has allowed OCCURS at an 01-level in
Working-Storage
> > for "years and years" - as an extension (obviously).  It causes some
REALLY
> > "odd" semantics.
> >
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T16:41:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9csja1$i1$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1V2F6.316$231.163449@wagner.videotron.net> <3AE5FD47.22D59A0C@home.com> <3ae6dda2_3@my.newsfeeds.com> <9cqkd7$js1$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3AF1B969.F7D9F585@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Hopefully one of the actual MERANT (or former Micro Focus) people will "jump
in".

I remember problems with:

  REDEFINES
  READ INTO / WRITE FROM
  SEARCH
  other "statements" that require you to reference an "unqualified"
data-item (no subscripting)

I can TRY and dig out more information if you really need/want it - but
generally, I think that Chuck's comment about "more trouble than it was
worth" is what I seem to recall about MF's implementation.
```

```
Oh, don't worry.  If it is prime in Cobol, it will be prime in C++ as well.
```

#### ↳ Re: Prime number program

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T14:10:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xFdI6.21220$sP6.1074358@news3.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
MOVE 'Y' TO ITS-PRIME.
COMPUTE HALF-TEST = TESTNUM / 2.
PERFORM VARYING NUM FROM 3 BY 2 UNTIL NUM > HALF-TEST
     IF MOD (TESTNUM NUM)  = 0
        MOVE 'N' TO ITS-PRIME
        END-IF
   END-PERFORM.

Notice the clever optimization techniques: Starting at 3 and
incrementing by 2 for example.

"Archimedes" <brenjoe@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
> Can anyone help me in writing a program that can determine whether a
number
> is prime in Cobol?
>
>
```

```
     IF MOD (TESTNUM NUM)  = 0
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Prime number program

- **From:** Frank Ranner <franner@tpg.com.au>
- **Date:** 2001-05-05T00:44:28+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF2C04C.4455D747@tpg.com.au>`
- **References:** `<476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <xFdI6.21220$sP6.1074358@news3.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
> 
> MOVE 'Y' TO ITS-PRIME.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Hmm, you are testing up to the halfway point, which is far more than
necessary. 
You only need to try numbers up to the square root of testnum. If there
is a factor
greater than the square root, the other factor has to be smaller, which
means you 
have already tested it.

Regards, Frank Ranner
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Prime number program

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James" <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T22:31:55-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_AJI6.9365$R2.7420801@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```
Actually since the definition of a PRIME number is: (my rewording)
any number that can ONLY be divided by itself and one without
yielding a remainder (and Fractions (other than those equal to 1/1)
are EXCLUDED from consideration, then if you know the
Square Root and it is a WHOLE Number, then you KNOW it
is NOT PRIME.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Prime number program

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-05T12:44:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_ASI6.121660$vg5.6810787@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <xFdI6.21220$sP6.1074358@news3.aus1.giganews.com> <3AF2C04C.4455D747@tpg.com.au> <X3HI6.71294$8j.8686760@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <_AJI6.9365$R2.7420801@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```

"James" <mangogrower@telocity.com> wrote in message
news:_AJI6.9365$R2.7420801@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net...
> Actually since the definition of a PRIME number is: (my rewording)
> any number that can ONLY be divided by itself and one without
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, but COBOL allows ANY number to be divided by another (non-zero)
number without a remainder. You don't get a remainder without the
REMAINDER clause. No REMAINDER clause, no remainder. No remainder =
Prime.

As for the square-root test, well that test fails on irrational,
transcendental, imaginary, Imperial, and negative numbers. Doesn't
matter whether you use the REMAINDER clause. No, best forget about
root-tests.

And nobody can tell me that 10 (Bo Derek) isn't prime.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Prime number program

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Anonymous <anonymous@anonymous.anonymous>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T04:01:29+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3af42338_2@anonymous>`
- **References:** `<476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <xFdI6.21220$sP6.1074358@news3.aus1.giganews.com> <3AF2C04C.4455D747@tpg.com.au> <X3HI6.71294$8j.8686760@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <_AJI6.9365$R2.7420801@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <_ASI6.121660$vg5.6810787@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
This is the best entertainment I've seen in CLC for years...ROFL!

1.A man posts some code that is decidedly questionable and says  we should
note what a clever example it is...

2. The first problem with it is gently pointed out by Richard Plinston.

3. Code is hastily fixed to accommodate noted exception.

4. Kiwi gently points out that it is wrong in second case...(a logical
progression from the first case, indicating that the solution was not
thought through)

5. Code is hastily fixed again...

6. Person of Nationality unknown points out that "exemplary code" is largely
redundant...

7. Man who wrote it blames COBOL's division facility as, unless a remainder
is requested, there isn't one and this indicates the number is prime. Now
most people would accept that in cases where you NEED a remainder, it is
incumbent on the PROGRAMMER to get one.

Blaming the Language for not doing it, is like blaming the sidewalk for
grazing your knee... You knew the concrete was rough before you decided to
run along it...<G>

You're probably wondering why I find this all so amusing (in the nicest
possible way...<G>)

Well the same Guy who posted the code (and didn't even bother to compile and
run it...twice...!!)  has had a go at me on a previous occasion... here are
a couple of quotes:

"This is either a poor commentary on the educational processes in New
Zealand or, more likely, insufficient knowledge of physics."

(We really must have a discussion on Quantum Chromodynamics, Kaluza-Klein,
Yang-Mills, and Superstring Theory sometime, Jerry. If your Physics is like
your programming it should be a real entertainment...<G> And I certainly
don't need to remind you that I am not the only Kiwi who posts here...)

"Programmer's should have a better handle on logic."

(Or at least test their code before going public with it...<LOL> I was
really wounded by this one but I feel MUCH better now...<G>)

<Tee hee>

While I realise it is very unworthy of me to be gleeful in these
circumstances, I can't help it...this is really.....well, "prime" just about
says it all...<LOL>

OLD CHINESE PROVERB: "Keep your words soft and sweet, for you may have to
eat them..."

Pete  Dashwood
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Prime number program

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T09:16:42+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF5086A.598E45FD@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <xFdI6.21220$sP6.1074358@news3.aus1.giganews.com> <3AF2C04C.4455D747@tpg.com.au> <X3HI6.71294$8j.8686760@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <_AJI6.9365$R2.7420801@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <_ASI6.121660$vg5.6810787@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
> 
> Yes, but COBOL allows ANY number to be divided by another (non-zero)
> number without a remainder. You don't get a remainder without the
> REMAINDER clause. No REMAINDER clause, no remainder. No remainder =
> Prime.

The DIVIDE does result in a remainder regardless of the REMAINDER
clause, without the clause you simply choose to ignore the remainder. 
Ignore remainder = ignore whether it is prime or not.

> As for the square-root test, well that test fails on irrational,
> transcendental, imaginary, Imperial, and negative numbers. Doesn't
> matter whether you use the REMAINDER clause. No, best forget about
> root-tests.

Can 'irrational, transcendental, imaginary, Imperial, [or] negative
numbers' _be_ prime ?

But in any case the 'square-root' is not a test for being prime it is
setting a limit for the test.  Your code will test for divisors up to
n/2.  This is completely unnecsessary (and is nowhere near optimal as
you claim).  it is only necessary to test for divisors up to the square
root of the number being tested.

For example 3 x 17 is 51.  There is no need to find that 51 can be
divided by 17 by testing divisors up to 25 because the test would have
failed the divide by 3.  It is only necessary to test up to 7.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Prime number program

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T00:19:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zM0J6.82307$8j.9744966@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <xFdI6.21220$sP6.1074358@news3.aus1.giganews.com> <3AF2C04C.4455D747@tpg.com.au> <X3HI6.71294$8j.8686760@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <_AJI6.9365$R2.7420801@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <_ASI6.121660$vg5.6810787@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3AF5086A.598E45FD@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
OK. I guess it's time to apologize. Sorry friends.

Part of my thesis in mathematics involved prime numbers and their
computation (not much, admittedly, but some).

In trying to hone my finely tuned sense of humor to spoof a request
for The Sieve of Eratosthenes in COBOL, I apparently missed the mark.

I was too clever by half. I'll practice on the cat until I'm ready for
prime time (no pun intended).



"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3AF5086A.598E45FD@Azonic.co.nz...
> Jerry P wrote:
> >
> > Yes, but COBOL allows ANY number to be divided by another
(non-zero)
> > number without a remainder. You don't get a remainder without the
> > REMAINDER clause. No REMAINDER clause, no remainder. No remainder
=
> > Prime.
>
> The DIVIDE does result in a remainder regardless of the REMAINDER
> clause, without the clause you simply choose to ignore the
remainder.
> Ignore remainder = ignore whether it is prime or not.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> But in any case the 'square-root' is not a test for being prime it
is
> setting a limit for the test.  Your code will test for divisors up
to
> n/2.  This is completely unnecsessary (and is nowhere near optimal
as
> you claim).  it is only necessary to test for divisors up to the
square
> root of the number being tested.
>
> For example 3 x 17 is 51.  There is no need to find that 51 can be
> divided by 17 by testing divisors up to 25 because the test would
have
> failed the divide by 3.  It is only necessary to test up to 7.
```

```
One thing we illustrated is that the person writing this program needs to
start off with a test plan.

Make the program callable, and create a program to call it, looping
through test data, as a start.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Prime number program

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T15:50:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d4deu$sld$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <xFdI6.21220$sP6.1074358@news3.aus1.giganews.com> <3AF2C04C.4455D747@tpg.com.au> <X3HI6.71294$8j.8686760@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <_AJI6.9365$R2.7420801@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <_ASI6.121660$vg5.6810787@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3AF5086A.598E45FD@Azonic.co.nz> <3af4c2c1_6@Usenet.com>`

```
I have not (and am not) going to try to "write" a program to do this, but
this SEEMS to me to indicate that ONE (sort-of-interesting) way to solve the
question is to use several "intrinsic functions".  Does the following
pseudo-code seem like it would work to determine if a specific number is a
prime or not?

Get Number-to-test
Compute highest-number-to-test = the INTEGER-PART of SQRT of Number-to-Test
Perform varying test-number from 2 by 1
  until  Integer-Factor-Found
      or test-number > highest-number-to-test
          With Test before   *>  this makes certain that 1 and 2 will show
up as primes
        If MOD (number-to-test test-number) = 0
            Set Integer-Factor-Found
        End-If
End-Perform
If Integer-Factor-Found
    Then its NOT a primer
Else
   It is a prime
End-If
```

```
number for prime using a formula, several years back.  While it could not
factor the number, it could test it for prime.

I searched for it when this thread came up, and could not find it.  The
algorithm was documented in an article on producing 150 digit primes for
public/private key encryption.  (Start at a random 150 digit number, and
test forward).  It obviously did not use factoring like the one starting
this thread.

Anyone in the NG familiar with it?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Prime number program

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-05-07T10:05:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jUxJ6.30$h%.25939@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <xFdI6.21220$sP6.1074358@news3.aus1.giganews.com> <3AF2C04C.4455D747@tpg.com.au> <X3HI6.71294$8j.8686760@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
"Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:X3HI6.71294$8j.8686760@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...

> Actually, for purposes of the original poster's question, going only
> half-way is probably not the best idea. Check everything, including
> even numbers. Maybe even fractions.

I do not get this.  The original post was:

"Can anyone help me in writing a program that can determine whether a
number is prime in Cobol?".

Even numbers, once you have checked for two, would be completely redundant.
Fractions would be completely irrelevant, as they have nothing to do with
primes.  Prime numbers are integers, by definition.

If you are going to use factoring as the method (IE divison and check for
remainder), then the only numbers that you need test against are other
primes.  Once you have checked for 2, you do not need to check for multiples
of two.  Once you have checked for three, then you can ignore multiples of
three, etc.  The fact that the low order digit is even eliminates half of
all tests, and is trivial. Similarly, numbers ending in 5 (base ten) cannot
be prime.  You do not need division to check.

The most efficient factoring algorithm builds a table of known primes, and
uses them to test with.

What you say above makes no sense, and could result in a program running for
practically infinite lengths of time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Prime number program

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-09T15:46:53+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3af8c28a_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <xFdI6.21220$sP6.1074358@news3.aus1.giganews.com> <3AF2C04C.4455D747@tpg.com.au> <X3HI6.71294$8j.8686760@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <jUxJ6.30$h%.25939@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Don, (and possibly, Howard, who asked if anyone had seen an algorithm which
didn't use division),

I am not a mathematician but I believe the "shortcuts" you are alluding to
(like checking the last digit) were formalised into a "system" of "quick"
mathematics by a Nazi concentration camp victim named Tractenberg.

Apparently while Tractenberg was incarcerated in one of the Death Camps he
managed to keep himself sane by occupying his mind in searching for better
ways to do simple arithmetic.

The result was the "Tractenburg System of Speed Mathematics" which was
published back in the 50s.  (So either he or his system, hopefully, both,
must have survived...) I have never studied the system, but I remember
browsing through a copy of it at a Jumble Sale in the 70s...sadly, I didn't
buy it. I haven't done a Web Search but it is highly likely that there is
some record of it somewhere....(anyone here know of it?)

I know that factoring was a major part of it and it was possible to tell
certain factors of any given number, simply by looking at it. I would be
surprised if it couldn't be programmed into an algorithm for detecting
primes.

Sadly (in a way), the advent of pocket calculators rendered the Tractenberg
system virtually obselete. (I say "in a way" because there is no way I would
trade a calculator for "any" system of arithmetic, but it is a bit sad that
a very clever system, developed under such circumstances, should be
relegated to the scrapheap by the march of technology...)

For those here who ARE mathematically inclined, it might be worth doing a
bit of checking and maybe coming up with a completely different algorithm?

Pete.
```

```
>Don, (and possibly, Howard, who asked if anyone had seen an algorithm which

Both.

A Google search on Trachtenberg turns up the following, among others:
http://www.geocities.com/CapeCanaveral/Hangar/8802/books/trach.html
http://www.mypage.onemain.com/jimloy/arith/trachten.htm
http://hucellbiol.mdc-berlin.de/%7Emp01mg/oldweb/1mutrach.htm

I regard this method as an interesting curiosity, but not particularly useful 
for performing actual computations. Speaking for myself, anyway, it seems to 
be much faster to simply perform the computations directly than to have to 
memorize all the different rules required to use the Trachtenberg system.
```

#### ↳ Re: Prime number program

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T20:06:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cv241$fd7$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <476I6.2954$4f7.196656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
Archimedes <brenjoe@worldnet.att.net> writes:
>Can anyone help me in writing a program that can determine whether a number
>is prime in Cobol?
>
>
Basically if a number is not divisibly by 2 through number -1 then it is
prime.
if you use the modulus you get the remainder.  If the reamainder is zero it is
not a prime number.

You have to do this with loops.  This could take forever if you dont stop
incrementing the number at a reasonable number.
or just use a counter and stop after say 10000.
If you can use boolean variable with your compiler that might make it easy.

you just increment the test number till you get to the point when they are
equal.
Then increment the number being tested until it is about sAY 10000.
if you dont stop the loop it could take forever.

you could accept the time and keep checking the time and see if a certain
amount of time has elapsed and make it run for say 10 hours at night, 10
minutes or until you get too many records.  The higher you go, the longer it
takes to find another prime number.  You have to know what the highest number
you can store in a numeric value is and dont exceed it or your program could
abend CALCULATE has an overflow switch I think.
```

```
Excel 9 on our web site.

First.....go here:

Next.....download the Excel file.

site.  His e-mail address is on the web page.

trouble....I'll be happy to assist you in getting it into a different
format (RTF or whatever).



Bob Wolfe, flexus

Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-ec84847e40"></a>
## Thread: Function Mod philosophy — 9 msgs

### Function Mod philosophy

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T09:36:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF17B01.56126D03@brazee.net>`

```
The only thing I have ever used FUNCTION MOD is to check to see whether
or not it is zero.

The following are examples of other values (from an IBM on-line manual):

The returned value is argument-1 modulo argument-2. The returned value
is defined as:

argument-1 - (argument-2 * FUNCTION INTEGER (argument-1 / argument-2))

The following table illustrates the expected results for some values of
argument-1 and argument-2.

    ________________________ _________________________
________________________
   | Argument-1             | Argument-2              |
Return                 |

|________________________|_________________________|________________________|

   |  11                    |   5                     |
1                    |

|________________________|_________________________|________________________|

   | -11                    |   5                     |
4                    |

|________________________|_________________________|________________________|

   |  11                    |  -5                     |
-4                    |

|________________________|_________________________|________________________|

   | -11                    |  -5                     |
-1                    |

|________________________|_________________________|________________________|

Why would someone want a return value of 4 or -4 above?   They had to
design that function to work that way for a purpose, I just don't know
what that purpose is.  (maybe to copy an old math function which isn't
useful for business needs).
```

#### ↳ Re: Function Mod philosophy

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-03T18:12:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9csolk$6cf$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<3AF17B01.56126D03@brazee.net>`

```
I use it to find the day-of-the-week:

05  yyyymmdd           pic 9(8).
05  day-of-the-week    pic 9.

    move function current-date (1:8) to yyyymmdd.
    compute day-of-the-week =
      function mod (function integer-of-date (yyyymmdd), 7)

    result is 0=sun 1=mon 2=tue etc.
    I usually add 1 to the result so I can use it as subscript to
    get the day-name out of a table for printing.

Ron.
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

```
Check digits attached to every number used to address a unique item
(Driver's license, Visa cards, Parts, Books & CDs, everything- well,
everything except your Social Security Number).

For example:

Partnumber => (some process) => "X" => MOD ("X" 10) => "Y"

NewPartNumber = PartNumberY

Interestingly, the check digit for books is modulo-11
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Function Mod philosophy

- **From:** stringer@es.co.nz (stringer)
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T05:43:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ctfha$6hu$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3AF17B01.56126D03@brazee.net> <y8nI6.60272$8j.7866533@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
In article <y8nI6.60272$8j.7866533@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>, 
jerryp@bisusa.com says...
>
>Check digits attached to every number used to address a unique item
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have a trust account progamme in which my clients are given numbers which are 
modulo 11. It was originally programmed in Softbol, a variant of DEC's CoBOL.

The idea is that when you enter a number, it does a modulo 11 validation check, 
and if you've miskeyed the number, it should (!) get rejected.

This is why they are used for IRD (IRS) numbers etc. I believe some 'mission 
critical' applications may even use both modulo 10 and modulo 11 checks.

I found a lot of info from the IBM manuals, as all I had at first was the data, 
and had to work out the record formats etc, but thanfully the users manual gave 
some hints and referred to modulo 11 numbers.

Using MOD this self check digit system is easy to implement.

David Stringer
(The lawyer)
stringer@es.co.nz
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Function Mod philosophy

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T14:12:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KNyI6.66003$8j.8306372@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3AF17B01.56126D03@brazee.net> <y8nI6.60272$8j.7866533@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9ctfha$6hu$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`

```

"stringer" <stringer@es.co.nz

> I have a trust account progamme in which my clients are given
numbers which are
> modulo 11. It was originally programmed in Softbol, a variant of
DEC's CoBOL.
>
> The idea is that when you enter a number, it does a modulo 11
validation check,
> and if you've miskeyed the number, it should (!) get rejected.
>
> This is why they are used for IRD (IRS) numbers etc. I believe some
'mission
> critical' applications may even use both modulo 10 and modulo 11
checks.
>
> I found a lot of info from the IBM manuals, as all I had at first
was the data,
> and had to work out the record formats etc, but thanfully the users
manual gave
> some hints and referred to modulo 11 numbers.
>
> Using MOD this self check digit system is easy to implement.

There has been a lot of mathematical analysis of check-digit schemes
in recent decades in an attempt to discover the best formula for
detecting common transcription errors. The most common errors are, of
course, mistaking one number for another ("7234" instead of "1234")
and transposition errors ("1324" instead of "1234").

I believe one of the most common techniques is (abcd... are digits in
the number):

CheckDigit = MOD { (Sum [a + 3xb + c + 3xd...]) 10}

That is,
Sum the odd-numbered digits
Sum the even-numbered digits and multiply that sum by 3
Add the two sums
Take the modulo-10 of the total of the two sums
Attach the result to the end of the original number.

i.e.:
1234 = 1 + 2x3 + 3 + 4x3 = 22
Mod (22 10) = 2
New number = 12342

Test:
7234 check digit is 0 which differs from 2  ***FAILURE***

1324 check digit is 4 which differs from 2  ***FAILURE***

Modulo-11 may catch more errors than modulo-10 -BUT- Modulo-11 creates
another problem. The Modulo-11 answer may sometimes be TEN (10) (in
the case of books, represented by "X" ) which, in turn, complicates
data entry, barcodes, and all that sort of machine stuff.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Function Mod philosophy

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T16:53:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF2DEBF.239E3A78@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AF17B01.56126D03@brazee.net> <y8nI6.60272$8j.7866533@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9ctfha$6hu$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <KNyI6.66003$8j.8306372@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```


Jerry P wrote:

>
> Modulo-11 may catch more errors than modulo-10 -BUT- Modulo-11 creates
> another problem. The Modulo-11 answer may sometimes be TEN (10) (in
> the case of books, represented by "X" ) which, in turn, complicates
> data entry, barcodes, and all that sort of machine stuff.

Unfortunately I don't have the reference now, but some thirty years ago
the (UK) NCC in Manchester had a book referring to Mod 11 - and for the
most part it gives you some 99% accuracy.

Your point about getting a check-digit of 'X' ( = 10). Big deal - this
came up in mid sixties when a guy wanted to use Mod 11 for Kimabll tags,
(fashion merchandise). He was distraught - the Litton ticket producers
couldn't provide an 'X'. Very complicated solution - just ignore the
bloody numbers where you have an 'X'.

I believe it was you that said check digits weren't used on US SSN - Mod
10 is used for Canadian SSNs, (from memory, weightings of 1,2,1, 2
etc....).

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Function Mod philosophy

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T10:53:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFC271C.772C6D90@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<3AF17B01.56126D03@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> ...
> Why would someone want a return value of 4 or -4 above?   They had to
> design that function to work that way for a purpose, I just don't know
> what that purpose is.  (maybe to copy an old math function which isn't
> useful for business needs).

I assume I am one of "they".  We did it that way because that is the
mathematically correct way to do it.  Also, the mod functions in other
languages and in the ISO mathematical function guidelines do it this
way.  Nobody in their right mind would use it with negative numbers, but
it has to be done in the correct fashion.  Just because it doesn't seem
right for business does not mean it is not right.  I would assume
business would want mathematically correct answers as opposed to
something incorrect.

Don Nelson
```

## Thread: include in Fujitsu-Powercobol — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### include in Fujitsu-Powercobol

- **From:** g.friedrich@fiuka.de
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T10:12:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ctv9m$ao7$1@news.netmar.com>`

```
Is anybody using #include statements (=copy) in Fujitsu-Powercobol?
I use #include for my file-descriptions. My problem is that I have to 
use an external editor to manage these copy-files. And Powercobol ignores
changes. If i change a copy-file and "build", powercobol doesn't compile
because it has no changes detected.
Or is #include not a good way. If I avoid #include, then I have to put
the file descriptions and so on in all of my modules. Or is there an other
way?
Guenther
```


#### ↳ Re: include in Fujitsu-Powercobol

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T13:35:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WeyI6.26802$sP6.1396212@news3.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9ctv9m$ao7$1@news.netmar.com>`

```

<g.friedrich@fiuka.de> wrote in message
news:9ctv9m$ao7$1@news.netmar.com...
> Is anybody using #include statements (=copy) in Fujitsu-Powercobol?
> I use #include for my file-descriptions. My problem is that I have
to
> use an external editor to manage these copy-files. And Powercobol
ignores
> changes. If i change a copy-file and "build", powercobol doesn't
compile
> because it has no changes detected.
> Or is #include not a good way. If I avoid #include, then I have to
put
> the file descriptions and so on in all of my modules. Or is there an
other
> way?
> Guenther
>

Which brings up the question: What ARE you supposed to use to manage a
copy book so that PC will recognize changes?

Don't use COPY in the Data Division nor INCLUDE in the Procedure
Division. You'd think they would act the same if a pre-processor was
not involved; they don't.

For example, a COPY statement in the Data Division often generates
spurious error messages that you don't get with INCLUDE.

Go figure.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: include in Fujitsu-Powercobol

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T15:27:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eTzI6.111$6l3.6679@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<9ctv9m$ao7$1@news.netmar.com> <WeyI6.26802$sP6.1396212@news3.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
You use a make facility, the same as on a mainframe.  Fujitsu have a GUI
project manager that will build all the make files automatically, if you
wish to use that instead.  I do not know of *any* Cobol that has the make
facility built into the actual compiler, but then there are lots of
compilers that I have not used.

The problem is that PowerCobol generates the code, and makes all that stuff
invisible.  PowerCobol *is* a GUI code builder, and actually calls the
standard compiler.  If you write ordinary Fujitsu Cobol, rather than using
PowerCobol, then the problem does not exist.  You set up dependancies much
the same as with any other Cobol.
```

```
using these two API calls, you can do this with Fujitsu COBOL:

GetModuleHandle
GetProcAddress
```

```
Just for the record (Michael and I have resolved this privately) --
Windows makes use of a C runtime, and a Visual C runtime that is NOT
the MFCxx.  The MFCxx is used in support of Microsoft Foundation
Classes.

On Sun, 29 Apr 2001 23:37:33 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
<michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

>Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
>news:3aec1371.213008963@news1.attglobal.net...
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone writing console apps using win32api ?

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T12:05:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3af29a76.28527435@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net> <9ciqb9$29p$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
You can search msdn.microsofr.com for free.

However, why not just port your code to "regular" ACCEPT/DISPLAY code
using whichever win32 COBOL compiler you like?  

Another alternative, is to use COBOL sp2 from flexus - converting your
existing infrastructure to make use of the sp2 features.  You
certainly can still have a "text mode" application using sp2 -- if you
desire -- and gain the benefit of Thin Client in the process.


On Mon, 30 Apr 2001 06:44:28 +0100, "Alister Munro"
<alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>Thanks all for coming back with answers ?.
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone writing console apps using win32api ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T02:22:53+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d2592$hht$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<9aq2g1$654$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3ae02584.119540331@news1.attglobal.net> <9ciqb9$29p$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <3af29a76.28527435@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
> However, why not just port your code to "regular" ACCEPT/DISPLAY code
> using whichever win32 COBOL compiler you like?

I used to use ACCEPT/DISPLAY commands but found them to generate too
much code. I also found them to be slow and really disliked the way the
cursor
would jump around the screen. Another problem was that I was restricted to
80
column displays with my MF WB  v2.4 compiler. I moved away from the
supplied screen & now file IO operations to make my code portable between
the various vendor formats. I also find it very easy to convert my code to
'C'
(I don't like OO as it is very messy) as I use COBOL as I would use a macro
assembler.

I should have said in my original post that I was looking to front-end my
code
with out involving another product. Performance and code size is still
important to me as I enjoy making these optimisations. I still have clients
who
use 386 machines in there factories.

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:3af29a76.28527435@news1.attglobal.net...
> You can search msdn.microsofr.com for free.
>
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-fee12089a2"></a>
## Thread: Mainframe COBOL JCL question — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Mainframe COBOL JCL question

- **From:** "Bruce Nashtut" <Altcom@san.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T17:02:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hBI6.230801$GV2.48310347@typhoon.san.rr.com>`

```
I am working on the program that will be executed in 2 different mainframe
environments. 1 - production, 2 - another logical partition (LPAR) on the
same OS390.
There are should be some differences in the program logic based on the
environment it will be executed in.
I can setup either parameter or another file that will provide environment
information, but I would like to make something more slick.
I am looking either for JCL or COBOL instruction/verb that will pull LPAR
information. Kind of like COBOL ACCEPT verb pull system time in COBOL or
&SYSUID pulls TSO userid info.
Our technical support suggested to look for SYSID or SMFID but I could not
find anything.
IF anyone is avere of the JCL, COBOL verb or IBM COBOL II called module that
can return the need info your help is appreciated.
Thank you in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe COBOL JCL question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-04T14:17:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9cuv9f$jp$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7hBI6.230801$GV2.48310347@typhoon.san.rr.com>`

```
Check out the COBOL FAQ that has a reference to program provided by Gilbert
Saint-Flour for getting at the "system control blocks" on OS/390.  This does
require VS COBOL II (or later) as it uses USAGE POINTER, but I think it will
do what you want.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe COBOL JCL question

- **From:** "David de Jongh" <ddejongh@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-05T16:38:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n0WI6.6102$kA2.470315@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7hBI6.230801$GV2.48310347@typhoon.san.rr.com> <9cuv9f$jp$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Whoa back!!!
Sure, it's easy to get to control blocks - just redefine a PIC S9(9) COMP
VALUE +16 as a POINTER to get to the CVT and then chain forward from there
to wherever you want to go. Look at the OS/390 MVS Data Areas manual to find
the links if you really want to do this.
BUT, you are almost certainly putting in code that no COBOL programmer is
going to understand, and that will have to be changed next time the SYSID
changes.
It's much more flexible to use a JCL PARM= string to say 'PROD' or 'TEST' if
that's all you really want to know.
Now, if you are doing something like retrieving the JOBNAME, or JOBID, or
executing program name, which is specific to the current instance, then
control block chaining is the way to go.  Just don't do something because
it's slick i.e. arcane.
D de J

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9cuv9f$jp$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net...

will
```

```
The Data Areas  Manuals are licensed... does anyone know where thy can be found on-line?
```

```
I suspect DCB and DEB are in one of the DFSMS manuals.

See if you can get your employer to get you a copy of the OS/390 collection
on CD-ROM.  It's about $100 for eight or nine CD's with a couple of thousand
publications.  You can load the bookshelves you want onto your hard drive
and get instantaneous access, or onto one of your network drives.
Let me know if you decide to do this - there are a couple of tips you will
need.
D de J
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2001-05-05T10:27:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_989058283@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

<a id="t-1d72513f57"></a>
## Thread: Is This What COBOL is all about? — 26 msgs

### Is This What COBOL is all about?

- **From:** "l i z a r d" <oabhari1@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-05T18:46:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jUXI6.119623$rX5.6480288@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
Hello,

First time post. I am taking COBOL at school. This is my second course.
Please bare with me. So far, we've covered many things. Headers, error
checking,
merging, sorting, updating, creating a new master from an old master and
trans files, tables and arrays (which I don't understand very well)...

I have a couple of questions:

1. Is this what you would mostly be dealing with in a business environment
concerning Cobol? meaning, is this the type of stuff you'll be asked to do?
merge, sort, update, error check, etc... or is there a lot more?

2. I'm using Mainframe Express at school, and Merant at home. I dislike
both. Is there a reasonabley priced Cobol compiler that I can get?

3. I need extensive help with tables. I really want to learn this. It is not
my goal just to pass. I have straight A's. I find passing very very easy.
Yet, the main reason I'm going to school is learn and obtain knowledge. So,
where do I go to learn more about tables and arrays. I'm looking for sample
programs and excercises.

4. I will never post asking for someone to do my homework for me. But if I
ever encounter problems is it okay to post asking for general help and
direction?


Thanks alot for any help you can provide me with.

Sincerely,

Omar "Lizard" Abhari.
```

```

"l i z a r d" <oabhari1@home.com
>
> 1. Is this what you would mostly be dealing with in a business
environment
> concerning Cobol? meaning, is this the type of stuff you'll be asked
to do?
> merge, sort, update, error check, etc... or is there a lot more?

This is about one-quarter. You're learning how to use tools (like a
wrench or a screwdriver). In the world, you'll be asked to use these
tools to build (more likely fix) the engine (go-cart to rocket-ship or
anything in between) that powers your company. The other 3/4 comprise
problem definition, interfaces, business logic, integration, design,
and a lot more. ("Lizard, your assignment this week is to rework the
double-declining-balance methodolgy of our leashold improvement
amotorization table to use the technique I saw in a recent issue of
'Rolling Stone' magazine - I forget which one.")

>
> 2. I'm using Mainframe Express at school, and Merant at home. I
dislike
> both. Is there a reasonabley priced Cobol compiler that I can get?

I've not used those two, but a couple of compilers cost zero
(www.adtools.com and tinycobol). The next step up from zero is many
hundreds of dollars.

>
> 3. I need extensive help with tables. I really want to learn this.
It is not
> my goal just to pass. I have straight A's. I find passing very very
easy.
> Yet, the main reason I'm going to school is learn and obtain
knowledge. So,
> where do I go to learn more about tables and arrays. I'm looking for
sample
> programs and excercises.

Consult another book on COBOL (DON'T look at a COBOL reference book).
Sometimes a different description is all it takes to ring the bell.
Think EXCEL spreadsheet.

>
> 4. I will never post asking for someone to do my homework for me.
But if I
> ever encounter problems is it okay to post asking for general help
and
> direction?

Sure. We all (well, some) learn by sharing.
```

```
Omar,

The answer to your question is "Yes". This is largely what COBOL is all
about.

The wheels of Commerce are kept oiled by COBOL code all over the World and,
while it may not be as exciting as putting Men in Space, it certainly is
demanding, absorbing, and, ultimately, rewarding.

You demonstrate by your attitude that you are extremely likely to succeed,
not just as a COBOL programmer, but, much more importantly, as a Human
Being. (You have realised already that the "Paper Chase" is not what's
important; it is the "yearning for learning". Some of the best educated
people I have ever met had no diploma, were self taught, driven by their
love of the particular subject, and were much fuller Human Beings than
certain extremely qualified Academics I have encountered...Guess who I
learned most from?)

I'll be glad to see your questions and I suspect there are many here who
feel the same. Please post on generalities pertaining to COBOL if you want
to (always with the proviso that you are not expecting homework to be done).
In the very unlikely event that you don't get a solution, mail me privately.

Pete Dashwood.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is This What COBOL is all about?

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T04:03:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010506000331.29945.00000654@ng-fw1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<jUXI6.119623$rX5.6480288@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
>1. Is this what you would mostly be dealing with in a business environment
>concerning Cobol? meaning, is this the type of stuff you'll be asked to do?
>merge, sort, update, error check, etc... or is there a lot more?


First, a lot of what you spend technical time on isn't COBOL.   I probably
spend as much time coping with Database and Telecomms stuff as I do coding.  
Then there's Testing...

How you use COBOL depends on what type of business you're doing.  If you do
batch payroll systems, you're getting what sounds like a full tour.  If you do
online transaction processing
it can be rather different.  You spend a lot more time doing routing and
context checking,
and you may go years between writing a call to SORT.  You also spend more time
learning to parse data from other systems and doing conversions between data
formats.   And error handling takes on a somewhat different twist- you can't
just spit out some console messages and die gracefully.  You spend a lot of
time trying to suss out what happend on the other end of a comm link. 

It's all good stuff to know.

Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA 
Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is This What COBOL is all about?

- **From:** Rob Lec <RobLec@cinci-rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T13:54:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF55781.5ACADD0E@cinci-rr.com>`
- **References:** `<jUXI6.119623$rX5.6480288@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <20010506000331.29945.00000654@ng-fw1.aol.com>`

```

For lizard...

These comments are especially true (actually everything so far in this 
thread) but I thought FRLSFLY identified where a lot of time is spent 

... additional comments follow...

FRLSFLY wrote:
> 
> you may go years between writing a call to SORT.  You also spend more time
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Personally I haven't used COBOL's internal sort in over a decade.  

Outright data conversions are indeed a frequent task, but more often
you'll be faced with trying to figure out someone else's database
or file layout.  (You will probably encounter EDI, which is ALL
about data formatting and conversion).

COBOL is all about DATA.  This should be the first sentence
in every COBOL book.  Most of my "thought-work" is in the 
Data Division.  The procedures then just fall into place.

Tables and arrays are just the same as in any other language.
It's not specific to COBOL.  Once you understand the concept
in one language, you will be able to relate to arrays in ANY
programming language.

Communication problems and error logging (or error messages)
and dealing with the users, trying to figure out just what 
happened, is a big part of the job.  Time spent actually 
writing code is considered "quality time".
 
Don't judge the COBOL language based on some vendor's
implementation or the development environment you're currently
using.  Each complier has it's strong points and weaknesses.
Getting to know each one's quirks takes a few weeks to maybe a 
few months, but then you will become quite productive with it
until faced with a different one due to job change, upgrade
or whatever.  Again, it's part of the job, and remember that
it's just one of the tools you use.

-------------------------------------------------
(To reply by email change dash to dot in address)
```

#### ↳ Re: Is This What COBOL is all about?

- **From:** "Barry K. Clarke" <bkclarke@qwest.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T11:06:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF5929A.E6DC608@qwest.net>`
- **References:** `<jUXI6.119623$rX5.6480288@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
Yes, the items you mentioned in your e-mail will be very close to work in the
real world.  However, a lot of sorting is done externally using SyncSort or
DFSort instead of program internal.  Check out VisualAge COBOL sold by IBM Corp,
go to www.ibm.com/cobol, I think its 300 or 400 hunderd, runs on the PC, well
worth it.  The computer is based on mathematics, a COBOL table or array is
called an array in the mathworld.  Think if it as a row of mailboxes numbered 1
... 100 or whatever, within each mailbox you will put your data, which can be
obtained by reference the mailbox number, or COBOL subscript, ie mailbox(13) =
your type of data put into this mailbox.  Consult cobol book.  Construct a
simple COBOL shell program.  Put some arrays or tables and displays to see what
is in the array.  Programmers use a lot of displays in solved COBOL problems.
Consult math book on arrays.   Most cobol programmers in the real world don't
know about file status codes nor use them, so you're ahead of the pack.  Study
hard and Good Luck.  I am a mainframe COBOL programmer for 12 years working at
the Boeing company on conttract.

l i z a r d wrote:

> Hello,
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Omar "Lizard" Abhari.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is This What COBOL is all about?

- **From:** Stephen J Spiro <donotreply@interbulletin.bogus>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T19:10:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AF5A1A6.3CD73584@interbulletin.com>`
- **References:** `<jUXI6.119623$rX5.6480288@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AF5929A.E6DC608@qwest.net>`

```
Replying to a couple of posts:

In the IBM Mainframe world, external sorts are terribly inefficient, harder to maintain (usually because no-one puts comments in the control cards), and not really easier to code.

If you have an INPUT PROCEDURE and/or and OUTPUT PROCEDURE, the external sort requires that you pass the file an extra time -- both as input and output. Yes, I know there are control cards for selection and editing, but they are hardly ever used, and COBOL is easier to understand and maintain.

If you use the FASTSRT compiler option, the compiler will use the house standard (DFSORT, SyncSort) sort instead of the COBOL Compiler's sort. This, of course, applies only to USING and GIVING, but is much more efficient than the COBOL compiler's own sort. This was not introduced until about 1989 by IBM.

Since external sorts require both control cards and extra JCL, I don't really see them as easier to code than a COBOL program.

I have hardly ever used a USING or a GIVING in quite a number of years.  The file usually has to be manipulated before sorting, and then used somewhere else.  INPUT and OUTPUT PROCEDUREs. 

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
stephenjspiro at hotmail.com
_______________________________________________
Submitted via WebNewsReader of http://www.interbulletin.com
```

```
"Stephen J Spiro" <donotreply@interbulletin.bogus> wrote in message
news:3AF5A1A6.3CD73584@interbulletin.com...
> Replying to a couple of posts:
>
> In the IBM Mainframe world, external sorts are terribly inefficient,
harder to maintain (usually because no-one puts comments in the control
cards), and not really easier to code.

In the IBM Mainframe world, external sorts are far faster than internal
sorts, and are easier to maintain, when comments are included. They are
really easy to code, and are very versatile. It is worth learning the basics
of Synchsort. Other "easier" tools such as batch FileAid aren't much easier
to code, and are far slower.

Furthermore, stand-alone sorts provide foolproof functionality such as
filtering (include/omit/etc), reformatting and trimming/padding of records),
that would require extra code to validate in a COBOL program. Synchsort also
provides a really easy  way of adding the 2-digit century to dates. And as
for the "programmer": on my team, that wanted to code an RBA sort inside a
COBOL program, l,et's just say I discouraged him from doing this!


>
> If you have an INPUT PROCEDURE and/or and OUTPUT PROCEDURE, the external
sort requires that you pass the file an extra time -- both as input and
output. Yes, I know there are control cards for selection and editing, but
they are hardly ever used, and COBOL is easier to understand and maintain.
>
> If you use the FASTSRT compiler option, the compiler will use the house
standard (DFSORT, SyncSort) sort instead of the COBOL Compiler's sort. This,
of course, applies only to USING and GIVING, but is much more efficient than
the COBOL compiler's own sort. This was not introduced until about 1989 by
IBM.
>
> Since external sorts require both control cards and extra JCL, I don't
really see them as easier to code than a COBOL program.
>
> I have hardly ever used a USING or a GIVING in quite a number of years.
The file usually has to be manipulated before sorting, and then used
somewhere else.  INPUT and OUTPUT PROCEDUREs.
>
> Stephen J Spiro
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is This What COBOL is all about?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T18:09:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d4lj4$er6$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<jUXI6.119623$rX5.6480288@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AF5929A.E6DC608@qwest.net> <3AF5A1A6.3CD73584@interbulletin.com> <6wjJ6.81685$181.13096410@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <7uibftse0llijsj0aakk00fsle5lbajg7l@4ax.com>`

```
WARNING:
   This thread has turned into a "SORT in IBM Mainframe environments"
discussion.  If you are NOT interested in IBM Mainframe environments, then
please "disregard" as most/all of this does *NOT* apply to other COBOL or
SORT environments.

    ***

Whether to use "external" or "internal" SORTs in an IBM mainframe
environment is almost as "religious" a war as ISPF vs XEDIT - or (for
COBOLers - paragraphs vs sections). (Almost all programmers have a STRONG
preference - and most shops have at least a "default" that you need to
"justify" an exception to.)

HOWEVER, there is one thing that is simply WRONG in what Binyamin says below
(that surprises me - as I find his posts usually QUITE accurate),
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is This What COBOL is all about?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "David de Jongh" <ddejongh@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T23:59:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GzlJ6.7595$t12.619831@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<jUXI6.119623$rX5.6480288@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AF5929A.E6DC608@qwest.net> <3AF5A1A6.3CD73584@interbulletin.com> <6wjJ6.81685$181.13096410@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <7uibftse0llijsj0aakk00fsle5lbajg7l@4ax.com> <9d4lj4$er6$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
1. To those who believe external sorts are faster, I say, "show me your
benchmarks".
2. Sorts are almost invariably done as input to a program that will do some
sort of processing on the sorted data, and frequently as part of an
Extract/Sort/Report process.  Why do six lots of EXCP's when you only have
to do two?
3. Why add an extra layer of maintenance (coding changes to the sort control
cards as well as the COBOL copybook)?

In the high and far-off times, when memory was expensive, it was very
inefficient to code an internal sort - the sort was cramped for work-space
in memory.  Today, a large percentage of sorts are actually done in main
memory - just check your stats - because the typical region size is 32MB.

D de J
```

```
:>WARNING:

:>discussion.  If you are NOT interested in IBM Mainframe environments, then
:>please "disregard" as most/all of this does *NOT* apply to other COBOL or
:>SORT environments.

:>    ***

:>Whether to use "external" or "internal" SORTs in an IBM mainframe
:>environment is almost as "religious" a war as ISPF vs XEDIT - or (for
:>COBOLers - paragraphs vs sections). (Almost all programmers have a STRONG
:>preference - and most shops have at least a "default" that you need to
:>"justify" an exception to.)

:>HOWEVER, there is one thing that is simply WRONG in what Binyamin says below
:>(that surprises me - as I find his posts usually QUITE accurate),

:>Bill Klein
:> wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

:>"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
:>news:7uibftse0llijsj0aakk00fsle5lbajg7l@4ax.com...

:>> On Sun, 06 May 2001 21:39:14 GMT "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com> wrote:

:> <snip>
:>> :>In the IBM Mainframe world, external sorts are far faster than internal
:>> :>sorts,

:>> Why would they be? The same sort program is used.

:> <more snip>
:>> If one is only USING and GIVING, then, yes, the program should be skipped.
:>> Once you have an INPUT or OUTPUT procedure, you may as well do it in the
:>COBOL
:>> program and skip multiple passes over the data.

:>If you use the NOFASTSRT compiler option, then *all* SORTs are done via
:>COBOL (and/or LE) "run-time" routines and are *never* performed by DFSort,
:>SyncSort, whatever.

:>If you use the FASTSRT compiler option then *only* those phases that use
:>USING or GIVING are actually done by DFSort, SyncSort, whatever.  Those
:>"phases" that are done via an INPUT  or OUTPUT procedure *are* done (again)
:>by the COBOL run-time.

I believe that you are confused.

That option refers to whether the sort program or the COBOL runtime does the
actual I/O to the input (USING) or output (GIVING), or to be more technical,
whether COBOL invokes SORT with a DDNAME or an E15/E35 routine.

From the manual:

"Specifies fast sorting by the IBM DFSORT licensed program.  NOFASTSRT is the
default, and specifies that IBM COBOL will do sort/merge input/output."
                                                         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^

:>There are a "few" fairly obscure reasons that NOFASTSRT is actually
:>*required* for ANSI COBOL conformance - but these are not reasons (that I
:>believe) justify using that option.  The major reason would be *if* (and I
:>have never seen any "real-world" program that did this) you had a FILE
:>DECLARATIVE that you wanted to get "control" if there was an I/O error
:>during a SORT (or MERGE).  Otherwise, I *strongly* recommend making FASTSRT
:>a "shop default" - as the compiler (and run-time) will switch it to
:>"non-FASTSRT" if there is any code that requires it.

:>HOWEVER,  it is also true that whenever you ARE using INPUT/OUTPUT
:>procedures you do *not* get SyncSort or DFSort "support" (via E15/E35
:>exits) - but instead get a SORT that is handled by "COBOL" itself.

You are incorrect.

:>NOTE: There is also the option of letting SORT (DFSort or SyncSort) "be on
:>top" and write E15 or E35 exits in COBOL.   I used to see this done in the
:>70's and 80's - but don't (from personal experience) think that many sites
:>still do this.  The support for OMIT and other SORT control cards has pretty
:>much made this "redundant" and/or irrelevant.

If it can be done.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is This What COBOL is all about?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-07T13:40:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d6q6s$vlt$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<jUXI6.119623$rX5.6480288@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AF5929A.E6DC608@qwest.net> <3AF5A1A6.3CD73584@interbulletin.com> <6wjJ6.81685$181.13096410@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <7uibftse0llijsj0aakk00fsle5lbajg7l@4ax.com> <9d4lj4$er6$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <f5fcft8e0e6nmfr88c37h44iab30rqkpid@4ax.com>`

```
I stand corrected.

  FASTSRT/NOFASTSRT *does* impact ONLY the I/O not the SORT "software".

The only time (on an IBM mainframe) that the "normal" system SORT is not
used via the COBOL SORT verb is the (little used) option of having a SORT
verb in a CICS program. In that case, IBM *does* document that you (the
"application") need to come up with your own "sort" - but tells you what
will get "passed" to the routine.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is This What COBOL is all about?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T23:59:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jzlJ6.81954$181.13168244@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`
- **References:** `<jUXI6.119623$rX5.6480288@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AF5929A.E6DC608@qwest.net> <3AF5A1A6.3CD73584@interbulletin.com> <6wjJ6.81685$181.13096410@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <7uibftse0llijsj0aakk00fsle5lbajg7l@4ax.com>`

```
Ok. Let me rephrase then.
When I look at the supposed advantages of incorporating a sort inside a
COBOL
program, I don't think they are completely valid.

1. Efficiency - if it uses the same Synchsort as a stand alone, what tuning
modifications can be used in a COBOL, that aren't available in a stand alone
sort?

2. Comments - yes, if no comments are used in a SYSIN, it can take time to
determine what fields are being referenced. But as part of my walkthrough's,
I wouldn't sign off on SYSIN that didn't include comments. They are real
easy
to include.

3. After a QUALITY data anlysis has been done, there are documented data
structures available to code SYSIN from, and it most sorts can be coded from
SYSIN in under an hour.

4. Maintenance. If you include the sort in a program, you need to test it,
even if you
make changes that are unrelated to the sort. Correctly commented SYSIN can
be
cchanged & tested in a fraction of the time it takes to do the same for a
COBOL
program;

Then there is the whole concept - if a COBOL program has INPUT PROCEDURE,
SORT & OUTPUT PROCEDURE, why not keep it as 3 separate functions. Time
and again, consolidating multiple functions has caused problems later, when
changes
are needed.

As for the RBA sort - it's an independent function, that is one of the few
times that
a little thought is needed. But my clients have custom-written exit
routines, and it
works a treat as a stand-alone sort.

records),

and
> no program is required to format the output file ......
>

also

>

as

a

external

but
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is This What COBOL is all about?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-07T09:36:10+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<amfcftoat8pid7skee5l03bstp8v4umh9v@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<jUXI6.119623$rX5.6480288@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AF5929A.E6DC608@qwest.net> <3AF5A1A6.3CD73584@interbulletin.com> <6wjJ6.81685$181.13096410@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <7uibftse0llijsj0aakk00fsle5lbajg7l@4ax.com> <jzlJ6.81954$181.13168244@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
On Sun, 06 May 2001 23:59:11 GMT "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com> wrote:

:>Ok. Let me rephrase then.
:>When I look at the supposed advantages of incorporating a sort inside a
:>COBOL
:>program, I don't think they are completely valid.

:>1. Efficiency - if it uses the same Synchsort as a stand alone, what tuning
:>modifications can be used in a COBOL, that aren't available in a stand alone
:>sort?

If the imput/output procedure logic can not be coded in the sort language.

:>2. Comments - yes, if no comments are used in a SYSIN, it can take time to
:>determine what fields are being referenced. But as part of my walkthrough's,
:>I wouldn't sign off on SYSIN that didn't include comments. They are real
:>easy
:>to include.

The issue is that a copybook change now affect sort input and a recompile does
not install the change.

:>3. After a QUALITY data anlysis has been done, there are documented data
:>structures available to code SYSIN from, and it most sorts can be coded from
:>SYSIN in under an hour.

It is easier to calculate the offsets of the fields than to use the field
names?

:>4. Maintenance. If you include the sort in a program, you need to test it,
:>even if you
:>make changes that are unrelated to the sort. Correctly commented SYSIN can
:>be
:>cchanged & tested in a fraction of the time it takes to do the same for a
:>COBOL
:>program;

The amount of run time required to do testing should not be that big a factor
in deciding how to code something. One certainly can pay for the additional
run time during testing by reducing the run time during production.

:>Then there is the whole concept - if a COBOL program has INPUT PROCEDURE,
:>SORT & OUTPUT PROCEDURE, why not keep it as 3 separate functions. Time
:>and again, consolidating multiple functions has caused problems later, when
:>changes
:>are needed.

Because rather than reading one file and producing one file, one is reading
one file producing temp1, sorting temp1 and producing temp2, and then reading
temp2 to produce the output.

This might end up being a lot more I/O and a great increase in run time.

:>As for the RBA sort - it's an independent function, that is one of the few
:>times that
:>a little thought is needed. But my clients have custom-written exit
:>routines, and it
:>works a treat as a stand-alone sort.

And might very well work better as part of a program.

I will grant that if the sort language provides the tools that the input and
output procedures are not needed, that it is better to do a standalone sort.
Also, if the exact same sort one the exact same file will provide input for
multiple programs then it is a good idea to externalize the sort (though one
could argue that the choice of file organization be reconsidered).

Once one has to massage the data on input/output, the external sort just adds
more steps makes things in general more complicated. Of course, if one has
only junior programmers who do not know how to do an internal sort ....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is This What COBOL is all about?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-06T21:54:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dKjJ6.1089$ig1.596905@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<jUXI6.119623$rX5.6480288@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AF5929A.E6DC608@qwest.net> <3AF5A1A6.3CD73584@interbulletin.com>`

```
Stephen J Spiro <donotreply@interbulletin.bogus> wrote in message
news:3AF5A1A6.3CD73584@interbulletin.com...
> Replying to a couple of posts:
>
> In the IBM Mainframe world, external sorts are terribly inefficient,
harder to maintain (usually because no-one puts comments in the control
cards)

Noone minus one. I always comment my SYSIN cards.

Of course, I only use external sorts because a client sez they don't like
internal COBOL sorts, which I much prefer.

When I do use internal sorts, on the USING..GIVING....I've found it pretty
handy to go with this...

   SORT file-one
     ASCENDING KEY  aaa
       USING  xxxxx
         OUTPUT PROCEDURE  yyyyy

RETURN in the OUTPUT PROCEDURE takes the place of OPEN, READ/READ NEXT,
CLOSE, saves a DD name, etc.

(You can also do INPUT PROCEDURE ... GIVING...  Easy way to do a 'INCLUDE
COND=' when coding the INCLUDE would be a lot harder than writing it in
COBOL).


MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is This What COBOL is all about?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-07T07:26:27-06:00

```
I do put comments in my external sorts, but even if I didn't I find it easier to maintain than internal sorts.   Maintenance is easier when tasks are broken apart and simplified.   Since Endeavor has its own compiler options for our site, we don't have the FASTSRT option.   I use external sorts 3/4 of the
time and find them efficient and easy to use.

Even when I do use internal sorts, I quite often use either USING or GIVING.  I never use both, because then I would use an external sort.
```

```
On Sun, 06 May 2001 19:10:30 +0000, Stephen J Spiro
<donotreply@interbulletin.bogus> enlightened us:

>Replying to a couple of posts:
>
>In the IBM Mainframe world, external sorts are terribly inefficient, harder to maintain (usually because no-one puts comments in the control cards), and not really easier to code.
>

This is absolutely not true in the IBM (and like) mainframe world.
IBM's sort is extremely fast and efficient as are some third party
vendor's sorts as well.  

Besides, once the specifications are given, control of the sort JCL
and control cards passes to an Operations person as it is their duty
(in most shops) to maintain JCL.  The programmer rarely gets involved
in this unless the file changes or the sort key or sort requirements
change.

>If you have an INPUT PROCEDURE and/or and OUTPUT PROCEDURE, the external sort requires that you pass the file an extra time -- both as input and output. Yes, I know there are control cards for selection and editing, but they are hardly ever used, and COBOL is easier to understand and maintain.
>

In the over 25 years that I've been a programmer, I've worked for 2
different companies in 2 different industries.  My career has caused
me to program in Cobol-D all the way up to the current version.  I've
yet to encounter a Cobol program in either company that included an
internal sort.  I'm not saying it is never used.  I am saying that the
external sort has been around for a long time and is used
exponentially more than Cobol internal sorts.

>If you use the FASTSRT compiler option, the compiler will use the house standard (DFSORT, SyncSort) sort instead of the COBOL Compiler's sort. This, of course, applies only to USING and GIVING, but is much more efficient than the COBOL compiler's own sort. This was not introduced until about 1989 by IBM.
>

My memory says this is not correct, but I can't swear to it.  Maybe
someone else will clarify.

>Since external sorts require both control cards and extra JCL, I don't really see them as easier to code than a COBOL program.
>

Then you know little about it.  They are very simple to set up.  Try
setting up a Sort option card in Cobol as opposed to setting it up in
JCL.

>I have hardly ever used a USING or a GIVING in quite a number of years.  The file usually has to be manipulated before sorting, and then used somewhere else.  INPUT and OUTPUT PROCEDUREs. 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote in article 

>>Replying to a couple of posts:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>change.
...........
In most shops, the COBOL programmer is responsible for the JCL and the Control Cards.  
I just finished a contract where half the files in the shop changed because of a field size change on "Branch Number" from 2 to 3 bytes.  *Sigh* The field was in the middle of the key on half the keyed files in the house....

............
>
>>If you have an INPUT PROCEDURE and/or and OUTPUT PROCEDURE, the external sort requires that you pass the file an extra time -- both as input and output. Yes, I know there are control cards for selection and editing, but they are hardly ever used, and COBOL is easier to understand and maintain.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>exponentially more than Cobol internal sorts.
.............
In the over 25 years that I've been a programmer, I've worked for 31
different companies (just counted them from my resume!) in about 10 - 15 different industries.  I see internal and external sorts in about even numbers, rarely are there house standards.  2 companies is not a big sample.  
.............

>
>>If you use the FASTSRT compiler option, the compiler will use the house standard (DFSORT, SyncSort) sort instead of the COBOL Compiler's sort. This, of course, applies only to USING and GIVING, but is much more efficient than the COBOL compiler's own sort. This was not introduced until about 1989 by IBM.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

Frequently, I have an input procedure that takes data from multiple files and builds a new file to be sorted.  The data must be cross-edited, and some records will be ignored completely, but this cannot be determined until the record's data is cross-edited with data from another file.  Sometimes tables or other data areas must be built from data in one or more files because the resultant file is not synchronous with the input files. Outputs frequently require not merely the formatting of the file, but the interaction of the sorted records with one or more other input files, and perhaps the extraction of data to create additional output files. Frequently, one or more reports are required as part of the output.
Does someone want to tell me that this can be written and MAINTAINED more easily with control cards than COBOL? Fuhgeddiboutit!

Stephen J Spiro
stephenjspiro at hotmail.com
```
