```
"Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net> wrote in message
news:ezUL6.22224>
> Pete,
>
> As the "original poster", I have a few comments.
>
> I'm a day-to-day coder, not the Tintoretto of  Cobol.

Now I wonder who that would be...?

>
> If I ran across the code originally posted during a maintenance project, I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Good. Neither would I. Possibly the last, but not deliberately...<G>

> If I've been asked to change the program, my job is to do just that - not
> prove my adherence to the latest Cobol religion.
>

I'm a devout Atheist...

> I WOULD...
> - decompose an IF statement into a more readable form if I was having
> trouble understanding it (you see the result),

Is your string of EVALUATES any more readable than the compound IFs? It is
certainly arguable.

> - replace it in the program only if I felt sure I could test the resultant
> code within my allocation of hours,
> - print it and pin it to my cube wall for next time if I couldn't.
>
 I have scantily clad women on mine, but each to his own...

> Now...
>
> I submit that my code, in my humble opinion, is easier to read and
> understand than the originally posted version and does not intrude on
other
> functioning code.

I agree. In fact, having spent much more time on it than I should have, I
almost like it...<G> It certainly meets the criteria above.

> My purpose in submitting it was within this context, not
> to be sniffed at by Cobol "instructors" for its lack of, well, whatever.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

All very fair comment.  I apologise (genuinely) for thoughtlessly offending
you.

I wasn't "sniffing " at your code for any perceived lack. It is just that
this particular example is so horrible that there is very little that can be
done to improve it.

Certainly substituting a bunch of EVALUATEs for a bunch of IFs doesn't cut
it, at least as far as I'm concerned. As you are obviously miffed, I'll show
you my solution and you can feel free to comment, criticise or generally
"get your own back"  <G>. (If you had read the whole thread you would've
seen it.)

And please don't take it so personally. You really can survive without my
approval...<G>

Here's my response to Eileen:
<start quote>
I thought it was a beautiful IF statement...<G>

It was certainly jolly interesting...

Instead of doing the daily crossword today I resolved to see if it could be
simplified and how much would be gained.

I converted it to symbolic notation, did a Boolean simplification on it (it
would've been MUCH more "interesting" if we had some NOTs in it...<G>) and
arrived at the following, which purports to be logically equivalent (unless
I made a mistake in the simplification or symbol transitions...not
unknown...I haven't time or inclination to check it on my computer so I'll
just risk the red face if it is wrong...)

Anyway, although there are some savings, there are not as many as you might
think when first looking at it...

====================================== Pete's solution ==================
if AIHD-SALES-CLS = 11 OR 34 OR 61 OR 35
                         OR
    SPECIAL-CHRY-CLS
                          OR
    AIHD-CUST-NO = 08221 OR 05659 OR 00879 OR
                     08228 OR 10418 OR 11010 OR
                     08288
                          OR
    (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 14 AND
    (WS-DEST-CD-1 = 'AP' OR 'WE' OR 'EE' OR 'EF' OR 'FD' OR
                                       'PP' OR 'CC' OR 'IE'  OR
    WS-DEST-CD     = 'TC04A' OR 'TC11A' OR '0100A'  ))
                          OR
    (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 30 AND
    (AIHD-CUST-NO  = 09937 OR 10100 OR 10181 OR 10190  OR
                                       11100 OR 09941 OR 09949 OR
                                       10222 OR 01552 OR 11109  OR
                                       10108 OR
     WS-DEST-CD = '04179' OR '12014' OR 'LSC07' OR '31   '))
                            OR
     (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 18 AND
      (WS-DEST-CD = '48063' OR '17576'))
        perform XXXX
end-if
===================== End Pete's solution - 24 lines =====================
I am NOT proposing that the above is "elegant" code, but at least it has
removed the redundant tests (or it should have, if I got my sums
right...<G>). This in turn removes the "precedence" which someone else in
the thread has already identified.

In an on-line environment the tests could be prioritised and shuffled around
the "OR"s so that the most likely events were tested for first; in Batch, it
really doesn't matter...

I agree that a better approach would be to break it down and derive "logic
values" which could be tested.

Thanks for an interesting 20 minutes...<G>

Pete.
======================= Eileen's original =================================
YukonMama wrote in message <20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com>...
>We get a lot of students either posting or lurking here so the other day
while
>working on a particularly nasty program (8000 some lines of spaghetti code
with
>polish logic) I came across this particular IF statement no less than 9
times
>in the program and thought they would like to see what's in store for them.
>
>This is what you get to see in the real world :)
>
================= Very nasty person on Eileen's site solution ==============
>IF (AIHD-SALES-CLS = 11 OR 34 OR 61) OR
>    ((AIHD-SALES-CLS = 14 OR  35) AND
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>END-IF
======================== End very nasty "solution"   - 25 lines
================
>
>There are lower case x's at the PERFORM to indicate that different
paragraphs
>are performed each time the IF appeared in the program.  The paragraphs
were
>identical except for one or two variables from the map (this is a CICS
>program).  These variables are actually an occurance but the map is not
>redefined so each time the field in question is used such as QTY1 or QTY2
the
>paragraph is duplicated.  Makes maintenance a real nightmare.
>
>You will also notice that there are a few ORs that preclude the execution
of
>other ORs (that is it doesn't matter as something before it takes
precedent -
>assignment: see if you can find it).
>
>The fields being tested are in the header record which is read at the
beginning
>of the program.  The IF statement is now reworked and a switch set if
>everything makes it thru the tangle and the switch checked where each of
the
>original IFs were.  Just my part of making this nightmare a bit more
managable.
>
>Eileen
>
===================== Cliff's solution  ==========================
EVALUATE TRUE
WHEN SPECIAL-CHRY-CLS
WHEN AIHD-CUST-NO   = 08221
WHEN AIHD-CUST-NO   = 05659
WHEN AIHD-CUST-NO   = 00879
WHEN AIHD-CUST-NO   = 08228
WHEN AIHD-CUST-NO   = 10418
WHEN AIHD-CUST-NO   = 11010
WHEN AIHD-CUST-NO   = 08288
WHEN AIHD-SALES-CLS = 11
WHEN AIHD-SALES-CLS = 34
WHEN AIHD-SALES-CLS = 35
WHEN AIHD-SALES-CLS = 61
    PERFORM XXXXXX

WHEN AIHD-SALES-CLS = 14
    EVALUATE WS-DEST-CD-1
    WHEN 'AP'
    WHEN 'WE'
    WHEN 'EE'
    WHEN 'EF'
    WHEN 'FD'
    WHEN 'PP'
    WHEN 'CC'
    WHEN 'IE'
        PERFORM XXXXXX
    END-EVALUATE

WHEN AIHD-SALES-CLS = 18
    EVALUATE WS-DEST-CD
    WHEN '48063'
    WHEN '17576'
        PERFORM XXXXXX
    END-EVALUATE

WHEN AIHD-SALES-CLS = 30
    EVALUATE WS-DEST-CD
    WHEN '04179'
    WHEN '12014'
    WHEN 'LSC07'
    WHEN '31   '
        PERFORM XXXXXX
    WHEN OTHER
        EVALUATE AIHD-CUST-NO
        WHEN 09937
        WHEN 10100
        WHEN 10181
        WHEN 10190
        WHEN 11100
        WHEN 09941
        WHEN 09949
        WHEN 10222
        WHEN 01552
        WHEN 11109
        WHEN 10108
            PERFORM xxxxxx
        END-EVALUATE
    END-EVALUATE
END-EVALUATE.

======================== end Cliff's solution - 56 lines==============

My Choice?

NONE of the above!

I WILL however, agree that your solution is much better than the
original...<G>

(Actually, in the course of writing this it has kind of grown on me, and I
like it much more now than I did at the start... <G>  I still think all
three solutions stink: the only thing mine has going  for it is that it is
mathematically "pure"; it certainly isn't a fine example of how to write
COBOL).

Cliff, it is only computer programming, not life and death... your value as
a person does not depend on your code, any more than mine does. (Just as
well in my case...<G>)

Can we be friends now <G>?

Pete.

                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T09:59:26+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0392EE.AE8839FB@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com> <SfnK6.13740$4f7.1067825@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <0NvK6.228$uV4.28222@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3afb3b49_2@Usenet.com> <ezUL6.22224$4f7.1763596@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Cliff Peterson wrote:
> 
> I submit that my code, in my humble opinion, is easier to read and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> this code, what is your solution, Pete?

All these 'solutions' may be very much better than the original, but
they all miss the point entirely: Data items are being built into the
code.  To test this IF statement properly it would be necessary to give
it data with every combination of values and to check each of several
thousand results.

The only usable solution is to have a flag in the customer record, the
class record and the destination (DEST?) record and have these set by
whoever cares about the outcome of the IF.  Then the system need not be
rebuilt everytime there is a change in the status of a customer, nor the
necessity of retesting the system.

As an interim step the code could be changed such that the IF statement
is coded as if these flags are in the data and the values that are
currently in the IF can be taken out to a separate set of tests:

   IF (AIHD-CUST-NO = 08221 OR 05659 OR 00879 OR
                      08228 OR 10418 OR 11010 OR
                      08288)
	MOVE "ALWAYS"		TO AIHD-CUST-Process
   ELSE
   IF (AIHD-CUST-NO = 09937 OR 10100 OR 10181 OR 10190
                            OR 11100 OR 09941 OR 09949
                            OR 10222 OR 01552 OR 11109
                            OR 10108)
	MOVE "GRP02"           TO AIHD-CUST-Process
   ELSE
   IF ( AIHD-CUST-NO = ..
        MOVE "GRP42"           TO ...
   ELSE
        MOVE "NEVER"           TO ...
   END-IF
   END-IF
   END-IF

   IF ( AIHD-SALES-CLASS = 11 OR 34 OR 61 )
        MOVE "CLSON"           TO AIHD-SALES-Process
     ....

The purpose of this is to give meanings to the groups of data items so
that the problem can be understood as a _business_ solution.  Those
customers must be of a specific set of types.  For example they have
signed a particular trade agreement or non-disclosure.  They are
'special' in some way(s).  This should be in the customer record as a
value or even a group of values to memorialise this.  Then it can also
be shown on the enquirey screen and changed as required.

The Sales Class, too, is some sort of special set of products perhaps. 
Why is the class record, or the product records not indicating this fact
?

If it is impossible to change the data records then set up a text file
with, say:

     *CUSTOMER=ALWAYS
     08221 
     ...
     *CUSTOMER=GRP02
     10100
     10181
     *CLASS=ALWAYS
     11
 
Read this into tables and apply tests to set a group of flags or 88
levels.

Once the _business_ model is understood and implemented then the code
can be written in terms of that model using meaningful values instead on
anonymous codes.

A decision table should then be used to establish how the flags fit into
whether the processing is done or not.

The savings that this will give are:

     Reduction in testing to samples of each setting.
     The data list and decision table can be verified
        and/or changed by those that care *.
     The system won't be rebuilt everytime there is a
        new customer or product.

  * I am assuming that the programmer neither knows nor
    cares who the customers actually are or why they 
    get this special processing, but the marketing or
    sales manager will, so may the development or
    production manager.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ugly IF Statement

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-21T12:24:37+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B08FAF5.580733C5@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010410225436.26397.00004167@ng-cf1.aol.com> <SfnK6.13740$4f7.1067825@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <0NvK6.228$uV4.28222@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3afb3b49_2@Usenet.com> <ezUL6.22224$4f7.1763596@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3B0392EE.AE8839FB@Azonic.co.nz> <o216gtgtfuuf1eun0irku89uj172s0e499@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:

> :>All these 'solutions' may be very much better than the original, but
> :>they all miss the point entirely: Data items are being built into the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> the record, 

1) Enough to satisfy the _business_model_.  It is the complexity of how
the customers, sales classes, and other items need to be described that
should determine this.  Currently the business model is not even
embodied in the IF syatement, just a series of apparently unconnected
codes tied together in various combinations or ANDs and ORs.

If the business model is actually investigated then there may, in fact,
be simplifications that could never be apparent from the IF statement.

What does it all mean ?

2) "None", if my alternate scheme of setting the flags for groups of
codes using separate IF statements to preset the flags in WS is used.
eg:

      SET Customer-Normal     TO TRUE
      IF ( Customer-Code = lsit of anonymous codes 1 )
           SET Customer-Beta-Tester     TO TRUE
      ELSE
      IF ( Custome-Code = list of codes 2 )
           SET Customer-Alpha-Tester    TO TRUE
        etc    
     
3) "None" if my alternate scheme of describing these codes in a text
file is used.

> i.e., how complex a conditional statement do you need before you
> add the flag?

The resulting conditional statement need not be more complex at all.  If
will in fact become simpler to understand.  Instead of:

      IF (customer code is one of anonymous list)

it becomes:

      IF ( customer is of type )

and this can be a direct 1:1 replacement in the original IF of a list of
codes by a type comparison.

Why would it be 'more complex' ?
```

## Thread: Student needs a simple answer — 6 this month / 21 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

_Thread started [2001-04](2001-04.md#t-c8295574c5)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student needs a simple answer

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Stephen J Spiro <donotreply@interbulletin.bogus>
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T05:42:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFA2A49.12EE6E12@interbulletin.com>`
- **References:** `<lv7y6.24030$PR.200487@news1.wwck1.ri.home.com> <9ab4f4$8l8$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <1h9y6.209$jz.14745@www.newsranger.com> <9abd20$m04$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`

```
Regarding READ INTO and WRITE FROM

(Luis Gomez-Almeida) wrote in article 
: 
The reason for this is that if the program abends
>(abnormally ends, or just craps out) whatever was in the record being read
>by the program might not show in the dump, but what is in working storage
>does.
===============
This answer really bothers me.  Your buffers (or other file I/O areas) should be in the dump.  Having trouble finding the last record read or written is NOT a professional excuse.  On large files, or files with long records, the overhead associated with READ INTO and WRITE FROM can wreck your batch window.  

===============
Regarding other comments in this thread, Boolean data types are being added to the new COBOL standard.  When first introduced, the draft made it legal to do binary arithmetic   on boolean fields .... until someone raised the question, "So, if you have a field for sex, and you add 2 men, you get a woman and a carry?"  
The draft has been fixed.



Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
stephenjspiro at hotmail.com
_______________________________________________
Submitted via WebNewsReader of http://www.interbulletin.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student needs a simple answer

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T17:25:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S9AK6.1337$bi2.109579@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<lv7y6.24030$PR.200487@news1.wwck1.ri.home.com> <9ab4f4$8l8$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <1h9y6.209$jz.14745@www.newsranger.com> <9abd20$m04$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <3AFA2A49.12EE6E12@interbulletin.com>`

```
Hi Stephen,

While I agree with your point that a professional should be able to locate a
buffer rec in a dump, I don't see anything wrong with the from/into approach
(aside from performance consideratios). It simplifies the search for the rec a
bit, and if the file is not humongous, the o'head incurred can be acceptable.   

Another benefit is that this approach allows the pgm to ref the last rec read
even after EOF - a huge benefit (by simplifying the code) when comparing 2 or
more files.

Regards, Jack.


In article <3AFA2A49.12EE6E12@interbulletin.com>, Stephen J Spiro says...

…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
John H Sleight Jr wrote:
> >
> >Regarding READ INTO and WRITE FROM
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >This answer really bothers me.  Your buffers (or other file I/O areas) should be in the dump.  Having trouble finding the last record read or written is NOT a professional excuse.  On large files, or files with long records, the overhead associated with READ INTO and WRITE FROM can wreck your batch window.

We use them so the master record can be written as many times as
required without reloading all fields.  It has nothing to do with ABENDS
and record dumps.

A WRITE FROM leaves the source record as formatted at time of write.
A WRITE leaves the source record emptied of values (low values as I
recall)

	Susan A
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student needs a simple answer

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Stephen J Spiro <donotreply@interbulletin.bogus>
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T05:26:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFF6C7A.1CEC9413@interbulletin.com>`
- **References:** `<lv7y6.24030$PR.200487@news1.wwck1.ri.home.com> <9ab4f4$8l8$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <1h9y6.209$jz.14745@www.newsranger.com> <9abd20$m04$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <3AFA2A49.12EE6E12@interbulletin.com> <S9AK6.1337$bi2.109579@www.newsranger.com> <3AFAFEB2.FE0BF151@boeing.com> <7KGK6.1081$uV4.240090@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote >
>A file buffer is "undefined" following a WRITE if you follow the standard.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The whole concept of a dump is not defined in the standard.... dumps depend on the machine and the operating system and other things.  In MVS, all of the buffers are still in the dump until the last record in the last block is written successfully... but if the ABEND is on the WRITE of that record, the buffers will still be there.

Stephen J Spiro

PS: Yes, writing from a template is a good reason for using WRITE FROM.  I have found that careful logic USUALLY (not always) can eliminate the need to save the contents of the previous record.  My observation was a general rule, subject to circumstances.


_______________________________________________
Submitted via WebNewsReader of http://www.interbulletin.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student needs a simple answer

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T07:41:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B022105.41095B21@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<lv7y6.24030$PR.200487@news1.wwck1.ri.home.com> <9ab4f4$8l8$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <1h9y6.209$jz.14745@www.newsranger.com> <9abd20$m04$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <3AFA2A49.12EE6E12@interbulletin.com> <S9AK6.1337$bi2.109579@www.newsranger.com> <3AFAFEB2.FE0BF151@boeing.com> <7KGK6.1081$uV4.240090@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> programmers)....

30 years ago, using the ICL 1900 XE13 compiler (or possibly XE20 or
XEKB) I discovered a _very_ interesting result when updating UDAS ISAM
files.  Normally when a WRITE fails one would expect that the record in
the record area could be output to some other recovery file and this
merged back in later.  However, due to the way the overflow system
worked, it was actually possible that the record being written _did_ get
put into the file and that the failure occurred as it was moving some
other completely different record into overflow, a record that may have
been in the file for some time, and was being held in the record buffer
while the block buffers were swapped (this was a 16Kword multitasking
machine).

(description of UDAS ISAM files with first level and second level
overflow and how this problem could occur available on request, please
apply in writing on the back of a signed blank cheque).

It was only when the write failed by attempting to displace an existing
record into an already full overflow that this was actually a problem,
but after a successful or failed WRITE the record area may have had
_any_ value: some other record, or overflow tags.

Other than that, and that it had less grunt than my watch, it was a nice
system.
```

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<3AFAE393.D2E2BBD0@yahoo.com>`

```
Does anyone know of a tool to use for generating COBOL (Micro Focus
environment) working storage areas needed for use with Oracle tables
(UNIX environment).  My experience has been IBM 390 with DB2 and
COBOL-II.  DB2 has a DCLGEN tool, although for the past 9 years I've
been using a data dictionary tool that generates this for me into a
copybook from view associated with each program.  

I'm sure I could write something eventually, but I have a deadline and
have been thrust into this new environment suddenly.

Thanks in advance.
```

```
import to ACCESS) my DECLGEN will provide COBOL record layouts and Host
Variables which would then be OK for use with ORACLE.

with ORACLE that there is no similar utility?

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu PoweCobol: TreeView Control, problem...

```
I have not yet tried to use the treeview control,
but I think the tree must be "walked" (scanned) to find the selected
node. Each node has a "path" property, and a "selected" property.

There is not an "index" property like the "listindex" of a listbox
control, or the "isselected(a)" flag array (also used by a listbox).

You will want to read the user's guide and the reference manuals.

HTH,

Jeff Campbell
n8wxs@arrl.net

fulvio wrote:
> 
> I'm, trying to use the "TreeView Control" like a "menù" to call my programs.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fulvio
```

<a id="t-7ec83aa515"></a>
## Thread: OT - Humor, but ... — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - Humor, but ...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T14:31:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9deqcv$2j5$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
From a usually "humorous" source <G>,

-----Original Message-----

     Programmer to Team Leader: "We can't do this proposed
     project. **CAN NOT**. It will involve a Major design change
     and no one in our team knows the design of this legacy
     system. And above that, nobody in our company knows the
     language in which this application has been written. So even
     if somebody wants to work on it, they can't. If you ask my
     personal opinion, the company should never take these type
     of projects"
>
     Team Leader to Project Manager: "This project will involve a
     design change. Currently, we don't have any staff who has
     experience in this type of work. Also, the language is
     unfamiliar to us, so we will have to arrange for some
     training if we take this project. In my personal opinion, we
     are not ready to take on a project of this nature."
>
     Project Manager to 1st Level Manager: "This project involves
     a design change in the system and we don't have much
     experience in that area. Also, not many people in our
     company are appropriately trained for it. In my personal
     opinion, we might be able to do the project but we would
     need more time than usual to complete it."
>
     1st Level Manager to Senior Level Manager: "This project
     involves design re-engineering. We have some people who have
     worked in this area and others who know the implementation
     language. So they can train other people. In my personal
     opinion we should take this project, but with caution."
>
     Senior Level Manager to CEO: "This project will demonstrate
     to the industry our capabilities in remodeling the design of
     a complete legacy system. We have all the necessary skills
     and people to execute this project successfully. Some people
     have already given in-house training in this area to other
     staff members. In my personal opinion, we should not let
     this project slip by us under any circumstances."
>
     CEO to Client: "This is the type of project in which our
     company specializes. We have executed many projects of the
     same nature for many large clients. Trust me when I say that
     we are the most competent firm in the industry for doing
     this kind of work. It is my personal opinion that we can
     execute this project successfully and well within the given
     time frame."
>
```

<a id="t-3709848700"></a>
## Thread: opening multiple files — 22 msgs

### opening multiple files

- **From:** Mike Schmidt <mschmidt@accunet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T14:58:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFAF2D6.B7F9D9FA@accunet.net>`

```
I have Fujitsu COBOL v 6.1 (& Power COBOL).  I want an easy way to open
more than one file at a time.  Basically, I want a user to be able to
choose a list of files & then have the COBOL program run through the
data in each file & do certain checks.  I don't care if I have to just
use regular COBOL & have the user key in wildcards (file*) in a DOS
window brought up through a batch file or if I have to design something
visual in Power COBOL.  I'm somewhat of a novice obviously.  I messed
around with the FileList control in Power COBOL & can select multiple
files, but then I'm not sure how to reference each of the files
selected.  The SelString property only seems to return the highlighted
file (ie the most recently selected file).

To be honest though, it would be just as convenient for us if there was
just some common way to do what we do already.  My boss has set up a
batch file that asks for the file name & then calls the COBOL program.
So the program gets the input file from the user input.  The program
then makes checks on each record in the file & spits out an error
report.  However, it doesn't work when the user uses the asterisk for a
wildcard to try to select a group of files & my boss is not sure how to
go about doing this.

Any help would be appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: opening multiple files

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hUDK6.60533$_f3.1140695@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3AFAF2D6.B7F9D9FA@accunet.net>`

```

As I said, I am a novice.  Neither my boss or I are completely familiar with
everything.  If you are referring to looping within the batch file so that the
user just keeps entering a new file name after a file has been processed, that
isn't practical.  We work with a large number of files so it'd be too time
consuming.  As I said earlier, my boss has a batch file where (among other
things), a DOS window is opened & prompts the user for a file name & that name
is used by the COBOL program as an input file.  But if you use a wildcard
(Example: ST125*), an error occurs.
```

```
No, I am not refereing to looping within a batch file.

When a wild card is entered, you have to do a find first, then successive
find nexts.  The original program can handle these one at a time, so it has
to be modified to do the find first in the start up, then the first find
next and repeat if not found down near the end, somewhere.
```

```
In article <UTSK6.10985$h%.2184433@news20.bellglobal.com>, "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
>No, I am not refereing to looping within a batch file.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>next and repeat if not found down near the end, somewhere.

Maybe I'm missing something important... but why couldn't you type this at a 
DOS prompt (we are talking about MS-DOS, right)?

C:\> for %f in (ST125*.*) do myprog.exe %f

Then MS-DOS will take care of the wild-card expansion *and* the loop. All your 
program needs to be able to do is read the name of its input file from the 
command line.

>
>"Mike Schmidt" <mschmidt@accunet.net> wrote in message
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

```

solution is that trying to open the file simultaneously, which was what was
originally infered, is not going to work.  At some level, he has to set up a
loop, and do them one at a time.
```

```
I'm sorry.  I obviously didn't explain myself clearly enough.  I didn't mean to
make it sound like I want to open all of the files simultaneously (even though
that's almost exactly what I said - DOH).  I want to do like you say & just loop
through the files.  However, as I said earlier, I am new to this so I'm just not
figuring out how to go about this (please pardon my ignorance).  It seems to me
that the file name is either coded in the program or the user can be prompted to
enter the file name.  In our case the program would be run every day using
different files, so the user would have to be prompted.  The example that my
boss has set up prompts the user from the batch file & then assigns what is
entered to INFILE.  But when a wildcard is entered when the user is prompted
(ST0504*), the COBOL program then gives an error - saying "OPEN ERROR.
FILE=INFILE. 'NON-FILE  '".

Maybe I'm just in over my head.  I don't even know how to go about doing the
find first & find next that you talked about in your previous message.

Somewhere I've got to be accepting a wildcard, right?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: opening multiple files

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Mike Schmidt <mschmidt@accunet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T17:04:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFC61E7.D95A6E07@accunet.net>`
- **References:** `<3AFAF2D6.B7F9D9FA@accunet.net> <hUDK6.60533$_f3.1140695@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFB1301.EE1EA66E@accunet.net> <UTSK6.10985$h%.2184433@news20.bellglobal.com> <3afc2634$0$88186$a05e1490@news.netdirect.net> <VNVK6.11521$h%.2289956@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFC4B62.7DBBF0F8@accunet.net> <WSYK6.17458$yw.565331@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
There are a lot of different programs that are going to have to do this
(different programs for different types of data).  Some of these programs exist
& work (have for quite a while - my boss actually wrote all of those & the batch
files).  But in the past, all of the data from each file was put into one large
file (on an old system) before it got put on the PC & run through the COBOL
program.  However, things have changed & it would be much more convenient to run
each file through individually.

Currently, the users start the program by running a batch file.  The batch file
prompts for a filename & then does some renaming & copying of things before it
passes along INFILE to the COBOL program.  You're right, I just need to repeat
with a group of files, but let me explain further.  When the file gets read,
each record is checked for certain errors & any errors are output to a different
file (it's just a text file that the batch file sends to the printer after the
COBOL program has run).  So we wouldn't want to have the first file run through
& create an error report & then have the second report run through & create a
second error report & so on.  We would want to be able to put all of the errors
from each file all into the same error report.

We certainly could work around this point, but we're shooting for ease of use
(the users are actually just our employees).  We just want to have as few steps
as possible with as few chances of doing anything wrong as possible.

Donald Tees wrote:

> Ok.  Lets start at the begining.  Are you writing this program from scratch,
> with an example to work with, or are you attempting to convert an existing
…[189 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

```
Ok.  then it sounds like this is what you could do.  First, you want to
write a generic little program that will simply go through all the files in
a wildcard specification.  That will be compiler specific, but most
compilers have a subroutine library that will do the job. To test it, you
can just have it display the filenames.

Using that as a mainline, convert all existing code from being the mainline
to being a subroutine that accepts the filename as subroutine arguements.

Pretty well everything else can be done in batch files.  You might also want
to check the batch loop command that Jerry suggested.  I am far from a DOS
batch expert.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: opening multiple files

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-13T02:08:17+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3afd43a9_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3AFAF2D6.B7F9D9FA@accunet.net> <hUDK6.60533$_f3.1140695@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFB1301.EE1EA66E@accunet.net> <UTSK6.10985$h%.2184433@news20.bellglobal.com> <3afc2634$0$88186$a05e1490@news.netdirect.net> <VNVK6.11521$h%.2289956@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFC4B62.7DBBF0F8@accunet.net> <WSYK6.17458$yw.565331@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFC61E7.D95A6E07@accunet.net>`

```
Mike,

This really doesn't need a COBOL solution.

You can run the existing program which you know works OK, using the single
DOS command suggested by Doug Miller.

If the errors are the only output from the program, you can append them by
extending Doug's very elegant solution as follows:

C:\> for %f in (ST125*.*) do myprog.exe %f >> errfile.txt

This command will present each member of the recordset of filenames which
match the wildcard pattern "ST125*.*" to your program (myprog.exe). The
output will be APPENDED to the "errfile.txt" file, so, as each iteration of
the myprog.exe runs, its output will be placed on a single error file
(errfile.txt).

Alternatively, you could send the errors direct to the printer with:

C:\> for %f in (ST125*.*) do myprog.exe %f > LPT1:

(Note that in this case there is no need to append unless you don't want a
page throw and headings at the start of each report).

You will lose nothing by trying these commands.

If the program has several outputs then the error file solution is not
viable and you would need to amend the program to OPEN APPEND on the text
file.

I would strongly recommend that you do NOT change the program code if you
can run it using the above "JCL".

Pete.



Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------

----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

```
I like this idea.  Can this line be used in a batch file.  I'd like that for a
couple of reason.  One is ease of use - as I had said earlier, we want as few
places as possible that the user can mess something up - & typing a line like
this every time they want to run the edit is not exactly foolproof (also not as
quick).  Another reason is that when I just type this at the DOS prompt, I
still have problems (I am running Windows 2000).

First of all when I test it out using a wildcard that picks up two files, I get
an error in the DOS window that says "The process cannot access the file
because it is being used by another process."  I get this when it tries to
process the second file - as if it's moving on to the second file too quickly.
But also, I still get an error from COBOL because in the program, I have this
"SELECT IN-FILE   ASSIGN TO INFILE."  So I need to assign INFILE somewhere.  Am
I correct in assuming that when I assign the file I either have to hard code
what I'm assigning (the exact file name) or I have to assign it to a variable.
Since the files change each time that the program gets run against, I can't
hard code it.  So it's assigned to a variable.  But anytime INFILE gets
assigned to wildcards (st0504*.*), I get the error that says "OPEN ERROR.
FILE=INFILE. 'NON-FILE  '."

I did try to stick the line of code you suggested into the batch file.  But
when I run it, the window just closes.  I don't get any errors, but the program
certainly doesn't get run.  Here is the coding from the batch file:

CLS
ECHO ST Pre-Process
@ECHO 0N

C:
CD \COBOL\ST
@ECHO ON
CLS
DIR/W/P
@ECHO OFF
ECHO Key the filename you want!

CALL PROMPTER INFILE,

ECHO %INFILE%
PAUSE

for %f in (INFILE) do stedit.exe %f  >> errfile.txt

PAUSE
```

- **References:** `<3AFAF2D6.B7F9D9FA@accunet.net> <hUDK6.60533$_f3.1140695@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFB1301.EE1EA66E@accunet.net> <UTSK6.10985$h%.2184433@news20.bellglobal.com> <3afc2634$0$88186$a05e1490@news.netdirect.net> <VNVK6.11521$h%.2289956@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFC4B62.7DBBF0F8@accunet.net> <WSYK6.17458$yw.565331@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFC61E7.D95A6E07@accunet.net> <3afd43a9_1@Usenet.com> <3B003324.321BC7D3@accunet.net>`

```
Mike,

comments and, hopefully, "fixes" below...<G>

"Mike Schmidt" <mschmidt@accunet.net> wrote in message
news:3B003324.321BC7D3@accunet.net...
> I like this idea.  Can this line be used in a batch file.  I'd like that
for a
> couple of reason.  One is ease of use - as I had said earlier, we want as
few
> places as possible that the user can mess something up - & typing a line
like
> this every time they want to run the edit is not exactly foolproof (also
not as
> quick).  Another reason is that when I just type this at the DOS prompt, I
> still have problems (I am running Windows 2000).
>
Yes, you can use this command in a batch file. I have amended your code
below.


> First of all when I test it out using a wildcard that picks up two files,
I get
> an error in the DOS window that says "The process cannot access the file
> because it is being used by another process."  I get this when it tries to
> process the second file - as if it's moving on to the second file too
quickly.

That's a bit of a worry. I don't have Win 2000 so I can't investigate it.
Try rebooting into DOS and run it in a pure DOS environment. Just to see if
it works. If it does, then we know the problem is with running DOS under
Win2K and we can seek a suitable fix.

> But also, I still get an error from COBOL because in the program, I have
this
> "SELECT IN-FILE   ASSIGN TO INFILE."  So I need to assign INFILE
somewhere.  Am
> I correct in assuming that when I assign the file I either have to hard
code
> what I'm assigning (the exact file name) or I have to assign it to a
variable.

 Yes, you are correct in your assumption. It must be assigned to a variable,
but NOT an Environment Variable.  What is needed here is assignment to a
dataname, so INFILE must be defined in the Working-Storage section. You are
assigning it to an Environment Variable I think and that can never work.
(well, I guess it COULD work if we changed the Batch file to iteratively set
the EV, but that is very dodgy). Go for the simple solution. It must be a
dataname defined in your program; I suggest "WS-INFILE" to avoid confusion
with the EV used by PROMPTER. Change your assign to:

"SELECT IN-FILE   ASSIGN TO WS-INFILE."

 WS-INFILE must be loaded from the command line before you OPEN the file

When you start the program  you must obtain the filename from the command
line, using whatever facilities your particular COBOL allows for you to do
this (example: Accept WS-INFILE from COMMAND-LINE, or whatever...check your
COBOL documentation.) Do this BEFORE you OPEN the file!

The DOS "for" command will ensure that the name your program obtains, is the
next one in the list of wild cards. Check your program by running it from
the DOS command line with a single file name: stedit.exe myfile.txt
...f'rinstance...


> Since the files change each time that the program gets run against, I
can't
> hard code it.  So it's assigned to a variable.  But anytime INFILE gets
> assigned to wildcards (st0504*.*), I get the error that says "OPEN ERROR.
> FILE=INFILE. 'NON-FILE  '."

You must assign INFILE to the wild card string, using the PROMPTER batch
file, as you do now. You set up WS-INFILE with the name in the DOS command
line as described above. DOS actually places each individual file name into
the command line so your program can pick it up.
>
> I did try to stick the line of code you suggested into the batch file.
But
> when I run it, the window just closes.  I don't get any errors, but the
program
> certainly doesn't get run.  Here is the coding from the batch file:
>
I am amending the coding, but remember it is predicated on your program
picking up the file name correctly from the DOS command line and loading it
into WS-INFILE (defined in Working-storage) before you OPEN the file...
> CLS
> ECHO ST Pre-Process
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Note the %s around INFILE in the "for" statement.

Make sure your program CLOSEs the print file before STOP RUN.

Here's a "hard copy" batch file to test it with before you go for the
variable version where the user keys the wild card string....

CLS
 @ECHO OFF
 ECHO ST Process... hard code test.
 C:
 CD \COBOL\ST
 CLS
 DIR/W/P
ECHO Beginning process...
ECHO Here is the recordset that will be processed...
echo.
for %f in (st0504*.*) do echo %f
echo.
PAUSE
for %f in (st0504*.*) do stedit.exe %f  >> errfile.txt

 PAUSE
>

Let me know how you get on...

Pete.

----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

```
Screw it. Let's try a five-cent solution.

If you're gonna use a DOS batch file, do this:

DIR XYZ*.DAT > XXX.XXX

Then read the XXX.XXX file in your COBOL program and process each file
in the XXX.XXX file.
```

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2001-05-18T12:24:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b051f85$0$88184$a05e1490@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<3AFAF2D6.B7F9D9FA@accunet.net> <hUDK6.60533$_f3.1140695@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFB1301.EE1EA66E@accunet.net> <UTSK6.10985$h%.2184433@news20.bellglobal.com> <3afc2634$0$88186$a05e1490@news.netdirect.net> <VNVK6.11521$h%.2289956@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFC4B62.7DBBF0F8@accunet.net> <WSYK6.17458$yw.565331@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFC61E7.D95A6E07@accunet.net> <3afd43a9_1@Usenet.com> <3B003324.321BC7D3@accunet.net> <3b013ef5_4@Usenet.com> <3B04065E.A255BA13@accunet.net> <JPXM6.68902$Oo.3599263@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <3B044D63.32E2B4E5@accunet.net>`

```
In article <3B044D63.32E2B4E5@accunet.net>, Mike Schmidt <mschmidt@accunet.net> wrote:
>Actually, I think we just got it working using the 'for %f .....'
>statement working in the batch file.  But we had to use %%f to get it to
>work.  Go figure. 

No mystery; that's documented in the MS-DOS manuals. 

In a nutshell, MS-DOS batch files use %1, %2, etc. to indicate the command 
line parameters passed into the batch job. If you want to use % in commands 
*inside* the batch file, you have to use %% so that COMMAND.COM can tell that 
you're not referring to one of the batch job's command line parameters.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: opening multiple files

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Kevin Solomon" <kjsolo@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T15:51:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%ZcL6.120978$HF.28213752@news4.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AFAF2D6.B7F9D9FA@accunet.net> <hUDK6.60533$_f3.1140695@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFB1301.EE1EA66E@accunet.net> <UTSK6.10985$h%.2184433@news20.bellglobal.com> <3afc2634$0$88186$a05e1490@news.netdirect.net> <VNVK6.11521$h%.2289956@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFC4B62.7DBBF0F8@accunet.net> <WSYK6.17458$yw.565331@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFC61E7.D95A6E07@accunet.net>`

```
Mike:

To achieve what you want try this.

1.  Continue to use the DOS window to prompt the user for input of file
names.  The shell will parse the command line and produce a list of file
names that match the wild cards.

2.  The key is to redirect the output list of matching file names to a file.

3.  You then open this file and read the names into a table defined with an
OCCURS DEPENDING ON NUMBER-OF-FILES  clause.  Increment NUMBER-OF-FILES for
each read until EOF.

4.  You then MOVE the names to working storage with this structure.

01 AF-ALL-FILES.
    05 FILLER           PIC X(7)
          VALUE "SELECT ".
    05 AF-FILE.
        10 AF-FILE-FIRST    PIC X(5)
              VALUE  "FILE-".
        10 AF-FILE-NUMBER OCCURS n TO p
                TIMES DEPENDING ON
                NUMBER-OF-FILES
                INDEXED BY WS-INDEX.
                15 FILLER    PIC X.

01 AS-ASSIGNMENT            PIC X(17)
          VALUE "    ASSIGN TO 'C:\".
            (or whatever directory)
    05 AF-INPUT-FILE-NAME        PIC X(12).
            (names from the file of file names go here)
    05 FILLER                      PIC X(2)
          VALUE "'."

01 OR-ORGANIZATION    PIC X(32)  VALUE
       "ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL."
        (or whatever organization you need)

5.  Write the three lines to a file until NUMBER-OF-FILES is reached.  These
will become your FILE-CONTROL statements.

6.  Your next step is to CALL or LINK to a program that will COPY  this file
into it's FILE-CONTROL section.  Do you know how to set up and use
libraries?  It looks like this...

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
FILE-CONTROL.

COPY INFILES.DOC. (or whatever name)

SELECT ERROR-FILE
ASSIGN TO etc.

7. Remember to keep NUMBER-OF-FILES in scope for the called program.  Your
OPEN and CLOSE statements can be done in a loop using NUMBER-OF-FILES as the
control and varying an index for the file number like this.

200-OPEN-FILES.
    PERFORM VARYING WS-INDEX-A FROM
        1 BY 1 UNTIL WS-INDEX-A >
            NUMBER-OF-FILES.
        OPEN INPUT FILE-(WS-INDEX-A).
    END-PERFORM.

8.  The called program can then use an index for its READS and process one
file at a time until EOF.  You will wind up with one ERROR-REPORT that can
easily have the file-name and record sequence number on it for each error
record.

The advantages of this approach are that you avoid your wildcard problem and
you can use as many files as you like without knowing the names beforehand.

The disadvantage is that it's very awkward.

I hope this helps.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: opening multiple files

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T11:43:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<el9L6.178$Io1.26994@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3AFAF2D6.B7F9D9FA@accunet.net> <hUDK6.60533$_f3.1140695@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFB1301.EE1EA66E@accunet.net> <UTSK6.10985$h%.2184433@news20.bellglobal.com> <3afc2634$0$88186$a05e1490@news.netdirect.net> <VNVK6.11521$h%.2289956@news20.bellglobal.com> <3AFC4B62.7DBBF0F8@accunet.net>`

```
Psuedo-code....you'll have to add error checks, may not like the style, etc
but this should get you started.

"find first" and/or "find next"  can either be O/S service calls, or even
DISPLAY/ACCEPT to prompt the user

01 FILES-TO-PROCESS-TABLE
   05 FILE-NAME                      PIC X(256) OCCURS 100.    <<< 100
"long" file names
       INDEXED BY FILE-NAME-IDX.
01 FILE-COUNT                        PIC S9(04) COMP.

....
MAIN.
    SET FILE-NAME-IDX TO +1
    PERFORM GET-FILE-NAME UNTIL NO-MORE-INPUT
    SET FILE-NAME-IDX  TO +1
    PERFORM  PROCESS-FILE  FILE-COUNT-TIMES.
    GOBACK

GET-FILE-NAME.
   " Operating System specific"

     Set file-found to false
    "FindFirstFIle"

     IF file-found
        set file-found to true
        MOVE Found-file to file-name (file-name-idx)
        set file-name-idx up by +1
        PERFORM UNTIL file-found = false
            "Find Next FIle "
             If next-file-found
              set file-name-idx up by +1
               move found-file-name to file-name (file-name-idx)
             else
               set file-found to false
            end-if
     ELSE
       SET no-more-files to TRUE
     END-IF

     CONTINUE.

PROCESS-FILE.
  MOVE FIle-name (File-name-idx) to file-to-be-processed
  Call/perform process-the file
   set file-name-idx up by +1
   continue.
```

#### ↳ Re: opening multiple files

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T00:25:58+00:00

```
How many files are we talking about here and what do you want to do
with them once they're opened? I mean are you going to do something
with each file you find as you find it, close the file, then go to the
next file, or are you going to open multiple files simultaneously (!)?

If you want to go after files via wildcards, you'll probably have to
use the API FindFile call. Email me and I'll send you ours.


"Mike Schmidt" <mschmidt@accunet.net> wrote in message
news:3AFAF2D6.B7F9D9FA@accunet.net...
> I have Fujitsu COBOL v 6.1 (& Power COBOL).  I want an easy way to
open
> more than one file at a time.  Basically, I want a user to be able
to

just

messed

multiple

highlighted

> To be honest though, it would be just as convenient for us if there
was

program.

for a
```

<a id="t-8a51756054"></a>
## Thread: cobol as a structured language — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### re: re: cobol as a structured language

- **From:** rdriehle@nps.navy.mil ("Riehle, Richard")
- **Date:** 2001-05-10T22:37:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F5AD48747FC0324EB21B2B2BD27D5E8698B511@Saipan>`

```
William Klein wrote:


I am curious - what "definition" of "structured programming" 
A) requires "local" data 
B) prohibits PERFORM 
The *only* definitions that I have seen of structured programming require 
1) "single entry / single exit" from routines and structures 
2) use of "specific" structures (which *does* include "DO loops" - both DO
UNTIL and DO WHILE) 
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
------------------------
Unfortunately, this is all too common as well as too narrow a view.   Also,
unfortunate is
 the lack of good  books and articles still in print to deal with this
subject.  Everyone 
wants to jump on OOP bandwagon and no is writing new books about structured 
programming.  

One of the best treatments of the subject was a book on Structured Design
written by
Larry Constantine with input from Ed Yourdon.   If you can find a copy of
this book
in a used book store, buy it and guard it.   It goes into practical detail
about the really
important ideas of structured programming as well as structured design.  

The book by Constantine and Yourdon covers Modularity in depth.   Chapters 6
and
7 deal with cohesion and coupling.    They have some excellent observations
related
to the COBOL of the time.    

This is just one of a few of out-of-date books on structured methods.  Many
of the COBOL 
books on structured programming fall short, especially those published
in the late 1970's through the mid-1980's.   For the most part, the authors
of those books 
were unschooled in anything except the language and knew little of computer
science.
Not true of all of them, but true of a lot of them.

The book:  Structured Design, published by Yourdon Press/Prentice-Hall.  The
most 
recent edition I have at hand was 1979.   I believe there were some later
editions.

Richard Riehle
```

#### ↳ Re: re: cobol as a structured language

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T00:12:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dnm2s$lco$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<F5AD48747FC0324EB21B2B2BD27D5E8698B511@Saipan>`

```
I suppose it inevitable that, every year or so, someone must
clarify the difference between "structured programming" and
"modular programming." So Richard Riehle, Pete, Jimmy,
and others, here is the clarification.

Structured programming refers to the arrangement of
statements in a procedure and uses words like: sequence,
choice, and iteration, to describe the structure of the procedure.

Modular programming refers to the decomposition of systems
and uses words like: cohesion, coupling, encapsulation, and
interface, to describe the relationship between separate
procedures.

Procedural decomposition (PD) and object-orientation (OO)
are types of modular programming. Both PD modules and
OO methods may consist of "structured" code (or they may be
spaghetti).

There is no comparison or contrast between "structured
programming" and "object-oriented programming". They
occur at different levels.

Pete, OOP will never replace "structured"; though it may
replace PD. ;-)

Jimmy, you have made "my skin crawl" at least two dozen
times since my last post on MP vs SP. ;-)

Richard, what consitutes structured programming is
well-defined and your description is so broad as to be
incorrect. See, Youdon, "Techniques of Program Structure
and Design," Prentice-Hall, 1973, for more complete
descriptions of both modular programming and structured
programming. You might choose to re-read the chapters
on "Modularity" in "Structured Design" with the idea that it
is a discussion of modular programming; not structured
programming. ;-)
------------------
Rick Smith

"Riehle, Richard" wrote in message ...
>William Klein wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-
>------------------------
>Unfortunately, this is all too common as well as too narrow a view.   Also,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>The book by Constantine and Yourdon covers Modularity in depth.   Chapters
6
>and
>7 deal with cohesion and coupling.    They have some excellent observations
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>The book:  Structured Design, published by Yourdon Press/Prentice-Hall.
The
>most
>recent edition I have at hand was 1979.   I believe there were some later
>editions.
>
>Richard Riehle
```

```
OK, it's a fair cop! Thanks for the quote; I certainly can't argue with
that...<G>

I see now that it was incorrect usage of "Structured Programming" and I
shall be more careful in future.

(Whether I care or not, I USUALLY remember what I have written...Nice to
know that someone is actually reading my posts here...<G>)

>
> Incidently, I consider components to be a new addition to modular
> programming whether the interface is or is not object-oriented.
> I know you care about components!

Yes, passionately. I see this technology as the means to enable true
computing for everybody, and to abolish the Technocracy. The more minds that
are able to use a computer to do what they want/need, the better it will be
for Humanity in the long run. The "Technology" has been a barrier for way
too long; at last there are signs that it is crumbling.

I had a nine-year-old looking over my shoulder a little while back, as I
amended some HTML for a Web Page. She understood everything I was doing
apart from a couple of infrequently used Tags which she queried. I explained
their use and gave her an on-line HTML reference. She noted it all down very
seriously in a little red notebook and thanked me for the explanation. I
asked her where she had learned HTML and she said "at school, but we don't
really get enough access to the computers so I use Mummy's when she isn't
using it...". ( I checked with her Mother and she has many other interests
and is outside a lot of the time, so the kid is not just some computer
nerd...)

I find this incredibly encouraging. We need to empower the general
population with available and easy user-friendly access to computers. There
is a generation coming on which will be much better prepared for it than we
were, and if the User interface is made easier, then eventually, the
convergence of these two things (more computer aware users, and a smarter,
more forgiving, User interface) will place computer power firmly in
everyone's hands.

And that's where it belongs.

Pete.


> ------------------
> Rick Smith
>
>
```

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Great post, Rick!
>
> You obviously care very deeply about this.
>
> I'm afraid I don't, but I still enjoyed and noted what you wrote.

Them's my sentiments too <G>.

> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote in message
> news:9dnm2s$lco$1@news.hitter.net...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Well it's debatable 'if I''m loveable', but I'm curious as to what I wrote that
makes his skin crawl. If as the 'purist' that he is with regard to OO, I have
been an heretic - then I'm sorry - tough ! (I'm damned sure I haven't dropped
any pearls of wisdom with regard to non-OO).

Regardless of programming language, and whether or not COBOL structured or OO
there are two things that really count :-

1. Does it work ?
2. Is it maintainable ?

Even spaghetti code with umpteen GO TOs does work - although we *know* it can be
a nightmare for # 2 - maintainability. Rules and techniques are being 'invented'
all the time and your good friend Yourdan is one of the worst offenders - much
of it just renaming what existed 40 years ago..

Regardless of theory, if it works, can it be made to work better. The answer
always has to be 'Yes' - but rehashing to get the nth degree of efficiency can
be economically non viable. Can it be coded better for a particular situation -
yes, if a second party looks at existing code and suggests a 'neater' way,
specifically where it creates short-cuts or more importantly reusability.

>
> Now, I suspect that Richard will not let this go by so I'll refrain from
> comment...<G>
>

I'll put money on the above statement <G>

PS: Just going off at a tangent with something that 'worked'. Dowding, C-in-C
Fighter Command and Park AOC No. 11 Group 'won' the Battle of Britain. (Well
actually the Luftwaffe 'lost it' because in a fit of pique Hitler and Goering
decided to divert to attacking London, just when they had the RAF fighter
airfields on their knees). The RAF strategy, using early radar, was to get the
South Eastern squadrons off the ground as quickly as possible. Meanwhile
Leigh-Mallory, AOC 12 Group, along with the vocal crippled air ace Douglas
Bader, clamoured for 'Group Wings' - marshalling squadrons in the sky before
approaching the Luftwaffe. Just like a bomber operation, this takes assembly,
and meanwhile the orbiting fighters use up urgently needed fuel reducing
effective operational flying time.

Dowding and Park persisted and they 'won'. One of the dirty 'events' from WW2 -
Churchill allowed a Court of Inquiry to be convened on Fighter Command's
effectiveness - both Leigh-Mallory and Bader had plenty to say. The 'heroes' of
the B. of B. were retired as a result of the Inquiry. The 'practice' had worked,
but the theories espoused by Leigh-Mallory and Bader had a more 'delightful'
appeal to the politicos.

Jimmy
```

### Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T16:05:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3afb643c_2@Usenet.com>`

```

SkippyPB wrote in message
>
>>Give me a KISS program that's written top down with no deeper than 3
>>perform levels and a couple of go to exits and I'm as happy as a drunk
>>in Jack Daniels' garage.
>>
Amen!

I have followed this debate with some interest. My own belief is that
Structured Programming has been (or will be soon) replaced by Object
Oriented Programming. I realise there are many here who will vehemently
disagree and I don't care strongly enough about it to argue it.

Object Oriented Programming has already been pushed into Component
Development and it is at this point that the obsession with development and
maintenance of Source code becomes irrelevant.

How you write your COBOL really doesn't matter, because the way things are
going, NO ONE else will ever maintain it.

If you really want to argue this, please read my recent article ("Meet the
Future") on www.aboutlegacycoding.com. I will happily defend any points made
there, publicly or privately, but you better hurry because the site is due
to change features...<G>

Now,  I don't s'pose you could be a bit more specific as to the location of
Jack Daniels' garage, could you Skippy ...<G>?

Pete.
```

```
G Moore wrote in message ...
>"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Sorry, I don't understand what "mb replacement" means.

My comment (which I agree is provocative in today's environment) is
predicated on the fact that I believe there will be a shift away from SOURCE
code to OBJECT code. If you build systems with object code, there is no
point in attempting to maintain them as we do today (you probably won't have
the source code available, and it could be in any one of a dozen languages,
including COBOL...actually, the component will have been written in the best
language for the job it has to do...).

My reasons for believing this and the case to support it, has been covered
at length elsewhere. No-one will maintain the object code because it will
not need maintenance.

(Yes, I know this is hard to take when looked at in today's environment, but
I was not talking about today's environment. You need to see the full
context of my argument and then you can see that it is just a logical
extrapolation from trends which are already emerging. Hint: Think in terms
of modular hardware technology in electronics. No one fixes "bad" boards any
more; just bin the broken one and replace it.)

Only specialist shops will be in the business of producing components as it
will not be economically viable for most Enterprises to do it on a regular
basis. They can outsource this stuff for a fraction of what it costs to
produce in house.

The job of "COBOL Programmer" as we understand it today will change so
radically it will not be recognisable, and I believe this will happen within
the next 7 - 10 years. However, I have suggested alternative roles which
today's programmers could ideally fill if the suggested scenario eventuates.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-05-13T21:25:44+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-EC1DAF.21254313052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<3afb643c_2@Usenet.com> <krcpftcurb4pdou1f0057mu850rrafmqb4@4ax.com> <3afd3562_6@Usenet.com>`

```
In article <3afd3562_6@Usenet.com>,
 "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz> wrote:
> 
> The job of "COBOL Programmer" as we understand it today will change so
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
If I understand you correctly, you are advocating what essentially is a 
unix shell approach to programming. Here you have a number of objects 
code tools - grep, sed, awk,... - that need to be glued together. But 
then there is the problem of gluing these things together. So you merely 
shift the programming effort from the specific design of software to 
complete a given task to the more general writing of glue to utlise 
standard programs to achieve the same effect.

What has changed?

Of course to be flexible enough to handle a wide range of solutions, the 
tools have to include a lot of baggage. This also applies to the OO 
paradigm. When I use an object, I necessarily inherit all parent classes 
of the objects class - including heaps of code that may never be used.
Yes I know that there are tools to prune the list of classes that are 
not actively used, and this may indeed be a good thing for OO that is in 
it's favour verses the module and script approach. 

However, fundamentally, I see no difference in either of these 
approaches to what we do today. And still we require heaps of 
programmers to get the job done.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T20:19:08+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-E64B17.20190814052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<3afb643c_2@Usenet.com> <krcpftcurb4pdou1f0057mu850rrafmqb4@4ax.com> <3afd3562_6@Usenet.com> <josephk-EC1DAF.21254313052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <3afe98b4_1@Usenet.com>`

```
In article <3afe98b4_1@Usenet.com>,
 "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz> wrote:

Hi Peter,

I read your article. VERY NICE. This is the ONLY concept of OO that I 
actually admire and like. The ability to drop in components. Of course 
we have had that capability for decades - ever since COBOL had a CALL 
statement - and many companies have created libraries which could be 
purchased or downloaded and used.

What appears to be new here is the specification of a glue such that any 
language may participate in the use of the components. Whilst this also 
is not new - re: calling protocols on mainframes and PC's - the 
extension of a single paradigm to all platforms is new and possibly will 
be better for programmers productivity than the supposed JAVA write once 
run anywhere.

Because the glue is fully specified, the construction of components into 
solutions can now be automated. That is tools may be created to assist 
the programmer in the task of building the solution. For example no 
circuit designer today designs the layout of the tracks on a circuit 
board. The logic diagram is input and along with certain parameters, the 
circuit board layout programs design the layout by themselves. This 
means that this burdomsome task has been removed and the designer can 
concentrate on the logic and performance of the design - not the 
mechanics.

I agree that this is the wave of the future, but I think that for the 
vast majority - mainframe and non-mainframe - programs will continue to 
be written the old way. This state of affairs will continue until the 
programming community sorts out amongst themselves the standards to be 
used in the new component world. I say this because I like many would 
not be caught dead using any of Microsofts tools in the consruction of 
any code I might write. I'm sure that there are many of the inverse 
opinion and many other opinions as well.
```

```
"Joseph J Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:josephk-E64B17.20190814052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au...

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Thanks for the positive reaction. (I'm sure you can see now why I am loth to
debate this in this forum unless the background has been covered...it's a
3000 word article, probably too long, but they were kind enough to publish
it without editing.)

Yes, I agree that we have been re-using code ever since we first moved to
decomposed modules. The difference with this and the components is that the
component is shared, so ONLY ONE copy is necessary on the entire system
(Registration has a plus side!). And it is late bound, (dynamically linked).
While we could and did do this with modules, the component has this "drag
and drop" functionality which makes it very appealing once you start
developing in a Visual Environment (a consequence of being "wrapped"). I
believe that many of the Mainframers reading this think it is irrelevant
because of this..."We use SPF, Visual Development Environments are for VB
guys...". This perception will change, and, as the mainframe becomes more
firmly integrated into the Corporate LAN (and it will once a common Runtime
is available) I think we will see a gradual change in attitude. (Sure hope
so...Visual Programming is real fun...I hate going back to SPF now and would
probably only do so when it was absolutely necessary or I needed some
REXX...).

> What appears to be new here is the specification of a glue such that any
> language may participate in the use of the components. Whilst this also
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Absolutely, 100%!

> I agree that this is the wave of the future, but I think that for the
> vast majority - mainframe and non-mainframe - programs will continue to
> be written the old way. This state of affairs will continue until the
> programming community sorts out amongst themselves the standards to be
> used in the new component world.

Very fair comment. The Universities are currently producing standardised
Methodology for Component Based Design, and the COM and CORBA standards are
being constantly refined (extended) to provide a consistent and stable
standard for the interfacing of components. Many Corporations are already
looking at setting up their own Component Repositories and Software vendors
are scurrying to give them the tools to do it.

>I say this because I like many would
> not be caught dead using any of Microsofts tools in the consruction of
> any code I might write. I'm sure that there are many of the inverse
> opinion and many other opinions as well.
>
No comment. <G>

Pete.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aff33b7_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3afb643c_2@Usenet.com> <krcpftcurb4pdou1f0057mu850rrafmqb4@4ax.com> <3afd3562_6@Usenet.com> <ctkrft8jstkuf2q64ak6qnen1g0l02upgi@4ax.com>`

```

G Moore wrote in message ...
>>>>How you write your COBOL really doesn't matter, because the way things
are
>>>>going, NO ONE else will ever maintain it.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks! <G>

>>My comment (which I agree is provocative in today's environment) is
>>predicated on the fact that I believe there will be a shift away from
SOURCE
>>code to OBJECT code. If you build systems with object code, there is no
>
>bad idea. they had object code in y2k, and remember what happened when
>they lost the source?
>

The World didn't stop. However, I am not talking about legacy object code, I
am talking about components as objects. This is NOT a bad idea; it is a very
GOOD idea!



>>point in attempting to maintain them as we do today (you probably won't
have
>>the source code available, and it could be in any one of a dozen
languages,
>>including COBOL...actually, the component will have been written in the
best
>>language for the job it has to do...).
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>be done thru the interface?


Good point.

Yes, as a programmer myself, I know that we are used to having to make the
odd tweak.

However,  component based system development requires a different approach
than the one we currently use. The design phase identifies specific required
functionality (and it is non-technical). Eventually, the Business will sit
down with a Visual Development Environment and select functions which will
be put together just like LEGO blocks. All of the technical interfacing and
parameter passing will be completely removed from them.

If we used the analogy of housebuilding, your point would be that there are
occasions when a brick doesn't fit exactly and has to be cut or modified
slightly.

In the scenario I am describing, it would be impossible for this to be
critical functionality.  (In the same way that your house is still quite
habitable if one of the bricks protrudes a bit...)
It would be an enhancement to make things more user friendly or more
efficient. As such, it would be possible to consider an alternative
component that had this feature in it, or, if no such component was
available, it would be possibe to contact the vendor and have it enhanced.

Most component builders (in a small way, and for my own purposes, I am
one...and I have dealt with a number over the last 2 years) are VERY keen to
improve the particular component(s) they are offering. Feedback from the
real world is a great way to do this. Typically, the turnaround on a
requested enhancement is days rather than weeks.

Balance this against the cost of maintaining a multi-lingual IT department
and it is a no brainer to see which is more cost-effective....

>>(Yes, I know this is hard to take when looked at in today's environment,
but
>>I was not talking about today's environment. You need to see the full
>>context of my argument and then you can see that it is just a logical
>>extrapolation from trends which are already emerging. Hint: Think in terms
>>of modular hardware technology in electronics. No one fixes "bad" boards
any
>>more; just bin the broken one and replace it.)
>
>modular hardware isn't.(modular)
>
OK. But it is still common practice to replace boards that fail, rather than
actually fix them. And very often, the new board may have even more
functionality than the old one (Think of Sound cards f'rinstance...)

>>Only specialist shops will be in the business of producing components as
it
>>will not be economically viable for most Enterprises to do it on a regular
>>basis. They can outsource this stuff for a fraction of what it costs to
>>produce in house.
>
>all you are talking about is retirement without rehiring programmers.

I don't think so, but that is certainly one possible scenario as to how an
Enterprise could scale down their existing IT department and achieve a cost
benefit.
>
>>The job of "COBOL Programmer" as we understand it today will change so
>>radically it will not be recognisable, and I believe this will happen
within
>>the next 7 - 10 years. However, I have suggested alternative roles which
>>today's programmers could ideally fill if the suggested scenario
eventuates.
>
>yea, i'll agree that programmers will change. they will go from
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That will happen in the near future. What I'm describing is a little further
down the track, when the system itself will provide this, totally
transparently to the User.

By an iterative interaction with some fairly bright software,  non-technical
Users will be able to get exactly what they want from their computer
systems.

It will happen because 300,000,000 computer users are not about to become
"programmers" in the sense that we understand the word. The old days when
they could be held to ransom by the IT department are gone.

The marketplace required more effective, more user friendly, less technical
software and they are getting it. Look around at the number of your friends
who never trained as programmers, but are extremely adept at WordProcessing,
Spreadsheets, Web Page development, and so on.

This trend will continue to the point where Technical knowledge at the level
required in the past, will not be necessary. (Actually, it will still be
there, but Humans won't do it. Specialised diagnostic components and
computers will take on this role, with networked "Knowledge Bases" far
beyond anything a Human engineer could ever hope to have, even after a
lifetime of experience.)  At that stage we can say computing is truly in the
hands of the masses.

Personally, I'm glad to see it. It shows we did our job...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Standards vs "the Standard" vs "draft Standard" vs "Standards process" (was: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-13T20:41:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dnd6e$jt5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3afb643c_2@Usenet.com> <krcpftcurb4pdou1f0057mu850rrafmqb4@4ax.com> <3afd3562_6@Usenet.com> <ctkrft8jstkuf2q64ak6qnen1g0l02upgi@4ax.com> <3aff33b7_1@Usenet.com>`

```
I just thought that I would "re-start" discussion of some of the parts of
the previous thread - indicating that I think some posters are speaking at
"cross purposes" (i.e. not NECESSARILY comparing apples to oranges).

It seems to me that there are a "variety" of different (but DEFINITELY
related) issues:

1) What can/should happen with the current draft Standard (FCD)?

2) What will happen with the COBOL language when (if?) this draft Standard
becomes approved (i.e. will vendors implement all or part of it - will
programmers use the "new" features that the implementors provide them)

3) What is the "best" way to keep the COBOL language "vibrant" (viable?) in
the future (what Standard?  what features?  what programming paradigms, etc)

4) Has there been a problem getting to "where we are" - and if so, is it
being addressed for the future?  (If not, that is "fine" too - but not
necessarily the common perception).

5) What will be the "general" data processing (information system, computer
programming, whatever) "paradigm" in the future (regardless of COBOL) - will
it differ in "mainframe" environments from "workstation environments"? Will
some languages (notably COBOL) be relegated to "maintenance of legacy
systems" while other languages (systems? facilities?) provide the "tools" of
the future?

  ***

I have some FAIRLY strong opinions on each of these - and do see that some
people have already commented on "much" of this.  However, I do think that
it helps "discussion" if we all know what each person it "talking about" and
not assume that
 - the person who is talking about which structures will be used in
maintaining a legacy mainframe COBOL business application
 - should get an answer talking about how to do new development in a
"real-time" networked environment (where there is little or no existing user
expectation)
```
