###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards vs "the Standard" vs "draft Standard" vs "Standards process" (was: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T11:23:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dp7qn$mjb$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3afb643c_2@Usenet.com> <krcpftcurb4pdou1f0057mu850rrafmqb4@4ax.com> <3afd3562_6@Usenet.com> <ctkrft8jstkuf2q64ak6qnen1g0l02upgi@4ax.com> <3aff33b7_1@Usenet.com> <9dnd6e$jt5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <josephk-52D4F0.20333014052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```

Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:josephk-52D4F0.20333014052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au...

in response to Bill Klein's questions (marked >>):

> > 1) What can/should happen with the current draft Standard (FCD)?

> I'm not sure that it matters anymore.

It may well not.  But if the FCD/DIS/IS process is derailed, we'll never be
able to answer that question.  I'm willing to do what I can to see the
current process through to the logical conclusion and would much rather
base my judgments on what actually takes place at that point.


> > 2) What will happen with the COBOL language when (if?) this draft
Standard
> > becomes approved (i.e. will vendors implement all or part of it - will
> > programmers use the "new" features that the implementors provide them)

> Likewise, I don't think that companies care whether the IT department
> uses ratified languages or waves Merlins wand - they just want the
> results.

True, but they also want some degree of portability, and the FCD provides a
heck of a lot more of it than any of its predecessors did.

> > 3) What is the "best" way to keep the COBOL language "vibrant" (viable?)
in
> > the future (what Standard?  what features?  what programming paradigms,
etc)

> I've voiced my opinion that Open Source Complilers are the only way to
> get the necessary feedback loop close enough to the end users of the
> product - the programmers.

I can see your point, but I am far from convinced that Big Business (or Big
Government, for that matter) is going to be all that thrilled about
investing Many Bucks in software that is dependent on a dynamically-evolving
set of programming conventions!   Stability is not a bad thing ...

> > 4) Has there been a problem getting to "where we are" - and if so, is it
> > being addressed for the future?  (If not, that is "fine" too - but not
> > necessarily the common perception).

> Of course there has been a problem. But I think that it has gone way
> beyond repair.

I'm not convinced that it's beyond repair, but I am convinced the only way
we're going to find out whether the standard survives as a standard is to
continue the current process as expeditiously as possible.

>                     I think tat the standards bodies will be entirely
> bypassed for program language development within 4 to 5 years as Open
> Source Compilers come on stream. Likewise with current compiler
> companies, and in my opinion about time. I have always though that
> programming languages should be free to all.

That may well happen, and I've said that before.  But unless and until both
Open Source Compilers and ISO/IEC Standard Compilers are competing on a
level playing field, we'll never know.

For reasons I've already stated, I understand that there's a part of the
COBOL user community that wants to be able to invent their own syntax and
semantics for a new functionality and make it available to the world, but I
remain unconvinced that businesses are going to be eager to use that feature
until some significant consensus has been established in the international
COBOL community that the feature is of significant and general value.

Perhaps I'm misled as to what "open source" entails.  But I have seen some
features added to COBOL compilers (by users themselves, by marketing groups
catering to a body of users, or even by the implementors under duress from
marketing) just because user programmers wanted them,  that turned out to be
not only non-portable but conceptually flawed and incompatible with better,
more carefully thought out, and well-negotiated designs accomplishing the
same task but produced by international agreement.

Incidentally, for many years the Burroughs Large System (now Unisys MCP/AS,
previously A Series) COBOL(68) and COBOL74 compilers were provided as part
of the system software, and all source files were available at no cost to
the user.  This practice was discontinued at least a decade ago, but the
source files for the COBOL74 compiler are still available for a charge, and
some users actually do customize their compilers.

> > 5) What will be the "general" data processing (information system,
computer
> > programming, whatever) "paradigm" in the future (regardless of COBOL) -
will
> > it differ in "mainframe" environments from "workstation environments"?
Will
> > some languages (notably COBOL) be relegated to "maintenance of legacy
> > systems" while other languages (systems? facilities?) provide the
"tools" of
> > the future?
>
> See my earlier comments.
>

I think the jury is out on COBOL.  But I also feel quite strongly that if
the FCD is rejected, or derailed, the jury will come in quick, and it won't
be pretty.   COBOL got a lot of the blame in much of Big Business for the
Y2K fiasco, and the costs associated with that have led many managers to vow
that as soon as the economy revives they'll drop their legacy COBOL code
like a hot rock, and never, ever use COBOL again.

We can focus our energies toward publicizing how broken the COBOL process is
and the best way to fix it is to allow every COBOL user to do his/her own
thing as far as COBOL syntax goes.

Or we can focus our energies on publicizing the great new features the New
Standard COBOL has in it, including how its enhanced portability features
help protect a businessman's investment.

Which do you think will stand a better chance of convincing a businessman to
invest his money in further COBOL development?

I believe the survival of COBOL as a useful language in the business
community is dependent on the reaffirmation of the current standard and on
the expeditious adoption of the FCD as the new one.  I also believe, along
with a number of the commentors in favor of the reaffirmation of the current
standard, that the absence of an internationally-negotiated, formal COBOL
standard would be fundamentally harmful to more businesses than the
existence of such a standard would be.

        -Chuck Stevens
```

of such a standard would be.>>

existence of such a standard would be.>>

    -Chuck Stevens

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message

If it doesn't matter, then it doesn't matter, so whether we can answer the
question or not is irrelevant. You keep talking about the standard being
"derailed" by public criticism, but it was derailed in the first place by
your inablility to get it out in a time frame which was meaningful and
pertinent. Small wonder it has been "overtaken by events".

> > > 2) What will happen with the COBOL language when (if?) this draft
> Standard
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> True, but they also want some degree of portability, and the FCD provides
a
> heck of a lot more of it than any of its predecessors did.
>

They are much more concerned with running their Mission Critical systems
than they are with portability. I have always been a strong proponent of
portability, but I realise the World has changed and although it is still
desirable, it is by no means the TOP priority for most installations.

I agree with Joe's comments above.

That the FCD provides it is good, but it is not ESSENTIAL.


> > > 3) What is the "best" way to keep the COBOL language "vibrant"
(viable?)
> in
> > > the future (what Standard?  what features?  what programming
paradigms,
> etc)
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I can see your point, but I am far from convinced that Big Business (or
Big
> Government, for that matter) is going to be all that thrilled about
> investing Many Bucks in software that is dependent on a
dynamically-evolving
> set of programming conventions!   Stability is not a bad thing ...
>
"Dynamically evolving" is what the whole industry has been doing for the
last 40 years!  Part of the problem COBOL is experiencing is caused by the
fact that certain people want "stability" based on a standard. This would be
fine if the standard could be refined, reviewed, and was dynamically
responsive within a reasonable timeframe. Events have shown that it isn't,
so we have two choices:

1. Fix it. (Sadly, as I have remarked previously, there is NO evidence of
that happening. There isn't even acceptance of culpability, which is the
first step towards fixing anything. (Accept that it needs fixing...)).

2. Drop it altogether and just get on with it. Despite protestations from
vested interests like yourself, Chuck, this is really not so bad... I
believe that "Big Business" and "Government" will vote with their feet. If
it makes sense to use COBOL they will use it. And they will not be unaware
of the existing investment they already have. Why would they throw that away
just because an ANSI standard wasn't ratified? It doesn't make sound
business sense and I don't believe it will happen.

> > > 4) Has there been a problem getting to "where we are" - and if so, is
it
> > > being addressed for the future?  (If not, that is "fine" too - but not
> > > necessarily the common perception).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> continue the current process as expeditiously as possible.

Again I agree with Joe. It HAS gone beyond repair if nobody comes out
officially and accepts responsibility for it. Why should we support
"continuing the current process as expeditiously as possible" when it is
patently flawed and there is no intention to fix it? Why should we even care
"whether the standard survives as a standard" ? There is no consideration of
the needs or wants of the COBOL Community here, it is just the fact that
ANSI (a profit making organisation) needs to get it out to avoid some
embarrassment and make some cash out of it.
>
> >                     I think tat the standards bodies will be entirely
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> That may well happen, and I've said that before.  But unless and until
both
> Open Source Compilers and ISO/IEC Standard Compilers are competing on a
> level playing field, we'll never know.

OK, Chuck, on this one I agree with you. Personally, I have never required
or expected something for nothing and I accept that the vendors are driven
by the Marketplace because that's where the profit is. Open Source is great
and I wish them well, but the existing vendors have a huge investment in
COBOL and they (for the most part) DESERVE to get a return on it.

>
> For reasons I've already stated, I understand that there's a part of the
> COBOL user community that wants to be able to invent their own syntax and
> semantics for a new functionality and make it available to the world, but
I
> remain unconvinced that businesses are going to be eager to use that
feature
> until some significant consensus has been established in the international
> COBOL community that the feature is of significant and general value.
>
Why not? If you can use a feature you can use a feature. Why do you need
endorsement from some dubiously motivated, ineffective ,Standards body you
tell you what is a Good Thing?


> Perhaps I'm misled as to what "open source" entails.  But I have seen some
> features added to COBOL compilers (by users themselves, by marketing
groups
> catering to a body of users, or even by the implementors under duress from
> marketing) just because user programmers wanted them,  that turned out to
be
> not only non-portable but conceptually flawed and incompatible with
better,
> more carefully thought out, and well-negotiated designs accomplishing the
> same task but produced by international agreement.
>

Oh, and we've NEVER had a standard that was rubbish?!

> Incidentally, for many years the Burroughs Large System (now Unisys
MCP/AS,
> previously A Series) COBOL(68) and COBOL74 compilers were provided as part
> of the system software, and all source files were available at no cost to
> the user.  This practice was discontinued at least a decade ago, but the
> source files for the COBOL74 compiler are still available for a charge,
and
> some users actually do customize their compilers.
>
Good on 'em, if that's what they want to do. Just reinforces Joe's argument
regarding  priority for users.

> > > 5) What will be the "general" data processing (information system,
> computer
> > > programming, whatever) "paradigm" in the future (regardless of
COBOL) -
> will
> > > it differ in "mainframe" environments from "workstation environments"?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> the FCD is rejected, or derailed, the jury will come in quick, and it
won't
> be pretty.   COBOL got a lot of the blame in much of Big Business for the
> Y2K fiasco, and the costs associated with that have led many managers to
vow
> that as soon as the economy revives they'll drop their legacy COBOL code
> like a hot rock, and never, ever use COBOL again.
>
Well, there are shortsighted Managers just as there are astute ones. To
throw something away in a fit of pique is not recommended business practice.
The FACT is that the Millennium crisis was survived due to the efforts of
COBOL programmers all around the World, so there was no problem. Besides,
the whole Y2K thing had a lot to do with the media and once funding was
allocated there were many projects which sneaked through as "Y2K fix". So
many Managers know it did them no harm at all...and they were heroes by
January second...

> We can focus our energies toward publicizing how broken the COBOL process
is
> and the best way to fix it is to allow every COBOL user to do his/her own
> thing as far as COBOL syntax goes.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Or we could just stand up and say we want accountability from ANSI. Post to
public forums and Computer Trade press, and make the point that losing the
ANSI standard is NOT the end of COBOL; it could be the making of it.

It isn't COBOL that is broken.

It's pretty obvious which of the above options I embrace. And I'll continue
to do so until the points I mentioned previously are acknowledged.

In fact, Chuck, you have no right to be soliciting support here UNTIL there
is a public acknkowledgement by ANSI that they got it wrong and are prepared
to fix it (and we see some meaningful evidence that they will). If that was
to happen I would be prepared to support the standard.


> Which do you think will stand a better chance of convincing a businessman
to
> invest his money in further COBOL development?
>

The needs of his Business and only that. The politics of Computer technology
are irrelevant.

> I believe the survival of COBOL as a useful language in the business
> community is dependent on the reaffirmation of the current standard and on
> the expeditious adoption of the FCD as the new one.  I also believe, along
> with a number of the commentors in favor of the reaffirmation of the
current
> standard, that the absence of an internationally-negotiated, formal COBOL
> standard would be fundamentally harmful to more businesses than the
> existence of such a standard would be.
>
 I disagree.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards vs "the Standard" (LONG)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T15:53:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dsc0q$hp1$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3afb643c_2@Usenet.com> <krcpftcurb4pdou1f0057mu850rrafmqb4@4ax.com> <3afd3562_6@Usenet.com> <ctkrft8jstkuf2q64ak6qnen1g0l02upgi@4ax.com> <3aff33b7_1@Usenet.com> <9dnd6e$jt5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <josephk-52D4F0.20333014052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <9dp7qn$mjb$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b007725_1@Usenet.com>`

```

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@free.net.nz> wrote in message
news:3b007725_1@Usenet.com, in response to my comments marked by >>:

> > It may well not [matter to the future of COBOL whether the FCD is
adopted].
> > But if the FCD/DIS/IS process is derailed, we'll never be
> > able to answer that question.  I'm willing to do what I can to see the
> > current process through to the logical conclusion and would much rather
> > base my judgments on what actually takes place at that point.

> If it doesn't matter, then it doesn't matter, so whether we can answer the
> question or not is irrelevant.

No, Mr. Dashwood, that's not what I said, nor is it what I meant to
indicate.    We can *only* answer the question as to whether the standard
has had a direct impact on COBOL when that standard exists.   The survival
of COBOL *does* matter.  I believe the adoption of the FCD as a standard
*assists* in the survival of COBOL, and *enhances* its value to the user
base.  *Whether* that adoption ultimately saves COBOL remains to be seen,
but I believe strongly that the chances of its survival as a long-term
strategic tool for businesses will be seriously compromised if it the
adoption of the FCD does not proceed apace.  It *might* not matter, and if
it *would have* mattered, I believe it would be a great loss

>>You keep talking about the standard being "derailed" by public criticism,

It is not criticism of the standard that I am concerned about, it is demands
for an *immediate* change in the process that concerns me.  You have no
doubt already noted that there are about a dozen people actively working to
put the finishing touches (and finishing suggestions) on the FCD.   If that
process is allowed to continue it is likely that a standard will be adopted
by ISO/IEC and promulgated next year.   I do not believe that interfering
with that process will result in a standard of like quality in anything like
the same time frame.

Furthermore, I believe strongly that standards formed by formal
international consensus are a Good Thing.  It is not clear that forming a
parallel organization to ISO/IEC, and the equivalent national bodies, is
either necessary or appropriate for the sole purpose of coming up with a
standard for COBOL, and I do not believe such a set of organizations could
be "up and running" in a time frame that would have them producing effective
intermediate results, much less complete a standard, within the next five
years.

I believe STOPPING the current process and ESTABLISHING A NEW process will
derail the CURRENT process, and have the effect of derailing the adoption of
the International Standard, which I believe should be adopted.

I believe constant haranguing of those who are striving to complete the FCD
and guide it toward its adoption as an IS because of their perceived
deficiencies fifteen, ten, even five years ago in failing to get the vast
body of user-demanded features consolidated into a relatively cohesive
whole, will have as its most visible consequence the *further* delay of the
adoption of the standard, and thus is subject to the same complaint that is
being lodged against the process to begin with!   Demanding that the process
be changed is, *for the FCD*, part of the *problem*, not part of the
*solution*.

>>but it was derailed in the first place by your inablility to get it out in
a time
>>frame which was meaningful and pertinent.

I can only speak authoritatively for myself, and *my* record is open on this
subject.  I have worked very hard in my tenure on J4 to ensure that the FCD
was moved forward as expeditiously as possible, and I believe my
contributions have been significant.   What grounds have you for publically
assailing my competence as a member of J4?

Actions *have* been explicitly and directly taken in an effort to derail the
reaffirmation of the *current* standard, and I believe actions are being
explicitly taken to prevent the adoption of the FCD.   I am working
strenuously to do as much as I can to ensure that the current standard is
reaffirmed and the FCD adopted.    So are almost all of the other members of
J4.  Given significant evidence of direct actions taken to interfere with
both of these processes, I am personally more inclined to consider those
taking such direct actions as being responsible for the delays than I am
those who have done what they can to undermine decades of work on the part
of dedicated and talented people.

>>Small wonder it has been "overtaken by events".

Nearly half of the April J4 meeting was spent drafting responses to the
Public Comments on reaffirmation of the existing standard.  I believe the J4
members are virtually unanimous in their opinion that this time would have
been more productively spent responding to Public Comments on the FCD.  In
the history of X3 and NCITS, so far as I know, the reaffirmation of an
existing standard for COBOL has *never* been regarded as controversial, has
*never* precipitated any public comments.   I also believe that the author
of the first public comment ever (to my knowledge) submitted in opposition
to the reaffirmation of a COBOL standard bears the sole and total moral and
financial responsibility for all of the past, current and future
consequences of his actions.   The events by which the process were
"overtaken" were indeed explicit and acknowledged actions on the part of one
person.   Whether there were "sins of omission" on the part of ANSI, NCITS,
WG4 or J4, they were process-related and not intentional.  Again, If the
question is what contributed more to a slowdown or a derailing of a given
process, I am inclined to give more "weight" to those actions that had the
specific purpose of interfering with the process than I am the honest
mistakes of those who were doing their best to keep it moving.

>They are much more concerned with running their Mission Critical systems
than they are with portability. > I have always been a strong proponent of
portability, but I realise the World has changed and although it > is still
desirable, it is by no means the TOP priority for most installations

I am not convinced that the portability of applications has *decreased* in
importance.  I furthermore believe that the fact that so many features of
the FCD are specifically intended to *enhance* portability, and those
features are enthusiastically supported by *implementors*,  is an indication
that that factor is important to the *customers* of the compiler vendors.

> That the FCD provides [portability] is good, but it is not ESSENTIAL.

NO feature is essential.  Heck, COBOL is not essential.  COBOL *programmers*
aren't essential.  Computers aren't essential.  It's either *valuable* or
it's *not valuable*.  I think enhanced portability is an enhancement in
value.

> > I can see your point, but I am far from convinced that Big Business (or
> > Big Government, for that matter) is going to be all that thrilled about
> > investing Many Bucks in software that is dependent on a
dynamically-evolving
> > set of programming conventions!   Stability is not a bad thing ...

> "Dynamically evolving" is what the whole industry has been doing for the
> last 40 years!  Part of the problem COBOL is experiencing is caused by the
> fact that certain people want "stability" based on a standard. This would
be
> fine if the standard could be refined, reviewed, and was dynamically
> responsive within a reasonable timeframe. Events have shown that it isn't,
> so we have two choices:

I believe it is *equally* valid to argue that the demands from the *user
base* for what the new standard must absolutely without question contain
contributed significantly to the long gestation period.  Given what was
demanded of J4 in this process (and given the direct interference they have
encountered!) I believe they've done an outstanding job.

> 1. Fix it. (Sadly, as I have remarked previously, there is NO evidence of
> that happening. There isn't even acceptance of culpability, which is the
> first step towards fixing anything. (Accept that it needs fixing...)).

I am doing all that I can to "fix it", but I am of the firm belief that the
time to fix it is *after everyone pulls together to ensure that the FCD is
adopted*.

You seem to have two complaints:  (1) that the nearly-complete FCD took too
long to produce; and (2) that those responsible for producing it
demonstrated their incompetence by taking too long to produce it.

I can devote my energies toward the completion of the process.   Or I can
devote my energies toward the reinvention of it.  My opinion is that my
efforts are better applied toward the former than toward the latter.

It may well need fixing.  So might lots of things.  J4 is in the final
stages of "fine-tuning" the FCD, in the process of turning it into a
standard.   Those who are working hard to ensure that this draft is as
well-designed as possible in as short a time as possible should not be
expected to divert their attention to examining whether the design methods
and the approval process used in the process (to continue an analogy hinted
at) of constructing the prototype's engine block were sufficiently
economical of raw materials or whether there's a way to get the block out of
the machine shop more quickly in future.  Or to exploring whether the frame
and engine would be better constructed if a new factory were built to
construct them!   There are times for such explorations, but now is not that
time.

> 2. Drop it altogether and just get on with it. Despite protestations from
> vested interests like yourself, Chuck, this is really not so bad... I
> believe that "Big Business" and "Government" will vote with their feet.

Among my contacts are highly-placed Human Resources professionals for a
major automotive manufacturer and its adjunct companies, and these folks are
active in management organizations that communicate with other significant
businesses.

These folks have commented to me that many of the managers who have control
of LARGE IS shops with LARGE checkbooks  feel that the COBOL community truly
gouged them for the Y2K effort.  The managers that have the say-so on these
issues are going to be directing the abandonment of legacy applications
written in COBOL, to prevent such heavy dependence on that language in the
future, and also directing that the language be avoided for new development.
The only thing that is stopping these folks from replacing their COBOL
applications today is the current economic downturn, and the minute the
economy shows signs of significant improvement, they expect to see COBOL
applications being replaced as rapidly as possible by "more modern"
languages, and the HR folks are hiring, and setting their hiring procedures
and expectations, accordingly.

They are indeed about to vote with their feet, and there's a strong
likelihood they're going to walk as rapidly away from COBOL as they can.

I think we stand a *chance* of improving this perception of COBOL by
adopting the FCD as an International Standard expeditiously.  But I believe
strongly that if a subset of the community derails the *current* standard,
and a subset of the community prevents or delays the adoption of the FCD as
the *new* standard, we will have lost  our chance, and the use of COBOL will
begin an inexorable decline to the point where it is as relevant to most
businesses as ALTAC or IBM 7080 Autocoder.

> If it makes sense to use COBOL they will use it. And they will not be
unaware
> of the existing investment they already have. Why would they throw that
away
> just because an ANSI standard wasn't ratified? It doesn't make sound
> business sense and I don't believe it will happen.

I believe many businesses have already made that decision, and have decided
that whether COBOL makes sense or not they won't put their businesses in the
position that they found themselves in with Y2K, ever again.  Their reasons
for throwing COBOL away don't have much to do with the acceptance or
rejection of the standard, but the COBOL community might be able to use the
acceptance of the new standard as an argument to influence them to
reconsider their decision based on new information.    I believe the erosion
has in large part begun, and if it is to be stemmed, it's up to the COBOL
community to herald the improvements in feature content that the proposed
International Standard represents, to help improve COBOL's reputation within
management.

> > I'm not convinced that it's beyond repair, but I am convinced the only
way
> > we're going to find out whether the standard survives as a standard is
to
> > continue the current process as expeditiously as possible.

> Again I agree with Joe. It HAS gone beyond repair if nobody comes out
> officially and accepts responsibility for it.

The folks who would be responsible for trying to fix it are also the folks
who are trying to *complete* the existing process.   Energy devoted to one
is energy depleted from the other.

I am not in a position to "accept responsibility" for a process defined in
procedures for J4, for NCITS (and its predecessor X3), for ANSI, for
ISO/IEC, for WG4.  It is public knowledge that I have been actively
participating in the standards process for a grand total of just under six
months now.   I simply am not in a position to address what happened in J4
in 1986, 1990, 1993, 1995, or anytime before December 2000 when I attended
my first meeting of that committee.

I cannot accept responsibility for any actions taken by J4 or J4-TAG that
occurred before I became a member of the group.   Other than my tireless
efforts to get the FCD into a form suitable for adoption as an International
Standard during the course of my entire tenure in the J4 committee, what
precisely is it that I should accept responsibility for?

Others have taken direct and explicit actions with the goal of preventing
the reaffirmation of the existing standard, and I believe others are taking
action to prevent or discourage the acceptance of the FCD as a new standard.
If the FCD (or for that matter the reaffirmation of the '85 standard and its
amendments) are under threat of destruction by the direct actions of their
opponents, and have been delayed and interfered with during the course of
many years, it is my considered opinion that those who have fought *against*
progress in these arenas bear at least some responsibility for having
delayed the process!

>  Why should we support "continuing the current process as expeditiously as
possible" when it is
> patently flawed and there is no intention to fix it?

Because there is no guarantee that trying to "fix it" will accomplish
anything except further delays in a standard that you yourself have
indicated has many worthwhile features.   I have no objection to "fixing it"
when I am done trying to resolve exactly that issue that leads to your
complaint that the process needs "fixing"!

Moreover, I'm not in charge of the process, but several government standards
bodies are.  Given for example DIN's paper on the economic benefits of
formal international standardization (to which the Austrian and Swiss
standards bodies contributed), I am not convinced that a "de facto" standard
(as contrasted with a fundamentally "de jure" one) will be viewed kindly by
those bodies.

> Why should we even care "whether the standard survives as a standard" ?

Asked and answered, repeatedly, here and in other threads.  What is "sauce"
to the S/390 programmer is not necessarily sauce to the LINUX programmer.  I
believe the standardization of the language provides a valuable basis for
both, and I believe the FCD provides *significant* improvements in features
valuable in the full range of environments.

> There is no consideration of the needs or wants of the COBOL Community
here,

Wrong.  The HR professionals I referred to above have indicated to me that
COBOL programmers are regarded in general as dinosaurs, as much as IBM 7080
Autocoder or Philco 2000 ALTAC programmers were in 1970.   These are the
folks that are reading our resumes.  And I believe the fragmentation that
will result if the current standard is not reaffirmed, and/or the progress
from FCD to IS is delayed or entirely abandoned will hasten the flight of
businesses from COBOL-based applications.

Your accusation/characterization is simply false as it applies to me; I am
not only concerned for the very survival of the COBOL community, I am
alarmed at the distinct possibility of its ultimate demise, and my stated
positions are based on that concern.

>it is just the fact that ANSI (a profit making organisation) needs to get
it out to avoid some
> embarrassment and make some cash out of it.

Really?   "ANSI is a private, non-profit organization (501(c)3) that
administers and coordinates the U. S. voluntary standardization and
conformity assessment system." reads their web site at
http://www.ansi.org/public/about.html .  Are you aware of some aspect of the
appropriate non-profit-organization laws that they are violating?  Are you
certain of their profit motives, or are you simply speculating as to what
their motives might be.

While you may not like ANSI, your characterization borders on slanderous
because it claims that they are violating the law.

And as to ANSI's relationship to and responsibility for the FCD, please see
later in this post.

> > That may well happen, and I've said that before.  But unless and until
both
> > Open Source Compilers and ISO/IEC Standard Compilers are competing on a
> > level playing field, we'll never know.

> OK, Chuck, on this one I agree with you. Personally, I have never required
> or expected something for nothing and I accept that the vendors are driven
> by the Marketplace because that's where the profit is. Open Source is
great
> and I wish them well, but the existing vendors have a huge investment in
> COBOL and they (for the most part) DESERVE to get a return on it.

My concerns about the marketplace are primarily that the *standards* process
imposes a degree of portability that the *individual compiler implementors*
might not otherwise provide.  The standards provide both a foundation and a
Sword of Damocles to all implementors, and I truly value both roles.

I am truly more concerned for the ultimate users of COBOL compilers (of all
flavors) than I am for the individual vendors as a group.  I would far
rather see any compiler implementor implement a standards-conforming feature
than an implementor-specific one, and that includes me.

> Why not? If you can use a feature you can use a feature. Why do you need
> endorsement from some dubiously motivated, ineffective ,Standards body you
> tell you what is a Good Thing?

Precisely because it's in the standard.  If I have a compiler on machine <x>
that conforms to the standard, and I use a feature of standard cobol in that
program, I can reasonably expect to use that feature when I decide that
machine <y> better suits my needs.  That helps protect my investment.   A
feature that is super-useful on a S/390 may be unavailable to the user of a
PC (whether it would be useful there or not), and vice versa.   I have
already mentioned many times that I have seen compilers built without regard
to any COBOL standard, and migration ain't a pretty sight.

> Oh, and we've NEVER had a standard that was rubbish?!

I don't know of any standard, COBOL or otherwise, that I'd dismiss so
totally as "rubbish".  I certainly don't think the FCD falls into that
category; in fact, I think it's a vast improvement over the '85 standard.
What standard do you have in mind that you would characterize as "rubbish"?

> Good on 'em, if that's what they want to do. Just reinforces Joe's
argument
> regarding  priority for users.

Well, it certainly causes a problem when they attempt to migrate to
"released" compilers, or even to migrate to other COBOL dialects or to other
vendors.   I'm not sure encouraging them to produce "site-specific"
enhancements to their COBOL compilers is ultimately in their best interests.

> Well, there are shortsighted Managers just as there are astute ones. To
> throw something away in a fit of pique is not recommended business
practice.
> The FACT is that the Millennium crisis was survived due to the efforts of
> COBOL programmers all around the World, so there was no problem.

To replace equipment -- in this case, programs -- that just cost the company
a TON of money to maintain, on the other hand, is sound business practice.

The FACT that those "shortsighted managers" had to shell out Big Bucks to
deal with the Millennium crisis by hiring contract COBOL programmers, who in
turn in many cases gouged the heck out of them, did not help those managers
understand the fundamental altruism of those contractors, or to view the
company's continued reliance on their costly expertise as in the company's
best interest!

> Besides the whole Y2K thing had a lot to do with the media and once
funding was
> allocated there were many projects which sneaked through as "Y2K fix". So
> many Managers know it did them no harm at all...and they were heroes by
> January second...

Some, yes.  Some, no.  I know of some Y2K issues that actually surfaced just
as the major effort was ramping up at one large company ...  I also know of
one very profitable company that "sold itself" to another so that their
customers would be incorporated into the latter company's software,
precisely because their best in-house estimates for correcting the Y2K issue
in their own programs indicated that their entire net income for the
following seven years would be consumed by the process of revising their
applications ...

> Or we could just stand up and say we want accountability from ANSI. Post
to
> public forums and Computer Trade press, and make the point that losing the
> ANSI standard is NOT the end of COBOL; it could be the making of it.

Why ANSI?  The FCD is being produced by J4, a committee of NCITS, which
coordinates with ISO/IEC.  While the '85 standard was produced first by ANSI
and then adopted by ISO/IEC, the FCD is produced by J4 *at the request of
WG4 of ISO/IEC JT1*, as I understand it, and is subject *first* to approval
by ISO/IEC and only *secondarily* to ANSI.   The FCD is copyrighted by
ISO/IEC, not ANSI.

You may want ANSI to be accountable for the drawn-out development of the
FCD, but it's not clear *to me* that ANSI *is* accountable for it, any more
than DIN or AFNOR or JISC is.  ISO/IEC JTC1/SC22/WG4 delegates the technical
*development* of the standard to NCITS/J4, but ISO/IEC retains ultimate
authority over its technical *content*, as I understand it, and retains
copyright.

I don't see ANSI involved in this process at all.  Have you contacted your
national body in ISO/IEC to express your displeasure?  Perhaps you've been
barking up the wrong tree all this time.

> It isn't COBOL that is broken.

If COBOL is not broken, why risk its further fragmentation into a
proliferation of vendor-specific dialects?

> It's pretty obvious which of the above options I embrace. And I'll
continue
> to do so until the points I mentioned previously are acknowledged.

I don't know what it is that ANSI is supposed to acknowledge.  Again, have
you contacted your member body in ISO/IEC to register your complaint?

> In fact, Chuck, you have no right to be soliciting support here UNTIL
there
> is a public acknkowledgement by ANSI that they got it wrong and are
prepared
> to fix it (and we see some meaningful evidence that they will).

I have no right?   I am really surprised to learn that my right to speak on
behalf of those who support the adoption of the FCD as a standard is somehow
eliminated by ANSI's action, and I wasn't aware that my right to do so was
limited in some way by CLC's charter.  Could you explain the logic that
leads you to this conclusion?  This is beginning to look suspiciously like a
flame, and I believe it's unwarranted.

> If that was  to happen I would be prepared to support the standard.

Doesn't look to me like you're going to support the standard even if the
responsible committees acknowledge their fundamental incompetence and change
their processes ...

> > Which do you think will stand a better chance of convincing a
businessman to
> > invest his money in further COBOL development?

> The needs of his Business and only that. The politics of Computer
technology
> are irrelevant.

The response does not address the impact of either of my proposed scenarios
on "the needs of business", and thus it begs the question, particularly
since I've already established that "the politics of Computer technology"
are today determining what IS managers and CFO's are willing to spend money
on and what they are not!

> > I believe the survival of COBOL as a useful language in the business
> > community is dependent on the reaffirmation of the current standard and
on
> > the expeditious adoption of the FCD as the new one.  I also believe,
along
> > with a number of the commentors in favor of the reaffirmation of the
> > current standard, that the absence of an internationally-negotiated,
formal COBOL
> > standard would be fundamentally harmful to more businesses than the
> > existence of such a standard would be.

>  I disagree.

Which of course you are free to do.   But I know of no corporate entity or
national body that has claimed they or their customers would be directly
harmed by the presence of a standard, and I know of several of the former
and at least one of the latter who have formally claimed that the absence of
a COBOL standard would be harmful to them and those they serve.

Their logic and their reasoning strikes me as sound.  Considering the number
of misattributions, erroneous assumptions, and flat misstatements of fact I
have noted in the premises contained in your post, I am not eager to accept
for my own the conclusions you have drawn from those premises.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards vs "the Standard" (LONG)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T20:16:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dskap$vcp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3afb643c_2@Usenet.com> <krcpftcurb4pdou1f0057mu850rrafmqb4@4ax.com> <3afd3562_6@Usenet.com> <ctkrft8jstkuf2q64ak6qnen1g0l02upgi@4ax.com> <3aff33b7_1@Usenet.com> <9dnd6e$jt5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <josephk-52D4F0.20333014052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <9dp7qn$mjb$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b007725_1@Usenet.com> <9dsc0q$hp1$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:9dsc0q$hp1$1@mail.pl.unisys.com...
>
 <snip>
> In
> the history of X3 and NCITS, so far as I know, the reaffirmation of an
> existing standard for COBOL has *never* been regarded as controversial,
has
> *never* precipitated any public comments.   I also believe that the author
> of the first public comment ever (to my knowledge) submitted in opposition
> to the reaffirmation of a COBOL standard bears the sole and total moral
and
> financial responsibility for all of the past, current and future
> consequences of his actions.

Chuck,
  This is an interesting example of "not learning from history" (and not
even bothering to look at it). You (and possibly others in J4) might want to
ACTUALLY look at the reaffirmation process of the current Standards (both in
1991 and 1998).  In *both* cases negative comments DID come in and did need
to be processed by J4 (or X3J4 - in those days).

There have been NEGATIVE reactions to
  - reaffirmation (every time)
 - corrections amendment
 - Work Project for the full revision

I *do* understand that you (Chuck) were not individually on the committees
in those days, but I think you need to "check your facts" before making
claims like this.  (You do say "as far as you know" - so you aren't in
error - but the IMPLICATION of your statement is simply WRONG - and - IMHO -
shows yet again why J4, not me, was responsible for the problems on the
reaffirmation.)

As far as the processing of the FCD goes, I think there is little doubt that
it will be progressed to approval by the end of 2002.  There is a "question"
about the copyright that might need resolution, but even that will not (as
far as I can tell/guess) impact the final approval.  HOWEVER, we (J4 - in
the US) still haven't seen what comments come in from other countries, so
anything is still possible.
```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:9dsc0q$hp1$1@mail.pl.unisys.com...
>

>The survival  of COBOL *does* matter.

That's not what we're arguing. That is axiomatic in a COBOL forum...

>I believe the adoption of the FCD as a standard
> *assists* in the survival of COBOL, and *enhances* its value to the user
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't think it necessarily does, and that's where most of the issue
arises.

> >>You keep talking about the standard being "derailed" by public
criticism,
>
> It is not criticism of the standard that I am concerned about, it is
demands
> for an *immediate* change in the process that concerns me.  You have no
> doubt already noted that there are about a dozen people actively working
to
> put the finishing touches (and finishing suggestions) on the FCD.   If
that
> process is allowed to continue it is likely that a standard will be
adopted
> by ISO/IEC and promulgated next year.   I do not believe that interfering
> with that process will result in a standard of like quality in anything
like
> the same time frame.
>
Well, suppose I accept what you say and I desist from urging change in the
process. When IS such change likely to occur? Who will implement it? How
will it be defined?

See, these are really unfair questions because, as you identify below, you
personally, and probably the other members of J4 also, have no control over
it. The process is locked into procedures laid down by the governing body
and there is (apparently) no mechanism to change this.

That's why I question the whole value of having such a standard at all.


> Furthermore, I believe strongly that standards formed by formal
> international consensus are a Good Thing.

They are certainly a Good Thing for the people who control them and
establish exclusive rights to them.

>It is not clear that forming a
> parallel organization to ISO/IEC, and the equivalent national bodies, is
> either necessary or appropriate for the sole purpose of coming up with a
> standard for COBOL,

Who suggested that? I had some private mail from Stephen where he seemed to
think that that was where I was coming from also. I see no point in
replacing one bad system with another (I've read "Animal Farm"...<G>).

I will gladly go on record here and now as saying I have no vested interest
in establishing another "Standards" body. I want to see the existing
procedures completely reviewed and reformed, or an Open Source style review
process as we see currently with Java.

There, no hidden agendas, I have stated what my goals are. Actually, I
reserve a third option; just give up on the whole thing and move on without
it. (This last is becoming daily more attractive and I think I may not be
the only one who feels this way...)

>and I do not believe such a set of organizations could
> be "up and running" in a time frame that would have them producing
effective
> intermediate results, much less complete a standard, within the next five
> years.

Well, that's speculation and it is irrelevent anyway, because that's not
what we're arguing about...
>
> I believe STOPPING the current process and ESTABLISHING A NEW process will
> derail the CURRENT process, and have the effect of derailing the adoption
of
> the International Standard, which I believe should be adopted.
>
OK. Let's not stop the current process.  Despite your "conspiracy theory"
outlined below, there never was one. I can't speak for others, but I have
made very clear what my personal issues are. They haven't been addressed, I
see no sign of them being addressed, and that's why I'm debating this here
with you.

>>>
> I believe constant haranguing of those who are striving to complete the
FCD
> and guide it toward its adoption as an IS because of their perceived
> deficiencies fifteen, ten, even five years ago in failing to get the vast
> body of user-demanded features consolidated into a relatively cohesive
> whole, will have as its most visible consequence the *further* delay of
the
> adoption of the standard, and thus is subject to the same complaint that
is
> being lodged against the process to begin with!   Demanding that the
process
> be changed is, *for the FCD*, part of the *problem*, not part of the
> *solution*.
>
<<<
Er, actually, it wasn't me who started the "haranguing"...it was certain
people seeking to assure us of what a wonderful thing the new FCD is and how
everybody sweated blood and we should all be grateful.

As for "demanding change", I'm sorry if that's a little inconvenient right
now...Maybe some forethought could have avoided the necessity for it?


> >>but it was derailed in the first place by your inablility to get it out
in
> a time
> >>frame which was meaningful and pertinent.
>
> I can only speak authoritatively for myself, and *my* record is open on
this
> subject.  I have worked very hard in my tenure on J4 to ensure that the
FCD
> was moved forward as expeditiously as possible, and I believe my
> contributions have been significant.   What grounds have you for
publically
> assailing my competence as a member of J4?
>
Well, I never intended this to be personal, I don't doubt a bunch of very
dedicated people have made a huge effort, but the fact remains, it didn't
succeed.

It's like Physics...you can apply all the force in the world to a large
boulder but until it moves, no work is done.

So where is the problem? You tell me... bright people, dedicated, and
industrious, after 10 years, no standard. What IS wrong? Is it the
procedures? Is it the Management? Is it loss of focus? Trying to take on too
much and failing to assign priorities and timeboxes? Can't release anything
until everything is perfect? All of the above?

Then it's time for a review and possibly some changes. But if we make
changes or even investigate the possibility right now, it will be
"de-railed"... So what WOULD you suggest? Of course, "just support it and
help us get it through..." but you WOULD say that!

>>>
> Actions *have* been explicitly and directly taken in an effort to derail
the
> reaffirmation of the *current* standard, and I believe actions are being
> explicitly taken to prevent the adoption of the FCD.   I am working
> strenuously to do as much as I can to ensure that the current standard is
> reaffirmed and the FCD adopted.    So are almost all of the other members
of
> J4.  Given significant evidence of direct actions taken to interfere with
> both of these processes, I am personally more inclined to consider those
> taking such direct actions as being responsible for the delays than I am
> those who have done what they can to undermine decades of work on the part
> of dedicated and talented people.
<<<
Just because you're paranoid, doesn't mean we're not out to get you...<G>

 If any group or individual IS actively seeking to de-rail the process have
you ever thought about finding out WHY anyone would want to do that?
Personally, I am so bitterly disappointed by it all (You might have gathered
that...<G>) that I really no longer care whether you get it out or you
don't; I certainly wouldn't waste my time trying to de-rail it.

My only hope was that there might be SOME chance of salvaging something from
the exercise and getting the procedures changed so it won't happen again. I
am coming to the conclusion that that is NEVER going to happen, so there is
nothing for me in continuing this debate. The only conclusion I can draw is
that the process is flawed, the people involved LIKE it that way, and,
thankfully, no one really gives a damn anyway.

Your standard is irrelevant no matter what you say or do to try and bolster
its "importance". It is irrelevant because it has no bearing on the life of
the average COBOL Programmer. The "shop floor" has no input or feedback to
it and the mechanisms which are supposed to encourage this are so badly
broken that no-one will attempt to use them. Out of an estimated 2.5 million
COBOL programmers on the planet, how many have actually "waded through the
molasses" to submit their opinions regarding the FCD itself or
re-affirmation or anything else connected with the bureaucratic monolith
that is ANSI?

<snipped loads on Chuck running scared because "Big Business" are moving
away from COBOL.>

<<<
Definitely speculating. No evidence at all. My speculation goes like this:

1. A "non-profit" Organisation seeks to appropriate with it's own Copyright,
something which has traditionally been Public Domain and was intended by the
people who set it up to BE Public Domain.

2. If they succeed, they can limit access to it, charge other people who
want to reproduce it or access it on-line (and currently, these are NOT just
"token fees to cover expenses"...oops! maybe I'm wrong on that...with their
internal procedures as totally ineffective as we have seen, it probably
costs them a fortune to do ANYTHING! OK, I withdraw my comments regarding
ANSI...<G>
>
>>>
> While you may not like ANSI, your characterization borders on slanderous
> because it claims that they are violating the law.
<<<
Doesn't "border on it"... it was slanderous (although I never claimed they
were breaking the law...other than moral law that is). I see now I was
wrong, they are in fact a "Non Profit" organization (maybe they didn't plan
it that way, but that's how it worked out...<G>) and I unreservedly withdraw
my suggestion otherwise.

<snipped loads of stuff on the "advantages" of having a standard, none of
which is really pertinent to the issues at hand (should it be an ANSI
standard or, specifically, THIS FCD)>


>>>
> The FACT that those "shortsighted managers" had to shell out Big Bucks to
> deal with the Millennium crisis by hiring contract COBOL programmers, who
in
> turn in many cases gouged the heck out of them, did not help those
managers
> understand the fundamental altruism of those contractors, or to view the
> company's continued reliance on their costly expertise as in the company's
> best interest!
>
<<<
You've had too many good lunches with your HR buddies...

It was a MARKETPLACE.

COBOL expertise was required (at least everybody was led to believe that the
World would stop if it wasn't forthcoming). The Laws of Supply and Demand
regulate a Marketplace. If these Managers were stupid enough to leave Y2K
until November 1999, then there is little point in them complaining that the
rates for contractors went through the roof. It wasn't "gouging", it was a
penalty they incurred for their own short-sightedness and meanness.
(everybody knew when the Millennium was coming...they had 1000 years to
prepare for it.)

I confess to a bias here as I work as a contractor, and I managed several
Y2K connected projects. The people who employed me are very happy to do so
again and I don't think they feel they were "gouged". I always try and give
value for money, but, yes, I did charge more on one of those projects.
(mainly because it is a Marketplace and I could've sold the same skill for
more money elsewhere. There was no malice in it, just simple business. The
people concerned nurse no resentment.)

>>>
> > Besides the whole Y2K thing had a lot to do with the media and once
> funding was
> > allocated there were many projects which sneaked through as "Y2K fix".
So
> > many Managers know it did them no harm at all...and they were heroes by
> > January second...
>
> Some, yes.  Some, no.  I know of some Y2K issues that actually surfaced
just
> as the major effort was ramping up at one large company ...  I also know
of
> one very profitable company that "sold itself" to another so that their
> customers would be incorporated into the latter company's software,
> precisely because their best in-house estimates for correcting the Y2K
issue
> in their own programs indicated that their entire net income for the
> following seven years would be consumed by the process of revising their
> applications ...
<<<
So...? The fact is the World didn't stop and, despite a lot of media
generated antipathy towards COBOL and COBOL people, we did our jobs anyway.

<snipped debate over whether ANSI (or anybody, according to Chuck...) is
responsible>

>
> > In fact, Chuck, you have no right to be soliciting support here UNTIL
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I have no right?   I am really surprised to learn that my right to speak
on
> behalf of those who support the adoption of the FCD as a standard is
somehow
> eliminated by ANSI's action, and I wasn't aware that my right to do so was
> limited in some way by CLC's charter.  Could you explain the logic that
> leads you to this conclusion?  This is beginning to look suspiciously like
a
> flame, and I believe it's unwarranted.
>
It's not a flame and, if it were, it would be unwarranted. I never
questioned your right to speak and, as this is an unmoderated NG, I
shouldn't think there is any "charter".

My comment was prompted from my frustration at the continued calls for
support of something which I see as a disaster, and worse, a disaster that
no one is apparently prepared to be responsible for, or even acknowledge
needs repair. In my opinion it doesn't DESERVE support and supporting it
will only perpetuate the agony until we arrive at the next standard.

The "right" I was referring to was a moral one, certainly not any other
kind.

>>>
> > If that was  to happen I would be prepared to support the standard.
>
> Doesn't look to me like you're going to support the standard even if the
> responsible committees acknowledge their fundamental incompetence and
change
> their processes ...
>
<<<
Now why would you say THAT?

Whatever else you may think of me it should be apparent by now that I call
'em like I see 'em and I don't prevaricate. (I may get it wrong
occasionally, but at least it is honest...).

I said I would support it under certain circumstances, spelled out exactly
what those circumstances would be, and then you say "No, you wouldn't."

Can't think of what else is required...

> > >
 Which do you think will stand a better chance of convincing a
> businessman to
> > > invest his money in further COBOL development?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> The response does not address the impact of either of my proposed
scenarios
> on "the needs of business", and thus it begs the question, particularly
> since I've already established that "the politics of Computer technology"
> are today determining what IS managers and CFO's are willing to spend
money
> on and what they are not!
>
<<<
Claiming you have established something (especially when the claim is
disputed) does not make it so.

You mention a handful of "Managers" who are swayed by IT politics. I contend
that the majority are swayed by the bottom line. (Some IT Managers may be
swayed by politics to some extent, but how many CEOs came up through IT?)

If an IT manager is swayed by politics (rather than sound assessment and
investigation) and bases his decisions on real or imagined hurts and
paybacks, it won't be too long before that IT department stops providing the
necessary service to the Corporation. This then attracts the attention of
senior management who may consider replacement of the Manager who is
failing. These things are self correcting in the long term. The availability
or not of a standard will have VERY little bearing on it.

<snipped repetition of  dire consequences following  on failure to adopt
standard>

I

accept

erroneous assumptions, and flat misstatements of fact" you have noted in the

standard COBOL this year!!! Most of my co-workers have no idea what the standard
is and don�t care either. Use of new verbs is seldom and when it does occur it�s
left to the PC COBOL we use, Microfocus version 4.5. The code works but it can
be rather nasty at best and it�s very unstructured.  So, getting up to speed
with the old 85 standard is a larger concern of mine and not the new standard.

I would love to pitch in and help out but.. I doubt I could get the support of
my co-workers or employer. It�s a shame but most places will not care about the
standard and new features that might benefit the firm. It�s short sighted but
that�s the way it is.

Jeff

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T07:04:34+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B021872.F63E271C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3afb643c_2@Usenet.com> <krcpftcurb4pdou1f0057mu850rrafmqb4@4ax.com> <3afd3562_6@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> >
> >>How you write your COBOL really doesn't matter, because the way things are
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> language for the job it has to do...).

Yet you seem to have completely failed to notice that a current trend is
_away_ from pre-packaged object code and towards 'open source' where the
source code is provided.

> (Yes, I know this is hard to take when looked at in today's environment, but
> I was not talking about today's environment. You need to see the full
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> more; just bin the broken one and replace it.)

But maintenance of business software is _NOT_ done because the code goes
'bad', software doesn't 'wear out' or break in that way, it is done to
keep the system working the way that the business does when the business
changes. Or it may be done to add additional features. Obviously there
may be bugs and such that may appear but a replacement of the object
code would _still_ have that bug unless the replacement had been changed
in the source and recompiled.

Your analogy is not only flawed, it is completely irrelevant.  At the
manufacturer they don't 'throw out the old' whenever they want a new
increment model, they reuse the design and change small parts of it to
increase functionality or to make it work with other systems. 

> Only specialist shops will be in the business of producing components as it
> will not be economically viable for most Enterprises to do it on a regular
> basis. They can outsource this stuff for a fraction of what it costs to
> produce in house.

The reason that corporates have their own programmers is that it allows
them to have a system that adequately reflects how the organization
works.  The computer and its systems is a tool of the business, not the
other way round.  The business operates the way that it needs to and
changes to suit new conditions, the computer system must change to keep
up.  'Component systems' will only work where the business is
componentised, such as with a franchise.

Businesses cannot allow their operation to be hindered by the lack of an
appropriate component.  For example if they devise a new way of
marketing their product using, say, a discount based on some criteria,
that no component can actually do, then either they cannot operate the
business the way thgey want, or they issue typewriters all around to
replace the computer invoicing system.  Neither is acceptable.

This happens _all_ the time.

Paying the specialists to write a new component may be more expensive
than having an in-house system, and also that component would become
available to the competitors nullifying the business advantage of it.
 
> The job of "COBOL Programmer" as we understand it today will change so
> radically it will not be recognisable, and I believe this will happen within
> the next 7 - 10 years. However, I have suggested alternative roles which
> today's programmers could ideally fill if the suggested scenario eventuates.

This was said exactly as you have 30 years or so ago when high level
languages replaced the assemblers, and 20 years ago when 4GLs came
along, and 10 years ago when OO hit the ramps, and will be said again in
10 years and in 20 years time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T13:03:14+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b0328ae_4@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3afb643c_2@Usenet.com> <krcpftcurb4pdou1f0057mu850rrafmqb4@4ax.com> <3afd3562_6@Usenet.com> <3B021872.F63E271C@Azonic.co.nz> <3b01d420_2@Usenet.com> <josephk-A1CC86.22033516052001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```

"Joseph J Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message news:josephk->

> I can't let you get away with that.

I am not trying to "get away" with anything, Joe. I have stated my position
and it is clear. If someone has the background provided by the article, I
will defend my arguments gladly. Otherwise, I won't.

I can afford the luxury of spending time here in CLC at the moment as I am
actually on holiday and have no particular pressing deadlines or
committments. That will change in October when I travel to CW2001 and thence
back to the hurly burly of Consulting in Europe. Even so, I don't want to
waste the time I have, and you may have noticed I have been using much of my
time of late debating a much more important  and immediate issue than
whether Component technology will be established as the future of
Computing....

>I have read your article,

Yes, you have, and that is why you are receiving this response...<G>

>and whilst
> I agree it's a nice target to aim for, it appears that the previous
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
There is a fundamental divergence here. You are looking at things from the
point of view of a program developer; the rest of the World isn't.

Source code is only important to developers, not to users.

The "current trend" that you and Richard refer to is certainly true for some
installations (so I didn't "completely" fail to notice it...<G>) I just
don't think it will have significant impact on the way systems are developed
in the future. (I agree that  it is arguable...)

While I do not in any way wish to minimise or disparage the extremely
valuable work being done by the Open Source movement, I believe the
technology is moving so fast that the whole business of maintaining code
(whether it is Open Source or not) in house, will gradually disappear in the
next 10 years.

Why? Because (as I mentioned in my article) the development of software in a
Network Environment REQUIRES more than one language to support it. This
means that in-house support must be multi-lingual. This, in turn, pushes the
cost of it to a new level.

Meanwhile, the cost of Components is falling. Eventually, for the majority
of shops, it becomes a no-brainer. If they use Components (and smart
development environments which are currently in the pipeline) they no longer
need to maintain expensive technical development. Instead, they need
Business and Application knowledge and they already have that.

Remember, the components will have been developed in the best language for
whatever the Component needs to do, and in many cases, they will have been
written by specialists in that particular area, so they will encapsulate
particular expertise. (I'm sure you realise that as knowledge expands, it
becomes more and more difficult for any given individual to manage it all,
and this requires more and more specialization. The same is certainly true
of Computer Technology...30 years ago I felt I had a pretty good handle on
things computer, and was competent in all areas (compared to today, we
didn't do a lot...<G>) I accept that today there are areas of this
technology which I have very little understanding of, while others make it
their Life's work...(Image enhancement, Mathematical Modelling,
Biomechanics, etc.  (don't want to give too long a list...it's embarrassing
<G>!)

From our point of view, as developers, I agree that Open Source is very
attractive and it is right and proper that we should support and use it.
However, I really can't see the Commercial world, rushing to embrace Open
Source modules when it means they must continue to maintain multi-lingual
programming skills.

In the short term it is certainly arguable.

I definitely support the Open Source Movement and It is very possible that
many Components will be written using Open Source (not just for COBOL, but
other languages too...). I certainly hope so.

In the long term I believe the Marketing clout of around 400,000,000
computer users will demand that the emphasis shift AWAY from the
technicalities of program development and towards the direct implementation
of Business  (or Application) knowledge. In the past, it has not been
possible. With the advent of Component technology, it is.

Pete.


Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol as a structured language

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T13:33:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B082A03.B9E038A@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3afb643c_2@Usenet.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> How you write your COBOL really doesn't matter, because the way things are
> going, NO ONE else will ever maintain it.

Provacateur!  :-)

Peter, heir to "wild-eyed Cassandra" or Jeremiah with yellowing toenails,
speaks
of a COBOL that slouches toward its doom, while others seek options for rescuing

it from its Bowery of disrepute.

COBOL has survived the doomsayers for over forty years.   I remember that it was

obsolete in 1964, a mere eight years after its was introduced.   It was then
obsolete
in 1968,  the date of its release as a standard.    It became obsolete in
1974.   Then
it became obsolete in 1977.    It seems the language is declared obsolete about
every four years.

What are the alternatives?   PL/I was going to make it obsolete.   Where is PL/I

today?   Pascal was going to make it obsolete?    Nobody programs in Pascal
anymore. Automatic program generators were the rage for a while.  Does anyone
really use any of those in practice anymore?   Ada was going to replace COBOL
in the DoD.   Most DoD shops that used COBOL still use COBOL.   dBase??
was going to replace COBOL in the PC world.   All the dBase family is dead
although some descendants survive.   C++ became the darling of many data
processing shops until they discovered that C++ is its own virus.   Object
technology would surely kill COBOL!  Not quite.  Edward Markham has a
poem,

                  "He drew a circle to keep me out,
                  Heretic,  ... a thing to flout,
                  But love and I had the wit to win,
                  We drew a circle to include him in."

COBOL simply drew a circle to include object technology rather than let itself
be excluded.   Readers of this forum know that I am critical of the early
versions
of COBOL.   It deserves criticism.   However, much of the fundamental language
design is resilient enough to evolve as new ideas about software development
are discovered.   In fact, the language seems more resilient than some of those
who use it.

Perhaps it would be useful for someone to study the language in terms of its
virtues,
lay out the principles discovered in that study, and identify how those
principles
can contribute to the continued evolution of the language.

Large COBOL users are not going to abandon the language wholesale.  There is
too much at stake.  It would be a bad business decision.   Certainly Java, Ada,
and
other sensible languages can make a difference when used intelligently.
However,
anyone who chooses C++ to replace COBOL is either demented or has insufficient
technical expertise to understand the kinds of problems we need to solve in
business data processing.

I predict that in four years, someone else will predict the demise of COBOL.

Richard Riehle
```

>From: Richard Plinston riplin@Azonic.co.nz 
>Date: 2001-05-21 07:28 Eastern Daylight Time

>You forgot 4GLs and system generators.  I understand that 
>Burroughs LINK is still staggering along after 25 years, but it

Is it LINK, or LINC?  
An interesting package.  With it, you could generate a full-blown
working accounting system in about 4 hours--complete with
screens, DMSII database, reports, and update programs.
Applying it to a manufacturing system, or a stockholders 
system, just required a little tweaking.  And that's when the
fun started.  

One of my colleagues said, "LINC makes easy things easier,
and hard things harder."  I have a hunch that holds true
for most generators. 


Ross

product.

MERANT was formed from the merger of Micro Focus & Intersolv (tho' the name
change came later). Micro Focus was actually the acquirer in this
transaction, but some high jinks at the board level, left the Intersolv
executive team running the new company.

APS was not "CA'd" (new verb?). it's a mature product that is at the end of
its life. however, i understand that many existing customers are still very
much committed to APS, so the product is still actively supported.

thanks for the URL -- hopefully that will help Prakash.
