This is what i have done so far could somebody please scan for major errors, i
have a few that i cant seem to solve here is my code. BTW i know its far from
done.

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.    PROJ5.

****************************************
* Purpose:                             *
* Class: CIS260A.                      *
* Author: Derrick.                     *
* Written: 04/25/01.                   *
* Compiler: Fujitsu Cobol 4.0.         *
****************************************

 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
 OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.

 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.
     SELECT TRANSACTION-FILE
         ASSIGN TO "A:\STOKTRAN.DAT".
     SELECT SORT-FILE
         ASSIGN TO SORTWORK.
     SELECT AUDIT-REPORT
	       ASSIGN TO "A:\ADUIT.TXT".
     SELECT MASTER-FILE
	       ASSIGN TO "A:\STOCKMAST"
	       ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
	       ACCESS IS RANDOM
	       RECORD KEY IS MAST-KEY.
 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.
 FD TRANSACTION-FILE.
 01 TRANSACTION-REC.          
       05 TRANS-NAME   PIC X(10).
       05 TRANS-CODE   PIC X(10).
 SD SORT-FILE.
 01 SORT-RECORD.
     05 SORT-KEY.
         10 SK-NAME          PIC X(8).
         10 SK-CODE          PIC X(1).
     05 SORT-DATA            PIC X(35).              
 FD AUDIT-REPORT.
    01 AUDIT-RECORD		   PIC X(80).
 FD MASTER-FILE.
    01 MAST-KEY.
       05 STOK-NAME       PIC X(8).
       05 MASTER-RECORD   PIC X(26).
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 COPY FDTRAN.
 COPY FDMAST.
 01 END-OF-FILE              PIC X VALUE SPACE.
    88 EOF-FLAG           VALUE "Y".
 01 HEADING-1.
	  05 FILLER  PIC X(80) VALUE "PROJECT 5 AUDIT REPORT".
 01 HEADING-2.
    05 FILLER  PIC X(80) VALUE "HEADING-2".
 01 DETAIL-LINE.
    05         PIC X(80) VALUE "DETAIL".
 01 TOTAL-LINE.
    05	     PIC X(80) VALUE "TOTAL".
 01 PROGRAM-SWITCHES.
	  05   RECORD-FOUND-SW   PIC X VALUE SPACE.   
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 MAINLINE.

     PERFORM 100-INITIALIZATION.
     PERFORM 200-PROCESSING.
     PERFORM 300-TERMINATION.

     STOP RUN.

 100-INITIALIZATION.
     DISPLAY "Project 5 start".
 110-ADD-A-RECORD.
 120-CHANGE-A-RECORD.
 130-DELETE-A-RECORD.
 200-PROCESSING.
     DISPLAY "Project 5 in progress".
     SORT SORT-FILE
         ASCENDING KEY SORT-KEY
         INPUT PROCEDURE 210-SORT-INPUT
         OUTPUT PROCEDURE 220-SORT-OUTPUT
         .
 210-SORT-INPUT.
     OPEN INPUT TRANSACTION-FILE
         PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG
             READ TRANSACTION-FILE
                 INTO WS-TRANSACTION-RECORD
             AT END
                 SET EOF-FLAG TO TRUE
             NOT AT END
              	MOVE TRANS-NAME TO SK-NAME 
                  MOVE TRANS-CODE TO SK-CODE
  		MOVE TRANSACTION-REC TO SORT-DATA
             END-READ
         END-PERFORM
         CLOSE TRANSACTION-FILE
         .
 
 220-SORT-OUTPUT.
      MOVE N TO EOF-FLAG
      OPEN  OUTPUT AUDIT-REPORT
* format and write audit headings
      WRITE AUDIT-RECORD FROM HEADING-1 AFTER PAGE
      WRITE AUDIT-RECORD FROM HEADING-2 AFTER 2
      PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE
      RETURN SORT-FILE
       AT END
       SET EOF-FLAG TO TRUE
       NOT AT END
       	MOVE SORT-DATA TO WS-TRANSACTION-RECORD
		MOVE TR-NAME TO MAST-KEY
		READ SORT-KEY
			INTO WS-MASTER-RECORD
		INVALID KEY
		    MOVE 'N' TO RECORD-FOUND-SW
		NOT INVALID KEY
		    MOVE 'Y' TO RECORD-FOUND-SW
          END-READ
       	EVALUATE TR-TRANS-CODE
		    WHEN "A" 
			IF RECORD-FOUND DISPLAY 'ERROR'
                  ELSE
			PERFORM 110-ADD-A-RECORD
		  WRITE MASTER-RECORD
			  FROM WS-MASTER-RECORD
			INVALID KEY
			MOVE 'N' TO RECORD-FOUND-SW
			NOT INVALID KEY
			MOVE 'Y' TO RECORD-FOUND-SW
		   END-WRITE
			END-IF.
*  ADD NOTES HERE
			WHEN "C" PERFORM 120-CHANGE-A-RECORD
*  HERE	
			WHEN "D" PERFORM 130-DELETE-A-RECORD
* HERE
			END-EVALUATE
			END-PERFORM
		      DISPLAY DETAIL-LINE
             CLOSE AUDIT-REPORT.
 300-TERMINATION.
     DISPLAY "Project 5 complete".
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol help please

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T22:24:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eEZK6.29688$ho6.1640437@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<20010511112231.13807.00003452@ng-cj1.aol.com>`

```
Made a couple of corrections.

In my view, nested read-write-return-rewrite-etc., code is not much
different from nested IF statements; hard to read, hard to fix.

For example, your snippet:

>       RETURN SORT-FILE
>        AT END
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-IF.

Could be rewritten as:

PERFORM MYSORT-RETURN UNTIL END-OF-FILE.

MYSORT-RETURN.
   RETURN SORT-FILE
      AT END MOVE 'Y' TO END-OF-FILE
      NOT AT END
         PERFORM MYSORT-IN.
MYSORT-IN.
   MOVE SORT-DATA TO WS-TRANSACTION-RECORD.
   MOVE TR-NAME TO MAST-KEY.
   PERFORM MYGET-MASTER.
MYGET-MASTER.
   READ MASTER-FILE
      INVALID KEY PERFORM MYGET-MASTER-NOJOY
      NOT INVALID KEY PERFORM MYGET-MASTER-JOY
      END-READ.
MYGET-MASTER-NOJOY.
   DISPLAY 'ERROR'.
MYGET-MASTER-JOY.
   etc.

This technique, in my experience, is easier to understand and easier
to add processing statments such as formatting a screen instead of
DISPLAY "ERROR"

Just my three cents.

"Deek163" <deek163@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010511112231.13807.00003452@ng-cj1.aol.com...
> This is what i have done so far could somebody please scan for major
errors, i
> have a few that i cant seem to solve here is my code. BTW i know its
far from
> done.
>
…[89 more quoted lines elided]…
>                  SET EOF-FLAG TO TRUE        *** MOVE 'Y' TO
END-OF-FILE
>              NOT AT END
>               MOVE TRANS-NAME TO SK-NAME
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol help please

- **From:** Stephen J Spiro <donotreply@interbulletin.bogus>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T02:01:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFC998E.7274B51A@interbulletin.com>`
- **References:** `<20010511112231.13807.00003452@ng-cj1.aol.com>`

```
deek163@aol.com (Deek163) wrote: 
>This is what i have done so far could somebody please scan for major errors, i
>have a few that i cant seem to solve here is my code. BTW i know its far from
…[98 more quoted lines elided]…
>         CLOSE TRANSACTION-FILE.
------------------------------------

First, you are moving the TRANSACTION-FILE record to WORKING-STORAGE, (presumably in FDTRAN). Nevertheless, you use the data names in the FD as the source of your MOVE instructions.
Second, these fields are each 10 bytes long in the source, but 8 and 1 bytes in the target record. Is this correct?
Third, having moved truncated values into the sort key, you then take the same 20 bytes, and move it into the 35 byte data area of the sort key. Is this correct?  It is inefficient to build a sort record larger than you need.
Fourth, you need a RELEASE SORT-RECORD statement before the END-PERFORM.
Fifth, I recommend against AT END ... NOT AT END.  If you use File Statuses (and you CAN use File Statuses with sequential files), you can do a structured test. Not only that, you do not need a separate end-of-file flag; the file status IS the end-of-file flag! And you do not have to test explicitly for end-of-file, the PERFORM statement will do that for you!:

     PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG
*       If you make this an 88-level on the TRAN-RETURN-CODE, otherwise
     PERFORM UNTIL TRAN-RETURN-CODE IS EQUAL TO '10' 
     -
     - 
     IF TRAN-RETURN-CODE IS EQUAL TO ZERO
	 MOVE TRANS-NAME TO SK-NAME 
         MOVE TRANS-CODE TO SK-CODE 
  	 MOVE TRANSACTION-REC TO SORT-DATA
     ELSE
     IF TRAN-RETURN-CODE IS NOT EQUAL TO '10'
         PERFORM ERROR-ROUTINE
     -
     -
     END-PERFORM.

One of the reasons I do not like AT END .... NOT AT END is because, in a production environment, you will be expected to check for a bad READ. NOT AT END assumes a good READ.  If you get a return code of '04' or something, you will fall thru and corrupt everything until you (possibly) ABEND.

------------------------------------------
 
> 220-SORT-OUTPUT.
>      MOVE N TO EOF-FLAG
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>
---------------------

There are other problems with the program; you should try to resolve all of your error messages before you ask for help.  You should not ask for help until all of your syntax errors have been resolved.  THEN, we can help you with the logic errors.
-----------------------

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4


_______________________________________________
Submitted via WebNewsReader of http://www.interbulletin.com
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol help please

- **From:** foodman123 <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T13:32:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dYaL6.2684$j65.209964@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<20010511112231.13807.00003452@ng-cj1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20010511112231.13807.00003452@ng-cj1.aol.com>, Deek163 says...
>
>This is what i have done so far could somebody please scan for major errors, i
>have a few that i cant seem to solve here is my code. BTW i know its far from
>done.

Hi:

Your messages, as above, seem to always disregard conventions regarding
grammar, punctuation, capitalization and composition. This undesirable
trait may carry over into your programming. . .

For example:

>     SELECT AUDIT-REPORT
>	       ASSIGN TO "A:\ADUIT.TXT".


Convention would dictate that a level 01 in an FD would be a record name.
I would hope that a key to a file would not be the entire record. It is 
illogical that a record would be part of a key.

> FD MASTER-FILE.
>    01 MAST-KEY.
>       05 STOK-NAME       PIC X(8).
>       05 MASTER-RECORD   PIC X(26).

If you are going to be a COBOL programmer, you must strive for uncompromising
accuracy and clarity in the DATA DIVISION. Poorly designed records and W-S
items will lead to poorly coded and misleading code in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.

Since I despise structured programming, I will not comment on the rest of it.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

condition tests to GO TO a paragraph to do some small piece
of work... and then GO TO some other  paragraph to do another

Structured programming is always important in any programming 
language.

<a id="t-092953b6f8"></a>
## Thread: New to NetExpress...SQLCODE -3010 — 12 msgs

### New to NetExpress...SQLCODE -3010

- **From:** Tom Tate <ttate@?home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T17:59:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KLVK6.8185$I5.2180267@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
I'm trying to get data from MSAccess.  Singleton selects work fine, but
when I try to open a cursor I get a -3010 (which isn't really an SQL
code but returned from the ODBC driver I guess.)  Can anyone shed some
light on this?
```

#### ↳ Re: New to NetExpress...SQLCODE -3010

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-13T01:39:08+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3afd3c87_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<KLVK6.8185$I5.2180267@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```

Tom Tate wrote in message ...
>I'm trying to get data from MSAccess.  Singleton selects work fine, but
>when I try to open a cursor I get a -3010 (which isn't really an SQL
>code but returned from the ODBC driver I guess.)  Can anyone shed some
>light on this?

Tom,

I don't use NE but I do use the ACCESS ODBC Driver frequently. The ACCESS
error code 3010 (as returned by VB) has nothing to do with cursors (it
indicates that you are trying to create a table which already exists.

I use SQLSTATE rather than SQLCODE and (under FujitsuCOBOL) the ACCESS ODBC
driver returns an SQLSTATE of S1010 when a cursor sequence error occurs.

(Probably addressed the wrong cursor name in an OPEN-FETCH-CLOSE sequence.
Can be caused by ï¿½cloningï¿½ cursor code.)

As this is the closest code I can find to the one you describe, and as it is
pertinent to cursor error, hopefully it might be some help.

The ODBC driver is much more sensitive to syntax than say, Mainframe DB2, so
you need to check this carefully also. If you can't solve it, send me the
code and the Access DB.

Pete.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New to NetExpress...SQLCODE -3010

- **From:** "James J.Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T18:02:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B001E08.781B9E45@home.com>`
- **References:** `<KLVK6.8185$I5.2180267@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com> <3afd3c87_2@Usenet.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Tom Tate wrote in message ...
> >I'm trying to get data from MSAccess.  Singleton selects work fine, but
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I was hoping Pete would jump in on this one. I'm just starting to 'dabble' with
N/E, Access and SQL - so this would be a case of the 'blind leading the blind'.
However I note you referred to a 'Singleton' - are you using the N/E SQL Code
Generator ?.

I'm not, because your SQL code is then tied into one product. I assume you are
using the 'sqlca.cpy' file. Note it limits sqlerrmc to 70 characters, so
although you will get that Error 3010 - your sqlerrmc is truncated - you don't
see the full message. You could possibly change sqlca.cpy to reflect new field
sizes, and that might then give you a full message text..

Perhaps Pete would clarify for us both - why use SqlState instead of SqlCode -
seeing that the latter gives 0 = found or update OK,  1 =  a field(column)  has
a null value (???) and 100 = not found, i.e. at end of table?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to NetExpress...SQLCODE -3010

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T17:39:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b00c388_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<KLVK6.8185$I5.2180267@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com> <3afd3c87_2@Usenet.com> <3B001E08.781B9E45@home.com>`

```

"James J.Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3B001E08.781B9E45@home.com...
>
>
> Perhaps Pete would clarify for us both - why use SqlState instead of
SqlCode -
> seeing that the latter gives 0 = found or update OK,  1 =  a field(column)
has
> a null value (???) and 100 = not found, i.e. at end of table?
>
>
Gladly.

It is quite OK to use either (both).

I use SQLSTATE because it offers more situations and is more consistent
across platforms in my experience, especially where ODBC is being used. It
is just as easy to check for '00000' = OK, or '02000' = NOT FOUND  as it is
to check for 0 or 100. SQLCODEs seem to be vendor dependant and, in most
cases they try and make them the same as IBM implemented for DB2. (-100 =
NOT FOUND, for example.)

I have no strong feelings either way but when things go wrong and you want
more information, I reckon SQLSTATE is the best bet. I guess it's analogous
to using "File Status" instead of "AT END"/"NOT AT END" when dealing with
flat files. They all work fine and they are all useful. It just comes down
to preference and experience.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to NetExpress...SQLCODE -3010

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T11:13:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rb8M6.32$Uf5.27442@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<KLVK6.8185$I5.2180267@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com> <3afd3c87_2@Usenet.com> <3B001E08.781B9E45@home.com> <3b00c388_5@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@free.net.nz> wrote in message
news:3b00c388_5@Usenet.com...
>
> I have no strong feelings either way but when things go wrong and you want
> more information, I reckon SQLSTATE is the best bet.

You can cheat. If you call SqlGetDiagRec on SQL_ERROR you get both SQLSTATE
and a text message and don't have to code for all possible SQLSTATEs. (Also
available for the most part with SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO)

I use something like this..

rc =SQlExecute or SQLExecDirect
if rc = SQL_SUCCESS
  good things
elseif rc = SQL_SUCCESS_WITH _INFO
   check SQLSTATE to see if really matters
elseif rc = SQL_ERROR
  just treat as an error, get and show SQLSTATE and the text message and
bail out
endif

One note ... SQLCODEs are part of the ODBC specs, and SQL_SUCCESS,
SQL_SUCESS_WITH_INFO and SQL_ERROR do not vary in value for SQL which is
ODBC compliant. Can's say about any specific vendor's product, though.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to NetExpress...SQLCODE -3010

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T01:18:40+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b0130c8_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<KLVK6.8185$I5.2180267@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com> <3afd3c87_2@Usenet.com> <3B001E08.781B9E45@home.com> <3b00c388_5@Usenet.com> <rb8M6.32$Uf5.27442@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael, there is something very important that you are missing here...

This is a COBOL forum.

The calls you are making are not available through standard COBOL as part of
Embedded SQL.

They probably work fine in Basic or C++ but they are not provided for in
Embedded SQL as used in COBOL.

All of the diagnostic fields SQLSTATE, SQLCODE and SQLMSG are provided as
"known" Host Variables in COBOL. There is no need to even use the Linkage
blocks provided for DB2 (SQLCA and SQLDA); these variables are "known" to
COBOL.

Standard Embedded SQL (ESQL) is transportable across platforms; the
low-level calls you are describing are not.

While I take no issue with people using whatever works, it is simpler for
most programmers (especially people making the transition to RDB for the
first time), to simply learn SQL rather than all the ODBC API calls. (COBOL
compiler pre-processors will implement the API for you transparently,
exactly as they do with the Windows API when you use screens...)

With your level of experience, this is not a problem; for many beginners it
is. And there is no need for ANYONE to go to this level if they use COBOL.
Using simple standard ESQL in COBOL, with the RDB treated purely as a data
repository (no DB Procedures, Macros, or other platform dependant code AND
no direct ODBC API calls....<G>) it is possible to write code that can
access RDBs on PCs, mid-range, and Mainframe and the code can be re-compiled
to run on any of these platforms. (Of course it will always run from a PC to
access data on any of these platforms, but sometimes it is nice to use the
PC as a test bed for systems targeted  for the Mainframe, for example.)

I strongly advocate the simplest possible approach to RDB. That means no
low-level calls, just simple, transportable, SQL.

more below...

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:rb8M6.32$Uf5.27442@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@free.net.nz> wrote in message
> news:3b00c388_5@Usenet.com...
> >
> > I have no strong feelings either way but when things go wrong and you
want
> > more information, I reckon SQLSTATE is the best bet.
>
> You can cheat. If you call SqlGetDiagRec on SQL_ERROR you get both
SQLSTATE
> and a text message and don't have to code for all possible SQLSTATEs.
(Also
> available for the most part with SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO)
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
None of this is standard ESQL. The constants are not part of the COBOL SQL
environment  (and COBOL does NOT support underline characters) and the Calls
are low-level API calls that really have no place in a COBOL program. It is
quite possible to set up the constants but you would need to declare 88
levels on the SQLSTATE Host Variable. Here is ONE POSSIBLE equivalent in
COBOL...(NOT pseudocode, Basic, VB, or C++....COBOL...remember, the "dying
language" ?<G>)

(You know I almost NEVER post code to public forums but this one warrants
it, so here goes...<G>)

Data Division.
Working-Storage Section.
...
        EXEC SQL BEGIN DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC
*> Declare Host Variables....
 01   SQLSTATE pic x(5).
         88 SQL-success    value '00000'.
         88 SQL-not-found  value '02000'.
 01   SQLMSG     pic x(128).
        EXEC SQL END DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC
...
 Procedure Division.
...
         EXEC SQL
               SELECT something
                   FROM  sometable
                WHERE  somecondition
         END-EXEC
         *>  Check what happened....
         if NOT SQL-success
            if NOT SQL-not-found
               *> we could simply out the SQLSTATE and the corresponding
error message
               *> which have been placed into the Host Variables for us by
the SQL call,
               *> but let's pretend we want to do it thoroughly....<G>...

               call 'badsql' using SQLSTATE    *> analyse and report the
nature of the error
               set abort to TRUE
            else
                  call 'setmsgbx' using
                                            by value 'Sorry, no data was
found...'
                                            *> notify user we found
nothing...
                  end-call
             end-if
          else
                *> process the retrieved data...
          end-if

Obviously the above can be tailored to cater for other expected results and
I have made no attempt to show all possibilities. The point is that COBOL
AUTOMATICALLY returns SQLSTATE and the appropriate error message, just as
Michael described,  (and SQLCODE if you define it), into the Host Variables
allocated for them. There is NO NEED to make a low-level API Call. You can
do it in standard (transportable) COBOL!

Pete.

                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to NetExpress...SQLCODE -3010

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T16:01:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<spcM6.657$Uf5.186843@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<KLVK6.8185$I5.2180267@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com> <3afd3c87_2@Usenet.com> <3B001E08.781B9E45@home.com> <3b00c388_5@Usenet.com> <rb8M6.32$Uf5.27442@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3b0130c8_2@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@free.net.nz> wrote in message
news:3b0130c8_2@Usenet.com...
> Michael, there is something very important that you are missing here...
>
> This is a COBOL forum.
>
> The calls you are making are not available through standard COBOL as part
of
> Embedded SQL.
>
> They probably work fine in Basic or C++ but they are not provided for in
> Embedded SQL as used in COBOL.

But can't COBOL programs call external routines? And should one choose to do
so, does that not obviate the requirement for and expense of a precompiler?

Embedded SQL in COBOL is darn handy, and when available I will jump on it.
But for those who perhaps just want to get familiar with the use of SQL, it
seems to me using APIs provided by the various ODBC interfaces available on
many platforms is a way to 'get in' without a significant investment. A
little more work, maybe, but nonetheless functional.

Take an example: a simple INSERT:.

In COBOL, you could...

*    Build the SQL statement :
      STRING  "INSERT INTO " delimited by size
                      table-name delimited by size
                       "VALUES ("    delimited by size)
                          value-1 delimited by size
                          [value-2 delimited by size, value-n delimited by
size]
                      ")" delimited by size
               INTO the-statement

   CALL "SqlExecDirect"
     USING            hStmt, The-statement, FUNCTION LENGTH(The-statement)
(' may vary by platform')
     RETURNING  SQLCODE

...as well  as ...
EXEC SQL
    INSERT INTO :table-name VALUES (:value-1, :value-2, ...:value-n)
END-EXEC

When executed, you still have to check SQLCode, and may or may not want to
check SQLSTATE, and the "error-checking code" is identical.

Granted,  a SELECT ...INTO  is a lot (a LOT) hairier if you have to do your
own binding of parameters, but in the end, you get the same results.

It's fair to say, "an ESQL pre-compiler makes SQL database access a hell of
a lot easier", which kinda explains why those who do a lot of database
access will pay real money for the precompiler license.

My point is, "you don't have to license a precompiler to use SQL with a
database in a COBOL program."

I happen to think discussions of "other ways to skin the cat in COBOL"
are, in fact, very relevant to COBOL programming in general. Since "Embedded
SQL" is AFAIK NOT part of standard COBOL ( ESQL is A) usually licensed
separately; B) not universally supported by all COBOL vendors; and C)
available for other languages, e.g., Pascal), "Embedded SQL" is itself not
necessarily a "pure" COBOL topic.

MCM
```

```

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:spcM6.657$Uf5.186843@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net...
>
> But can't COBOL programs call external routines? And should one choose to
do
> so, does that not obviate the requirement for and expense of a
precompiler?
>
> Embedded SQL in COBOL is darn handy, and when available I will jump on it.
> But for those who perhaps just want to get familiar with the use of SQL,
it
> seems to me using APIs provided by the various ODBC interfaces available
on
> many platforms is a way to 'get in' without a significant investment. A
> little more work, maybe, but nonetheless functional.
>
There is NO separate license for the pre-compiler, which is transparent to
the user (It simply runs as another pass of the compiler and is available
with Net Express, Fujitsu COBOL, and, of course, Mainframe COBOL. In fact, I
know of NO modern COBOL compiler where it ISN'T bundled (OK, maybe the free
version of Fujitsu, Version 3, but that is no longer intended for serious
development.)

So if you haven't "jumped on it", I really don't know what COBOL compiler
you are using...<G>
(Truth is, you are doing most of your development in Basic and COBOL is of
only passing interest, right <G>? Nothing wrong with that, but, like I said
before, this is a COBOL forum...)

I never quibbled with the fact that you CAN use API calls; I just quibble
with the fact that there is no NEED to for COBOL users. It's like making
your own Windows API calls (and we both know there are times when you NEED
to do this, usually because the facility you want is not available in
standard COBOL), but MOST people DON'T do this as a PREFERENCE. Exactly the
same argument applies to the ODBC API.

I reject your cost argument as specious because there IS no additional cost.

And I strongly recommend COBOL people who are interested in moving to RDB to
use the facilities of ESQL provided by their compiler, rather than get to
grips with ODBC API calls from day one.

>>>>
> It's fair to say, "an ESQL pre-compiler makes SQL database access a hell
of
> a lot easier", which kinda explains why those who do a lot of database
> access will pay real money for the precompiler license.
>
<<<
Thank you, I knew you'd see it my way if you thought about it...<G>. I remid
you that the "paying real money bit" is simply NOT true. Most people with
modern COBOL compilers ALREADY HAVE this facility, they just don't KNOW they
have!

>>>
> My point is, "you don't have to license a precompiler to use SQL with a
> database in a COBOL program."
>
<<<
Point taken. You are absolutely right.

> I happen to think discussions of "other ways to skin the cat in COBOL"
> are, in fact, very relevant to COBOL programming in general. Since
"Embedded
> SQL" is AFAIK NOT part of standard COBOL ( ESQL is A) usually licensed
> separately; B) not universally supported by all COBOL vendors; and C)
> available for other languages, e.g., Pascal), "Embedded SQL" is itself not
> necessarily a "pure" COBOL topic.
>
Not going to argue this one, but watch that "AFAIK" ...<G>

 In fact I've really said all I want to say on this topic...<G>

Pete.

I did not know that. Last time I worked with ESQL/COBOL was about five years
ago on a Unix box with Microfocus; before that on IBM/MF. Without doing the
research myself, I accepted the explanation that it was a separate product.
(Call it youthful indiscretion?)

Maybe someone (the compiler vendors who lurk?) can answer, "is ESQL support
an included part of the COBOL compiler product for vendor xxxx?"  "Is it a
priced "bolt-on?" (If the vendors don't want to burden the list, they are

No, that's not quite correct. The past *two months* I've been working mostly
in
BASIC. For about a year prior to that, I was mostly working on a large
project using IBM/MF COBOL (no database).

But it is true that I am fascinated by the entire "Windows Development
Experience" and have found so much utility in the Win/32 API that I am
amazed that so much business software written using "Microsoft Windows
development products (i.e., MS Visual Anything)" is of such unimaginative
design. (I do NOT use MS Visual BASIC).

I also work a lot with commercial EDI translation and mapping software such
as Trading Partner (MVS and PC), Mercator (PC) and Gentran (AS/400). My real
specialty is ANSI/EDIFACT EDI data translation, mapping and applications
interface. While the translation, mapping and subsystem design are usually
left to me, the applications interface is often done and maintained by other
client personnel. (After I adapt the data to a more "user
(programmer)-friendly" format than "raw" ANSI/EDIFACT data).

(FWIW, the Mercator mapping product uses a form of ESQL. Yes, this
capability is included in the base product).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to NetExpress...SQLCODE -3010

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T09:15:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9du1vp$asa$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<KLVK6.8185$I5.2180267@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com> <3afd3c87_2@Usenet.com> <3B001E08.781B9E45@home.com> <3b00c388_5@Usenet.com> <rb8M6.32$Uf5.27442@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3b0130c8_2@Usenet.com> <spcM6.657$Uf5.186843@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3b01cd11_2@Usenet.com> <JCvM6.176$AB1.99251@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
As far as I know, the SQL COBOL language support varies completely from
compiler to compiler and database to database.

For example, with MERANT (Micro Focus)
   "language" support for Microsoft SQL (and IBM) is included BUT support
for Oracle comes with the Oracle database

For IBM VisualAge COBOL, support for IBM's (PC) DB2 is included - and I do
not think they support ANY other SQL (thru add-on or with the product)

For IBM's mainframe COBOL, "traditional" SQL support is provided ONLY via
the DB2 product (and a precompiler) - however, IBM has recently introduced
an "integrated" DB2 support.  But support for Oracle or other 3rd party SQL
is provided by the SQL vendor.

Therefore, I do not think that "any" general comment can be made about which
compilers support which SQL engines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to NetExpress...SQLCODE -3010

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T13:45:10+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b033513_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<KLVK6.8185$I5.2180267@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com> <3afd3c87_2@Usenet.com> <3B001E08.781B9E45@home.com> <3b00c388_5@Usenet.com> <rb8M6.32$Uf5.27442@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3b0130c8_2@Usenet.com> <spcM6.657$Uf5.186843@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3b01cd11_2@Usenet.com> <JCvM6.176$AB1.99251@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <9du1vp$asa$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

You have missed the point that in the PC environment if ODBC is used, all of
the major compilers support the necessary ESQL.

I'm not arguing that different ODBC drivers may implement different elements
of SQL (or even that there can be variations between the same elements in
different drivers), but for the most part the connection to ODBC can be set
for any of the major PC COBOL compilers.

It is only on the mainframe that "Language" support is necessary. On the PC,
given an appropriate ODBC driver, it is possible to interface to any RDB
through the COBOL ESQL.

I have accessed ORACLE, and Mainframe DB2 from a PC in this way, despite
there being no SPECIFIC support in Fujitsu COBOL for these RDBs. (The DB2
ODBC driver was provided by IBM; I believe there is also an UDB driver
available...)

ODBC is exactly that; OPEN Database Connectivity. It works and it works with
ESQL.

Comments below...

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9du1vp$asa$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net...
> As far as I know, the SQL COBOL language support varies completely from
> compiler to compiler and database to database.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Or you can use the ORACLE ODBC driver...(which comes with the Oracle system
as you noted). You could also access a MS ACCESS database, or INGRES, or
anything else which is set up as an ODBC data Source, and for which you have
an ODBC driver installed.


> For IBM VisualAge COBOL, support for IBM's (PC) DB2 is included - and I do
> not think they support ANY other SQL (thru add-on or with the product)
>
I haven't used it but it was based on MicroFocus COBOL so I would be
astounded if it doesn't support ODBC.

> For IBM's mainframe COBOL, "traditional" SQL support is provided ONLY via
> the DB2 product (and a precompiler) - however, IBM has recently introduced
> an "integrated" DB2 support.  But support for Oracle or other 3rd party
SQL
> is provided by the SQL vendor.
>
This is an interesting area. AFAIK, it is not possible to connect to PC ODBC
drivers from the mainframe so, in this environment, it IS necessary to have
"Language" support. (Wonder how long it will be...? Mainframe components are
in the pipeline and these will almost certainly utilise ODBC...)

> Therefore, I do not think that "any" general comment can be made about
which
> compilers support which SQL engines.
>
Here's a general comment:
" Use ODBC on your PC. Use it thru ESQL provided with your compiler. You can
access ANY RDB which is on the LAN and has an appropriate ODBC driver
installed and configured."

If you stick to standard simple SQL, your programs will be transportable (if
you care about that...)

Pete.

I just thought I'd follow up because I finally got this thing to lie
down flat.  Obviously the -3010 was bogus.  The SQLSTATE (I think, I'm
not at work) was 7001 -- "invalid number of parameter markers," which
was just about as helpful.   The actual error message was "Too few
parameters.  Expected: 1".  I finally did a WEB search on that and
discovered--get this--I had misspelled a column name in the cursor
declaration.

With regards to Michael's post regarding doing the ODBC calls directly,
that's certainly an option.  I inherited a bunch of stuff done in
Workbench that does just that, but it's so nasty I'm going to rewrite it
to do the embedded SQL calls.  I'd rather take a beating than maintain
that stuff.

Thanks for all the thoughtful replies.  I imagine this old mainframe DB2
dog will limp back here for help soon.

## Thread: ODBC API/Statement Type?? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### ODBC API/Statement Type??

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T18:33:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dgWK6.952$%_4.276686@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Does anyone know of an ODBC call (regular v  3.5 or less API) which can tell
you if a statement just executed was a CREATE, DROP, ALTER, SELECT, INSERT,
DELETE or UPDATE?

I have some code which gets the results of each, but I'm trying to build
something with a user-entry screen which can take 'any' SQL statement and
process it; then return the results.

However, other than parsing the statement myself, I can't find anything
(like a  'SQLGetStmtAttr') which will tell me what the last statement was.
Do
I just need to interrogate the rows affected or number of result columns?

Thanks,

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

#### ↳ Re: ODBC API/Statement Type??

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T11:59:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3afc7fd4_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<dgWK6.952$%_4.276686@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael,

The answer to your question is "No".

It IS possible to derive what the last Verb executed was, but NOT via
standard ODBC (at least as far as I know; different ODBC drivers may have
different features, but I've never seen one with what you are asking for. I
spend a lot of time dealing with RDBs on PCs and Mainframes and have done
since they were invented (1983).

There is no need for you to have "code which gets the results of each", just
use dynamic SQL.

This will allow you to process ANY statement input by your users and you
will always know what it was because it will be in the PREPARE area.

Check out dynamic SQL, PREPARE and EXECUTE.

Early implementations were pretty inefficient, but this is no longer true.

Another possibility would be to use EXPLAIN, but this has different results
in different environments and I wouldn't recommend it.

Pete.

Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>Does anyone know of an ODBC call (regular v  3.5 or less API) which can
tell
>you if a statement just executed was a CREATE, DROP, ALTER, SELECT, INSERT,
>DELETE or UPDATE?
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

## Thread: Call c function from COBOL — 3 msgs

### Call c function from COBOL

- **From:** "CJAA" <altamira@orden.cl>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T03:44:41-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3afc3fde$1@dnewserver.firstcom.cl>`

```

    How to pass one Strutc with the long value from COBOL to function C.

    The Fileds CampoStr, CampoStr2 is OK, but de field CampoLong  no is OK
in C.

    What is the problem...?

    Please , help me.

    Atte
    CJAA


PROGRAM IN C
==================
#include <stdio.h>
struct INPUT
{
   char CampoStr[11];
   char CampoStr2[11];
   long *CampoLong;
};

char *cfunc2(XX, c)
struct INPUT *XX;
int          *c;
{
    printf("\n Msg Recibido from COBOL:
CampoStr[%s][%s]CampoLong[%ld]c[%d]\n ", XX->CampoStr , XX->CampoStr2 \
                                       ,(XX->CampoLong)  , *c );
    return "[From cfun2]Msg de C.";
}

PROGRAM IN COBOL
==================
      $set rtncode-size"4"
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01 Return-Pointer usage pointer.
       01 Send-Pointer   usage pointer.

       01 ESTRUCTURA.
          03 CampoStr    pic X(10)  Value "CAMPOSTR".
          03 Filler      pic X(01)  Value  x"00".
          03 CampoStr2   pic X(10)  Value "CAMPOSTR2".
          03 Filler      pic X(01)  Value  x"00".
          03 CampoLong   pic S9(09) COMP-5 Value  77.

       01 counter pic 9(8) comp-5 value 8.

       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01 NombreRcv   pic X(30).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       call-c section.

       CALL "cfunc2" USING ESTRUCTURA, counter
                          RETURNING Return-Pointer
       IF return-code not = zero
           display "ERROR"
       ELSE
           display "OK"
       END-IF
       SET ADDRESS OF NombreRcv  TO Return-Pointer

       DISPLAY "***** MENSAJE RECIBIDO DE C *****"
       DISPLAY NombreRcv
       STOP RUN.
```

#### ↳ Re: Call c function from COBOL

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-05-11T20:44:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3afc4e19.262175228@news>`
- **References:** `<3afc3fde$1@dnewserver.firstcom.cl>`

```
You need to pass the structure by its address; a "C" pointer:  In
COBOL, you specify this using BY REFERENCE.  Values are passed BY
CONTENT, like this:

       CALL "cfunc2" USING BY REFERENCE  ESTRUCTURA
                                            BY CONTENT      counter
                                            RETURNING Return-Pointer

Since COBOL passes BY REFERENCE by default, your counter was not being
passed correctly; rather, COBOL was passing a pointer to the variable
"counter".

There are generally a couple of other minor things concerning the
format of calls to C from COBOL, and they should be in your COBOL
manual; they vary by compiler.

Regards,
Kevin

On Sat, 12 May 2001 03:44:41 -0400, "CJAA" <altamira@orden.cl> wrote:

>
>    How to pass one Strutc with the long value from COBOL to function C.
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

```
*** New Book Announcement ***

          From COBOL to OOP: Changing to Object-Oriented Programming 

                                by 

                         Markus Knasm�ller


          dpunkt.verlag, 2001, ISBN 3-932588-95-9

For many years, several companies made use of COBOL and similar
software as programming languages. For this main reason, an amplitude
of Software packages have been written in COBOL, a language mastered
by a vast number of developers. However, the tendency to operate with
more maintainable, as well as more efficient software has opened the
door and led companies to prefer working with object oriented
programming.


This book has been created and issued for COBOL-programmers who are
seeking a methodical change to OOP in a step-by-step and effective
manner. The principles of object-oriented development are, to a great
extent, represented independently of a special language; Examples will
be presented in exemplary fashion in Java and Delphi.


Besides the basics of objective orientation, modern software
techniques such as automatic testing or version management will be
explained. In addition to that, the experience of BMD Systemhaus GmbH
- Austria�s leading manufacturer of accounting software - a company
that has actually switched its accounting system from COBOL to OOP,
will be used as back-up and assistance. As a result of this, the
reader not only acquires knowledge of how to develop new object
oriented software, he will also be given the opportunity to obtain
auxiliary aids to re-engineer current COBOL-programmes.


On the enclosed CD-ROM, you will find a large variety of source code
examples in the form of Delphi and Java programmes, trial versions of
respective development areas (Delphi, Jbuilder) that may be tested at
will as well as PowerPoint-Presentations of the book�s contents.

Book website
http://www.dpunkt.de/buecher/3-932588-95-9.html

Unfortunately this book is currently just available in german
language, if you are interested in a german version please do not
hestitate to contact me (knasmueller@bmd.at).

## Thread: HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION. — 9 msgs

### HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION.

- **From:** "just_looking_m35" <just_looking_m35@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T12:04:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6A0A057F5145C41C.E34D574C40977678.BD3446916A3E813C@lp.airnews.net>`

```
I currently reading a file, that stores a count in a one byte HEX. How do
convert this value to a three byte demical number. I tried using a redefine
clause to make it binary, then move it to the demical field. Note: Binary
require me to set up a working storage as COBOL-85 lowest field for binary
is two bytes. I thought this would work, but no luck. Could use a little
help on this problem.

05 BINARY-GRP-X.
    10 BYTE-1X      PIC X(01).
    10 BYTE-2X      PIC X(01).

05 BINARY-NUM REDEFINES BINARY-GRP PIC 99 BINARY. - ECT.
05 COUNT-IN-HEX   PIC X(01).

05 COUNT-IN-DEMICAL  PIC 9(03).

MOVE ZEROS TO BINARY-NUM.
MOVE COUNT-IN-HEX TO BYTE-2X.

MOVE BINARY-NUM TO COUNT-IN-DEMICAL.
```

#### ↳ Re: HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION.

- **From:** "Kevin Solomon" <kjsolo@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T17:58:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yReL6.121124$HF.28389093@news4.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<6A0A057F5145C41C.E34D574C40977678.BD3446916A3E813C@lp.airnews.net>`

```
01 HEX-BYTE.
    88  DECIMALS    PIC X
            VALUE 0 THRU 9.
    88  TEN                PIC X
            VALUE "A".
    88  ELEVEN        PIC X
            VALUE "B".
    88  TWELVE        PIC X
            VALUE "C".
    88  THIRTEEN      PIC X
            VALUE "D".
    88  FOURTEEN    PIC X
            VALUE "E".
    88  FIFTEEN         PIC X
            VALUE "F".

01  DECIMAL-BYTE        PIC 9(2).


EVALUATE HEX-BYTE
    WHEN DECIMALS
        MOVE DECIMALS TO DECIMAL-BYTE
     WHEN TEN
        MOVE 10 TO DECIMAL-BYTE
      WHEN ELEVEN
        MOVE 11 TO DECIMAL-BYTE

        (and so on)

END-EVALUATE.
```

#### ↳ Re: HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION.

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T18:49:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010512144955.18397.00000862@nso-fe.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6A0A057F5145C41C.E34D574C40977678.BD3446916A3E813C@lp.airnews.net>`

```
In article <6A0A057F5145C41C.E34D574C40977678.BD3446916A3E813C@lp.airnews.net>,
"just_looking_m35" <just_looking_m35@yahoo.com> writes:

>
>05 BINARY-GRP-X.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'd say that you have the right idea, just need to clean-up
your data name references and fix your BINARY declaration.
Best rule of thumb for working with BINARY is
2-bytes = 9(04) BINARY
4-bytes = 9(08) BINARY
8-bytes = 9(16) BINARY
Also be aware that a 9(04) BINARY can hold values up to 
9(05), so be careful about truncation.
```

#### ↳ Re: HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-13T00:01:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x9kL6.4198$_Y4.226091@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<6A0A057F5145C41C.E34D574C40977678.BD3446916A3E813C@lp.airnews.net>`

```
just_looking_m35 <just_looking_m35@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:6A0A057F5145C41C.E34D574C40977678.BD3446916A3E813C@lp.airnews.net...
> I currently reading a file, that stores a count in a one byte HEX. How do
> convert this value to a three byte demical number......

AS I OFTEN SAY: "THERE IS NO SUCH THING AS 'HEX' DATA. DATA ARE DATA."

Assuming "hex byte" means an eight-bit character in which each bit
represents a power of two, that is,

hexbyte (1) =  x'01'
hexbyte (2) =  x'02'
hexbyte(15) = x'0F'
hexbyte (16) = x'10'
hexbyte(255) = x'FF'

... the below will work.

OTOH, if "hex byte" means a display character in the ranges '0' - '9' and
'A' -'F' then that's a horse of a different color and below will not work.


01  VALUE-1.
     05 FILLER     PIC X(01) value x'00'.
     05 THE-CHAR  PIC X(01)
01  VALUE-NUM REDEFINES VALUE-1    PIC S9(04) BINARY.
move byte-from-file to the-char
move value-num to anynumericfield

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION.

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-13T12:55:22+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gda9oa0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<6A0A057F5145C41C.E34D574C40977678.BD3446916A3E813C@lp.airnews.net>`

```
On Sat, 12 May 2001 12:04:46 -0500, just_looking_m35 wrote:

>I currently reading a file, that stores a count in a one byte HEX. How do
>convert this value to a three byte demical number. I tried using a redefine
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi,

What platform are you on? If it is on INTEL pc, be aware that binary fields
are low-order byte first, so you should do the move to BYTE-1X

Also, make sure that the picture you use for the field defines a two byte
field.

Hope this helps,

Willem Clements
BrainBench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

#### ↳ Re: HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION.

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-13T19:34:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<olBL6.172292$8j.17487598@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<6A0A057F5145C41C.E34D574C40977678.BD3446916A3E813C@lp.airnews.net>`

```
There may be a compiler option to allow 1-byte computational fields.
DO NOT USE THIS COBOL EQUIVALENT OF ANTHRAX. Hundreds of other
problems will be created that you will spend your career trying to
find.

You have eight bits (1 byte) containing a count. These 8-bits
(XXXXXXXX) are now addressable as one byte. For an INTEL PC you have
to get these 8 bits into two bytes that look like

XXXXXXXX 00000000

Then address these 16 bits (2 bytes) as a  number defined as COMP-5.

So, a definition like:

01  DISPLAY-DATA.
     02  WORK-CHAR    PIC X.
      02  FILLER       PIC X VALUE LOW-VALUES.
01  FILLER REDEFINES DISPLAY-DATA.
      02  MYNUMBER   PIC S9(3) COMP-5.

will work swell. Move the single byte from the record to WORK-CHAR,
add 1 to MYNUMBER, then put WORK-CHAR back in the record.

BUT. If you really want to be cute, do this:

COMPUTE MYNUMBER = FUNCTION ORD(IN-CHAR) - 1

ORD returns the number in the collating sequence of a single
character. You've got to subtract 1 from whatever ORD returns since
the 1st value in the sequence is zero. A hex '64' comes back as 101.

Unfortunately, there is no UNORD to get your result BACK to one byte.

You could, I guess, do this:

COMPUTE MYNUMBER = FUNCTION ORD(record-byte).
MOVE WORK-CHAR TO record-byte.

which has the effect of incrementing record-byte by one.


"just_looking_m35" <just_looking_m35@yahoo.com> wrote in message

<a id="t-9e422c85b6"></a>
## Thread: HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION. — 5 msgs

### HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION.

- **From:** "just_looking_m35" <just_looking_m35@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T12:11:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ED0FEF96BDAC9C4.15B3237DA27AA1D6.18D4A0515236F1FC@lp.airnews.net>`

```
I currently reading a file, that stores a count in a one byte HEX. How do
convert this value to a three byte demical number. I tried using a redefine
clause to make it binary, then move it to the demical field. Note: Binary
require me to set up a working storage as COBOL-85 lowest field for binary
is two bytes. I thought this would work, but no luck. Could use a little
help on this problem.

05 BINARY-GRP-X.
    10 BYTE-1X      PIC X(01).
    10 BYTE-2X      PIC X(01).

05 BINARY-NUM REDEFINES BINARY-GRP PIC 99 BINARY. - ECT.
05 COUNT-IN-HEX   PIC X(01).

05 COUNT-IN-DEMICAL  PIC 9(03).

MOVE ZEROS TO BINARY-NUM.
MOVE COUNT-IN-HEX TO BYTE-2X.

MOVE BINARY-NUM TO COUNT-IN-DEMICAL.
```

#### ↳ Re: HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION.

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T17:31:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<preL6.2910$j65.226546@www.newsranger.com>`

```

Regards, Jack.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION.

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T18:14:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B4fL6.23160$yw.650816@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<9ED0FEF96BDAC9C4.15B3237DA27AA1D6.18D4A0515236F1FC@lp.airnews.net> <preL6.2910$j65.226546@www.newsranger.com>`

```
> just_looking_m35 says...
> >
> >I currently reading a file, that stores a count in a one byte HEX. How do
> >convert this value to a three byte demical number. I tried using a
redefine
> >clause to make it binary, then move it to the demical field. Note: Binary
> >require me to set up a working storage as COBOL-85 lowest field for
binary
> >is two bytes. I thought this would work, but no luck. Could use a little
> >help on this problem.
> >


The following is Fujitsu code that works.

01 HEX-CHARACTER        PICTURE X.
01 BINARY-BYTE REDEFINES CHARACTER-IN PICTURE 9(9) COMP-5.
01 THREE-BYTE-NUMBER        PICTURE 9(9).

    MOVE HEX-BYTE-FROM-FILE TO HEX-CHARACTER.
    MOVE BINARY-BYTE TO THREE-BYTE-NUMBER.
```

#### ↳ Re: HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-13T14:53:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dmopf$4lh$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9ED0FEF96BDAC9C4.15B3237DA27AA1D6.18D4A0515236F1FC@lp.airnews.net>`

```
You don't tell us which compiler (or operating system) you are using.  If
you have a compiler that supports Intrinsic Functions, the EASIEST way to
convert a one-byte binary field to a "decimal" value is by using the
intrinsic function "ORD", i.e.,

  Compute Decimal-Field = (Function Ord  (Hex-one-byte-field)) - 1

The "minus 1" is because FUNCTION ORD goes from 1 to 256 instead of the
"usually" desirable 0 to 255.

If you do NOT have intrinsic functions available to you, then there are a
variety of other ways - but tell us about your compiler and OS and we can
answer appropriately.
```

#### ↳ Re: HEX TO NUMERIC - QUESTION.

- **From:** Stephen J Spiro <donotreply@interbulletin.bogus>
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T05:07:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AFF6813.443D2D02@interbulletin.com>`
- **References:** `<9ED0FEF96BDAC9C4.15B3237DA27AA1D6.18D4A0515236F1FC@lp.airnews.net>`

```
"just_looking_m35" <just_looking_m35@yahoo.com> wrote  : 
>I currently reading a file, that stores a count in a one byte HEX. How do
>convert this value to a three byte demical number. I tried using a redefine
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
01  BINARY-GROUP-X.
    04  BYTE-1X      PIC X  VALUE X'00'.
    04  BYTE-2X      PIC X.
01  HALFWORD REDEFINES BINARY-GROUP-X
                     PIC 9(04) USAGE IS BINARY.    >>* or USAGE IS COMP.
-
-
    05 BINARY-NUM REDEFINES BINARY-GRP PIC 99 BINARY. - ECT.
    05 COUNT-IN-HEX   PIC X(01).
-
    05 COUNT-IN-DEMICAL  PIC 9(03).
-
-
    MOVE COUNT-IN-HEX TO BYTE-2X.
    MOVE HALFWORD TO COUNT-IN-DEMICAL.

If your computer does not support hex literals, use
    MOVE LOW-VALUES TO BYTE-1X.


This assumes that you are not using Intel, and that the high-order bits are on the left.

You need true binary data.... moving zero to an alphanumeric BYTE-1X gets you DISPLAY zeroes, not binary.



Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems

PS: The word is DECIMAL, not demical.
_______________________________________________
Submitted via WebNewsReader of http://www.interbulletin.com
```

<a id="t-19dd390327"></a>
## Thread: Questions about COBOL on NT — 3 msgs

### Questions about COBOL on NT

- **From:** mano@mano.com (freewheelin)
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T19:21:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3afd801b.3200143@news.uswest.net>`

```
I have to choose a COBOL compiler for an NT platform which will use MS
SQL Server for database access.   The app has no screens - it's a
suite of batch transaction processor programs being ported from an
older platform. 

Microsoft has promoted their server synchronization products to
address what we're doing, however, after reviewing their Replicator,
it simply does not let us scale our business logic without
re-engineering every line of code we have.

 I'm trying to understand if this can be developed and deployed in
this environment by simply rewritting the database interfaces.  The
folks at Merant tend to reply with sales brochures - not much help.  

I require COBOL programs to run on the server and access MS SQL Server
running on the same server, so I'm not clear on the topology, the
requirements, the ability for it to work.

1.  Can it be done ?  Has anyone done this and what did you use?

2.  Must a COBOL runtime package be deployed on the server platform?
Isn't this different from database access originating from a client?

3. I expect the ODBC API will do the translation - yet the client
resides on the server.  Is there anything extra I'm missing here?

4.  We'll be replacing the older database CALLs with SQL.  
Must I embedd SQL or do I have the option to store procedures and
access them with COBOL? 


Thanks.

John.
```

#### ↳ Re: Questions about COBOL on NT

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-12T21:17:36+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<il4rftkkt99qi3sqcm9uk41vigu5v20nar@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3afd801b.3200143@news.uswest.net>`

```
Hi,

See below.

On Sat, 12 May 2001 19:21:59 GMT, mano@mano.com (freewheelin) wrote:

>I have to choose a COBOL compiler for an NT platform which will use MS
>SQL Server for database access.   The app has no screens - it's a
>suite of batch transaction processor programs being ported from an
>older platform. 
It might help you mention what was the original platform as well as
any other detail that may be of use in order to determine whether
there are tools available to convert such programs ir required, or
even use them without conversion.

(and do you really need to go to MS?? Could you not use Oracle (and
all the possible platforms it works on)?? (just an idea))

>
> I'm trying to understand if this can be developed and deployed in
>this environment by simply rewritting the database interfaces.  The
>folks at Merant tend to reply with sales brochures - not much help.  
See above

>
>I require COBOL programs to run on the server and access MS SQL Server
>running on the same server, so I'm not clear on the topology, the
>requirements, the ability for it to work.
It really doesn't matter where the server is. It could even be
accessed over Internet (if things are done properlly, (and ignoring
all security issues...)).
I have clients that have the server accessed via a 128K leased line,
and others that use the main application in the same server as the SQL
server.
>
>1.  Can it be done ?  Has anyone done this and what did you use?
Is it can, and yes there are a few that have done something similar.
>
>2.  Must a COBOL runtime package be deployed on the server platform?
>Isn't this different from database access originating from a client?
This will depend on what and how you intend on doing it.
>
>3. I expect the ODBC API will do the translation - yet the client
>resides on the server.  Is there anything extra I'm missing here?
It doesn't matter. See above.
>
>4.  We'll be replacing the older database CALLs with SQL.  
>Must I embedd SQL or do I have the option to store procedures and
>access them with COBOL? 
Depending on what you have and depending on the COBOL compiler you may
use both or either.
Also note that using ODBC calls is not the same as using embedded SQL.

And (again) which was the old database??


I would get a evaluation of both Merant and Fujitsu COBOL (and
eventually IBM Visual Age ???) and try them before deciding on which
to use.

Cheers

Frederico Fonseca
```

- **From:** mano@mano.com (freewheelin)
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T04:48:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aff611c.7856448@news.uswest.net>`

```
Free Artificial Intelligence Neural Network Library!

If you are proficient with Cobol, then I invite you to visit
http://www.rgsoftware.com to download the NN50.DLL
library. The regular cost of registration for this library is $240.00

We will be offering FREE copies of this library to those proficient
in various languages - for a limited time. This is an effort to have
our NN50 example project ported to many different languages.

To obtain your free copy of NN50.DLL (a $240.00 value):

1) Check http://www.rgsoftware.com/nn50port.htm
   to see if your language is still open. If it is not,
   please send us an email pledging your work so we can
   update our page. Send your email to support@rgsoftware.com

2) Download NN50.DLL from http://www.rgsoftware.com

3) Familiarize yourself with NN50.DLL by using the
   pseudo Visual Basic code and NN50.chm help file.
   Contact support@rgsoftware.com for technical support.

4) Port the Example project to your language and include
   error handling (this code will be redistributed). Also, please
   make sure to comment your code extensively!
   You may also comment in your company name and address
   which will be redistributed with your code.

5) Submit your example code and a compiled version of the
   example to sales@rgsoftware.com

After your code has been reviewed, you will receive the registration
for the NN50.DLL library.

You may also design GUI and database projects for NN50.DLL
Please contact sales@rgsoftware.com for more information about
compensation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I remove a record from an indexed (ISAM) file?

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-05-13T17:44:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-NOdnqoLXHlHF@24-108-102-82.ivideon.com>`
- **References:** `<3afea7c0$0$22672$62ce1842@SSP1NO55> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-TclyB40ejBUm@24-108-102-82.ivideon.com> <3afebcb4$0$16696$62ce1842@SSP1NO55>`

```
On Sun, 13 May 2001 16:56:49, "Roman Prem" <roman@a1plus.at> wrote:

> <lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca> schrieb
> > On Sun, 13 May 2001 15:27:25, "Roman Prem" <roman@a1plus.at> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> delete myfile

The above line must be

DELETE myfile RECORD

> 
> or if not: what is wrong with it?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The DELETE myfile statement is used to delete the entire
file rather than a record within the file. The delete for a 
file only works when the file is not opened (this is a safety
measure to prevent a random coding mistake resulting
in a no longer present data file - which is NOT a good thing :-)
```

Reference Summary GX20-1850-07")- one you can
keep in a desk drawer- from the OS/390 Internet
Library: http://www-1.ibm.com/servers/s390/os390/bkserv/. Click on
"How to Order IBM Publications", "IBM Publications
Center US Web Site", "Search for Publications". It
costs $1.95.

<a id="t-81968e530e"></a>
## Thread: COBOL HELP — 1 msgs

### Re: COBOL HELP

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-13T20:45:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dnde9$ssm$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9dm5f5$d7t$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
1) Use of "comp.lang.cobol" rather than "alt.cobol" OFTEN gets better/faster
replies

2) "run time errors" are NOT universal, so we would need to know (at a
minimum) which compiler (vendor and release) and operating system you are
using.

3) Do you get ANY other "run-time error" message besides the "45"?  If so,
what?

4)  Without any other information, the first GUESS that I would make (and
this may be completely wrong) is that you are getting a "file status 45" on
some I/O operation.  Look that up in your reference material and see if it
might apply to where your program is having a problem.
```

---

for screwing up path additions.

- **References:** `<9doarp$jlk$1@news.btv.ibm.com> <85823CCDC6046573.B61E40ECAB83B5B4.5D5B33F08206E400@lp.airnews.net> <9dqhb6$ld6$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`

```
Prakash Narayanaswamy <nprakash@in.ibm.com> wrote in message
news:9dqhb6$ld6$1@news.btv.ibm.com...
>     . ... if I have to start from scratch and go on searching the net then
why am I
> subscribed to the group. If someone here has taken all the trouble to do
> something similar then rather than me spending a lot of time starting from
> scratch I could very well use his resourcefulness. Hope you agree on that.

Well, I *almost* agree. Had you asked a SPECIFIC question, you might have
gotten a SPECIFIC answer.

But getting a reply to this  vague and general inquiry ("any
information..." ) will generally happen only if your 'subscription' is fully
paid up, and paid in the only currency accepted: your own contributions.

Most people with paid subscriptions are professionals who DONATE their time
and expertise. They (we?) will help you,  but most of us have a congenital
aversion to 'doing the work for you.'  Basic research is your job.

In fairness, there is some enlightened self-interest at work here: by
facilitating your 'instant expertise'  with SAS, we 'cheapen'  the
consulting profession as a whole and thus our own market value.

Perhaps your choice of words was ill-considered; but it sure comes across as
" I have an ISP and a newsgroup reader; therefore I am entitled to the
collective wisdom of others." It don't work that way.
```

## Thread: 88 Level Q — 14 msgs

### 88 Level Q

- **From:** Aaron J Roquena <aroque1@uic.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T09:47:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.GSO.4.10.10105140931520.6546-100000@icarus.cc.uic.edu>`

```
        I've just been thrown into some COBOL programming recently. I got
a COBOL book but it doesn't discuss scope very well. I was wondering if
something like this is legal. COND-1 and COND-2 are conditionals used for
2 fields. I would like to use the same name becuase logically they
represent identical things. Should I make  FIELD-1's conditionals have a
different nameS than FIELD-2's? No one over here knows.

01 FILE-LAYOUT.
   05 FIELD-1                    PIC X(1).
      88 COND-1                   VAL '0'.
      88 COND-2                   VAL 'A'.
   05 FIELD-2                    PIC X(1).
      88 COND-1                   VAL '0'.
      88 COND-2                   VAL 'A'.

 Thanks for your time!
```

#### ↳ Re: 88 Level Q

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T15:26:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HOSL6.5041$DW1.227334@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.GSO.4.10.10105140931520.6546-100000@icarus.cc.uic.edu>`

```
In article <Pine.GSO.4.10.10105140931520.6546-100000@icarus.cc.uic.edu>,
Aaron J Roquena  <aroque1@uic.edu> wrote:
>        I've just been thrown into some COBOL programming recently.

Then your boss deserves what s/he gets.

>I got
>a COBOL book but it doesn't discuss scope very well. I was wondering if
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>different nameS than FIELD-2's?

I would say 'yes'... and I believe that a few compilers might agree with
me.

>No one over here knows.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for your time!

Well, as Wittgenstein tells us, 'Logic is a game played by a particular
set of rules'... so I would argue that the two conditions do *not*
'logically... represent identical things'.  Consider:

01  FILE-LAYOUT.
    05 FIELD-1                    PIC X(1). 
       88 FLD1-CONTAINS-A-ZERO     VALUE '0'.
       88 FLD1-CONTAINS-AN-A       VALUE 'A'.
    05 FIELD-2                    PIC X(1).
       88 FLD2-CONTAINS-A-ZERO     VALUE '0'.
       88 FLD2-CONTAINS-AN-A       VALUE 'A'.

To say that these are logically identical seems like granting such
equivalence to conditions such as:

05  YOUR-BANK-BALANCE PIC S9(11)V99 VALUE +0.
    88  YOUR-BALANCE-IS-HIGH VALUE +1000000.
05  MY-BANK-BALANCE   PIC S9(11)V99 VALUE +0.
    88  MY-BALANCE-IS-HIGH   VALUE +1000000.
...
MOVE +1000000 TO YOUR-BANK-BALANCE.
EVALUATE TRUE
    IF MY-BALANCE-IS-HIGH
        PERFORM TITHE-TO-CHARITIES THRU TTC-EX
         UNTIL APPROPRIATE-OBLIGATIONS-FULFILLED
        PERFORM LIVE-WELL-N-EASY THRU LWNE-EX
         UNTIL CONSIDERED-A-CREDIT-2-COMMUNITY
    END-IF
END-EVALUATE.

... et and cetera.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: 88 Level Q

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T18:45:11+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<suuvftop19gfrjetaolrkljlrnn8f75s69@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.GSO.4.10.10105140931520.6546-100000@icarus.cc.uic.edu>`

```
On Mon, 14 May 2001 09:47:29 -0500 Aaron J Roquena <aroque1@uic.edu> wrote:

:>        I've just been thrown into some COBOL programming recently. I got
:>a COBOL book but it doesn't discuss scope very well. I was wondering if
:>something like this is legal. COND-1 and COND-2 are conditionals used for
:>2 fields. I would like to use the same name becuase logically they
:>represent identical things. Should I make  FIELD-1's conditionals have a
:>different nameS than FIELD-2's? No one over here knows.

:>01 FILE-LAYOUT.
:>   05 FIELD-1                    PIC X(1).
:>      88 COND-1                   VAL '0'.
:>      88 COND-2                   VAL 'A'.
:>   05 FIELD-2                    PIC X(1).
:>      88 COND-1                   VAL '0'.
:>      88 COND-2                   VAL 'A'.

You don't have to, but the fields become quite clumsy if you don't - you would
have to qualify them, i.e., COND-1 of FIELD-1. 
```

Hi Aaron,

Here's a few ways to "work around" the prob:

Code the conds F1-COND1, F2-COND1, etc
Move fld1 fld2 to a wrk-fld with the 88s there, then code IF WRK-COND-1, etc.
not sure about this: code IF COND-1 OF FIELD-2, etc. Not sure if OF works
w'conditionals.

Hope this helps, Jack.

Roquena says...

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

Yes, "of" works with 88-levels.  Whether or not it is a "good" way to
program is another question.  I find it fairly unusual and medium-ugly - but
there is nothing "theoretically" wrong with it.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 88 Level Q

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T16:58:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0105251558.21b69011@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.GSO.4.10.10105140931520.6546-100000@icarus.cc.uic.edu> <9em39m$7he$3@news.utelfla.com>`

```
weberm@news.polaris.net (Michael Weber) wrote here knows.
> : 
> : 01 FILE-LAYOUT.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> If FIELD-1 and FIELD-2 are the same, why use two variables?

Come on! FIELD-1 and FIELD-2 are NOT the same, they are two different
fields whose data may or may not be the same. They MAY contain the
same values, or they may not!

Stephen J Spiro

#### ↳ Re: 88 Level Q

- **From:** gencbl <azabala@cobol.com.ar>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T19:12:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e8yP6.425$G45.24150@news1.onlynews.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.GSO.4.10.10105140931520.6546-100000@icarus.cc.uic.edu>`

```


Aaron: write VALUE (no VAL) This source code can help you ?

       working-storage section. 01 FILE-LAYOUT. 05 FIELD-1 PIC X(1). 88
       COND-1 VALUE '0'. 88 COND-2 VALUE 'A'. 05 FIELD-2 PIC X(1). 88
       COND-1 VALUE '0'. 88 COND-2 VALUE 'A'.

       procedure division. entra. set cond-1 of field-1 to true set
       cond-2 of field-2 to true

           if cond-1 of field-1 display 'Condicion 1 del field-1 true'
           end-if if cond-2 of field-1 display 'Condicion 2 del field-1
           true' end-if

           if cond-1 of field-2 display 'Condicion 1 del field-2 true'
           end-if if cond-2 of field-2 display 'Condicion 2 del field-2
           true' end-if .



Aaron J Roquena  wrote:
  > I've just been thrown into some COBOL programming recently. I got a
  > COBOL book but it doesn't discuss scope very well. I was wondering if
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
  >  Thanks for your time!

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 88 Level Q

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T22:03:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010526180358.25580.00001676@ng-ca1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<e8yP6.425$G45.24150@news1.onlynews.com>`

```
>Date: 2001-05-25 15:12 Eastern Daylight Time
>Message-id: <e8yP6.425$G45.24150@news1.onlynews.com>
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Just to add some explanation to Mr. Zabala's code:  

The "05 FIELD-1" and "05 FIELD-2" does not mean they are
the same data.  Quite the opposite.  It means that they
are two different pieces of data of the same hierarchical rank. 
If this is all the fields declared under the "01" level, then
you have a record with a length of 2 bytes.  The first byte
is named "FIELD-1" and the second byte is named 
"FIELD-2."  
Although I cannot recommend using the same name for
the conditions, it is legal.  To interrogate a particular condition
you must "qualify" the reference--that is, tell the compiler
specifically which of your conditions you are talking about.  
So you must say "COND-2 OF FIELD-1" if you mean 
"FIELD-1  =  'A' " and you must say "COND-2 OF FIELD-2"
if you mean "FIELD-2 = 'A' " [double-quotes are mine.] 

Identical names (requiring the "OF" qualification) were very
popular when programs had to keyed in on punch cards.  
Giving boolean-like 88-level identifiers the same name
mystifies me.  It negates whatever dubious advantage
there may be to using 88's.  

Though the same code letter (zero of 'A') is being used, I
don't think they are "logically the same."  To me, it looks
like sheer coincidence.  
If FIELD-1 represents "DWI violations" and FIELD-2 represents 
"DUS violations" and if in both cases the code '0' means
"never got one" and code 'A' means "All the frigging time" 
then the two fields (not the conditions) are logically 
similar--but not identical.  Right?  After all, next week the
director may decide to add Code 'J' (meaning Just once but
it was a long time ago) as a possibility for the Driving-While-
Impaired field, but not for the Driving-Under-Suspension field.  
The following week he may announce that henceforth the
DWI field will be using the code 'A' to mean "Alcohol blood 
level of 2.2 or higher" and that code 'C' is going to mean 
"Constantly getting busted for drunk driving." 

I would rewrite your datanames as:  
01 AARONS-DRIVING-RECORD-LAYOUT. 
    05 ADR-FIELD-1-DWI-VIOLATIONS PIC X(1). 
         88 NO-VIOLATIONS-DWI       VALUE 'N'. 
         88 MANY-VIOLATIONS-DWI  VALUE 'A'. 
    05 ADR-FIELD-1-DUS-VIOLATIONS PIC X(1). 
         88 NO-VIOLATIONS-DUS       VALUE 'N'. 
         88 MANY-VIOLATIONS-DUS  VALUE 'A'. 

(Just to be extra picky, I have changed your term "FILE-LAYOUT"
to "RECORD-LAYOUT."  You're happy, right?
Also, I don't like your mixing numbers and letters--purely
for aesthetic reasons--so I changed your '0' to 'N.'  You're
still happy, right?)



  

Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 88 Level Q

- **From:** "Sal Cambareri" <sal@lasermax.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T13:44:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GEdQ6.6217$X4.649600@nntp2.onemain.com>`
- **References:** `<e8yP6.425$G45.24150@news1.onlynews.com> <20010526180358.25580.00001676@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
An alternative to unique condition names and qualification.

I'm not sure about the COBOL standard, but I know that AcuCobol supports the
following alternative for Aaron's code"

01 FILE-LAYOUT.
    03 FIELD-NAME       PIC X       OCCURS 2.
        88 COND-1            VALUE '0'.
        88 COND-2            VALUE 'A'.

Then in the procedure division COND-1(n) uniquely identifies the condition.

"Ross Klatte" <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message

they

#### ↳ Re: 88 Level Q

- **From:** idnonada@home.com (TePeTo)
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T18:10:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d1ee8a26.0105301710.65c92f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.GSO.4.10.10105140931520.6546-100000@icarus.cc.uic.edu>`

```
Aaron J Roquena <aroque1@uic.edu> wrote in message news:<Pine.GSO.4.10.10105140931520.6546-100000@icarus.cc.uic.edu>...
> I've just been thrown into some COBOL programming recently. I got
> a COBOL book but it doesn't discuss scope very well. I was wondering if
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Thanks for your time!
Perhaps the best ("quality") feature of Cobol is readability and self
documentation, IF the QUALITY of source code is kept in mind with the
rules of KISS.  Keep It Simple...  Reusing condition names or data
tags, or qualifying with "such-and-such" OF "so-and-so" is confusing
at best.  My experience has been that COMPILERS ARE DIFFERENT.  What
works right with one compiler may work differently with another
compiler *EVEN IN THE SAME SHOP*, and especially if multiple operating
systems (AND/OR) compilers are used.  Also keep future changes in
vendors (IBM or NCR to UNISYS), etc.  There is little I can add to
what others have written in response to your question, as they all
have good points.  Use techniques that will be compatible WITH ANY
COMPILER.  Also ensure that code such as (IF NOT condition OR NOT
condition) is ALWAYS AVOIDED.  Use (IF NOT condition AND NOT
condition) instead.  Avoid NOT OR NOT.
Good luck.  Jim
```
