<a id="t-130a59d0f9"></a>
## Thread: cobol as a structured language — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### re: re: re: cobol as a structured language

- **From:** rdriehle@nps.navy.mil ("Riehle, Richard")
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T16:38:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F5AD48747FC0324EB21B2B2BD27D5E8698B517@Saipan>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com <mailto:ricksmith@aiservices.com>>
wrote:

>I suppose it inevitable that, every year or so, someone must clarify the
difference 
>between "structured programming" and "modular programming."

Any attempt to do structured programming without paying attention to
modularity
is likely to be a failure.    In COBOL, this fails because structured
programming
has been so dependent on Perform statements, traditionally implemented on 
modules that refer to global data.    

 >So Richard Riehle, Pete, Jimmy, and others, here is the clarification. 
>Structured programming refers to the arrangement of statements in a
procedure 
>and uses words like: sequence, choice, and iteration, to describe the
structure 
>of the procedure. 

I am quite familiar with this notion of fundamental structures.   In fact,
my original
argument was that these were inadequate prior to ANSI-85 because there was
no
support for elementary structures.   A sequence has a sequence terminator.
The period
in COBOL was primitive support that that.   A selection statement did not
have a 
terminator that permitted arrangement of well-scoped if statements.    An
iteration could
only be formed using a perform of a module.    

>Modular programming refers to the decomposition of systems 
>and uses words like: cohesion, coupling, encapsulation, and interface, to 
>describe the relationship between separate procedures. Procedural
decomposition (PD) 
>and object-orientation (OO) are types of modular programming. Both PD
modules 
>and OO methods may consist of "structured" code (or they may be spaghetti).


Sorry, must respectfully disagree.   You are certainly correct that the
original meaning of
structured programming was founded on the work of Jacopini and Bohm along
with the
famous paper by Dijkstra.   I have read the literature.   We simply have a
different 
interpretation of it.   The literature, and the theory of structured
programming, did not
stop with the pronouncements of the early writers.   

>There is no comparison or contrast between "structured programming" and
"object-oriented 
>programming". They occur at different levels. Pete, OOP will never replace
"structured"; 
>though it may replace PD. ;-) 

It is true that elementary structures are essential to well-formed programs.
An elementary 
structure has an entry point and a scope.    COBOL structures, prior to
ANSI-85, did not 
sufficiently satisfy this requirement.     In OOP, both elementary
structures and modularity
are essential.    The kind of modules (classes) are different, but they are
modules.

>Richard, what constitutes structured programming is well-defined and your
description 
>is so broad as to be incorrect. 

And yours is too narrow to be useful.  

>See, Youdon, "Techniques of Program Structure and 
>Design," Prentice-Hall, 1973, for more complete descriptions of both
modular programming 
>and structured programming. 

Structured programming requires modular programming.    They are so closely
intermixed, for
any kind of production code that only toy programs could be written without
considering them
both in the same way.    You may want to believe that one can do structured
programming without
well-formed modules, but that would not get you very far in writing
structured programs.   

>You might choose to re-read the chapters on "Modularity" in 
>"Structured Design" with the idea that it is a discussion of modular
programming; 
>not structured programming. ;-) 

There is a very good reason why this set of chapters is in the referenced
book.   Structured
Design requires modularity.     Even if you want to assert that structured
programming does
not require modularity,  it is difficult to imagine doing structured design
without it.   And
modularity requires some understanding of the scope, visibility, protection,
and interfaces
of modules.   

We have come a long way from the "two elementary formations" of Jacopini and
Bohm.   In fact,
a strict interpretation of their paper would suggest that  the early Yourdon
statements were too
extreme.   A technology does not stand still.     A definition of structured
programming that fails
to acknowledge the integral importance of modularity, is a definition that
falls short of its intention. 

Richard Riehle
```

#### ↳ Re: re: re: cobol as a structured language

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T11:18:55+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b006ab8_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<F5AD48747FC0324EB21B2B2BD27D5E8698B517@Saipan>`

```
Ah, Richard, I had a feeling you would respond to Rick's post... <G>

Good stuff. Obviously, interpretation has much to do with the perceptions we
all have.

What I have learned from this correspondence is that we (well, me,
actually...<G>) should be careful and meticulous about the terms we use to
describe Programming Techniques as they may have different degrees of
accuracy, depending on how strongly the recipient feels about it.

I have followed your posts on this with interest and can see that guys like
yourself and Rick feel very strongly  (and are extremely well-informed) on
these matters.

Personally, I am happy to write code that works and can be maintained, and
I'm really not too bothered whether it complies with an Academic yardstick.

But I can afford that luxury because most of what I write these days is for
myself. I no longer make my living as a "Programmer", and continue to write
code simply because I love to do it and have done it all my working life.

CLC is richer for the correspondence that has occurred between posters like
yourself and Rick.

Thanks for that.

Pete.



Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: re: re: cobol as a structured language

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T00:40:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9dr5kd$1jh8$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<F5AD48747FC0324EB21B2B2BD27D5E8698B517@Saipan>`

```
Each discipline has words and phrases used to communicate
the knowledge of that discipline. The definition of those words
and the meaning of those phrases are discussed and refined
until they well-understood by the practitioners of that discipline.
This knowledge is passed to current and new practitioners.
It has been that way for thousands of years.

The meanings of "structured programming" and "modular
progamming" were discussed and refined, by the mid-70's.

I am suggesting, that by providing a meaning for "structured
programming" that is different than the meaning discussed
and refined, earlier; you violated a few thousand years of
tradition in education. If I seem too serious, that is probably
the reason! ;-)

Other than the implications of my comments, above, you
and I are in substantial agreement.
------------------
Rick Smith

"Riehle, Richard" wrote in message ...
>"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com <mailto:ricksmith@aiservices.com>>
>wrote:
[...]
>>Modular programming refers to the decomposition of systems
>>and uses words like: cohesion, coupling, encapsulation, and interface, to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>and OO methods may consist of "structured" code (or they may be
spaghetti).
>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>stop with the pronouncements of the early writers.
[...]
>>Richard, what constitutes structured programming is well-defined and your
>description
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>modularity requires some understanding of the scope, visibility,
protection,
>and interfaces
>of modules.
>
>We have come a long way from the "two elementary formations" of Jacopini
and
>Bohm.   In fact,
>a strict interpretation of their paper would suggest that  the early
Yourdon
>statements were too
>extreme.   A technology does not stand still.     A definition of
structured
>programming that fails
>to acknowledge the integral importance of modularity, is a definition that
>falls short of its intention.
>
>Richard Riehle

<a id="t-f72bd9fe17"></a>
## Thread: Coding an ODBC statement — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Coding an ODBC statement

- **From:** "Stephen Plotnick" <thepla@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-14T18:10:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b006832_4@news1.prserv.net>`

```
I have been able to create an SQL statement directly into Access to get a
desired result. I am now trying to create this statement into my Realia 3.1
ODBC and I am not being successful. Does anybody know the correct syntax for
the following statement?

SELECT *
FROM LongDesc
WHERE (LongDesc_Description LIKE 'drill*' or LongDesc_Description LIKE '*
drill*') and (LongDesc_Description LIKE 'master*' or LongDesc_Description
LIKE '* master*');

I am successful with single entry exact matches and starting with matches, I
need to substitute a "%" for the "*" for the starting with scenario.

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Coding an ODBC statement

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T19:01:32+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b00d8e5_4@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3b006832_4@news1.prserv.net>`

```
Stephen,

What you are doing is excellent and I recommend people to "try" their SQL in
ACCESS Query mode, then cut and paste it into COBOL. (I do this myself,
especially where complex joins are involved, and it has saved me hours of
sweat.)

There are a couple of pitfalls though...

1.The syntax through ODBC may not be EXACTLY the same as that used in ACCESS
SQL Query (although it is usually very close...)

2. You must NOT specify the semi-colon terminator used in ACCESS, when using
COBOL.

You don't say whether the "failure" is due to a syntax error or whatever.
Please give the SQLSTATE when requesting assistance.

Here's a stab at what you requested, but without the SQLSTATE I can't be
sure...

EXEC SQL
   SELECT *
   FROM LongDesc
   WHERE ((LongDesc_Description LIKE 'drill*') OR
                   (LongDesc_Description LIKE '*drill*'))
                                                 AND
                  ((LongDesc_Description LIKE 'master*') OR
                  (LongDesc_Description LIKE '* master*'))
END-EXEC

Although it is syntactically correct, it is logically invalid and will
always return SQLSTATE 02000.

Cut and paste it into your program and you'll see what I mean.

Furthermore, ACCESS (used from COBOL) may grizzle at the underlines.  If so,
try removing them from the column names.

Hope this helps,

Pete.

3.1

for

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-c4007d9f32"></a>
## Thread: Structured Programming — 2 this month / 43 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Structured Programming

- **From:** "charles" <fjlk@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T00:55:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w8%L6.10374$K5.863735@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
Oh my what is structured programming?

This is a can of worms.

According to my original Business Programming Logic book,

The 4 major structures are:
    Sequence Structures.
    Decision Structures.
    Do-while structures.
    Do-until structures.

The programs are all a collection of paragraphs called Processes.  These are
a kind of pieces of reusable code that do a specific task.  The whole object
is to have specific ways of doing things.

The term procedure is only used in conjuction with the term
Procedure-Division.  In this sense a COBOL program is divided up into 4
separate divisions one of them being the procedure division.  According to
the three books I have the procedure division is made up of Paragraphs or
Routines which none of my books refer to procedures.  I guess you can call
them a procedure if you want.  Sometimes these paragraphs are called a
process.  Usually Prcedure is what the entire procedure division does.

In my experience or lack thereof newer books just invent terms so you have
even more terms to understand.  I just stick to the terms that refer to the
language they were designed for.  For instance in C++ you have functions and
in Java you have methods.  COBOL isnt that mystical.

On the other hand the term Procedure is used in my newer RPG book to refer
to single procedure programs with a single entry point.  According to the
book, "Procedures are IBM's most recent RPG enhancement for developing
modular, reusable code."  (Programming in RPG IV by Judy Yaeger). According
to the book peices of code that are compiled separately and called as needed
are called sub-procedures.  In the past programs were written as a single
procedure.  Thus a source to back up the concept of a program being a
procedure.  Therefore the procedure division is simply the part of the
program where the procedure is performed.

Charles.

p.s.  Reading is a dangerous thing.
```

the
> language they were designed for.  For instance in C++ you have functions
and
> in Java you have methods.  COBOL isnt that mystical.

Indeed. I tried writing Forth, back in the early '80's. It was made up of
simple
instructions, that were built up into individual "objects". Ditto for
Modula-2; a
modularised version of Pascal, that I tried in the late 80's. When I learnt
Cobol
in the late '80's, I ws first taught data-oriented programming methodolgy,
which
caused program structures to be created in the form of the input/output data
structures. Sometimes, this ended with "objects", each referring to a
separate
data structure & associated process block.

I'm not suggesting that Java & C++ are just rehashing old techniques, but
rather
they seem to be the latest and perhaps the most successful itteration of the
concepts that keep going round and round every few years.

>
> On the other hand the term Procedure is used in my newer RPG book to refer

<a id="t-033d7b864f"></a>
## Thread: Ugly EVALUATE Statement — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Ugly EVALUATE Statement

- **From:** "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T01:49:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qW%L6.23225$4f7.1816676@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
To complement the "Ugly If" thread, I submit this:

This is, by the way, NOT incremental code - it was all done under a single
change control.

This person loves EVALUATE.  Makes me think of the old saying - "when you
have a hammer, everything looks like a nail".

EVALUATE TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE
  WHEN ADD-ACTION
   ALSO W-DRM-ORIG-PRODUCT EQUAL CC-DDA
   ALSO W-DRM-DRIVER-TYPE EQUAL CC-TB
   ALSO W-DRM-REL-PRODUCT EQUAL CC-DDA
   ALSO W-DRM-INSTRUCTION-CD EQUAL CC-A
    MOVE P-REL-ACCESS-ACTY TO TCB-ACTIVITY
    PERFORM CA000-CALL-PEM
    EVALUATE TRUE
      WHEN M-RESULT-T50000D NOT EQUAL DGR-OK  OR
           M-RESULT-U50810D NOT EQUAL DGR-OK
        CONTINUE
      WHEN OTHER
        EVALUATE TRUE
          WHEN M-DRC-TB-NO-REL-ACTS EQUAL CP-999
            MOVE ZERO TO W-DRM-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
            MOVE COND-CODE-REL-DRC TO TCB-USER-CC
            PERFORM EX000-CALL-CCP
            GO TO ZZ000-END-OF-PROCESSING
          WHEN OTHER
            MOVE M-DRC-TB-NO-REL-ACTS TO M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
            PERFORM UNTIL M-RESULT-U50812D EQUAL DGR-NO-FIND
                       OR M-RESULT-U50812D EQUAL DGR-END-DATA
              ADD +1 TO M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
              MOVE DGA-READ-KEY-EQ TO M-ACTION-U50812D
              MOVE P-REL-ACCESS-ACTY TO TCB-ACTIVITY
              PERFORM CA000-CALL-PEM
            END-PERFORM
            MOVE M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO TO W-DRM-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
        END-EVALUATE
    END-EVALUATE
  WHEN OTHER
    CONTINUE
END-EVALUATE
```

<a id="t-f62c7a4529"></a>
## Thread: J4 and the Standard — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### J4 and the Standard

- **From:** Warren G. Simmons <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T05:09:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103_989899793@micron-450>`

```
Hi Stephen,

As a reader of this newsgroup, and a former member of CODASYL, the 
COBOL Committee, etc. you have given a fair and nearly complete picture 
of the tasks you people handle.  I know from much experience that some 
members contribute lots more than others for the reasons you mentioned.

If a permanent group were established, the problems might varry up or 
down AND the US Standards procedures would still have to be followed.  
Before the effort to make COBOL a standard, things may have worked 
better from time to time. However, there has always been the problems 
you site, and unless a standard is the product of a single organization, it 
will take a lot of time when the differences are not just the size of the 
threads, or the 
interface for some IEEE spec. As some predict, the future holds a change 
in method what will more closely fit the IEEE approach to design.

Meanwhile, I'll tell you is what my boss and member of the EC said to me 
many times from the start. "You have done, are doing, and will continue to 
do good work.  I'm proud of you."  I believe that is true of your group, and I 
also feel that some procedure changes at the standards level would make 
things easier.  The voting is worse than the Floriday Presidential Election.
<G> All of this is from the memory of someone too old to remember, and 
to proud of the effort to forget.

BTW. formerly, consultants were considered vendors, and not users in 
the desired mix of users and vendors.  The lack of "real users" to 
particapate on a continuing basis made this change seem reasonable.

Warren Simmons
```

#### ↳ Re: J4 and the Standard

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T18:30:46+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b00d1b9_4@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<1103_989899793@micron-450>`

```

"Warren G. Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:1103_989899793@micron-450...
>
> As a reader of this newsgroup, and a former member of CODASYL, the
> COBOL Committee, etc. you have given a fair and nearly complete picture
> of the tasks you people handle.
>
Where? I can't find this despite having searched the whole server. Was it a
public post, Warren?

Is it possible that some server messages are not being echoed outside North
America?

The question I would have to ask Stephen, given that the above is true,
would be:

"Having served on CODASYL which went to great pains to ensure that COBOL
would always be in the Public Domain (I'm sure you are familiar with the
famous charter which is supposed to preface all COBOL publications, and
which recognises the hard work and generosity of a number of Corporations
who ALL agreed to donate their efforts, and not claim any part of it as
their own...), how do you reconcile the fact that ANSI (a profit making
corporation) has chosen to remove this charter from the draft standard, and
replace it with a standard ANSI copyright that gives them the sole
copyright, publishing and on-line access rights?

As we have seen with other ANSI administered standards, they make a
non-trivial charge for on-line access and their hard copy publications are
also not cheap.

Under the charter set up by CODASYL it should be illegal to try and
copyright COBOL (It is certainly immoral...<G>)

This is the total antithesis of what was intended and set up by CODASYL in
1959, so I'd be interested to hear how someone who was on both committees
(CODASYL and ANSI) manages to decide that this is OK.

Awaiting Reply ...
(Well, we are being asked to ACCEPT this...<G>)

Pete.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: J4 and the Standard

- **From:** Stephen J Spiro <donotreply@interbulletin.bogus>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T15:29:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B014B71.2A4743BC@interbulletin.com>`
- **References:** `<1103_989899793@micron-450> <3b00d1b9_4@Usenet.com>`

```
Here is the post to which Warren referred. It was listed uder "COBOL as a structured language".

From: Stephen J Spiro    Subject: Re: Cobol as a structured language 
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Everyone is unhappy about how long it takes to update the COBOL standard, and the lack of sensitivity by the "guardians" of COBOL to the COBOL "community".

Ok, here is the truth.
Since I joind the J4 COBOL Committee in 1995, membership has ranged from 10 to 15 members at a time. Currently, we have 12 members.
6 from compiler makers:
IBM
Merant (MicroFocus)
Fujitsu
Unisys
Compaq (Tandem)
Hewlett-Packard
1 from a foreign committee - Information Technology Standards Committee of Japan
1 from a user company - EDS
4 independent consultants:
Stephen J Spiro (ME!) from Wizard Systems
Barry Tauber (self)
William Klein (self)
Bob Karlin (Karlinskorner).

The independent consultants not only pay their own way, and pay an $800/year documentation fee to NCITS, we also LOSE billing time. Barry and I contribute to the discussions , but not many papers. Bill's and Bob's contributions (prodigious lately!) sometimes depend on other factors such as how much their clients need of their time. 

There are some other individuals/companies who SOMETIMES contribute papers or discussion. 

SOME of the other people on the committee get a lot of time from their companies to work on the standard, some do not. Or it can vary.

The COBOL Standard has been completely rewritten from the 1985 edition... the format has changed to be much more readable. It is an incredible effort, by a relatively small number of people. 
It is NOT the fault of NCITS or ANSI or ISO/IEC that it has taken so long. It is because we have added -- and argued over -- an enormous number of new features. 
SPECIFIC changes must be written up in a "Full Revision Change Proposal", which is then discussed at a J4 meeting. Remember, we are writing a STANDARD, which means the language must be complete, accurate and unambiguous. All other places in the standard which may be impacted must be referenced. Impacts (if any) on previous standards must be documented. Etc, etc, etc. After discussion, the proposal is "accepted", "accepted as modified" (which requires a rewrite, but goes directly to the project editor), "accepted, but must be reviewed after suggested changes are made", or "rejected".
THIS IS A LOT OF WORK! 

You want it to go faster? Join the committee. Get your employer to sponsor you for the committee. Write papers even if you are NOT on the committee. Joe Katnic, too late for this standard, but if YOU write up the syntax for the Operating System interfaces (we will get you a template for the format), there is a good chance the features would be in the next standard.

------------
Personally, in spite of all the really good things in the standard, I think there are enough BAD features that I voted AGAINST advancing the standard at the J4 meeting last month. Features are being picked up by compiler makers in response to client requests.... I would rather have the new standard "Excellent and later", rather than "fair and now". 
------------

If you are not contributing to the solution, I cannot take your complaints about delay seriously. Bill Klein I take seriously. I have no reason to believe that better/faster progress would be made if the work of the committee were under different sponsorship. In spite of ISO's grumblings, COBOL really IS in the Public Domain. Anyone out there want to start their own committee to develop the language? If you can convince me that it will do a better job, I'll join you --- but I don't think anyone will take me up seriously.

Stephen J Spiro
President, Wizard Systems
Box 4451
Metuchen, NJ 08840
USA
(732) 549-8965
stephenjspiro at hotmail.com

Member, J4 COBOL Committee
_______________________________________________
Submitted via WebNewsReader of http://www.interbulletin.com

ALL of the members of J4 are unhappy about attempts to copyright the COBOL standard. We are actively negotiating with (i.e., objecting to) NCITS/ANSI/ISO regarding this issue.
The price of the hardcopy relects the costs of producing and distributing a relatively small press run.
If we have anything to do with it, there will always be a free download available.
If anyone actually tries to ENFORCE a copyright on the COBOL standard, I would give serious consideration to suing them for "theft of work product" for my contribution.
To the best of my knowledge, ANSI (as well as NCITS and ISO) is a non-profit organization.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Committee


_______________________________________________
Submitted via WebNewsReader of http://www.interbulletin.com
```

frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).

"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".

where available

CobCy

Windows/98, and Windows/2000.

 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang

Hello together,

after searching everywhere (HP-homepage, searchengines, ....) I hope

#### ↳ Re: OT - PCL in Cobol-"WRITE"-Statement

- **From:** "Eric Gauthier" <ericg@dgcsolutions.qc.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T12:30:38-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iRcM6.6292$_n.746099@weber.videotron.net>`
- **References:** `<3B01337B.9C7F1E44@Poerringer.de>`

```
Stephan,

Make sure to respect the UPPERCASE and lowercase when using PCL code,
otherwise the printer will ignore your escapes code.  The last letter in you
command should be in UPPPERCASE.

Print to a text file and edit this file to see if the escape code "PCL" is
alright and not cut, it append sometime, it depend on how it is programmed.


We use a lot of PCL form that we call with a macro and it work fine.  Also
we can do bold, landscape, barcode...  It's fun when you know how.


If you know how to print a graphic (a company logo), tell me how because I
have never been able to print a logo, or to send the logo into the printer
memory and call it with a macro id.

Eric.

- **References:** `<3B01337B.9C7F1E44@Poerringer.de> <iRcM6.6292$_n.746099@weber.videotron.net> <9dsc45$hh7$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
If you use Fujitsu's PowerForm, it has barcodes built in.

Otherwise, there seem to be two choices:

1. You can use a barcode font (almost any symbology is available free
if you look hard enough), or
2. You can develop a career sending bits representing a single
symbology of a single size to a specific printer.

You may think I'm prejudiced in favor of fonts: You're right. With a
font, you don't have to worry about scaling or printer technology
(laser, ink-jet, dot-matrix).

We found it so much trouble to fuss with hand-crafted barcodes, that
we opted to buy BARCODE PRINTERS that do all that stuff in firmware.
Now, with Windows handling all the code for us, we can configure our
software to print barcodes on almost any printer (usually sheet-fed,
but we do have some users who print on dot-matrix - they need PostNet
barcodes on mailing labels).

Alister,

We use Printer barcode and we also use a soft font that we download into the
memory of the printer and with a macro call (PCL)  we print the barcode.  We
use code 128 so we had to develop a checksum procedure, but it is not hard
to do and it work great.

We buy a soft font generate with Soft Font Generator from The Barcode
Software Center, Measurement Equiment Corporation see http://www.mecsw.com
There may be other soft font you can find for free.

Eric.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Barcode ?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-21T08:15:20+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B08C088.6B8D8A15@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3B01337B.9C7F1E44@Poerringer.de> <iRcM6.6292$_n.746099@weber.videotron.net> <9dsc45$hh7$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Alister Munro wrote:
> 
> How do you do a barcode with PCL ? Just starting to look into this :)

I do it by drawing lots of tall rectangles in black.  

I based this on a public domain C program that I found on the net and
recoded it to Cobol, changing it so that I could call the routine and
have it emit the appropriate PCL code for various sizes.  It does
code-39.

Basically there is a table with an item for each valid character
containing the pattern in terms of 9 '1's and '0's.  To print the
barcode first grab the position on the page then process each character
in the code and emit rectangles wherever there is a '1' in the pattern,
stepping along by the bar width.

This does, however generate quite a bit of text and may affect whatever
else you want on the line.  I store away the barcode requests as I print
the page, remembering the position, size and code, into a table and then
output from this this table at the end of the page.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - PCL in Cobol-"WRITE"-Statement

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T20:02:15+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9drqob$vp8$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3B01337B.9C7F1E44@Poerringer.de>`

```
These are the PCL codes I use :)

Pound Sign x'9C'
Hash Sign x'23'
Carriage Return x'0D'
Reset x'1B45'
Landscape x'1B266C314F'
Portrait x'1B266C304F'
Fixed Spacing x'1B28733050'
Courier x'1B28733430393954'
CPI Selection x'1B283132551B28733070' + STR + x'68307330623430393954'
    Replace STR with cpi desired (eg. "10", "12", "17.6")
LPI Selection x'1B266C' + STR + x'44'
    Replace STR with lpi desired (eg. "6", "8", "10")
Bold On x'1B28733342'
Bold Off x'1B28732D3342'
Underline On x'1B26643044'
Underline Off x'1B266440'

All above codes are in hex except for STR which is just an ascii string.

Its a good idea to setup a file containing printer codes and parse print
lines
replacing literals with the actual printer codes. My reports ask for Device.
Printer, Paper, & Margin Size's. Other than standard forms I can the scale
the printed report on a best fit basis for the printer/paper combo. I use a
copy-book containing literals such as 'op-fx-bold-on', 'op-fx-bold-off',
etc.

If it helps, send me an email and ill send you my code for parsing print
lines.

news:3B01337B.9C7F1E44@Poerringer.de...

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T09:21:54+01:00

- **References:** `<3B01337B.9C7F1E44@Poerringer.de> <9drqob$vp8$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B037494.EEA7BB6@Poerringer.de>`

```
On Thu, 17 May 2001 08:49:56 +0200, Stefan Schneck
<Schneck_Stefan@Poerringer.de> wrote:

>Hello !
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>But that does not work :-(((

If you are working with Windows then you need to change the printer
settings so it prints in "RAW" mode.
If you don't do this then all "esc" sequences will be ignored.

The best way to see if a sequence is correct is to create a text file
with "edit" (not wordpad or notepad), and printing it in a DOS box.
Such file would have some sequences to set up different font sizes and
then print a line of text for each one.

Once this is working then the COBOL program should also work
(if everything is correctly configured in the COBOL side).

Cheers


FF
```

Hi:

I use PCL codes all the time with an HP LaserJet. Microsoft COBOL v5.

To simplify testing, you might want to put your PCL codes in a text
file and just copy that file to LPT1 or wherever. It could be that
your PCL is wrong. If it doesn't work that way it probably won't
work via COBOL.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

<a id="t-69d7983cdb"></a>
## Thread: Portability — 6 msgs

### Portability

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T10:43:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9drio6$ghf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
(Ignoring - for the moment the phrase "portability of programmers" raised in
another thread).

It seems to me that there is an "interesting" question that has been asked
about "who cares" about portability (of COBOL source code) in another
thread.  I have some opinions on this - but others may certainly disagree.

1) For operating environments where *multiple* COBOL compilers are
available, it is OFTEN considered desirable (by programmers) and UNdesirable
(by *some* vendors) to provide portability among COBOL compilers.  This is
true in the Workstation (PC, Unix, Linux) environment where I think it is
"medium-common" to want to port source code from one compiler to another -
and from environment to environment with the same compiler. However, this
represents a RELATIVELY small amount of the existing COBOL source code.  It
may well, however, represent a growing amount IF AND ONLY IF the
"perception" of COBOL changes in these environments.

2) For "proprietary" operating systems where the OS vendor is ALSO the COBOL
vendor (one and only), then portability of source code is usually (not
always) a NON-issue.  IBM certainly discovered this when they TRIED to
introduce "SAA COBOL" for portability between MVS, VM, OS/400, and OS/2.  I
believe that the percentage of COBOL source code that is EVER ported from
one proprietary environment to another is MINISCULE (e.g. Unisys to Tandem,
or IBM to HP, etc).  This *does* happen occasionally, but it is such an
unusual event that it should NOT be viewed as a "major" motivating factor
for the future of COBOL.

3) "Right-sizing" of COBOL source programs (from a proprietary COBOL
mainframe environment to a client/server or workstation environment) has
been tried a LOT.  All evidence that I have heard seems to indicate that the
only way to do this successfully is by approaching it as a "major
development" project and NOT as a simple "source code migration".  This is
due BOTH to the change in application design *and* differences in COBOL
implementations (even when both are "ANSI conforming").  It is CERTAINLY
true that the "closer" the original code is to being limited to "ANSI
conforming" and the more that the original design separated user-interfaces
from business logic from external data access, then the EASIER the migration
was.  However, again, I view this as a "relatively" unusual situation with
existing COBOL applications - but a "desirable" way to design future ones.
This, however, raises the separate question of how much NEW COBOL
application development is being done (rather than enhancements and
maintenance of existing COBOL systems).

4) I agree with those who think (in large enterprises especially) the *TERM*
"portability" is viewed as not only a "good think" but also as having
business advantages. However, I don't think that this is any better
justified than many other "middle management" desires to look for "silver
bullets" to fix application development backlogs.

   ***

Comments?
```

#### ↳ Re: Portability

- **From:** "Steven O. Ellis" <soellis@soltec.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T21:56:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B01EC72.3D0EE6BE@soltec.net>`
- **References:** `<9drio6$ghf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

I haven't read the prior postings on these threads, but thought I would
throw in my bit anyway.  The telephone billing company I work for runs
the COBOL code in a service bureau environment on an IBM mainframe.  The 
code is also ported to the AS/400, RS/6000, and PC environments.  Before 
all else, the code must be portable.  However, we do use a preprocessor
to isolate code, as in FILE-CONTROL statements, that cannot be ported.

I can't think that this scenario is all that unusual, but I've only
worked in two COBOL shops.  The experience of others may indicate that
this is a rare development environment.

Steven


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> (Ignoring - for the moment the phrase "portability of programmers" raised in
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

- **From:** randall_bryan@g1.com (Luke Bryan)
- **Date:** 2001-05-18T08:29:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad0a59aa.0105180729.3459bfc3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9drio6$ghf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3B01EC72.3D0EE6BE@soltec.net>`

```
"Steven O. Ellis" <soellis@soltec.net> wrote in message news:<3B01EC72.3D0EE6BE@soltec.net>...
> Bill,
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

That's exactly what we do.  We develop in MicroFocus COBOL-85 under
Windows.  Then we port to MVS, VSE, AS/400, DEC VAX, DEC Alpha, Unix
(many flavors), and Unisys A.

We port from 50 to 80 million lines of COBOL-85 code per year to these
platforms.  The resulting executables are packaged and shipped to our
several thousand customers.  Almost all of our customers are on a
maintenance agreement that requires us to ship them updated programs 4
times a year.  We have been doing this since 1990.

Bill, the only reason you don't hear people like us complain is
because we work around the portability problems with a preprocessor. 
And there are way too many portability problems.

One of our highest priorities is portability of COBOL.  The fact that
COBOL is still the most portable lanaguage was the deciding factor to
go COBOL in 1990.  If some other language was more portable, we
probably would have gone to that.

> 
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
> >  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-05-18T15:52:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010518115222.03880.00001113@nso-fk.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3B01EC72.3D0EE6BE@soltec.net>`

```
In article <3B01EC72.3D0EE6BE@soltec.net>, "Steven O. Ellis"
<soellis@soltec.net> writes:

>code is also ported to the AS/400, RS/6000, and PC environments.  Before 
>all else, the code must be portable.  However, we do use a preprocessor
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I too have worked in a multi-platform environment with 'single-source'
development criteria.  Our older stuff was based off of an old SOLT Tape 
method that blended the code with markers for the target platform.
ALL development had to be platform independent or where there were 
platform dependencies the programmer was responsible for 'creating'
a similar function on the other supported platforms to ensure that the 
resulting product functioned the same in all environments.

Even now , there is a situation that we a re looking at using some compiler 
directive events like PUSH/POP/OMIT where the compiler decides which
segments to compile base on some variable.  Being as we are now looking
at two mainframe platforms and the PC arena for our processing environment.
```

#### ↳ Re: Portability

- **From:** "John Bradley" <k5mwe@austin.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T13:54:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZDvM6.63299$Cc1.14479903@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<9drio6$ghf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9drio6$ghf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net...
<snip>...
 1) For operating environments where *multiple* COBOL compilers are
> available, it is OFTEN considered desirable (by programmers) and
UNdesirable
> (by *some* vendors) to provide portability among COBOL compilers.  This is
> true in the Workstation (PC, Unix, Linux) environment where I think it is
> "medium-common" to want to port source code from one compiler to another -
> and from environment to environment with the same compiler.

Maybe this is a little generous.  We actually see a fairly small movement
between compilers among our customers.  In almost all cases where it
happens, it is unplanned.  A great deal of "lip service" is paid to the
concept of source portability in this mode, but it usually is not high on
the customers' value list.

On the other hand, object and data portability between platforms (but
without changing source language or compiler type) is the bread and butter
of our market.

>However, this
> represents a RELATIVELY small amount of the existing COBOL source code.
It
> may well, however, represent a growing amount IF AND ONLY IF the
> "perception" of COBOL changes in these environments.

Yes, we estimate that the market we serve (the above) represents at most 10%
of the whole COBOL application base (by value).
>
> 2) For "proprietary" operating systems where the OS vendor is ALSO the
COBOL
> vendor (one and only), then portability of source code is usually (not
> always) a NON-issue.  IBM certainly discovered this when they TRIED to
> introduce "SAA COBOL" for portability between MVS, VM, OS/400, and OS/2.
I
> believe that the percentage of COBOL source code that is EVER ported from
> one proprietary environment to another is MINISCULE (e.g. Unisys to
Tandem,
> or IBM to HP, etc).  This *does* happen occasionally, but it is such an
> unusual event that it should NOT be viewed as a "major" motivating factor
> for the future of COBOL.

I too believe this is true, although we do not participate directly in this
process.

> 3) "Right-sizing" of COBOL source programs (from a proprietary COBOL
> mainframe environment to a client/server or workstation environment) has
> been tried a LOT.  All evidence that I have heard seems to indicate that
the
> only way to do this successfully is by approaching it as a "major
> development" project and NOT as a simple "source code migration".  This is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> conforming" and the more that the original design separated
user-interfaces
> from business logic from external data access, then the EASIER the
migration
> was.  However, again, I view this as a "relatively" unusual situation with
> existing COBOL applications - but a "desirable" way to design future ones.

Very true.  Rehosting COBOL applications (if they are very big) is usually a
great deal of effort.  The common base language probably leads to more false
optimism than actual ease of movement.  In the case of the most important
migration (again, by value), IBM 3X0 -> something else, the problems are
enormous due to the fact that, in most cases, the application is really an
amalgamation of COBOL code, CICS artifacts, DB2/IMS/Adabase etc., and 360
environmental oddities.

I have noticed a curious view of the COBOL "standard" in the IBM developer
community.  As often as not, when they refer to the "standard," they are
actually referring to the particular IBM COBOL that they use.  This has led
to many misunderstandings over the years (in our business).  They seem
largely unaware of the content of the ANSI/ISO/FIPS standard.

> This, however, raises the separate question of how much NEW COBOL
> application development is being done (rather than enhancements and
> maintenance of existing COBOL systems).
>

I believe the only "new" COBOL (hyperbole here) is being done by COBOL
programmers who find their way into a new development environment or
company, and have the freedom to choose the language for their project.
These are usually fairly small line-of-business or independent
organizations.

> 4) I agree with those who think (in large enterprises especially) the
*TERM*
> "portability" is viewed as not only a "good think" but also as having
> business advantages. However, I don't think that this is any better
> justified than many other "middle management" desires to look for "silver
> bullets" to fix application development backlogs.

Yep.  The managers (decision makers) seem to place great "value" on souce
portability.  The actual programmers, however, will use non-standard
features without hesitation in almost every instance.  I am not sure ANY of
our customers have significant applications that are written entirely in
either 1974 or 1985 standard COBOL.  This is not to say they don't "believe"
they have standard COBOL.  There are, however, some add-on development tool
suppliers that have products written mostly, or entirely, in standard COBOL.
They can do this because they are usually replacing or augmenting a standard
COBOL feature, and they have little interaction with the host application
flow or data, other than through their defined interfaces.

By the way, and slightly off topic, I continue to be amazed at the lack of
use of 1985 standard enhancements in actual applications.  I would guess
that some of the most difficult (i.e., expensive) features for us to
implement, such as nested programs, have never been used by a single one of
our customers in the 15 years the've been available.  Many (perhaps 50+%) of
our customers' applications have used few or or no '85 standard  extensions.
Conversely, virtually all of our customers use our (extra-standard)
extensions to the language.

What all this means (to me) is probably too long and too controversial for
me to compose as part of this response.  I'll try to summon the energy, if
anyone is interested.

Regards,
John Bradley
CEO
Liant Software Corporation
>
>    ***
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-137b42c10b"></a>
## Thread: cobol as a structured language — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### re: cobol as a structured language

- **From:** rdriehle@nps.navy.mil ("Riehle, Richard")
- **Date:** 2001-05-15T18:19:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F5AD48747FC0324EB21B2B2BD27D5E8698B51E@Saipan>`

```

Rick Smith wrote:

    [in response to Richard Riehle's wild-eyed ranting]

>I am suggesting, that by providing a meaning for "structured programming"
that is 
>different than the meaning discussed and refined, earlier; you violated a
few 
>thousand years of tradition in education. If I seem too serious, that is 
>probably the reason! ;-) 

Iconoclast that I am, a few thousand years of anything seems in need of
violation.   Also, in my educational tradition, violating earlier accepted
meanings for things is encouraged.    Ideas continue to move ahead, evolve
and get changed.   Words change from benign meanings to politically
offensive or politically correct.   Even technical terms are not sacrosanct.
So I have no problem with extending the scope of a given terminology
to better reflect its reality in practice, beyond its theoretical
boundaries. 

My original, somewhat outrageous, remark was that true structured 
programming was not possible in COBOL prior to the ANSI-85
standard.   The replies were as I expected.   No surprises there.  Your
reply did take me by surprise, at least a little bit.  

Those of us with some enduring affection [perhaps misguided] affection
for COBOL should be pleased that it continues to evolve and improve.  I
suspect those who frequent this forum are pleased.   However, we should
not be unwilling to acknowledge the shortcomings of prior versions of 
the language.   It is important that COBOL continue to get better with each 
well-reasoned discovery in software practice.  Yes, we need to avoid 
jumping on each fad, but some new ideas simply seem right and are.

>Other than the implications of my comments, above, you and I are in 
>substantial agreement. 

Dogggggggggonnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnit!    Does this mean you don't want
to fight anymore?    :-)    [please notice the smiley]

Richard Riehle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Power 6.1 Install

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-05-18T15:52:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010518115220.03880.00001112@nso-fk.aol.com>`

```
In article <3B050AB3.1C2B3AF1@attglobal.net>, Carlos Lages
<clages@attglobal.net> writes:

>> > does anybody Knows if I install version 6.1  in a HOTPLUGABLE
>> HardDISK
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My first thought is that this would not work as the Windows Operating System
would be the first to complain rather profusely about the SYSTEM.DAT
information not matching the configuration of the 'new house' that the 
OS finds itself in.
If you manage to get past that part of the situation, then I would imagine
that PowerCOBOL would operate in a 'fat-hapy-dumb' mode as all the 
registry/file markers are contained on the portable drive (assuming that
this portable drive has the Boot OS and all).
If you are talking of a pluggable drive separate from the main Boot drive,
I would very much doubt the probability of success.

just my two-bits worth here.
```

## Thread: Retrieving values from a table — 16 msgs

### Retrieving values from a table

- **From:** "eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T05:24:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
I have an array set up as follows:

      */ fee type array.*
       01  FEETYPE-ARRAY.
           03 FEETYPE-TABLE.
              05 FILLER         PIC 9(6) VALUE 84.
              05 FILLER         PIC 9(6) VALUE 90.
              05 FILLER         PIC 9(6) VALUE 96.
              05 FILLER         PIC 9(6) VALUE 100.
              05 FILLER         PIC 9(6) VALUE 120.
              05 FILLER         PIC 9(6) VALUE 144.
           03 FEETYPE1 REDEFINES FEETYPE-TABLE.
              05 FEE-AMT OCCURS 6 TIMES PIC 9(6).

The problem I'm having is that I want to perform the following operation:
```

#### ↳ Re: Retrieving values from a table

- **From:** "eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T05:27:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZcoM6.55785$ff.432157@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`
- **References:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
      *     MOVE ZEROS TO ARRAY-INDX.
      *     PERFORM G30-IDENTIFY-PRICE VARYING ARRAY-INDX
      *             FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL ARRAY-INDX > 6.
      *     END-PERFORM.*

       G30-IDENTIFY-PRICE
      *     IF FEE-TYPE IS EQUAL TO ARRAY-INDX
      *        COMPUTE TEMP-PRICE = 100 * FEETYPE1(ARRAY-INDX).
      *        MOVE TEMP-PRICE TO NEW-PRICE
      *     END-IF.*

I can't seem to do it. The compiler does not like the FEETYPE1(ARRAY-INDX)
part. Even if I try to move the value in FEETYPE1(ARRAY-INDX) to NEW-PRICE
the compiler says the operand can not be subscripted.

What can I do?

eRRaTiK
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Retrieving values from a table

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T08:26:57+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gdfh8x0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <ZcoM6.55785$ff.432157@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
On Wed, 16 May 2001 05:27:21 GMT, eRRaTiK wrote:

>      *     MOVE ZEROS TO ARRAY-INDX.
>      *     PERFORM G30-IDENTIFY-PRICE VARYING ARRAY-INDX
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hi,

You can only subscript the fields that are defined with the OCCURS clause

Hope this helps


Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Retrieving values from a table

- **From:** "eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T06:45:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UlpM6.55918$ff.433025@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`
- **References:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <ZcoM6.55785$ff.432157@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gdfh8x0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
If you look at my declaration

           03 FEETYPE1 REDEFINES FEETYPE-TABLE.
              05 FEE-AMT OCCURS 6 TIMES PIC 9(6).

I do use the OCCURS clause. Why doesn't it work? How would you normally
retrieve a numerical value from an array?

eRRaTiK

"Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com> wrote in message
news:jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gdfh8x0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es...

You can only subscript the fields that are defined with the OCCURS clause

Hope this helps


Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Retrieving values from a table

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>

```
No, that is incorrect.  You can do exactly what you want, you just have to
say it correctly. FEETYPE1 is *not* the name of an array element; it is the
name of the entire array.  FEE-AMT is the name of a single element that is
repeated and must be subscripted.

"eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:0xuM6.56693$ff.439873@news-server.bigpond.net.au...

the
> value contained within that element. Is that correct?

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Retrieving values from a table

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T21:22:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jcCM6.4592$6j3.415250@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <ZcoM6.55785$ff.432157@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
Hi E,

There are a few things you didn't tell us.

You didn't tell us the range of valid values for FEE-TYPE or where it comes
from.

If FEE-TYPE has the range of 1-6 and their amts range from 84-144 respectively
as listed in your table,
Then you don't need a perform. You can just execute the compute stmt directly
using:

100 * FEE-AMT(FEE-TYPE) 

If, on the otherhand, the FEE-TYPE can be any #, then you have to construct the
table with the amts AND
their associated fee type #s. Then you can use the perform or a search as Jimmy
has suggested. In either
case though, you search against the fee type not the amt. You use the amt
pointed to by the search hit.  

Jack  


In article <ZcoM6.55785$ff.432157@news-server.bigpond.net.au>, eRRaTiK says...
>
>      *     MOVE ZEROS TO ARRAY-INDX.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Retrieving values from a table

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-05-19T06:09:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0105190509.75dceb23@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <ZcoM6.55785$ff.432157@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
"eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<ZcoM6.55785$ff.432157@news-server.bigpond.net.au>...
> *     MOVE ZEROS TO ARRAY-INDX.
>       *     PERFORM G30-IDENTIFY-PRICE VARYING ARRAY-INDX
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> eRRaTiK


1    In your COMPUTE statement you have referenced the dataname FEETYPE1
instead of FEE-AMT, which is the dataname that has the OCCURS clause.
Only data items that have an OCCURS clause or are subordinate to data
items that have an OCCURS clause can be subscripted or indexed.

2    You have used the variable ARRAY-INDX as a subscript in the COMPUTE
statement, that would be OK if ARRAY-INDX is an integer data item, but
unsafe if it is a real COBOL index that is associated with another
table, because COBOL indexes are specific to the tables for which they
are defined and can only be used for the original table or other tables
that have identically sized occurring elements.  You haven't told us how
ARRAY-INDX is defined.

3    The term subscript is normally used for data items defined as
integers that are used as subscripts, while the term index is normally
used for indexes defined as being explicitly associated with specific
tables via the OCCURS clause.

4    The statement MOVE ZEROS TO ARRAY-INDX is unnecessary, because the
PERFORM statement specifies the initial value required in the BY phrase.

5    While not essential for the correct operation of your program, your
nomenclature for the data items use to construct your table of fee
amounts is potentially confusing as all group items have a prefix of
FEETYPE, whereas "FEETYPE" as a dataname refers to an independent
variable for which you have not provided a definition.  I would also
prefer to use a name such as FEE-INDX rather than ARRAY-INDX to show its
relation to the table being processed and even better use the name
FEE-SUBSCRIPT or FEE-SUB if it really is a subscript and not an index.
Futhermore it would be even better to specify ARRAY-INDX as the index
for the table containing FEE-AMT and then give the index the name
FEE-INDX. This could be done by using the following statement for the
definition of FEE-AMT:

05  FEE-AMT PIC 9(6) OCCURS 6 TIMES INDEXED BY FEE-INDX.
```

#### ↳ Re: Retrieving values from a table

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T17:16:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B02B62F.AB673EFD@home.com>`
- **References:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```


eRRaTiK wrote:

> I have an array set up as follows:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> The problem I'm having is that I want to perform the following operation:

Believe it or not, I coded my first SEARCH last night - it works - see if
this helps :-

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "sqlstate-message".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Working-Storage section.
01 SqlState-T1.
   05 pic x(76) value
      "01000 General warning".
   05 pic x(76) value
      "01002 Disconnect error".
   05 pic x(76) value
      "01004 Data truncated".
 05 etc..........


01 SqlState-T2 redefines SqlState-T1.
   05 SqlStateTable occurs 90
      Indexed by Idx.
      10 SqlStateNumber             pic x(05).
      10                                        pic x.
      10 SqlStateMessage            pic x(70).

01 ls-message                       pic x(70).

Linkage section.
01 lnk-SqlState                     pic x(05).
01 lnk-TableName                    pic x(20).

Procedure Division using lnk-SqlState, lnk-TableName.

   set Idx to 1
   SEARCH  SqlStateTable
      at end move "Unknown error condition" to ls-message
       when lnk-SqlState = SqlStateNumber(idx)
         move SqlStateMessage(Idx) to ls-message
   END-SEARCH

   invoke self "message-box"
     using ls-message, lnk-SqlState, lnk-TableName

End Method "sqlstate-message".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Retrieving values from a table

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-05-19T05:39:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0105190439.24e9d39e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <3B02B62F.AB673EFD@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message news:<3B02B62F.AB673EFD@home.com>...
> eRRaTiK wrote:
> 
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
> *>-------------------------------------------------------------

This seems to be a good example of a linear search.  However, it doesn't
seem to be the best solution to the problem.  Firstly a binary search
using SEARCH ALL would probably be preferable.  Secondly, IBM and probably
other vendors too, already has a table of sqlstate and sqlmessage that
can be accessed, in the case of IBM, via the subroutine DSNTIAR, which is
documented in the DB2 manuals.  It must be said that unless other vendors
provide an equivalent to DSNTIAR, its use is not portable across platforms.
Even so, if done manually, the definition of such a table would best be done
as a one-off activity to include all sqlstates and messages of which there
are many more than 90 and to be incorporated as a subroutine for general use
by all programs within an installation.  Because the table would be quite
large, such a subroutine would best be developed as a separate load module
that can be linked dynamically, so that it isn't loaded until needed and/or
can be shared as a single instance by all programs in a multitasking environment.
```

Robert Jones wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message news:<3B02B62F.AB673EFD@home.com>...
>
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> can be shared as a single instance by all programs in a multitasking environment.

Ah, somebody's all ready beaten you to the punch Roberto - a Dutch gentleman retired in sunny
southern Spain. It now reads  :-

    SEARCH  ALL SqlStateTable
       at end move "Unknown error condition" to ls-message
        when lnk-SqlState = SqlStateNumber(idx)
          move SqlStateMessage(Idx) to ls-message
    END-SEARCH

I was however concerned with your comment about the proliferation of SQLSTATES - my list of
90 is taken from the NetExpress on-line help, plus I added 2002 - just in case I missed it in
my business logic.

And Robert you used that ugly word PORTABILITY. Happens to be what I consider to be a very
nice word in its association with COBOL. Plus, from the  comment by the UK ISO representative
'.... we need portability of standard OO classes so that vendors are not tempted into doing
their own thing" - I vote a 1,000% for that one !

Well, I don't know what you personally thought of the J4 get together in May last - but when
I read what Chuck and Bill are currently discussing about the legalese - and who gets to vote
'No' or 'Yes' - Gawd Help us ! Can any sensibly minded person logically refute what Pete
Dashwood wrote in his last message on the Standards.

The bloody system is broken and requires a MAJOR change of mindset to make the damn thing
work. I'm not talking about COBOL 2002 but the POST MORTEM which should occur after that is
put to rest. As caring people the J4 team (which is INTERNATIONAL) should be looking at this
circus called ANSI/ISO and all their sibling departments/committees. Throw down the gauntlet
- we (ALL of us in COBOL, that should be), should want ONE COMMITTEE - if necessary take the
extreme measure of pulling out of both ANSI and ISO.

Can't be done ? Where's the ANSI or ISO standard for Java ? Does that stop the US government,
FIPS or God knows who else from using Java.

Now back to SQL - this should be on J4's wish list after COBOL 2002. COBOL as a language
should ACKNOWLEDGE that SQL92 exists, not just add-ons by vendors. Using the ISO SQL92, (yes,
I read briefly somewhere there is a draft SQL99 - but I believe ANSI/ISO or both played a
role in screwing that up too !), there should be a common COBOL standard acknowledging the
syntax to recognize the 'standard' SQL statements -  not the neat ad-ons that might be
provided by other SQL engines, such as Oracle etc.. Then we could come up with the standard
list of messages as you suggest

What's your weather like in Gloucester ? I'm actually south of you, but brrrrrh !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards & SQL (WAS: Re: Retrieving values from a table

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T05:42:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0105200442.d69fc0e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <3B02B62F.AB673EFD@home.com> <fcd09c56.0105190439.24e9d39e@posting.google.com> <3B06DA00.9416682B@home.com>`

```
James Gavan wrote

> 
> Ah, somebody's all ready beaten you to the punch Roberto - a Dutch gentleman retired in sunny
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

Hello Jimmy,

Yes your original search was a good example, especially as a beginner,
though the rest of my comments hold.  You can criticise my first OO COBOL
program, if or when I get around to writing one.

The point about SQLSTATEs is that it is intended to be a set of error codes
defined by the SQL standard.  Previously, SQLCODE was used, but different
vendors used different codes for the same purpose.  SQLCODEs are still used
extensively on older systems.  Use of SQLSTATE instead of SQLCODE provides
much better portability.  I have a feeling that some codes for SQLSTATE changed
between the 1989 and 1992 standards and conceivably could do so again.  This is
an argument for setting up a common copybook of condition-names for testing
common conditions in conjunction with the value of SQLSTATE, so that changes
can be more easily accommodated and tests look more readable (for example:
IF SQL-END-OF-TABLE, rather than IF SQLSTATE = "02000" (or whatever it should
be)), but I shall have to think about that some more.

Several years ago, I bought a book called "SQL The Standard Handbook" by
Stephen Cannan and Gerard Otten, published by McGraw-Hill in 1993 at a cost of 
ï¿½35. Presumably the ANSI standard can also be bought independently, but this
book seems pretty good and I am not planning to spend any more at present.
Both should contain a full list of codes for SQLSTATE.  However, I am now by no
means certain that this is so, because my copy of IBM's messages and codes for
DB2 version 4 contains a much larger list.  Possibly the initial two digits of
SQLSTATE are consistent, as are those three digit suffixes defined by the
standard, but implementors may be free to add other suffixes as required.
Perhaps someone else can shed some light on this.

I have heard that there is a continuing development of SQL, in particular with
respect to object orientation, but I haven't been able to find anything more
about it.  Nothing seemed to be happening in NCITS or ISO last time I looked.

I was very impressed by the WG4/J4 meeting in May 2000 and the extent and depth
of knowledge of the main participants.  I know the new standard is taking a
long time, but I certainly know I couldn't do better myself.  I am afraid to
say that the reaffirmation issue just looks ridiculous and can't do ANSI or
COBOL any good.

With regard to how COBOL should be developed in future, I suppose that
ultimately that depends upon the joint interests and financial support of the 
vendors and users.  The imposition of copyright restrictions by ANSI and ISO
might make going independent harder.

It's a nice sunny day here, in amongst the cloudy spells, and I am just about
to go out and enjoy it, the nights are still a bit cold though.

Robert, Gloucester, UK
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards & SQL (WAS: Re: Retrieving values from a table

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T12:52:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0105201152.55b4579c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <3B02B62F.AB673EFD@home.com> <fcd09c56.0105190439.24e9d39e@posting.google.com> <3B06DA00.9416682B@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message news:<3B06DA00.9416682B@home.com>...
> Robert Jones wrote:
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>>    __________SNIP _________

A Binary search (in IBM Asembly Language) has more overhead than a
linear search (as I recall, the index must be moved to a register and
divided by 2. Then the index is applied to the compare instruction). 
About 25 years ago, I was told that the break-point was a table of
about 75 items. More than that, and there are fewer enough accesses of
the table to compensate for the machine time overhead; fewer than
that, and the overhead for the binary search takes longer than the
additional accesses used in a linear search. Obviously, you could get
a hit on the first read either way, but this number was also said to
take into account the "odds" of a match, using either search.

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems

Hey! YOU could be a member of the J4 Committee, and make a difference.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Retrieving values from a table

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T13:04:19+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b071b02_2@news3>`
- **References:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <3B02B62F.AB673EFD@home.com> <fcd09c56.0105190439.24e9d39e@posting.google.com>`

```
Robert,

I noted your response on this and some other threads and the advice you are
giving is clear and accurate. (Nice to see...<G>)

In this particular case it is not quite so incisive because you are relying
on a background in mainframe COBOL and DB2.

Jimmy's solution is intended for OO COBOL on a PC (although it could be
transported to the mainframe environment.) If it was transported, all of
your comments are absolutely correct. But it would still work...it just
isn't the "best" way to do it (as you pointed out).

However, when dealing with ODBC drivers on a PC, this solution is a very
good one.  An appropriate table for each driver could be accessed and
loaded, and the "sqlstate-message" Method can be invoked as part of the SQL
Access Class that Jimmy has probably defined. Requires no changes to
anything, despite there being sometimes significant differences between ODBC
drivers and their error codes.

Your point about SEARCH...ALL is correct, for cases where large (many
entries) tables are involved, but again, Jimmy's solution will work, and for
90 entries, searching on a 5 character table key (in a worst case scenario
where the entry is the last in the table), it would be under 1 millisecond
on a Pentium...not the kind of interval a human is likely to notice. It
should also be remembered that this code is executed on extreme exception;
if the system is closing down or the process is abending anyway, it really
doesn't matter if it takes a second or two...

SEARCH ALL (as I'm sure you know) requires the table to be sorted in Key
sequence, so that a binary chop can be made on it. There is an argument
(particularly in on-line mainframe environments like CICS and IMS-DC) that
it can be more efficient (not that we should be too obsessed about
efficiency when we use today's platforms...) to have your table ordered in
statistical frequency and NOT use SEARCH...ALL. (Organise it so that the
most likely hits are at the "top" of the table...) Like everything, the
whole environment needs to be considered and there may well be exceptions to
the general rules, based on specific requirements.

I would stress that I am not taking issue with the points you raised, when
applied to a mainframe environment (it was an excellent post). However,
there are many small, independent developers using COBOL who are less
concerned with "installation standards" and who need to take a broader view
than is necessary in a mainframe environment.

Your points about dynamic linkage and setting the table up as a Load Module
are as true today as they were in 1967 when I first did this...(It just
doesn't matter as much today...<G>), and the dynamic loading is well handled
by Object Oriented COBOL on the PC

I liked Jimmy's solution. And he did say it was his first attempt to use
SEARCH...(a significant move forward for many COBOLlers...).

Pete.



"Robert Jones" <robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:fcd09c56.0105190439.24e9d39e@posting.google.com...
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:<3B02B62F.AB673EFD@home.com>...
> > eRRaTiK wrote:
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

operation:
> >
> > Believe it or not, I coded my first SEARCH last night - it works - see
if
> > this helps :-
> >
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> provide an equivalent to DSNTIAR, its use is not portable across
platforms.
> Even so, if done manually, the definition of such a table would best be
done
> as a one-off activity to include all sqlstates and messages of which there
> are many more than 90 and to be incorporated as a subroutine for general
use
> by all programs within an installation.  Because the table would be quite

> that can be linked dynamically, so that it isn't loaded until needed
and/or

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Retrieving values from a table

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T02:29:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B072C38.B4C86069@home.com>`
- **References:** `<yaoM6.55776$ff.432104@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <3B02B62F.AB673EFD@home.com> <fcd09c56.0105190439.24e9d39e@posting.google.com> <3b071b02_2@news3>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

I liked your observations. Couple of points worth noting.

1. When I send you my draft code you will see I'm setting up each Table as a
separate Class and primarily it has methods, (not dissimilar to my approach to
COBOL files)  :-

Method-id. "cobol-to-host".
Method-id. "delete-record".
Method-id. "field-positioning". (used for creating collections/dictionaries)
Method-id. "host-to-cobol".
Method-id. "insert-record".
Method-id. "make-collection".
Method-id. "select-record".            ( a Singleton)
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "set-errmsg".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Procedure Division.
  invoke super "sqlstate-message"                    *> see 2 below......
         using SQLSTATE, ws-TableName
End Method "set-errmsg".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

Method-id. "update-record".

From above list I don't have a method for "select-multi-rows" - my thinking on
this is to use "make-collection", or a variant, to stay within the method and
return a collection 'of collected rows/data columns', based on selection
criteria,  as an object collection to the 'caller' and 'unpack' them into text
in the Business Logic class. (This fits in with my design concept of restricting
SQL syntax to Super SqlMethods and DB Class Tables).

2. Each Class Table is subordinate to SqlMethods which contains :-

Method-id. "connect-it".
Method-id. "disconnect-it".
Method-id. "sqlstate-message".
Method-id. "message-box".
Method-id. "set-parent".        ( so I can display a messagebox )

3. As you observed, SQLSTATE is the exception - not the norm (although
sqlstate-message is useful in my learning stage.
That's how I discovered SQL doesn't like NULLs for columns in rows !!!). For
Robert's info - bearing in mind sqlstate-message is the exception, the super
method is only called(loaded) when required, and runtime caching would 'throw it
away' when it observes it is not in the 'current popularity list' of methods
being invoked.

4. I'm debating in my mind, reference to collections, whether I include the
following methods in the Class Table or put them elsewhere :-

method-ids. "add-to-collection", "change-collection", "delete-from-collection"
- this one's a bit of a toss-up;  I could go a couple of ways.

5. Regrettably using a DB I can't finish up with one generic class to handle
them all - as I have for Relative, Line Sequential, Sequential and ISAM.

PS: I still LIKE COBOL files <G> <G> - and as I've pointed out to Pete
elsewhere, my major interest in going the DB route is so I don't have the
problem of creating ASCII-delimiteds(CSVs) for end-users - Tell 'em to extract
what they want from Access !! No conflict here - the end-user is not the 'little
guy' - they are the big bucks oil companies that George W. Jnr. is about to
'court'.

Jimmy

> Robert,
>
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Executable files

- **From:** "Calin Macrinici" <cmac@pathcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T22:27:47+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<D9DM6.38211$yw.990349@news20.bellglobal.com>`

That's very smart, but you're probably gonna have to link it, too!

The linkers are (usually) separate from the COBOL packages (i.e. on Windows
you would use the Microsoft linker), although in most cases the COBOL
vendors include the correct linker for your environment with their product.
Also, depending on the COBOL environment you're using, you will probably
have to link some libraries in your exe.

In some cases (as with Merant, for example, when you put more functionality
than "hello world" in your program), you will probably have to ship your exe
and some auxilliary files (they're called run-time components, and they can
be libraries, dlls, or proprietary format files).

That's about it, enjoy!

Calin, TORONTO

"Robert Heady" <r.heady@liant.com> wrote in message
news:80BM6.66$kc4.1735@nnrp1.sbc.net...

When you compile the program it is converted into the machines own
language which results in an object file which can then be 'link'ed to
produce the final executable version. Neither the obj or the exe produced
from these process's resembles the source code that you wrote.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Executable files

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-18T19:35:50+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<tg5e91kb0pbh61@corp.supernews.com> <80BM6.66$kc4.1735@nnrp1.sbc.net> <tg7v4h5s38t4a9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
yotch27@hotmail.com wrote:
> 
> Uh, I did compile it, I didn't want to know how to make it run, I wanted
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks for the useless smart ass answer though.  I appreciate it.

'Compile it' is the actual answer to your question and wasn't being
'smart ass'.

Most compilers read the source code and write an executable file that
may be name.EXE or name.INT or somesuch.  Sometimes these can be
completely stand-alone but some cobol systems require a 'run-time' in
order for these to execute.  This run-time may require to be purchased,
in other cases free distribution may be possible.

It all depends on the compiler that you use.  As you omitted this vital
piece of information then it becomes impossible to be any more specific
than 'compile it' or 'RTFM'.

If I were to make a guess then I would hazard that you use MF's student
edition of Cobol.  This product has no means of creating a distributable
executable, nor would distributing the compiler itself be legal.  This
is a deliberate restriction of the student edition.

Now start again, this time giving adequate information to get a useful
answer.
```
