<a id="t-20d10f0dc7"></a>
## Thread: Is the Standards process broken? — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Is the Standards process broken?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-16T15:40:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9duvkt$krr$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Various people at various times have asserted that the process by which the
COBOL standard is being developed is "broken", that the people responsible
for "breaking" it should acknowledge their failings and strive to repair the
damage they have done.

Some of these have pointed the finger at ANSI and demanded that that
organization be held accountable.

ANSI bore responsibility, as I understand it, for the development of the
current "American National Standard" COBOL ('85) and its amendments.  This
standard (and its predecessors, I believe) was developed in cooperation with
the various national standards bodies of other countries, but was developed
and published first and foremost an *American* standard.  Basic
responsibility both for the *development* process and the *content* of the
standard lay in the United States; any other input, however carefully
considered, was advisory.

But the FCD is a different matter.  The FCD is intended from the very
beginning as an *International Standard*, with "one country, one vote" over
the technical content of the document.  It is true that the International
Standards Organization through its various subcommittees has delegated
responsibility for producing the document itself to NCITS/J4, but it is also
true that ISO/IEC JTC1/SC22/WG4 maintains responsibility for its technical
*content* and exercizes significant oversight into technical matters.  J4
may *recommend* changes, enhancements, revisions to WG4, but it's WG4 that
ultimately decides, and the United States position on any topic carries no
more weight than that of any other national member body, as I understand it.
ISO/IEC procedures, at least as they apply to this standard, seem to be
based on the premise "one country, one vote" from all I can gather.   The US
contingent has no more say than the British or the Danish or the Japanese
contingent.

I believe this change in responsibility, and the changes in process
resulting from that change in responsibility, stem ultimately from the
perception that (however cooperative X3J4 may or may not have been during
the development of the current and prior standards), the "American National
Standard" was fundamentally a 'Murkin product developed by 'Murkins for
'Murkins and while the 'Murkin developers might listen politely to other
views they were under no obligation to incorporate those views.   Yes, I
know, that's probably an exaggeration, but ...

I believe the ultimate authority for the *approval* and *negotiation*
processes -- including these  "one country, one vote" conventions associated
with the CD-to-FCD-to-DIS-to-IS processes -- lie with ISO/IEC.   Whether I
think ISO/IEC should use those procedures is not a whole lot more important
or more influential than my opinion as to whether Great Britain should
retain or abolish the monarchy.  I have no standing in the latter case, only
a little in the former.

This change in process, authority, and focus were introduced as a response
to a perceived need.  I believe that perception was legitimate.  And if
enough interested parties in enough member countries felt neglected enough
by the ANSI process to cause ISO/IEC to issue an issued an ultimatum to the
effect "Turn technical content responsibility for the next COBOL standard
over to us, or we won't approve it like we have its predecessors", I can
certainly understand why they would have done so.

With this change in authority, made as the result of a perceived need, comes
acceptance of the rules by which that new authority operates.   ISO/IEC has
a right to its "one country, one vote" conventions, with all the
negotiations that entails.  They have every right to maintain those
conventions whether I think they should or not.

Moreover, I believe the division of authority (J4 for developing the
standard, WG4 for its contents) makes  the process of developing the
standard somewhat more ponderous than it would be were authority not divided
and input from other bodies deemed advisory, as was the case in the past.

It seems to me that each of these issues -- the fact that the United States
for many years decided what COBOL should look like and the international
community was largely pressured into accepting their juidgment on the issue,
that users in other countries found that unacceptable, that the members of
ISO/IEC agreed with those users and took over content authority for the
International Standard while leaving development authority with J4, that
ISO/IEC procedures allow each national delegation an equal say in any issue
regardless of the number of COBOL users that feature would impact (in or out
of that nation), and the fact that individual national bodies have sometimes
taken opposite positions on the same subject, leading to lengthy
negotiations -- has contributed to the fact that the process of developing
the FCD has been slower than it might have been.

It has been nigh onto sixteen years since the promulgation of the last COBOL
standard, and the draft that is being proposed has a GREAT many new
features, some of which are perceived as already obsolete.   It may well be
"too much, too late", and in hindsight I can certainly see that the COBOL
community would likely have benefited from multiple revisions introduced
more frequently.

But the current standards process *is* a replacement process already, and
each of the differences from its predecessor process is evidence of a change
intended to fix something within that process that was perceived as
seriously "broken".

Does the fault lie with the US-centric nature of the earlier standards
processes?

Does the fault lie with those who lobbied to have ISO/IEC take over
authority for the next COBOL standard from ANSI?

Does the fault lie with those member national bodies of ISO/IEC who demanded
equal votes to their more populous brethren in the process of setting up
that organization?

Does the fault lie with those member national bodies who insisted on the
inclusion of certain features in the FCD?  Or insisted that they be omitted?

Does the fault lie with those who submitted a plethora of "better
mousetraps" for inclusion into the standards, and lobbied their national
bodies for the inclusion and integration of those features?

Does the fault lie with the consensus of WG4 for insisting on the inclusion
of such features rather than deferring them to a later revision, whether J4
or J4-tag felt it was appropriate?

Does the fault lie with J4 for attempting to be all things to all nations?

And if so for more than one of these, to what degree do we apportion the
blame among them?

While the current process is arguably ponderous, I'm not convinced it's
*wrong*.  I believe international standards arrived at by formal consensus
are a good thing in and of themselves, and I believe there is no way to
avoid intrinsic limitations to any process by which such international
formal consensus can be reached.  But I do not believe the processes should
be abandoned because their limitations or even their intricacies have in
some cases slowed or otherwise restricted progress toward that final
consensus.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: Is the Standards process broken?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@free.net.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T15:25:01+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

So it is now a more "democratic" process than it was? I wasn't aware of
this, and read with interest what you posted above. Are you now saying that
the delay has been caused because International approval/agreement was not
forthcoming?

States

issue,

issue

out
> of that nation), and the fact that individual national bodies have
sometimes

> the FCD has been slower than it might have been.
>

Ah, I see that you are saying that...<G>

> It has been nigh onto sixteen years since the promulgation of the last
COBOL
> standard, and the draft that is being proposed has a GREAT many new
> features, some of which are perceived as already obsolete.   It may well
be

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

change

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does the fault lie with those member national bodies of ISO/IEC who
demanded

> inclusion of certain features in the FCD?  Or insisted that they be
omitted?
>
> Does the fault lie with those who submitted a plethora of "better
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does the fault lie with the consensus of WG4 for insisting on the
inclusion
> of such features rather than deferring them to a later revision, whether
J4
> or J4-tag felt it was appropriate?
>
> Does the fault lie with J4 for attempting to be all things to all nations?
>

Or, Does the fault lie with the simple fact that the whole process is
unresponsive and unaccountable?


> And if so for more than one of these, to what degree do we apportion the
> blame among them?
>

Apportionment of blame is a fruitless exercise. Responsibility for a problem
and the willingness to learn from it is the best that can be hoped for from
a bad situation.

> While the current process is arguably ponderous, I'm not convinced it's
> *wrong*.  I believe international standards arrived at by formal consensus
> are a good thing in and of themselves, and I believe there is no way to
> avoid intrinsic limitations to any process by which such international
> formal consensus can be reached.  But I do not believe the processes
should
> be abandoned because their limitations or even their intricacies have in
> some cases slowed or otherwise restricted progress toward that final
> consensus.
>

So now it's the fault of the International Community <G>?

I don't personally care if God and all the Angels, or Satan and all the
Demons are responsible; the fact is it has failed.

Given the description you have laid out, Chuck, what can be done about it?

If we want to make the review process more interactive and timely (and I
certainly do), do we now need the approval of half the World?

What mechanisms are in place to obtain such approval? (I bet it involves
submission and review and submission and review and submission and
review...)

Why can't we set up an Internet Forum (with a preset Topic for each section
of the standard, and another one for General Comments) where ANYBODY can
post their suggestions and comments on the standard (irrespective of where
they happen to live)?

Run such a forum for a given period (Not to exceed, say 4 months), then
implement what the consensus is, as an Official Draft Standard. No closed
committees, completely open, with all interested parties (not vested
interests) having their say.

Where there is controversy over a feature, we allow 2 weeks on the Internet
for concerned parties to post their arguments, then 1 week for the User base
to vote. The final count is binding and people will accept that  they win
some and lose some (in the original spirit of Democracy). No petulant posts
or bomb threats from disgruntled losers, please...<G>.

Vendors will implement conforming  and partially conforming versions of this
standard and, again, a centralised Internet Forum can be used to elicit
feedback from the User Community. If there isn't any, then Good! We got it
right!

We then have an "Official standard" (I really don't care who ratifies and
controls it...ANSI, ISO whoever (provided they keep it in the Public
Domain...as was originally intended), so the people who care about that can
be happy.)

The vendors will be getting direct feedback (the most valuable Market
intelligence, and this is what drives them.)

And the COBOL Community gets an involvement in the Language and no longer
need feel sidelined and impotent regarding what is in it.

Repeat the above process for the remaining years of COBOL's Life Cycle.
(about 20, I reckon...although general use of the Language will cease well
before that.)

I know the above won't happen, but I've taken the time to post the scenario
anyway because if just one person thinks "yeah...why can't we do that?",
they may then question how they are currently being "served".

And the current fiasco is no "service" to anybody, least of all COBOL.

Pete.


Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is the Standards process broken?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T10:26:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9e11kf$1is$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<9duvkt$krr$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b034a77_4@Usenet.com>`

```

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@free.net.nz> wrote in message
news:3b034a77_4@Usenet.com (noted by leading >) in response to my prior
comments (noted by leading > >):

> > Various people at various times have asserted that the process by which
the
> > COBOL standard is being developed is "broken", that the people
responsible
> > for "breaking" it should acknowledge their failings and strive to repair
the
> > damage they have done.

> Unreasonable?

Depends on whether it's true or not.  That's the question I'm asking, and I
cite reasons to presume it might not be.

> >  ... With this change in authority, made as the result of a perceived
need, comes
> > acceptance of the rules by which that new authority operates.   ISO/IEC
has
> > a right to its "one country, one vote" conventions, with all the
> > negotiations that entails.  They have every right to maintain those
> > conventions whether I think they should or not.

> So it is now a more "democratic" process than it was?

I can't say whether it's more "democratic"; if you mean the term narrowly,
that depends on the mechanisms that the individual national bodies use to
arrive at their conclusions, and there is no requirement within ISO/IEC
rules that specifies that that must be a democratic process.   But the
process does boil down, certainly at the WG4 level, to "one country, one
vote".   The "Technical Advisory Group" sub-sub-committee of J4, "J4-TAG",
has the role of deciding the US position on these issues, and it follows the
rules of NCITS.  What processes BSI or AFNOR or DIN or any of the other
national standards bodies use to arrive at a national position is up to
their national standards bodies.

I think it's actually more responsive to minority interests -- for example,
Americans are used to using "$" as a leading floating currency symbol, but
in forty years no approved standard has yet allowed Venezuelans to use their
symbol "Bs".  The new process has allowed that.

> I wasn't aware of
> this, and read with interest what you posted above. Are you now saying
that
> the delay has been caused because International approval/agreement was not
> forthcoming?

I can't say whether it's more "democratic" or not; I can say that interested
parties from other countries now are specifically given authority in the
process, where in the development of prior standards their input was merely
advisory.

But I am saying that it takes rather longer to obtain formal agreement from
*two* different groups -- one to whom development responsibility has been
delegated and one which retains responsibility over the ultimate contents --
than it does when both levels of authority and responsibility lie in the
same body, as they basically did for the prior standards.  It takes longer
to raise and answer the question formally at *two* levels of authority than
it does to do so at *one*, even if everybody voting is in agreement.

> >  ... and the fact that individual national bodies have sometimes
> > taken opposite positions on the same subject, leading to lengthy
> > negotiations -- has contributed to the fact that the process of
developing
> > the FCD has been slower than it might have been.

> Ah, I see that you are saying that...<G>

No; we will only know if agreement is *not* forthcoming if the national
standards bodies do not *reach* agreement.  What I was describing is when
they did not *start out* in agreement.

> > Does the fault lie ... < list of several possibilities>

> Or, Does the fault lie with the simple fact that the whole process is
> unresponsive and unaccountable?

Responsive to whom?  According to what definition?   How much less or more
responsive is it than other processes that have been tried in the same
context?

The prior *formal process* was basically that you had a formal say in the
contents of American National Standard COBOL if you were somehow allied with
an American organization, and if you weren't, well, too bad, maybe we'll
listen to your suggestions and maybe we won't.  Is that process responsive
to the needs of programmers in Japan or Denmark?

And I'm trying to figure out how a process can be "accountable".   The
individuals involved in the process are accountable to their national
standards organizations, and the joint committees formed by those national
standards bodies are accountable to ISO/IEC.  To what *formal* organization
do you believe the process should be accountable?  I am not a proponent of
each implementor using Humpty Dumpty's   approach to the definition of the
language (see Chapter 6 of "Through the Looking Glass"), so I feel it is
appropriate for *some* formal body to be in charge of any given standard,
including this one.

Note also that despite all the protestations I have heard to the contrary, I
have not yet seen evidence sufficient to convince me that the current
process is broken because it is *un*responsive to the desires of the
worldwide COBOL community; in fact, I am inclined to believe that, if the
process is flawed in that department, it is because it was *too* responsive
to the desires of the worldwide COBOL community.  The various committees
have tried to accommodate just about everybody's desires.   Now, when
they've cried "Enough!  Time to get the standard *published*!", there's a
hue and cry because they're not adding this last little feature or that last
little adjustment!    That's how it looks to *this* observer, anyway!

> > And if so for more than one of these, to what degree do we apportion the
> > blame among them?

> Apportionment of blame is a fruitless exercise.

I agree here.   That's one of the reasons I brought it up.

> Responsibility for a problem and the willingness to learn from it is the
best that can be hoped for from
> a bad situation.

It is not clear to me that there's a problem for which responsibility can be
assigned.

> > While the current process is arguably ponderous, I'm not convinced it's
> > *wrong*.  I believe international standards arrived at by formal
consensus
> > are a good thing in and of themselves, and I believe there is no way to
> > avoid intrinsic limitations to any process by which such international
> > formal consensus can be reached.  But I do not believe the processes
should
> > be abandoned because their limitations or even their intricacies have in
> > some cases slowed or otherwise restricted progress toward that final
> > consensus.

> So now it's the fault of the International Community <G>?

What do you mean by "it"?  American xenophobia in the 1960's?  The cold war?
The perception that ANSI had a stranglehold on the development of language
standards and programmers in other countries had no say in that process?

> I don't personally care if God and all the Angels, or Satan and all the
Demons are responsible; the fact > is it has failed.

Only if everyone ignores the standard has the standard failed.  That may
happen, it may not.  That the standards process has failed to do what *you*
wanted it to do in the time frame *you* wanted it to do it does not mean
that the standards process has failed everyone utterly.

> Given the description you have laid out, Chuck, what can be done about it?

I'm not yet convinced anything should.  But if it *is* necessary to address
this issue, I believe *when* is an important question, and my response to
that is "probably in early 2003, once the current FCD has been adopted."

> If we want to make the review process more interactive and timely (and I
certainly do), do we now need > the approval of half the World?

Yes, I think we do.  What half would you seek to disenfranchise?

> What mechanisms are in place to obtain such approval? (I bet it involves
> submission and review and submission and review and submission and
> review...)

If the process is readdressed, your speculation is just that.

>  Why can't we set up an Internet Forum (with a preset Topic for each
section
> of the standard, and another one for General Comments) where ANYBODY can
> post their suggestions and comments on the standard (irrespective of where
> they happen to live)?

I'm not sure all the interested parties have access to Internet Forums.  And
some countries and national standards bodies may not agree that comments
from five thousand American programmers on a topic involving, say,
localization, should outweigh five commentors from Korea by a factor of a
thousand-to-one in the final decision process!   While I believe the J4 and
J4-TAG  procedures to be basically on the "representative republic" model,
and those of WG4 on the US Senate model, there is no requirement that any of
the processes be this "democratic", nor am I convinced that it's wrong if
they're not.

> Run such a forum for a given period (Not to exceed, say 4 months), then
> implement what the consensus is, as an Official Draft Standard.

No, I don't think that'll work.  I have seen democracy at work in its pure
form.

> No closed committees, completely open,

Can't speak to the other national bodies.  But membership in J4 is
straightforward.  And the meetings of J4 and J4-TAG are open to guests.

> with all interested parties (not vested interests) having their say.

I'm not sure I know what distinction you are attempting to draw between
"interested parties" and "vested interests", what your definitions for each
are, and why one should be precluded from providing input.

> Where there is controversy over a feature, we allow 2 weeks on the
Internet
> for concerned parties to post their arguments, then 1 week for the User
base
> to vote. The final count is binding and people will accept that  they win
> some and lose some (in the original spirit of Democracy). No petulant
posts
> or bomb threats from disgruntled losers, please...<G>.

No, I don't think I agree that that's the *best* way to resolve problems
that may be absolutely vital to a small subset of the community.

> Vendors will implement conforming  and partially conforming versions of
this
> standard and, again, a centralised Internet Forum can be used to elicit
> feedback from the User Community. If there isn't any, then Good! We got it
> right!

I'm not opposed to that, though (e.g.) the mailing list for J4 has been
published on the internet for quite some time, so that's not as big a change
as you seem to indicate!

As to *e*liciting feedback, I doubt that.  *So*licit, maybe.  But the
absence of feedback is no more indicative of perfection than it is of ennui.

> We then have an "Official standard" (I really don't care who ratifies and
> controls it...ANSI, ISO whoever (provided they keep it in the Public
> Domain...as was originally intended), so the people who care about that
can
> be happy.)

I do care, because I believe it does need to be an *International* as well
as *internationally-recognized* standards body.

> The vendors will be getting direct feedback (the most valuable Market
> intelligence, and this is what drives them.)

Are you of the opinion that COBOL vendors (and COBOL implementors) are *not*
getting feedback from the users of their products today?

> And the COBOL Community gets an involvement in the Language and no longer
> need feel sidelined and impotent regarding what is in it.

Participation in the process is already voluntary and open to anyone who
wants to participate.   Participation also allows anyone the opportunity to
gain a better understanding as to why their suggestions might be rejected.

> Repeat the above process for the remaining years of COBOL's Life Cycle.
> (about 20, I reckon...although general use of the Language will cease well
> before that.)

And that in itself isn't a problem for me either.

> I know the above won't happen,

Some of it might just, Peter.  Some of it isn't practical.  And some of it
reintroduces the very problems that the current process was implemented to
correct.

> but I've taken the time to post the scenario
> anyway because if just one person thinks "yeah...why can't we do that?",
> they may then question how they are currently being "served".

> And the current fiasco is no "service" to anybody, least of all COBOL.

I'm not convinced of that.  You have already stated elsewhere that the
proposed standard contains a number of features you think are worthwhile.
Are you now unhappy enough with its contents overall to dismiss it utterly
as a "fiasco"?   Are you unhappy because the standard has *too few* new
features?  *Too many*?

To me, if the FCD has a significant shortcoming it's that it introduces *so
many* new features.  I am of the personal opinion that the COBOL community
*might* have been better served had fewer features been introduced sooner,
and I suspect that you agree with me on that point.  In fact, I believe the
inclusion of *so many* features involving *so many* topics was arguably the
most significant factor in the length of time it took to get the document to
Final Committee Draft state.  But I, and you, are not the only ones with
opinions on this topic.

These decisions are currently made by a group of *volunteers* who take the
time and trouble and resources to show up and to work out the details.  The
opinions arrived at by those volunteers are subjected to the formal scrutiny
of similar bodies around the world.  This process is sometimes ponderous, I
concede.  Only if rapidity is the most important factor in the standards
process does that ponderousness represent a fundamental failure, and I have
not yet been convinced that the caution that ponderousness represents is a
fundamental failure.

Moreover, I believe, despite its flaws, it's more likely to produce a better
product than the alternative you propose, though I believe some of your
ideas can be incorporated.

But as a basic process, I am thoroughly unconvinced that the appropriate way
to decide how Korean banks should handle their character sets when dealing
with Thai businesses, or to decide whether Venezuela deserves to have its
currency represented as readily and as conveniently as the United States,
Britain, or even Mexico, much less what mechanisms either group should be
using to accomplish that end, is by conducting internet-based polls (in
English or otherwise).  I believe that stands a significant risk of
returning us to the days of COBOL as a fundamentally 'Murkin product, and I
do *not* think that is a Good Thing.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is the Standards process broken?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T13:52:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9e16j0$jvr$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9duvkt$krr$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b034a77_4@Usenet.com> <9e11kf$1is$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:9e11kf$1is$1@mail.pl.unisys.com...
>
<snip>
>
> > So it is now a more "democratic" process than it was?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> vote".
 <snip>

Chuck,
  This is a statement that I *must* correct.  It is true that at the Working
Group level, each country has a "single delegation" and that all delegations
are "treated the same".  However, it is important that anyone trying to
understand the ISO rules be VERY aware that

   VOTES ARE *NEVER* taken at the WG level.

There is *no* concept of one country - one vote - because there are *never*
votes.   Furthermore, even at levels *above* the working group, the concept
of "vote" is pretty UNCLEAR.  For example, in the current "question" of
whether the FCD is or is not "approved" by the ISO "call for
international/country input" - there are EXPLICITLY no rules for determining
whether or not the "vote passes" (other than the rule that the ISO
secretariat - with consultation of the WG convenor "determines" whether or
not "significant consensus" is obtained.)

This means that it is ENTIRELY possible (and has happened) that the MAJORITY
of countries "vote NO" - but the FCD is judged to "pass".  This "subjective"
nature of ISO (not true in ANSI) is one of the things about their procedure
that I (personally) dislike.

It should also be noted that one other MAJOR difference between ANSI rules
and ISO rules is that in ANSI all documents and meetings are "open to the
public" while for ISO *explicitly* no meetings (or minutes of meetings) are
"public" and documents are *not* to be "divulged" outside of the
working-group (or appropriate body) except when explicitly designated (and
this does NOT happen very often).
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

    -Chuck Stevens

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is the Standards process broken?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-18T10:32:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9e3ma7$scj$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<9duvkt$krr$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b034a77_4@Usenet.com> <9e11kf$1is$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9e16j0$jvr$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9e16j0$jvr$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...

> Chuck,
>   This is a statement that I *must* correct.  It is true that at the
Working
> Group level, each country has a "single delegation" and that all
delegations
> are "treated the same".  However, it is important that anyone trying to
> understand the ISO rules be VERY aware that
>
>    VOTES ARE *NEVER* taken at the WG level.

At that level, that may well be true; a quick perusal of
http://www.jtc1.org/directives/main.htm doesn't reveal specific information
one way or another.

> There is *no* concept of one country - one vote - because there are
*never*
> votes.

Just out of curiosity, do you have a reference that indicates votes are
prohibited?   Also see below.

> Furthermore, even at levels *above* the working group, the concept
> of "vote" is pretty UNCLEAR.  For example, in the current "question" of
> whether the FCD is or is not "approved" by the ISO "call for
> international/country input" - there are EXPLICITLY no rules for
determining
> whether or not the "vote passes" (other than the rule that the ISO
> secretariat - with consultation of the WG convenor "determines" whether or
> not "significant consensus" is obtained.)

At the International Technology Task Force level, the rules not only exist,
they are very clear.  See Section 9.6 in the document to which the above URL
points; some details below.

> This means that it is ENTIRELY possible (and has happened) that the
MAJORITY
> of countries "vote NO" - but the FCD is judged to "pass".  This
"subjective"
> nature of ISO (not true in ANSI) is one of the things about their
procedure
> that I (personally) dislike.

If they're voting on a DIS, two-thirds of the "P-members" of the ITTF must
support a measure, and not more than a quarter of those votes may be
negative.  If not more than 50% of the eligible P-members vote in the first
place, the measure fails.  And these votes are most definitely "one national
standards body per country, one vote per national standards body", as I read
the procedures.

Thus, I am unconvinced that your assertion "There is *no* concept of one
country - one vote - because there are *never* votes." is entirely accurate
as it applies to ISO/IEC procedures relating to either the technical content
or the administrative progress of the FCD into DIS and ultimately IS status
(it may be true at the WG4 level, but from what I can tell, not higher).
The chapter cited above was last modified 8/13/1998; perhaps you missed the
change, and perhaps your experiences predate that change.  But right now,
there *are* votes within ISO/IEC required for the adoption of the IS, and
the rules for them are quite specific.

> It should also be noted that one other MAJOR difference between ANSI rules
> and ISO rules is that in ANSI all documents and meetings are "open to the
> public" while for ISO *explicitly* no meetings (or minutes of meetings)
are
> "public" and documents are *not* to be "divulged" outside of the
> working-group (or appropriate body) except when explicitly designated (and
> this does NOT happen very often).

I had kind of gathered that, and I see some evidence of a desire *within
ISO* not to publicize partial or intermediate results.

But this draft was developed by J4 (admittedly under the authority of
ISO/IEC JTC1/SC22/WG4), and the meetings at which changes to the draft were
enacted/approved were held under ANSI rules.  That means the meetings were
open to the public, and it means that minutes are available for them,
including the changes made at WG4's request.   Moreover, the draft in its
various stages of completion has been available on the internet for some
time now.

So from the standpoint of interaction between J4 and WG4 (and SC22 and JTC1
and the ISO/IEC secretariat itself), I'm not sure the mechanism by which
ISO/IEC/JTC1/SC22/WG4 arrives at a decision that will impact the contents or
the processing of the FCD matters anything like as much to me as what that
decision was.  I do know that ANSI has a "say" in that decision.

That ANSI's "say" in this has no more weight than, say, Norway's, *and vice
versa*, is enough for me.

I also believe it would be arrogant of me to attempt to impose my
expectations of proper procedures as gleaned from my citizenship in the
United States onto the citizens and the governmental bodies of other
countries, or onto consortia of such bodies (which is what ISO/IEC JTC1
actually is).  JTC1 is going to follow JTC1's procedures to come out with
the decisions JTC1 concludes JTC1 members should abide by, and those
procedures for arriving at decisions seem to suit JTC1's membership just
fine.

The sort of presumptuousness behind the expectation that ISO/IEC
JTC1/SC22/WG4 would follow, for example, ANSI's lead in its internal
mechanisms and procedures just because 'Murkins think ANSI's procedures work
better *for everyone* is exactly the same sort of attitude that I think was
largely responsible for the transfer of responsibility for the standard from
ANSI's lap into ISO/IEC's in the first place.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is the Standards process broken?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-18T14:06:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9e3rqg$q48$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9duvkt$krr$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b034a77_4@Usenet.com> <9e11kf$1is$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9e16j0$jvr$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9e3ma7$scj$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:9e3ma7$scj$1@mail.pl.unisys.com...
>
> William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
> news:9e16j0$jvr$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...
>
 <snip>
>
> > This means that it is ENTIRELY possible (and has happened) that the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> negative.  If not more than 50% of the eligible P-members vote in the
first
> place, the measure fails.  And these votes are most definitely "one
national
> standards body per country, one vote per national standards body", as I
read
> the procedures.
>
> Thus, I am unconvinced that your assertion "There is *no* concept of one
> country - one vote - because there are *never* votes." is entirely
accurate
> as it applies to ISO/IEC procedures relating to either the technical
content
> or the administrative progress of the FCD into DIS and ultimately IS
status
> (it may be true at the WG4 level, but from what I can tell, not higher).
> The chapter cited above was last modified 8/13/1998; perhaps you missed
the
> change, and perhaps your experiences predate that change.  But right now,
> there *are* votes within ISO/IEC required for the adoption of the IS, and
> the rules for them are quite specific.
>

Chuck,
  I did distinguish between WG and ISO as far as votes go.  The former
doesn't happen - that latter being "unclear" (in my words).

You are correct that there are rules for voting on FDIS - but notice that
these apply to FDIS and do *not* apply to going from FCD to FDIS.

You are correct that I (slightly) overstated what happens at the Working
Group level.  There are "straw votes" taken - but those are (at WG4) usually
taken on a "person by person" level and NOT by country.  Then *if* there
doesn't seem to be "consensus" there are country "straw votes".  The reason
that they are called such is that there are NO rules for judging what
"passes" and what doesn't.  There are also votes (which are almost always
limited to "unanamous" topics) at the end of the meeting.

Personally, I would suggest that you (Chuck) or anyone else that is
interested in participating in their country's "delegation" to a COBOL
working group meeting, contact their "head of delegation" to find out
exactly how all of this is handled at the WG level.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is the Standards process broken?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@fnospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-19T13:04:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b05cae5_1@news3>`
- **References:** `<9duvkt$krr$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b034a77_4@Usenet.com> <9e11kf$1is$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9e16j0$jvr$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9e3ma7$scj$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
This will be my last post on this topic.

"Is the standards process broken?"

Yes. It is.

(Fortunately, for the vast majority of us, it doesn't matter.)

We have seen a lengthy debate which has attempted every kind of
prevarication and smokescreen possible:

1. The people on the committees work hard and are dedicated. It is churlish
not to support them by re-affirming the standard. (Unfortunately, the result
is still "too little, too late".)

2. NOBODY is responsible for it being late. (In fact NOBODY is responsible
for it, full stop.)

3. COBOL will die if there is no Official standard. (It won't... certainly
not any quicker than it will "die" anyway...)

4. The procedures which are in place are fine; there are mechanisms for
feeding back your views. (If you are a pedantic bush lawyer, enjoy
submitting legalese, and don't mind wading through molasses to do so...).

5. It has to be "right" and that is why it has taken so long. (Nope. It can
be iteratively reviewed and gradually get "righter" and "righter"...RAD
versus the Waterfall.)

6. EVERYBODY agrees that it shouldn't be Copyright, but that is how ANSI
have always operated, so it has to be. (Jury still out on this one...)

7. It is pertinent and relevant. (yet less than .012% of the estimated COBOL
community have EVER posted any opinion about it, using the existing
procedures.)

8. Delay has been caused by the necessity to get International approval and
the fact that the whole process of Standardisation has been made arguably
"more democratic". (yeah, right...)

9. If there is no standard, portability will be lost. (Only if there is no
"de facto" standard; whether it is "official" or not is irrelevant. Vendors
are driven (quite properly) by the marketplace and anyone who is "out of
step" will see a corresponding decline in profit. )

10. If there is no standard, COBOL will lose credibility. (Like, the fact
that a standard can't be produced in 10 years gives us credibility...?!)

As  a COBOL Programmer, I am bitterly disillusioned and disappointed by the
whole deal. As far as making a living goes, I realise it really doesn't
matter a bit.

I also realise that there is NO hope of EVER fixing the current process,
because the people involved LIKE it the way it is.

The only people who are really preoccupied or concerned with this are those
who are involved in preparing it.

Now we see a stream of legalistic arguments over the interpretation of the
fine points. The same kind of English in the document itself.

Rock on guys...enjoy yourselves...

You have produced a document which is out of touch, couched in language
which borders on unreadable,
and now you are enjoying arguing amongst yourselves about the interpretation
of the "Laws" which govern its production.

Broken? It is shattered....

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is the Standards process broken?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T14:42:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0105201342.53a5f67e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9duvkt$krr$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b034a77_4@Usenet.com> <9e11kf$1is$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9e16j0$jvr$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <9e3ma7$scj$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote > 
> The sort of presumptuousness behind the expectation that ISO/IEC
> JTC1/SC22/WG4 would follow, for example, ANSI's lead in its internal
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens

----------------

My recollection is that what became the 1985 COBOL standard was being
held up inordinately by the Prudential Insurance Company (a member at
that time of the COBOL Committee), which threatened to sue ANSI if
certain changes were made to the standard (most specifically,
elimination of the ALTER statement).  At that time, COBOL became first
an American Standard, and then was adopted by ANSI. Eventually, the
international community got fed up, and
submitted it to ISO/IEC, which adopted it. ANSI ratified the standard
later, and now we have this procedure.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Committee.

PS: YOU could join the COBOL Standards Committee and make a
difference!
(Not you, Chuck! *LOL* We are grateful to have you already!)
```

#### ↳ Re: Is the Standards process broken?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-19T00:23:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9e4vvg$1hd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9duvkt$krr$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
I wanted to add a couple of things to Chuck's original post.  In many ways I
don't disagree with his analysis - just with how/when things can be
"improved".

1) There were (IMHO) *three* major reasons for the EXTREME delay in the full
revision:

A) It took MULTIPLE years to get an electronic "base document" (copy of the
'85 Standard with amendments applied) for various technical, political, and
"personality" reasons.

B) Although WG4 set a SPECIFIC set of priorities and LIMITED number of
features that were "required" for the new revision, WG4 and J4 *both* ended
up implementing lots of "non-required" items while NOT finishing the
"required" ones.  This even included some items that were not in the
original "desirable" list.  Basically, the two committees had serious
problems with ever saying NO - to any addition - no matter how late.

C) Besides not saying "no" to new things, the committees repeatedly reversed
(and re-reversed) decisions that they had previously made.  This along with
the "two committee" process made the entire process MUCH longer and slower
than necessary.

2) The "standards committees" have tried multiple things to try and "fix"
perceived problems with "past" standards.  None of these seem to have
worked; they remind me a LOT of large companies that "re-org" after every
failure and never really look at the underlying problems.  Some of these
"attempts" have included:

A) "combining" committees.  - (Contrary to what Chuck said, the '85 Standard
"development" was actually done by one committee CODASYL COBOL Committee -
while the "direction" and "editing" were done by another ANSI X3J4).  This
was thought to be "inefficient" so the two committees "combined" at about
the same time the ANSI/ISO COBOL directions were being done.  (There have
been "coordination" requirements between ANSI and ISO for quite a while -
but "whose on top" has changed.)

B) "small and faster" enhancements - This was the thought behind the
"Amendment" process that lead to Intrinsic Functions and Correction
Amendment.  J4 and WG4 discovered that they couldn't do "even these" quickly
so "gave up" on them and went back to a small (LARGE GRIN) revision.

C) more frequent meetings (It used to be 4 or 5 - five day meetings per
year).  We are now up to 5 or 6 meetings lasting 5 to 10 days.  This may or
may not be "effective" - it certainly does seem to me that there is less and
less work being done BETWEEN meetings and those who try and follow the
meetings without attending find it more and more difficult to participate.

3)  My personal opinion is that one of the reasons that the current revision
is "late" is that there was NO attempt to figure out the "process" or the
"content" of this revision while the last one was "winding down".  I see
this happening again.  J4 (and WG4) seem to think that now is NOT the time
to think about what will be in the next revision or how to "process it" -
and don't seem like they will face these issues until this revision is "done
and out".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is the Standards process broken?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-19T16:34:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PgxN6.92400$ho6.5845012@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9duvkt$krr$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9e4vvg$1hd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9e4vvg$1hd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...
> I wanted to add a couple of things to Chuck's original post.  In
many ways I
> don't disagree with his analysis - just with how/when things can be
> "improved".

My two cents:

In organizations that are predominantly male, process is as important
(sometimes more so) than product. In male-dominated organizations we
see an abundance of pomp, organization, hierarchy, tradition,
precedence, regalia, chain-of-command, rules (think military,
legislative bodies, the Shriners, sports, educational institutions,
Hell's Angels).

In 'organizations' populated by women, no one is really in charge,
there is usually (apparent) great confusion (sometimes great emotion),
and the task gets completed on time and under budget  (think PTA or
Thanksgiving dinner).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is the Standards process broken?

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T14:19:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0105201319.fe7248d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9duvkt$krr$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9e4vvg$1hd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <PgxN6.92400$ho6.5845012@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
The standards process is NOT broken. And it doesn't need much in the
way of an overhaul. What COBOL DOES need is more people to do the work
of developing the standard. There are only 12 of us now (with
occasional help from non-members). It is not enough.

After the Corrections Ammendment to the 1985 Standard (ISO 1991, ANSI
1993), the target date for the new standard was 1995 or 1996 (I
forget).  Two MAJOR projects have delayed it all this time.

One was Internationalization, which involved MOCS (Multiple Octet
Character Sets) such as Japanese, Locales (which would set a number of
default features, such as Sort sequences, date and time formats,
currency symbols, etc), and different "Roman" alphabets (e.g., in some
alphabets, and accented E comes after unaccented E; in others it comes
after Z. And then there are all the different letters that are not in
the English Alphabet.),  and some other geographical considerations.

Part of the problem with internationalization was a lack of immediate
expertise on the committee, requiring inquiries and external reviews
of the proposed standard.  Additionally, there are MORE THAN ONE
standards committees working on computer representations  of
alphabets.

Lest  anyone complain that this is a waste of time and it will be a
totally unused feature of COBOL, let me say that this was/is a hot
item for both the Deutsche Institut fuer Normung and the Information
Technology Standards Committee of Japan.

The second feature which seriously delayed the standard was Object
Orientation.
OO development was mostly handled by an "ad hoc" subcommittee of J4
(at that time, X3J4), because not enough of the regular members had
sufficient expertise.  Meetings were held in conjunction with J4 (and
sometimes alone).  But since there were no meeting fees, no NCITS
fees, and no commitment, companies and individuals were willing to
participate on this limited basis.
At one time, the new standard was scheduled for 1996. However, about
this time, X3J4, in consultation with the OO ad hoc, decided that the
syntax/organization of the OO features was flawed and "too much out of
synch with the developing OO techniques."  A conscious decision was
made to make the COBOL OO format match the CORBA model, even tho that
meant a major re-write of the features, and a major delay in the
standard.  CORBA was chosen over COM because CORBA was not
proprietary.

The third feature which delayed the standard was "Binary, Bit and
Boolean".  These are really crucial to bring COBOL into the modern
computing paradigm.  Unfortunately, there awere a lot of unexpected
side effects:
What is the padding character in a Bit Field (or a National field, for
that matter)? A MAJOR change to COBOL fundamentals.  How about the
padding character in a group which consists of all Bit Fields (or
National fields)? That took months of argument: One of the oldest
rules in COBOL is that all Group Names are alphnumeric.  We finally
came up with a new syntax feature: GROUP-BIT and GROUP-NATIONAL, where
the padding characters are binary zeroes and "National" spaces,
respectively.  There were other ways to do it... hence the lack of
initial agreement.

There were also "minor" features which took up a lot of time. For
instance, "Dynamic Tables" was high on the list of desired features. 
There were several proposals (I wrote one of them), but when we
finally adopted it, several compiler makers advised us that there was
"No way we can implement this efficiently."  Ultimately, it was backed
out of the standard.

Work was still being done on OO, Internationalization and BBB just a
few months before the FCD was isssued.  A STANDARD has to be done
RIGHT! If it is full of vagueness, incomplete specifications and
conflicting rules, It is not a standard, it is a recommendation or a
guideline!

While work was being done on these, the other items on the "desirable"
 list, and even the "nice, but not important" list were done. 
Depended on who had the interest to write up the proposal.  But I do
not think that these efforts slowed down the standard at all.  A few
times the meeting even broke up early.

Even now, the OO ad-hoc is working on a Class Library, to be issued in
a year or so as a Technical Addendum to the Standard. (See, there WILL
be changes before the next complete standard!)

In Particular, the committee needs representatives of the COBOL USER
community to work on the language. We need more programmers, and also
more support from the COBOL compiler "community". Realia (Computer
Associates), Ryan-McFarlane (Liant), AccuCOBOL, Tiny COBOL, GNU COBOL,
DEC (Now part of Compaq, which IS represented on the committee by the
Tandem Compiler guru, but that still isn't DEC), and ICL are NOT
represented on the committee.  Membership on the Committee would be a
GREAT assignment/opportunity for a Graduate student in Computer
Science, or a great source of publishable papers for a Computer
Science Faculty member.  There might even be grant money for THOSE
folks!

It's not the process, folks, it's the amount of work and the lack of
help.

YOU can be a member of the COBOL Standards Committee, and make a
difference!

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Committee
```

- **From:** markyoung01@home.com (Mark Young)

On Wed, 16 May 2001 16:02:10 GMT, Michael <nospam@newsranger.com>
wrote:

>Hi, My name is Michael Lorenc, I am 21 years old from Cape Town, South Africa.
>I am currently doing a course in Cobol, but have run into some problems in a
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Try this kind of layout....

01 AGENT-RECORD.
	10 AGENT-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER        PIC 9(6).
	10 AGENT-SURNAME                            PIC  X(15).
	10 AGENT-INITIALS                                PIC  X(3).
	10 AGENT-COMPANY-CODE              PIC 99.
	10 AGENT-SALES-TABLE OCCURS 5 INDEXED BY SALES-INDEX.
	    15 AGENT-POLICY-TYPE                  PIC 99.
                  15 AGENT-AMT-SOLD                      PIC  9(4)V99.


Makes it cleaner....

Also... "more than 2 policy types"?  Do you mean the agent has to make
more than two sales or he has to have sales in two different types of
policies?

You are handling for two or more sales but keep in mind the customer
may mean for you to track agents who are making more than one policy
type sale...  (I think you are on the right track in this case)

>Read the records in... subscript across bump a counter on 
>
…[186 more quoted lines elided]…
>

1. Use Indentions liberally to make it more readable and
maintainable... The advantage that COBOL has over other languages is
the maintainability in the long run.   That works only if the program
is well written.

2. Number your paragraphs as a prefix.

>PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>SORT-EMPLOYEE-RECORDS.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
3. Try having one Write and performing it...  There are arguements
both ways...

move space to print-line
perform 600-printline thru 600-exit.

>WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM SPACES.   
>DISPLAY "PRESS ANY KEY TO CONTINUE".                   
>ACCEPT PAUSE

What is this running on?  not OS390.


>DISPLAY SPACES ERASE.
>STOP RUN. 

4. This is your mainline paragraph....  010-mainline or 010-Driver
would be a good name...

>CALC-AND-EXTRACT.
>OPEN INPUT EMPLOYEE-FILE
>                        COMPANY-FILE
>OUTPUT PRINT-FILE
>PERFORM READ-EMPLOYEE

5. Perform THRU allows you to say exactly what you mean and if future
paragraphs are added, then the logic is not changed accidently.

>PERFORM PROCESS-EMPLOYEE-RECORDS UNTIL NO-DATA-REMAINS
>CLOSE EMPLOYEE-FILE
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>PERFORM PROCESS-ONE-COMPANY 
      UNTIL NO-DATA-REMAINS 
           OR HEADING-COMPANY NOT EQUAL PREV-COMPANY.


try that way....
>
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>    RELEASE SORT-RECORD

Don't ya want to wait to reset Policy-Counter with each new record?
Also if you try initializing it before the read of a new record, you
won't SOC7 the first time you add 1 to Policy-counter...
>
>INITIALISE-COMPANY.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>      ADD 1 TO POLICY-COUNTER.

Boom right here first time thru if you don't set it to zero before
reading a record....


>READ EMPLOYEE-FILE
>     AT END MOVE 'NO' TO DATA-REMAINS-SWITCH
>END-READ.


If you have carriage control defined in the first print line then you
would just move the heading-line-one in and
perform the Write print-line

How about 

900-Print-Headers.
>
>WRITE-HEADING-LINES.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
900-exit. 
        exit.

950-Print-detail.
>WRITE-DETAIL-LINE.
>MOVE SRT-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER TO DET-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL-LINE.

950-exit.
        exit.




>

   Line up your moves and To to make it more readable...

Cheers!
Mark Young

<a id="t-35d6689030"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu Cobol 3.0 DLL and CA-Realizer — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Cobol 3.0 DLL and CA-Realizer

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T02:37:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.lang.ca-realizer
- **Message-ID:** `<6QGM6.26502$4f7.2034330@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Greetings to All,

I'm attempting to write some file management DLLs in Fujitsu Cobol 3.0
(32-bit).  Towards that end, I've been teaching myself and perusing the
online doc and trying different things.

CA-Realizer is Computer Associates' answer to MS Visual Basic.  Unless
something drastic happens at CA, it will never be a strong competitor to
MSVB, but it's what I've got, so I use it.

I've created two different DLLs.  The first one doesn't receive any
parameters, but is called by the Realizer program, DISPLAYs a string, then
exits the program.  This one works fine.

The second one is setup with STDCALL linkage and a 2-byte binary field (PIC
S9(4) COMP-5).  I am trying to call it in the same manner as the first DLL,
passing it a 2-byte integer, and it's causing CA-Realizer to barf all over
the place.  I've tried it with PASCAL linkage (which reverted to STDCALL
linkage) and with C linkage (which compiled OK, but still didn't work).

I'm not using the Windows interface to do the compiling, instead, relying on
a .BAT file to automate the process as much as possible.  I'm an old DOS guy
and find the command line more appropriate to writing programs.  But I may
be leaving something out of the .BAT file that I'm not aware of.  I don't
know what it could be (obviously).  I've copied the example in the online
doc directly and, as I said, the first DLL worked.

I've also taken a quick look at http://www.adtools.com and it simply
directed me here.

An obvious answer is that I'm doing something wrong in the CA-Realizer call.
I've done these calls before, but only with the Win32 API functions and
PowerBASIC DLL code.  I'm continuing to look in that direction, but I don't
think that's necessarily going to be successful.

Has anyone had any experience with this (admittedly marginal) endeavor?  Any
advice to give?  Words of encouragement?  If anyone would like me to post
code, batch files, other information, I will gladly cooperate.

TIA,

Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol 3.0 DLL and CA-Realizer

- **From:** jschroth@gmx.de (Juergen Schroth)
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T14:34:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.lang.ca-realizer
- **Message-ID:** `<9e0k0v$obg$06$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<6QGM6.26502$4f7.2034330@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Hi Paul,

i'm a little bit short on time at the moment, but i can tell you, that i
wrote some DLL's with Delphi. Data exchange between the DLL and CAR
works without problems.

Most problems arise because the definition of the call by CAR was done
in an incorrect format (by value or by reference).

Does the Cobol DLL await a pointer to the binary field, or awaits it the
value of the field?

You may contact me by Email and supply your definitions from CAR.

CU, Juergen
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol 3.0 DLL and CA-Realizer

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T15:30:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010517113044.21890.00000819@nso-cq.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6QGM6.26502$4f7.2034330@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <6QGM6.26502$4f7.2034330@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>, "Paul
Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net> writes:

>
>Has anyone had any experience with this (admittedly marginal) endeavor?  Any
>advice to give?  Words of encouragement?  If anyone would like me to post
>code, batch files, other information, I will gladly cooperate.
>

I have not worked with CA-Realizer , or with the interfacing of VB to Fuji,
but the basics of how to get things to work might become apparent when 
looking at Fujitsu's sample of a VB program calling a COBOL DLL.
I am reasonably certain that there is such a sample on the V3.0 distribution
and on the adtools.com web-site.
My questions would relate to your COBOL code:
specifically,  What does LINKAGE section look like, What does the
PROCEDURE DIVISION statement look like, and finally the CA call
parameter list.  There may be some information we could glean from 
the BAT files used to compile/link the COBOL and the CA programs.
Another thought is the COBOL Compile options, which you probably 
already have resolved since the first DLL test worked.

I have some limited experience with COBOL calling COBOL/ASM/C/PL1
or PL1 calling ASM/COBOL/C.

Hope that this helps get you further down hte road to success.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol 3.0 DLL and CA-Realizer

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T17:31:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.lang.ca-realizer
- **Message-ID:** `<iWTM6.27734$t12.2102590@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<6QGM6.26502$4f7.2034330@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Thank you both Juergen and Sff5ky...

Juergen, your little note jiggered something in my mind.  The example with
the compiler is for VB and shows

    CALL MYCOBOL( vbInteger, vbString )

In the DECLARE, the vbInteger is passed BYREF (as the default) and the
string BYVAL (explicitly defined).  I began playing with the Realizer CALL
and what I ended up doing was define the EXTERNAL as

    (POINTER(INTEGER), POINTER)

which did what I wanted.  It had been

    (INTEGER, POINTER)

Changing the EXTERNAL from the second to the first, the COBOL DLL
successfully modified the integer (first parameter) and the string (second
parameter).  And my system didn't crash either, which is always a good sign.
I might would have figured it out eventually, but thanks to your shaking the
box, it was a lot sooner.

Thanks again for your help.

Paul

Paul Pigott <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:6QGM6.26502$4f7.2034330@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
> Greetings to All,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> The second one is setup with STDCALL linkage and a 2-byte binary field
(PIC

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

call.

- **Date:** 2001-05-24T06:56:43+00:00

```
I can't get AcuCOBOL to access my index files. The index file "STUDENT" is
in fact two separate physical files -> STUDENT.DAT and STUDENT.IDX.

how can I do it?

...

I also want to know how it is possible to compile a program that calls a
subroutine. Do I compile the main program and then the subroutine
separately, then run the main program? or is there a specific command I must
use in order for the main program to link to the subroutine correctly?

...

Any assistance with these problems would be appreciated.

eRRaTiK

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Index Files and Subroutines (using AcuCOBOL)

- **From:** Gary_guizio@ci.juneau.ak.us (GaryG)
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T13:48:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cf4aea75.0105241248.1291ce4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5MJM6.57547$ff.449955@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <_%RM6.1630$5R2.76518687@news1.van.metronet.ca> <Lg2P6.74156$ff.570372@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
"eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<Lg2P6.74156$ff.570372@news-server.bigpond.net.au>...
> I can't get AcuCOBOL to access my index files. The index file "STUDENT" is
> in fact two separate physical files -> STUDENT.DAT and STUDENT.IDX.
> 
> how can I do it?

If you are using Acucobol version 4 of the Vision file system I
believe the index portion should be named STUDENT.VIX. This may not be
the problem because the extension, I believe, can be changed using the
"name_ext" configuration variable.
> 
> ...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
If you mean by "subroutine" another Acucobol program then you would
compile them separately and call the second program from the first by
using the CALL function as in: CALL "program-name" USING "parameter".

If your "subroutine" is a C program, you must first link the program
in to the runtime and then call it the same way you would if it was
another Acucobol program.
> ...
> 
> Any assistance with these problems would be appreciated.
> 
> eRRaTiK
```

If you use the Windows Spooler then it matters (and if you are using
networked printers in a Windows network you will most probably be
using it).
If you are printing directly to LPTx then it doesn't.

Best

FF

<a id="t-ada61fbf60"></a>
## Thread: How to access a dataset with DSORG=DA — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to access a dataset with DSORG=DA

- **From:** "Steven Hughes" <sjhughes1@eircom.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T17:47:41+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xhTM6.326$Fk7.1415@news.indigo.ie>`

```
Hi,
    I am trying to write a Cobol 370 program to access a dataset with a
DSORG of DA.

    I have defined the file in the program with a RELATIVE KEY IS relkey
clause, with relkey defined as PIC 9(04) COMP.  The OPEN INPUT statement
abends with a File-Status of 37.

    When I changed the file to a VSAM RRDS, it works fine.  Why can I not
access a DA dataset?

Thanks in advance,
                                Steven.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to access a dataset with DSORG=DA

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T15:28:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9e1c7a$iee$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<xhTM6.326$Fk7.1415@news.indigo.ie>`

```
I can't find any "explicit" statement that you can't use DSORG=DA files in
current COBOL

HOWEVER, the list of what *is* supported does not include them.

Check out,

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igypg205/1.7.2

which is "1.7.2 Choosing file organization and access mode" in the
Programming Guide
```

#### ↳ Re: How to access a dataset with DSORG=DA

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T21:10:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O6XM6.27921$t12.2114757@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<xhTM6.326$Fk7.1415@news.indigo.ie>`

```
I don't recall accessing a DA file, but if I recall correctly, DA stands for
direct access, which means you've got to specify such things as volume,
cylinder, head, track and sector to actually read the data.  I once did
something like that (if I recall correctly) on a VSE system, accessing a
third party date file to get calendar information.  The routine was actually
written in 370 Assembler because there was no way to do it in COBOL.  It's a
very hardware specific thing.

Paul


Steven Hughes <sjhughes1@eircom.net> wrote in message
news:xhTM6.326$Fk7.1415@news.indigo.ie...
> Hi,
>     I am trying to write a Cobol 370 program to access a dataset with a
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: How to access a dataset with DSORG=DA

- **From:** JD <pb@dazoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T20:49:48-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0471AC.CB4BA710@dazoo.com>`
- **References:** `<xhTM6.326$Fk7.1415@news.indigo.ie>`

```


Steven Hughes wrote:

> Hi,
>     I am trying to write a Cobol 370 program to access a dataset with a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

DSORG=DA.  A direct access dataset.  Access methods are BDAM, EXCP.
Essentially, a relative record file.  Can be blocked or unblocked.  If
unblocked, relative block 1 and relative record 1 are the same.  If blocked,
you will probably need to manually unblock the logical records within the
physical record (at least in assembler, I've never accessed a BDAM with
COBOL, and don't know that you can).

>
> Thanks in advance,
>                                 Steven.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to access a dataset with DSORG=DA

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-18T11:02:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9e3h22$ahj$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<xhTM6.326$Fk7.1415@news.indigo.ie> <3B0471AC.CB4BA710@dazoo.com>`

```
For a discussion of BDAM (and *lack* of support in the current COBOL
compilers), see


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igymg202/4.1.4.2?
```

#### ↳ Re: How to access a dataset with DSORG=DA

- **From:** "Steven Hughes" <sjhughes1@eircom.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-18T18:00:28+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HzcN6.569$Fk7.2903@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<xhTM6.326$Fk7.1415@news.indigo.ie>`

```
OK, I cheated.  Wrote it in PL/1.  Defined as REGIONAL(3) file.

Thanks to all.
                       Steven.

>Hi,

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

## Thread: accessing vision-files — 2 msgs

### accessing vision-files

- **From:** "Philip Foncke" <philip.foncke@advalvas.be>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T16:41:13+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AqRM6.1635$35.170496@iguano.antw.online.be>`

```
anybody who has accessed acucobol vision-file (v3) from a c-program ?
```

#### ↳ Re: accessing vision-files

- **From:** "Ray Smith" <Ray.Smith@fujitsu.com.au>
- **Date:** 2001-05-18T09:35:48+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9e1jea$g2e@newshost.fujitsu.com.au>`
- **References:** `<AqRM6.1635$35.170496@iguano.antw.online.be>`

```
Philip Foncke <philip.foncke@advalvas.be> wrote in message
news:AqRM6.1635$35.170496@iguano.antw.online.be...
> anybody who has accessed acucobol vision-file (v3) from a c-program ?
>

I haven't done it but believe it is possible.

The actual File IO routines ACU uses are written in C and linked into the
runtime.

If you contact ACU support they should be able to give you details of the C
routine
which is available from the Acu Runtime called "I$IO".  I assume this could
be linked
into any C program you like.

Ray Smith
```

<a id="t-3fb6da56bf"></a>
## Thread: Final CfP: BABEL Workshop on Multilanguage Infrastructure and Interoperability — 1 msgs

### Final CfP: BABEL Workshop on Multilanguage Infrastructure and Interoperability

- **From:** "Nick Benton" <nick@microsoft.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T15:45:03+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.ada,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b03e3f7$1@news.microsoft.com>`

```
                               BABEL'01
                         FINAL CALL FOR PAPERS

                   First Workshop on Multi-Language
                 Infrastructure and Interoperability

           http://research.microsoft.com/~nick/babel01.htm

                         Satellite to PLI'01

                  Firenze, Italy, 8th September 2001

                  Submission Deadline: 1st June 2001

                 ** The Submission Site is now open **
               http://cmt.research.microsoft.com/BABEL01/

AIMS AND SCOPE

Recent years have seen a resurgence of interest in multi-language
tools and intermediate languages, and in interoperability between
programs and components written in different programming
languages. Shared infrastructure such as code generators, analysis
tools and garbage collectors can greatly ease the task of producing a
high-quality implementation of a new programming language, whilst
being able to interoperate easily with code written in existing
languages is essential for such an implementation to be useful in
practice.  This workshop aims to bring together researchers and
developers working on multi-language integration. Contributions are
welcome on topics including, but not limited to:


* Compilation of high level languages to common executable formats
  such as Sun's Java Virtual Machine, the DRA's ANDF or Microsoft's
  .NET Common Language Runtime.

* Defining and using bindings for component middleware such as OMG's
  CORBA or Microsoft's COM.

* Language constructs to support interoperability between different
  languages, particularly from different paradigms (e.g. OO/functional).

* Multi-language backends for compilation and/or analysis
  (e.g. MLRISC, FLINT, C--, TAL, SUIF).

* Type theoretic and semantic foundations for multi-language work.

* Multi-language development environments and tools (e.g. debuggers,
  profilers).

Submissions may address any programming paradigm.  Experience papers
which describe the use of multiple languages in application
development are also welcome. Authors unsure of the appropriateness of
a potential submission should email the PC chair (nick@microsoft.com).


PROGRAMME COMMITTEE

Nick Benton (chair)          Microsoft Research
Fergus Henderson             University of Melbourne
Andrew Kennedy (organiser)   Microsoft Research
Greg Morrisett               Cornell University
Martin Odersky               Ecole Polytechnique Federale de Lausanne
John Reppy                   Bell Labs
Andrew Tolmach               Portland State University
David Wakeling               University of Exeter


INVITED SPEAKERS

Zhong Shao                   Yale University


IMPORTANT DATES

Deadline for submission      1st June 2001
Notification of acceptance   9th July 2001
Final version due            10th August 2001


SUBMISSION DETAILS

Papers should be at most 14 pages and should be submitted in
Ghostscript-compatible PostScript or PDF format and be printable on
both US letter and A4 paper.  Authors are strongly encouraged to use
ENTCS style files (see http://math.tulane.edu/~entcs/).  Papers should
be submitted electronically via the submission site at
            http://cmt.research.microsoft.com/BABEL01/
Submissions should not overlap significantly with work which has
already been published or submitted to any other conference or
journal.  An informal proceedings will be published as a technical
report and distributed at the workshop.  It is intended that a final
proceedings will be published in a volume of ENTCS.
```

---

- **From:** Robaire <rmiller@linux.ca>

#### ↳ Re: Need answer to Cobol trivia

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@fnospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-18T15:01:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b05188d_2@news3>`
- **References:** `<20010315034757.19316.00001039@ng-bd1.aol.com> <3ab0cd9d_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <rlp7gtscln96gu9l8tfq770m2ik80lean8@4ax.com>`

```
Despite apocryphal stories that the name is derived from Engineers referring
to them as "Those Motherfucking boards..." (which MIGHT be true <G>), I
believe the Professor was trying to encourage the students to think about
the functions performed by the Motherboard.

It is the "heart" of the system, just as Mother is the "heart" of the
family.

It provides the essential services without which the "family" cannot run.

It is responsible for "system Housekeeping" .

It corrects errors when they occur (picks you up when you graze your
knee...)

It communicates with all the members of the family.

It faithfully and dutifully carries out the duties required of it by the
software.

Without it, there is nothing.

Pete.



"Robaire" <rmiller@linux.ca> wrote in message
news:rlp7gtscln96gu9l8tfq770m2ik80lean8@4ax.com...
> Le Fri, 16 Mar 2001 02:50:41 +1300, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> a ï¿½crit:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Your Professor was right to set this task...anyone of average
intelligence
> >and imagination can figure out why it is called a Motherboard within a
few
> >minutes. But it does require thought...
> >
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need answer to Cobol trivia

- **Message-ID:** `<9e4c6v$bc2$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010315034757.19316.00001039@ng-bd1.aol.com> <3ab0cd9d_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <rlp7gtscln96gu9l8tfq770m2ik80lean8@4ax.com> <3b05188d_2@news3> <jj9bgt0186kmo3v56l5uimro7og988pf7g@4ax.com>`

In article <jj9bgt0186kmo3v56l5uimro7og988pf7g@4ax.com>, rmiller@linux.ca 
says...
>
>Le Fri, 18 May 2001 15:01:39 +1200, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Actually (don't you hate people who say that?) I knew the term as an 
electronics technician working with electronic organs long before the rise of 
the IBM PC. Basically, Pete is correct, but to complete the analogy, those 
other boards which we simply refer to as 'cards' ( ie I/O card, or Video card) 
all come under the generic term of daughter boards.

As a Family Court lawyer, I have to approve, of course!

The only thing I don't know is, why only a female connection in what used to be 
almost exclusively a male domain?

D J H Stringer
Barrister & Solicitor
Christchurch
New Zealand
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need answer to Cobol trivia

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiammD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-19T06:19:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B061035.8C84CC78@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<20010315034757.19316.00001039@ng-bd1.aol.com> <3ab0cd9d_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <rlp7gtscln96gu9l8tfq770m2ik80lean8@4ax.com> <3b05188d_2@news3> <jj9bgt0186kmo3v56l5uimro7og988pf7g@4ax.com> <9e4c6v$bc2$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`

```
stringer wrote:
> 
(snip)
> >
> Actually (don't you hate people who say that?) I knew the term as an
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> almost exclusively a male domain?

Machinery, like boats, is generally considered to be of the feminine
persuasion (as were hurricanes until a few years ago), e.g., in French
it's "la machine", not "le machine", i.e., feminine, I think Italian has
"la machina", etc. Also racing cars, e.g., "she'll come right by
Sunday".

As far as why female in a male domain, it might be the most female
contact many engineers get <vbg>

LiamD
```
