###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need answer to Cobol trivia

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T22:37:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uGXN6.1297$Jm1.504720@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<20010315034757.19316.00001039@ng-bd1.aol.com> <3ab0cd9d_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <rlp7gtscln96gu9l8tfq770m2ik80lean8@4ax.com> <3b05188d_2@news3> <jj9bgt0186kmo3v56l5uimro7og988pf7g@4ax.com> <9e4c6v$bc2$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <5o2dgtsg1clfp9l4e2g4155adjdtk9pmq9@4ax.com> <t7xN6.92295$ho6.5842388@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <tYNN6.1947$YZ.453264@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <tu4ggtcpcdqn6stm9f6e68223498mdeeqi@4ax.com>`

```
You will note both "offensive" and "insensitive" were placed in quotes. This
was to emphasize that there is, in fact, nothing offensive or insensitive
about the word, "niggardly." This is why both the resignation its acceptance
were the actions of imbeciles. (Deliberate suppression of quotes).

I think you are in Germany, and might not be terribly familiar with American
colloquial vulgarity.

The reason the use of the word "niggardly" evoked an outcry among some on
this side of the pond is its phonetic similarity to the word, "nigger,"
which is a vulgar American term for a black person (a Negro).

I do not know European culture much at all; I guess the closest parallel I
could make would be to refer to a German in public by saying something like,
"Oh, ignore Volker, he's just a kraut."  "Kraut" is an American term for
Germans coined during WWI and re-used during WWII for propaganda purposes to
paint the enemy as something less than human.

Today neither "nigger" nor "kraut" (nor about fifteen other ethnic
appellations with which I shall not bore you) is considered suitable for use
in polite society.

Sometimes this politeness goes a bit too far and becomes political
correctness. This is why we no longer refer to autumn as the time when
there's a little nip in the air; why we no longer clean our homes until they
are spic-and-span. We don't jerry-rig solutions when we don't have the
correct parts for the job; if one is considered vulnerable, we can't say he
has a chink in the armor.

Hell, there's even a movement afoot to ban all references to doo-wop music.


MCM

Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote in message
news:tu4ggtcpcdqn6stm9f6e68223498mdeeqi@4ax.com...

>

> miserly. [Middle English nigard, perhaps from nig, stingy person, of
Scandinavian origin.]
>

Believe it or not, there have been attempts in the past by various
people to have both books banned from Public School libraries.

have "Catcher in the Rye", "Lord of the Flies" and "Animal Farm"
banned.

banned.  At least I hope so as I still enjoy a good old doo-wop song
now and then.

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

hell is just having to listen to our grandparents
breathe through their noses when they're eating

>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Yep - never avoid an argument you cannot win <g>

>But your own statement below. You gotta be kidding. If they are trying to take
>it to this degree of absurdity, how long before they ban 'Uncle Tom's Cabin' or
>'Huckleberry Fin' ?

Oh, it hasn't been banned yet?  Now I _am_ surprised....  Ever heard about Lord of the
Flies?  Or Catcher in the Rye?  When I was attending a High school in Connecticut in 1972,
that was required reading.  Now it is banned insome places

BTW - How can literature  be banned anyways??  The German Grundgesetz (=Fundamental Law ,
similar to Constitution) says: 

Eine Zensur findet nicht statt.

(There is nor censoring)  How does this relate to banned literatue?

- **Date:** 2001-05-21T21:13:20+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5loigtgpt0bib3m2s8jd4h0ksif0enask2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<20010315034757.19316.00001039@ng-bd1.aol.com> <3ab0cd9d_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <rlp7gtscln96gu9l8tfq770m2ik80lean8@4ax.com> <3b05188d_2@news3> <jj9bgt0186kmo3v56l5uimro7og988pf7g@4ax.com> <9e4c6v$bc2$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <5o2dgtsg1clfp9l4e2g4155adjdtk9pmq9@4ax.com> <t7xN6.92295$ho6.5842388@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <tYNN6.1947$YZ.453264@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <tu4ggtcpcdqn6stm9f6e68223498mdeeqi@4ax.com> <uGXN6.1297$Jm1.504720@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```




On Sun, 20 May 2001 22:37:14 GMT, "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>You will note both "offensive" and "insensitive" were placed in quotes. This
>was to emphasize that there is, in fact, nothing offensive or insensitive
>about the word, "niggardly." This is why both the resignation its acceptance
>were the actions of imbeciles. (Deliberate suppression of quotes).

Yes, I did, and I am not so uneducated about languages not to have noticed.  One thing, of
course, is for sure: If person A uses the term "niggardly" and person B accepts the
resignation, both _are_ used the term in an offensive, abusive meaning.  Still, the word
itself isn't offensive at all 

>
>I think you are in Germany, and might not be terribly familiar with American
>colloquial vulgarity.
Well, I am (in Germany) and I am (a little bit) familar with American colloquialisms. 


>The reason the use of the word "niggardly" evoked an outcry among some on
>this side of the pond is its phonetic similarity to the word, "nigger,"
>which is a vulgar American term for a black person (a Negro).
I understand all of this, but even calling a person a NEGRO in the US is "off limits" now.
In Germany, we callthe NEGER (obviously the same linguistic root) or SCHWARZER/FARBIGER
(which would be BLACK/COLOURED)Nobody thinks bad about this. Now I understand NEGRO is a
politically uncorrect term. What is the correct term? 

>
>I do not know European culture much at all; I guess the closest parallel I
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>paint the enemy as something less than human.

When I was in the US (many years ago), I was introduced to quite a few folks as "And this
is our visitor/guest/friend Volker.  He is a Kraut, but usually quite okay"  I did not
take that as offensive or insuling

>
>Sometimes this politeness goes a bit too far and becomes political
>correctness. This is why we no longer refer to autumn as the time when
>there's a little nip in the air;
Sorry, this I do _not_ understand

>We don't jerry-rig solutions when we don't have the
>correct parts for the job;
Sorry, but isn' tthe correct term jury-rigged?

>if one is considered vulnerable, we can't say he
>has a chink in the armor.
And why not? Dent is more familiar, but why not chink (as opposed to Chink)
 
>
>Hell, there's even a movement afoot to ban all references to doo-wop music.
Don't even know what doo-wop music might be (well, I did look it up right now, but still
don't know: a) what it is, and b) why the use of the term should be banned


Anyways - You Americans don't fail to amaze me (and don't take this as a compliment <g>)


With kind regards

Volker Bandke

      
      
      
      
                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_

        (Another Wisdom from my fortune cookie jar)
```

PC Quiz: What percentage of the population of London is
'Afro-American?' Answer below.

Meanwhile, not long ago, the USS Constitution,"Old Ironsides,' was
part of the Millennium celebration. During the celebration, a ranking
Naval officer said: "This is a fine tribute to the men and women who
served aboard the Constitution." No women have ever served on the USS
Constitution.

Answer to above: Virtually zero. Any dark-skinned inhabitant of London
would be an "Anglo-African" (or somesuch).

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-22T03:11:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B09D89E.16B4B9BD@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<20010315034757.19316.00001039@ng-bd1.aol.com> <3ab0cd9d_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <rlp7gtscln96gu9l8tfq770m2ik80lean8@4ax.com> <3b05188d_2@news3> <jj9bgt0186kmo3v56l5uimro7og988pf7g@4ax.com> <9e4c6v$bc2$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <5o2dgtsg1clfp9l4e2g4155adjdtk9pmq9@4ax.com> <t7xN6.92295$ho6.5842388@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <tYNN6.1947$YZ.453264@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <tu4ggtcpcdqn6stm9f6e68223498mdeeqi@4ax.com> <uGXN6.1297$Jm1.504720@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <5loigtgpt0bib3m2s8jd4h0ksif0enask2@4ax.com>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> >Sometimes this politeness goes a bit too far and becomes political
> >correctness. This is why we no longer refer to autumn as the time when
> >there's a little nip in the air;
> Sorry, this I do _not_ understand

I believe that both "nip" and "jap" are derogatory terms for persons
of Japanese descent.

> 
> >Hell, there's even a movement afoot to ban all references to doo-wop music.
> Don't even know what doo-wop music might be (well, I did look it up right now, but still
> don't know: a) what it is, and b) why the use of the term should be banned

a) I am no expert, but I thought doo-wop music referred to singing
that included nonsense syllables (specifically, "doo-wop"), sort of
like scat singing in jazz.  The only songs I know that actually used
"doo-wop" were 1950's rock and roll songs.

b) Of course you realize that "wop" as well as "guinea" is a
derogatory term for persons of Italian descent.

I suppose since I have some French ancestors that I should be deeply
offended by the word "frog", however it might be used.

There was a feminist professor at a local university who refused to
hold seminars.  She called them "ovulars".

> 
> Anyways - You Americans don't fail to amaze me (and don't take this as a compliment <g>)

Sometimes we amaze and disgust ourselves!

> 
> With kind regards
> 
> Volker Bandke

Likewise!
```

- **References:** `<3ab0cd9d_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <rlp7gtscln96gu9l8tfq770m2ik80lean8@4ax.com> <3b05188d_2@news3> <jj9bgt0186kmo3v56l5uimro7og988pf7g@4ax.com> <9e4c6v$bc2$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <5o2dgtsg1clfp9l4e2g4155adjdtk9pmq9@4ax.com> <t7xN6.92295$ho6.5842388@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <tYNN6.1947$YZ.453264@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <tu4ggtcpcdqn6stm9f6e68223498mdeeqi@4ax.com> <uGXN6.1297$Jm1.504720@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <5loigtgpt0bib3m2s8jd4h0ksif0enask2@4ax.com>`

On Mon, 21 May 2001 21:13:20 +0200, Volker Bandke
<vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> enlightened us:

>
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The politically correct term is African-American.  Of course this
makes no sense if the Black person happens to be from Jamaica or The
Bahamas or Haiti or somewhere else where black people are predominant.
Negro used to the correct term for a black person but it has taken on
derisive qualities over the years.  It is still correct to use
although some people take offense to it.

>>
>>I do not know European culture much at all; I guess the closest parallel I
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well being of "Kraut" decent, it's never bothered me either.

>>
>>Sometimes this politeness goes a bit too far and becomes political
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Nip is a derogatory term for a person of Japanese person (from
Nippon).

>>We don't jerry-rig solutions when we don't have the
>>correct parts for the job;
>Sorry, but isn' tthe correct term jury-rigged?
>

I believe Volker is correct -- it is jury rigged.

>>if one is considered vulnerable, we can't say he
>>has a chink in the armor.
>And why not? Dent is more familiar, but why not chink (as opposed to Chink)
>

Chink is derogatory term for a Chinese person.
 
>>
>>Hell, there's even a movement afoot to ban all references to doo-wop music.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Wop is a deragatory term for an Italian person. 

 Doo-Wop music came about in the 50's.  It was a precurser to
Rock-and-Roll and featured 3 or 4 part harmonies and songs which could
be sung A Cappella.  It was recorded with the least amount of
instrumentation as possible..

>Anyways - You Americans don't fail to amaze me (and don't take this as a compliment <g>)
>
>

Michael,

this is an absolute gem. ROFL!

Sadly, what you say is true, but spelling it out gives us all a chance to
see how pathetic it is becoming.

I just couldn't believe that ANYONE could confuse "nigger" and "niggardly".
Like other readers (not in the US) I was bewildered by what was going on. As
the thread unfurled and we finally got to see what this was about, well, I
was astounded.

So, a government official loses his job because his Boss doesn't understand
English?

What hope for the World's greatest democracy?

I have to believe that your little samples are not real and stem from your
own wit, right? Please tell me this was you being funny, not America being
serious...

Pete.


"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:uGXN6.1297$Jm1.504720@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net...
> You will note both "offensive" and "insensitive" were placed in quotes.
This
> was to emphasize that there is, in fact, nothing offensive or insensitive
> about the word, "niggardly." This is why both the resignation its
acceptance
> were the actions of imbeciles. (Deliberate suppression of quotes).
>
> I think you are in Germany, and might not be terribly familiar with
American
> colloquial vulgarity.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> could make would be to refer to a German in public by saying something
like,
> "Oh, ignore Volker, he's just a kraut."  "Kraut" is an American term for
> Germans coined during WWI and re-used during WWII for propaganda purposes
to
> paint the enemy as something less than human.
>
> Today neither "nigger" nor "kraut" (nor about fifteen other ethnic
> appellations with which I shall not bore you) is considered suitable for
use
> in polite society.
>
> Sometimes this politeness goes a bit too far and becomes political
> correctness. This is why we no longer refer to autumn as the time when
> there's a little nip in the air; why we no longer clean our homes until
they
> are spic-and-span. We don't jerry-rig solutions when we don't have the
> correct parts for the job; if one is considered vulnerable, we can't say
he
> has a chink in the armor.
>
> Hell, there's even a movement afoot to ban all references to doo-wop
music.
>
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >accepted the deputy's resignation based on the use of "offensive" (or
was
> it
> > >merely "insensitive?" I forget.)  language.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need answer to Cobol trivia

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-05-23T01:30:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010522213023.18129.00000932@ng-cj1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0b0b2f_6@Usenet.com>`

```
>From: "Peter E. C. Dashwood" dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz 

>So, a government official loses his job because his Boss 
>doesn't understand English?
>
>What hope for the World's greatest democracy?

What does this thread have to do with Switzerland? 

>I have to believe that your little samples are not real and stem 
>from your own wit, right? Please tell me this was you being funny, 
>not America being serious...
>

Actually there are at least three cases of people being punished
for using an indelicate synonym for "miserly."  In one case, the
boss who fired the offending employee retracted the sacking
and apologized publicly.  I think the other two held up.  

There are also several dozen school libraries that have removed 
Huckleberry Finn.  My children's school library also attempted
the same thing.  Unfortunately, they got confused and
mistakenly removed Huckleberry Hound.  



Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

No longer the world's greatest democracy. It was until 1976. Before
then, Switzerland hadn't been in a war in, oh, 1000 years, had zero
unemployment, 100% literacy, and everyone could yodel.

In 1976 women got the vote.

Since then, yodeling has taken a severe turn for the worse.

- **Date:** 2001-05-23T17:08:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0BEEE2.1BE6F0D9@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20010315034757.19316.00001039@ng-bd1.aol.com> <3ab0cd9d_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <rlp7gtscln96gu9l8tfq770m2ik80lean8@4ax.com> <3b05188d_2@news3> <jj9bgt0186kmo3v56l5uimro7og988pf7g@4ax.com> <9e4c6v$bc2$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <5o2dgtsg1clfp9l4e2g4155adjdtk9pmq9@4ax.com> <t7xN6.92295$ho6.5842388@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <tYNN6.1947$YZ.453264@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <tu4ggtcpcdqn6stm9f6e68223498mdeeqi@4ax.com> <uGXN6.1297$Jm1.504720@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3b0b0b2f_6@Usenet.com> <i6mmgt8m6tdauofrs60eq4er71hg5bu0br@4ax.com> <3B0BB520.E7C998D7@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> Volker Bandke wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> In area, Canada is the second largest country in the world behind Russia.

Shhhh! With 0.5% of world population we've got 25% of fresh water. Biggest exporter of
oil(petroleum) and natural gas to the U.S. - though the statistics are 'minimal' for your
energy supply - 15% and 14% respectively. Although more expensive to get out of the ground,
(SAGD - Steam Assisted Gas Drilling), the Athabasca Tarsands (N. Alberta), has more reserves
than Saudi Arabia. Then there's the estimated 800 years supply of low sulphur coal in Alberta
and Saskatchewan.

Above of course doesn't illustrate what is also available in Eastern Canada and on the East
Coast, (off-shore drilling).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **References:** `<20010315034757.19316.00001039@ng-bd1.aol.com> <3ab0cd9d_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <rlp7gtscln96gu9l8tfq770m2ik80lean8@4ax.com> <3b05188d_2@news3> <jj9bgt0186kmo3v56l5uimro7og988pf7g@4ax.com> <9e4c6v$bc2$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <5o2dgtsg1clfp9l4e2g4155adjdtk9pmq9@4ax.com> <t7xN6.92295$ho6.5842388@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <tYNN6.1947$YZ.453264@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <tu4ggtcpcdqn6stm9f6e68223498mdeeqi@4ax.com> <uGXN6.1297$Jm1.504720@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3b0b0b2f_6@Usenet.com> <i6mmgt8m6tdauofrs60eq4er71hg5bu0br@4ax.com> <3B0BB520.E7C998D7@brazee.net> <3B0DC6AC.4F237739@optonline.net>`

```


Liam Devlin wrote:

> > In area, Canada is the second largest country in the world behind Russia.
>
> Is Canada bigger than Australia?
>
> LiamD

Bejejuz - trust somebody with an Irish name to ask this one. I didn't know, and don't really care
- but as we are into trivia and I have my Rand McNally CD handy - in sq. kms (millions) :-

Russia        17.0
Canada        9.9
US               9.8
China           9.5
Brazil           8.5
Australia      7.6
India            3.2 (no wonder they are crowded !)
Argentina     2.7
Kazakhstan   2.7
Sudan           2.5

Few years ago, but last time I checked - population of Canada was less than that of California !
The other side of the coin - population of Calgary (0.8 mill) is greater than that of Montana,
just to the south of us <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need answer to Cobol trivia

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@ont.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T10:27:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B07FE69.DCF9E2A0@ont.com>`
- **References:** `<20010315034757.19316.00001039@ng-bd1.aol.com> <3ab0cd9d_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <rlp7gtscln96gu9l8tfq770m2ik80lean8@4ax.com> <3b05188d_2@news3>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> Despite apocryphal stories that the name is derived from Engineers referring
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

I lost my 1963 copy during one of my moves, but my 1974 copy of Radio
Shack's Dictionary of Electronics has: "motherboard-A relatively large
piece of insulating material on which components, modules, or other
electronic subassemblies are mounted and interconnections made by
welding, soldering, or other means, using point-to-point or matrix wire,
or circuitry fabricated integrally with the board."

The electronics use probably pre-dates the use in the computer field.

I know I heard the name used while I was in the Marines, 1963-1969, but
we were all avionics technicians, so we may have just used it during
computer/electronics classes-for me 1964 and 1967. We did use it while
working on the Univac 1206 and 1230, 1968 and 1969, which were used in
the tactical trailers of an A6 Mobile Flight/Weapons Systems Trainer.
(YE OLDE Paper-tape and Flexwriter days...)

I did graduate to punched cards in 1970 for Assembler/Exec-8, and
COBOL/IBM BAL/RPG in 1970, also with punched cards. I only mention that
because we also expanded the use of the "mother" prefix to create
COBOL-inspired-compound-words like " Mother-xxxxxxx-card-reader".

Gary
```

<a id="t-c2df91cac6"></a>
## Thread: menu problem — 4 msgs

### menu problem

- **From:** rdenkers@home.nl (rick denkers)
- **Date:** 2001-05-17T21:13:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b043cdd.11273819@news>`

```

Hi,
Im having a slight problem
Im trying to make a menu work. 
Im also expirimenting with the CALL statement.
The  only problem is that the input doesnt seem to work correctly.
after choosing 3 out of 4 inputs i cannot return to a previous value.
Only the fourth option is available.
Could anyone shine a light on this one?

Thanks !

rick

main programme :

	IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
	PROGRAM-ID ADRES.
	AUTHOR RICK DENKERS.
	ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
	CONFIGURATION SECTION.
	SPECIAL-NAMES.
		CRT STATUS IS KEYBOARD-STATUS.
	DATA DIVISION.                          
  FILE SECTION.
	WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
	01 DUMMY-FIELD PIC X VALUE SPACES.
	01 KEYBOARD-STATUS.
		03 ACCEPT-STATUS PIC 9.
		03 SYSTEM-USE    PIC X.
		03 DUMMY-FILLER   PIC X.
	01 KEY-PRESSED  PIC X.
	
	01 DONE-FLAG       PIC X VALUE SPACES.
			88 ALL-DONE VALUE "Y".

	SCREEN SECTION.

	01 MAIN-SCREEN BLANK SCREEN
		BACKGROUND-COLOR IS 1
		FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 7.
  	03 LINE 6 COLUMN 16 VALUE "                 Rick zijn Adres
boekje ".
          03 LINE 7 COLUMN 16 VALUE
"____________________________________________________" .
		03 LINE 10 COLUMN 16 VALUE "1 Naam invoeren.".
          03 LINE 11 COLUMN 16 VALUE "2 Naam lezen. ".
		03 LINE 12 COLUMN 16 VALUE "3 Naam wijzigen.". 
          03 LINE 13 COLUMN 16 VALUE "4 Stoppen. ".
		03 LINE 17 COLUMN 16 PIC X USING KEY-PRESSED.	
          03 LINE 19 COLUMN 16 VALUE
"____________________________________________________" .
*

	PROCEDURE DIVISION.
* DIT IS HET HOOFDPROGRAMMA
* IF F1 PRESSED PERFORM SCHRIJF 
* IF F2 PRESSED PERFORM LEES.
* IF F9 PRESSED PERFORM STOP.
	ADRES-START.
		PERFORM UNTIL ALL-DONE
		MOVE SPACES TO KEY-PRESSED
		DISPLAY MAIN-SCREEN
		ACCEPT MAIN-SCREEN
          IF KEY-PRESSED = "1"
		THEN CALL "SCHRIJF"
		 ELSE
		IF KEY-PRESSED = "2"
		THEN CALL "LEES"
		ELSE
		IF KEY-PRESSED = "3"
		THEN CALL "HERSCHRIJF"
		ELSE
		IF KEY-PRESSED = "4"
		THEN SET ALL-DONE TO TRUE
		ELSE
		MOVE SPACES TO KEY-PRESSED
          END-IF
		END-IF
		END-IF
	        END-IF
		END-PERFORM
		STOP RUN.

and the sub-programs:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
	PROGRAM-ID HERSCHRIJF.
	AUTHOR RICK DENKERS.
	ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
	CONFIGURATION SECTION.
	SPECIAL-NAMES.
		CRT STATUS IS KEYBOARD-STATUS.
	INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
	FILE-CONTROL.
		SELECT OPTIONAL NAME-FILE ASSIGN TO "ADRES.TXT"
		ORGANIZATION IS  SEQUENTIAL
		FILE STATUS IS NAME-FILE-STATUS.
  DATA DIVISION.
	FILE SECTION.
	FD NAME-FILE.
  01 NAME-RECORD.
 		03 ACHTERNAAM    PIC X(30).
		03 VOORNAAM      PIC X(8).
		03 ADRES	 PIC X(30).
		03 POSTCODE	 PIC X(8).
		03 WOONPLAATS    PIC X(30).
		03 TELEFOON	 PIC X(12).
		03 MOBIEL        PIC X(11).
		03 EMAIL	 PIC X(20).
		03 TEKST	 PIC X(30).
	WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
	01 WERKNAAM.
		03 WERKACHTERNAAM   PIC X(30).
		03 WERKVOORNAAM     PIC X(8).
		03 WERKADRES	    PIC X(30).
		03 WERKPOSTCODE	    PIC X(8).
		03 WERKWOONPLAATS   PIC X(30).
		03 WERKTELEFOON	    PIC X(12).
		03 WERKMOBIEL       PIC X(11).
		03 WERKEMAIL	    PIC X(20).
		03 WERKTEKST	    PIC X(30).
	01 KEYBOARD-STATUS.
		03 ACCEPT-STATUS PIC 9.
		03 FUNCTION-KEY PIC X.
			88 F9-PRESSED VALUE X"09".
			88 F4-PRESSED VALUE X"04".
		03 SYSTEM-USE PIC X.
	01 FILE-ERROR-FLAG PIC X VALUE SPACE.
			88 FILE-ERROR VALUE "Y".
	01 NAME-FILE-STATUS PIC XX VALUE SPACES.
			88 NAME-FILE-SUCESS VALUE "00" "05".
			88 END-OF-FILE VALUE "10".
	01 ERROR-MESSAGE PIC X(50) VALUE SPACES.
	SCREEN SECTION.
  01 NAME-ENTRY BLANK SCREEN
		BACKGROUND-COLOR IS 1
		FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 7.
		03 LINE 1 COLUMN 28 VALUE "Rick zijn Adresboekje".
          03 LINE 5 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Achternaam:".
		03 LINE 5 COLUMN 15  PIC X(30) USING ACHTERNAAM.
		03 LINE 7 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Voornaam:".
		03 LINE 7 COLUMN 15 PIC X(8) USING VOORNAAM.
		03 LINE 9 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Adres:".
		03 LINE 9 COLUMN 15 PIC X(30) USING ADRES.
		03 LINE 11 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Postcode:".
		03 LINE 11  COLUMN 15 PIC X(8) USING POSTCODE.
		03 LINE 11 COLUMN 30 VALUE "Plaats:".
		03 LINE 11 COLUMN 37 PIC X(30) USING WOONPLAATS.
		03 LINE 13 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Telefoon:".
		03 LINE 13 COLUMN 15 PIC X(12) USING TELEFOON.
          03 LINE 13 COLUMN 30 VALUE "Mobiel:".
		03 LINE 13 COLUMN 37 PIC X(11) USING MOBIEL.
		03 LINE 15 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Email:".
		03 LINE 15 COLUMN 15 PIC X(20) USING EMAIL.
		03 LINE 17 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Vrije tekst:".
		03 LINE 17 COLUMN 15 PIC X(30) USING TEKST.
         	03 LINE 20 COLUMN 1 PIC X(50) FROM ERROR-MESSAGE.
		03 LINE 21 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Druk op F4 om te
updaten.".
		03 LINE 22 COLUMN 01 VALUE " Druk op F9 terug te keren
naar het hoofdmenu".
  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
	LEES-START.
		PERFORM OPEN-FILE
		IF NOT FILE-ERROR
		PERFORM PROCES-INPUT UNTIL F9-PRESSED OR FILE-ERROR OR
END-OF-FILE
		END-IF.
		PERFORM CLOSE-FILE
		GOBACK. 
	OPEN-FILE.
		OPEN I-O NAME-FILE
	        IF NOT NAME-FILE-SUCESS
		MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		STRING "OPEN ERROR" NAME-FILE-STATUS
		DELIMITED BY SIZE
		INTO ERROR-MESSAGE
		PERFORM DISPLAY-AND-ACCEPT-ERROR
		END-IF.
   PROCES-INPUT. 
	        MOVE SPACES TO ACHTERNAAM
		MOVE SPACES TO VOORNAAM
		MOVE SPACES TO ADRES
		MOVE SPACES TO  POSTCODE
		MOVE SPACES TO  WOONPLAATS
		MOVE SPACES TO  TELEFOON
		MOVE SPACES TO  MOBIEL
		MOVE SPACES TO  EMAIL
		MOVE SPACES TO  TEKST
		PERFORM READ-RECORD
		IF NOT FILE-ERROR
		DISPLAY NAME-ENTRY
		ACCEPT NAME-ENTRY
		MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		IF F4-PRESSED AND NOT END-OF-FILE
		PERFORM REWRITE-RECORD
		END-IF
		END-IF.
	REWRITE-RECORD.
		REWRITE NAME-RECORD FROM WERKNAAM
		IF NAME-FILE-SUCESS
		MOVE " Oude record geupdated" TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		ELSE 
		MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		STRING " HERSCHRIJF FOUT " NAME-FILE-STATUS
		DELIMITED BY SIZE
		INTO ERROR-MESSAGE
		END-STRING
		END-IF.
	READ-RECORD.
		READ NAME-FILE INTO WERKNAAM
          AT END MOVE "END OF FILE " TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		END-READ
		IF NAME-FILE-SUCESS OR END-OF-FILE
		CONTINUE
		ELSE
		MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		STRING " READ EROR " NAME-FILE-STATUS
		DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO ERROR-MESSAGE
		END-STRING
		PERFORM DISPLAY-AND-ACCEPT-ERROR
		END-IF.
 	DISPLAY-AND-ACCEPT-ERROR.
		SET FILE-ERROR TO TRUE
		DISPLAY NAME-ENTRY
		ACCEPT NAME-ENTRY.
	CLOSE-FILE.
		CLOSE NAME-FILE.
	
  IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
	PROGRAM-ID LEES.
	AUTHOR RICK DENKERS.
	ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
	CONFIGURATION SECTION.
	SPECIAL-NAMES.
		CRT STATUS IS KEYBOARD-STATUS.
	INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
	FILE-CONTROL.
		SELECT OPTIONAL NAME-FILE ASSIGN TO "ADRES.TXT"
		ORGANIZATION IS  SEQUENTIAL
		FILE STATUS IS NAME-FILE-STATUS.
  DATA DIVISION.
	FILE SECTION.
	FD NAME-FILE.
  01 NAME-RECORD.
 		03 ACHTERNAAM    PIC X(30).
		03 VOORNAAM      PIC X(8).
		03 ADRES	 PIC X(30).
		03 POSTCODE	 PIC X(8).
		03 WOONPLAATS    PIC X(30).
		03 TELEFOON	 PIC X(12).
		03 MOBIEL        PIC X(11).
		03 EMAIL	 PIC X(20).
		03 TEKST	 PIC X(30).
	WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
	01 WERKNAAM.
		03 WERKACHTERNAAM   PIC X(30).
		03 WERKVOORNAAM     PIC X(8).
		03 WERKADRES	    PIC X(30).
		03 WERKPOSTCODE	    PIC X(8).
		03 WERKWOONPLAATS   PIC X(30).
		03 WERKTELEFOON	    PIC X(12).
		03 WERKMOBIEL       PIC X(11).
		03 WERKEMAIL	    PIC X(20).
		03 WERKTEKST	    PIC X(30).
	01 KEYBOARD-STATUS.
		03 ACCEPT-STATUS PIC 9.
		03 FUNCTION-KEY PIC X.
			88 F9-PRESSED VALUE X"09".
		03 SYSTEM-USE PIC X.
	01 FILE-ERROR-FLAG PIC X VALUE SPACE.
			88 FILE-ERROR VALUE "Y".
	01 NAME-FILE-STATUS PIC XX VALUE SPACES.
			88 NAME-FILE-SUCESS VALUE "00" "05".
			88 END-OF-FILE VALUE "10".
	01 ERROR-MESSAGE PIC X(50) VALUE SPACES.
	SCREEN SECTION.
  01 NAME-ENTRY BLANK SCREEN
		BACKGROUND-COLOR IS 1
		FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 7.
		03 LINE 1 COLUMN 28 VALUE "Rick zijn Adresboekje".
          03 LINE 5 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Achternaam:".
		03 LINE 5 COLUMN 15  PIC X(30) USING ACHTERNAAM.
		03 LINE 7 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Voornaam:".
		03 LINE 7 COLUMN 15 PIC X(8) USING VOORNAAM.
		03 LINE 9 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Adres:".
		03 LINE 9 COLUMN 15 PIC X(30) USING ADRES.
		03 LINE 11 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Postcode:".
		03 LINE 11  COLUMN 15 PIC X(8) USING POSTCODE.
		03 LINE 11 COLUMN 30 VALUE "Plaats:".
		03 LINE 11 COLUMN 37 PIC X(30) USING WOONPLAATS.
		03 LINE 13 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Telefoon:".
		03 LINE 13 COLUMN 15 PIC X(12) USING TELEFOON.
          03 LINE 13 COLUMN 30 VALUE "Mobiel:".
		03 LINE 13 COLUMN 37 PIC X(11) USING MOBIEL.
		03 LINE 15 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Email:".
		03 LINE 15 COLUMN 15 PIC X(20) USING EMAIL.
		03 LINE 17 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Vrije tekst:".
		03 LINE 17 COLUMN 15 PIC X(30) USING TEKST.
         	03 LINE 20 COLUMN 1 PIC X(50) FROM ERROR-MESSAGE.
		03 LINE 22 COLUMN 01 VALUE " Druk op F9 terug te keren
naar het hoofdmenu".
  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
	LEES-START.
		PERFORM OPEN-FILE
		IF NOT FILE-ERROR
		PERFORM PROCES-INPUT UNTIL F9-PRESSED OR FILE-ERROR OR
END-OF-FILE
		END-IF.
		PERFORM CLOSE-FILE
		GOBACK. 
	OPEN-FILE.
		OPEN INPUT NAME-FILE
	        IF NOT NAME-FILE-SUCESS
		MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		STRING "OPEN ERROR" NAME-FILE-STATUS
		DELIMITED BY SIZE
		INTO ERROR-MESSAGE
		PERFORM DISPLAY-AND-ACCEPT-ERROR
		END-IF.
   PROCES-INPUT. 
	        MOVE SPACES TO ACHTERNAAM
		MOVE SPACES TO VOORNAAM
		MOVE SPACES TO ADRES
		MOVE SPACES TO  POSTCODE
		MOVE SPACES TO  WOONPLAATS
		MOVE SPACES TO  TELEFOON
		MOVE SPACES TO  MOBIEL
		MOVE SPACES TO  EMAIL
		MOVE SPACES TO  TEKST
		PERFORM READ-RECORD
		IF NOT FILE-ERROR
		DISPLAY NAME-ENTRY
		ACCEPT NAME-ENTRY
		END-IF
		MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-MESSAGE.
	READ-RECORD.
		READ NAME-FILE INTO WERKNAAM
          AT END MOVE "END OF FILE " TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		END-READ
		IF NAME-FILE-SUCESS OR END-OF-FILE
		CONTINUE
		ELSE
		MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		STRING " READ EROR " NAME-FILE-STATUS
		DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO ERROR-MESSAGE
		END-STRING
		PERFORM DISPLAY-AND-ACCEPT-ERROR
		END-IF.
 	DISPLAY-AND-ACCEPT-ERROR.
		SET FILE-ERROR TO TRUE
		DISPLAY NAME-ENTRY
		ACCEPT NAME-ENTRY.
	CLOSE-FILE.
		CLOSE NAME-FILE.
	
	IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
	PROGRAM-ID SCHRIJF.
	AUTHOR RICK DENKERS.
	ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
	CONFIGURATION SECTION.
	SPECIAL-NAMES.
		CRT STATUS IS KEYBOARD-STATUS.
  INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
	FILE-CONTROL.
		SELECT NAME-FILE ASSIGN TO "ADRES.TXT"
		ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
		FILE STATUS IS NAME-FILE-STATUS .
	DATA DIVISION.                          
  FILE SECTION.
  FD NAME-FILE .
  01 NAME-RECORD.
		03 ACHTERNAAM    PIC X(30).
		03 VOORNAAM      PIC X(8).
		03 ADRES	 PIC X(30).
		03 POSTCODE	 PIC X(8).
		03 WOONPLAATS    PIC X(30).
		03 TELEFOON	 PIC X(12).
		03 MOBIEL        PIC X(11).
		03 EMAIL	 PIC X(20).
		03 TEKST	 PIC X(30).
		
	WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
	01 KEYBOARD-STATUS.
		03 ACCEPT-STATUS PIC 9.
		03 FUNCTION-KEY  PIC X.
			88 F9-PRESSED VALUE X"09".
		03 SYSTEM-USE    PIC X.
	01 FILE-ERROR-FLAG       PIC X VALUE SPACE.
			88 FILE-ERROR VALUE "Y".
	01 NAME-FILE-STATUS      PIC XX VALUE SPACES.
			88 NAME-FILE-SUCESS VALUE "00".
	01 ERROR-MESSAGE         PIC X(50) VALUE SPACES.
	01 KIES PIC X VALUE SPACE.
	SCREEN SECTION.
* HIER STAAT NAME-ENTRY. HiER KAN OOK NAME-DISPLAY BIJ. DE REWRITE
OOK.
	01 MAIN-SCREEN BLANK SCREEN
		BACKGROUND-COLOR IS 1
		FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 7.
		.
  01 NAME-ENTRY BLANK SCREEN
		BACKGROUND-COLOR IS 1
		FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 7.
		03 LINE 1 COLUMN 28 VALUE "Rick zijn Adresboekje".
          03 LINE 5 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Achternaam:".
		03 LINE 5 COLUMN 15  PIC X(30) USING ACHTERNAAM.
		03 LINE 7 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Voornaam:".
		03 LINE 7 COLUMN 15 PIC X(8) USING VOORNAAM.
		03 LINE 9 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Adres:".
		03 LINE 9 COLUMN 15 PIC X(30) USING ADRES.
		03 LINE 11 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Postcode:".
		03 LINE 11  COLUMN 15 PIC X(8) USING POSTCODE.
		03 LINE 11 COLUMN 30 VALUE "Plaats:".
		03 LINE 11 COLUMN 37 PIC X(30) USING WOONPLAATS.
		03 LINE 13 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Telefoon:".
		03 LINE 13 COLUMN 15 PIC X(12) USING TELEFOON.
          03 LINE 13 COLUMN 30 VALUE "Mobiel:".
		03 LINE 13 COLUMN 37 PIC X(11) USING MOBIEL.
		03 LINE 15 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Email:".
		03 LINE 15 COLUMN 15 PIC X(20) USING EMAIL.
		03 LINE 17 COLUMN 1 VALUE " Vrije tekst:".
		03 LINE 17 COLUMN 15 PIC X(30) USING TEKST.
 
		03 LINE 20 COLUMN 1 PIC X(50) FROM ERROR-MESSAGE.
		03 LINE 22 COLUMN 01 VALUE " Druk op F9 terug te keren
naar het hoofdmenu".
	PROCEDURE DIVISION.
* DIT IS HET HOOFDPROGRAMMA
	MAIN.
    	PERFORM OPEN-FILE
		IF NOT FILE-ERROR
		PERFORM PROCES-INPUT UNTIL F9-PRESSED OR FILE-ERROR
		PERFORM CLOSE-FILE
		END-IF.
		GOBACK.
* HIER KOMEN DE MODULES	
	OPEN-FILE.
		OPEN OUTPUT NAME-FILE
	        IF NOT NAME-FILE-SUCESS
		MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		STRING "OPEN ERROR" NAME-FILE-STATUS
		DELIMITED BY SIZE
		INTO ERROR-MESSAGE
		PERFORM DISPLAY-AND-ACCEPT-ERROR
		END-IF.
	PROCES-INPUT. 
	        MOVE SPACES TO ACHTERNAAM
		MOVE SPACES TO VOORNAAM
		MOVE SPACES TO ADRES
		MOVE SPACES TO POSTCODE
		MOVE SPACES TO WOONPLAATS
		MOVE SPACES TO TELEFOON
		MOVE SPACES TO MOBIEL
		MOVE SPACES TO EMAIL
		MOVE SPACES TO TEKST
		DISPLAY NAME-ENTRY
		ACCEPT NAME-ENTRY
		MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		IF NOT F9-PRESSED
		PERFORM WRITE-RECORD
		END-IF.
	WRITE-RECORD.
		WRITE NAME-RECORD
		IF NAME-FILE-SUCESS
		MOVE "RECORD WRITTEN" TO ERROR-MESSAGE
		ELSE
		STRING " WRITE ERROR" NAME-FILE-STATUS
	        DELIMITED BY SIZE 
		INTO ERROR-MESSAGE
		END-IF.
	DISPLAY-AND-ACCEPT-ERROR.
		SET FILE-ERROR TO TRUE
		DISPLAY NAME-ENTRY
		ACCEPT NAME-ENTRY.
	CLOSE-FILE.
		CLOSE NAME-FILE.
```

#### ↳ Re: menu problem

- **From:** goble@gtech (David. E. Goble)
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T08:00:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b0678d5.2565627@news.adelaide.on.net>`
- **References:** `<3b043cdd.11273819@news>`

```
On Thu, 17 May 2001 21:13:43 GMT, rdenkers@home.nl (rick denkers)
wrote:
>
>Im having a slight problem
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hi rick;

Your posts format was all over the place...

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
* DIT IS HET HOOFDPROGRAMMA
* IF F1 PRESSED PERFORM SCHRIJF 
* IF F2 PRESSED PERFORM LEES.
* IF F9 PRESSED PERFORM STOP.
 ADRES-START.
*============
	PERFORM UNTIL ALL-DONE
		MOVE SPACES TO KEY-PRESSED
		DISPLAY MAIN-SCREEN
		ACCEPT MAIN-SCREEN
		IF KEY-PRESSED = "1"
		THEN CALL "SCHRIJF"
		ELSE
		    IF KEY-PRESSED = "2"
		    THEN CALL "LEES"
		    ELSE
		        IF KEY-PRESSED = "3"
		        THEN CALL "HERSCHRIJF"
		        ELSE
		            IF KEY-PRESSED = "4"
		            THEN SET ALL-DONE TO TRUE
		            ELSE
		                MOVE SPACES TO KEY-PRESSED
                            END-IF
		        END-IF
		    END-IF
	        END-IF
	END-PERFORM.
STOP RUN.

I think you will see it is your nested if statements that is giving
you grief. Try this...

 ADRES-START.
*============
	PERFORM UNTIL ALL-DONE
		MOVE SPACES TO KEY-PRESSED
		DISPLAY MAIN-SCREEN
		ACCEPT MAIN-SCREEN
	        EVALUATE KEY-PRESSED
			WHEN "1"
				CALL "SCHRIJF"
			WHEN "2"
				CALL "LEES"
			WHEN "3"
				CALL "HERSCHRIJF"
			WHEN "4"
				SET ALL-DONE TO TRUE
		END-EVALUATE	
		MOVE SPACES TO KEY-PRESSED	
	END-PERFORM.
STOP RUN.

Anyway I hope this helps :>
```

Hi david,

Thanks for the advise. In an earlier stage i used the evaluate- end
evaluate. It didnt solve the problem.
But youre advise inspired me to look again at the program.
As allways the answer of a problem lays in the problem itself !.
Silly me !. Forgot that my input screen uses a record that is
specified in the working storage. 
It brought me closer to solve it. It still doenst work fine.
Ive also changed  open -file piece.
Thane Hubbells  (great) book also pointed out that i cannot write to a
file in a "output" . Changed it to extend. It works better.
( yes i am a true hubbell believer .. :)  )

But i keep in mind that rome wasnt build in a day either !

regards,

rick

jim

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL PROGRAMS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "nnrp-corp.news.cais.net" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T09:54:18-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ej3qb$155d$1@allbad.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<3B0ABEE9.735E5D0F@boeing.com> <20010522194328.26073.00000677@ng-bk1.aol.com>`

```
If that is what he is looking for they Stefano, you may want to take a look
at evolveIT which is sold by The Progeni Corporation www.progeni.com.

This product consists of a discovery workbench and an analysis workbench.
The discovery workbench reads through all of the code in an entire system
(or just one program if you want) including copybooks, JCL, etc.  The
discovery workbench then lets you view and manipulate a wide range of cross
linked reports, diagrams, charts, etc.  It's a fantastic tool for analysis
of legacy code, understanding code for maintenance or web enablement tasks
or just traning new programmers.

I've only played with it a little but I'm amazed at what this thing can do,
especially in its ability to graphically represent entire systems in a way
that let you instantly see relationship and then click down to specific
lines of code.

"Merhottin" <merhottin@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010522194328.26073.00000677@ng-bk1.aol.com...
> I think what he is looking for is a flowchart, then the coding that goes
with
> the flowchart, to see it from begining to end.  Right ?
```

## Thread: A Programmer's Day, Week, Year — 16 msgs

### A Programmer's Day, Week, Year

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-05-19T05:37:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010519013733.23119.00000413@ng-fh1.aol.com>`

```
<gripe mode on>
It has been one of those weeks.  I've been working on one of our programs from
the underworld (the one with that ugly IF statement) putting in what should
have been a fairly easy change.  Of course the BA who made up the specs didn't
bother to note that the same change had to go into 2 other programs, but that's
beside the point.  I'm not real familar with the whole system so it wasn't
obvious at the time I got the specs to ask the other teams about their
programs.  It has been duly noted in my 'lessons learned' notebook that when I
get specs from this particular BA to immediately ask around about the change -
especially when he says it doesn't affect anything else.

Anyway, I put it into production and about an hour later the one of the
warehouses calls and says it isn't working and a truck is about to leave and
needs their paperwork (truckers are always about to leave - they never seem to
stick around for a cup of coffee and chit chat).  Pull out the program and put
in the prior version. 

Run the new one thru Xpediter again - don't see anything particularly wrong so
let's compare to the original.  Hot dang - the original does exactly the same
'mistake'! Which means the dang thing never worked right in the first place -
but since I'm the last one to touch it I have to fix it!  Putter around in the
program, figure out what has to change and put it back into production.

It's some gawd awful time of the morning and the phone rings.  Actually I'm
already up but my first cup of coffee is just begining to circulate but I'm not
out of the navel contemplation phase of the morning.  It's my boss - the
program is doing something weird again - am I coming in today?  Duh - I guess
so - did I give you any notice that I'm taking time off?  Maybe I will since
you've asked.  Do you have any idea what's the problem.  Uh duh no - but then
right now I have the brain of an ameoba and the reflexes of a snail so don't
expect any miracles, call back after my 2nd cup and I'll consider the
situation.

I'm at work - in once piece - no thanks to the Greyhound on the x-way that
wanted to be in the same lane I was in at the same time and place that I was
already occupying.  He probably flunked physics which is why he's driving a
bus. 

I grab another cup of coffee (caffein junky) and put the program back into test
and Xpedite again.  Interesting - there is a field that isn't justify correctly
so it doesn't read this other file correctly so the incorrect information is
put on the file when it is updated.  Pull in the original - check it out - well
hot dang again!  It doesn't read the file correctly either - but it did some
crazy tweaking of the incorrect field so that when the update was done it had
the right record but the update was wrong because the original fields weren't
there due to the not found the first time thru. The change corrected the
original mistake and let the other come glaring out as the change removed the
funky tweaking. Wasn't found in original testing because I couldn't get to all
the permutations of some of the IF statements.  Ok - put that change into
production - or try to.  Sent the paperwork down to the helpless desk at 4:30. 
All paperwork has to be in by 5pm. We're supposed to have 15 minute turn around
for on-line programs.  Batch goes in whenever during the day unless you have a
job down and then it's supposed to be now (assuming you can get thru on the
phone to tell them it has to go now).  I wait, and wait, and wait.  No response
that the program has been moved.  It's now 5:10 and no production move.  My
boss calls down and everyone has gone home except for one gal working late that
is strictly a network person.  Call the on-call and wait. He calls about 5:30
grumbly he's late for the theater but he'll get to it when he gets home.  Uh -
sorry - but (you guessed it) the truck is about to leave and needs their
paperwork.  Program goes in.  I pick up my purse and hightail it out the door.

It's now about 7:30pm and I'm elbow deep in nailpolish (hey, gotta keep those
digits nice to key all day).  My cell phone rings.  It's the on-call.  The
warehouse can't get another set of paperwork done in a different program.  The
support person of the other team says it's the fault of 'that' program. (The
on-call for the other team is this young snert who lectured me about how he or
another one of his team are called when one of their programs doesn't work and
how he doesn't appreciate being called when he's in bed at 11:00pm etc etc -
and why can't I move my process to some more human like time like when he's at
the office instead of at 11:00pm and so on -like I had no idea they woke people
up at that time of night!) Ok - no way am I going to be near a computer for
another hour so the on-call passess the call on to the next in line on-call
because he's at the theater.

Get home - find out that quick change has to go into this other program.  Put
it in, compile it, check it out, no obvious problem.  Call the 2nd on-call to
move it in. I get a call back - it didn't compile clean.  Look at the errors -
END-IF not defined, huh?  Invalid character (reference modification), huh? 
Back to the top of the compile.  Compiled on May 18 1901!  Turns out the
production compile proc is using OS/VS Cobol while the test proc is using
OS/390 Cobol.  How long has this been going on?  Probably since Nov 1999.  Why
no one has noticed I have no idea since we've had several new programs go in
written by people who do use END-IF and reference modification all the time
that are of this ilk (on-line cobol w/DB2).

Monday I get to listen to the young snert again,  explain the tech manager that
the production compile proc is incorrect (he never makes mistakes), scan the
source library for all programs of this particular type and see how many we
have to recompile (they want everyting above the line if possible).  If I'm
lucky the warehouse won't call and I can get back to more important things like
tracking down the 7000 headlamps that are lost in traffic.

I love my job, I love my job, I love my job.................
<gripe off>
```

#### ↳ Re: A Programmer's Day, Week, Year

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-19T01:38:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9e54au$sg1$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010519013733.23119.00000413@ng-fh1.aol.com>`

```
Sure sounds like the "traditional time" to ask the usual (humorous)
question,

    "But other than that, how was the play Mrs. Lincoln?"

P.S.  That OS/VS COBOL vs IBM COBOL for OS/390 "problem" sound pretty fishy
to me.  Someone somewhere MUST have been doing something funny in your
"production turn over" to not find this out til now.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A Programmer's Day, Week, Year

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-05-21T03:33:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010520233354.11466.00000690@ng-mp1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<9e54au$sg1$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
>Subject: Re: A Programmer's Day, Week, Year
>From: "William M. Klein" wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com 
>Date: 5/18/01 11:38 PM Pacific Daylight Time

>P.S.  That OS/VS COBOL vs IBM COBOL for OS/390 "problem" sound pretty fishy
>to me.  Someone somewhere MUST have been doing something funny in your
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com


I thought that very strange too.  Now that I've had time to get some sleep and
think about it I have a hunch that this is a quirk of Librarian and the the
program has a bad history record.

During the Y2K conversion we changed the history records on a lot of programs
to cause them to use the then Cobol II compiler instead of the OS/VS compiler.
Once LE was completely installed the compiler was changed to OS/390 but the
type tag on the Librarian history records remained the same as the Cobol II,
only the proc was changed to point to the new compiler.  If the change in
history record was not done correctly it would appear that it had been changed
when it wasn't.  This may be the case and since no one else who has changed the
program used END-IF or any other 'new' construct it wasn't apparent until now.

Something to look into Monday morning (after coffee of course). 

Eileen
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A Programmer's Day, Week, Year

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T04:15:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010524001535.23307.00001125@ng-fh1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3B097D03.5FE349F8@larich.com>`

```
>Subject: Re: A Programmer's Day, Week, Year
>From: "David W. Furin" dfurin@larich.com 
>Date: 5/21/01 1:39 PM Pacific Daylight Time

>Of course the BA who made up the specs didn't
>> bother to note that the same change had to go into 2 other programs, but
>that's
>> beside the point.

>In this context, what is a "BA"?  (Sorry if this should be obvious.)
>

There are days I want to say BadA** but it really means Business Analyst.  This
is a person who may or may not at one time have been a programmer but gets all
requests for maintenance/enhancements/development and checks out the systems
and determines what has to be changed and how long it will take.  They are
supposed to know the business to determine the impact of the request over all. 
They then make up the specs we work from.  These can be as simple as a note on
an envelope to a whole booklet.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Programmer's Day, Week, Year

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T05:38:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010528013826.09323.00001824@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<346bf752.0105262351.6bbc356e@posting.google.com>`

```
>From: lemonflower@jcn.net  (lemonflower)
>Date: 5/27/01 12:51 AM Pacific Daylight Time

>Thoroughly enjoyed your narration, YukonMama. I'm looking forward to more.
>Misery loves company?
>
>lemonflower

LOL - there may be more.  I have a 'simple change' in one of our programs from
the loony bin (as apposed to the nether world).

These were all written by a programmer who delighted in using all possible
techniques available at the time the program was written.  At one time he was
told one of his load modules was too large so he compensated by putting all his
source code on one long continuous line (per paragraph). Didn't help the load
module any and I don't think anyone has returned sane from making changes to
that particular program. 

As COBOL standards change and CICS is changed (these are all CICS programs) and
IBM closes loop holes (he must have found/used them all) we have had to delve
into the haunted halls and correct them. 

Thank goodness this isn't one of those situations but it's bad enough.

He's still out there programming someplace too so who knows what other treats
he's thinking up now.
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3B097D03.5FE349F8@larich.com> <20010524001535.23307.00001125@ng-fh1.aol.com> <346bf752.0105262351.6bbc356e@posting.google.com> <3B12107B.B8D14B96@lea.mpsc.mot.com>`

```

<naragon@lea.mpsc.mot.com> enlightened us:

>Could you tell me where did you took this from? it is very nice.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> when nobody's watching".

The full saying is:

Work like you don't need the money.
Love as though you have never been hurt.
Dance as though no one is watching you.
Sing as though no one can hear you.

One reference I found to it is at:

http://www.nursefriendly.com/nursing/inspiration/work.like.you.don't.need.the.money.htm

and another at:

http://members.tripod.com/pages2001/inspiration/stories/work.htm

Although they do not list the author.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

In the United States we have the most divided
government we have ever had:

The House is controlled by the Republicans,
The Senate is controlled by the Democrats and, 
The White House is controlled by Exxon-Mobile. 


DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge
Read the real facts at:

http://www.worldwildlife.org/arctic-refuge/

also

http://www.savepolarbears.org/


Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

<a id="t-c8c6223d38"></a>
## Thread: Help Desperately Needed in Student Cobol Project — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Help Desperately Needed in Student Cobol Project

- **From:** goble@gtech (David. E. Goble)
- **Date:** 2001-05-19T13:03:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b04d471.28673354@news.adelaide.on.net>`
- **References:** `<6wxM6.4098$6j3.356899@www.newsranger.com>`

```
On Wed, 16 May 2001 16:02:10 GMT, Michael <nospam@newsranger.com>
wrote:
>
>Write a program that will produced the following report using the SORT routine
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Just some personal suggestions on code format...
>
>PROCEDURE DIVISION.
********************
>SORT SORT-WORK-FILE
>	ASCENDING KEY SRT-SURNAME            
>	DESCENDING KEY SRT-AVG-SALES
>       	       SRT-COMPANY-CODE
>	INPUT PROCEDURE CALC-AND-EXTRACT
 	OUTPUT PROCEDURE PRINT-REPORT.
>
If you are using cobol74 standard you need to use sections, but your
reading of employee uses end-read. The new standard. In this case you
can just use procedures with your sort.
>
>	WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM SPACES.   
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>CALC-AND-EXTRACT.
*=================
>	OPEN INPUT EMPLOYEE-FILE
>		    COMPANY-FILE
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>READ-EMPLOYEE.  
*==============
>	READ EMPLOYEE-FILE
>		AT END MOVE 'NO' TO DATA-REMAINS-SWITCH
>	END-READ.
>
>PROCESS-EMPLOYEE-RECORDS.
*=========================
>	PERFORM WRITE-HEADING-LINES.
>	PERFORM PROCESS-ONE-COMPANY UNTIL NO-DATA-REMAINS OR
>		HEADING-COMPANY NOT EQUAL PREV-COMPANY.
>PROCESS-ONE-COMPANY.
*====================
>	PERFORM INITIALISE-COMPANY.
>	PERFORM SEARCH-COMPANY.
>
I like using (..) in my ifs
>	IF(WS-AVG-SALES >= 3000 AND POLICY-COUNTER > 2)
	THEN
>		MOVE EMPLOYEE-NUMBER TO SRT-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER
>		MOVE SURNAME TO SRT-SURNAME
>		MOVE INITIALS TO SRT-INITIALS
>		MOVE WS-AVG-SALES TO SRT-AVG-SALES
	END-IF.(?)
>	RELEASE SORT-RECORD.
>
>INITIALISE-COMPANY.
*===================
>	MOVE HEADING-COMPANY TO PREV-COMPANY.
>	INITIALIZE WS-AVG-SALES.    
>SEARCH-COMPANY.
*===============
>	SET COMP-INDEX TO 1.
>	SEARCH COMPANY-DETAILS
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>CALCULATE-AVG.
*==============
>	ADD AMOUNT-SOLD (SALES-INDEX) TO WS-AVG-SALES. 
>	IF(POLICY-TYPE (SALES-INDEX) NOT EQUAL ZEROES)
>	THEN
		ADD 1 TO POLICY-COUNTER
	END-IF.(?)
>
	PERFORM READ-EMPLOYEE.
>
>WRITE-HEADING-LINES.
*====================
>	WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM HEADING-LINE-ONE AFTER PAGE.
>	WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM HEADING-LINE-TWO AFTER 2.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>WRITE-DETAIL-LINE.
*==================
	>MOVE SRT-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER TO DET-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER.
>	MOVE SRT-SURNAME TO DET-EMPLOYEE-SURNAME.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Using NetExpress, can I click-sort a table? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Using NetExpress, can I click-sort a table?

- **From:** "Paul Hoberg" <paulhoberg@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T06:24:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hqJN6.10228$Ce6.4710185@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
I will soon receive a trial version of Merant NetExpress 3.1.  Assuming that
I will purchase a copy, I want to use Dialog to develop a program with
screens like those in Outlook Express, in that one can click on a column
header so the data in the table will be sorted ascending, then with another
click, descending, on that column.  Can I do that with Dialog, or will I
have to buy some third party tool to do it, or expend much effort to code
the details myself?  My only experience was with an early version of Dialog
and MicroFocus COBOL.  At that time it was clumsy and limited.  I paid a
talented developer to implement an application, using Clarion, that has this
ability (see www.verifycharges.com), but he had to code this feature himself
.  Now I want do another application, and COBOL is my preferred language.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3b0775d5_1@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<hqJN6.10228$Ce6.4710185@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```

#### ↳ Re: Using NetExpress, can I click-sort a table?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T11:33:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sYNN6.1946$YZ.453175@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<hqJN6.10228$Ce6.4710185@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
Paul Hoberg <paulhoberg@home.com> wrote in message
news:hqJN6.10228$Ce6.4710185@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com...
> I will soon receive a trial version of Merant NetExpress 3.1.  Assuming
that
> I will purchase a copy, I want to use Dialog to develop a program with
> screens like those in Outlook Express, in that one can click on a column
> header so the data in the table will be sorted ascending, then with
another
> click, descending, on that column...
>.. My only experience was with an early version of Dialog
> and MicroFocus COBOL.  At that time it was clumsy and limited.  I paid a
> talented developer to implement an application, using Clarion, that has
this
> ability (see www.verifycharges.com), but he had to code this feature
himself...


The kind of output you see in Moicrosoft Outlook Express is a standard
Windows(r) Listview control, into which "sort on the clicked column" is
built as a standard feature. The listview control is part of the "Common
Controls" series of the Win/32 API.

I don't know how Net Express implements the Listview control (I do not write
in any any language using the "properties and methods" syntax; I use the
"raw" API calls),  but I would think that implementation would include the
sort interface.

Now, the Outlook Express screens which feature multiple panels which
automatically resize to fit inside the client area of the "main" window
without overlapping each other, that's horse of another color. (Or at least
it was about a year ago when I started playing with it).

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Using NetExpress, can I click-sort a table?

- **From:** "Paul Hoberg" <paulhoberg@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T15:15:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<hqJN6.10228$Ce6.4710185@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
I appreciate the quick answers, but can anyone answer my original question:
can I accomplish what I want with Dialog, or do I need a third party tool?
If so, how do I find that tool?


"Paul Hoberg" <paulhoberg@home.com> wrote in message
news:hqJN6.10228$Ce6.4710185@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com...
 I will soon receive a trial version of Merant NetExpress 3.1.  Assuming
that
I will purchase a copy, I want to use Dialog to develop a program with
screens like those in Outlook Express, in that one can click on a column
header so the data in the table will be sorted ascending, then with another
click, descending, on that column.  Can I do that with Dialog, or will I
have to buy some third party tool to do it, or expend much effort to code
the details myself?  My only experience was with an early version of Dialog
and MicroFocus COBOL.  At that time it was clumsy and limited.  I paid a
talented developer to implement an application, using Clarion, that has this
ability (see www.verifycharges.com), but he had to code this feature
himself.  Now I want do another application, and COBOL is my preferred
language.
```

#### ↳ Re: Using NetExpress, can I click-sort a table?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T18:35:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B080EC7.133D9BFE@home.com>`
- **References:** `<hqJN6.10228$Ce6.4710185@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```


Paul Hoberg wrote:

> I will soon receive a trial version of Merant NetExpress 3.1.  Assuming that
> I will purchase a copy, I want to use Dialog to develop a program with
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> .  Now I want do another application, and COBOL is my preferred language.

I use GUIs and OO, not Dialog System. The only thing I can suggest is that you
look at the demo programs for Dialog System when you get your copy of N/E - as I
said, I don't use D/S but am aware it has more whistles and bells since your
last use.

As to whether you can do that 'header click' for ascending/descending, you'll
have to check out. Failing that, it might be a partial use of features in Dialog
System and using some OO methods to achieve what you want.

Best bet, I would suggest, is ask Merant directly.

PS: Before you ask - I don't 'header click' in OO. Where necessary I have three
buttons, associated with a Listbox,  allowing user to select ByName (default),
ByKey and ByOther (if appropriate); I then go back to my (Sorted)collection,
rearranging the listbox text so that I get a complete sort of data, (all
columns), ascending.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Using NetExpress, can I click-sort a table?

- **From:** "Yiorgo Terzis" <gnterzis@otenet.gr>
- **Date:** 2001-05-21T00:25:34+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9e9chf$onv$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`
- **References:** `<hqJN6.10228$Ce6.4710185@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
As far as I know Dialog System does work with listview control by clicking
on column title.
Restriction : It only sorts table ascending column.
I have not managed to make it sort by descending column data (taht is to
work as a toggle button).
```

I have no idea what the issue is here, have not visited the Hal Turner site,

However a subject line suggesting murder catches my attention...<G>

If we are seriously going to advocate freedom of expression, then that
freedom must include the freedom to criticize the fact that there is freedom
of expression...(without having your life threatened...<G>)

Otherwise it is just "freedom to express views I agree with"

Now, anyone written any good COBOL lately <G>?

Pete.

### Portability

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-05-20T15:11:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0105201411.6975898b@posting.google.com>`

```
You guys writing for multiple platforms are going to LOVE "Conditional
Compilation"!  This feature in the new standard enables you to pass a
parm to the compiler, and it will compile the appropriate sections
based on IF ... THEN ... ELSE compiler-directing statements.  I didn't
like it originally, because when I first heard about it, it was always
discussed as an alternative for DEBUGGING MODE.  But for software
vendors, it is going to be glorious!

Stephen J Spiro
Member , J4 COBOL Committee

YOU can be a member of the COBOL Standards Committee, and make a
difference!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T17:45:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f3pll$3lb$1@allbad.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105201411.6975898b@posting.google.com> <3B0887BE.3FEC177A@soltec.net> <4145699b.0105211713.249bc948@posting.google.com> <9ecp67$ece$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3B0DC404.A7E7FC61@optonline.net>`

```
If you are seriously interested in COBOL portablility, you have to check out
1Spec by The Progeni Corporation www.progeni.com .  I guess you would call
this product a "pre processor" but they call it a "natural extention" to
COBOL.  In any event, 1Spec "verbs" can be added inline to an existing COBOL
program or you can write an entire application in it.  If you write in 1Spec
(it's very much a COBOL like syntax) you get almost a 30 to 1 expansion -
ie. 100 lines of 1Spec will generate 300 lines of COBOL.  It generates nice
clean, formated COBOL for a very wide range of compilers, platforms and
screens.  For example, from the same "Spec" you can generate COBOL for a
Unisys A series Mainframe with a green screen interface, or an IBM 390 with
a Windows GUI interface or Web Interface, or you can generate for MicroFocus
or other Micro Compilers for NT, Unix, etc. and they will all run
identically.

It not only makes portability easy, it drastically increases efficiency in
maintaining and writing COBOL code.  And because Web enablement is built in,
it also makes taking COBOL to the web pretty easy.

This company has been around for 20 years, it moved from New Zeland to the
U.S. about 10 years ago.  Their large client base includes companies like
Intrieve who write software in 1Spec for over 30 major banks.  They keep 1
copy of the source and generate it for whatever hardware/software platform
the bank is using.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T23:51:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010530195116.21890.00001206@nso-cq.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<9f3pll$3lb$1@allbad.news.cais.net>`

```
In article <9f3pll$3lb$1@allbad.news.cais.net>, "Editor"
<Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com> writes:

>If you are seriously interested in COBOL portablility, you have to check out
>1Spec by The Progeni Corporation www.progeni.com .  I guess you would call
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My experience with 1Spec is not such a glowing review.
I will grant that the 'concept' of a 4GL language independent product
is supposed to provide portability across platforms and target languages.
Unfortunately, what I have seen generated from a totally 1Spec file is
some of the nastiest spaghetti code that you would ever want to try and 
debug.  It also creates an environment where you can never be certain
that the generated code will work the same way twice.

I have had favorable results in developing Q&D 'fix' programs.
Personal rules that I have come up with for my own sanity is to
stay away from the IF macro and ALL DBxxx macros.
A large majority of the generated code seems to be geared for 
compatibility to COBOL68 syntax rather than taking advantage of
the more recent language extensions.

They have yet to define a SKEL file to properly handle multi-program 
sources (nested programs).

This 'opinion' is based on experience with Progeni used at three locations
and something in the neighborhood of 8 years of active use.
```

"Sff5ky" <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
news:20010530195116.21890.00001206@nso-cq.aol.com...
> In article <9f3pll$3lb$1@allbad.news.cais.net>, "Editor"

call

COBOL
> >program or you can write an entire application in it.  If you write in
1Spec

> >ie. 100 lines of 1Spec will generate 300 lines of COBOL.  It generates
nice
> >clean, formated COBOL for a very wide range of compilers, platforms and
> >screens.  For example, from the same "Spec" you can generate COBOL for a
> >
>

I haven't used the Progeni product and am not associated with it in any way
(despite it having been developed in NZ <G>), but your comments made me
think about some other "4GL" products I have come across and the objections
which were raised to them by programmers.

The comments below are not intended to "take sides", just to examine what is
being said

>>>
> My experience with 1Spec is not such a glowing review.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have heard exactly the same comment about some other packages and even
about Jackson Structured Programming (remember that...<G>?)

I think the question we need to ask is: "Do I ever HAVE to maintain this
generated code?" If the answer is "No", then should we really care whether
it is spaghetti or not? We don't get into a lather about the object code
generated by our COBOL compiler (unless performance is suffering). Isn't it
really the same concept?

I have heard similar shock horror reactions when people see the code
generated from my beloved Fujitsu PowerCOBOL. But I don't have to amend this
code. When I want to see the code I actually wrote, PowerCOBOL shows it to
me without any problem and allows me to amend it. The project is then
rebuilt and everyone is happy.

Now, if the whole point about 1Spec is that you DO need to maintain the code
it generates, then the criticism is valid.

> It also creates an environment where you can never be certain
> that the generated code will work the same way twice.
>
Oops! That one is serious. I hope you mean on different platforms...<G>?

> I have had favorable results in developing Q&D 'fix' programs.
> Personal rules that I have come up with for my own sanity is to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yeah, that's what we tend to do...develop our own "rules" and "workarounds"
which kind of defeats the object of a tool that is supposed to make things
easier. Personally, I wouldn't be too worried whether they generate the
latest and greatest code; I'd be much more concerned that what they DID
generate worked CORRECTLY. (and I don't need to maintain it, of course.)


> This 'opinion' is based on experience with Progeni used at three locations
> and something in the neighborhood of 8 years of active use.
>
And very thought provoking comments too. The whole question of the value of
4GLs hinges around whether or not they really make things "easier".

It is also true that we (as programmers) may need to adjust our mindset in
order to get the best from them. We are used to having control and doing
everything; along comes a "tool" which says: "Sit back and let me do that.
Look I can increase your productivity 3000%..." When we do so, we find that
the tool's solution is far removed from what we would have done and the
general reaction is one of shock...

Hence, I suggest that the evaluation of such tools comes down to the
following:

1. Do I have to maintain what it generates? (Responsibility for the
generated code rests with the tool in my book...)

2. Does what it generates work correctly? (without requiring "tweaks" from
me).

3. Does it actually save me time and effort? (setting aside any personal
bias, when I do actual quantitative analysis of time spent, do I come out
ahead?)

4. Do I feel comfortable using it? (If my mindset is such that I cannot
trust it and will never feel comfortable with it, then it is academic
whether it is a "good" tool or not, I'll just never be happy with it.)

I submit that any tool which passes the above tests is OK. Otherwise, I
stick to what I do...

Pete.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T12:43:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0CF3CD.ECBC9330@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105201411.6975898b@posting.google.com> <1m7kgt4ulmtodh6ag2boe3gapic2g919mk@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> 
> :>You guys writing for multiple platforms are going to LOVE "Conditional
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Glad that COBOL is getting into step.

The reason that there has not been a lot of clamouring for 'conditional
compilation' in the way that, eg C, has it is that there is no (or
little) need for it.

In C its portability was endowed primarily through conditional
compilation.  That is a C program of a few years ago was 'portable'
because the differences in the compilers, environments and operating
systems was catered for by having several sets of different code in the
source file, the appropriate set being chosen at compile time by the
conditionals.

With Cobol the portability is endowed primarily by not needing any
differences in the source code.

Where there are differences it is usually possible to cater for this at
run time.  For example I have source code that accesses system functions
by performing or calling routines that are in COPY books.  The programs
have flags available to them that indicate the compiler used and the
operating system and these are used to execute the particular routines
that are appropriate.

Rather than conditional compilation, I do much the same by conditional
execution at run-time.
 
For example to get a list of files I perform 'GetFile' with a 'first' or
'next' flag.  This will actually CALL 'UGETFILE' or 'MGETFILE' or
'CGETFILE' dynamically depending on which operating system is being run
on.

C doesn't (generally) have uniform data items, or dynamic calling, or
(until recently) standards and so it needs mechanisms to resolve
differences at compile time, hence it needs conditional compilation
where Cobol does not.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T17:25:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b0c9ad3_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105201411.6975898b@posting.google.com> <1m7kgt4ulmtodh6ag2boe3gapic2g919mk@4ax.com> <3B0CF3CD.ECBC9330@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Totally endorse Richard's mail here.

Keep a standard interface in the source code, and resolve stuff that is
conditional on the environment being run in, at run time.

I have only ever used "Conditional Compilation" in Assembly languages (when
it technically becomes "Conditional Assembly") and then only when ABSOLUTELY
UNAVOIDABLE.

Anything that lends complexity to Source Code is anathema to COBOL and
should be avoided like the plague.

I wonder how long the Standards committee debated before deciding to include
this "essential feature" (that is neither needed nor wanted) into COBOL?

Pete.


"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3B0CF3CD.ECBC9330@Azonic.co.nz...

> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > This "new and exciting" feature has been in PL/1 more than 20 years ago
and
> > has been available in most assemblers for at least that long.
> >
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> where Cobol does not.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T06:44:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010524024410.08109.00001129@ng-ce1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0c9ad3_6@Usenet.com>`

```
Portability is a marketing concept.

The only way to keep code portable across  dissimilar systems is code to a
common functional subset.

This only works reasonably well for pretty basic functions- 'go to the bucket &
get the bytes.  Write the bytes on a simple device."  Once you start trying to
do useful work, you start adapting to an environment. Goodbye portability,
hello functionality.

I've seen a couple of Java apps now that only handle clients with a particular
version of Netscape, and the apps server has to be under a particular release
of  Unix, with the "right" version of Oracle.

Still, they got sold as "portable" apps.

It's true i 'spose- you can port 'em to any Sun box running the same OS release
and the same version of Oracle.

And let's not even get into IDEs and libraries..

Oh well.  It all makes work for the working man to do.
Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T01:34:21+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b0d0e9a_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0c9ad3_6@Usenet.com> <20010524024410.08109.00001129@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```

"FRLSFLY" <frlsfly@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010524024410.08109.00001129@ng-ce1.aol.com...
> Portability is a marketing concept.
>
> The only way to keep code portable across  dissimilar systems is code to a
> common functional subset.
>
While I agree with the rest of your post, Nathan, I take mild issue with the
above.

Many years ago, before PCs were invented, it was considered almost
impossible to port code between mainframes. Furthermore, every few years a
new release of database software, screen handling, or Operating System meant
that all existing applications had to be converted.

This was costly and tedious and one day (for a bet, originally) I suggested
that if we layered our systems and separated the Application code from the
System dependent code, we could probably convert not only to a new OS
release but even to a completely different mainframe. A bet is a bet, and in
the next 2 years in my spare time I wrote such a system. All system
dependent calls were to a standard interface called "ACE" (Application
Common Environment). Instead of embedding IMS-DC or CICS calls in the COBOL
code you simply called ACE. Instead of coding DL/1 calls you called ACE.
Instead of handling ISAM files, you called ACE. You could also get ACE to
store stuff across dequeue boundaries, do some basic Task Management, obtain
memory for you and terminal dependent buffers, check security and logons and
even handle abends in a consistent and standard way. The interface between
ACE and the Application was consistent and simple. Programmers with no
knowledge of IMS (for instance) could access and maintain IMS databases
without ever using a DL/1 call.

Obviously, the ACE calls were implemented by "system  dependent modules"
which were transparent to the Application Programmers. However, many of
these modules were also written in standard COBOL and could be easily
re-compiled for the target environment. We developed several successful
medium size projects using ACE, then ported one of these from the IBM 360
series to an ICL 1902. The ACE "ScreenMan" (Screen Management) subsystem was
converted from CICS to Driver beads for the ICL system, the Indexed files
handled by "DataMan"  (Flat file Data Management) were simply re-compiled
and everything else ("TaskMan", "StoreMan" and "PoliceMan" (Task, Storage,
and Security Management, respectively),  worked perfectly. NO Application
code required modification!

The system proved itself some years later when it was used for a Major
on-line implementation in Spain.

I was paid for the original bet, and also received a substantial sum for the
software (eventually). It was evaluated by British Aerospace whose technical
people loved it, but the bean counters couldn't see the necessity for it and
wouldn't authorise the expenditure....

Finally, when PCs came along I converted it to the PC, and to this day I
could develop applications on the PC which  would be immediately runnable on
any system which has an ACE environment (currently IBM mainframes and ICL
1900s...don't see too many of those around these days <G>). This makes it
perfectly feasible to design, build, and debug mainframe Applications
totally on the PC. Once deliverable they are simply re-compiled on the
target mainframe and run in the provided ACE environment.

I still have a screen painter I wrote which generates MID, MOD, DIF and DOF
for IMS DC, CICS MAP for CICS, or Screen Section in COBOL for PC, and all of
them can be handled by the same common interface (ACE ScreenMan),
communicating with the Application by means of an ACE interface block which
contains the Message buffer, an action code, a reply code and a few other
modifiers used on various calls.

Now, the point about all this is that portability need not be a myth. It is
achievable by consistent, layered, interfaces which separate Application
(Business logic) from System dependent code. The modern equivalent of this
is, of course, Component technology, which encapsulates methods and data,
and provides a completely consistent interface (COM or CORBA).

ACE is sadly (well, to me anyway...) overtaken by events and is no longer
pertinent to the PC environment, where it has been replaced by OO and
Components. It will take a little while before this occurs in the mainframe
world, but I believe it is inevitable.

I console myself with the thought that the fundamental idea I had 25 years
ago has come to fruition and we DO have completely portable code in the form
of Components. It is achieved by encapsulating  (separating) and
interfacing, almost exactly as I envisaged. I never once imagined that it
would be Object code, rather than Source code, though....<G>

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

While most of this discussion has stressed portability across operating
systems, for my $0.02 worth, I think programs written for portability across
OSs or across different brand compilers for the same OS generally end up far
easier to maintain  "while they live on the original system;"  that is, the
decision to 'code for portabilty'  results in 'code for maintainability'
which cannot ever be a Bad Thing.

MCM


Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b0d0e9a_1@Usenet.com...

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

a
> > common functional subset.
> >
> While I agree with the rest of your post, Nathan, I take mild issue with
the
> above.
