###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T18:33:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0D5474.6F8EE257@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0c9ad3_6@Usenet.com> <20010524024410.08109.00001129@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```


FRLSFLY wrote:

> Portability is a marketing concept.

I disagree with your first sentence. It is a very 'non marketable concept' so far
as compiler vendors are concerned. Consider, John Bradley CEO of Liant didn't see
any justification for portability in his message. Another compiler vendor senior
official is also on record with a similar comment.

To be fair to John - it doesn't seem 'necessary' to have portability. Why would you
want to switch a large application designed in RM/COBOL to Fujitsu or Net Express.
It is NOT just a simple 'switcheroo' - there is a learning curve associated with
any compiler and you bump up against unforeseen 'gotchas'. Further there is the
time and cost to be VERY seriously considered. The same approach applies if you are
talking mainframe - you have got to be out of your mind if you switch from Big Blue
to Unisys or vice versa, without a damn good reason. Mainframe wise the cost could
be HUGE - as John  pointed out, it is a combination of O/S, compiler, RDBs and
whatever other features you will need.

However, interestingly, others have commented that they do need portability (or
standard code) to make their applications 'fly' under different O/S. They
illustrate a legitimate case.

To my mind, where true 'portability' comes in for COBOL, is in an area as yet
untapped - libraries of common routines, written in Standard COBOL. (Remember the
other languages are replete with libraries of routines, including all the stuff
written for Linux). Now I *know* a certain gentleman is going to jump in  and say
something to the effect '...but you don't need common routines - you've got 'em
with Components". However, I contend that there are routines which would be useful
to ALL and the intended UDF(User Defined Functions), the equivalent of an OO
method, could be a boon. Certainly UDFs will help to achieve 'localizing
variables'.

Associated with portability there is one further aspect as it applies to OO. As of
December 2002 we will have a new COBOL Standard, including OO. BUT - that document
will contain nothing about standard classes, collections, dictionaries - this is a
feature of both Fujitsu and Net Express (Visual Age ??? - don't know), and going
into 2002 this feature will STILL be an 'extension' in the Fujitsu and Net Express
compilers.

The implication is I could write very general routines to produce collections to be
used with GUIs, SQL, accumulating lines for print routines, or assembling product
lines making up an invoice, etc.... Fine, if you are a Net Express user - but my
code is not a damn bit of good  to you if you use another compiler.

I may have mentioned this before - the UK ISO representative(s) are on record as
saying, "We want standard OO classes otherwise vendors will be tempted into doing
their own thing....."

In the context of portability, both Fujitsu and Net Express offer CBL_FILE routines
to assemble filenames, check existence, copy, rename, delete etc. These are
extensions - and yet this must be a very general requirement for both Unix and
Windows applications, and I am not aware what other vendors provide. (Want to
delete a whole set of files using a wildcard ? Dead easy in Net Express where I can
use a collection  Even though CBL_FILE routines exist in Fujitsu - my routine wont
work - the collection syntax is different !)

Unfortunately there seems to be a determination to keep the Standard 'vanilla
flavoured' and not acknowledge this very REAL need - because it is specific to an
O/S. I contend that the Standard SHOULD HAVE this feature as an annexure -
acknowledging the need in a non mainframe environment.

You reading this Stephen - just guess what J4 reaction will be to above suggestion.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T18:15:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0105241715.a34f217@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0c9ad3_6@Usenet.com> <20010524024410.08109.00001129@ng-ce1.aol.com> <3B0D5474.6F8EE257@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote 
> In the context of portability, both Fujitsu and Net Express offer CBL_FILE routines
> to assemble filenames, check existence, copy, rename, delete etc. These are
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

Jimmy, in most commercial production environments, most of these
features would be prohibited by installation security rules. 
Accordingly, for those users, there is not only NO DEMAND, there is
FIRM OBJECTION.

Checking for existence is easy: OPEN the file and check the file
status.  There is additional flexibility thru the use of OPTIONAL
files.

In the MVS and VSE worlds, all of the other features are handled
easily with Job Control Language and utilities.

They are interesting features, YOU could propose them for the NEXT
standard.  The decision would be up to WG4, by the way, not J4.  J4
often makes recommendations to WG4, but WG4 determines the "programme
of work", and J4 implements what WG4 directs.

As a "high level language", COBOL has generally operated at the record
level, not the file level.  What you propose is getting into the murky
waters of System Programming.  On the other hand, so is the proposed
"Finalizer" for Object Orientation.

Nevertheless, I do not think anyone in J4 would object in principal to
these features.

However, it might bring up the question of adding another very new
feature to the standard that would specifially allow/require
compiler-makers to give the
purchaser of the compiler the specific ability to restrict/prohibit
the use of compiler features on an installation-wide basis.  Already
there are preprocessors/extensions which do this....

It might have other implications.... but, if you want it, put your
ideas into an e-mail and send them to Don Schricker, as a candidate
for the next revision.
We will certainly give it consideration.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee

PS: The next J4 meeting is in Kokomo, Indiana, USA from Sunday, June 3
to Saturday, June 9.  If anyone would like to attend all or part of
the meeting, either as a Observer (with the possibility of joining the
committee) or as a guest (no charges, either way), please contact me
and I will get you some more information.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T15:26:22+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b0dd26b_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0c9ad3_6@Usenet.com> <20010524024410.08109.00001129@ng-ce1.aol.com> <3B0D5474.6F8EE257@home.com>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3B0D5474.6F8EE257@home.com...
> Now I *know* a certain gentleman is going to jump in  and say
> something to the effect '...but you don't need common routines - you've
got 'em
> with Components".
Nope. I don't need to jump in and say that, you said it for me...<G>

Besides, for Source code development (which is what we are talking about
here), that argument is not applicable. It applies to APPLICATION
development, by people who are NOT trained as programmers, and have no need
for, or interest in, Source code.

> However, I contend that there are routines which would be useful
> to ALL and the intended UDF(User Defined Functions), the equivalent of an
OO
> method, could be a boon. Certainly UDFs will help to achieve 'localizing
> variables'.
>
Well, you need to realize that these UDFs could be a two-edged sword, Jimmy.
We've seen it on sites where they have their own specific modules
(installation standard functions). Programmers don't like using stuff they
didn't write themselves and will often ignore these "libraries".

The argument goes: "Hey, by the time I found out there was an installation
standard module to do it, got the documentation, read what it does and how
to interface to it, then decided whether it was pertinent to my needs, I
could've coded, debugged, and implemented a couple of paragraphs to do it
for me. And at least I'd know they weren't going to give me any problems..."
Anyone who has managed teams of programmers will have heard variations on
this theme.

Of course, the correct course of action is to insist that the code be
replaced by a Call to the installation standard module. But it would be a
very brave Manager indeed who will make this call under the pressure of an
approaching deadline, when the code is working perfectly and there are still
many problems to be solved...

I have doubts also about your equating a UDF with an OO Method. They are
really NOT the same thing.

> Associated with portability there is one further aspect as it applies to
OO. As of
> December 2002 we will have a new COBOL Standard, including OO. BUT - that
document
> will contain nothing about standard classes, collections, dictionaries -
this is a
> feature of both Fujitsu and Net Express (Visual Age ??? - don't know), and
going
> into 2002 this feature will STILL be an 'extension' in the Fujitsu and Net
Express
> compilers.
>
> The implication is I could write very general routines to produce
collections to be
> used with GUIs, SQL, accumulating lines for print routines, or assembling
product
> lines making up an invoice, etc.... Fine, if you are a Net Express user -
but my
> code is not a damn bit of good  to you if you use another compiler.
>
> I may have mentioned this before - the UK ISO representative(s) are on
record as
> saying, "We want standard OO classes otherwise vendors will be tempted
into doing
> their own thing....."
>

Ah, CLASSES. Totally different from Object Methods. I agree that standard
CLASSES would be a "Good Thing". A Standard Class Library where users can
override, add to, and overload the provided Class Methods would be a VERY
Good Thing. Maybe such a Class Library could be provided as a series of
Prototypes...

> In the context of portability, both Fujitsu and Net Express offer CBL_FILE
routines
> to assemble filenames, check existence, copy, rename, delete etc. These
are
> extensions - and yet this must be a very general requirement for both Unix
and
> Windows applications, and I am not aware what other vendors provide. (Want
to
> delete a whole set of files using a wildcard ? Dead easy in Net Express
where I can
> use a collection  Even though CBL_FILE routines exist in Fujitsu - my
routine wont
> work - the collection syntax is different !)

If you really MUST have a collection, it is not the Collection syntax that
is the problem here. Whatever that syntax is, you simply need to "walk" the
collection and return each filename to the CBL_ routine. ANY Collection will
have a facility to do this, otherwise the Collection is a bit pointless...At
the worst, you could simply index through the collection with
perform...varying...on the collection index.

I would try giving a wildcard to the CBL_ routine...

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T04:50:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0DE4FA.C7D3B0A4@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0c9ad3_6@Usenet.com> <20010524024410.08109.00001129@ng-ce1.aol.com> <3B0D5474.6F8EE257@home.com> <3b0dd26b_3@Usenet.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

>
> If you really MUST have a collection, it is not the Collection syntax that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> I would try giving a wildcard to the CBL_ routine...

Dont have to - use Class Filename to achieve above - possibly could make routine
more slick - but it works.

Business Class
       *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "delete-temp-files".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Working-storage section.
       01 Ucs-WildCardName.
          05               pic x(22)        value "\ucs\4-Temporary-Data\".
          05 Ucs-Client    Pic x(03).
          05               pic x(01) value "-".
          05 Ucs-Facility  pic x(02).
          05               pic x(02) value "*.".
          05 Ucs-suffix    pic x(03) value "*  ".
          05               pic x(01) value space.

       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-length                    pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-program                   object reference.
       Procedure Division.
          invoke FileName2 "new" returning ls-program
          move   ws-Client(1:3)     to Ucs-Client
          move   ws-Facility        to Ucs-Facility

          evaluate true
            when DeleteAll
                 perform UCS-ALL
            when DeleteUCS etc...
            when DeleteErrors etc.
          End-evaluate

          invoke ls-program "finalize" returning ls-program

       EXIT METHOD.

       UCS-ALL.

          move "*" to UCS-suffix
          compute ls-length = function length (UCS-Wildcardname)
          invoke ls-program "delete-using-wildcard"
                 using ls-length Ucs-WildCardName.

       UCS-ERRORS.
        etc......

       UCS-FILES.
        etc...
                End Method "delete-temp-files".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------

Class Filenam2 (other routines) but calls M/F Class Filename
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "delete-using-wildcard".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 n                         pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-collection             object reference.
       01 ls-filename               pic x(100).
       01 ls-length                 pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-size                   pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-StatusCode             pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-string                 object reference.

       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-length                pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnk-WildCardName.
          05 pic x occurs 1 to 50 depending on lnk-length.

       Procedure Division using lnk-length lnk-WildCardName.

        invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue"
               using     lnk-length  lnk-WildCardName
               returning ls-string
        invoke FileName "expandwildcards" using     ls-string
                                          returning ls-Collection
        invoke ls-Collection "size" returning ls-size

        perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ls-size
           move spaces to ls-filename
           invoke ls-Collection "at" using n returning ls-string
           invoke ls-string "getValueZ" returning ls-filename
           call "CBL_DELETE_FILE" using     ls-filename
                                  returning ls-StatusCode
        End-perform

        invoke ls-Collection "deepfinalize" returning ls-Collection

       End Method "delete-using-wildcard".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
```

Now, I'm going to jump into the Fray....

I've seen some of this type of thing when using Java as well.  

The code example uses OO syntax to "wrap" a simple call.  That doesn't
make it OO!  It's a sham <G>.  No offense Jimmy, but the more I study
this subject the more I see where the IMPORTANT pieces of the puzzle
are design.  Start with the DATA, define your objects, define the
actions on those objects etc.  Properly designed this "class" could be
a base class that each "file" object inherits from (that's the beauty
of multiple inheritence which COBOL supports) -- and maybe that is
what you are doing and I should just shut up.  Anyway - a Class should
define an object.  I don't see the object that this class defines.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T23:02:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B10366C.F404D651@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0c9ad3_6@Usenet.com> <20010524024410.08109.00001129@ng-ce1.aol.com> <3B0D5474.6F8EE257@home.com> <3b0dd26b_3@Usenet.com> <3B0DE4FA.C7D3B0A4@home.com> <3b0fc6c0.36438759@news1.attglobal.net>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:

> Now, I'm going to jump into the Fray....
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No need to shut up, as I was merely illustrating in response to Pete how the file
delete routine can be used. The trouble with only seeing pieces of the puzzle can
lead to misinterpretation. So let's see if you change your mind with the following
explanation <G>

Firstly this is old code used with, guess what, those damned Ascii-Delimited(CSV)
files  - one good reason for going DB and SQL to get away from this crap !

So it's a two stage thing :-

1. Converting RM/COBOL '74 data files, (using M/F CONVERT3)  to Net Express data
files

2. Generating and receiving back CSVs for Net Express files.

Let's just concentrate on # 1 and its input to # 2. Stages (a) through (d) below, are
'linked' being controlled by the Business Class "Convert Data Files", this class in
turn invokes other classes to perform production units as described below :-

(a) First from RM files (6 different formats) build a set of RM data in CSV. Without
exiting the app let the user invoke an Editor (OLE) to 'nurse' any of the data as
necessary.

(b) When he thinks it is 'clean' now use the CSVs as input to create N/E data files -

this uses Dictionary look-up tables to generate new codes required for the N/E
system, plus a complete validation on ALL fields and keys. Here we get some error
files.

(c) View/print  the error files (OLE) and clean up files from (b) and re-run (b).
Still errors found, so repeat (b).

(d) Stage (c) is now A-OK so we can generate the finalized N/E data files.

Now for (a) through (d), yes I'm using file objects as you are already aware - like
the Standard Line Sequential with instances for CSVs or Error Files. Same goes for
generating the N/E files.

This exercise, depending upon what else he is doing, might be going on over a 3-6 day
cycle until he gets it clean. Note the Controlling Class lets him jump in at stages
(a) through (d) as necessary - he should at least know where he left off (???). Doing
file lookups to ensure data is present, it will 'push' him back to the previous stage
if something is missing.

Meanwhile in that temporary directory we've got a host of CSVs, error files, plus
temporary ISAM files I've also generated for this exercise.

So when we get the A-OK from Step (d), the easy way is to message-box him "OK it's
all clean. Now Exit the application, and via DOS or Windows Explorer delete those
damned files in the temp directory". That to me seems a bit of a shabby approach when
we can automate - just like I let him call his Editor(OLE) from the app. Agreed I had
file objects knocking around at the time he was processing individual units - but not
now 6 days later. All I know is there is something 'lurking' in that temporary
directory that can be got rid of.

So the Controlling Class "Convert Data Files" signals all is clean and then deletes
those 'temporaries' as I illustrated.

Not to the same extent as # 1 but #2 - CSVs for N/E. If he generates CSVs from N/E,
(even though they are what I generated), I do NOT take them back into N/E without
first putting them through the same validity checks, with accompanying error files as
necessary.

Still bullshit - or do you buy into it with the explanation <G> Certainly 'design'
had to go into thinking this one out, to make the whole process bullet-proof - and it
was a pain in the ass !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T15:05:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b111693.122422235@news1.attglobal.net>`

Still the class does not define an object.. <G>.

However, this may be one of those cases where the wrapping makes sense
anyway.  I'm not such an elitist that I insist that you can't do this.
Anway, perhaps it's just a matter of perspective?

One could still have a "TemporaryFile" collection class.  Then, the
object collected could be these "Temporary Files".  One method of the
Collection Class could be to "Go find the temporary files".  Then one
creates an instance of the "TemporyFile" one wishes to delete, and
then invokes the "DeleteMe" method, and then removes the object
reference from the Collection.

Pedantic Exercise of Tedium?  Perhaps - but one could then reuse this
for any and all temporary data files under virtually any circumstance.


After the run, during the run, hold the references until the end of
the run and then do a DeleteMe on all objects in the collection.
Hmmm... maybe form one's mainline finalizer?

Raise the Shields Mr. Checkov.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T13:22:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ejjgu$jq9$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105201411.6975898b@posting.google.com> <1m7kgt4ulmtodh6ag2boe3gapic2g919mk@4ax.com> <3B0CF3CD.ECBC9330@Azonic.co.nz> <3b0c9ad3_6@Usenet.com>`

```
Sorry Pete - but "conditional compilation" was added to this revision based
on SIGNIFICANT user input on the advantageous that it already offered in
those COBOLs (and other 3GL's) where it was available (e.g. Micro Focus
COBOL and PL/I).

It is especially "useful" as it gets rid of the "need" for debugging lines -
which (as much as SOME PC programmers may not know it) are still in WIDE use
for those with "production" vs "test" versions of programs.

One "nice" thing that conditional compilation (in the draft COBOL Standard)
provides is the ability to "bring in" usefully named copy members -
depending on WHICH operating system (or compiler) is being used for the same
"source program".  Today, programmers EITHER
 - have to name such members identically (very error prone)
 - have to "change" their copy statements depending on which environment the
compile is occurring in (even MORE error prone).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T14:46:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ejvet$3e8$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105201411.6975898b@posting.google.com> <1m7kgt4ulmtodh6ag2boe3gapic2g919mk@4ax.com> <3B0CF3CD.ECBC9330@Azonic.co.nz> <3b0c9ad3_6@Usenet.com> <9ejjgu$jq9$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9ejjgu$jq9$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net...

> Sorry Pete - but "conditional compilation" was added to this revision
based
> on SIGNIFICANT user input on the advantageous that it already offered in
> those COBOLs (and other 3GL's) where it was available (e.g. Micro Focus
> COBOL and PL/I).

I know of multiple Burroughs application software products that were
maintained in COBOL (this would have been the '68 dialect) as single source
files on a B6700 system, with a rudimentary form of conditional compilation
being used to distinguish among "small system" (B1700), "medium system"
(B2/3/4700) and "large system" (B6/7700) as far back as the early 1970's,
without the need for a "pre-processor" to split the sources out for the
various target systems.  Many third-party application vendors were doing the
same to distinguish among the various Burroughs platforms when I joined
Burroughs in 1974.

As best I can tell, conditional compilation has been a feature of
(ex-Burroughs Unisys, at least) Unisys compilers much valued by our
customers for better than a quarter of a century.   I can verify that the
MCP/AS COBOL(68) compiler had this feature as far back as Mark 2.5, which
version was released, as best I can determine, somewhere around 1973 or so.

A quick perusal of "IBM DOS Full American National Standard COBOL" (fifth
edition, May 1973) reveals some features of that compiler that look
suspiciously like conditional compilation, though without a compatible
system handy to check it out on I can't be sure!

> One "nice" thing that conditional compilation (in the draft COBOL
Standard)
> provides is the ability to "bring in" usefully named copy members -
> depending on WHICH operating system (or compiler) is being used for the
same
> "source program".  Today, programmers EITHER
>  - have to name such members identically (very error prone)
>  - have to "change" their copy statements depending on which environment
the
> compile is occurring in (even MORE error prone).

Although our historical version of conditional compilation lacks many of the
features specified in the draft standard, and it certainly isn't the same
syntax, COPY statements could always be in conditionally-included source.

And to the original point:  the primary use of conditional compilation in
these COBOL compilers has always been in support of portability of source
code for application developers.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T14:14:24+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b0dbf92_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105201411.6975898b@posting.google.com> <1m7kgt4ulmtodh6ag2boe3gapic2g919mk@4ax.com> <3B0CF3CD.ECBC9330@Azonic.co.nz> <3b0c9ad3_6@Usenet.com> <9ejjgu$jq9$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
There's no need to apologise, Bill...<G>

I accept and respect that others will have a different opinion. That is
right and proper...

As far as Debugging lines go, anyone who includes them in production
programs deserves whatever they get.

In the old days (running under DOS on 360) it was fairly common on some
installations I worked on to set an external (UPSI) switch (Switch 8, if I
remember correctly) when a production program failed.

ALL COBOL programs contained in their "Initialisation" section the
statement:

IF SWITCH-8-ON READY TRACE.

This meant that all the trace overheads were compiled into production
programs, but it was considered worth it. That was the ONLY diagnostic aid
(apart from the dump) which we were allowed.

Looking back on this , with the experience of years it seems pretty
primitive, but it did the job.

I believe that Debugging lines are for exactly that...debugging. However, I
guess I can't argue against the fact that if TRACE is OK, then debugging
lines must also be OK.

The argument that Conditional Compilation replaces these lines, I'm still
not happy with. From experience with Assembler programs, I find that it
clouds the Source code. In Assenbler this is not so bad because the source
code is not immediately obvious anyway...<G>; in COBOL, it defeats the whole
object of clarity and ease of maintenance.

At least, I think it does. I noted Benyamin's (and some others) dissension,
arguing that it can avoid duplicated COPY books, and I'm not sure about that
one.

I accept that it is "arguable" but no way will you ever persuade me that it
is "essential".

Richard made the point that code which is conditional on the environment you
are running in, is best resolved at run time, and there are a number of ways
in which you can do this. The description of ACE which I posted describes
ONE such method (separating and layering). I certainly agree with the
"resolve at run time (NOT compile time)" approach.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T12:14:41+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ifjpgtg899j5vs8ovt10j84hqpkf5sbcnl@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105201411.6975898b@posting.google.com> <1m7kgt4ulmtodh6ag2boe3gapic2g919mk@4ax.com> <3B0CF3CD.ECBC9330@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
On Thu, 24 May 2001 12:43:09 +0100 Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
wrote:

:>Binyamin Dissen wrote:
 
:>> :>You guys writing for multiple platforms are going to LOVE "Conditional
:>> :>Compilation"!  This feature in the new standard enables you to pass a
:>> :>parm to the compiler, and it will compile the appropriate sections
:>> :>based on IF ... THEN ... ELSE compiler-directing statements.  I didn't
:>> :>like it originally, because when I first heard about it, it was always
:>> :>discussed as an alternative for DEBUGGING MODE.  But for software
:>> :>vendors, it is going to be glorious!
 
:>> This "new and exciting" feature has been in PL/1 more than 20 years ago and
:>> has been available in most assemblers for at least that long.
 
:>> Glad that COBOL is getting into step.

:>The reason that there has not been a lot of clamouring for 'conditional
:>compilation' in the way that, eg C, has it is that there is no (or
:>little) need for it.

:>In C its portability was endowed primarily through conditional
:>compilation.  That is a C program of a few years ago was 'portable'
:>because the differences in the compilers, environments and operating
:>systems was catered for by having several sets of different code in the
:>source file, the appropriate set being chosen at compile time by the
:>conditionals.

:>With Cobol the portability is endowed primarily by not needing any
:>differences in the source code.

:>Where there are differences it is usually possible to cater for this at
:>run time.  For example I have source code that accesses system functions
:>by performing or calling routines that are in COPY books.  The programs
:>have flags available to them that indicate the compiler used and the
:>operating system and these are used to execute the particular routines
:>that are appropriate.

:>Rather than conditional compilation, I do much the same by conditional
:>execution at run-time.
 
:>For example to get a list of files I perform 'GetFile' with a 'first' or
:>'next' flag.  This will actually CALL 'UGETFILE' or 'MGETFILE' or
:>'CGETFILE' dynamically depending on which operating system is being run
:>on.

:>C doesn't (generally) have uniform data items, or dynamic calling, or
:>(until recently) standards and so it needs mechanisms to resolve
:>differences at compile time, hence it needs conditional compilation
:>where Cobol does not.

Not a major C programmer, but have done mucho coding in assembler and PL/1.

The great advantage of conditional assembly is in included code, aka COPY
books.

The copy book can generate different code based on which fields and files are
defined in the DATA DIVISION as well as their attributes. Thus rather than
have 20 different copies of certain business logic, one can have a single copy
which generates the appropriate code for each program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T09:11:59+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0E13CF.E40D1B7@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105201411.6975898b@posting.google.com> <1m7kgt4ulmtodh6ag2boe3gapic2g919mk@4ax.com> <3B0CF3CD.ECBC9330@Azonic.co.nz> <ifjpgtg899j5vs8ovt10j84hqpkf5sbcnl@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> 
> :>> :>You guys writing for multiple platforms are going to LOVE "Conditional
> :>> :>Compilation"! This feature in the new standard enables you to pass a
> :>> :>parm to the compiler, and it will compile the appropriate sections
> :>> :>based on IF ... THEN ... ELSE compiler-directing statements.  

> Not a major C programmer, but have done mucho coding in assembler and PL/1.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> defined in the DATA DIVISION as well as their attributes. 

Are you talking 'conditional compilation' here (as described above) or
macro expansion ?

> Thus rather than
> have 20 different copies of certain business logic, one can have a single copy
> which generates the appropriate code for each program.

Except that, with 'conditional compilation' there will be 20 different
blocks of code for each variable item in the copybook only one being
included based on the appropriate IF. 

Now with 'macro expansion' things are rather different, it is like a
COPY REPLACING (which Cobol has had for quite some time).
```

:>Except that, with 'conditional compilation' there will be 20 different

:>included based on the appropriate IF. 

:>Now with 'macro expansion' things are rather different, it is like a
:>COPY REPLACING (which Cobol has had for quite some time).

May be more, but that piece of business logic is completely in one place.

- **Message-ID:** `<3B08965C.6F34D5C9@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<9cukad$nn68@news-dxb> <q9n6ft0pbn903d5gqqbjfjns0ae0kfvra9@4ax.com> <9d6b6e$1bt$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87snihxiwc.fsf@frown.here> <9dbi83$sji$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87heyu7cqd.fsf@frown.here> <9dc20p$hh15e$1@ID-37382.news.dfncis.de> <sashank.varma-0905011801100001@129.59.212.53> <9dclnt$9ic1@news.cis.okstate.edu> <3B02C05B.BA4B63C6@raytheon.com> <slrn9g5hdr.asg.stephan@pcrm.win.tue.nl>`

```


stephan@pcrm.win.tue.nl wrote:

> I definitely nominate COBOL. It will have the added advantage that
> whatever comes next will look as an improvement. (OK, there's still
> INTERCAL).

I assume this was a joke.   In reality, there are still a lot of people successfully
developing software with COBOL.  Moreover,   the current COBOL standard,
and its planned successor, are both quite easy for applications developers to
learn and use.  One of COBOL's virtues has been that, in large corporations,
people who understand the problem domain have been able to transition to
the role of programmer and create solutions.   Typically, these people have
little training in computer science.  They are able to learn on the job.

I have seen a lot of excellent COBOL programmers who have made the
leap from clerical jobs to programming jobs and who have done really
good work.   Also, COBOL is not going away anytime soon, contrary
to popular opinion.    And it is still better for business data processing
applications than C++, any day of the week.   I would have to say it is
better for most of these kinds of applications than Perl or Scheme, Smalltalk
or even Java.    Ada might be the exception because it has a built-in decimal
type and a well-defined Information Systems Annex.    However, I don't
see Ada replacing COBOL anytime soon any more than I see any of those
other languages replacing it.   COBOL continues to evolve and improve,
but few in the programming community keep up-to-date about those
changes.  So many of them wrote it off long ago that they don't know
how it has improved over what it once was.    It is much like those who
still criticize Ada based on the old Ada 83 standard or Smalltalk based
on an outdated view of that language.   Hmmmm. Maybe Ruby will do. :-)

Richard Riehle
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner's Language?

- **From:** ebfg01@bluewin.ch (Marie-No���lle Baechler)
- **Date:** 2001-05-21T23:30:51+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.ada,comp.lang.lisp,comp.lang.smalltalk,comp.lang.basic,comp.lang.functional,comp.lang.scheme,comp.lang.perl,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns90A8E9104ebfg01bluewinch@195.186.4.29>`
- **References:** `<9cukad$nn68@news-dxb> <q9n6ft0pbn903d5gqqbjfjns0ae0kfvra9@4ax.com> <9d6b6e$1bt$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87snihxiwc.fsf@frown.here> <9dbi83$sji$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87heyu7cqd.fsf@frown.here> <9dc20p$hh15e$1@ID-37382.news.dfncis.de> <sashank.varma-0905011801100001@129.59.212.53> <9dclnt$9ic1@news.cis.okstate.edu> <3B02C05B.BA4B63C6@raytheon.com> <slrn9g5hdr.asg.stephan@pcrm.win.tue.nl> <3B08965C.6F34D5C9@ix.netcom.com>`

```
>I assume this was a joke.   In reality, there are still a lot of people
>successfully developing software with COBOL.  Moreover,   the current
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>science.  They are able to learn on the job. 

In my employer, a teaching hospital, COBOL was still very important 
a few years ago, but it will have disappeared by January 1, 2002. 
The only system still using it, our patient management system will 
be replaced at that time.

Here, COBOL is disappearing because informations systems based on 
relational databases are used for all the major tasks. We also tend 
to rely on packages, we try to minimize local adaptations and they 
are developped as specific extensions by our suppliers.

Local extensions of our major information systems are mostly very
specific queries and they are developped in ORACLE SQL. We have a
few programs developped in PL/SQL.

Some of our large packages are not yet client/server. When they 
are, the client is developped in C++ (with one major exception 
using UNIFACE). A few less important applications have been 
developped as Intranet applications in ORACLE PL/SQL. Small 
specific client/server applications have been developped with 
a VB client.

So, for us, COBOL is finally disappearing. But it took 10 more
years than what I expected.

Marie-Noelle Baechler
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Beginner's Language?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-22T17:38:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0AA48E.E8650E3F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9cukad$nn68@news-dxb> <q9n6ft0pbn903d5gqqbjfjns0ae0kfvra9@4ax.com> <9d6b6e$1bt$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87snihxiwc.fsf@frown.here> <9dbi83$sji$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87heyu7cqd.fsf@frown.here> <9dc20p$hh15e$1@ID-37382.news.dfncis.de> <sashank.varma-0905011801100001@129.59.212.53> <9dclnt$9ic1@news.cis.okstate.edu> <3B02C05B.BA4B63C6@raytheon.com> <slrn9g5hdr.asg.stephan@pcrm.win.tue.nl> <3B08965C.6F34D5C9@ix.netcom.com> <Xns90A8E9104ebfg01bluewinch@195.186.4.29>`

```


"Marie-Noï¿½lle Baechler" wrote:

>
> In my employer, a teaching hospital, COBOL was still very important
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Marie-Noelle Baechler

For those who pooh-pooh the idea that other languages are having an impact on
COBOL - here is a typical unsolicited case. It doesn't matter what the
reasons were for 'dropping' COBOL and whether or not they are valid, the fact
is it is happening.

We are well aware that what is being described above can be EASILY handled in
COBOL - but at some point in their design cycle, COBOL was seen to be
deficient. (Could it be that somewhere in the cycle when he refers to  C++,
that COBOL was seen to be lacking with regard to GUIs ?)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-f0c5c55c60"></a>
## Thread: Do you have Standards Committee in your language? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Do you have Standards Committee in your language?

- **From:** Just_Curious <Just_Curious@my-deja.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-21T14:54:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c,comp.lang.clu,comp.lang.cplu,comp.lang.crass,comp.lang.eiffel,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B098EA9.B3895E50@my-deja.net>`

```
All,

Thinking about role of Standards Committee in my favorite
language (Fortran) I decided to hear advice from folks in other
languages first.

What is the role of Your Language committee?

Does Your Committee expand horizons and enlightens compiler
developers with their recommendations? Or opposite,
not being creative, they are working like brake for language
development and experimenting and in fact are negative among
users?

Do you think that compiler vendors and users themselves are not
able to maintain backward compatibility without such committees?

And in general, do you see some analogies between Language Committees
and other regulations in other areas of real life ?

I think these questions are of common interest.
This is post in several language compiler newsgroups,
hence please mention your language unless you specially
don't want this. You may find responds on other languages
searching Deja with subject as above

cheers
```

## Thread: COBOL and my college — 1 this month / 33 total

_Thread started [2001-03](2001-03.md#t-d1b9d27fdd)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and my college

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-05-21T23:13:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0105212213.7962a920@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20010329172002.28981.00000810@ng-ft1.aol.com> <XLME6.1127$yW2.16942@ord-read.news.verio.net> <%KNE6.258306$3J6.17096142@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
I've been programming for about 25 years now, and my secret to keeping
down the number of errors is that I keep the paper copy of the
Programmers' Reference Manual open on my desk, and the Programmers'
Guide on the shelf.  If I am not absolutely positive of the syntax, I
look it up before I write it.

I hope your college teaches about structured walkthru's .... when you
find a shop that does them, you will be in heaven.  You will always
make errors; the trick is to learn from them.

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
732-549-8965

PS: YOU can join the COBOL Standards Committee and make a difference!
```

"Patho" <mark.roosen@advalvas.be> wrote in message
news:990529621.192665@seven.kulnet.kuleuven.ac.be...
> how ???
> with the software " AcuCobol " from acucorp

It's not possible. AcuCobol requires a runtime. Either as an .EXE-file or as
a .DLL-file.

<-emo->
```

- **From:** Nestor Aragon <naragon@lea.mpsc.mot.com>

```
Does anybody outside there works with Hewlett Packard Micro Focus COBOL
for UNIX.?
Does anyone of you knows where to find or to obtain support for this
matter?

thanks in advance
Nestor Aragon
```

#### ↳ Re: HP Micro Focus COBOL/UX

- **From:** "William Wood" <beavis27@nospambigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T21:41:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xCzQ6.45147$9D5.2641452@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3B0A7010.7913C702@lea.mpsc.mot.com>`

```
We have MF COBOL running on HP UNIX macines, but we are currently phasing it
out.  We use MFCOBOL 1.3 which has not been supported for something like 8
years.  I don't know if they currently market for that OS anymore.  I think
your best bet is to go out to their site and send someone an e-mail asking
them if they support it.

Bill

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HP Micro Focus COBOL/UX

- **From:** Nestor Aragon <naragon@lea.mpsc.mot.com>

- **References:** `<3B0A7010.7913C702@lea.mpsc.mot.com> <xCzQ6.45147$9D5.2641452@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3B1389B6.E3DE05A5@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Thank you, both, I visited Merant website, and I found they give support by
contract, unfortunately we did not buy this support when we bought COBOL.
It happens that I just solve the problem we had, but I wanted to ask if this is a
COBOL fault.
When making calculations (compute, add, substract) between variables of different
lenghts it gives an execution error like "trying to access beyond the memory
boundaries", for example:
a pic 9(4); b pic 9(8).
substract 1 from a giving b
it should never give an error, but it does.
I solved the problem addign a pivot variable ie. c pic (4)
substract 1 from a giving c
move c to b    and it works!!
in other cases compute b=a*1000, for example, then I made c pic(8) and following
move a to c
compute b=c*1000, it works too!!, but the problem is that I have about 2 Megalines
of COBOL code.
of course those examples are the easiest I had, but with more complicated
sentences the thing goes worst.

Nestor aragon



Richard Plinston wrote:

> William Wood wrote:
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> >   HP 9000 Series 700 & 800    HP-UX 10.20 (6)   4.1.30
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HP Micro Focus COBOL/UX

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T21:29:51+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Have you checked that these variables do not contain space characters as
you are using numericdisplay formats ? Another thing to check is any
REDEFINES statements within your code. I found a compier bug in an old
version of MF WB for UNIX where an "access beyond memory" message
appeared when opening files within an EVALUATE statement :(

Sorry, I forgot to add the pic's are 9(4) comp and 9(8) comp. Those are only
numeric variables. Maybe it has to be with something of what you say, because
those errors are reached when calling procedures and then making calculations
between variables and parameters, as far as I have detected. I can tell you
that I did not write the code, I am mantaining it, and it makes a little harder
the task.
Anyway I think that it has to be a complier bug. I even was looking for some
switches which activated some arithmetic or so, but I found none.
Nestor

When you want to edit several languages, from HTML to COBOL, Multiedit can't
be beat.  It is a totally configurable editor with swappable configurations
already written for almost all major languages.  It understands columns, can
colorcode  keywords and constructs.  It's awsome.

For older versions of MultiEdit, you can get the COBOL macros for free at
www.progeni.com.
The latest version of MultiEdit comes with the Progeni COBOL macros already
installed.
```

"nnrp-corp.news.cais.net" wrote:

> When you want to edit several languages, from HTML to COBOL, Multiedit can't
> be beat.  It is a totally configurable editor with swappable configurations
> already written for almost all major languages.  It understands columns, can
> colorcode  keywords and constructs.  It's awsome.

I loved it before Windows took over, when I could enter ME file.ext and it knew
where to find file.ext.    One nice thing about ME is that when you download an
upgrade, you can keep the SETUP.EXE file.   What's so nice about this?   If you
upgrade computers, you can install your version of Windows, copy your MEW
directories across, and then run SETUP to set up the registry and desktop
settings.   The basic design of Windows and Windows products seems to be based
upon the assumption that you will never migrate an application from one computer
to another or even between two versions of Windows on the same computer.

I suppose ME's design makes it easier to steal, but it definitely makes it
easier for honest people as well.
```

The last release of the 'real' SPF-PC by Command Technology was 4.0.
It came on installation diskettes with versions for both DOS and OS/2.
I am still using it under OS/2 and Windows/NT with no problems. If you
can find a copy of it available anywhere, go for it. However, I doubt
that Command Technology is still willing to sell this version, so you
will have to find it elsewhere.

The reason I say 'real' SPF-PC is that their newest version actually
just vaguely resembles SPF-PC and in fact has some sort of corruption
of the product name in an attempt to convey this. In my opinion, they
should have just come up with another name and sold the new product as
just another Windows/?? GUI editor, which is what it is. It doesn't
even support REXX as the scripting language.

Regards,
Jay Moseley, CCP

## Thread: Ready For This? Favorable COBOL Press From Computerworld! — 3 msgs

### Ready For This? Favorable COBOL Press From Computerworld!

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 2001-05-23T06:51:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010523025104.15626.00001574@ng-fo1.aol.com>`

```
--> Snipped From
http://computerworld.com/cwi/story/0,1199,NAV47_STO60683,00.html

WILLIAM M. ULRICH

Remember Cobol? If You Don't, Get Reacquainted
  
When I mention the word Cobol to IT people, they look at me as if I just awoke
from a 20-year coma. Many IT professionals consider Cobol, like Latin, to be a
dead language. But rumors of Cobol's death have been greatly exaggerated.
Companies can't ignore their Cobol software assets and need to incorporate them
into their IT strategies. 

IT people treat Cobol like a pariah. Most universities have dropped it from
their curricula. Vendors shun the word Cobol in their marketing literature,
even when Cobol tools are responsible for much of their revenue. Programmers
who know Cobol tend to de-emphasize it on their resumes. 

In spite of its reputation, Cobol remains a resilient force in IT. Dale
Vecchio, research director at Gartner Inc., says there are roughly 180 billion
lines of Cobol worldwide. This isn't surprising, given that Cobol has been
around for more than 40 years. What is surprising is Gartner's comment in a
February research note stating that 15% of all new application functionality
through 2005 will be in Cobol. 

In the same research note, Gartner stated that 80% of all deployed applications
through 2004 will include legacy extensions. These extensions involve Web-based
applications triggering legacy system transactions that perform operational
business functions and access legacy databases. In January's issue of "The
Cobol Report," Scott Ankrum wrote that CICS transaction volume grew from 20
billion a day in 1998 to 30 billion a day this year. This 50% growth in
mainframe usage has largely been driven by Web-based systems accessing back-end
Cobol applications. 
...
• Protect your Cobol talent. The number of Cobol programmers is shrinking,
with many headed for retirement. Consider bonus programs for them. 
...
• Recognize the need for new Cobol skills to upgrade existing systems and
build new functionality. You may need to enhance in-house training programs and
rethink hiring plans. 

IT may need to launch an awareness campaign to gain funding support for these
action items. One way to do this is to remind management that the replacement
cost of these systems, conservatively estimated at $25 per line of code, is
typically in the billions of dollars. 

Cobol has outlived newer programming languages, hardware platforms, operating
systems and Y2k. It's not going away and must be leveraged to meet strategic
business requirements in a timely and effective manner. So, revisit the role
Cobol plays in your corporate IT plans.
```

#### ↳ Re: Ready For This? Favorable COBOL Press From Computerworld!

- **From:** "nnrp-corp.news.cais.net" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T10:35:19-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ej677$839$1@allthetime.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<20010523025104.15626.00001574@ng-fo1.aol.com>`

```
Thanks for pointing this out.  I'm seeing more and more positive press on
COBOL.  It's amazing how the pendulum swings.
I think when the web first hit with a vengance, COBOL compilers and
Enterprise level systems in general weren't ready for web related
technologies.  And since the mission critical nature of those systems
require very long, slow and carefully planned and executed changes - the
Mainframe technologies couldn't keep up with the "apparent" pace of the
"just build something, throw it on the web and let the end user test it"
Web.

Now, however, companies and developers are discovering that:
1. Mainframe technologies and especially COBOL have caught up - and can
integrate and even drive newer technologies.  In fact, some notable dot com
failures have taught the industry that there is no substitute for careful
planning and design, production control, and (horrors) testing!  We have
also learned that when you need to process a huge volume of transactions
behind that web site - there is no substitute for big iron.
2. We have returned to the old principle of "use the best tools for the
job."  If you want to write event driven code, such as an operating system -
use C++.  But if you want to write a business applications that can be
easily understood and maintained for generations, use COBOL!

And if you want to move from an older, expensive to maintain Mainframe to a
newer midrange or even super Micro system - why recode.  COBOL actually runs
under UNIX and NT.  AMAZING but TRUE!  And a code migration to a new
platform is nearly always MUCH cheaper than recoding.

In fact, we have a good case study coming up in next weeks issue of
www.aboutlegacycoding.com about an insurance company that did just that.

Pardon the rant - I know I'm preaching to the Choir here *grin*
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ready For This? Favorable COBOL Press From Computerworld!

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T09:11:27-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0D249E.D6174DFE@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<20010523025104.15626.00001574@ng-fo1.aol.com> <9ej677$839$1@allthetime.news.cais.net>`

```


"nnrp-corp.news.cais.net" wrote:

> Thanks for pointing this out.  I'm seeing more and more positive press on
> COBOL.  It's amazing how the pendulum swings.
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pardon the rant - I know I'm preaching to the Choir here *grin*

Also, a lot of early movement away from CoBOL was based upon theory.  In theory
a big integrated system has tremendous efficiencies.  But in practice, the same
things that makes it undesirable in governments, make it costly in computer
systems.  Stand alone applications might not automatically upgrade when the
enterprise changes direction - but they won't fail big time when the everything
isn't working correctly.
```

### A very strange affair indeed? OK I NEED YOU EXPERT COBOL PROGRAMMERS TO HELP ME :oP

- **From:** dan.theman@genie.co.uk (CoBoL CoNfUzEs mE)
- **Date:** 2001-05-23T02:57:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0e9ca5c.0105230157.52107d5c@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi 

I am writing a datre validation program for my college everything was
going well but i need to validate the string that is entered (the
date) is 12 or 13 characters
for example

jan 21st 2000
(13 chars)
jan 1st 2000
(12 chars)

I dont know of any length checking teqniques appart from inspect
tallying

this wil be hard because of the spaces in the date

i guess if their was a way to check from the right that would be fine

my date string has a maximum of 80 chars

Please help soon I am going to cry my work has to be in on friday

thankyou

You guys on this group have helped me out once before and I only got
flamed once

(good for a newsgroup I guess)

im not lazy this is a minor part of my project and I just can't get my
head round it

Dan

:o)
```

#### ↳ Re: A very strange affair indeed? OK I NEED YOU EXPERT COBOL PROGRAMMERS TO HELP ME :oP

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-05-23T11:10:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010523071050.27474.00000963@ng-xb1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<b0e9ca5c.0105230157.52107d5c@posting.google.com>`

```
>From: dan.theman@genie.co.uk  (CoBoL CoNfUzEs mE)
>Date: 2001-05-23 05:57 Eastern Daylight Time

>jan 21st 2000
>(13 chars)
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>I guess if there was a way to check from the right that would be fine

>my date string has a maximum of 80 chars

80 seems awfully long...  
In any case, you declare a variable DATE-FIELD of 80 chars.  
Then you redefine it as DATE-ARRAY, an array of 80 1-byte fields. 
Move the input date to DATE-FIELD
Then you can 
(A) set the index to 80 
     see if the 80th byte of DATE-ARRAY is a space.
     if it is, stop
     if it isn't, decrement the index by 1
     etc.
(B) inspect the DATE-FIELD looking for the first occurrence of
two spaces.  
If the input date is coming from a human, this may not
always work. 


Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

#### ↳ Re: A very strange affair indeed? OK I NEED YOU EXPERT COBOL PROGRAMMERS TO HELP ME :oP

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-05-23T10:39:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0BD9BD.3D6977C2@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<b0e9ca5c.0105230157.52107d5c@posting.google.com>`

```
CoBoL CoNfUzEs mE wrote:

> Hi
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Seems to me that you need to do some work on the input of this, to
guarantee that the several parts of the date are to be found in absolutely
definite parts of the string.

I.E.: the month name or abbr is in the first 5 positions, the numeric part
of the day in positions 6 & 7, etc.

Otherwise you are going to have to contend with unlimited variations and
your solution (which can be implemented using UNSTRING etc. as already
suggested) will never work for everything.
```

#### ↳ Re: A very strange affair indeed? OK I NEED YOU EXPERT COBOL PROGRAMMERS TO HELP ME :oP

```
I would parse the input string 1 character at a time. I never use the
INSPECT verb and stay away from the STRING / UNSTRING
commands as more efficient code can be generated coding it the
long way.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A very strange affair indeed? OK I NEED YOU EXPERT COBOL PROGRAMMERS TO HELP ME :oP

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-23T21:53:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WjWO6.43609$T7.3526895@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b0e9ca5c.0105230157.52107d5c@posting.google.com> <9eh44l$ned$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```

"Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:9eh44l$ned$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk...
> I would parse the input string 1 character at a time. I never use
the
> INSPECT verb and stay away from the STRING / UNSTRING
> commands as more efficient code can be generated coding it the
> long way.

Your point has merit in some instances (one star). But,

Whether he uses UNSTRING, subscripts, ALTER GO TO's or calls a
different DLL for each character, the program will finish his 80-byte
input field before the operator can remove her finger from the ENTER
key. (Well, maybe I exaggerate a little).

In my view, coding for execution efficiency to validate operator input
is inefficient (and most other places as well).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A very strange affair indeed? OK I NEED YOU EXPERT COBOL PROGRAMMERS TO HELP ME :oP

- **From:** Nestor Aragon <naragon@lea.mpsc.mot.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T13:10:03+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0CEC0B.C4757E07@lea.mpsc.mot.com>`
- **References:** `<b0e9ca5c.0105230157.52107d5c@posting.google.com> <9eh44l$ned$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
      *    Left Justify
      *
      *        This subroutine left justifies a string by deleting
      *    leading spaces, underlines and binary zeros.
      *        Any underlines, binary zeros or blanks imbedded in
      *    the string are also deleted.

       environment division.
       configuration section.


       input-output section.
       file-control.

      /
       data division.
       working-storage section.



      *local-storage section.

       01  input-subscript                   pic 9(4) comp.
       01  output-subscript                  pic 9(4) comp.

       linkage section.

       01  input-data.
          05  input-datax                    pic x(01)
                                             occurs 9999 times.
       01  input-data-length                 pic 9(8) comp.

       procedure division  using  input-data,
                                 input-data-length.

          if  input-data-length  not >  zero
              go to   0010-exit.

          move  +1  to  input-subscript.
          move  +1  to  output-subscript.

       0010-loop.
          if  input-subscript  >  input-data-length
              go to  0010-exit.

          if  ( input-datax (input-subscript) = space )
              add  +1  to  input-subscript
              go to  0010-loop.

          if  ( input-datax (input-subscript) = '_' )
          or  ( input-datax (input-subscript) = low-values )
              move  space  to  input-datax (input-subscript)
              add  +1      to  input-subscript
              go to  0010-loop.

          if  input-subscript  =  output-subscript
              add  +1  to  input-subscript
              add  +1  to  output-subscript
              go to  0010-loop.

          move  input-datax (input-subscript)  to
                input-datax (output-subscript).
          move  space  to  input-datax (input-subscript).
          add  +1      to  input-subscript.
          add  +1      to  output-subscript.
          go to  0010-loop.

       0010-exit.
          subtract 1 from output-subscript giving input-data-length.
        exit program.


Alister Munro wrote:

> I would parse the input string 1 character at a time. I never use the
> INSPECT verb and stay away from the STRING / UNSTRING
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> > :o)
```

#### ↳ Re: A very strange affair indeed? OK I NEED YOU EXPERT COBOL PROGRAMMERS TO HELP ME :oP

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-23T21:48:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KeWO6.43529$T7.3520924@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b0e9ca5c.0105230157.52107d5c@posting.google.com>`

```
Easy way:

01 DATE-DECODE.
  02  DATE-DECODE-MONTH PIC X(10).
  02  DATE-DECODE-DAY PIC X(10).
  02  DATE-DECODE-YEAR  PIC X(10).

MOVE SPACE TO DATE-DECODE.
UNSTRING INPUT-STRING DELIMITED BY ALL ' ' INTO
    DATE-DECODE-MONTH
    DATE-DECODE-DAY
    DATE-DECODE-YEAR.

(validate month)
(validate day)
(validate year)

STOP RUN.

"CoBoL CoNfUzEs mE" <dan.theman@genie.co.uk> wrote in message
news:b0e9ca5c.0105230157.52107d5c@posting.google.com...

>

was

fine

my

#### ↳ Re: A very strange affair indeed? OK I NEED YOU EXPERT COBOL PROGRAMMERS TO HELP ME :oP

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T18:03:38-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ep8nr$23jk$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<b0e9ca5c.0105230157.52107d5c@posting.google.com>`

```
Dan,

Sometimes it's easier to think backwards on problems such as these.  And
believe it or not, one COBOL function in a Unisys V-Series brings the date
back in exactly this format, with commas, and times to boot.  However,
certain things are fixed, i.e. year is 4, alpha month is always three, etc.
so if you start from the back and move towards the front checking for
numeric in key places, these fields can be moved without a lot of stringing,
unstringing etc.

Think about it a bit, and we can help further if need be.

Denny

"CoBoL CoNfUzEs mE" <dan.theman@genie.co.uk> wrote in message
news:b0e9ca5c.0105230157.52107d5c@posting.google.com...
> Hi
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> :o)
```

## Thread: Reverse Engineering Cobol Apps — 22 msgs

### Reverse Engineering Cobol Apps

- **From:** steven_jones@nas.adp.com (Steve Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-05-23T12:09:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f2b3dd25.0105231109.6d1b6ec@posting.google.com>`

```
We have a Cobol application that will be rewritten in Java ... be this
good, bad, or ugly ... the decision has been made and there is no
talking us out of it !
  
Our first step is to reverse engineer the Cobol application such that
the Java engineers can use the output of this effort as the input to
their endeavor.  Our application is currently in Micro Focus Cobol v4
... on Win95, NT, and Unix.

Is anyone aware of a good (or half-way descent) tool that we can use
to run against this application and produce some sort of output ...
diagrams, functional decomps, whatever ... that might be either
useable as specs for the Java guys or that we could use as the first
step toward producing something that they can work from.

Much Thanks !

-Dr. Jones
```

#### ↳ Re: Reverse Engineering Cobol Apps

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-23T14:30:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9eh33q$npd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<f2b3dd25.0105231109.6d1b6ec@posting.google.com>`

```
I have heard of some "medium poor"products that attempt to do this for IBM
mainframe COBOL programs - but none for PC products. If your program (which
is pretty unusual for a production Micro Focus program) actually sticks to
"ANSI" COBOL or IBM compatible COBOL, then you COULD try porting it to that
environment and use one of those tools.

As others will probably respond to this, the only semi-reliable "tool" for
doing this (usually) is a knowledgeable COBOL programmer.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reverse Engineering Cobol Apps

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-23T22:44:44+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sv3ogt80cc10boksdpktiil2v69aegon1u@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<f2b3dd25.0105231109.6d1b6ec@posting.google.com>`

```
On 23 May 2001 12:09:09 -0700 steven_jones@nas.adp.com (Steve Jones) wrote:

:>We have a Cobol application that will be rewritten in Java ... be this
:>good, bad, or ugly ... the decision has been made and there is no
:>talking us out of it !
  
:>Our first step is to reverse engineer the Cobol application such that
:>the Java engineers can use the output of this effort as the input to
:>their endeavor.  Our application is currently in Micro Focus Cobol v4
:>... on Win95, NT, and Unix.

Why would you take that approach?

Why not determine the business function that the application performs and then
have the Java coders write the application?

As COBOL and Java are quite different, merely translating the code will create
a program that neither COBOL nor Java programmers will be able to reasonably
maintain. 

:>Is anyone aware of a good (or half-way descent) tool that we can use
:>to run against this application and produce some sort of output ...
:>diagrams, functional decomps, whatever ... that might be either
:>useable as specs for the Java guys or that we could use as the first
:>step toward producing something that they can work from.

Determine the business function and start from scratch.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reverse Engineering Cobol Apps

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-05-23T23:12:47+01:00

- **Message-ID:** `<OlQspHBfXDD7IwHB@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`

```
In article <f2b3dd25.0105231109.6d1b6ec@posting.google.com>, Steve Jones 
<steven_jones@nas.adp.com> writes
>We have a Cobol application that will be rewritten in Java ... be this
>good, bad, or ugly ... the decision has been made and there is no
>talking us out of it !

Interesting........ I'd love to hear the reasoning behind this if you 
have the time.

>
>Our first step is to reverse engineer the Cobol application such that
>the Java engineers can use the output of this effort as the input to
>their endeavor.  Our application is currently in Micro Focus Cobol v4
>... on Win95, NT, and Unix.

Probably a poor strategy. If you're going to throw away the investment 
you have in your business logic, you need to do it 'properly'.......

>
>Is anyone aware of a good (or half-way descent) tool that we can use
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>step toward producing something that they can work from.

In this context "something they can work from" is a functional 
specification. Don't be too surprised if a "Java guy" doesn't recognise 
one. Even if it does bite him / her in the ass. ;^)

I really can't offer much help (or indeed, hope!). I'd really like to 
hear how the project works out though. Really.

Regards

Nigel
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reverse Engineering Cobol Apps

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@ont.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T14:37:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> Another suggestion - go to http://www.legacyj.com and just recompile
> the program and get a .java file out of it.
> 

Thane, 
I did that, just to see what would happen. It worked, but it's not 
native JAVA (I think). There is a run-time fee, but much less than 
some COBOL. I thought it would be easier to port to java, and 
distribute, but the run-time looks about like COBOL in size.

I wrote a cross-reference program for an 8051 assembler and it did 
work. It ran a little slower than the MF Personal COBOL, comparing 
it to compiled code didn't seem to be fair...


Here is some of the generated code-just in case anyone is interested.
You don't have to touch it, but I thought COBOL was the "long-winded"
language. (Well, the new breed of "software engineers" say that!)
----------------------------------------
// Import Java Packages

import com.synkronix.cobol.*; // main import
import java.util.*;
import java.sql.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.applet.*;

// Data Division
    static // initializer
    {
        Variable.validate("9JJALL0KYXJL7VCP5Z","PERCobol(TM) COBOL
runtimes Copyright 1996-2001 LegacyJ Corp.");
    }
    public class Fil implements Section
    {
        // Create Variables
        public final Variable in_line_OF_input_record_OF_input_file=new
Variable();
        public final Variable in_chars_OF_input_record_OF_input_file=new
Variable();
        public final Variable input_record_OF_input_file=new Variable();
        public final Variable in_symbol_OF_sort_record_OF_sort_file=new
Variable();
        public final Display in_num_OF_sort_record_OF_sort_file=new
Display();
        public final Variable type_flag_OF_sort_record_OF_sort_file=new
Variable();
        public final Variable sort_record_OF_sort_file=new Variable();
        public final Variable p_line_OF_giving_record_OF_giving_file=new
Variable();
----------------------------------------

The procedure code is not that hard to walk though, but I'm glad I
don't have to try to "maintain" code like this. Again, you don't have
to touch the generated code, it does work, and the run-time is 
cheaper than the COBOL ones being *bitched* about in this group.

So, it does work as advertised. Like any generated language, names are
llllllloooooooonnnnnnnggggggg, and I would pity anyone who tryed to 
keep them! However, even changing the names to shorter ones would still
leave the problem of the run-times hiding much of the COBOL 
built-in capabilities, and would not be *pure* java programming. 

Anyone trying to convert COBOL to java would be better off using
PERCobol as it is, because it will stil be COBOL. I talked to the
Sales Manager, and BlueJ is what they say to use for new, or 
graphical type development, but again-still COBOL...

Gary

By the way, I know enough about java to spell it ;^)

cases
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reverse Engineering Cobol Apps

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T21:53:47+00:00

```

"gary drummond" <gdrumm@ont.com

> Anyone trying to convert COBOL to java would be better off using
> PERCobol as it is, because it will stil be COBOL. I talked to the
> Sales Manager, and BlueJ is what they say to use for new, or
> graphical type development, but again-still COBOL...
>

So you're saying: Keep and maintain the COBOL source but instead of
compiling it, PerCOBOL it?

Well, why the hell not?

After all, a COBOL compiler simply converts COBOL source into cryptic
assembly/object which virtually nobody looks at anyway. Same could be
true for the java output of PerCOBOL. Just gotta keep the Java
programmers away from the 'executable(?)' code. Better still, don't
have any Java programmers in the building.

Get the best of both worlds: Business logic and investment in
experience with the COBOL and the latest/greatest fad with Java. Works
for me.

P.S.,

I made a similar suggestion to George McGovern many years ago on
school busing. My plan was to send children to the school closest to
their home. BUT the kids would have to take a two-hour bus trip
through a neighborhood of differing ethnic makeup to and from school.

McGovern never acknowledged my suggestion.
```

But the original thread was about developing/converting to java. Leaving

management-types who want someone to say "Yep, it's a JAVA app now!".
Now-the BlueJ environment sounds OK. A graphical screen, a buzz word
enjoyed by many a manager, but supported in COBOL code, THAT sounds like
something worth following up on, because you're not hiding the fact that
it's all in COBOL. Flexus' SP2, which I'll get to test when I get my new
CD, sounds just as good, if not better.

See my earlier post on "Internet Enabled COBOL - NO! You can't because
it works!", because it (tongue-in-cheek?) states the "real" reasons
COBOL is "BAD", at least in the opinion of the current
software-engineer/CTO/PM/Acadamia-nut  people. For some reason they
don't understand the world of data processing, which I thought was to
develop applications to do your business, following the business rules
required, by designing/coding something that can be maintained over a
long period of time.

Sorry about that, but that little third-party viewpoint summed up my
feeling for the last 5-6 years. There are times to use the newer
technology, but just "because it's there" is not a good reason. There
are a LOT of dot.com's who "could" have told you why...
>

programmer.

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

to close ALL the schools in the "wrong areas", and bus the ones that
needed to get to school. I am not embarrassed to admit that it worked
out much better when they let the kids pick their high school. They
created "magnet" schools, with special funding and teachers. My sons
picked the engineering/math schools, and did *much* better than I did.
So-I've been wrong, and I will be again... (BUT NOT NOW! ;^)

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reverse Engineering Cobol Apps

- **From:** "Charles F. Townsend" <ctowns@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T08:08:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B13BB68.88C2A241@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<f2b3dd25.0105231109.6d1b6ec@posting.google.com> <3b0c92ca.5476205@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane,
Actually, LegacyJ PERCobol currently allows the user to see the
"intermediate" .java but the executable .class file is the generated result
of the compile.  PERCobol runtimes permit the executables to continue to
perform or behave as a COBOL including and will reading and writing COBOL
files.  LegacyJ has spent some time thinking about deploying extensive
runtime libraries with individual applications and determined that COBOL
programmers needed a way to "deploy" only those runtime elements that are
needed by the COBOL program.  Now we include with PERCobol on all graphical
platforms a graphical deploy tool that creates and executable .jar file
containing the application, runtime and other program elements necessary
for program execution.

The PERCobol COBOL Language manuals are available with the product or
viewed from the LegacyJ website at http://www.legacyj.com/perc_doc.html

Charles Townsend
LegacyJ
http://www.legacyj.com

Thane Hubbell wrote:

> Another suggestion - go to http://www.legacyj.com and just recompile
> the program and get a .java file out of it.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> >-Dr. Jones
```

```
Hi Steve,

     I work in a COBOL Shop, and we outsourced our web development, and werent
very happy with it. (was written in Java)   We found some software out there
that allowed us to "web enable" or "internet enable" our COBOL programs.
     We are using Fujitsu 5.0 (32 bit compiler) , Flexus SP2 (screen drivers),
and Flexus Thin Client.  
    We waited a year for the web company to deliver our web site, and it really
sucked wind to put it nicely.  We had our cobol program up and running in under
2 months.  If you are a COBOL shop, it is definatly the way to go.  We are very
very excited about it.  check out www.Flexus.com & read up on it.  
     They offer a demo version (intact), and full support during demo.  They
always responded within an hour or 2.
     Java is not meant for business applications, we had a ton of problems with
our relative simple program.  Any questions feel free to ask. but again, Flexus
offers full support, and they are more then happy to help.  

John
```

#### ↳ Re: RM/COBOL-85 v2.01

- **From:** Michael Allen <m.allen@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T15:32:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0D6FFA.752CA21B@liant.com>`

```
The current version (7.0) of the RM/COBOL User's Guide is available in PDF
format on the Liant web site here:

http://www.liant.com/docs/

This is 32 bit Windows documentation, but much of the information is still
applicable.

As for why the your DOS box is closing, I'm not really sure what's going on.
How are you invoking the compiler?  Are you using Windows' Explorer and simply
clicking on the file rmcobol.exe, or are you typing rmcobol.exe from a already
open DOS box (recommended)?

Certainly try using the "l" option to cause the compiler to create a listing
file.  This would at least give you the ability to capture the error(s) or
message(s) for viewing.  You could also contact Liant at 1-800-RM-COBOL - I'm
sure sales would be able to help you purchase a manual or upgrade.

Hope this helps,
-Michael

<a id="t-7c4ec9b8b0"></a>
## Thread: RM/COBOL85 PROBLEM? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### RM/COBOL85 PROBLEM?

- **From:** "������������������������ ���������������������" <dksoft@otenet.gr>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T09:54:45+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9eiau6$o86$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```
Running an application in Rm/Cobol with Runtime Dos 10 users and operating
system Ms-Win 2000 Professional or
win 2000 Server i receive a message (COBOL ERROR 30,MS-DOS ERROR 87) when i
print to a share printer of another machine.

The printer name can be LPT1(normal execution) ,
LPT3(ubnormal execution),\\machine name\share printer name(ubnormal
execution).

Can anybody solve this problem.
Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: RM/COBOL85 PROBLEM?

- **From:** Michael Allen <m.allen@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T15:52:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0D7480.184F33@liant.com>`
- **References:** `<9eiau6$o86$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```
We were able to duplicate your problem, for which we have two potential
solutions:

1) Upgrade to the 32 bit Windows runtime.  The Windows runtime has been
specifically designed and tested for 32 bit Windows operating systems like
Win2K, NT, 9x, etc. - and does not exhibit this behavior.  The current version
is 7.00.03.  See http://www.liant.com/ for more information.

2) The other solution gets a little ugly. Here's how we were able to get the
DOS runtime to print to a networked printer from Windows 2K.  Also note that
this is not specific to 2000, but also fails on NT 4.0.  It does not appear to
fail on 9x.

First, go to Control Panel -> Printers, and select "Add Printer".
I selected a "local" printer - even though eventually, it's won't be.
Now, select an unused "Local Port".  I used LPT3:  I suppose you could use any
one of the unused ports.  You may even be able to create a new one using "Add
Port...". Click "Next", and pick the appropriate printer driver type... then
just follow the prompts until you're done.
After that, open a "Command Prompt" (DOS box), and type "net use LPT3:
\\machine_name\share_printer_name".

In your COBOL program, set your printer name to LPT3: (or use an environment
variable), and you should now be able to print to the device.

Hope that helps,
-Michael

"ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½" wrote:

> Running an application in Rm/Cobol with Runtime Dos 10 users and operating
> system Ms-Win 2000 Professional or
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks
```
