#### ↳ Subroutines in Cobol

- **From:** Philip Erdelsky <PErdelsk@acucorp.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T15:29:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9332A1B4B5CED2119FCE00105AD16A4D1F2288@hubcap.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<RL9P6.75370$ff.580967@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
Cobol isn't very good at subroutines, at least compared to a
language like C or Pascal.

In AcuCobol-GT, you must compile a subroutine separately and
make sure the .ACU file is accessible at run time.

The PERFORM statement can be used in some cases to call what
amounts to an internally-defined subroutine.

> -----Original Message-----
> From: eRRaTiK [mailto:regulate@hotmail.com]
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Subroutines in Cobol

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T17:35:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0105311635.795bd98d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<RL9P6.75370$ff.580967@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <9332A1B4B5CED2119FCE00105AD16A4D1F2288@hubcap.acucorp.com>`

```
Philip Erdelsky <PErdelsk@acucorp.com> wrote 
> Cobol isn't very good at subroutines, at least compared to a
> language like C or Pascal.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> > 
snip
> > ...
> > 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

If you use Nested Programs, they are compiled from a single source
unit into a single run-time unit.

If you use a static call (using the name of the called routine as a
literal directly in the calling program), they are compiled
separately, but link-edited into a single run-time unit.
EXAMPLE: 
            CALL 'SUBPROG1' USING parameter list.

If you use a dynamic call (using a field name containing the name of
the called program, or, in some operating systems, a Link-editor
parameter), they are compiled separately, and the called program must
be available at run time.
EXAMPLE:
       01  CALLED-PROGRAM            PIC X(08).
           -
           -
           MOVE 'SUBPROG1' TO CALLED-PROGRAM.
           CALL CALLED-PROGRAM USING parameter list.
    

The subprogram does not care if it is called statically or
dynamically.

Stephen J Spiro
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subroutines in Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T20:38:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f6rl8$s4u$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<RL9P6.75370$ff.580967@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <9332A1B4B5CED2119FCE00105AD16A4D1F2288@hubcap.acucorp.com> <4145699b.0105311635.795bd98d@posting.google.com>`

```
FYI - the "following" information is absolutely correct in an IBM mainframe
environment - it is NOT a portable definition as the COBOL ANSI/ISO
Standards do *not* have the concept of "static" and "dynamic" CALLs.  In
fact, if you read the "fine print" in the IBM documentation, they do
EXPLICITLY state that when you are using static CALLs, that you are running
in *NON* ANSI conforming mode.

(The reason that using static CALLs is not ANSI conforming is:
  A) ignore the CANCEL statement
  B) may or may not correctly put subprograms into initial state when the
ANSI/ISO Standard requires it)

P.S.  You can tell that the sample code below is NOT ANSI conforming (for
the '85 Standard) as it uses "single quotes' rather than "double quotes".
The draft of the next COBOL Standard *does* (finally) allow for the use of
single quotes (aka apostrophes) in alphanumeric literals - as many (but not
all) implementors have allowed as an extension for YEARS.
```

## Thread: ESQL & ODBC Drivers — 19 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### ESQL & ODBC Drivers

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T16:15:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0D341A.221BF1BB@home.com>`

```
OK - so switch to an RDB using MS Access 2000, (as the data repository)
and use SQL statements in COBOL to retrieve data. Basically I've done
that, figured out syntax, (well almost), but here's the BIG PROBLEM - so
you DB guys, including my mentor down there in NZ - put on your thinking
caps.

Current COBOL file structure :-

\MyApp\General-Data
        Clients.dat
        Descrips.dat

\MyApp\Plant-Data (this is actually subdivided into 'folders' per
client/plant)

        AMC-22-Items.dat
        AMC-22 etc.....

        HAV-01-Items.dat
        HAV-01 etc....

So when we come to Access I replace those General-Data files by "A.mdb",
with its resulting tables.  Similarly I can have a "B.mdb" which is the
master set  to replace
Plant-Data. Note that "A.mdb" is applicable to all "B.mdb" and it just
isn't practical to have one mdb to include tables for A and B.

Now the first problem with having A and B is that I will have, depending
upon Business Logic program sequencing :-

        CONNECT TO A (e.g. to access Client Table)
        DISCONNECT FROM A
        CONNECT TO B (e.g. to access Items Table)
        DISCONNECT FROM B
        CONNECT to A etc.......

A nuisance but it is controllable from COBOL.

User wants a 'new set' of B for Client PAN-45 - using CBL_FILE routines,
copy and rename "B.mdb" to "PAN-45.mdb". Similarly if he had an existing
"PAN-66" zipped and archived, he just retrieves that set.and unzips. (To
illustrate volumes - over a 20 year period, one user has the equivalent
of 700 "B.mdbs". Agreed many of those are now 'defunct' clients, but
nevertheless, although archived, they can be retrieved should he do new
work for a particular client).

Now here's what I see as the BIG PROBLEM - unless I've missed something.
In order to use Embedded SQL with COBOL I must use the Microsoft ODBC
Data Source Administrator - i.e. I must 'register' each mdb so that
COBOL/Windows will function correctly. (PS: I'm using Net Express V
3.1).

I can register "A.mdb" and I can register "B.mdb". But somehow I don't
think I can  register 700 .mdbs for copies of B.mdb - apart from which I
don't want to !!!!!

So given I have a master set "B.mdb", registered in the ODBC driver -
how do I access "ABC-01.mdb", "ABC-33.mdb" etc....... ??????

I can't help but reflect that RDBs are so much easier to use than COBOL
files <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
It supports your application without the necessity for additional tables or

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ESQL & ODBC Drivers

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T04:27:16+00:00

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Jimmy,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> you. If you have difficulty with it, mail me privately.

We can review privately, BUT something which I failed to include in my previous
message. Let's forget my application and use a parallel situation - an
accounting firm that uses a DB to do clients' books. Given that they only have
one DB with a set of tables, this is the scenario for an annual audit cycle :-

Do processing for ABC Corp in May
...........................DEF.............June
...........................GHI ............July

Other than the odd query, which might come up in ensuing months, they are NOT
interested in having the above data gobbling up hard disk space, growing like
Topsy, and the respective set of tables (SEPARATE mdb) per company can be
archived/zipped until required again.

Now in my case the work scenario is roughly a three week cycle - in which they
might be involved with 3-6 plants overlapping the following activities,
recording data, print some reports, quality test, print final reports. Deliver
reports. Send invoice. Get money. Finito. The set for Client A is no longer
required until 12 months time, and may *never* be required again if the
inspection company doesn't get the contract for next year.

The alternative is ONE DB - recall I mentioned a certain US company that is now
part of BP - they got involved in a $2 million fiasco which did EXACTLY that !

You *hate* it if I don't quote you 'exactement'  - so you to me dated 2001 March
4 :-
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

>
>Question - bearing in mind for COBOL files I generate a set for each new
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>                        "c:\ucs\A*****\Pound-Creek\AMC-23-Readings.mdb"


Well, RDB theory says NO...but on this occasion, I'm with you <G>... You
SHOULD build ONE database (many tables) with a key extended to allow it to
support individual ClientFacilities. (This key would be allocated across all
the tables and you could then access data for any or all
Client/Facilities.). Using separate databases is a two-edged sword but,
until you get the hang of it, it is probably safer.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Frankly I think I have to go with the 'two-edged sword' you mention. Reflect -
we are not talking about one DB for a Mom & Pop Retail POS system, but for a
service company that needs SEPARATE data for each client/plant combination.
(Ironically just as you might claim I'm 'steeped' in OO - the same goes for you
- you are 'steeped' in RDBs <G>). Regardless of technology, gotta bend a little,
give a little ......

I'll be interested to see if anyone else suggests 'YOU GOTTA HAVE ONE DATABASE'
- hence my posting here for additional viewpoints.

More - when I send the source.

Jimmy
```

```
Where I work we have "monthly" sets of data where we wish to keep 3
months worth of data.  When we have accumulated 4 months of data, we
simply "drop" the oldest one.  How?  Using Oracle, each month is
inserted into the SAME table, but in a DIFFERENT Oracle Partition.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ESQL & ODBC Drivers

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T01:17:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HAYP6.1993$pa4.262008@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3B0D341A.221BF1BB@home.com> <3b0dceb1_5@Usenet.com> <3B0DDF88.17BF7046@home.com> <3b101d0b.58532792@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:3b101d0b.58532792@news1.attglobal.net...
> Where I work we have "monthly" sets of data where we wish to keep 3
> months worth of data.  When we have accumulated 4 months of data, we
> simply "drop" the oldest one.  How?  Using Oracle, each month is
> inserted into the SAME table, but in a DIFFERENT Oracle Partition.
>

Wouldn't it have been easier to have a "year-month" number in each row? Then
when you want to trash the old stuff, you just delete from thetable where
yearmonth < whatever.

If you don't want to screw up your existing code by having it suck in the
old data if it hasn't been cleaned out yet, you could create a VIEW like...

CREATE VIEW Last_three_months  AS SELECT * FROM thetable where (year_month +
3)
 <= currentyearmonth

If  the name of the table is a problem, I think there are probably ways to
rename the view as the table name; but if you are using stored procedures,
change the table access procedure and poof, you're in like Flint.

No partitions, no screwing around, etc. etc. etc.

MCM
```

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

```
I endorse your solution here 100% Michael and it is the one I would use.

However, I can see the sense in what Thane is saying also. Automatic backups
of ORACLE partitions make it attractive as easily operable and secure.

There is nothing innately "wrong" with either solution.

But using the date as a key on the database has served me well in the past.

Personally, I wouldn't use a stored procedure, but I accept many people find
them useful. (I WOULD use the VIEW you described IF the data was huge...).

It would be quite feasible for Jimmy to use this solution but he is still
thinking in Indexed datasets, therefore, multiple databases...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ESQL & ODBC Drivers

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T23:29:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010527192931.02612.00000252@nso-cu.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3b106e6e_6@Usenet.com>`

```
In article <3b106e6e_6@Usenet.com>, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> writes:

>
>It would be quite feasible for Jimmy to use this solution but he is still
>thinking in Indexed datasets, therefore, multiple databases...
>

Would it not be like adding the Company/client Id as a KEY field?
I imagine that the separate file concept is so that all the previous 
software/utilities continue to work without having to re-compile/re-design
based on altering the file structure itself to include the Company/Client Id.

In my case, financial industry, we are accustomed to linking everything to 
an Institution number before anything else is done.  From that point on 
down the rules for handlnig a transaction/account are governed by the 
parameters/options selected by the Institution to which the account belongs.

Unfortunately, we have yet to fully adopt the 'advantages' of database use.
To date, we still find flat files, ISAM, and thrift index structures provide as
good
if not better performance for most applications.  Database design and access
sets require too much overhead to maintain a high performance ratio.
With DB knowledge limited to the DBA and application developers coding to 
the specifications of a Business analyst that receives no input from the DBA,
products seldom get the best out of a database.
In the case of a one-horse shop, it is entirely possible to have a DB product
turned out that make optimum use of the DB features.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ESQL & ODBC Drivers

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T04:04:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B11CED1.7BECB291@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b106e6e_6@Usenet.com> <20010527192931.02612.00000252@nso-cu.aol.com>`

```


Sff5ky wrote:

> In article <3b106e6e_6@Usenet.com>, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
> <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> writes:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> turned out that make optimum use of the DB features.

Certainly taking Pete's approach having the ClientID column is a MUST so
that your
syntax reads :-

        SELECT *
            INTO :xxx :xxx :xxx etc
        WHERE ClientID = (?) and Key1 = (?) and Key 2 = (?) etc....

**No corrections to above please - just a 'general' example

However, in my case it most certainly wouldn't hurt to have the
ClientID, even in
separate DBs per client - if you view the DB (manually through MS
Access) and see
the Client ID, at least you know for sure there hasn't ben a mix-up.

Your comment "Database design and access sets require too much overhead
to maintain
a high performance ratio". Until now I've been leery of DBs because of a
suspicion
that might be the case - but with nothing to back it up. Is your
statement an
observation or is it based on parallel runs between using COBOL files
and DB data
sets ? No experience, but I still 'suspect' it might be the case, the
larger the DB
grows. In my case, separate DBs per Client would not give the same
problems,
because individually the data sets do not fit the category LARGE/HUGE -
my sole
concern is that I see a reasonably close performance compared to using
COBOL files.

To re-iterate, COBOL files have served me well for 20 years and their
performance
is superb, nor using ISAM techniques have I found instances where I
said, "I wish I
had this feature.........". Further, my SOLE reason for wishing to
switch to DBs is
the ability to easily pass the data set to a third party without
generating CSVs.
Plus if they get up to snuff on SQL, they can do their own ad hoc
enquiries.

So let's get back to Pete's original comment above and his objection to
my wanting
to keep separate data sets per Client/Facility. Firstly for Pete, how
many DBs are
you aware of that run on four wheels - no I'm not kidding. Example -

Inspection Team #1 inspecting an oil plant in Northern Alberta, their
"office"
being the back of a van with a PC and a copy of the 'omnibus' DB. Then
we've got
Inspection Team # 2, same set up but they are inspecting in
Saskatchewan. Meanwhile
back at Head Office in Calgary we have another copy of the 'omnibus' DB.
All three
locations entering/changing  data. Ask yourself, "Who has the latest
copy of the
'omnibus' DB ?" (Yep. I can already guess at one possible response to
this -
include Client Id in every row and Date/TmeStamp. When the field guys
get back to
Head Office play 'musical chairs' as to which records are and which
records are not
added to the master DB).

1. COBOL FILES :

Pseudo code as it has worked with COBOL files for TWENTY years.

- Start app Check Default file, which holds last Client ID used
- check do files exist.:
    Yes - open them and process
    No  - files missing - Do you want to open empty data set or Exit
             - (Y)es - open empty data set and process.
            - (N)o exit app and user searches  for the backup diskette
that he
needs
                to load. (Could of course minimize the  app window,
while loading
the
                backup diskette). Once loaded, open and process.

- Later in the day switches to a new Client - go back through above
procedure.

Automatic and no delays - with the exception of where the user goofed
and failed to
load a data set from diskette that he needs.

2. DB DATA SETS :

Note that here I'm using those N/E and Fujitsu CBL_FILENAME routines.

2.1. In \MasterDirectory I have an empty  master copy  "Plant.mdb". This
is what is
registered with the ODBC driver, as :-

        \MyApp\Data\Plant.mdb.

2.2. As above for COBOL files, start app does the default set exist,
e.g. :-

        \MyApp\Data\AMC-32.mdb

2.3. Yes exists - RENAME "AMC-32.mdb" to "Plant.mdb". Open and process
under this
'alias'. (Only Client processed today, so just before STOP RUN - RENAME
"Plant.mdb"
to "AMC-32.mdb".)

2.4. No - doesn't exist, either open empty data set or exit and allow
user to load
from diskette. AND - to open that empty data set :-

    COPY "\MasterDirectory\Plant.mdb"  to \MyApp\Data\Plant.mdb"

2.5. Half way through the day user wants to switch to new Client, so
when the new
Defaults he selects have been triggered by an OK pushbutton -

- Step 1 - RENAME \MyApp\Data\Plant.mdb" to \MyApp\Data\AMC-32.mdb"
- Step 2 - this is taking us back to 2.2. above

2.6. So during the course of the day (and I'm talking Head Office here),
he
switches between 6 different Clients/Plants - the last one being
"PAN-88.mdb" -
just before STOP RUN :-

    RENAME "\MyApp\Data\Plant.mdb to "PAN-88.mdb".

2.7. Don't let us forget those two teams out in the boonies doing
inspections - one
may be using "EXN-17.mdb" and the other "HUK-87.mdb". On their machines
it follows
the same procedure and they offload their copy to a diskette -
subsequently used by
Head Office.

OK - so it doesn't meet your criteria for a 'global' database, but it
sure as hell
looks a lot simpler to me, and also avoids the additional time and
selective
archiving that Michael suggested for backups.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ESQL & ODBC Drivers

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T03:51:12+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b1273c7_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3b106e6e_6@Usenet.com> <20010527192931.02612.00000252@nso-cu.aol.com> <3B11CED1.7BECB291@home.com>`

```
Some very quick responses:

1. Having a few remote locations (mobile or otherwise) does NOT mean you
require separate databases for each one. I have designed and installed a
database which was (and is) accessed from up to 1500 remote locations
simultaneously. One (very large and complex) database. No problem.

2. You may be confusing the functions of data entry and real time
transaction processing. Data entry doesn't need the full DB on line;
transaction processing does.

If transaction processing is what is required, then remote mobile links to
the database (wireless Internet or WAP protocols are just 2 options) must be
established. If you do this, the RDB software automatically takes care of
conflicting updates.

I believe that most of these remote teams are sensing corrosion and entering
data? That doesn't require either multiple databases or direct transaction
processing.

I wrote a whole lot more stuff based on your post but I've deleted it.

I'm sure there are others here who will gladly discuss the pros and cons of
what you are proposing.

No more from me on this subject.

Pete.

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3B11CED1.7BECB291@home.com...
>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >It would be quite feasible for Jimmy to use this solution but he is
still
> > >thinking in Indexed datasets, therefore, multiple databases...
> > >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > software/utilities continue to work without having to
re-compile/re-design
> > based on altering the file structure itself to include the
Company/Client Id.
> >
> > In my case, financial industry, we are accustomed to linking everything
to
> > an Institution number before anything else is done.  From that point on
> > down the rules for handlnig a transaction/account are governed by the
> > parameters/options selected by the Institution to which the account
belongs.
> >
> > Unfortunately, we have yet to fully adopt the 'advantages' of database
use.
> > To date, we still find flat files, ISAM, and thrift index structures
provide as
> > good
> > if not better performance for most applications.  Database design and
access
> > sets require too much overhead to maintain a high performance ratio.
> > With DB knowledge limited to the DBA and application developers coding
to
> > the specifications of a Business analyst that receives no input from the
DBA,
> > products seldom get the best out of a database.
> > In the case of a one-horse shop, it is entirely possible to have a DB
product
> > turned out that make optimum use of the DB features.
>
```

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Some very quick responses:
>

Well at least one more from me on this topic. Ironically for some one who is a
professed atheist you come across more like a Vatican official interrogating
Galileo about the world not being round. If it doesn't  fit your perception -
then it must be wrong. And recall telling me not to ASSUME ?.

>
> 1. Having a few remote locations (mobile or otherwise) does NOT mean you
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yep that's a possibility. You could communicate from Northern Alberta if you
were a member of the Blackfoot tribe. The method is light a bonfire and wave a
wet blanket over it. Communication from the back of beyond is a non-starter,
even for the ubiquitous cell phone..

>
> 2. You may be confusing the functions of data entry and real time
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No confusion in my mind. Can be both. Obviously prime effort is recording data,
but at night time whilst they are eating, they can leave the computer running to
do calculations, print some draft reports, so they can quality check.

>
> I wrote a whole lot more stuff based on your post but I've deleted it.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I wasn't initially going to comment on the following, but while we are in
'terse' mode :-

Thread : ISAM to RDB :-

"What I care about is that you will blame RDB and dismiss it as "another
academic crap technology" before you have ever really understood it. It is
the injustice of it, and the fact that other fragile minds who hang on your
every word may be persuaded by you, that pisses me off...<G>"

The phrase I used was 'Unquestioningly accept......". In my Thesaurus that is
not in the same category as "Dismiss......". Not being proud by nature, you will
be the first to know if I admit to falling flat on my face with RDBs. With
regard to "fragile minds who hang on your every word....". If anybody reading
what I write falls into that category they are bloody idiots and should NOT be
in data processing. To the best of my knowledge I do not express CONCRETE ideas,
most of my messaging relating to sample code showing how I do it - with no
qualification that this is 'how it must be done'. For those many 'fragile minds'
that you are concerned about, I would tell them to read what others write, and
arrive at their own conclusions based on a set of different viewpoints.

Unlike you, I am not convinced that I have found the right and true path, even
within OO. I constantly worry-gut about could I have done it better. I will also
happily accept alternative suggestions to do it more effectively. However I will
NOT accept, "This is the way it must be done".

Jimmy
```

```
This is something which I did not take into consideration. The whole arena
of "partitions" and "backups" and "privileges" and all the other "DBA stuff"
is one in which I am quite weak.

This brings up one of my favorite topics: the difference between
"applications programmers" and "systems programmers."

While many applications programmers believe (or at least say they believe)
systems programmers are guys who could just not cut it in applications, I'm
sure a lot of systems programmers (which includes DBAs) guys consider
applications programmers a buch of guys who could not cut it in systems.

Truth is, we need each other.

MCM
(applications guy)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ESQL & ODBC Drivers

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T15:22:26+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.ge28he0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<3B0D341A.221BF1BB@home.com> <3b0dceb1_5@Usenet.com> <3B0DDF88.17BF7046@home.com> <3b101d0b.58532792@news1.attglobal.net> <HAYP6.1993$pa4.262008@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3b1118b6.122969829@news1.attglobal.net> <d%rQ6.147$824.43450@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
On Mon, 28 May 2001 13:02:02 GMT, Michael Mattias wrote:

>
>This brings up one of my favorite topics: the difference between
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have been both applications and systems programmer and I am sure that
knowing both areas improves your overall performance in both areas.

The most important quality for an application programmer is the ability to
understand the business rules of the application he has to program.
The more he knows about the system he uses, the more efficient his program
will be.

A systems programmer must understand the hardware and the operating system
(or system software) he has to maintain. 
The better he understands how the application people will be using "his"
system, the better it will run.

Unfortunately, it is nowadays very difficult to get a good background in
systems programming.



Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

```
Thanks Willem,

I couldn't have said it better and endorse 100%
what you wrote.

I too have worked in both camps and found the experiences invaluable.

There are also differences between APPLICATIONS, which can fall into DATA
PROCESSING (Payroll, Accounting, Sales Analysis,  etc., primarily Business
applications), COMPUTING (Scientific applications, trajectories, orbits,
weather forecasting, simulations, etc.) and NETWORK and WEB  enablement,
Network software, etc.). Again experience in any or all of these is
interesting and stimulating.

Why are we so keen to classify and divide? The fact is that ALL of it has
value to somebody...

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<3B0D341A.221BF1BB@home.com> <3b0dceb1_5@Usenet.com> <3B0DDF88.17BF7046@home.com> <3b101d0b.58532792@news1.attglobal.net> <HAYP6.1993$pa4.262008@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3b1118b6.122969829@news1.attglobal.net> <d%rQ6.147$824.43450@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.ge28he0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
Willem Clements <willem@horizontes-informatica.com> wrote in message
news:jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.ge28he0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es...
> On Mon, 28 May 2001 13:02:02 GMT, Michael Mattias wrote:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> system, the better it will run.


Specifically dealing with "DBAs" ....

A couple of years ago a wrote an application for a major insurance company
(I don't want to give you the name, but while I was there I was in "good
hands") . The application was to run on nine MVS mainframes with DB/2 as the
data access; all code was to be COBOL/ESQL.

I designed the database on paper, essentially creating a "data dictionary."
I included the proposed names of tables and columns, and datatypes; I added
a list of approximate number of rows to be stored per table, "how" the data
would be accessed for insert, delete, select etc, and a high-level narrative
overview of the application.

I then took the whole schmear to one of the DBAs (they had about six
full-time DBAs to support applications programmers).

After a couple of days, the DBA came back to me. He gave me 'final' table
names and column names (consistent with shop standards), changed a couple of
the datatypes I had used for better efficiency, told me about some indexes
he had created so my cursor accesses would perform better; and mentioned
some stuff about 'tablespaces' about which I had no clue (still don't). He
also said he had no idea "why" we were doing this application, but that was
really not important to him.

This still stands out in my mind as the best example of cooperation between
applications-types and systems-types I've encountered: The DBA did what he
did well, the application guy did what he did well. Wish I could enjoy this
more often.

MCM
```

<a id="t-307cbed41d"></a>
## Thread: new book: CICS for the COBOL programmer — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### new book: CICS for the COBOL programmer

- **From:** jay_moseley@attglobal.net (Jay Moseley, CCP)
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T16:33:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b0d36ad.8877445@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Mike Murach has just published an updated version of their 2 volume
CICS for COBOL programmers. If anyone is seeking to move into the CICS
world, this is a good way to get from basic concepts to fairly complex
applications pretty quickly and easily.  I'm not connected with
Murach, just have really gotten a lot of mileage out of their books
over the past 3 decades and like to pass along good info.

The Amazon.com link to the book is:

http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/189077409X/

Regards,
Jay Moseley, CCP
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88256A56.007739B5.00@spl-smtp2.splwg.com>`

```


Does anybody have any resources/examples on Unicode support by Cobol?
```

#### ↳ Re: Unicode support

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T17:12:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ek0us$jt9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<88256A56.007739B5.00@spl-smtp2.splwg.com>`

```
Any particular compiler or operating system?  It is supported (already) in
different ways in different environments.  There is "portable" <G> support
for it in the draft COBOL Standard.
```

#### ↳ Re: Unicode support

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T16:55:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f0k94$9da$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<88256A56.007739B5.00@spl-smtp2.splwg.com>`

```
In article <88256A56.007739B5.00@spl-smtp2.splwg.com>,
<david_mccracken@splwg.com> writes:
>
>
>Does anybody have any resources/examples on Unicode support by Cobol?
>
MOST COBOL COMPILERS ARE SYSTEM SPECIFIC.  Perhaps the compiler has an option
for a specific font.  I deal with EBCIDIC on my compiler and we use a program
to translate the font to ascii using a conversion table.  Whenever we
download something from the mainframe.  I have no idea what happens with a PC
compiler.  I would assume on a good PC compiler if you can install the font
it can be used.  There may be an option you have to set.  JAVA should be able
to convert the font if you know what font the compiler is using.  There is
support for encoding in a specific font in JAVA using a PrintWriter Class I
do beleive.
>
>
>-- 
>Posted from spl-smtp2.splwg.com [206.228.132.133]
```

### quite a simple problem

- **From:** "Psycho" <psycho.industries@virgin.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T01:07:24+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com>`

```
i am tring to write a program to take an accepted alpha input validate it's
length (5-12 char) check it for capital letters and finally make sure no
duplicate chars exist if anyone could help this would be great as this is
becomeing quite urgent. in each case if it does not pas the check a simple
error must be put to the user and must be prompted to re enter the keyword.

thanks. john
```

#### ↳ Re: quite a simple problem

- **From:** Robaire <rmiller@linux.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-05-24T20:11:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ei8rgtg9m7sptoth75so779idtspunjdgb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com>`

```
Here is your hint. Capital letters and lower case letters are bunched
in ranges in hexadecimal.  Look at each character as a hexadecimal
character, and see in which range it is.

Stephen J Spiro
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: quite a simple problem

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T13:25:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3tP6.836$pA.54571@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <4145699b.0105250008.3ef40759@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen J Spiro <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote in message > Here is your
hint. Capital letters and lower case letters are bunched
> in ranges in hexadecimal.  Look at each character as a hexadecimal
> character, and see in which range it is.
>

Too good to use ALPHABETIC-UPPER and ALPHABETIC-LOWER, are we?

Compiler does not support these natively?  Well, there's always
SPECIAL-NAMES (CLASS ...).

Last but not least, in the EBCDIC character set, the uppercase and lowercase
letters are NOT "bunched"  contiguously within the collating sequence. (They
*are* bunched when using the ASCII character set).

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: quite a simple problem

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T22:14:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bPAP6.645$NK4.116096@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com>`

```
The first part of the problem is easy enough:

MOVE 0 TO COUNT.
INSPECT DISPLAY-FIELD TALLYING COUNT FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE ' '.
IF COUNT < 5 OR COUNT > 12
   PERFORM MY-ERROR.


Next is 'check for capital' and 'check for duplicate'.

Is "Aa" a duplicate?

MOVE FUNCTION UPPER-CASE(DISPLAY-FIELD) TO DISPLAY-FIELD.

MOVE SPACE TO BYTE-FIELD-256.
PERFORM VARYING DISPLAY-INDX FROM 1 BY 1
   UNTIL DISPLAY-INDX > COUNT
   IF DISPLAY-FIELD(DISPLAY-INDX:1) < "A" OR > "Z"
      PERFORM NONLETTER-ERROR
   ELSE
      COMPUTE BYTE-INDX =
         FUNCTION ORD(DISPLAY-FIELD(DISPLAY-INDX:1))
      IF BYTE-FIELD(BYTE-INDX) NOT = SPACE
         PERFORM ALREADY-USED-ERROR
      ELSE
         MOVE 'X' TO BYTE-FIELD(BYTE-INDX)
         END-IF
   END-IF
   END-PERFORM.

Oh well. You get the idea.


"Psycho" <psycho.industries@virgin.net> wrote in message
news:0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com...
> i am tring to write a program to take an accepted alpha input
validate it's
> length (5-12 char) check it for capital letters and finally make
sure no
> duplicate chars exist if anyone could help this would be great as
this is
> becomeing quite urgent. in each case if it does not pas the check a
simple
> error must be put to the user and must be prompted to re enter the
keyword.
>
> thanks. john
>
>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: quite a simple problem

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T12:12:25+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0F8F99.F48FBB9D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <bPAP6.645$NK4.116096@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Jerry P wrote:

>    IF DISPLAY-FIELD(DISPLAY-INDX:1) < "A" OR > "Z"
>       PERFORM NONLETTER-ERROR

That's OK for ASCII, but fails to find all 'non-alpha' when used with
EBCDIC.  It also only allows 'English' letters and will flag as errors
somethings that Europeans think of as letters.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: quite a simple problem

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T04:40:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mz%P6.151645$ho6.9569126@news5.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <bPAP6.645$NK4.116096@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <p190ht0f2g2lrbbdo59eb4o1g0mdcqthsv@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com
>
> :>MOVE FUNCTION UPPER-CASE(DISPLAY-FIELD) TO DISPLAY-FIELD.
>
> Validation altering the data????

Sure. Why not?

Word processing programs correct spelling as you type.

Suppose a program had to validate a credit card number but the user
typed the number as displayed on the card (with imbedded spaces).
Wouldn't a user-friendly program squeeze out the spaces before the
validation test?

The NCIS computers (National Crime Information Center) routinely
converts user-supplied alphabetic "I" and "O" to one and zero before
using the query in a serial number look-up.

How about telephone numbers? If the program likes to store telephone
numbers as 1234567890 why not permit
(123)456-7890
123-456-7890
123.456.7890
as well as
1234567890

Same with dates:
01/01/01
01-01-2001
Jan 1, 2001
1 Jan 2001
should all be converted to (say) 20010101 before being tested for
validity.

I, for example, like ROUND numbers. All angular numbers (1, 7, 4,
sometimes 5) are converted to 2, 6, 0, and my favorite, 8, before I
let them in my systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: quite a simple problem

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T15:12:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WVtQ6.10217$fb7.700844@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <bPAP6.645$NK4.116096@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <p190ht0f2g2lrbbdo59eb4o1g0mdcqthsv@4ax.com> <mz%P6.151645$ho6.9569126@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3b11b2f8_6@Usenet.com> <3b11e826.7933141@news1.attglobal.net>`

```

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:3b11e826.7933141@news1.attglobal.net...

> He might Pete, but in 9-1-1 the angular numbers outnumber the round
> ones...

Angular numbers have their use, especially 911. When someone asks for
my telephone number, I almost automatically reply: "713-911-4553"
which, when their pesky salesman calls, results in a spate of
confusion.

The angular-round silliness developed when we tried to deduce just how
it could happen that book publishers print the wrong ISBN
(International Standard Book Number) on a book's jacket. We went back
to first principles. You would think they had a check-list:

1. Is name of book on jacket (Yes/No)
2. Is our company name on jacket (Yes/No)
3. Is author's name on jacket (Yes/No)
4. Is ISBN and barcode on jacket (Yes/No)
5. Is the price correct? (Yes/No)
etc.

Now imagine the book jacket's final approval process - getting the OK
from the publishing house Art Director. He comes in the room. The
underlings grovel. The Art Director approaches the display board,
ignoring the quite moaning in the background.

"Hmmm."

"Yes, those colors are usable. The typeface is acceptable."

"The female-person looks a little old, but it's the publisher's
'niece,' so that will have to do."

Placing on hand on his hip and turning his chin ever so, he says, to
no one in particular: "I just HATE those tacky stripes, but, what's
art without suffering?"

But then disaster!

"What's this?" he screams. "A SEVEN! I TOLD you I want ROUND numbers.
Seven is a STRAIGHT number. Make it a ROUND number, six, eight, I
don't care. Use your own judgment. My God why am I doing this - I
could be a florist!"

Art director swishes from the room.

Gloom descends.

Fade to black.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: quite a simple problem

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T07:37:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B13A615.E10C49DF@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <bPAP6.645$NK4.116096@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <p190ht0f2g2lrbbdo59eb4o1g0mdcqthsv@4ax.com> <mz%P6.151645$ho6.9569126@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3b11b2f8_6@Usenet.com> <3b11e826.7933141@news1.attglobal.net> <WVtQ6.10217$fb7.700844@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
My wife tells people phone numbers using too much English - for instance a
number such as (303)303-1716 can be said Three hundred-three, thirty,
three, seventeen, sixteen.   This is very hard to write down fast.
Besides the ambiguity, hearing the seven before the teen means we have to
back up.  I prefer saying each digit separately to make it easier on the
listener, but it would be interesting to write a program for data entry,
possibly which allowed them to enter dashes where they heard pauses, and
then found the most likely combination to be read back.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: quite a simple problem

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T21:49:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5QUQ6.16760$fb7.2079371@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <bPAP6.645$NK4.116096@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <p190ht0f2g2lrbbdo59eb4o1g0mdcqthsv@4ax.com> <mz%P6.151645$ho6.9569126@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3b11b2f8_6@Usenet.com> <3b11e826.7933141@news1.attglobal.net> <WVtQ6.10217$fb7.700844@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3B13A615.E10C49DF@brazee.net>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

> I prefer saying each digit separately to make it easier on the
> listener, but it would be interesting to write a program for data
entry,
> possibly which allowed them to enter dashes where they heard pauses,
and
> then found the most likely combination to be read back.

Yeah. Cool. (consider: 'naught-naught,' or 'double-ought,' or
'one-two-three-eight thousand' or "ILOVEYOU" - the mind reels).

Consider a program that takes WHATEVER is entered, rips out everything
that's not a number, check the result for validity, then formats the
result whichever way is standard for the application?

We do that here, so we accept things like:

(123)456-7890
123.456.7890
123-456-7890
123*456/78-90
double-sise
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: quite a simple problem

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T04:10:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b146ac3.5351668@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <bPAP6.645$NK4.116096@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <p190ht0f2g2lrbbdo59eb4o1g0mdcqthsv@4ax.com> <mz%P6.151645$ho6.9569126@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3b11b2f8_6@Usenet.com> <3b11e826.7933141@news1.attglobal.net> <WVtQ6.10217$fb7.700844@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3B13A615.E10C49DF@brazee.net> <5QUQ6.16760$fb7.2079371@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
On Tue, 29 May 2001 21:49:53 GMT, "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote:

>Yeah. Cool. (consider: 'naught-naught,' or 'double-ought,' or
>'one-two-three-eight thousand' or "ILOVEYOU" - the mind reels).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>result whichever way is standard for the application?

This kind of thing actually exists.  When you get deep inside "COM"
from MS you find that EVERYTHING is passed as a "VARIANT" to the COM
object.  It is COM's job to "Coerce" the data into the proper type.  I
like the use of the term Coerce here.  While the conversion is not
nearly as extensive as what you are talking about, I did find it
interesting.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: quite a simple problem

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T07:31:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B13A494.E67A725D@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <bPAP6.645$NK4.116096@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <p190ht0f2g2lrbbdo59eb4o1g0mdcqthsv@4ax.com> <mz%P6.151645$ho6.9569126@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3b11b2f8_6@Usenet.com> <3b11e826.7933141@news1.attglobal.net>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:

> He might Pete, but in 9-1-1 the angular numbers outnumber the round
> ones...

I like the way one draws numbers and counts the angles - it is hard to
represent these with typing (view as fixed width), but you should be able
to convert to drawing:


   0 0        11     2 2 2      3333        4      55555    66666
777777   88888  99999
  0   0      1 1         2        3        44      5        6       7
7    8   8  9   9
 0     0       1        2        3        4 4      55555    66666
7777   88888  99999
  0   0        1       2          3      4 44 4        5    6   6     7
7   8   8  99  9
   0 0         1     2 2 2      3333        4      55555    66666
7       88888  99999

What you can't see is that the "9" has a tail which wraps around to point
down, and that the "7" has a cross bar with a drop to it.   But "0" has
zero acute angles, "1' has one, "2" has two, etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: quite a simple problem

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T13:32:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ZQQ6.7595$v_2.552637@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<0nhP6.25696$m93.2954139@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <bPAP6.645$NK4.116096@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <p190ht0f2g2lrbbdo59eb4o1g0mdcqthsv@4ax.com> <mz%P6.151645$ho6.9569126@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3b11b2f8_6@Usenet.com> <3b11e826.7933141@news1.attglobal.net> <3B13A494.E67A725D@brazee.net>`

```
About 15 years back, I worked on a project to sort mail from handwritten
addresses.  One of the more workable OCR routines was to count enclosures
and tails.  It was an interesting project, 15 CPUs talking to each other
through dual ported memory.  Could sort 500 letters per minute with an
average of two rejects by the end of it.  Had to program it in assembler
though ... not a job for Cobol.  In fact, we ended up micro-coding our own
CPU's.
```

```
Hi Psycho,

Here's how I would approach the dups part of the problem:

Create a 256 byte table and init it w/hi-vals or some such.
After all other editing has been successful, loop thru the subject field and
using each byte of the field as the subscript into the table, 
check the table entry for "Y" or some such. If it is "Y" you have a dupe, if
not, move "Y" to the table entry and continue to the 
next byte.

Regards, Jack.  


In article <5ZQQ6.7595$v_2.552637@news20.bellglobal.com>, Donald Tees says...
>

…[39 more quoted lines elided]…

```

<a id="t-4164abb7c5"></a>
## Thread: How to use variable length literal parameters like DISPLAY — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to use variable length literal parameters like DISPLAY

- **From:** jburks@synchronics.com (Jamie Burks)
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T05:44:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ebbbb265.0105250444.7246f8df@posting.google.com>`

```
Using Micro Focus 4.1. Would like to create callable programs or ENTRY
points that take char parameters that are of variable length that can
be coded like the DISPLAY statement using literals. For example, I can
  DISPLAY "This is a test"
or
  DISPLAY "123"

How can I code parameters so that I can
  CALL "MY_FUNC" USING "This is a test"
or
  CALL "MY_FUNC" USING "123"

The problem is defining the LINKAGE parameters. Micro Focus has a
library call that returns the number of parameters passed, but it
doesn't return any other information about the parameters. I know that
Micro Focus stores the length of the literal in the object code, I
believe as a 16-bit integer immediately preceeding the text itself. Is
there any way to do this or am I just out of luck?

Thanks,
```

#### ↳ Re: How to use variable length literal parameters like DISPLAY

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T13:34:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9em8i5$lau$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ebbbb265.0105250444.7246f8df@posting.google.com>`

```
If you are passing a literal (by CONTENT - I assume), then this means that
YOU (the program writer) knows the length at compile-time.  Therefore, there
is no reason that you can't pass the LENGTH field as another parameter.
Then use that as the "object" of an OCCURS DEPENDING ON in the receiving
program's Linkage section item.

Having said that, passing an alphanumeric literal seems a FAIRLY "odd" thing
to do.  However, not knowing the real "business needs" of your application
there MIGHT be a reason to do it.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to use variable length literal parameters like DISPLAY

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T19:30:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0EB34B.E5E676C4@home.com>`
- **References:** `<ebbbb265.0105250444.7246f8df@posting.google.com>`

```


Jamie Burks wrote:

> Using Micro Focus 4.1. Would like to create callable programs or ENTRY
> points that take char parameters that are of variable length that can
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks,

Let me qualify - it's some years since I coded in non-OO COBOL, but the
problem is essentially the same whether you are CALLING, ENTRY-POINTS or
INVOKING. Below might appear as OO 'gibberish', but I think you can
probably sort it out to suit your own needs. Two examples :-

    1. Passing Data to a Dialog for display
    2. Returning Data (Entryfield) from Dialog when user presses OK or TAB

1. PASSING DATA

1A. Business Invoking class :
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "pass-entryfield".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-display-3                         pic zz9.
01 ls-display-31                        pic zzz.9.
01 ls-length                         pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-text.
   05 occurs 1 to TextLength
      depending on ls-length         pic x(01).
Linkage section.
01 n                                 pic x(4) comp-5.
Procedure Division using n.
 move n to Field
 Evaluate true

   when Field-percent1
        move length of ls-display-31 to ls-length
        move CLIENT-percent(1) to ls-display-31
        move ls-display-31 to ls-text

   when Field-Address1
        move length of CLIENT-Address1 to ls-length
        move           CLIENT-Address1 to ls-text
    when etc.....
 End-evaluate

 invoke os-Dialog "set-entryfield"
        using Field, ls-length, ls-text

End Method "pass-entryfield".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

1B. Dialog Invokee class

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "set-entryfield".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-object                         object reference.
01 ls-string                         object reference.
Linkage section.
01 lnk-Field                         pic x(4) comp-5.
01 lnk-length                        pic x(4) comp-5.
01 lnk-text.
   05 occurs 1 to TextLength
      depending on lnk-length    pic x(01).

Procedure Division using lnk-Field
                         lnk-length
                         lnk-text.

  invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue"
         using     lnk-length, lnk-text
         returning ls-string
  invoke os-ControlCollection "at"
         using lnk-Field returning ls-object
  invoke ls-object "setText" using ls-string
  invoke ls-string "finalize" returning ls-string

End Method "set-entryfield".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

2. RETURNING DATA

2A . Dialog Class returning the data:
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "return-entry-object".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 n                             pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-field                      pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-id                         pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-length                     pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-object                     object reference.
01 ls-string                     object reference.
01 ls-text.
   05 occurs 1 to TextLength
      depending on ls-length     pic x(01).

Procedure Division.

perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > MaxEntryFields
    invoke os-ControlCollection "at"
           using n returning ls-object

    if  os-CurrentObject = ls-object
         invoke os-CurrentObject "getText" returning ls-string
        invoke ls-string "trimblanks" returning ls-string
        invoke os-CurrentObject "Getlength" returning ls-length
        invoke ls-string "getValueWithSize"
             using ls-Length returning ls-text
        invoke ls-string "finalize" returning ls-string
        invoke os-Caller "return-entryfield"
           using n, ls-length, ls-text
         EXIT METHOD
    End-if
End-perform

End Method "return-entry-object".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
2B. Business Class receiving the returned data

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "return-Entryfield".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Working-storage section.
78 MNS                       value 25.     *> MethodnameSize
01 mn1.
   05 pic x(MNS) value "EF-01-client-number".
   05 pic x(MNS) value "EF-02-client-shortname".
   05 pic x(MNS) value "EF-03-percent1".
   *> this is a list of method names ("paragraphs") being used
    05 etc...

01 mn2 redefines mn1.
   05 ws-MethodName occurs MaxEntryField pic x(MNS).
Linkage section.
01 lnk-Field                    pic x(4) comp-5.
01 lnk-length                   pic x(4) comp-5.
01 lnk-text.
   05 occurs 1 to TextLength
      depending on lnk-length   pic x(01).

Procedure Division using lnk-Field
                         lnk-length
                         lnk-text.

  move lnk-Field to Field

  if     not Field-ClientNumber
         set RecordChanged to true
  End-if

  invoke self ws-MethodName(Field)
         using lnk-length lnk-text

Exit Method
End Method "return-Entryfield".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
```

---

## Thread: Long names are doom ? — 13 msgs

### Long names are doom ?

- **From:** 00001111 <00001111@my-deja.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T06:34:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net>`

```
Hi All,

  Anybody use variables/names longer than 31 character
and finds it really useful ?

Then please respond  why, where, when.
I have folks here in comp.lang.fortran who will die claiming that they

- "never seen a well written, legible program
  that uses any identifiers longer than 18-20 characters..".
- "long variables names are *hard* to read.  And, you have to
  read though all the characters of every instance of them...".
- "it degrades the legibility of a program to use identifiers that
  can't be easily remembered...."

As a result, despite 90% of computer languages have long, very
long or 'infinite' identifiers, fortran folks seems plan to stay
with their 6...aargh ...sorry this was just not far ago... 31 character
limit intil year 3000.

cheers
```

#### ↳ Re: Long names are doom ?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T07:42:38-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0E614E.551A95F0@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net>`

```
I've tried to a few times, usually by adding prefixes (sometimes with copy
replacing).

00001111 wrote:

> Hi All,
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> cheers
```

#### ↳ Re: Long names are doom ?

- **From:** "Dennis Edward" <temp081100@ipipeline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T15:28:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rSuP6.1764$5R2.83031627@news1.van.metronet.ca>`
- **References:** `<3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net>`

```
01 AP-TRANSACTION-FILE-OPEN-STAT    PIC 9.
    88 AP-TRANSACTION-FILE-OPEN            VALUE 1.
    88 AP-TRANSACTION-FILE-CLOSED        VALUE 0.

1) Circular reasoning. The speaker has decided that well-written, legible
programs use short variable names. Therefore, any program that uses long
variable names is not well-written and legible. Therefore, by definition,
they haven't seen any program that meets their definition. It's meaningless.

2) Ask them to look you straight in the eye and, without blinking or
cracking a smile, tell you that "AP-TRANSACTION-FILE-OPEN" is harder to
remember and more cryptic than "APTRNFOPN". If they are able to do it, be
impressed, but don't ever buy anything from them.

3) See #2.

Of course, a lot of this has to do with what people are used to. Obviously
the format that you are used to working with is the format that you will be
more comfortable with, and therefore more easily able to work with. Trouble
is, some people are unable to distinguish between "I like this better" and
"This is better". The former is a perfectly reasonable opinion. The latter
is a religious statement, unless the parameters and justification for the
statement are A) clearly defined, and B) aren't just more opinions expressed
as emphatic statements.


"00001111" <00001111@my-deja.net> wrote in message
news:3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net...
> Hi All,
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Long names are doom ?

- **From:** kanoako@netnet.net (Kano Ako)
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T10:39:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<677e6c81.0105250939.65621e66@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net>`

```
Greetings,

Certainly there is a point of diminishing returns on variable name
length.
I found that an old adage translates into this situation well.  Make
your variable names as long as needed to make them easy to figure out
and not a character longer!

More than 31 characters?  I am not certain if COBOL II allows more
than that.  I know I have had a compile or two bomb because of too
long of an identifier name.  In that one or two instances a little
thought quickly came up with a shorter identifier name.

The third point is true, but then if long names automatically made
names them harder to remember then why not have a two character limit?

Just because XYZ language has ABC feature does not automatically mean
that programmers should/will use it.  Frankly I like less limits upon
my identifier pool, but that does not mean I will automatically choose
identifiers that spread out over three or four lines!

I guess to answer your question I would say I rarely find 31
character+ identifiers useful, but rarely is not never!

ttyl & cheers,
KA


00001111 <00001111@my-deja.net> wrote in message news:<3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net>...
> Hi All,
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> cheers
```

#### ↳ Re: Long names are doom ?

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T07:43:34+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0ED206.87262B83@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net>`

```
I had to write a pgm a few years ago that generated another cobol
program. The obvious way to do this wouodl have been do have some
variable and paragraph names be the DB2 key values with a short
prefix. Unfortunately this would have made the names too long so
we had to build a lookup table, allocate random names (eg
N-99999) and then use them. This cost me several days work. It
resulted in output that was harder to understand and debug. 

This was a legitimate application - the savings in CPU costs were
millions of dollars per year, using generated code instead of
table lookups (let alone leaving the tables in DB2!).

IMO there should be no arbitrary limits unless it is really
essential. Someone will always have a need to go past the limit,
and it will be a lot of bother and cost to do so eg the Intel 286
and its infamous 'brain damaged' 64k segment size.

I say recently that IBM has introduced a limit for the size of MQ
Series messages in queues shared across a sysplex. That limit is
64k! Come the revolution, those responsible will be in big
trouble!

Tim Josling

00001111 wrote:
> 
> Hi All,
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> cheers
```

#### ↳ Re: Long names are doom ?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T21:58:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fAAP6.573$NK4.105631@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net>`

```

"00001111" <00001111@my-deja.net> wrote in message
news:3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net...
> Hi All,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I have folks here in comp.lang.fortran who will die claiming that
they

FORTRANers think symbol manipulation.

If only the compiler would permit Greek (both upper and lower case)
and Hebrew (plus sub- and super-scripts) FORTRAN programmers would
insist that a well-written program can be had with single-character
variable names. Except for mathematical functions like SQRT and COS,
of course. I once saw a FORTRAN program, admittedly small, that used a
differing number of "$" as variable names [$$$ = SQRT(1 + $$**2 -
$$$**3) ]. To the programmer, and, I admit, to me at the time, the
program made perfect sense.

COBOLers are describing the world to a program - and to each other -
so they generally use more characters.

Dress codes are different too. In disciplines where the truth is
empirically verifiable (mathematics, sometimes mathematical
physicists), adherents pay zilch attention to their appearance (socks
don't match, etc.).

In areas where truth is determined by majority vote (English
departments, business, etc.), uniforms are worn (three-piece suits,
wing-tips, rep-ties, etc.).
```

#### ↳ Re: Long names are doom ?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T10:12:11+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b0ed93d_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net>`

```
COBOL limits datanames to 30 characters.

You CAN make them longer on some compilers but only the first 30 characters
are recognised.

Pete.

"00001111" <00001111@my-deja.net> wrote in message
news:3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net...
> Hi All,
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>



Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Long names are doom ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T17:24:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9emm2b$2dk$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net> <3b0ed93d_6@Usenet.com>`

```
For a fairly "obscure" reason, this limit is raised to 31 in the draft
Standard.  (I *believe* that it was to bring it into "sync" with the current
SQL standard).  Your compiler *might* already accept this.

I do know of some compilers that ACCEPT greater than 30 (or 31) - but only
USE the 1st 30 (or 31) characters to determine "uniqueness".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Long names are doom ?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T15:09:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BGPP6.1790$T82.169710@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net> <3b0ed93d_6@Usenet.com> <9emm2b$2dk$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
My $0.02 on this topic.....

Once the program is compiled, the size of the source code datanames is
immaterial, because the datanames no longer exist.

Other than the "generate a COBOL source code file"  application mentioned
earlier, this suggests the permitted length of datanames is of interest ONLY
to the developer/maintainer, consuming no resources at run time. (Although
there is a small overhead at compile time related to the size of the symbol
table).

Therefore, it seems maximum dataname size should be considered part of the
"development environment" and (going back about 25 years), "if it feels
good, do it."

Portablility of source code? Good reason for some standards body to suggest
a maximim.
```

```
>a maximim.

Just to add my $0.05, being an OS/390 Assembler programmer first and a
Cobol programmer second, the ability of going from 8 to 30 character
variable names has always pleased me.  In a large Assembler program it
is sometimes difficult to make variable names that have a meaning that
makes sense if you revisit the program 1 year or more down the road!
Never had that problem with Cobol.  However, I have often times
violated the 30 character limit on paragraph names in an attempt to
make them descriptive.

I think, if there has to be a limit in Cobol, there is nothing wrong
with 30 or 31.

Regards,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Long names are doom ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T05:44:38+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B115926.E5A39258@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<11B7D63DA47A128E.FBB0B00A225FBDE7.2337E6839F4331C5@lp.airnews.net> <20010527150451.08020.00001633@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
Yes, but why have a limit at all? Sometimes it gets seriously in
the way (as in the example I gave previously). And COBOL has a
large number of different, and more or less arbitrary, limits,
that the programmer is going to run into.

In my compiler, the limit is 'available memory'. I had to add in
a check for 30/31 characters for standards compliance and issue a
warning. This slows down writing the compiler and also the
running of the compiler. There are *lots* of similar limitations,
all for no good reason IMO.

If there is no good reason for a limitation it should not exist.
If it has to exist it should be as large as is feasible. It
should efinitely not be a number that is guaranteed to cause
someone grief.

This is not just a problem in compilers, but in computer systems
generally. Intel decreed that segments woudl be 64k, asking 'who
needs more'. The result: Windows 3.1, and the six different
memory models along with other abominations. 

Tim Josling

Ross Klatte wrote:
> ...
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

#### ↳ Long Names?

- **From:** Philip Erdelsky <PErdelsk@acucorp.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T15:26:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9332A1B4B5CED2119FCE00105AD16A4D1F2282@hubcap.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<3B0E5F52.21C47AF4@my-deja.net>`

```
Meaningful names are rarely longer than about 31 characters, at
least in English. (Not so in German ;-))

What's important is that names be MEANINGFUL. Code maintainers
must often search source code for specific names. If a name is
misspelled, truncated or abbreviated, it may be very hard to find.

> -----Original Message-----
> From: 00001111 [mailto:00001111@my-deja.net]
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<snip!>
```

- **Message-ID:** `<677e6c81.0105250711.2a62c53d@posting.google.com>`

```
Greetings All,

I have a question about performing Pan Scans.  We are looking for an
include along the lines of:  XYZ-WE CAN NOT REMEMBER WHAT IS HERE-ABC.
 We basically need some sort of wildcard.  What can we use in place of
'WE CAN NOT REMEMBER WHAT IS HERE'?  We are working off site so we do
not have any sort of doc.s handy (even if we were onsite, the doc.s
probably would not be handy).

Anyway thanks
KA
```

#### ↳ Re: Pan scan question.

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T13:16:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F9CD66C754725A79.E390E8D368757B2D.8F9B8E18DB0FB468@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<677e6c81.0105250711.2a62c53d@posting.google.com>`

```
On 25 May 2001 08:11:01 -0700, kanoako@netnet.net (Kano Ako)
enlightened us:

>Greetings All,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>KA

Use the ? as the wildcard.  For example, if you are searching a
Panvalet library for anything that starts with XYZ-, then you put
XYZ-? or XYZ-?-ABC on the command statement.  I'm doing this from
memory so I may not be 100% correct, but give it a try.

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
```

```
web version at:

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links

frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).

"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".

where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

## Thread: ODBC Speed to MS Access 2000 — 12 this month / 13 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### ODBC Speed to MS Access 2000

- **From:** <mkellom@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-25T19:38:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b0efcd3_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
I am accessing a MS Access 2000 data base through ODBC. I have a 255
character field that I want to search for multiple words within the field to
find a match. I have successfully figured out how to do this. My problem is
speed. If I do a query from within Access itself it takes only a couple of
seconds to retrieve the results. Via ODBC the exact same query takes over a
minute. The table I am searching though has over 100,000 records. How can I
speed things up through ODBC to match the query directly within Access?
```

```
Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC Speed to MS Access 2000

- **From:** <mkellom@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T13:21:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b13ea64_1@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<3b0efcd3_2@news1.prserv.net> <3b0f8211_1@Usenet.com>`

```
I have a table that has three elements.

LongDesc_SKU (text)
LongDesc_ImageNo (text)
LongDesc_Description (text)

The field I am searching is LongDesc_Description.

The design view has the following options:
Field Size=255
Required=no
Allow zero length=no
Indexed=no
Unicode Compress=yes
all other options are blank.

I am allowing for up to ten words in the search.
I made a different cursor for each criteria. For example if there is only
one word I would set word-1 to true and declare a cursor LDDESCUR. For two
words I would set word-2 and use cursor LDDESC2CUR, etc.

If the user would to type in drill and bit. I set word-2 and the variables
WS-DESC1 = "drill%" WS-DESC1A = "% drill%" WS-DESC2 = 'bit%" and WS-DESC2A =
"% bit%". The results are accurate, but very slow. This same query done in
Access is fast.

I am using Access 2000 via ODBC and Realia 3.1.
IF WSTRDESL-DESC AND WORD-1

EXEC SQL

DECLARE LDDESCCUR CURSOR FOR

SELECT LongDesc_SKU,

LongDesc_ImageNo,

LongDesc_Description

FROM LongDesc

WHERE (LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC1 OR

LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC1A)

END-EXEC

END-IF.

IF WSTRDESL-DESC AND WORD-2

EXEC SQL

DECLARE LDDESC2CUR CURSOR FOR

SELECT LongDesc_SKU,

LongDesc_ImageNo,

LongDesc_Description

FROM LongDesc

WHERE ((LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC1 OR

LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC1A) AND

(LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC2 OR

LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC2A))

END-EXEC

END-IF.

IF WSTRDESL-DESC AND WORD-3

EXEC SQL

DECLARE LDDESC3CUR CURSOR FOR

SELECT LongDesc_SKU,

LongDesc_ImageNo,

LongDesc_Description

FROM LongDesc

WHERE ((LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC1 OR

LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC1A) AND

(LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC2 OR

LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC2A) AND

(LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC3 OR

LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC3A))

END-EXEC

END-IF.

IF WSTRDESL-DESC AND WORD-4

EXEC SQL

DECLARE LDDESC4CUR CURSOR FOR

SELECT LongDesc_SKU,

LongDesc_ImageNo,

LongDesc_Description

FROM LongDesc

WHERE ((LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC1 OR

LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC1A) AND

(LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC2 OR

LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC2A) AND

(LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC3 OR

LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC3A) AND

(LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC4 OR

LongDesc_Description LIKE :WS-DESC4A))

END-EXEC

END-IF.

5 thought 10 would follow here!



I do a Declare Cursor, than an open cursor, than fetches until I have
finished reading the records, at that time I do a close cursor.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC Speed to MS Access 2000

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** <mkellom@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T23:15:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b14757f_1@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<3b0efcd3_2@news1.prserv.net> <3b0f8211_1@Usenet.com> <3b13ea64_1@news1.prserv.net> <3b146c1e.5698628@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
I am using Workbench 3.1 and they have a pre-compiler.

The "Like" clause is the same clause I am using with Access 2000 query
language, without a speed problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC Speed to MS Access 2000

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** t.morrison@liant.com (Tom Morrison)
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T07:08:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2d819982.0105300608.3877bc79@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0efcd3_2@news1.prserv.net> <3b0f8211_1@Usenet.com> <3b13ea64_1@news1.prserv.net> <3b146c1e.5698628@news1.attglobal.net> <3b14757f_1@news1.prserv.net>`

```
<mkellom@attglobal.net> wrote in message news:<3b14757f_1@news1.prserv.net>...
> The "Like" clause is the same clause I am using with Access 2000 query
> language, without a speed problem.

I presume that this means you are observing Access fill a grid with
the first rows of the query's row set.  Of course, speed is very much
in the eye of the beholder, and the developers of Access know this. 
They work very hard to display (some) results as soon as possible.

However, the ODBC interface may not be quite as oriented toward first
impressions.  Clearly, the query you describe requires a full table
scan.  My guess is that the ODBC interface is doing the full table
scan before returning any rows, unlike the 'native' Access.

Just a guess...but one based on some experience with the subtleties of
Access.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC Speed to MS Access 2000

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T00:10:27+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b14e343_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0efcd3_2@news1.prserv.net> <3b0f8211_1@Usenet.com> <3b13ea64_1@news1.prserv.net>`

```
OK I've had a look at your SQL and agree it is OK... (sorry about delayed
response, I've been very busy just lately).

I'm sure you realise that searching text is always inefficient, and Thane
has already correctly pointed out that LIKE is slow...

As a suggestion, you might consider holding certain frequently used  words
(like "drill" or "bit" as codes or even as text in separate columns on the
database. These could then be separately indexed. (difficult to maintain and
clumsy, but possible...)

Another possibility would be to set up a hierarchy of tools like "Drills",
"Bits", "Spanners" etc. and run the cursor on this. As each record is
retrieved, search the description for your keywords in memory. (Normally, it
is MUCH better to let SQL filter and search the data, but sometimes it is
better to do it yourself...could be in this case.)

Finally, you could build a Keyword in Context (KWIC) index for each record.
Then you can avoid using LIKE altogether.

Given what you have...

Set the Design View Properties of LongDesc_Description to: "indexed=yes"
"Unicode compress=no"


(Although you can't index Memo fields, you CAN index text). Let ACCESS build
the necessary index for it and check that it is recognised as an index in
ACCESS 2000.

ACCESS Query is running faster than your SQL because it is probably building
an index the first time you run it, and saving it for the duration of the
session.

If you fail to see any improvement, then you must tune the ODBC driver.
Consult your Driver documentation for Help on index and data buffer sizes.
Sometimes simply changing from the default 512 bytes to 4096 can improve
performance out of sight (especially where sequential access via a cursor is
being done...). You could also look at the advanced options in ODBC32 to do
this, but make sure you note any existing settings before changing them, so
you can reset them if you have to, and ALWAYS read the Help before messing
with this...

Pete.
```
