###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC Speed to MS Access 2000

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T12:04:47+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b158a09_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0efcd3_2@news1.prserv.net> <3b0f8211_1@Usenet.com> <3b13ea64_1@news1.prserv.net> <3b14e343_6@Usenet.com> <3b14f2ff_4@news1.prserv.net>`

```
<mkellom@attglobal.net> wrote in message news:3b14f2ff_4@news1.prserv.net...
> Thanks for the information.
>>>>
> In my example I show the user typing in drill and bit, but as the system
is
> in use in the field the use may only type in dri and bit, not necessarily
> the entire word, if this is the case do I not need to use the "LIKE"
> command?
>
<<<
Yes, you DO need to use LIKE (as you are doing) for partial (generic)
searches. However you can improve it out of sight if you remove the wildcard
from the start of it.... e.g.  dri%  is MUCH quicker than  %dri%

>>>
> You also mention (KWIC) is that a standard setup option for a field in
> Access? How can I do this option?
>
<<<
Sorry, that may have been misleading. It is NOT a standard option in ACCESS
or anything else that I know of. It is a technique for organising text data
into a form that allows it to be searched in a meaningful way.

The technique was invented by IBM and goes back to the days before disks
were invented and we held everything on tape. You can understand that
searching text under these circumstances was very difficult.

What KWIC did was to enable you to input a "keyword" (like "drill") and it
would then return every record that had this keyword, and show you the
context in which it appeared (usually 10 words from the text on either side
of the keyword, but this was user definable), so you would get "...and there
is no need to sharpen this DRILL because it is self..." or " ....building
morale. DRILL is essential for new recruits..."

From the contexts returned you could tell which source you were interested
in.

I have some routines which I wrote for windows to do this, and you simply
click on the context you are interested in and it brings up the appropriate
source document.

The point is that in your case, the technique could be adapted to give you a
better search on text.
(The wildcards are eliminated because the search has to be on Keyword. Once
you have built the keyword list you could then do a secondary generic
search.)

As you are not familiar with this technique, is not likely to be viable and
I shouldn't have muddied the water describing it...sorry.

I believe that if you index the text field (even though I hate doing this)
you will improve performance, mainly because the index will be memory
resident (paged).

The real question you need to ask yourself is: "Can I structure this data so
it can be searched more efficiently?" Hence my suggestion that you use some
generic hierarchic classifications to quickly identify subsets of the data,
for further detailed processing.

Let us know how you get on.

Pete.


Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

## Thread: Drawing lines/boxes — 4 msgs

### Drawing lines/boxes

- **From:** "Cameron Poole" <poolecameron@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T00:18:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9enecj$a76o$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I am brand new to COBOL and to programming in general so please forgive me
when I sound like an idot.

My question is how do I draw a simple box in COBOL? I'm making an
interactive screen for a class project but our book does not tell us how to
draw lines and such. My book has a sample program with an example that uses
what looks like standard characters. Viewed in the editor it looks like
this:

05 line 12 col 13 value "+--------------------+".
05 line  13 col 13 value "|                               |".
05 line 14 col 13 value "+--------------------+".

I copied and pasted a few lines and got this. Are these the characters that
I should enter into the editor. If so, how? I told you I was a newbie.

05 line 06  col 37 value "������������������������Ŀ".
05 line 14  col 37 value "�
�".
05 line 15  col 37 value "��������������������������".

Any help or just a point to the right website would be greatly appreciated.

I'm using a student version of Micro Focus Animator

Thanks in advance
Cameron
```

#### ↳ Re: Drawing lines/boxes

- **From:** Foodman <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T11:14:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9eMP6.692$rn5.39400@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9enecj$a76o$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
In article <9enecj$a76o$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>, Cameron Poole says...
>
>I am brand new to COBOL and to programming in general so please forgive me
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Cameron


Hi:

There are special line-drawing characters for DOS.

Whether your editor will let you put them into your source
is another matter.

These characters can be typed by holding down the ALT key
and then, on the numeric keyboard, typing the three digit 
ASCII code. Try this at the DOS prompt to see them

There are double and single lines.

The codes are (not in any particular sequence):

218, 194, 191, 195, 197, 179, 180, 192, 196, 193, and 217 for single lines

and 201, 203, 187, 204, 206, 185, 186, 200, 205, 202, 188.

You should search the internet for ASCII tables which depict all 256 characters.

Good luck with them.

Tony Dilworth
```

#### ↳ Re: Drawing lines/boxes

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T17:51:18-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ep80m$4ire$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<9enecj$a76o$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Cameron,

This is an excellent question, and hopefully I can get some help from some
of the other "elders" in this newsgroup.  It seems to me, when I first
started developing screens for DOS, I ran into this problem and put a
command to load ANSI.SYS in the CONFIG.SYS file.  Since many folks running
Win98, Win2000, WinNT etc., don't even have this file, this suggestion may
not even apply to you. But as I recall, if I put a

DEVICE=C:\ANSI.SYS

line in the CONFIG.SYS file, the amulet type charaters that you describe
would resolve themselves to the proper charaters, assuming you keyed them as
suggested in other answers to your question.  Of course, you would need an
actual ANSI.SYS file to begin with, so if you don't have one, you need to
get some help from some other folks who may still have DOS files on their
PCs.  But in many cases, regardless of editors, when I loaded this file, I
could see the "boxes" properly in the editor.


Good luck,

Denny

#### ↳ Re: Drawing lines/boxes

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T07:49:03+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B10A35E.6473D703@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9enecj$a76o$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Cameron Poole wrote:
> 
> I am brand new to COBOL and to programming in general so please forgive me
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> this:

Line drawing characters on terminals are very hardware dependent, and
even code set dependent on a single type.  Using hypens and plus is
suitable for most if not all terminal types.

> I copied and pasted a few lines and got this. Are these the characters that
> I should enter into the editor. If so, how? I told you I was a newbie.

If what you enter into the editor shows up as line characters
_on_your_machine_ then those are most likely the ones that will show up
when Cobol displays them.

> 05 line 06  col 37 value "ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½Ä¿".
> 05 line 14  col 37 value "ï¿½
> ï¿½".
> 05 line 15  col 37 value "ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½Ä ".

These show up to _me_ as acented As and upside down question marks.  But
that is because of the codeset that I am running and may be exactly
right for your setup.  This is why the examples use standard characters.

If it is a PC that you are using then you can enter any code on the
keyboard using ALT + 3 character decimal code on the numeric keypad, so
Alt-down 1-8-6 Alt-up will give whatever character is defined in the
character set for number 186.

With my editor I select 'line graphics', the particular line type
required and just draw using the arrow keys.

> Any help or just a point to the right website would be greatly appreciated.
> 
> I'm using a student version of Micro Focus Animator

Your hardware type is more imprtant in this case.
```

<a id="t-97fcdd2f21"></a>
## Thread: Converting ISAM files to RDB — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Converting ISAM files to RDB

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T01:00:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b0fabd6_6@Usenet.com>`

```
A number of people are successfully using DECLGEN to convert ACCESS table
definitions into COBOL Host Variable definitions and record layouts, so
their Relational Databases (not just ACCESS) can be easily accessed from
COBOL, using ODBC.

However, a requirement has been expressed to do the OPPOSITE! That is to
take COBOL file definitions and automatically generate RDB tables from them.
(NEGLCED <G>?).  This would be the first step towards converting an
Application system based on the COBOL File system (primarily using Indexed
Datasets, but not necessarily only IDs) to a system using Relational
Database.

(Initially, MS ACCESS, but not limited to this in the long run.)

There is quite a bit of work involved in doing this as the COBOL FD and
corresponding 01 level(s) must be parsed for sublevel numbers, REDEFINES,
OCCURS, KEY field definitions, and PICTURE Clauses, and this data must then
be used to generate correct corresponding SQL CREATE definitions (or,
possibly, DAO definitions for MS ACCESS). At the same time, ideally, the
Database can be automatically Normalized to 2NF by removal of Repeating
Groups (OCCURS clauses) to appropriate tables with Foreign Keys. The final
normalization to 3NF (removal of key dependencies) would probably need to be
done manually. It still means that a large portion of the conversion can be
automated, and a Database in at least 2nd Normal Form can be automatically
obtained.

Now, I already have a fair proportion of the code required to do this (yes,
some of it is Components...<G>), but before embarking on the exercise I
wondered if any people here would be interested in such software. I will
make it available for free (at least the Beta version) as I did with
DECLGEN, and only for enhanced functionality like automatic generation of
code for Database Maintenance, would there be a charge.

If enough people respond to make it worth doing, it will happen.

If you would like to register your interest in such a tool, please either
respond here or (if you don't want to be seen to do so publicly) mail me
privately.  If there is no interest, I won't undertake it.

Thanks,

Pete.

Relational Database in favor of duplicating the present related-index-file

Pardon me whilst I bellow, e'er-so-eloquently... BLEARGH!

Yes, I know this is done All The Time and Everywhere but it still causes
me visceral upset... it is similar to the Corner Office Idiot calling in
The Troops and saying 'Ol' Bernice 'n Louie have been keeping our books
in bound folios, making entries with goose-quills... We want to bring
them into the Computer Age... so write something which duplicates - and
duplicates *exactly* - everything they do but on a computer.'

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting ISAM files to RDB

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T18:16:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B0FF37F.703E5A8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0fabd6_6@Usenet.com> <%vOP6.6628$DW1.292027@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```


NA wrote:

> In article <3b0fabd6_6@Usenet.com>,
> Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

I find your comments negative and non-productive. If somebody, (just as Michael
does with numerics), offers a piece of software to help development - I fail to
see the reasoning for your cynicism.

Is this in defence of Pete Dashwood - No ! Check out the thread "ESQL & ODBC" -
he is ALREADY royally pissed off at me - and we are no longer communicating
privately ! So be it.

What he is offering is a piece of software that will disassemble COBOL records
into meaningful DB formats. It provides a first stage conversion - it is then up
to the developer to take what is generated  and 'nurse' it to the format he
wants. Would I use it - No. My reasoning, (and I'm not going to go into a deep
explanation),  is that I do not unquestioningly accept the whole kerboodle
regarding DB Normalization, any more than I accept a 'purist' approach to OO
technology.

All tools *must* be adjusted to suit your perceived needs - and whether your
results fit the 'idealistic theory' doesn't matter a damn - the only thing that
counts is, does it work easily and efficiently, and as Howard would say,  "Is it
easily maintainable ?".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

I am not "Royally pissed off at you" (had no idea you were such a sensitive

not yours. As for not communicating privately, you have already received
private help from me since I expressed my dismay.

Yes Jimmy, a good argument. However it is really necessary to learn the
rules BEFORE you start breaking them. At least, it is if you REALLY want to
get the best results you can. (If you don't understand Normalization you
really can't just dismiss it as Academic theory.) I don't doubt for a minute
you will get a working result. However, I KNOW it could be better...

Actually, I really don't care whether what you end up with is perfect or
not.

What I care about is that you will blame RDB and dismiss it as "another
academic crap technology" before you have ever really understood it. It is
the injustice of it, and the fact that other fragile minds who hang on your
every word may be persuaded by you, that pisses me off...<G>

Pete.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting ISAM files to RDB

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T14:21:19+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b10670a_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3b0fabd6_6@Usenet.com> <%vOP6.6628$DW1.292027@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
I totally agree Doc. And I share the sentiments paraphrased in your last
paragraph (excellent analogy, by the way...<G>).

It is NOT something I would do myself, but, like I said, I have had a
request to do it.

Here are some gentle arguments as to why I will do it, if there is enough
interest in it.

Sometimes we can't afford the luxury of rebuilding from scratch, sometimes,
for commercial reasons rather than "optimum computer system design" it is
necessary to get things into the market place because people want it.

For example, (and this is an actual case), a small software house has a very
successful package which they have developed over years using the COBOL file
system (primarily, ISAM) on PCs. Their customers want better reporting
facility, and the Software house also want to continue improving the
product) and this means continuing to use COBOL to produce these reports.
But the file system is closed so it is necessary to write COBOL programs
(which must be maintained) if the user wants to export or share data with
other standard Office software.Customer says: "Well XXXX package uses
Database technology and I can write my own reports and easily Query it using
Crystal or SQL. Not only that but I can export EXACTLY the subset I want to
Excel and examine it there, even if I don't know today what my requirements
are going to be tomorrow". There is now a competitive pressure on the
software house to make their product open, accessible and scalable.
It is also good Marketing for the package to be seen to be using RDB
technology.

All of these are good reasons for converting as far as the Software House is
concerned. It really has little to do with the aesthetics of database design
which, in a perfect world, you and I would prefer to see implemented.

If you are still not convinced, how about this as another argument...

There are a huge number of applications written using ISAM. Most of them are
very successful, but you are 100% correct that simply converting them will
not unleash the full power of Relational Technology. (I stress that I have
no issue with you on this; I love to see people getting the full benefit a
good relational design).

However, people need to start somewhere. The argument is that is better to
start with an Application they already know backwards, than to come to grips
with the foreign concept of Relational Design from day one (although, I
agree there are circumstances where this can also be advantageous...green
field sites, f'r'instance).

Using the tool I am proposing they will get their system (which they already
know and understand) converted into an unfamiliar format which will not be
too threatening... they will find that tables can be accessed the same as
their old files were and there is minimum impact on their existing code. By
splitting out the repeating groups they get introduced to the concept of
Normalization and, if their existing ISAM system was fairly efficient
anyway, the new RDB based one will be reasonably useful as well.

Once it is achieved and they realise it was not too traumatic, they will
hopefully be encouraged to do more.

I agree that it is not a perfect solution, but it is a start.

It will certainly get people familiar with the basic concepts of RDB and
accessing them in COBOL with ODBC. And it will remove some of the fear of
this technology which has been engendered over the years. I believe that is
a major step forward. The next system they design will be better... so is it
ever.

Please don't be too disparaging; the world is not a perfect place...<G>

Pete.


" NA" <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
news:%vOP6.6628$DW1.292027@iad-read.news.verio.net...
> In article <3b0fabd6_6@Usenet.com>,
> Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >take COBOL file definitions and automatically generate RDB tables from
them.
> >(NEGLCED <G>?).  This would be the first step towards converting an
> >Application system based on the COBOL File system (primarily using
Indexed
> >Datasets, but not necessarily only IDs) to a system using Relational
> >Database.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

In article <3b10670a_5@Usenet.com>,
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>I totally agree Doc. And I share the sentiments paraphrased in your last
>paragraph (excellent analogy, by the way...<G>).

Pfoo... you'se jes' easily impressed.  *Some* folks, though, might think I
have a Bad Attitude.

>
>It is NOT something I would do myself, but, like I said, I have had a
>request to do it.

Hmmmm... a 'request' is one thing, a Purchase Order (or a reasonable
facsimile thereof) is quite another.

>
>Here are some gentle arguments as to why I will do it, if there is enough
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>necessary to get things into the market place because people want it.

This appears to be a variation of 'We don't have enough time to do it
right but we *will* have enough time to do it over'... again, a moderately
common situation.  The clause of '... because people want it' I will take
to signify '... because people will sign checks for it'.

>Customer says: "Well XXXX package uses
>Database technology and I can write my own reports and easily Query it using
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>software house to make their product open, accessible and scalable.

... so that folks will buy *it*, rather than the competition.  Notice,
however, that the customer's motive is to include a capability of
relational technology (in this case an increased ease for ad hoc
queries... yes, I know that some file-based systems have Right Powerful
Query Tools written for them but it has been my experience that folks who
use a database of some type are more ready to demand ad hoc capabilities.)

>It is also good Marketing for the package to be seen to be using RDB 
>technology.

... so that more folks can be made to want to buy it, sure.

>
>All of these are good reasons for converting as far as the Software House is
>concerned. It really has little to do with the aesthetics of database design
>which, in a perfect world, you and I would prefer to see implemented.

Weeeelllll... in my case it is more a 'right tool for the right job'
aesthetic... consider:

'I gotta pound in some nails... what tools do we got?'

'We got a hammer 'n we got a torque-wrench.'

'Hmmmm... both'll work but we'll be more marketable if we can say our team
has experience with torque-wrench technology... so let's everyone pound in
nails with *those*!'

>
>If you are still not convinced, how about this as another argument...

Really, it is not a matter of convincing... remember my assertion of 'Yes,
I know this is done All The Time and Everywhere but it still causes me
visceral upset...'

[snippage]

>However, people need to start somewhere.

... and this is why, away-back-when, so many companies kept on
bank-after-bank of 'hello girls' after their first PBX was installed...
no... wait, did it happen *that* way or did a change in the technology
used to accomplish a task cause a change in workforce composition?

>The argument is that is better to
>start with an Application they already know backwards, than to come to grips
>with the foreign concept of Relational Design from day one (although, I
>agree there are circumstances where this can also be advantageous...green
>field sites, f'r'instance).

... and then again a company which Spells Acronyms Properly comes at this
in an exact opposite manner; they propose that you study the business and
determine what it is that one needs human-being type folks to do and what
can be left to machines.

(yes, I know that this software doesn't always get installed right and
doesn't always work the way folks hope... I also know that these
observations have been made of many other kinds of softwary, as well.
sometimes things work, sometimes they don't)

[snip]

>Please don't be too disparaging; the world is not a perfect place...<G>

Never said that it was nor did I say that it should be... what I *did* say
was that I think I've seen things like this before and they make me go
'BLEARGH!'

DD

>

<a id="t-af2d20a7af"></a>
## Thread: OS/390 V2.7, CICS TX1.2 -- help? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### OS/390 V2.7, CICS TX1.2 -- help?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praul@isot.N0SPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-26T22:49:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b107a7f$0$16914@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
I'm on a OS/390 V2.7 system with TX 1.2 installed. The problem seems to be a
mismatch between the COBOL compiler and the CICS 5.2 system at the heart of
TX, and some kind of conflict with LE code.  More information is included
below, but I could
sure use some advice on where to start looking for this problem and how to
get around it, if possible.  For what it is worth,
this program is the 10 line CICS ACCT00 sample program, modified to do a
simple SEND FROM (WS-ITEM) instead of
transmitting a map.  Any pointers or information appreciated, including
pointers to what book to look into. :)
Thanks
-Paul


The COBOL compiler is:

PP 5688-197 IBM COBOL for MVS & VM   1.2.2

The exact error is:

DFHAC2206 18:17:53 CICS1 Transaction ACCT failed with abend APC3. Updates
to local recoverable resources backed out.

and the lookup on that code is:

--------------------------------------------------------------------------
APC3

 Explanation:  An attempt to run the program failed because the program
 appeared to be defined to CICS as Language Environment/370 but no Language
 Environment/370 support was present in the system and no other language
 environment was able to run the program.

 System Action:  The transaction is abnormally terminated and the program is
 disabled.

 User Response:  Redefine the program to CICS in a language other than
 Language Environment/370.

 Module:  DFHAPLI
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 V2.7, CICS TX1.2 -- help?

- **From:** far@moblixnet.dk (Frank Allan Rasmussen)
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T12:13:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b10eebd.460990@news.mobilixnet.dk>`
- **References:** `<3b107a7f$0$16914@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
On Sat, 26 May 2001 22:49:14 -0500, "Paul Raulerson"
<praul@isot.N0SPAM.com> wrote:

>I'm on a OS/390 V2.7 system with TX 1.2 installed. The problem seems to be a
>mismatch between the COBOL compiler and the CICS 5.2 system at the heart of
>TX, and some kind of conflict with LE code.

How is the program defined in RDO?
See if you can make a CEDA E G(*) PROG(xxxx) and check if the program
is defined as cobol.


Mvh
Frank Allan Rasmussen
mailto://far@mobilixnet.dk
http://www.mobilixnet.dk/~mob55192
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 V2.7, CICS TX1.2 -- help?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T09:33:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9er35o$o47$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3b107a7f$0$16914@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
At one time, there was a "start up parameter" that was required to tell CICS
that LE was available/in use.  I don't think that CICS 5.2 still requires
it, but I won't swear to it.

Also, do you have all the LE *CICS* libraries in the correct DD's in your
CICS start up JCL?  These are described in the COBOL Installation Guide
(among other places).  Let me know if you need an exact reference.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 V2.7, CICS TX1.2 -- help?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praul@isot.N0SPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T10:25:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b111dc9$0$1310@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<3b107a7f$0$16914@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net> <9er35o$o47$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Well, a quick browse through the bookreader files for the COBOL and CICS
installation didn't bring up anything
that I immediately recognized as being a startup parameter or a discussion
on which LE libraries to link. Would
you mind sending that reference along please?

Thanks
-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 V2.7, CICS TX1.2 -- help?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-Flour <gsf@home.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T21:09:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b12be68$3$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<3b107a7f$0$16914@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net> <9er35o$o47$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3b111dc9$0$1310@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net> <9esa9e$nri$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On 27 May 2001 at 20:40, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> said:

> http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea5100/8.0?

>"8.0 Chapter 8. Making Language Environment Available under CICS"

This section may date back to the early days of LE/370 and I find it quite misleading.  On most of today's OS/390 systems, LE is already in LPA (SCEELPA) and link-list (SCEERUN) and SCEERUN should NOT be added to the CICS STEPLIB.  No wonder the CICS STEPLIBs of so many OS/390 installations contain SCEERUN, which causes duplicate copies of huge modules (e.g. CEEPLPKA) to be loaded in private memory, wasting megabytes of real memory.  Also, it is unclear why SCEERUN has to be present both in the normal search (STEPLIB/LPA/LNKLST) _and_ in DFHRPL.

 Gilbert Saint-flour
 Automated Migration Services
 http://members.home.net/gsf/
```

---

<a id="t-6bfe7298cd"></a>
## Thread: Motivating Programmers — 95 this month / 105 total

### Motivating Programmers

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T18:11:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
Hi guys,
  I'm in a discussion with a colleague (we're both currently academics)
about the proper roll of the project leader (or any manager) in
motivating the team members, in particular, program developers, beit
they programmers, designers, analysts or anywhere else in the SDLC.  My
view (and I know my view has holes in it, I just don't know where those
holes are, that's why I am posting this question to CLC) is that the PL
shouldn't have to mess with this stuff.  Programming is a highly
profession position, and a person should have their act together before
they get to that level in the organization.  Now I'm not talking about
showing appreciation for a job well done.  I'm all for that, but there
is a fine line between appreciation and manipulation.  Nor am I talking
about hygenic factors, I am all for those too, for example, giving the
programmer her own printer even though the cost-benefit analysis shows a
slightly negative bias.   What I'm talking about is motivating the
programmer who doesn't even want to be there, someone who would rather
be home watching TV, or someone who just doesn't like doing that kind of
work and can't bring himself to focus on the task.  My opinion is that
the PL shouldn't waste his time on it and just get rid of the damn
person and try his luck with someone else.
  My colleague's response is that I am living in a dream world.  To her
IS work is a joke.  All group members are un-motivated.   She says I'd
only be wasting my time because the chances are so great that I'd only
get someone else with similar problems, and even if I did get someone
with a good work ethic, the rest of the group and the company would
bring that person down.  I guess her's is the "an eagle can't fly when
surrounded by turkeys" theory and my response is, in that case shoot the
damn turkeys.
  I be interested in reading practitioners views on this.
  Thanks,
John Grznar

P.S.  And no docdwarf, this isn't a homework assignment.  :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T18:47:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010527144723.08020.00001632@ng-ce1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
>From: John Grznar jgrznar@earthlink.net 
>Date: 2001-05-27 14:11 Eastern Daylight Time
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The management textbooks I've read say that you motivate
a team by striving to obtain consensus, by reporting team
progress, and by having a pizza party after any successful
team project.  
As a frequent team member, I go along with that. 
The textbooks also say you motivate individuals by publicly
recognizing them for any achievement.
As an individual, I go along with that. 
The Unabomber says that true happiness comes only
from the accomplishment of a difficult task.  
I go along with that, too.  

The textbooks also say that if you have an adamantly 
uncooperative employee, you should terminate him.  Such
employees are actually the exception, not the rule. 

Managers who sincerely believe that the average employee is 
unwilling to work do a lot of harm, especially in America.  
Unfortunately, there are a lot of that type of manager in business.  
Fortunately, none of them shows enough success rate to rise 
above middle management levels.  

Even the U.S. Army, the bastion of bureacracy and bullwhip 
mentality, has found in field experiments that soldiers perform 
better if you praise their successes and ignore their failures, 
rather than chewing them out for failure while ignoring success.  

Your colleague is full of shit.  (But you already knew that.) 




Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T23:45:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B12E7A2.D886CE6F@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <20010527144723.08020.00001632@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```


Ross Klatte wrote:

> The management textbooks I've read say that you motivate
> a team by striving to obtain consensus, by reporting team
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sorry, but I am not interested in hearing what textbooks have to say.  I know
what they say (at least, I think, pretty damn much so.)  But your statement
that you go along with it, is worth a response.  I'm all in favor of pizza
parties, IF THEY ARE GIVEN IN A SINCERE SPIRIT OF APPRECIATION, AND NOT
MANIPULATION.  Unfortunately, this is more a matter of perception than
substance.  Giving such a party in a cynical shop can really backfire.


> The textbooks also say you motivate individuals by publicly
> recognizing them for any achievement.
> As an individual, I go along with that.

This issue is far, far more complex than that.


>
> The Unabomber says that true happiness comes only
> from the accomplishment of a difficult task.
> I go along with that, too.

The Unabomber?  Um, let's make sure we keep this as email and never switch to
snail mail, ok?  Any proclamation as to what true happiness is is bullshit,
and I say that emphatically.  However, I do know that there is a joy to be
had by being very, very good at something worthwhile.  I know because I've
experienced it, I will neither claim nor disclaim that I am experiencing it,
but I know it is there.  I call it the joy of professionalism.  I am not
saying that everyone who is very, very good at what they do experiences that
joy, for example, they may have set their goals too high or too low, but that
joy is there to those people for the taking.

>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My feeling is that it is more 50-50.  My colleague feels it is more the rule
than the exception.  After all, that has been her experience.  I'll bet you
have worked with more cooperative employees than uncooperative ones (to use
your terms.)  And my experience has been 50-50...

>
> Managers who sincerely believe that the average employee is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

1.  Why "especially in America?"  Never mind, different, but very good,
topic.  Btw, I disagree with you on that.  I think poor management permeates
any country as it does here.
2.  Ahhh, the old Peter Principle.  People rise to their level of
incompetence and stay there.


> Even the U.S. Army, the bastion of bureacracy and bullwhip
> mentality, has found in field experiments that soldiers perform
> better if you praise their successes and ignore their failures,
> rather than chewing them out for failure while ignoring success.
>

Again, the issue isn't nearly that simple.

>
> Your colleague is full of shit.  (But you already knew that.)
>

You couldn't be more wrong.  She may be full of stinky, gaseous, hot air like
me, but she is not full of shit.  We have had many discourses over the years
and she has oftentimes raise very valid points that have caused me to
reevaluate my position, and she has done it a few times on this topic too.
And I'll tell you something else, I love it when someone does that.  I know I
may come across as a pig-headed old (well middle-aged at least) coot to a lot
of people, but the only reason I engage in deep discussions is to change me.
I couldn't care less what my "opponent" goes away believing, well that's not
entirely true, but close enough.

John Grznar
```

Peter,

Sorry but I did reply to your message.  But I too just noticed that for some
reason it has not been received at the newsgroup.  Fortunately, there was a copy
in my sent mail folder, so I copied and pasted my complete reply to your message
at the bottom of this one.  Also, I want to thank you very much for your reply.
Yours was the only one that I have read so far (others have been received but
I haven't gotten to them yet) that actually answered the question posed.  It is
precisely stuff like your husband-wife team anecdote that I am looking for.
Some of your other stuff I agree with, some of it I don't, but those are just my
opinions offered up for debate.

Again thanks.

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> John,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> No comment from you.

Peter, I could not have been several days ago because I just posted my question
yesterday.  Don't be so impatient.


>
> Meanwhile, you have studiously debated how many angels can occupy a pin head
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> I have read this thread with interest.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> However, having been a programmer for 35 years, I have some inkling into
the
> mindset...
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> chat, with a genuine desire to put issues on the table and address them,
and
> listen to what the other person says, is the ONLY chance of reaching the
> real problem.
> The Corollary: Never beat up the Team as a whole. If they are failing
that
> is MY fault, not theirs.

Your point is just good common sense, but your corollary may or may not be
true.  Sometimes it is your fault, but sometimes it is not. However,
if you want to believe that that is always the case, as a way of motivating
yourself,
then I certainly have no argument with that. But basically, you are putting
the
world on your shoulders.  Do you really think you are strong enough to
handle
it?

As a side point.  Let's reverse it.  If the team is a good team, is it
because
of your
efforts or theirs?

>
>
> 2. NEVER lie to the team or individual members of it. Keep your promises.
If
> there is something sensitive which cannot be divulged "yet", tell them
that
> and assure them that as soon as you ethically can inform them you will.
Give
> an assurance it will not affect the outcome if you can honestly do that.
If
> you can't, then tell them that we will operate on what we have at
present,
> until we hear differently.

You've been reading my mail.  I've already said this.  If a manager of good
professional team members is worthy of his position, then just being
straight
forward and honest with his team should be enough.  Of course, this does
not preclude giving advice when asked for.

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Agreed.  Lower level employees should be given at least three warnings.
But at
the professional level one is plenty.  Only for the grossest of reasons
should
anyone
be discharged without warning: harassing other employees, intentionally
destroying
expensive company equipment, farting in my office...

>
> You tell them what the project is hoping to achieve, tell them they will
be
> treated fairly (no matter what their Management have said or done to them
in
> the past), express your own enthusiasm for the project (and if you don't
> have any, don't be there...), tell them you want them as part of it and
it
> will be satisfying and fun.  Having fun, as well as working hard, is an
> essential part of the mix for successful Project Management and, sadly,
too
> many Managers overlook this, concerned that the people they answer to
will
> not approve. See next rule...

Now here is something I couldn't agree with more provided you can and will
back up your words.  In fact, I would go so far as to say that the biggest
difference
between a good manager and a poor one is that the good manager provides an
environment that is conducive to good employees (at any level) excelling.

>
>
> If the team Member fails to respond to your offer of a place on the team
> with a "clean slate" (4 weeks is a fair period to see what happens), tell
> them frankly there is no place for them and ask them what they want to
do.
> Get them removed from the team as quickly as possible.

Yes, and the key here is to get rid of them as "quickly as possible,"
before
they
infect everyone else.  IF IT WASN'T FOR THAT BAD ATTITUDE SPREADING,
my opinion would be different.

>
>
> I learned this one the hard way. As a new Project Manager I really didn't
> want to fire anybody (still don't) but I had some problems with a husband
> and wife team where he was really competent and she was about as useful
as a
> chocolate firescreen.
>
> Unfortunately, the two of them were extremely disruptive as she made
bullets
> for him to fire...Very difficult situation. I discussed it with my Boss
who
> was older, wiser, and much more experienced. His verdict: "Fire them
both.
> Do it now. It's your call; I'll support you." I was shocked and decided
to
> persevere... little chats with both of them together and separately.
>
> My attempt to be fair and avoid unpleasantness was perceived as weakness,
> and the attitudes and destructive activity hardened. Six months later I
> fired them both and apologised to my Boss for not doing it sooner. I
worked
> out later, my bad decision had cost us around 20 man weeks of work and an
> unquantifiable bad effect on morale in general. Once they were gone,
things
> rapidly improved and we got it in on time (just...).

I'm glad to hear you apologized, it shows you have class.

>
>
> Your colleague is right on this one, John, although I believe her
cynicism
> about the impossibility of ever getting anyone who is (or, arguably, can
be)
> motivated is unjustified.

After what you just said?  I'm not sure you understand who is saying what
here.
Unless, I'm misreading your post.

>
>
> 4. NEVER fear, attempt to propitiate, or gain Brownie points with the
people
> you report to. Develop an honest working relationship, keep them informed
> and get their help when things are stalled. The attitude should be: "My
> management are there to support my project when I need help, in exactly
the
> same way as I support my team when they need it. There is no "US" and
> "THEM"...we are working for the same overall goals."

Absolutely, always, be forthright and honest with those above you as well
as
below you and among your peers too.  If you are qualified to hold your
position,

that will be enough to get the job done well.  I stress this qualification,
because
no matter how forthright and honest I may be, I could not manage well a
professional
football team (ok DD, "coach" a professional football team.)

>
>
> There are many other points which are really common sense and can be
summed
> up as "treat people the way you want them to treat you".  I believe the
ones
> above are the MOST important but I certainly don't minimise things like
> encouraging and acknowledging individuals.

NO!  This is a VERY BIG AND COMMON mistake.  WHAT MOTIVATES
WORKERS IS NOT THE SAME AS WHAT MOTIVATES MANAGERS!
And I dare say Peter, this is why you prefer to be programmer rather than a
manager.

>
>
> I usually do some "team building" from time to time and allocate a
> percentage of the overall budget for this. It is not just pizza but can
be
> something a bit special when particular project milestones are achieved.
I
> always make the point that it is the Company's way of acknowledging what
we
> are doing. On some occasions I have paid for this out of my own pocket.
(the
> team had done an outstanding task and there was no money in the budget
for a
> party, or Company policy was that they didn't do this for employees). On
> those occasions I simply say that I really appreciate the efforts being
made
> (which is true; see 2 above.)
>

I hope you didn't let them know the money came out of your own pocket.  I
am
all in favor showing them appreciation, IF THE TEAM DID A TRULY GOOD JOB.
What I am opposed to is doing this as an attempt to change a bad team into
a
good
team-- THAT'S MANIPULATION.

>
> BOTTOM LINE:
>
> Programmers will respond to an environment where they are (ideally)
> acquiring new skills, being recognised for the effort they put in (not
just
> with a paycheque, but in a more personal way), and can feel that they are
> part of something that WILL be achieved and will be recognised by the
> Company. They should also feel that their Project Management is 100%
behind
> them and can be approached for help and advice on anything.
>
> Project Managers should have a sincere desire to "grow" team members and
> provide the kind of environment where this is possible.
>

Like growing children?  Come on now.  Being the parent of a 26 year old is
a lot

different than being the parent of a 6 year old.  You grow children, you
don't
grow
adults.  An adult has the right to feel she is her own person, and not
merely an

extension of you.  With a child it is proper to give advice without being
asked.  You
try to teach the child.  If the child doesn't want to learn then it is your
duty
to try
to motivate the child into wanting to learn.  With adults all you can
properly
do is to
be there for them when they come to you.

>
> I have a number of people who have worked with me on past projects and
have
> indicated they would always be happy to do so again.
>

I don't doubt it.

>
> Whether this comes down to "Motivating Programmers" or not, is not for me
to
> say.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Problem is, the eagle flys away and all you are left with is a bunch of
turkeys.

John Grznar



>
> "John Grznar" <jgrznar@earthlink.net> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> > programmer her own printer even though the cost-benefit analysis shows
a
> > slightly negative bias.   What I'm talking about is motivating the
> > programmer who doesn't even want to be there, someone who would rather
> > be home watching TV, or someone who just doesn't like doing that kind
of
> > work and can't bring himself to focus on the task.  My opinion is that
> > the PL shouldn't waste his time on it and just get rid of the damn
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > surrounded by turkeys" theory and my response is, in that case shoot
the
> > damn turkeys.
> >   I be interested in reading practitioners views on this.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 http://www.usenet.com

"John Grznar" <jgrznar@earthlink.net> wrote in message

>
> Sorry but I did reply to your message.  But I too just noticed that for

message
> at the bottom of this one.  Also, I want to thank you very much for your

but

Thanks for the response. Comments interspersed below.

Pete.

>
> Peter, I could not have been several days ago because I just posted my
question
> yesterday.  Don't be so impatient.
>
You forget that in New Zealand we are a day ahead of you. As I write this it
is already Tuesday here. My response was sent on Saturday. And Patience is a
virtue I have to grit my teeth to engage...except when training people; then
I seem to have an endless supply of it...<G>
>
> > The Corollary: Never beat up the Team as a whole. If they are failing
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> true.  Sometimes it is your fault, but sometimes it is not.

Actually, looking for elements to assign blame to is a pointless exercise.
It doesn't matter whose "fault" it is; what ,matters is what can be done it
and what you can learn from it. I really do accept total responsibility for
the projects I manage (I always ensure I have Management empowerment to do
what is necessary BEFORE starting, otherwise I wouldn't accept the
responsibility OR the Project...) Given that they have given me their full
support, then I really do believe that if it stuffs up, it is entirely my
problem. (So, far, touch wood, I've never had one go off the rails...)

 However,
> if you want to believe that that is always the case, as a way of
motivating
> yourself,
> then I certainly have no argument with that. But basically, you are
putting
> the
> world on your shoulders.  Do you really think you are strong enough to
> handle
> it?
>

Hahaha! You really don't know me, John. The question is whether the World is
strong enough...<G>

> As a side point.  Let's reverse it.  If the team is a good team, is it
> because
> of your
> efforts or theirs?
>

All of us. Look up the meaning of "Team" <G>

>
> You've been reading my mail.  I've already said this.  If a manager of
good
> professional team members is worthy of his position, then just being
> straight
> forward and honest with his team should be enough.  Of course, this does
> not preclude giving advice when asked for.
>

It also "works" with teams who are not "good professional team members".

I have sometimes been contracted to manage the "Team from Hell" because none
of the Corporate managers would go near them...(I accept these projects with
relish. It's a bit like being an ex-competition driver; I have all the
skills but I never get to exercise them until the conditions are adverse...I
LOVE to drive in snow, sleet, rain, on twisting (preferably unsealed - there
are still a few of these in NZ)  "dangerous" roads. It gives me a chance to
use what I know... so is it with Project Management. The "easy" projects,
although always challenging, are far less satisfying than the ones that
NOBODY expected to get done.

If you like anecdotes I could tell you about a certain project in Sweden
where the "team" made the Dirty Dozen look like a Church Choir. I fired one
guy on the second day (mainly as an example to the others <G>) and escorted
him to the airport and put him on the plane back to UK - he thought I was
bluffing the whole while - I then personally undertook his workload (in
addition to my own) so as the Project wouldn't slip.)

I had a fist fight with another member (I really thought I was gone on that
one, but the next day he came and saw me and said he had it coming (which he
did) and wasn't going to mention it again if I didn't. We shook hands and
all was fine after that...(I don't recommend this as a management technique
and that is the ONLY time I have ever smacked a project member - it was in a
disco, not in the office - What he said/did was beyond the pale and required
extreme admonition...<G>), gained the respect of a member whose IQ was well
into  the "Genius" level (he thought "Managers" were the next form of life
after pond algae and treated all of them (except me) with blatant contempt).

Nine months later when I was in some hot water with their Management, ALL of
these guys supported me to the point of putting their jobs on the line (it
was resolved satisfactorily and the Manager concerned realised he had got it
wrong and apologized to all of us). We got the Project in despite the
Swedish Winter and to the amazement of everyone in the Corporate back home.
These same guys who had hated, loathed, and detested me at first, said they
wouldn't go back unless I did...

> >
> > They get ONE chance.
> >
>
> Agreed.  Lower level employees should be given at least three warnings.

Nope. Not on my watch. There ARE no "lower level" employees. EVERYBODY is a
valued team member and gets treated exactly the same. ONE chance applies to
everybody.


> But at
> the professional level one is plenty.  Only for the grossest of reasons
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Nobody farts in my office...they sometimes shit themselves <G>

>>Having fun, as well as working hard, is an
> > essential part of the mix for successful Project Management and, sadly,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
There is little point in saying something you cannot/will not back up... I
covered your last sentence under "growing" the team. see below.

> >
> >
> > If the team Member fails to respond to your offer of a place on the team
> > with a "clean slate" (4 weeks is a fair period to see what happens),
tell
> > them frankly there is no place for them and ask them what they want to
> do.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> my opinion would be different.

Well, my concern is not really with the bad attitude spreading. I can deal
with that, and in general, intelligent people know when an attitude is
counter productive. I learned to get rid of them because they are
sandbaggers and freeloaders who will let other team members do their work,
and because keeping them has a bad effect on morale (nobody likes to see the
guy next store freeloading and getting away with it...) Carrying them is
UNFAIR. That's why they have to either shape up or ship out.



> >
> > My attempt to be fair and avoid unpleasantness was perceived as
weakness,
> > and the attitudes and destructive activity hardened. Six months later I
> > fired them both and apologised to my Boss for not doing it sooner. I
> worked
> > out later, my bad decision had cost us around 20 man weeks of work and
an
> > unquantifiable bad effect on morale in general. Once they were gone,
> things
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks. I call it "common courtesy" but I like your word better...<G>

> >
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Unless, I'm misreading your post.

Nope. I still believe there are many people in IT who do care about doing
the best they can. These people are self motivated. I also don't accept that
the "Company will only grind them down". If that happens, it is because the
person concerned allowed it to happen. (Not to say, that review of company
policy and Management implementation of it shouldn't be done, when staff
have issues or morale is generally low...)

YOU are responsible for how you feel about things and the condition you are
in. Not your colleagues. Not your workload. Not the Company. Not the asshole
manager you have. YOU.

I therefore totally reject your colleague's cynicism.

> >
> >
> > 4. NEVER fear, attempt to propitiate, or gain Brownie points with the
> people
> > you report to. Develop an honest working relationship, keep them
informed
> > and get their help when things are stalled. The attitude should be: "My
> > management are there to support my project when I need help, in exactly
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> that will be enough to get the job done well.  I stress this
qualification,
> because
> no matter how forthright and honest I may be, I could not manage well a
> professional
> football team (ok DD, "coach" a professional football team.)
>
You are confusing skill sets here. I know NOTHING about American Football
but would gladly coach a team if asked to. (I don't know how well we'd do,
but we'd have a Hell of a time doing it...<G>) It is a question of
priorities...personal growth has to be the highest priority (for ALL of the
team, including myself), always. See "growth" below.


> >
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> And I dare say Peter, this is why you prefer to be programmer rather than
a
> manager.
>
Definitely disagree here. It isn't what motivates WORKERS or MANAGERS, it is
what motivates INDIVIDUAL PEOPLE. Treat your team as PEOPLE, not according
to the "hat" they are wearing (Application Programmer, Business Analyst,
Manager, or whatever...)

I don't PREFER to be a Programmer; I AM a Programmer. I enjoy managing
projects, but I LOVE Programming. And it has nothing to do with my personal
motivation. I find writing COBOL relaxing, satisfying, and enjoyable. That's
why I do it. I do it for myself, not just because someone may be paying me
to do it.

I also enjoy the challenges of melding a team to achieve something. And it
is the best  (legal) way I know to make a living and have fun.

> >
> > I usually do some "team building" from time to time and allocate a
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> all in favor showing them appreciation, IF THE TEAM DID A TRULY GOOD JOB.

My teams ALWAYS do a good job....<G>

> What I am opposed to is doing this as an attempt to change a bad team into
> a
> good
> team-- THAT'S MANIPULATION.

No, that's propitiation. Trying to buy them off or curry their favour.
Manipulation is something totally different and can be very positive. (It is
when I do it...it mightn't be when you  do it...<G>)

Anyway, shouldn't we use ANY means to "change a bad team into a good team"?
(personaly, I don't see teams in terms of "good" and "bad"; there is a team.
It works well or it doesn't. If it doesn't then I failed...hasn't happened
yet, but I treat every new assignment with respect and never get blase about
success...it takes work. From all concerned.)
>
>>They should also feel that their Project Management is 100%
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Like growing children?  Come on now.

No, I don't advocate treating teams like children (even when they sometimes
behave that way), but I DO advocate helping each individual to grow. (Doing
this is usually good for my growth as well).

One of the reasons I am the Manager is because I have the experience and
knowledge to handle problems and people. If nobody on the project learned
anything from my stay there, then that project is a dismal failure in my
book, irrespective of whether it came in on time and on budget.

I have a duty to transfer skills. It is one of the things I'm paid for.
Those skills can be "technical" or "management" or even "personal attitude"
but you can bet somebody will acquire something from me during our
association. I, of course, will also acquire much from my interactions with
the other team members. That's what I mean by "Growth".

The most rewarding part of Project Management is seeing somebody smile when
they realise how to overcome a problem. Maybe you just acted as a "sounding
board" and walked them through the problem, maybe you pulled a technical
technique like a rabbit out of a hat, maybe you drew on past management
experience and helped them solve a problem that was worrying them, or maybe
it was just a personal word of encouragement. Whatever it was, they learned
something and the result is seen on their face. That is growth.

And the warm feeling it leaves you with is part of your growth.



Being the parent of a 26 year old is
> a lot
> different than being the parent of a 6 year old.  You grow children, you
> don't  grow  adults.  An adult has the right to feel she is her own
person, and not
> merely an
>
> extension of you.  With a child it is proper to give advice without being
> asked.  You
> try to teach the child.  If the child doesn't want to learn then it is
your
> duty
> to try
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well I agree that's all YOU can do. I try to be a bit more pro-active...<G>

> >
> > PS. An eagle will fly whether it is surrounded by turkeys or not. If the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> turkeys.

Thats why you pay the eagle to teach the turkeys to be the best damn turkeys
they can be...some of them will actually metamorphose into eagles if you do
it right...others will still be turkeys, but they will have learned not to
get in the way of eagles...and still others will be turkeys but with some of
the admirable characteristics of the eagle.

It is still a better flock of turkeys than it was before the eagle came..<G>

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T18:52:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1545D9.8BAD9057@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <20010527144723.08020.00001632@ng-ce1.aol.com> <3B12E7A2.D886CE6F@earthlink.net> <spjQ6.165314$ho6.10147363@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3B13214F.8BE16C1A@earthlink.net> <3b127f29_1@Usenet.com> <3B13E476.DC4E6D89@earthlink.net> <3b1331ce_1@news2.newsfeeds.com>`

```
Peter, I really do LOVE your style, man!  There are some MAJOR issues I strongly
disagree with you on, but then if there wasn't we'd have nothing worthwhile to
discourse on, now is there?   I think you and I are, in some ways, a lot alike.
I hope you don't take that as slam, because I sure don't.

About 99% of what you say is what I believed when I was an engineering
department manager.  Now don't go getting all huffy-puffy.  I'm not saying I'm
an old-salt and your a young whipinsnapper. I'm not saying that at all.  If you
want to argue as to who OUGHT to know more, I will gladly concede the point and
say you do (in fact, I'd be willing to concede that point to just about
anyone.)  Nor do I give squat as to who DOES know more, because that is a
completely meaningless question.

I like to check my theories out point by point. I know some of my ideas are
right and some are wrong, I just never know which ones are which.  If you feel
the same about yours, then what do you say we square off, go toe-to-toe,
no-holds-barred, and just find out which ideas are better? (and please note, I
didn't say "whose" I said "which.")

I think you are the kind of guy who isn't afraid to take or give a good punch.
And me, like Rocky says, "I can take a lesson, or give one."  So, to you I throw
down the gauntlet.

I assume you accept the challenge, so here comes the first punch.

The loyalty of the project leader should always (except in the ethical extreme)
be to upper management.  The PL should NEVER try to be a member of the project
team.  The PL is there as an agent of upper management to coordinate, monitor,
evaluate, arbitrate, supervise only when necessary, assist when asked, and act
as a liason between upper management and the team.  In the "assist" category, I
include not only technical assistance, but also resource assistance.  For
example, if the team wants a new printer then it is the PLs responsibility to
get authorization for it from upper management.  Just how vigorously the PL
makes this appeal is entirely up to the PL.  If the PL thinks the team should
really have that printer, he should be willing to fight tooth-and-nail with
upper management to get it.  If he thinks the group should not have the printer,
he should only pass on the request, with the recommendation it not be granted.

You, why you're too much of a pussy.  You're there to play with the other
people.  I'll bet when you were a kid you cried when you weren't allowed to play
with the other children.  You should maintain your distance from them, or else
your personal feelings will get in the way of your rational decision making.

That project the team is working on is their project.  It is not yours.  You
have your own project to play with.  If it is any consolation to you, your
project is far, far more important to upper management than their project is.
Furthermore, your project is far, far more difficult than theirs is.  Theirs is
trivial compare to yours.  For your project is to learn how to be a good PL and
manager in general!

John Grznar
```

> really have that printer, he should be willing to fight tooth-and-nail
with
> upper management to get it.  If he thinks the group should not have the
printer,
> he should only pass on the request, with the recommendation it not be
granted.
>

You call that a punch? ROFL!

Loyalty is to all parties. There is no conflict here. As I said before,
there is no US and THEM; EVERYBODY wants the project in, otherwise they
wouldn't be doing it. (They certainly wouldn't be paying for me to do it...I
don't come cheap.)

I'd simply buy the printer and claim it later. If they wouldn't pay I'd say
"OK" and take it with me when I left.

> You, why you're too much of a pussy.  You're there to play with the other
> people.  I'll bet when you were a kid you cried when you weren't allowed
to play
> with the other children.  You should maintain your distance from them, or
else
> your personal feelings will get in the way of your rational decision
making.
>

There is a time for pussy and a time for tiger. I have never found a
conflict between my personal feelings and getting my projects in.

(And I never was a kid...I was synthesized in Uncle Victor's Laboratory
during a thunderstorm and we escaped just before the howling mob broke the
castle doors down...)


> That project the team is working on is their project.  It is not yours.

Nope. It is the Company's. The ownership of it is established through the
Project Sponsor who allocates the budget and makes the Business case for it.
Although he "owns" it, once we start working on it, it is ours. By "ours" I
mean the team, and the team includes the Project Sponsor (among other people
seconded to the team from the Business, on a full or part time basis. This
comes into the area of DRAD versus the Waterfall, and I don't intend to
discuss that here. Come to CW2001 and I'll explain it there...)

> You
> have your own project to play with.  If it is any consolation to you, your
> project is far, far more important to upper management than their project
is.
> Furthermore, your project is far, far more difficult than theirs is.
Theirs is
> trivial compare to yours.  For your project is to learn how to be a good
PL and
> manager in general!
>

I have a number of personal goals when I undertake projects. I review them
every day of the project and doing that enables me to stay focussed. Your
trite statement of what YOU imagine my goal might be, is just one tiny part
of it.

Some points to consider:

1. Continually watching re-runs of Rocky movies can have a deleterious
effect on the intellect, in particular the ability to recognise what is real
and what is not. In real life, Rocky would've had the shit kicked out of
him... He would've then gone to an anger management centre where he would've
been re-programmed and finally found his niche as a flight steward on
American Airlines... Ability to mumble and extreme endurance and strength
are apparently pre-requisites for the job...

2. The confrontational macho approach to solving problems only works when it
is backed by overwhelming numbers or superior technological or strategic
advantage (not even then, always, viz. Agincourt, the Spartans at
Thermopylae, Drake against the Armada, The Viet Cong against the US...).
Even when force DOES win the day, it is an uneasy victory and inevitably
results in rebellion and unrest. At best a short term solution.

3. Goodwill, being prepared to examine any problem, and being prepared to
compromise in a spirit of fairness to all concerned, are the best long term
tools for settling disputes.

And if that makes me a pussy in your eyes, well, got any catnip? <walks off
purring with tail held high...>

Pete.




Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T03:00:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1709AC.AAC3657@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <20010527144723.08020.00001632@ng-ce1.aol.com> <3B12E7A2.D886CE6F@earthlink.net> <spjQ6.165314$ho6.10147363@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3B13214F.8BE16C1A@earthlink.net> <3b127f29_1@Usenet.com> <3B13E476.DC4E6D89@earthlink.net> <3b1331ce_1@news2.newsfeeds.com> <3B1545D9.8BAD9057@earthlink.net> <3b147079_1@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter, I promise I will try and act civilized, ok?

I don't know why but I do have a very difficult time getting people to
understand the direction I am coming from.  Too many people, it seems
to me are only interested in changing the world.  That sentence can
easily be read as trying to get stuff from the world.  I have not meet very
many people intent on changing themselves.  Probably because you can
not change yourself until you realize something in yourself that needs
changing.  But this realization requires a glimpse of yourself through the
TRUE eyes of other people.  But this RARELY seems to happen today
because people are nearly void of the ability to give and receive constructive
criticism. (Myself included.  I know my technique could use some
improvement.)

Please don't misunderstand me.  I am not interested in changing you.  You
are your own problem just as I am my own problem.

If you would rather hold our conversation privately, away from the group
that would be ok with me.  I have no preference one way or the other.

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:


> >
> I'll reserve judgement until (if) we meet... are you attending CW2001? Good
> deals for Academics...see www.cobolreport.com or www.aboutlegacycoding.com
>

It saddens me to know that a few others on this group do not have
your wisdom to reserve judgement.

I doubt if I can swing it, but I'll see.  I usually find those conferences a
waste,
but this one might be worth it just to sit down and have a drink with you.
BUT NO DISCO!


>
> >If you
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Contrary to popular opinion, I NEVER argue just for the sake of arguing.
I always have a purpose.  It is just that oftentimes I will keep my purpose
to myself.


>
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And your complacency is your problem <G>


>
> I also don't understand why you are using such an agressive analogy. I have
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not "better" between us, but me-to-me and you-to-you.


>
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> would I go looking for trouble? I have nothing to prove...

If you have nothing to prove to yourself, then you are dead.

RIP

John Grznar
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

```
"John Grznar" <jgrznar@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:3B1709AC.AAC3657@earthlink.net...
> Peter, I promise I will try and act civilized, ok?
>
Ah, Glasshopper, already you are modifying your behaviour to accommodate me.

Is this LEARNING?

Truly, as the rock is unmoved by the sea and the wind, so am I unmoved by
"civilized" or "uncivilized" <G>

(Quick tip: civilized is better...not for me, for you..<G>)

> I don't know why but I do have a very difficult time getting people to
> understand the direction I am coming from.

Then review your method of communication. (And try using the indicators
occasionally...<G>)

> Too many people, it seems
> to me are only interested in changing the world.

You spend a lot of time around young people. This is good for a tutor.

>That sentence can
> easily be read as trying to get stuff from the world.  I have not meet
very
> many people intent on changing themselves.  Probably because you can
> not change yourself until you realize something in yourself that needs
> changing.

Or you realize the serenity of Being that comes from not giving a Damn <G>

> But this realization requires a glimpse of yourself through the
> TRUE eyes of other people.  But this RARELY seems to happen today
> because people are nearly void of the ability to give and receive
constructive
> criticism. (Myself included.  I know my technique could use some
> improvement.)

"constructive criticism" is an oxymoron. Criticism is always destructive.
Sometimes it is necessary to tear something down in order to rebuild it, so
not all criticism is bad; but it is destructive. If you are totally reliant
on the view of others in order to see yourself clearly, then you have a
major problem. (In fact, it accounts for much of your previous posts).

Are the people whose opinions you seek so perfect that you should model
yourself on them?

When you encounter criticism ask only two questions: Is it true? Is it fair?

If the answer to both is "yes" then take it on board and do something about
it. Otherwise, get on with your life.

Be what you want to be, do what you want to do (provided it doesn't harm
others), and so attain to have what you want to have. You don't need praise
or criticism. Just do it.

You will NEVER be all things to all people. Even the Great Teachers were
unable to attain this.

>
> Please don't misunderstand me.  I am not interested in changing you.  You
> are your own problem just as I am my own problem.
>

Truly, Glasshopper, you are quick to assign problems. Can you be as quick to
solve them?


> If you would rather hold our conversation privately, away from the group
> that would be ok with me.  I have no preference one way or the other.
>

Have you thought about what the group might prefer?  I would think that by
now most people are pretty bored with this exchange and it is so WAY off
topic that they have every right to be...(mind you, wandering off topic is
pretty par for the course in this NG...)

>
> It saddens me to know that a few others on this group do not have
> your wisdom to reserve judgement.
>
> I doubt if I can swing it, but I'll see.  I usually find those conferences
a
> waste,
> but this one might be worth it just to sit down and have a drink with you.
> BUT NO DISCO!
>
LOL! That was a long time ago. These days I find discos vexatious to the
spirit. I am ALWAYS pleased to have a drink and a chat with ANYONE. I'm
looking forward to a number of these (drinks AND chats!<G>) at CW2001. The
indications are that this will be a very worthwhile Conference.

>
> Contrary to popular opinion, I NEVER argue just for the sake of arguing.
> I always have a purpose.  It is just that oftentimes I will keep my
purpose
> to myself.
>
I make it a policy not to argue with people who have hidden agendas. The
logic becomes twisted by the hidden agenda and the spin which is imparted
causes a confusing series of non sequiturs...just a pointless waste of time,
really. I've become very good at detecting hidden agendas now and the game
then becomes trying to suss out what the REAL agenda is, and either
addressing it or telling the person that you are not going to debate it
until they come clean.

> >
> > I don't feel the same about mine. Your uncertainty is your problem <G>.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Do not confuse "complacency" with spiritual serenity...<G>

> >
>
> Not "better" between us, but me-to-me and you-to-you.
>
I haven't the faintest idea what you're talking about here...

>
> If you have nothing to prove to yourself, then you are dead.
>
> RIP
>
So does that make me a dead pussy, then?

I refute your statement by typing this line... (not bad for a dead cat...)

Funny, despite your pronouncement, I feel remarkably alive... now where's
the catnip?
<strolls off purring with tail in the air, just like before...>

Pete.

PS While this may be an amusing diversion, it is inappropriate in this NG. I
will gladly give you the benefit of my incisive wit and wisdom at no charge,
provided you take it off line.
