###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Kent Feiler <kfeiler@cpiusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T11:42:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s9j7htcslt67t6n49sabski2fuatkq91vb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <20010527144723.08020.00001632@ng-ce1.aol.com> <3B12E7A2.D886CE6F@earthlink.net> <spjQ6.165314$ho6.10147363@news5.aus1.giganews.com> <3B13214F.8BE16C1A@earthlink.net> <3b127f29_1@Usenet.com>`

```
In a lot of groups, the problem is more that their PLs and higher
management tend to un-motivate people who would ordinarily be fairly
enthusistic.  Here are some good un-motivation techniques;

- Pay low salaries. (Particularly in Government agencies)
- Cancel projects half-way through for no apparent reason
- Set unreasonable target dates based on issues not related to the
  work to be done.
- Use consultants or software firms for all interesting projects

Some PLs treat upper management as the enemy and  the people who work
for them as family, others treat upper management as godlike beings
and the people who work for them as scum. Guess which PLs have the
motivated employees.

I find that programmers basically enjoy their work and you have to get
them to a point where they feel seriously unappreciated before they
get cynical and begin to under-achieve.


Regards,


Kent Feiler
kfeiler@cpiusa.com
www.enteract.com/~kfeiler/mypage.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praul@isot.N0SPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T14:22:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b11552f$0$3396@wodc7nh6.news.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
Well, your colleague has a lot of experience and thinking on her side.
But you both miss the point, you cannot motivate someone by
'management.'  You motivate them by leadership.

And leadership is a triangle in IT/IS worlds - it has to be a balance
between technical ability, management responsibility, and
a willingness to submerge your own ego in favor of building a winning team.
Not at all easy to do for most people. Lack of leadership is the #1
cause of middle-management disease in the U.S., at least, that is
what I think.

Your apparent style of management reminds me of people I know. If the people
working for them won't tell them what they want to hear, or agree with them
that the impossible is only a 30 day project, then those people just
don't have the right attitude.

That is pure horsehockey of course.

Your partner has obviously seen a lot of technical types being managed by
accountants. You probably have been more in an R&D environment. (I'm just
guessing here...)

As for letting someone difficult go - well, it has been my experience that
the
person who is being difficult is being so because some need they have is
not being met - either a lack of self confidence, or a lack or recognition,
or just plain they like it when you make a point of coming by their office
or
cube and saying 'good morning.' Only very rarely is it the case where the
person is incapable of doing the job.

It isn't a 'publish or perish' environment out here, but it is an
environment where
as managers, we have to learn to honestly respect our people and be
determined to
lead them. That is really hard work too, especially when you are faced with
a
bottom line.

-Paul



"John Grznar" <jgrznar@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net...
> Hi guys,
>   I'm in a discussion with a colleague (we're both currently academics)
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T00:34:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B12F314.D3B00B24@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <3b11552f$0$3396@wodc7nh6.news.uu.net>`

```
> <snip>

> But you both miss the point, you cannot motivate someone by
> 'management.'  You motivate them by leadership.
>

Don't give me that Peters & Waterman ethereal crap!  That's the kind of stuff we
teach MBAers.  And since you don't seem to have figured it out yet, I'll let you
in on a little secrete.  We teach it to most students just so they will have
some material to answer their essay questions with (seriously!)  For the
superior students (who usually are not straight A students, btw)  it's a vastly
different story.

>
> And leadership is a triangle in IT/IS worlds - it has to be a balance
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree with you 100%, your comments are pure horsehockey.  But kidding aside,
you
seem to come to rather profound judgments of people awfully quick.  That is a
nasty, nasty habit.
Take my advice and BREAK THAT HABIT.

>
> Your partner has obviously seen a lot of technical types being managed by
> accountants. You probably have been more in an R&D environment. (I'm just
> guessing here...)
>

Bad guess.

>
> As for letting someone difficult go - well, it has been my experience that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> person is incapable of doing the job.

You don't have a good grasp of the question, but ok, let's go with what you
have here.  As a manager, what have you done in these situations?  Now
remember, we're talking about an employee who is in a professional position.
Not someone who is just a coder, or someone just out of school with little
or no work experience.

>
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

IMHO the proper way for a manager to manage someone who is in a PROFESSIONAL
POSITION is to just be straightforward and honest with that person.  If you, the
manager,
are qualified to hold your position, then that should be enough.  If you are not
qualified,
then it won't be enough.  Now for non-professional, lower positioned employees
the
situation is different, but how to manage them is not the question.

John Grznar
```

```
for
an academic.

Your style is totally unsuitable for dealing with real world people who have
real world
jobs and real world problems.

In short, I was quite a bit more polite than you were in pointing out that
you have absolutely
no idea what you are talking about, how to manage people, or most
especially, how to
lead people.

My advice to you would be to go out and get a job.

-Paul
```

```
competenance.

competence you don't have is the same as cheating on an exam, or more
accurately,
like stealing from your mates.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T07:08:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B139F6A.45F20178@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <3b11552f$0$3396@wodc7nh6.news.uu.net> <3B12F314.D3B00B24@earthlink.net>`

```
John Grznar wrote:

> IMHO the proper way for a manager to manage someone who is in a PROFESSIONAL
> POSITION is to just be straightforward and honest with that person.  If you, the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> question.

Right - don't promise him an opportunity to learn a new technology then fail to give
him that opportunity.   But remember that he is motivated by opportunities to do new
stuff.

And don't assume he wants to be rewarded by being made one of you.  Just because you
like management doesn't mean he does.  A good manager figures out what the worker
likes and what he can be good at, and motivates him accordingly.
```

#### ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** ar656@freenet.carleton.ca (Luis Gomez-Almeida)
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T15:58:55-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ermbf$cel$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
IT shops are, for the most part, hierarchical groups. People in those groups
tend to do, act, and work according to what they are being told by the
hierarchy. The problem in most cases is that those in management have no
idea how to manage, let alone how to lead.  In most cases they are
technically ignorant, with no clue about what the company's business is, no
plan, no administrative knowledge, and the people skills of a skunk.

In more than 20 years in IT I have met only three PL that deserved respect.

Regards,
Luis
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T22:13:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a_eQ6.5003$pa4.614935@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <9ermbf$cel$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`

```
Luis Gomez-Almeida <ar656@freenet.carleton.ca> wrote in message
news:9ermbf$cel$1@freenet9.carleton.ca...
> IT shops are, for the most part, hierarchical groups. People in those
groups
> tend to do, act, and work according to what they are being told by the
> hierarchy. The problem in most cases is that those in management have no
> idea how to manage, let alone how to lead.  In most cases they are
> technically ignorant, with no clue about what the company's business is,
no
> plan, no administrative knowledge, and the people skills of a skunk.

I was recruited to be general manager of a software development firm in
1983. I could not program, hell, I could not turn on the damn computer
without help. (And yes, the programmers were somewhat skeptical at first).

Techincal expertise may be useful, but is hardly a necessity. The one
caution: I have encountered other managers in the IT industry who were as
technically weak as I was when I started; they just refused to acknowledge
it.  Fortunately, most of these guys don't last long.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T22:13:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9_eQ6.5002$pa4.615464@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net...
> Hi guys,
>   I'm in a discussion with a colleague (we're both currently academics)
> about the proper roll of the project leader (or any manager) in
> motivating the team members.....

Motivation of employees is not just a 'proper' function for a manager, it is
a mandatory function.


>  What I'm talking about is motivating the
>programmer who doesn't even want to be there, someone who would rather
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>person and try his luck with someone else.

You have good gut instincts, EXCEPT --- if the PL/mgr has not tried to
motivate the person, then he/she cannot possibly know this person posesses
all these negative qualities.

Investing time to motivate an employee is NOT a waste of time; it is the
job.  And, the good manager does not 'try his luck: the good manager
recruits and hires and trains well.

MCM
(manager in distibution/service industries, 1974-1993) (not in IT).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

#### ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T23:18:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YWfQ6.6800$DW1.302259@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
In article <3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>,
John Grznar  <jgrznar@earthlink.net> wrote:
>Hi guys,
>  I'm in a discussion with a colleague (we're both currently academics)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>shouldn't have to mess with this stuff.

My view is that your view is inaccurate.  If one's job involves relying on
the work of others - and the PL's job does so - then it behooves the one
relying to do anything within said person's abilities (and legal
restraings) to get a Good Job o' Work out of the 'rely-ees', if only to
satisfy Enlightened Self-Interest.

In other words... a PL's qualities are judged, in part, by the results
generated by said PL's team.  If a 'motivated' team gives better results
then a PL would be rather... short-sighted not to be concerned with such.

[snippette]

>I'm all for that, but there
>is a fine line between appreciation and manipulation.

That depends on the folks involved... I recall that somewhere in 'The Art
of War' Master Sun - or somebody else - speaks about why folks do
things... the lazy are motivated by getting away with as little effort as
possible, the greedy are motivated by gain, the scholarly are motivated by
the chance to show their own worth, etc.

[snippage]

>What I'm talking about is motivating the
>programmer who doesn't even want to be there, someone who would rather
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>person and try his luck with someone else.

One cannot turn a sow's ear into a silk purse, no... but there are
functions for both; one does not give a large dog a silk purse as a
chew-toy.

>  My colleague's response is that I am living in a dream world.  To her
>IS work is a joke.  All group members are un-motivated.   She says I'd
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>  I be interested in reading practitioners views on this.

It has been my experience that, quite frequently, when a person says 'Most
folks think/act in (manner)' said person is actually saying '*I* think/act
in (manner)...'; if this is what is happening here then it might be
concluded that your colleague is a bitter and unhappy person.

>  Thanks,
>John Grznar
>
>P.S.  And no docdwarf, this isn't a homework assignment.  :-)

Too late... I did it for you, anyhow.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T02:11:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1309D0.EA06A7E@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <YWfQ6.6800$DW1.302259@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```


NA wrote:

My view is that your view is inaccurate.  If one's job involves relying on

> the work of others - and the PL's job does so - then it behooves the one
> relying to do anything within said person's abilities (and legal
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, my view is that your view is inaccurate, so there!  :-P
If my performance as a PL depends on the performance of my team (and I of
course agree that it does,) then give me a good team to begin with.  Now
again,
I'm not talking about someone who has had a good professional attitude in the
past, but for some devastating reason is not currently performing up to snuff,

e.g., their spouse has just been diagnosed with terminal cancer, their kids
have
been giving them a lot of trouble, they've been suddenly struck with a heavy
financial burden, alcoholism, etc.  If that is the case, then the manager
should be
willing to give that team member a lot, and I do mean a lot, of leeway.  But
that is ALL a good manager should do.  He shouldn't try to pry into the person
with phony pep talks
or forcing UNCALLED FOR advice on the person.  Now if the good professional
asks for advice, then YES, by all means, spend hours talking about it with
them.  But
I am not asking about how to manage good professionals, that is fairly easy,
just don't
over manage them and just be aware that what motivates you isn't what
necessarily
motivates them.  What I am asking about is motivating the troublemaker, the
cynical
brat.  My hypothesis is that if the troublemaker is in a professional
position, don't even
try.  Just get rid of him and the sooner the better, before he infects
everyone else.


>
> [snippette]
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, different people are motivated by different things.  And PLs are
motivated by
different things than team members.  For example, PLs (read managers) are
motivated by getting more responsibility than team members.  So much so that
a manager would ALMOST be willing to take a pay cut to get it.  Team members
OTOH see being hit with additional responsibility as a form of punishment and
won't take it unless they are given an enormous pay increase.  Another
example,
team members work to support their life-style, managers structure their
life-style
to support their work.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T20:57:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010528165711.04455.00001811@ng-fv1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3B1407B8.74A0D28F@earthlink.net>`

```
>From: John Grznar jgrznar@earthlink.net 
>Date: 2001-05-28 16:14 Eastern Daylight Time

>Well, if you would remove your assumptions about the first part, 
>then you would see that both parts are the same.  (It's how would 
>you handle the "bad attitude professional level employee.")  I say 
>just get rid of him, my colleague says, no, it is the managers 
>responsibility to help him.

You stated that your colleague's estimate of worthless employees
in a software shop was 100%.  Her alleged opinion is that since
all are worthless, you can't fire the worthless ones, for that
would leave you with no one.  
You also stated that your own estimate was that only 50% of
the employees are worthless, so you could fire half your staff
and proceed from there.  

My opinion is that at hiring time, about 1% to 5% of new
employees are not motivated--they are just there to steal 
stuff, or to find customers for their drug business, or because
some regulation requires them to be employed every once in 
a while in order to get a government check.  Over time, 
the number of unmotivated workers gradually increases 
because of uncontrollable circumstances until it reaches a
level of somewhere between 5% and 20%.  

Excessive levels of unmotivated staff--anything over 20%, and
in some locations over 90%--are ALWAYS a result of bad
management.  In particular, the bad attitudes are generated
by lower and middle management--project leaders, section
leaders, department heads, etc.  So the solution for top 
executives is simple.  If unmotivated workers constitute
1% of the work force, replace the workers.  If unmotivated 
workers constitute 21% of the work force, replace the managers.  

I have no dislike of academics.  On the contrary, I appreciate
their efforts to find order where there seems to be only chaos.  
Certainly, before a theory can be put into practice, someone
has to devise the theory.  

I do have an instinctive aversion to management, I freely admit.
You have disavowed any real claim to being an academic.
That's good.  You certainly write less like a Ph.D. than like a
high school sophomore.  My guess is that you were a
middle manager.  Your negative attitude caused you to
suffer repeated failure, in either manufacturing or finance.  As
a result of that you were drummed out of the private sector
and have scurried for the relatively safe haven of some small
community college where you fill the heads of hapless
undergraduates with your own twisted views that the
average worker is a lazy brainless brute who needs to "be
motivated" by frequent use of an electric cattle prod.  

Now you are seeking a return to the private sector--away 
from the awkward questions of real academics, who actually
know something--this time in the guise of "infomation
sciences specialist / systems integration analyst / critical 
needs consultant / project manager / talk show host."  
We can hardly wait. 


P.S.  Happy Memorial Day.



Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T21:24:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b12c127.10390265@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <3B1309D0.EA06A7E@earthlink.net> <WbrQ6.6837$DW1.303968@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B13EC86.629DD594@earthlink.net> <mTxQ6.6876$DW1.305790@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B1407B8.74A0D28F@earthlink.net>`

```
On Mon, 28 May 2001 20:14:48 GMT, John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
wrote:


>
>Well, if you would remove your assumptions about the first part, then you would see
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There are some wonderful stories told by Paul Harvey about such people
that were not given up on and what they became.  

As a flight instructor I can tell you I have NEVER told anyone "You
don't belong in the air.".  For most of whom this *might* be true,
they figure it out for themselves.  I will never sign off on someone
who does not meet my (more stringent than the FAA) requirements for
competence and safety.  However, I WILL continue to work with them as
long as THEY keep coming back.  

So, the answer to the question of how to help the person who doesn't
want to be there.  Frank discussion (private).  Offer any and all
assistance they will ACCEPT - let them decide when it's time to give
up.  They decide by not accepting any further assistance, but yet not
making any progress.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T17:28:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B15325F.9BAA9076@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <3B1309D0.EA06A7E@earthlink.net> <WbrQ6.6837$DW1.303968@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B13EC86.629DD594@earthlink.net> <mTxQ6.6876$DW1.305790@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B1407B8.74A0D28F@earthlink.net> <3b12c127.10390265@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane, I thought about what you said, overnight, and was wondering if you would be kind
enough to answer a couple questions I have?

Before I ask my questions though, I would like to make one comment if I may.   I'm sure
there are also a lot of stories about people who were given up on and latter became great
successes (whether P.H. has them or not is beside the point.)  Firing someone is not the
equivalent of economic execution.  Sometimes being discharged is the best thing that can
happen to the person.  Sometimes it is not.  It's just that as a manager you can NEVER
know beforehand which way it will turn out.

Now my questions:
  What if you weren't teaching piloting, but rather sky diving?  Group sky diving, and
the group is entered in a very important competition for Saturday and all members are all
extremely excited about it.  But this one member that we are talking about, you know,
just isn't ready.  And if he messes up, the group is sure to lose.  Furthermore, there is
an alternate member to the group, who with that person on the team, the group has a good
chance of winning.  However, you can't put the alternate member on the squad until you
get rid of the other one.  Now what would you do?

Other question.
  Back to the original scenario where you are teaching piloting.  This person you are
training says he doesn't like YOUR attitude and wants another instructor!  What would you
say to him and why?


John Grznar

Thane Hubbell wrote:

> There are some wonderful stories told by Paul Harvey about such people
> that were not given up on and what they became.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> making any progress.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T11:51:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B13E191.27940BAC@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <3B1309D0.EA06A7E@earthlink.net> <WbrQ6.6837$DW1.303968@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B13EC86.629DD594@earthlink.net> <mTxQ6.6876$DW1.305790@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B1407B8.74A0D28F@earthlink.net> <3b12c127.10390265@news1.attglobal.net> <3B15325F.9BAA9076@earthlink.net>`

```
John Grznar wrote:

> Before I ask my questions though, I would like to make one comment if I may.   I'm sure
> there are also a lot of stories about people who were given up on and latter became great
> successes (whether P.H. has them or not is beside the point.)  Firing someone is not the
> equivalent of economic execution.

One of the great strengths of the U.S. economy is that people can fail.  A year or two ago,
Forbes had a cover "Failure, why it's so damn good for you".

It is much harder to get fired in Europe, but it is also much harder to get a new job there.
Here, we can fail in high school, and try a couple of times to get a college degree before
getting one's head together and graduating.  We can decide to try a profession which is wrong
for us, fail, and then try something else.  Or try the same thing, but more ready to do it
right.

I remember when a Mexican exchange student was visiting my family.  She was jealous of the
way teens in my family could have jobs and control spending money.  Her family wouldn't let
her do the same thing - only poor teens did that.  But we could learn how to work - and we
could fail without it following us throughout our life.

Sure it hurts to be fired.  But it also brings opportunities to succeed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T04:10:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b147087.6827884@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <3B1309D0.EA06A7E@earthlink.net> <WbrQ6.6837$DW1.303968@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B13EC86.629DD594@earthlink.net> <mTxQ6.6876$DW1.305790@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B1407B8.74A0D28F@earthlink.net> <3b12c127.10390265@news1.attglobal.net> <3B15325F.9BAA9076@earthlink.net>`

```
On Tue, 29 May 2001 17:28:51 GMT, John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
wrote:

>Thane, I thought about what you said, overnight, and was wondering if you would be kind
>enough to answer a couple questions I have?
... Observation snipped for space ...
>
>Now my questions:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ok, I'm the team captain right?  I selected my team members based on
interview and observation.  They were already the best individually,
now we are learning to act as a team.  If one is performing badly,
*THEY* know it.  They will volunteer to step aside, OR take more
practice to get ready.  If they do not, as a team, for this single
competition, we would of course give the alternate a chance to prove
his worth.

>Other question.
>  Back to the original scenario where you are teaching piloting.  This person you are
>training says he doesn't like YOUR attitude and wants another instructor!  What would you
>say to him and why?

A more interesting question.  I would obviously have realized this -
likely before the student.  If I could not adapt in such a way as to
make him/her more comfortable I would do what I have actually done in
the past - I would recommend another flight instructor more suited to
this persons personality.  We are all personalities.  I get along with
most, and others I clash with.  If the student doesn't like my
attitude and I fail to adapt he is free to choose another instructor.
He is the customer, the customer is always right.

Now, what we have done is show both sides of the argument from both
perspectives.  The key here, and perhaps that is what you were getting
at in the first place is: What is the proper conduct for a
"Professional".  As a professional (and no one can tell me Flight
instruction is not a professional occupation) I have an obligation to
understand where I fit and where I do not and to either adapt or
leave.  A good manager, with good professionals will rarely, if ever,
encounter the situation you originally posited.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T21:01:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B16B55E.494F4581@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <3B1309D0.EA06A7E@earthlink.net> <WbrQ6.6837$DW1.303968@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B13EC86.629DD594@earthlink.net> <mTxQ6.6876$DW1.305790@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B1407B8.74A0D28F@earthlink.net> <3b12c127.10390265@news1.attglobal.net> <3B15325F.9BAA9076@earthlink.net> <3b147087.6827884@news1.attglobal.net>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:

<first question snipped >

>Other question.

> >  Back to the original scenario where you are teaching piloting.  This person you are
> >training says he doesn't like YOUR attitude and wants another instructor!  What would you
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> likely before the student.

"Obviously?"  Do you really find it obvious?  People can be very, very
good at hiding their true feelings.  Do you really believe you have the
wisdom to peer into someone and see what is truly going on inside
there?  I am certain I sure don't.


> If I could not adapt in such a way as to
> make him/her more comfortable I would do what I have actually done in
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

He is the customer, the customer is always right.  YES!
Now in the company-employee relationship who is the
customer and who is the supplier?  Is not the company the
customer? and the employee the supplier?  Is it not the company
that is buying the services of the employee???

Let's take the sentence you wrote and translate it from the student-
instructor to the company-employee scenario.

You wrote: "If the student doesn't like my attitude and I fail to adapt
he is free to choose another instructor."

It becomes:  If the customer doesn't like the supplier's attitude and the
supplier fails to adapt the customer is free to choose another supplier.

Then:  If the company doesn't like the employees attitude and the
employee fails to adapt the company is free to choose another employee.

It seems to me it is very easy to accept the "the customer is always right"
philosophy when we are the customer.  But when we are the supplier,
we are quick to throw it right out the window.


> Now, what we have done is show both sides of the argument from both
> perspectives.  The key here, and perhaps that is what you were getting
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> encounter the situation you originally posited.

Yes, but just because something is rare does not mean it is not
important.  True?  Should not the good manager be prepare to handle
the rare situation should it arise? Furthermore, I'm not sure the "good
manager, with good professionals" scenario is the norm.  Is it?

(For what it's worth, I think Flight instruction is a very-highly professional
occupation.  There is much more to being a pilot than just flying a plane.)

John Grnzar

P.S.  Thane, I can not thank you enough for exchanging your ideas with me.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Gary Susott" <susott@atta-boy.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T19:24:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6SCQ6.44814$gc5.1810982@e420r-sjo3.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <3B13EC86.629DD594@earthlink.net> <mTxQ6.6876$DW1.305790@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B1407B8.74A0D28F@earthlink.net> <kBBQ6.6896$DW1.306516@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Might I ask how does the environment that the manager work in affect the
situation?  If the manager fires the unproductive employee (assuming that is
possible) will there be time in the schedule to hire a new employee and
bring that employee up to speed?  If the labor market is tight, will the
corporation even be able to find a replacement?  Could the project be
finished in time with less manpower?  If, as been proposed, the majority of
workers will not be satisfactory, how much time, resources and effort will
be diverted from the project in search of this replacement?

As with most things, it depends.  Does the problem employee create more
disruption so that other, effective employees are being hampered?  Will the
cost of training the inept employee outweigh the costs of bringing a new
employee online?   Might getting the problem employee working again cost
less in resources than replacement?  These and other questions the situation
bring up must be answered and weighed before summarily firing the problem
employee.


NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
news:kBBQ6.6896$DW1.306516@iad-read.news.verio.net...
> In article <3B1407B8.74A0D28F@earthlink.net>,
> John Grznar  <jgrznar@earthlink.net> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> one?  If your question is 'how do I force someone to do something they
do
> >> not wish to do' then this is a different question entire.
```

```
Gary Susott wrote:

> Might I ask how does the environment that the manager work in affect the
> situation?  If the manager fires the unproductive employee (assuming that is
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> employee.

Gary,
   You have a lot of good items there, and I thank you, but I already have those
things.  Let me try again to explain what I'm after by way of an (old) analogy.
Making a decision is a lot like using a balance scale.  You put all the stuff
favoring getting rid of the person on one side and all the stuff favoring
keeping the person on the other and seeing how it all balances out.  Now this
isn't a balance once and use forever situation.  Each time you need to make a
decision of this kind you need a different combination of ingredients.  Labor
market constraint factor: if I fire can I hire? If yes, pour a little on the
fire side, if no pour a little on the keep side.  Team morale factor:
Unacceptable adverse affect on team morale?  If yes, pour some "team morale
powder" on the fire side, if no, pour some on the keep side.  Etc.  Now, here is
the problem I've been having.  When I look in my cupboard and scan all the
ingredients I keep in stock, it seems to me that I have way too many more
ingredients that will usually cause a "fire" decision than will cause a "keep"
decision.   So I feel I should go to the store and get some "keep biased"
ingredients, but I don't know what to get!  (This is a real bad analogy of
decision making, I realize, but even though I have my doubts, I hope it will be
useful here.)

John Grznar
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T22:02:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B157286.D48BF741@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <YWfQ6.6800$DW1.302259@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B1309D0.EA06A7E@earthlink.net> <WbrQ6.6837$DW1.303968@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```


NA wrote:

<much sniping>

>
> I supported the suggestion that different people are motivated by
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> support a shifting, failing argument.

Here, I looked one up for you.  (see ain't I just a nice guy!)

Rapid Development.  Steve McConnell. page 252.

Now tell me, what the hell are you doing still playing around with references?  The
only thing you can LEARN by testing someone else's theories is how to test a
theory.  ANYTHING else must be LEARNED, and I mean REALLY LEARNED,  by testing your
own theories.

Let me try and explain this.  In college the only thing a student can learn is how
to learn.  We cannot provide students with knowledge, because all knowledge comes
from within (the person.)  The process of learning begins when deep inside of you,
something slowly forms and rises up to the surface of your conscience where you can
express it.  Once you can express your idea, you then should put it to the real
world for verification.  Once it has been verified to YOUR TRUE SATISFACTION then
you have LEARNED it.  The idea may still be wrong! but you have LEARNED it.  Of
course, nobody likes to learn wrong stuff.  But whether what we learn is right or
wrong depends on the verification process.

There are two valid ways to verify an idea.  The best way is through empirical
experiment.  The other way is through no-holds-barred, in-your-face, debate.  The
first way is preferred, but it is by no means foolproof.  The second method can be
done using polite sweet talking terms (more on this later.)  But the
no-holds-barred method is better between persons who yet gained mutual respect of
each other (more on this later, too.)  Here is why,  the degree to which something
is LEARNED depends heavily on the confidence the person has in the verification
process.  The probability of whether or not the idea is correct depends on the
performance of the debaters.  Now nobody likes having their ideas disproved,
because when an idea is disproved it becomes worthless.  So one moment someone is
holding on to an idea he thinks is very valuable, then someone else pokes a hole in
it and it vanishes. The person's loss causes his feelings to be hurt.   So instead
of showing someone their idea is wrong, we try to lead him to conclude himself that
his idea is wrong (Thanks Thane.)  How this is any better, I don't know, but it
certainly is a more difficult and time consuming approach.

Now once the person has full confidence that his debators are not holding anything
back, and the other debators are confident he isn't holding anything back (because
they too will indubitably be testing some of their ideas at the same time) the
gruffness of the conversation can be mellowed out and we then say they have gained
a degree of respect for each other.

Now it is far best to select a worthy opponent to debate with. (Plato and Aristotle
both realized this at the precise moment of their most vehement disagreement.)
If the adversary is too weak, then little confidence is there in the method.  If
the adversary is too strong, the debate may not be understood, thus no real
conclusion can be drawn from the verification process.

Of course, the same idea and be tested many times.

Now let me try to explain the what "learn" is instead of "LEARN."  Learn refers to
nothing but pattern matching, similar to that of an artificial neuro-network.
People who learn merely memorize list of terms and methods.   When faced with a
problem, they just go to their list and look for something that matches and they
use that.

Now, DD, I know you are capable of LEARNing.  How is it I know?  It is because I
know you can take a hit.  Now stop playing around with references and put yourself
to the test.

John Grznar

Everything in your outer world is merely symbolic of something in your inner world.

Your outer world is not what is real.  Only your inner world is truly real.
Wisdom is the meaning of the symbols. The key to that wisdom, is to LEARN.
(If any of you know where this saying came from, this may not be an exact quote,
I would sure love to know.)
```

```
Mr Grznar,

and very active thread.

colleague's comments.

You got replies based on personal experience, which you criticised for
essentially being anecdotal and not rigorously supported. You got
replies wich did cite sources, and you criticized them for essentially
being arid and without experiential support.  And when Thane Hubbell
gave you his experience AND cited the documentary source of his
expertise, your response was incomprehensible academic babble-speak.

from.  In THIS community, we would say that "your specifications are
inadequate".  YOU are the source of the problem.

Friend, and I use the term cautiously, I have avoided participating in
this discussion until now, altho I am a frequest poster in this
newsgroup.  I will not reply to your origianl post because it appears
to me that you seek neither information nor wisdom, merely
controversy.

Stephen J Spiro
```

#### ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** "James" <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-27T19:52:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<upgQ6.14794$ce.9947041@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
Actually, no one can motivate anyone else!
Motivation comes from within.

Motive:  something that causes a person to act.
Motivate: provide a motive.

You can provide all the reasons you think will get me to do something,
but if I don't want to do that, I won't.

Now LEADERSHIP, getting others to WILLINGLY do what you
want, is something the Military, (Army and Marines especially)
does well.

That said, even the Army in its training manuals on Leadership,
states that if a sodier is not doing a task correctly or acting
improperly, you must first determine whether it is due to a lack
of PROPER TRAINING and if so provide that.  If not it may
be due to personal problems that are taking too much of the
person's time / energy.

If the person can't do ther job after proper training, BUT is
trying to do it correctly, then you evaluate for a different
job.

If the person has personal problems, those must first be
addressed before the person will be trainable.

Deming, a genius in Management and the application of statistics,
says (essentially) all work problems are MANAGEMENT'S FAULT.
This is because of unclear or conflicting standards / goals,
failure to recognize good work, etc.

Most individuals WANT to be respected for the contributions made,
WANT to be told occassionally 'Hey, that was Great!  We had a really
tight schedule and you came thru.  OR We were all skeptical that this would
work, but your idea saved us X dollars / weeks, etc.'.

Surveys consistently show that we will take a job with less pay than another
if we think we will be treated better, given the credit due us.
'You can't pay me enough to be insulted or cheated'.

The Leader who finds out what is the issue with Joe the Dud, and
maybe splits his current work with a couple of others and says
'Joe take a couple of days off and get some rest'  or whatever is
reasonable will (arranges the training for the new tools that Joe
is afraid to use for fear of looking dumb, will
  a)  have earned Joe's and others' respect
  b) have saved the company time and money by keeping
    Joe onboard
  c) have shown the workers that Management DOES care
  and thereby enhance morale.

Also beware the 'self-fulfulling prophecy'.
Jack or Jill has worked at company X for n years.  Has always
been on time, done consistent good work.  Never had a serious
problem, etc.  Yet he/she has never or not in a long while, been
told " / we appreciate the good work you're doing!".  Soon, Jack or Jill
begins to think " They don't really care about me?!" and begins to show
less enthusiasm, be less carefull, stops volunteering to come in early
 or stay late to get that problem solved.  THIS OF COURSE GETS
NOTICED.  Jack or Jill now gets the "Jack, you've been sloppy
lately,(OR you haven't been a team player lately), if this keeps up,
it won't be good." speech.

Of course, Jack or Jill is REALLY P'D off now, and starts looking
for excuses to tally up every little 'slight'.  Soon Jack or Jill either
gives notice, or is fired.

All that was ever needed to PREVENT this is a regular review of the
work each person is doing to reinforce positive actions / attitudes and
reduce negatives.

To correct the problem once the reviews weren't done, all it would have
taken was an informal talk.  "Jack/Jill, I wanted to talk with you because
you are a VALUED member of the team and for the longest time, you've
never needed anyone to check on your work, you were always punctual,
etc.  And lately you've seemed to not be the same.  Is there anything I can
help with?".
```

#### ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T13:53:06+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b11af62_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
I have read this thread with interest.

For the past 15 years I have made my living (on the rarer and rarer
occasions when I feel like working...) as a Project Manager/Consultant,
rather than a Programmer.
However, having been a programmer for 35 years, I have some inkling into the
mindset...

Points about motivation/leadership and whether or not it is actually
possible to motivate other people have already been discussed, so I'll
confine myself to some "do"s and "don't"s, and some basic "rules".

I offer the following observations, John, based, not on textbooks, but on
real world experience (mainly outside America).

1. NEVER publicly admonish or criticise a team member.  A quiet private
chat, with a genuine desire to put issues on the table and address them, and
listen to what the other person says, is the ONLY chance of reaching the
real problem.
The Corollary: Never beat up the Team as a whole. If they are failing that
is MY fault, not theirs.

2. NEVER lie to the team or individual members of it. Keep your promises. If
there is something sensitive which cannot be divulged "yet", tell them that
and assure them that as soon as you ethically can inform them you will. Give
an assurance it will not affect the outcome if you can honestly do that. If
you can't, then tell them that we will operate on what we have at present,
until we hear differently.

3. The ones your colleague says don't want to be there...rather be home
watching TV...

They get ONE chance.

You tell them what the project is hoping to achieve, tell them they will be
treated fairly (no matter what their Management have said or done to them in
the past), express your own enthusiasm for the project (and if you don't
have any, don't be there...), tell them you want them as part of it and it
will be satisfying and fun.  Having fun, as well as working hard, is an
essential part of the mix for successful Project Management and, sadly, too
many Managers overlook this, concerned that the people they answer to will
not approve. See next rule...

If the team Member fails to respond to your offer of a place on the team
with a "clean slate" (4 weeks is a fair period to see what happens), tell
them frankly there is no place for them and ask them what they want to do.
Get them removed from the team as quickly as possible.

I learned this one the hard way. As a new Project Manager I really didn't
want to fire anybody (still don't) but I had some problems with a husband
and wife team where he was really competent and she was about as useful as a
chocolate firescreen.

Unfortunately, the two of them were extremely disruptive as she made bullets
for him to fire...Very difficult situation. I discussed it with my Boss who
was older, wiser, and much more experienced. His verdict: "Fire them both.
Do it now. It's your call; I'll support you." I was shocked and decided to
persevere... little chats with both of them together and separately.

My attempt to be fair and avoid unpleasantness was perceived as weakness,
and the attitudes and destructive activity hardened. Six months later I
fired them both and apologised to my Boss for not doing it sooner. I worked
out later, my bad decision had cost us around 20 man weeks of work and an
unquantifiable bad effect on morale in general. Once they were gone, things
rapidly improved and we got it in on time (just...).

Your colleague is right on this one, John, although I believe her cynicism
about the impossibility of ever getting anyone who is (or, arguably, can be)
motivated is unjustified.

4. NEVER fear, attempt to propitiate, or gain Brownie points with the people
you report to. Develop an honest working relationship, keep them informed
and get their help when things are stalled. The attitude should be: "My
management are there to support my project when I need help, in exactly the
same way as I support my team when they need it. There is no "US" and
"THEM"...we are working for the same overall goals."

There are many other points which are really common sense and can be summed
up as "treat people the way you want them to treat you".  I believe the ones
above are the MOST important but I certainly don't minimise things like
encouraging and acknowledging individuals.

I usually do some "team building" from time to time and allocate a
percentage of the overall budget for this. It is not just pizza but can be
something a bit special when particular project milestones are achieved. I
always make the point that it is the Company's way of acknowledging what we
are doing. On some occasions I have paid for this out of my own pocket. (the
team had done an outstanding task and there was no money in the budget for a
party, or Company policy was that they didn't do this for employees). On
those occasions I simply say that I really appreciate the efforts being made
(which is true; see 2 above.)

BOTTOM LINE:

Programmers will respond to an environment where they are (ideally)
acquiring new skills, being recognised for the effort they put in (not just
with a paycheque, but in a more personal way), and can feel that they are
part of something that WILL be achieved and will be recognised by the
Company. They should also feel that their Project Management is 100% behind
them and can be approached for help and advice on anything.

Project Managers should have a sincere desire to "grow" team members and
provide the kind of environment where this is possible.

I have a number of people who have worked with me on past projects and have
indicated they would always be happy to do so again.

Whether this comes down to "Motivating Programmers" or not, is not for me to
say.

It works for me...

Pete.

PS. An eagle will fly whether it is surrounded by turkeys or not. If the
turkeys get in the way, the eagle goes right through them...
```

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

```
Interesting.  I've never met a programmer that didn't like
programming.  Given that, all of the people I have met have been
motivated.  Some have not been as good or productive as others.  Some
are down right PRESICE in thier work - and it seems slower, but is of
generally higher quality.  Some just don't "Get it" one some things
and never ask for help.  

The particular group I am presently in has a large variety of
background and education.  Each person has their strengths.  We do
have managers - and they do just that manage.  They are not like
"bosses" but they manage the tasks so we have a common place to go to
resolve "design" conflicts and the like.  

Each of the "Developers" as they are called works virtually
unsupervised.  This works because of the individuals involved as well
as the managers.  The company has shown no loyalty to the employees -
yet these people show a great degree of caring and integrity - why?
They LOVE programming.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T19:04:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B13F738.F35286B5@earthlink.net>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:

> Interesting.  I've never met a programmer that didn't like
> programming.  Given that, all of the people I have met have been
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Agreed, good professionals don't need bosses.  Managers yes, but not bosses.
Managers coordinate, they don't dictate.  They arbitrate, they don't judge,
etc. etc.
But I am not asking about the good professional.  I want to know how you
would handle the "bad-attitude" professional level employee.  How much effort
would you put into trying to straighten out that person?  And not how much
time would you WANT to spend, but how much time WOULD you spend.  I make this
distinction because I think a good manager would want to spend a lot of time
trying to help that person, but he just cannot afford that luxury.  He has
just too many other things to consider, #1 the affect on the morale of the
other team members, #2 meeting the time schedule...  And would it make a
difference that instead of having an established, well-acquainted team you
had a new team where they were just getting to know you?  I don't have the
answers to these questions,  I just have my opinions.  That is why I posted
my question to this news group.  I thought I'd get a bunch of really poignant
responses. So far I've only received one, Peter Dashwood's.  I really hope
you will make it two.  I am most interested in hearing your response.

John Grznar
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T15:36:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%GxQ6.5520$zi7.652208@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <3b11e34f.6694446@news1.attglobal.net> <3B13F738.F35286B5@earthlink.net>`

```
I think that the answer depends on the employee.  There is a huge difference
betwen how you treat a new person that turns out to be less than you
exepected, and a long time employee that was once good, but is suffering
from burnout.

Certainly, I would feel no commitment to make a pro out of a beginner with a
bad attitude.  In fact, I would not only fire them, but figure that I was
doing the profession a favour.

A disallusioned long time employee is quite a different kettle of fish.  I
think the answer to your question is "there are no rules".  Every case is
entirely different.  Being an effective manger of people involves making
individual value judgements on each individual case.

Donald

"John Grznar" <jgrznar@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:3B13F738.F35286B5@earthlink.net...
>
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Agreed, good professionals don't need bosses.  Managers yes, but not
bosses.
> Managers coordinate, they don't dictate.  They arbitrate, they don't judge
,
> etc. etc.
> But I am not asking about the good professional.  I want to know how you
> would handle the "bad-attitude" professional level employee.  How much
effort
> would you put into trying to straighten out that person?  And not how much
> time would you WANT to spend, but how much time WOULD you spend.  I make
this
> distinction because I think a good manager would want to spend a lot of
time
> trying to help that person, but he just cannot afford that luxury.  He has
> just too many other things to consider, #1 the affect on the morale of the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> answers to these questions,  I just have my opinions.  That is why I
posted
> my question to this news group.  I thought I'd get a bunch of really
poignant
> responses. So far I've only received one, Peter Dashwood's.  I really hope
> you will make it two.  I am most interested in hearing your response.
>
> John Grznar
>
```

```
Thank you Donald.  That is just the kind of data I am looking for.  I'm hoping
to also get some contrary views, because I agree with you, except I might add
(IMHO, IIM) that I would feel I might also be doing the person a favor, but that
is something we can never know in advance.

Btw, a couple of years ago you sent a post to this newsgroup describing a
network you were setting up.  Apparently for some trucking outfit that stretched
from Northern Ontario to Southern Louisiana.  I just want to let you know I
copied that message, and every semester I show it to my students.  It has turned
on more than a few students to pursuing a career in information systems.  For
them and myself, thank you.

John Grznar
```

#### ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T12:51:27-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4E0A8B7D98884552.625E4148BA037D92.09E374CA1F1AD878@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
On Sun, 27 May 2001 18:11:33 GMT, John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
enlightened us:

>Hi guys,
>  I'm in a discussion with a colleague (we're both currently academics)
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Obviously neither you nor your compatriot have ever had real jobs
otherwise you'd certainly have a better view of things.  

An unmotivated programmer is no different than an unmotivated garbage
collector.  Only the task is different.  A manager must decide if the
employee is worth making an effort to motivate or not.  Given all
proper company policies have been followed, said un-motivated employee
is either transferred or fired or given an ultimatum under the guise
of counseling.  The manager must also look at the employee's history.
Has he/she/it always been unmotivated?  If so why is he/she/it still
here?  Is this a one time occurrence?  If so an attempt to find the
cause is warranted.

A person with a good work ethic when properly compensated for said
work ethic will not be dragged down by or influenced by the acts of
others.  However, if such effort does go unrewarded, look for said
employee to either join the group or seek employment elsewhere.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

In the United States we have the most divided
government we have ever had:

The House is controlled by the Republicans,
The Senate is controlled by the Democrats and,
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T21:48:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B141DDD.8EF92282@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <4E0A8B7D98884552.625E4148BA037D92.09E374CA1F1AD878@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Skippy, I've read your post over and over again because in my opinion, yours
is right on the borderline of deserving a slam or a mature response.  I've
decided to *try* to come down on the side of a mature response because I have
read your posts to this group over the past few years, and your opinion is
certainly one I was hoping to hear.

You miss the question in two ways.  First you talk only about employees in
general.  I'm not asking about employees in general, but only about "bad
attitude professional level" employees.  Second, you never state any reasons
for your conclusions.  Far, far more important to me than *what*  you believe
is *why* you believe it.  Can you answer the WHY part?  Btw, most people can
not.

Thanks for your input anyway.

John Grznar




SkippyPB wrote:

> On Sun, 27 May 2001 18:11:33 GMT, John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
> enlightened us:
…[79 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T11:02:13-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FC4FD73D0CAC1D82.3BFEFBE91C3C6499.7552C4C99FDCC672@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <4E0A8B7D98884552.625E4148BA037D92.09E374CA1F1AD878@lp.airnews.net> <3B141DDD.8EF92282@earthlink.net>`

```
On Mon, 28 May 2001 21:48:57 GMT, John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
enlightened us:

>Skippy, I've read your post over and over again because in my opinion, yours
>is right on the borderline of deserving a slam or a mature response.  I've
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

First, I did not miss a thing.  Your thread is entitled "Motivating
Programmers" and in your original post you state, "What I'm talking
about is motivating the programmer who doesn't even want to be there,
someone who would rather be home watching TV, or someone who just
doesn't like doing that kind of work and can't bring himself to focus
on the task. "  

Nowhere did I see anything about programmers with a "bad attitude".
All I see is a question about motivating a possibly unmotivated
programmer.  If you meant something else, then you need to state it.
A programmer (or any worker for that matter) can have a bad attitude
and still perform a great job.  I've seen it first hand.  So there is
a world of difference between an employee with a "bad attitude" and an
unmotivated employee.

I talked about employees in general because there is no reason to
treat programmers any different than you would an employee in any
other profession.  Would the steps necessary to motivate a nuclear
physicist be any different than those to motivate a programmer?  While
the specifics might be different, the general rules would not be.

You said I didn't state any reasons for my conclusions.  Okay, I
thought they might be obvious.  As with most things in life or on the
job, common sense is usually the best guide.  Having been an
application programmer, a systems programmer, a programming manager
and now a programmer on a tele-commuting basis, I've seen and
experienced a lot.  Common sense has served me well.

But back to your query.  As I said, a programmer with a bad attitude
can still perform an outstanding job and be a valuable member of the
team.  What a manager must decide is, is that programmer a disruption
to the team to the extent that he/she/it effects the performance of
the team.  I've seen cases where an employee with a bad attitude (i.e.
hates management, thinks he/she/it is under-paid, hates working more
than 8 hours a day, etc. etc. etc.) is such a valuable member of the
team that others just tolerate he/she/it.  Managers did the same.
Personally, during the nearly 10 years I was a manager, I've only had
1 employee that fit that description.  After some counseling and
intense discussions with the employee to learn the root of the
problem, I was able to change his attitude.  

So what is it you really want to know?  Do you want to know how to
motivate employees who seem to be unmotivated or do you want to know
how to deal with employees with bad attitudes?  The two are not always
synonymous.



>John Grznar
>
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
>>


Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

In the United States we have the most divided
government we have ever had:
```

#### ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T18:51:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f0r47$dt9$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
Most of the time the project manager wants to see something done that is
beyond the capabilities of the current system and is unwilling to tell
upper-level management the cold hard truth.  Instead they suck up and say I
think we can do that, when they know their people are already backlogged on
projects.  It takes time to properly test software before it is realeased.  A
programmer may be eable to write a program in days but it probably takes a
week or two to find those little glitches that can bring a company to its
knees.  Why don't managers spend the required time to do a proper
cost-benefit analysis before submitting their silly ideas.
```

```
It depends on the project itself, the leader and the other team mates


These things can demotivate even the most enthusiastic team
prgrammer/analyst
 * A crappy leader (critical, political, secretive, absent, indifferent,
hostile)

 * A waste of time project (doomed to failure before starting)

 * unrealistic management goals (cost/budget/time)

 * a lack of focus or loss of focus (what we are expected to do)


	Susan A
```

#### ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** "David P. Bretz" <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T09:17:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B14F2E4.C4102689@mescoma.com>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
I found it interesting reading thru some of the responses to this post.  I,
by no means, read all the responses, so if I'm restating something that
someone else has already pointed out, then please forgive me.

One of the things that disturbed me about what I read was the apparent
assumption that everyone is motivated by the same things (the pizza party
after the project is complete, for example).  I personally think the job of
any management type is two-fold.  First, discover what actually motivates
each individual on the team.  For instance, my motivation comes from knowing
that the company really is concerned with my well-being and  they recognize
my true worth.  Also, they're willing to let me and help me expand my IT
world by introducing me to new technology and giving me the opportunity to
use it.

Second, I believe it is the responsibility of management to simply remove
the impediments that hinder me from doing my job.  In other words, give me
the work and then get the hell out of my way.  Don't bog me down with
endless meetings, reports, unnecessary paperwork, etc.  Let me do my job the
best way I can, and move on to the next task.

If I could find a position with a company that with that sort of management,
I'd die a happy man.   Unfortunately, it won't happen in the US.

Dave

John Grznar wrote:

> Hi guys,
>   I'm in a discussion with a colleague (we're both currently academics)
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> P.S.  And no docdwarf, this isn't a homework assignment.  :-)
```

```
In article <3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>, John Grznar says...
>
>Hi guys,
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>person and try his luck with someone else.

Ok.. so what else has this person done wrong? Have they done work that is so
incorrect that is has cost the firm money? Have they been destructive or had a
bad influence on other workers?? I need a bit more to go on before I can answer
the question properly or even attempt to answer the question. All of these type
of decisions are case by case and it's impossible to come up with a "general"
rule to follow, IMO. 

>  My colleague's response is that I am living in a dream world.  To her
>IS work is a joke.  All group members are un-motivated.   She says I'd
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>damn turkeys.

So, your "colleague" thinks that all or most of IS workers have an attitude
problem?? What would lead anyone to that generalization?? Up to this part I
thought it was a "good" question but I find it odd that someone who is into
enlightening minds and seeking knowledge would oversimplify a complex subject.

Your "colleague" should know or at least be aware that IS is like the rest of
the world. Some people have good attitudes and some don't. It's not a black and
white issue it's very gray at best. It's scary to think that someone in academia
would make such a sweeping generalization.

So, what are the options? Fire the person or what???? Next time be a bit more
specific and then we can discuss the issue. 


>  I be interested in reading practitioners views on this.
>  Thanks,

YW.. I think??
>John Grznar
>
>P.S.  And no docdwarf, this isn't a homework assignment.  :-)

>
>
>
```

```
Hi
I was going to put together a program which would automatically read in a
COBOL record layout file and output a different file with an offset listing
of all the fields in the initial record layout.  Obviously, if such source
exists and is freely available (I'm sure it exist but whether it is freely
available is another matter) there is no point in me doing so.  It would
merely be a little time saving utility for me when I do my work.
Cheers
Paul
```

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us

- **References:** `<K6qQ6.2579$Ix.23079@news11-gui.server.ntli.net>`

```
In article <K6qQ6.2579$Ix.23079@news11-gui.server.ntli.net>, Seabass
<Rizlaz@hotmail.com> writes:
>Hi
>I was going to put together a program which would automatically read in a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
In COBOL you have to identify the input and the output record descriptions in
the body of the program.  There isnt much automatic about anything in COBOL. 
The programmer is expected to know what the file looks like and decide what
the output looks like.  Usually the programmer uses a print chart and just
designs the output which is coded and tested. Often a file description is
made up and catalogged on the system so it can be copied into the program. 
There are probably some third party products that can read file descriptions
or program sourcecode and do what you want.

However, if all you want to do is move input to output that is pretty simple. 
Often for a programming assignment I use to make a simple program that moved
the input all as one text field to the printline and just write one line at a
time.  That way someone else can see what the input looked like for the
program.  This makes a good simple first program for a class.

You will have to figure it out on your own.  Even the crudest of COBOL books
have some examples.  Try a book like Learn COBOL in 24 Hours.    
>


 -----  Posted via NewsOne.Net: Free (anonymous) Usenet News via the Web  -----

made through NewsOne.Net violate posting guidelines, email abuse@newsone.net
```

## Thread: Any Free Source ? — 1 msgs

### Re: Any Free Source ?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-28T13:02:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c%rQ6.145$824.43412@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<DTpQ6.2516$Ix.22511@news11-gui.server.ntli.net>`

```
Seabass <Rizlaz@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:DTpQ6.2516$Ix.22511@news11-gui.server.ntli.net...
> Hi
> I was going to put together a program which would automatically read in a
> COBOL record layout file and output a different file with an offset
listing
> of all the fields in the initial record layout.  Obviously, if such source
> exists and is freely available (I'm sure it exist but whether it is freely
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, there is at least one piece of compiled software which does this. You
can download the freeware COBFD.ZIP from
http://www.flexus.com/downloads.html .

This is an MS-DOS program I have been threatening to bring into the 21st
century (e.g., Windows(r)) for about two years. I had not considered making
the source code available, but if you want it, I will e-mail it do you for
your personal use.

That source code is written in PowerBASIC (um, I feel dumb saying this, but
PB is a BASIC dialect) , and uses some features of that language not
available in other BASICs (it also uses PB/DOS version 3.5, and some of the
features I used are not available in prior versions of PB). But it is
well-written BASIC code, which looks a lot like well-written COBOL code, and
if nothing else would give you an idea of some of the traps, pitfalls and
other goodies you might encounter on your trip.

If interested, drop me a note explaining your application and we'll go from
there. (Applies to anyone).
```

```
Hi,

for a really quick responce (as I'm already 2 days late delivering my
project!)

I'm hoping someone might have come across the following error when using
Acu4GL for MS SQL (5.1) on MS SQL server 2000 on Windows 2000 Server.

Everything seems to install OK but when I try to run the sql.acu sample
program
I get an error 9D,11.  The trace file displays an error that sp_AcuInit
storted
procedure does not exist.  Checking the database this is true.  It does not
exist
in the database.
Checking ms_inst.cmd (and ms_inst.sql) confirms that the stored procedure
sp_AcuInit
is not attempted to be added to the database. There is no reference anywhere
of sp_AcuInit.

database
as the standard installation process (ms_inst.cmd and ms_inst.sql) does not

Ray Smith.
```

## Thread: TinyCOBOL release 0.53 — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Open-source COBOL (GnuCOBOL, OpenCOBOL, TinyCOBOL)`](topics.md#open-source)_

### TinyCOBOL release 0.53

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T08:02:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B138FBD.B88121CB@arvotek.net>`

```
Announce: TinyCOBOL release 0.53

The TinyCOBOL compiler version 0.53 is available at the TinyCOBOL home
page (1) and iBiblio (2).

This release contains mainly bugs fixes, and enhancements.  

It includes updates to the preprocessor, math verbs, the regression test
suite, gdb debug stabs,  STRING/UNSTRING statements, ACCEPT from
COMMAND-LINE and ENVIRONMENT-VARIABLE statements. 

Support for the dynamic CALL statement, ACCEPT  DAY, DAY-OF-WEEK, ON
EXCEPTION and ERASE clauses, ADD and SUBTRACT (CORRESPONDING option)
statements, EXTERNAL clause in the SELECT statement, BINARY-CHAR, 
BINARY-SHORT, BINARY-LONG, BINARY-DOUBLE and COMP-1/COMP-2 (preliminary)
floating point types, support for the Debian package format have been
added to the compiler.

Please report any bugs and/or problems to the TinyCOBOL mailing list. 


1) TinyCOBOL Home Page
   http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/

2) iBiblio (formerly Metalab)
   http://www.ibiblio.org/pub/Linux/devel/lang/cobol/
```

<a id="t-3f76a56f65"></a>
## Thread: Printing with Merant Netexpress 3.1 over WINDOWS 9X/NT — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Printing with Merant Netexpress 3.1 over WINDOWS 9X/NT

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T14:41:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0105291341.3af8dcac@posting.google.com>`

```
Does anyone have experience of printing from NetExpress 3.1 over a
WINDOWS network where it is necessary to ensure that all data to be
printed has been printed. We have two situations: to print a test page
to allow the user to line-up (on continuous paper, for pre-printed
multi-part paper) and to ensure that all output has been printed so
that the user can confirm that everything has been printed correctly.

The second ought to be straightforward by closing the printer but I
have a suspicion that this may not occur until the program is exited.
But the first is the real problem because here we obviously don't want
to cause the printer to carry out any initialisations in case these
cause any extra lines to be printed. We are converting the code to use
the PC_PRINTER... routines but this doesn't seem to be the issue.

And of course this is all aggravated by the WINDOWS network; I suspect
that the real problem lies here and this is an area that I've not a
lot of direct experience of. We'd like to keep using the standard
COBOL routines to keep some platform independence so I hope the
solution isn't to use WIN32 API ...

Any comments gratefully received.

graham
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing with Merant Netexpress 3.1 over WINDOWS 9X/NT

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T22:48:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1427D2.50E59DEB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8a02b0a1.0105291341.3af8dcac@posting.google.com>`

```


graham wrote:

> Does anyone have experience of printing from NetExpress 3.1 over a
> WINDOWS network where it is necessary to ensure that all data to be
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Graham,

I can't offer a foolproof solution because I only print to a desktop
printer. However, as with all accesses to COBOL files where I ALWAYS check
file-status, I do exactly the same, check printer-status for ALL PC_PRINT
instructions.

If you wanted to reduce the 'traffic' by restricting the number of printer
status checks,
you could at least check the printer status as you page change. I would be
attempting to check the printer status as being 'clean' before I used
PC-PRINTER_CLOSE.
(I'm musing here - but the printer dialog that you get, I wonder might it
be possible to capture the number of pages being generated - and check
that the same number have been printed - nothing concrete, just a
thought....)

"The second ought to be straightforward by closing the printer but I
have a suspicion that this may not occur until the program is exited ".

Not clear what you mean by that - there's no necessity to wait until Exit
Program.
The printer is closed when you issue the PC_PRINTER_CLOSE. Assuming you
had a second report to the same printer, then Open, print lines and
PC_PRINTER-CLOSE again.

Wisdom suggests avoid the Win32 API routines - Ken Mullins down in Atlanta
is 'big' on Win32 APIs for Dialoging but his preference is PC_PRINT for
reports.

I've got something written in OO for PC_PRINT, which could be adapted to
non-OO - if you would like a copy, email me privately.

I know nothing of the product - but folks enthuse about Crystal Reports
?????

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Printing with Merant Netexpress 3.1 over WINDOWS 9X/NT

- **Date:** 2001-05-30T00:41:02+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<20010529204102.00403.00001697@ng-mi1.aol.com>`
