## Thread: help! remove embedded spaces from a string — 27 this month / 55 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help! remove embedded spaces from a string

- **From:** "DM Hancock" <dmh429@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T18:12:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
I have a variable which consists of data in no particular arrangement.  I
need to remove the embedded spaces.

I can't use inspect/replacing because the two arguments have to be the same
size.
I also can't use string/unstring.

Does anyone know of a function or intrinsic to do this? (I'm using an HP3000
v 6.5)


I am trying to stay away from a byte by byte comparison to remove the
spaces.  (It's my last resort.)

Thanks,

DM Hancock
```

#### ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T13:15:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b14495a_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
"DM Hancock" <dmh429@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net...
> I have a variable which consists of data in no particular arrangement.  I
> need to remove the embedded spaces.
>
<snipped>
>
>
> I am trying to stay away from a byte by byte comparison to remove the
> spaces.  (It's my last resort.)
>
You CANNOT stay away from a byte by byte comparison because it is necessary
to look at every byte in order to know whether to remove it. (Even if you
COULD use INSPECT or UNSTRING they will still generate byte by byte
comparisons...)

I haven't written any COBOL for a while and I'm having withdrawal symptoms
<G> so I've made a coffee and decided to post some code (something I almost
NEVER do) to CLC...<G>

(NO correspondence regarding style, flag values, use of sections, or any
other bullshit will be entered into... only if the sample code can be shown
NOT to work will I respond...<G>).

Working-Storage Section.

01   input-string.
        12 is-char     pic x occurs 256
                                        indexed by is-x1.

01   output-string.
        12 os-char     pic x occurs 256
                                         indexed by os-x1.

*> if your compiler supports 78 levels you might like to set the length of
both the
*> input and output strings up as 78 and include these in the occurs. I'm
*> continuing on the assumption that you HAVEN'T got this facility...

01  binary-fields usage comp-5.
*> Manually amend these values to match your OCCURS...
*> They DON'T have to be the same...
       12 in-length   pic s9(4) value 256.
       12 out-length pic s9(4) value 256.

01 flags.
      12 finish-flag pic x value space.
            88 finished                  value '1'.
            88 NOT-finished         value '0'.

Procedure Division.
...
remove-spaces section.
*>
*> This code scans the input string and leaves it in the output string
*>  with all spaces removed from it, apart from trailing ones.
*>  On exit, the index os-x1 will point to the last character in the output
string
*>  BEFORE trailing spaces (if any).
*>
rs000.
        set NOT-finished to TRUE
        set os-x1 to 1
        move spaces to output-string
        perform
              varying is-x1
                   from 1
                      by 1
                    until  is-x1 > in-length
                                    OR
                             finished
                          if is-char (is-x1) NOT = space
                             move is-char (is-x1) to os-char (os-x1)
                             set os-x1 up by 1
                              if os-x1 > out-length
                                  set finished to TRUE
                              end-if
                          end-if
         end-perform
         set os-x1 down by 1
         .
rs999.
          exit.

IF the input string was VERY long (more than 16K) you MIGHT need to amend
the above code to detect the last non-blank character in it and set the
position of this character into the input string length. There is no easy
and efficient way  (I can think of) to do this on the PC, but the mainframe
has storage instructions which can do it efficiently. For most normal
purposes it shouldn't be necessary (and besides, PCs are only "toy" machines
anyway, right Mainframers <G>?)

Hope this helps,

Pete.



Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

- **From:** "DM Hancock" <dmh429@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T20:39:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FdYQ6.60$56.181596@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <3b14495a_2@Usenet.com>`


##### ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

- **From:** Foodman <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T14:21:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vl7R6.5241$rn5.260360@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <3b14495a_2@Usenet.com>`

```
Hi:

Mr. Dashwood's post gives us all an opportunity to compare
a structured program with one written in a sensible manner. . . 

I wrote a (DOS) program to test my routine for doing what
Hancock wants. The ENTIRE program is listed at the bottom. 
It actually works (at least on my machine.)

Hancock said:

>>I have a variable which consists of data in no particular arrangement.  I
>>need to remove the embedded spaces.

>>I can't use inspect/replacing because the two arguments have to be the same
>>size.

I guess Mr. Dashwood gets some points for adding a feature which 
the user did not request, viz: letting the fields be different lengths.

This is Mr. Dashwood's incomplete version:

>Working-Storage Section.
>
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pete.

This is my complete version.

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.             ELIMINATOR.
AUTHOR.                 A. R. DILWORTH.
DATE-WRITTEN.           5/30/1.
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  W-FIELD-1.
02  W-CHARACTER-1      PIC X(01) OCCURS 50 TIMES.
01  W-FIELD-2.
02  W-CHARACTER-2      PIC X(01) OCCURS 50 TIMES.
01  W-STRING-SIZE          PIC 9(02) VALUE 50.
01  X1                     PIC 9(03) VALUE ZERO.
01  X2                     PIC 9(03) VALUE ZERO.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
A.
DISPLAY (1, 1) ERASE.
B.
ACCEPT (1, 10) W-FIELD-1.
IF W-FIELD-1 = SPACES
GO TO END-PROG.
PERFORM ELIMINATE-ROUTINE.
DISPLAY (2, 10) W-FIELD-2.
GO TO B.
ELIMINATE-ROUTINE.
MOVE SPACES TO W-FIELD-2.
PERFORM ELIMINATE-SPACES 
VARYING X1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X1 > W-STRING-SIZE.
ELIMINATE-SPACES.
IF X1 = 1 MOVE 1 TO X2.
IF W-CHARACTER-1 (X1) NOT = SPACE
MOVE W-CHARACTER-1 (X1) TO W-CHARACTER-2 (X2),
ADD 1 TO X2.                
END-PROG.
DISPLAY (1, 1) ERASE.
STOP RUN.

If anyone can convince me that the structured way is better in any
objective way, I'll eat your source-code in Macy's window.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T23:32:25+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b14daf0_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net> <3b146cf7.5915704@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane,

I completely endorse the separation of this code to a separate module or
Object Method, and I should have suggested such in my post.

Not sure that I like "SquishPete" as a name for it...you could be giving
certain people ideas...<G>

As to re-inserting removed spaces, as a string function, I contend that this
cannot be done without storing another string.

It is therefore not really viable.

Every string that you removed spaces from, would require another string to
save the information as to where the spaces go (in fact, it would probably
make better sense to provide an "undo" function and simply save the original
string in the Method which did the removal...) This would allow undo until
the next string had spaces removed from it...

Logically, when you remove the spaces from the string you are also removing
"information" and it is therefore not possible to re-construct the original
string, just using the information now contained in it.

Even if you went to all the trouble of saving the original strings on a
database you would still need to connect them to the "converted" string
somehow, yet without adding information to the converted string.

The only way I can see to do this would be to build a database of string
"pairs" with both the spaced and de-spaced strings connected together and
the de-spaced string used as a key....A HUGE amount of trouble for a dubious
benefit, hence my conclusion: not viable.

An interesting logical exercise though...<G>

Pete.

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:3b146cf7.5915704@news1.attglobal.net...
> Put Pete's code in a called module and your program only has one line
> of code:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-29T23:35:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f1t0n$1p2gi$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```
DM Hancock <dmh429@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net...
> because of the complexity of it, the program is already very complicated,
I
> like to write code so that the programs are easy to maintain.

What is complicated about this?

MOVE SPACES TO FIELD-B.
MOVE 1 TO SUB2.
PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB1 > FIELD-LENGTH
                                            OR FIELD-A (SUB1:) = SPACES
   IF FIELD-A (SUB1:1) NOT = SPACES
      MOVE FIELD-A (SUB1:1) TO FIELD-B (SUB2:1)
      ADD 1 TO SUB2
   END-IF
END-PERFORM.

It isn't much code, and you probably aren't going to get any easier than
this...


> "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk...
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T23:16:15+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b14d75d_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net> <9f1t0n$1p2gi$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`

```

"DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net> wrote in message
news:9f1t0n$1p2gi$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de...
>
> What is complicated about this?
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
While I agree it is not much code, I HAVE to ask the following questions:

1. What field is FIELD-LENGTH the length of? If it is FIELD-A, then why the
reference modified test for trailing spaces? The length will tell you when
you've finished.

2. Have you any idea of the code that will be generated to test for trailing
spaces on every iteration? .(..FIELD-A (SUB-1:) = SPACES)  As an example,
consider that FIELD-A is 256 bytes and has 60 bytes before trailing spaces.
A fairly reasonable sample...The byte compares required will be 16,175. Does
that not strike you as a little excessive for a field that is 256 bytes
long?

3. As there is no test to see if SUB-2 has overflowed after it is
incremented, what happens if the output field is shorter than the input one?
(The user said that they could be different length fields.)

Sometimes it is better to add a little more code and get it right... please
check further back up the thread to a previous post where I did this. (It
uses 256 byte compares, for a field which is 256 bytes long.)

(Oh, and never be too keen to post code to CLC...<G> especially if you are
easily wounded....)

Pete.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x3cR6.26538$fb7.2995072@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```

"DM Hancock" <dmh429@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net...
> because of the complexity of it, the program is already very
complicated, I
> like to write code so that the programs are easy to maintain.

even easier:

MOVE 0 TO J.
MOVE SPACE TO OUT-C.
PERFORM VARYING K FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL K > IN-LEN
   IF IN-C(K:1) NOT = SPACE
      ADD 1 TO J
      MOVE IN-C(K:1) TO OUT-C(J:1)
      END-IF
END-PERFORM.
MOVE OUT-C TO IN-C.

or, somewhat tighter:

MOVE 1 TO J
PERFORM VARYING K FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL K > IN-LEN
  IF IN-C(K:1) NOT = SPACE
      IF K NOT = J
         MOVE IN-C(K:1) TO IN-C(J:1)
         ADD 1 TO J
         END-IF
      END-IF
END-PERFORM.
MOVE SPACE TO IN-C(J:)

or, my favorite:

CALL 'DESPACE' USING STRING-LEN MYSTRING
```

- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net> <x3cR6.26538$fb7.2995072@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <c5fR6.98$AA4.12643@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```

"DM Hancock" <dmh429@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:c5fR6.98$AA4.12643@nnrp1.sbc.net...
> I ended up coding a byte-by byte comparison to remove the spaces.
> I'm curious about the DESPACE intrinsic you used.
> where can I get some info about it.
> I'm using an hp3000 with mpe v 6.5

Oops! Sorry for misleading you. it's not an intrinsic.

It's the same code as in the post, just compiled separately as a
subroutine and linked with the executable. FORTRANers would be
comfortable with the idea - New Age COBOLers (mistakenly) call it
Object Oriented Programming.

We've got dozens (hundreds?) of these around the shop. For example,

Left-justify a string while squeezing out multiple blanks.
Right-justify and zero fill.
Normal-Case (The quick brown fox... => The Quick Brown Fox...)
Any rendition of telephone number to a standard [(123)-456-7890,
123.456.7890, 1234567890, etc. -> (123)456-7890 ]
Date functions (01/01/2001 -> Jan 1, 2001 -> 20010101, etc.)
Decomma (["Apple,Banana","Dog, Cat",123,"5"] ->
[Apple,Banana(lv)Dog,Cat(lv)123(lv)5] ) to facilitate UNSTRINGING
Depunct - remove all punctuation from a string
KWIC - Extracts all keywords in a string, omitting trash (a, an, the,
to, over, etc.)

I suspect most shops and programmers have their own set of similar
routines; no sense in reinventing a left-justify routine for every
project nor cluttering up a listing with boilerplate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "DM Hancock" <dmh429@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T18:58:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LVAR6.86$PQ2.161606@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net> <x3cR6.26538$fb7.2995072@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Jerry P,
I wrote the code similar to the method you used.
since I needed to remove excess spaces, not all spaces,
I modified your code to:



MOVE 0 TO J.
MOVE SPACE TO OUT-C.
PERFORM VARYING K FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL K > IN-LEN
   COMPUTE L = K - 1
   IF L < 1
      MOVE 1 TO L
   END-IF
   IF IN-C(K:1) NOT = SPACE
   OR IN-C(L:1) NOT = SPACE
      ADD 1 TO J
      MOVE IN-C(K:1) TO OUT-C(J:1)
      END-IF
END-PERFORM.


HERE IS MY VERSION.  IT'S NOT AS CONCISE AS I'D LIKE IT, BUT IT WORKS.

LEGAL-DESCRIPTION IS A 200 BYTE FIELD WITH EMBEDDED SPACES
I NEEDED TO REMOVE THE EXCESS SPACES, AND PRINT IT ON
5 LINES ON A REPORT (145) SPACES



083300 01  LEGAL-DESC-VARIABLES.
083400     05  CUR-CHAR              PIC 9(03) COMP-3.
083500     05  PREV-CHAR             PIC 9(03) COMP-3.
083600     05  LEGAL-SUB             PIC 9(03) COMP-3.
083700     05  LEGAL-DESC-TABLE.
083800         10  LEGAL-DESC PIC X OCCURS 145 TIMES.
084500     05  LEGAL-DESCR-PRT-AREA  PIC X(29).
084600     05  LEGAL-DESCR-PRT-AREA2 PIC X(29).
084700     05  LEGAL-DESCR-PRT-AREA3 PIC X(29).
084800     05  LEGAL-DESCR-PRT-AREA4 PIC X(29).
084900     05  LEGAL-DESCR-PRT-AREA5 PIC X(29).



293100     PERFORM XXX-REMOVE-BLANK-CHARS
293200     VARYING CUR-CHAR FROM 1 BY  1 UNTIL CUR-CHAR > 199
293300     MOVE LEGAL-DESC-TABLE TO LEGAL-DESC-AREA
293400     .
293500 XXX-REMOVE-BLANK-CHARS.
293600     COMPUTE PREV-CHAR = CUR-CHAR - 1
293700     IF PREV-CHAR < 1
293800        MOVE 1 TO PREV-CHAR
293900     END-IF
294000     IF LEGAL-DESCRIPTION(CUR-CHAR:1) <> ' '
294100     OR (LEGAL-DESCRIPTION(CUR-CHAR:1) = ' '
294200     AND LEGAL-DESCRIPTION(PREV-CHAR:1) <> ' ')
294300          ADD 1 TO LEGAL-SUB
294400          MOVE LEGAL-DESCRIPTION(CUR-CHAR:1)
                     TO LEGAL-DESC(LEGAL-SUB)

"Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:x3cR6.26538$fb7.2995072@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "DM Hancock" <dmh429@swbell.net> wrote in message
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T07:02:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B14EF72.D5AADE0@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```


DM Hancock wrote:

> I have a variable which consists of data in no particular arrangement.  I
> need to remove the embedded spaces.
>
> I can't use inspect/replacing because the two arguments have to be the same
> size.

So you're not really removing the spaces, you're moving them to the end?

> I also can't use string/unstring.

Why is this?


> Does anyone know of a function or intrinsic to do this? (I'm using an HP3000
> v 6.5)
>
> I am trying to stay away from a byte by byte comparison to remove the
> spaces.  (It's my last resort.)

Why are you trying to do this?  It is a simple, quick, and easy way of doing it.
```

#### ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

- **From:** "charles" <fjlk@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T03:07:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rziR6.49544$Ji.1188032@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
One way to do this is to just redefine the  picture clause as an array, or
move it to a generic long field that is already redefined as an array.  Then
use a counter or varying to cycle thru the variable and concatanate with the
string command one character at a time, that is not a space, into a
temporary field. Then all of the white space is on the end.  If you use a
counter, you already know how long it is.  Then you can use the counter with
reference modification.

Something like this would be good for a crude bar graph or a statistic
diagram.

move temp-field (1:cntr) to new-field.

If you can use reference modification  with a counter it is a slick way to
do it in a loop.  Using 2 counters one for the sending field and one for the
receiving field can be quite useful.  Without reference modification you
have to redefine the field as an array and use sub-scripting one location at
a time.

01  Test-Fld-Table.
      05  Test-Field                   pic x(100).
      05  filler redefnes Test-Field.
            10  Test-Array occurs 100 times indexed by sub-1
                    pic x(01).

(I didnt test this)

The reference modification is just a throwback to Assembly.  In some
languages you could use reference modification and a shift operator <<.
That would just move part of a field over one byte.  This might be a good
project for an old Assembly programmer.  We use to do something like this in
assembly class to add a floating dollar sign to a numeric value.  It took 5
commands to accomplish without macros.


It is possible to determine the length without spaces by using inspect with
tallying and function reverse.  However, inspect is a memory hog and could
slow you down in a large program.  Sometimes the simpler, harder, clunkier
code is just faster.


"DM Hancock" <dmh429@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net...
> I have a variable which consists of data in no particular arrangement.  I
> need to remove the embedded spaces.
>
> I can't use inspect/replacing because the two arguments have to be the
same
> size.
> I also can't use string/unstring.
>
> Does anyone know of a function or intrinsic to do this? (I'm using an
HP3000
> v 6.5)
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
> 
> Here is some information, from Microsoft's Knowledge Base, that applies to your
problem:
> 
> This CD ROM was created using the Joliet file system, to enable the use of long
file names and file extensions greater than three characters.  Older DOS
16-bit
"real mode" CD ROM device drivers cannot read the non-8.3 portions of
the CD ROM;
this requires a 32-bit protected mode CD ROM device driver.
> 
> The symptoms experienced when installing on a system with real mode drivers
include the following:
> 
> 1. Autorun.inf will not auto-execute setup upon placing the CD ROM in the drive.
> 
> 2. If Setup.exe is executed manually, by using the Run option from the Start
menu, the error message "Cannot find the file [drive letter]:\setup.exe
(or one of
its components)" appears. The error message continues with "Make sure
the path and
file name are correct and that all required libraries are available."
> 
> 3. If Setup.exe is executed manually, by double-clicking on the file name, in
Windows's Explorer, the "Program Not Found" dialog box appears.  Certain
folders
on the CD ROM will not open.  Instead, the error message "The folder
[drive
letter]:\foldername does not exist" appears, or the folder will open but

empty.

> Pearson User Services
-------- My response----------
I wasn't trying to install it on my Windows box. I was looking at it on
a Solaris PC,
and on a Solaris Sparc system. I found my original V3 starter CD, which
has no problems being read on any of the systems. I was able to copy the
book files on one system, and transfer the files to my V4 system for
use.

The one from the book has been tried on W98, W98SE, NT, 2000PRO, Solaris
8/PC, Solaris 8/SunBlade-100. All of the above systems included the
latest driver/OS updates, and used Creative DVD, CDRW, CD, TEAC CD, and
a Sony CD. All are cleaned each month, and all have one problem or
another reading ONLY that CD. I talked to Thane Hubble, and tried
several cleaning solutions, including a cleaning solution I use in
making PCBs. 

Since I have recovered most of what I wanted off the CD, I can get by
making another CD with just the book materials on it.

I am more interested in the passwords I need for the Unix version, and
have sent Fujitsu a request.

Gary

Reply to Fujitsu:
The docs on the V3.0 release state there is NO expiration date.
There was no purchase price, so renewing it should be $0, but with no
expiration date ???
Come on, it is not usable in today's environment. I just want to see it
work in a Solaris environment. V3 for Win is still available with a
download!

If you want to change rules in the middle of the stream, I think the
newsgroup should know it. How many of us have PAID for the run-time in
6.x, only to find no requirement to pay in 6.x+???

For crying out loud! You are the only one currently charging RT licenses
for a mid-version. Even if it's cheaper than others, charging for a
"FREE" version would lose VERY much face for you. It's not like I would
try to make money on an old version, I just want to try the "FREE
LICENSE" quoted in the user's agreement.

A copy of this, and previous posts/e-mail will be placed on the
comp.lang.cobol newsgroup.
---------------------------------------------------------------------

Current message!


OK, I'm mad! I bought Thane's book to get myself up to speed on COBOL,
after a 5-10 year vacation. I couldn't READ the dammn thing! Sam's is at

Gary (I DON'T FEEL BETTER NOW!)
```

```
EXEC DLI commands. From this program I want to call a subroutine (CALL
```

```
Hi:

    The DL/I database is nothing more than IMS. If you want a subroutine of
your own to do something else and then do the DL/I calls just pass the PCB
info into your program and then pass the information to DL/I when you issue
the call.

Barry Steinholtz
```

<a id="t-3af121a993"></a>
## Thread: RePost of the code for imbedded blanks problem. — 11 this month / 13 total

### RePost of the code for imbedded blanks problem.

- **From:** Foodman <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T14:44:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lH7R6.5283$rn5.262199@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Hi:

I haven't the foggiest why the first post was all screwed up in
regard to vertical alignment. Obviously, it wouldn't compile
if it was the way it looked.

Anyway, the following looks fine in notepad and on my screen 
at this moment.

If it gets screwed up I give up, or, anyone know why it does this?



IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.              ELIMINATOR.
AUTHOR.                  A. R. DILWORTH.
DATE-WRITTEN.            5/30/1.
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  W-FIELD-1.
02  W-CHARACTER-1      PIC X(01) OCCURS 50 TIMES.
01  W-FIELD-2.
02  W-CHARACTER-2      PIC X(01) OCCURS 50 TIMES.
01  W-STRING-SIZE          PIC 9(02) VALUE 50.
01  X1                     PIC 9(03) VALUE ZERO.
01  X2                     PIC 9(03) VALUE ZERO.

PROCEDURE DIVISION. 	
A.
DISPLAY (1, 1) ERASE.
B.
ACCEPT (1, 10) W-FIELD-1.
IF W-FIELD-1 = SPACES
GO TO END-PROG.
PERFORM ELIMINATE-ROUTINE.
DISPLAY (2, 10) W-FIELD-2.
GO TO B.
ELIMINATE-ROUTINE.
MOVE SPACE TO W-FIELD-2.
PERFORM ELIMINATE-SPACES 
VARYING X1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X1 > W-STRING-SIZE.
ELIMINATE-SPACES.
IF X1 = 1 MOVE 1 TO X2.
IF W-CHARACTER-1 (X1) NOT = SPACE
MOVE W-CHARACTER-1 (X1) TO W-CHARACTER-2 (X2),
ADD 1 TO X2.                
END-PROG.
DISPLAY (1, 1) ERASE.
STOP RUN.
```

#### ↳ Re: RePost of the code for imbedded blanks problem.

- **From:** Foodman <nospam@newsranger.com>

```
I give up.
```

#### ↳ Re: RePost of the code for imbedded blanks problem.

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T15:22:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010530112257.11788.00001667@ng-ft1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<lH7R6.5283$rn5.262199@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Tony, let me have a crack at it and see if it shows up better, for whatever
reason:

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.              ELIMINATOR.
AUTHOR.                  A. R. DILWORTH.
DATE-WRITTEN.            5/30/1.
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01  W-FIELD-1.
         02  W-CHARACTER-1      PIC X(01) OCCURS 50 TIMES.
    01  W-FIELD-2.
         02  W-CHARACTER-2      PIC X(01) OCCURS 50 TIMES.
    01  W-STRING-SIZE          PIC 9(02) VALUE 50.
    01  X1                     PIC 9(03) VALUE ZERO.  
    01  X2                     PIC 9(03) VALUE ZERO.

PROCEDURE DIVISION. 	
A.
    DISPLAY (1, 1) ERASE.

B.
    ACCEPT (1, 10) W-FIELD-1.
    IF W-FIELD-1 = SPACES
        GO TO END-PROG.
    PERFORM ELIMINATE-ROUTINE.
    DISPLAY (2, 10) W-FIELD-2.
    GO TO B.

ELIMINATE-ROUTINE.
     MOVE SPACE TO W-FIELD-2.
     PERFORM ELIMINATE-SPACES 
     VARYING X1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X1 > W-STRING-SIZE.

ELIMINATE-SPACES.
    IF X1 = 1 MOVE 1 TO X2.
    IF W-CHARACTER-1 (X1) NOT = SPACE
    MOVE W-CHARACTER-1 (X1) TO W-CHARACTER-2 (X2),
    ADD 1 TO X2.                

END-PROG.
     DISPLAY (1, 1) ERASE.
     STOP RUN.

Let's take a look.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
and what about this without go to?
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
A.
    DISPLAY (1, 1) ERASE.
    ACCEPT (1, 10) W-FIELD-1.
   perform until W-FIELD-1 = SPACES
    PERFORM ELIMINATE-ROUTINE
    DISPLAY (2, 10) W-FIELD-2
    ACCEPT (1, 10) W-FIELD-1.

END-PROG.
     DISPLAY (1, 1) ERASE.
     STOP RUN.

ELIMINATE-ROUTINE.
     MOVE SPACE TO W-FIELD-2.
     PERFORM ELIMINATE-SPACES
     VARYING X1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X1 > W-STRING-SIZE.

ELIMINATE-SPACES.
    IF X1 = 1 MOVE 1 TO X2.
    IF W-CHARACTER-1 (X1) NOT = SPACE
    MOVE W-CHARACTER-1 (X1) TO W-CHARACTER-2 (X2),
    ADD 1 TO X2.
```

```
>PROCEDURE DIVISION.

Hi:

It's fine if your objective is to eliminate GO TO. The thing I
find depressing is that a lot of people (not necessarily you)
think that you have to completely eliminate GO TO without
regard to whether it makes sense. To me at least I have to
reread it a time or two to see what you are doing since the
style is foreign to me.  This is not the case with my original
code which is straightforward and utterly simple.

In the trivial example here, there is nothing wrong with GO TO.

Thanks for taking the time to comment.

Tony Dilworth
```

```
Foodman wrote:

> Hi:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tony Dilworth

To me, I had to reread yours a time or to for the same reason.   The big
advantage of his rewrite is that I could see the basic program all in one
spot:

> PROCEDURE DIVISION.
> A.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Once I see this, I drop down and see what ELIMINATE-ROUTINE does.  I
don't have to worry about moving around code to see the big picture.  I
would have preferred to leave out the END-PROG label though.  It isn't
the GO TO which bothers me, as much as is the dropping to a paragraph by
default, which has caused more maintenance errors.

Anybody can write this program fast, putting in dummy performs, and
fleshing them out after coding the basic logic.
```

```
compilers currently available for development on Win32 systems?

experience.

Merant NetExpress 3.1
AcuCorp AcuCobol-GT
Liant RM/Cobol-85

Am I missing someone obvious?
```

```
To complete an IBM mainframe-to-Dell 4300 server migration, Caldwell hired
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: From the dustbin, Cobol rises

- **From:** "David P. Bretz" <DBretz@mescoma.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T15:20:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1547FA.A63DD6E5@mescoma.com>`
- **References:** `<3B1520D2.651DEE90@brazee.net> <AJ9R6.7428$DW1.315916@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
What I find more interesting (and incredible) is how narrow minded both
employers and recruiters are when looking at a programmer trying to get a
position using a new skill.  I'm a 17 year veteran of mainframe applications
programming (COBOL, TICS, DB2, VSAM, JCL, etc.) who made the time and effort
(and expense) of taking the training and becoming a Siebel Certified
Consultant, just to  hopefully broaden my IT horizons.   Well, you'd think I
was some wet-behind-the-ears college grad with no programming experience at
all, based on the reaction I've gotten.

Never mind 17 years of programming experience.  Never mind the intuition for
problem solving.  Never mind the years of interacting with users, writing
business specs, technical specs, etc.  Since I don't have at least 1 year of
experience specifically with Siebel, I can't find a job!  How myopic is that?



NA wrote:

> In article <3B1520D2.651DEE90@brazee.net>,
> Howard Brazee  <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ From the dustbin, Cobol rises (or C doesn't float?)

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@ont.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T13:30:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B16A9FB.85A762F1@ont.com>`
- **References:** `<3B1520D2.651DEE90@brazee.net> <WxiR6.17807$v4.250564@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com>`

```
Grinder wrote:
> 
> > http://www.zdnet.com/eweek/stories/general/0,11011,2764006,00.html
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> about Pearl Harbor veterans?

The big question is will it cause a 13% increase in salaries for 
existing programmers? (COBOL of course!) 

I just scanned the Dallas area for COBOL positions, and it's running
25%-45% below what they are posting for C/C++ programmers.

If it's so valuable, why the difference? This looks like a good 
thread to keep going!

Let's add a few sub-threads and maybe break them into threads of 
their own.

1. Why call COBOL-based systems "legacy"? computerjobs.com has:
 Legacy Systems
  ADABAS, Assembler, CA-, CICS, COBOL, DB2,
  DEC/VAX, FOCUS, IDMS, IMS, MicroFocus,
  MQSeries, MVS, Natural, OS/2, OS/390, PL/1,
  REXX, SAS, Tape Operator, TCP/IP, Teradata, VM,
  VSAM
   
  as a major section when for searching for jobs. If TCP/IP, DB2,
  MQSeries, and SAS are "Legacy" systems, which means "85% of our
  income is based on these systems, but we offer 50%-70 % of the
  salary and benefits of other IT type people", is this a BAD label
  or just because we don't demand to be rewarded for our work? The
  HR industry has joined with the acadamia-nuts to label "business
  programmers" with a *name* that implies-Not really that valuable,
  Not wanted, but required for 6 months or so..., Well we can't
  TRASH them yet, but...

2. Why haven't we (COBOL literate) demanded to have the term 
  "maintenance" changed to updates, enhancements, modifications?
  If C/C++ programmers can change there names to "Software Engineer",
  Why can't we change ours to "Core Business Programmers"? Pick
  another-start another thread...

3. Why should the systems and systems admin types make more than the
   Core Business Programmers-they just keep the environment running
   for *our* do-or-die business systems programming?

4. Why should languages that can't keep rounding errors out of the
   final results be *better* than one that keeps the business
   accounts in balance?

5. Why does M$ acronyms pass the spell checkers, but ANYTHING
   "legacy" require the "learn" button?

6-? Good place to add you input...

Gary  (I feel MUCH better now!)



2. 

2. Why should a job be
```

### DSNAME including date and time

- **From:** jmindoc@mclaneco.com (John Mindock)
- **Date:** 2001-05-30T14:19:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<18bd37b1.0105301319.195836ea@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello -

In an IBM 3090 JES2 environment:
Is there a way (using JCL, JES2, etc.) to create datasets during a
job's execution that have names like:

MYSYSTEM.MYFILE.CREATED.&SYSDATE.&SYSTIME?

Where somehow &SYSDATE and &SYSTIME contain the date/time the file was
created?

Thanks in advance for any assistance.
John
```

- **References:** `<18bd37b1.0105301319.195836ea@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi John:

    You'd have to create the information and then have a program input
this information by modifying a Proc. However, without going crazy this
information is available just by listing the catalog.

Barry Steinholtz

John Mindock wrote:

> Hello -
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> John
```

#### ↳ Re: DSNAME including date and time

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T11:46:40+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gr0chtgm4hgre1r9ilusfm9ohu0q647ipd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<18bd37b1.0105301319.195836ea@posting.google.com>`

```
On 30 May 2001 14:19:04 -0700 jmindoc@mclaneco.com (John Mindock) wrote:

:>In an IBM 3090 JES2 environment:
:>Is there a way (using JCL, JES2, etc.) to create datasets during a
:>job's execution that have names like:

:>MYSYSTEM.MYFILE.CREATED.&SYSDATE.&SYSTIME?

:>Where somehow &SYSDATE and &SYSTIME contain the date/time the file was
:>created?

:>Thanks in advance for any assistance.

You can use the dynamic system symbols if your job is invoked via a START
command.

You can use a CLIST/REXX to dynamically build the JCL and SUBMIT it. 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DSNAME including date and time

- **From:** jmindoc@mclaneco.com (John Mindock)
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T09:10:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<18bd37b1.0105310810.7ea27508@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<18bd37b1.0105301319.195836ea@posting.google.com> <gr0chtgm4hgre1r9ilusfm9ohu0q647ipd@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message news:<gr0chtgm4hgre1r9ilusfm9ohu0q647ipd@4ax.com>...
> On 30 May 2001 14:19:04 -0700 jmindoc@mclaneco.com (John Mindock) wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> You can use a CLIST/REXX to dynamically build the JCL and SUBMIT it.


Thanks to all - I used the information and other sources to get a solution.

Using Control-M this construct: 
DSN=JMINDOC.TEST.IEBGENER.D%%RDATE.T%%TIME creates      
JMINDOC.TEST.IEBGENER.D010531.T110340.
```

#### ↳ Re: DSNAME including date and time

- **From:** Timothy_S_Coffey@bankone.com (Tim Coffey)
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T11:36:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e32280da.0105311036.3d7d93dd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<18bd37b1.0105301319.195836ea@posting.google.com>`

```
jmindoc@mclaneco.com (John Mindock) wrote in message news:<18bd37b1.0105301319.195836ea@posting.google.com>...
> Hello -
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> John


I do this all the time in COBOL. I have full-function dynamic
allocation (SVC99) routines written specifically for use by COBOL
programs. You can dynamically specify the DSN, UNIT, DCB...anything.
After you dynamically specify the file (or rather, the DD) you then
OPEN the FD associated with the file and process it as normal. After
the CLOSE, you can the dynamically unallocate the file, or just let
the unallocate happen at EOJ.

Let me know if you would like the routines.
```

<a id="t-8cf40a1267"></a>
## Thread: You learn something every day... — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### You learn something every day...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T11:07:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5CpR6.353$2G.34364@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
(Much easier to read in richtext/html)


As you know, I often refer CLC users to my COBFD software, which parses a COBOL FD and provides a report showing size and offset for each dataname in a record.

One of those who downloaded and used it wrote me with a little problem he was having...


USER:
I was just testing your program and have found that it does not appear to
handle signed data fields.  For example one of my fields is S9(5)V9(2) which
is a field of 8 in size (including the sign), however the report shows it to
be 7 in length and calculates it in the offset listing as such....

         [Output report attached by user, edited for size/relevance]

05-29-2001 14:30      Tal Systems' COBOL FD Analyzer v 1.50           Page    5
COBOL Source File C:\COBOL\EMECM.RL                (c) 1996,1998 M.C. Mattias

Level and Dataname                         Picture Type  Occurs Size    Offset
------------------                         ------- ----  ------ -----   ------

01 EMECM-REC                       Group                          458      0
...
05 ECM-REG-NO                                X(10)  AN             10     36
05 ECM-SUB-ACCOM                        S9(5)V9(2)  N               7     46
05 ECM-SUNDIRES                         S9(5)V9(2)  N               7     53
05 ECM-ENTERTAINMENT                    S9(5)V9(2)  N               7     60


05-29-2001 14:30      Tal Systems' COBOL FD Analyzer v 1.50           Page    6
COBOL Source File C:\COBOL\EMECM.RL                (c) 1996,1998 M.C. Mattias
COBOL Source File Listing
------------------------- 

       01  EMECM-REC.
               ....
               05  ECM-REG-NO                         PIC X(10).
               05  ECM-SUB-ACCOM                      PIC S9(5)V9(2).
               05  ECM-SUNDIRES                       PIC S9(5)V9(2).
               05  ECM-ENTERTAINMENT                  PIC S9(5)V9(2).
               ....
 End Of COBOL input file

                  [end of attachment]

MCM:

Sir;

PIC[TURE] [IS] S9(5)V9(2) IS size 7. The sign is overpunched in the LSB (or
in the MSB, if SIGN LEADING is used).

PIC[TURE] [IS] S9(5)V9(2) SIGN [IS] SEPARATE [LEADING]  would be size 8.



USER:
This is true if LEADING or TRAILING sign is specified.  If it is not
specified where the sign should go then the sign will take up a single
character at the beginning of the data item.  This is certainly true for
Acucobol.

for example

pic s9(05)v9(02) would take up 8 characters.  It would only take up 7 if
LEADING or TRAILING sign was specified.

MCM:
... this behavior is not true for any ANSI-standard compiler.
Perhaps your compiler has some kind of compile-time switch which causes
a default behavior of "DISPLAY NUMERIC IS SIGN LEADING SEPARATE" ....


USER COLLEAGUE:

I understand that a colleague of mine has been emailing you about COBFD.
Specifically about the sign character within a PIC clause.  You were quite
correct, we are using a compiler switch which forces the sign to be
separate.  The switch we use is the -Cr  compiler command.  This is maintain
compatibility with the original code which was written over 10 years ago.
Rather than converting all our files to have the sign overpunched, it was
decided that it would be easier to just add the -Cr switch to the compile
script.

The -Cr Switch forces the code to RM/COBOL compatibility mode.  Maybe this
could be a future enhancement for the COBFD program.  I am sure that we are
not the only company that uses this switch.

=====

I had never heard of this kind of switch before.  As I said, you learn something every day.
```

#### ↳ Re: You learn something every day...

- **From:** Rob Lec <RobLec@cinci-rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T14:13:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1651BA.B5027FCF@cinci-rr.com>`
- **References:** `<5CpR6.353$2G.34364@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

> Specifically about the sign character within a PIC clause.  You were quite
> correct, we are using a compiler switch which forces the sign to be separate.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

AcuCobol has so many options that it is dizzying. During my brief
experience
with the language I found it to be so flexible - so readily tailored to
mimic the peculiarities of each specific implementation - that it is
probably
the most ideal solution for *quickly* porting existing code from one
platform
to another... certainly moreso than any other compiler I've encountered.

The downside is that it does tend to perpetuate any quirks that may
exist
in the codebase due to some previous implementation.  That certainly
seems
to be the case in this instance.  Working with it over time, one tends
to
forget what standard code might have looked like originally, and often
being faced with developing ways to "work-around the work-arounds."

-------------------------------------------------
(To reply by email change dash to dot in address)
```

#### ↳ Re: You learn something every day...

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T10:24:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yotR6.4$PQ2.4849@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<5CpR6.353$2G.34364@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:5CpR6.353$2G.34364@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net...
[snip]
>USER COLLEAGUE:

>I understand that a colleague of mine has been emailing you about COBFD.
>Specifically about the sign character within a PIC clause.  You were quite
>correct, we are using a compiler switch which forces the sign to be
>separate.  The switch we use is the -Cr  compiler command.  This is
maintain
>compatibility with the original code which was written over 10 years ago.
>Rather than converting all our files to have the sign overpunched, it was
>decided that it would be easier to just add the -Cr switch to the compile
>script.

>The -Cr Switch forces the code to RM/COBOL compatibility mode.  Maybe this
>could be a future enhancement for the COBFD program.  I am sure that we are
>not the only company that uses this switch.

Just a note about 'RM/COBOL compatibility mode':

As far as I am aware, all of our competitors offer an 'RM/COBOL
compatibility mode' which forces this, and other, behaviors.  This was the
behavior of the ANSI 1974-based RM/COBOL, available on many minicomputers in
the 70s and on PCs in the early 80s.  Liant Software Corporation no longer
offers this version of RM/COBOL.

The current version of RM/COBOL was previously known as RM/COBOL-85, to
distinguish it from its ancestor.  Interestingly enough, even RM/COBOL has
an 'RM/COBOL compatibility mode' achieved by a particular set of compiler
switches, one of which forces separate sign.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

```
> Hi:
```

#### ↳ Re: Structured versus the other way.

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T15:10:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0105311410.35d6f677@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<VtqR6.6788$rn5.317795@www.newsranger.com>`

```
foodman <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message news:<VtqR6.6788$rn5.317795@www.newsranger.com>...
> Hi:
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


This style ALWAYS looks simpler for short programs which have a single
function.  I have seen this kind of example used for years and years
to justify "Top-down with forward GO TO's".

The problems are with maintenance.  
SCENARIO:
Your program is in production.  The user now wants to add five more
fields, with their own edits and with cross edits. Six months later,
the user needs the inputs to be validated against an input file. A
while later, certain validated input data is to be used to create a
new file.  Then, some data will be used to create a file, and some
will be used to update 2 different files. A month later, the
validation and update criteria change.

By now, you KNOW it's a mess of spaghetti.  Not only that, it will be
a testing nightmare because logically related procedures may be in
very widely spaced locations in the code -- easy to miss.

I'll take Mr Dashwood's style any day!  It's mine, too! 

Stephen J Spiro
```

## Thread: convert cobol — 4 this month / 81 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### convert cobol

- **From:** Oliver Füllgraf <fuellgraf@ngi.de>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T19:18:05+02:00

- **Message-ID:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1>`

```
Hi,

6 year old cobol, into visual basic or java ?

Olli
```

#### ↳ Re: convert cobol

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T16:42:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<62zR6.52692$W14.3644959@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1>`

```
I don't think you'll get much sympathy from this group on this
point, but I'm willing to speculate.  I'm not a long-term Cobol
users, but have sufficient experience with VB and Java.

I've not found any tools that purport to do this, but I suspect
they would be severely limited by the variety of coding styles
in use.

Some general thoughts, though:

Any cross-platform conversion of code will not be easy.  You
will have to inspect each line of source code by the end of the
project's conversion.

That said, however, the design of Cobol provides some benefits
in this area.

Namely, there little concept of "scope" in the language, so the
encapsulation of data structures will be relatively easy to
accomplish.  Some features of the language, such as unions
or redefines will present problems, but are resolvable.

Also, since there are no subroutines, the procedural code can
be "canned" fairly easily within the application.  A lot of code
aimed at moving data across program boundaries will no
longer be needed, but any attempt at automatically converting
this will be difficult.

A larger issues is the dynamics of the program.  Presumably,
your application follows a DOS-style of navigation.  Even if
you're using SP2 or another GUI designer, the underlying
code is procedural, not event-driven.  This is a significant
challenge.

A less ambitious approach may prove to be the best.  Cook
out the business rules, and use them as a specification for
your re-work.  Rather than carry vestigial presumptions
about presentation, it's better (in my opinion) to cut the
wheat from the chaff.

AcuCorp makes a reverse engineering tool to is said to be
amazing--at least by their sales force.  Flexus has an
excellent links page for those engaged in this enterprise.

Good luck.
```

## Thread: International Vote on COBOL Final Committee Draft — 4 this month / 9 total

### International Vote on COBOL Final Committee Draft

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T14:26:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0105311326.ed32907@posting.google.com>`

```
Here is the final vote. It would appear that Australia did not send in
a comment. Recommended changes will be applied, and it will be
submitted as a Final Draft Information Standard, when another vote
will be taken, and public comments will be solicited again.

SUBJECT: Summary of Voting on SC 22 N 3204, FCD Ballot on FCD 1989,
Information
Technology - Programming Languages - Cobol
----------------------------------------------------------------------
The following responses have been received on the subject of approval:
"P" Members supporting approval without comment

7 (Canada, Czech Republic, Denmark, Egypt, Ireland, Norway, Russian
Federation, )

"P" Members supporting approval with comments
5 (Germany, Japan, Netherlands, United Kingdom, United States)

"P" Members not supporting approval
0

"P" Members abstaining
1 (France)

"P" Members not voting
8 (Austria, Belgium, Brazil, China, Finland, Romania, Slovenia,
Ukraine)
```

#### ↳ Re: International Vote on COBOL Final Committee Draft

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-05-31T17:19:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f6g0v$b29$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105311326.ed32907@posting.google.com>`

```
The *actual* next steps are:

1) Resolve international comments

2) Countries are allowed (but almost never do) change their "YES" votes to
"NO" because of "fixes" done for other countries' comments

3) This is EXPECTED to be completed around the end of 1st quarter or
beginning of 2nd quarter of 2002.

4) There is then an international ballot on "DIS" (Draft International
Standard).  Public Review comments ARE *not* appropriate at this time.  It
is usually just a "yes" or "no" vote.  *NO* real changes are allowed at this
time (except those MINOR editorial changes required by the ISO secretariat).
This ballot period is (again) a 4 month ballot (internationally - there will
probably be a US ballot during that time).

5) ASSUMING this ballot passes, then the DIS (Draft International Standard)
becomes an IS (International Standard) - currently expected around the end
of 2002.

6) The US (and probably other countries) then "adopt" it as a national
Standard.  (In the US this is "automatic" - so it will become an ANSI
Standard "roughly" as soon as it becomes an ISO Standard)

FYI - to see the "official" set of ISO comments (and ballots) go to:

 http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/01-0373.pdf
```

```
Actually, the standard class library and the Finalizer (garbage
collection) are nearly finished.  We HAD a class library which was
pulled back for technical reasons beyeond my expertise, but the new
effort has been building on that work.  I know that a lot of work has
been done on the Finalizer, and there have been a couple of ad-hoc
committee meetings to complement the e-mail discussions.  We hope to
have both out soon, as Technical Reports (something like ammendments).
 And truthfully, the Brits weren't fair; it's 17 years only if you do
not count the 2 ammendments, otherwise, 10 years.
```
